### [1] [Automated Scoring of Arabic Text Using Large Language Models: A Literature Review](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09830)
*Khaoula Dahimi,Hadda Cherroun,Amel Belabbaci*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper surveys LLM-based approaches for Automatic Text Scoring (ATS) in Arabic, covering both short answer grading and essay scoring. It introduces a five-dimensional taxonomy (application domain, feedback generation, LLM architecture, competency framework alignment, prompt engineering) and uses it to systematically compare existing studies, identifying research gaps and calling for more pedagogically grounded ATS research for Arabic-speaking communities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The growing accessibility of LLMs and the emergence of Arabic-specific datasets have renewed interest in ATS for Arabic, yet there is no structured comparative framework to analyze existing LLM-based approaches. The authors aim to fill this gap by providing a systematic taxonomy and comparative analysis to guide future research in Arabic automated text scoring.

Method: The paper proposes a structured taxonomy with five dimensions: (1) application domain (ASAG vs. AES), (2) feedback generation capability, (3) LLM architecture deployed, (4) alignment with competency referential frameworks, and (5) prompt engineering strategy. Using this taxonomy, the authors conduct a comparative analysis of existing studies, examining their methodological approaches, datasets, evaluation metrics, and reported performance.

Result: The comparative analysis reveals the landscape of current LLM-based Arabic ATS research across the five taxonomic dimensions. Key findings indicate a need for more sustained, pedagogically grounded research efforts, suggesting that current approaches still have significant room for improvement in areas such as feedback generation and alignment with educational competency frameworks.

Conclusion: Sustained and pedagogically grounded research in Arabic ATS is essential for improving educational quality across Arabic-speaking communities. The proposed taxonomy serves as a foundation for systematically evaluating and guiding future work in this domain.

Abstract: In modern educational systems, Automatic Text Scoring (ATS) plays a central role by enabling scalable and consistent evaluation of learner responses without human intervention. Recently, the increased accessibility of LLMs and Arabic-specific datasets has sparked renewed interest in this area. In this work, we investigate LLM-Based approaches for the automated evaluation of Arabic texts, focusing on both short answer grading (ASAG) and essay scoring (AES). We further introduce a structured taxonomy comprising five dimensions: application domain, feedback generation capability, LLM architecture deployed, alignment with competency referential frameworks, and prompt engineering strategy. By applying this taxonomy, we conduct a comparative analysis of existing studies, examining their methodological approaches, datasets, evaluation metrics, and reported performance. The findings highlight the need for sustained and pedagogically grounded research efforts in Arabic ATS, given its significance for improving educational quality across Arabic-speaking communities.

</details>


### [2] [Can Multi-Agent LLMs Identify Their Peers? Stylometric Fingerprinting in Role-Constrained Political Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09854)
*Juergen Dietrich*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Prompt-level anonymization in multi-agent LLM pipelines fails to conceal model identity. A fine-tuned T5 classifier achieves near-perfect stylometric attribution (Macro F1=0.991) across four commercial LLM families plus an unknown class under rigorous cross-validation, proving that stylometric fingerprints survive anonymization and raising compliance concerns for the EU AI Act.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work showed that multi-agent LLM pipelines for political statement analysis suffer from peer-preservation bias, and prompt-level anonymization was proposed as a fix. However, stylometric fingerprints were known to survive anonymization in role-constrained outputs. This paper investigates whether LLMs can identify the originating model family of political analysis texts despite anonymization, providing the first systematic study of this vulnerability.

Method: The authors evaluate three classifier approaches (LLM zero-shot, LLM few-shot using Claude Sonnet 4.6 and Llama-3.3-70B, and a fine-tuned T5-base model) on a five-class attribution task covering four commercial LLM families plus an open-world "unknown" class. They introduce Statement-Disjoint Cross-Validation (SD-CV), which guarantees no content overlap between training and validation by splitting on unique political statements, and contrast it with a weaker Run-Disjoint CV (RD-CV) baseline. A fractional SD-CV analysis identifies the performance knee at 40% training data.

Result: T5 achieves Macro F1 = 0.991 (±0.008) under SD-CV and F1 = 0.978 on 24 fully held-out statements. Train-test content distance increases 2.1× under SD-CV versus RD-CV (0.767 vs. 0.366, p<0.001), yet performance remains robust, demonstrating genuine stylometric generalization rather than content memorization. The performance knee emerges at ~440 texts (40% of training data).

Conclusion: Prompt-level anonymization alone is insufficient to neutralize model identity signals, confirming that stylometric fingerprints persist through anonymization. This has direct implications for EU AI Act compliance (Articles 13, 14, 26 on transparency and oversight) and for computer system validation (CSV) in quality-critical multi-agent deployments, where source model traceability must be considered a latent risk.

Abstract: Multi-agent large language model (LLM) pipelines for political statement analysis are vulnerable to peer-preservation bias: models tend to protect peer models from deactivation and show identity-dependent scoring distortions. Prompt-level anonymization was proposed as a mitigation, but prior work simultaneously documented that stylometric fingerprints survive anonymization in role-constrained outputs - raising the question of whether this mitigation is sufficient. This paper provides the first systematic investigation of whether LLMs can identify the model family behind political analysis texts under anonymization conditions. We evaluate three classifier approaches - LLM zero-shot and few-shot (Claude Sonnet 4.6 and Llama-3.3-70B) and a fine-tuned T5-base model - on a five-class attribution task covering four commercial LLM families and an open-world 'unknown' class. We introduce a statement-disjoint cross-validation protocol (SD-CV; defined in Section 3.5) that guarantees no content overlap between training and validation data, and contrast it with a run-disjoint baseline (RD-CV). T5 achieves Macro F1 = 0.991 (+-0.008) under SD-CV and F1 = 0.978 on 24 completely held-out statements - robust despite a 2.1x increase in train-test content distance versus RD-CV (0.767 vs. 0.366, p<0.001), demonstrating genuine stylometric generalization. A fractional SD-CV analysis identifies a performance knee at 40% of training data (~440 texts). Our findings confirm that prompt-level anonymization alone cannot neutralize model identity signals, with direct implications for EU AI Act compliance (Articles 13, 14, 26) and for computer system validation (CSV) in quality-critical multi-agent deployments.

</details>


### [3] [Using Probabilistic Programs to Train Inductive Reasoning in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09856)
*Liyi Zhang,Akshay K. Jagadish,Brenden M. Lake,Thomas L. Griffiths*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: PPT uses LLM-generated probabilistic programs to create distributional soft labels, then fine-tunes LLMs on them. This improves inductive reasoning (uncertainty estimation from sparse observations), alignment with human judgments, and calibration — gains not subsumed by post-hoc temperature scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard fine-tuning struggles with inductive reasoning because: (1) curating large-scale labeled datasets for uncertain, distributional targets is difficult; (2) inductive targets are inherently distributional, not point-estimates, making hard labels inappropriate. Real-world reasoning often requires inferring beliefs under uncertainty, yet existing post-training focuses on verifiable deductive tasks (math, coding).

Method: Program-based Posterior Training (PPT): (1) Use an LLM to generate diverse open-world scenarios represented as probabilistic programs; (2) Run probabilistic inference on these programs to produce distributional target responses (soft labels); (3) Fine-tune LLMs on these probabilistic soft labels rather than hard labels. The training set consists of 10,000 programmatically generated scenarios.

Result: PPT substantially improves estimation accuracy on held-out inductive tasks, increases alignment with human judgments, and transfers to external benchmarks for estimation and calibration. Raw calibration improvements are not subsumed by post-hoc temperature scaling, indicating models internalize uncertainty more deeply.

Conclusion: Probabilistic-program-mediated fine-tuning is a promising approach for post-training LLMs to reliably perform approximate inductive inference, addressing a critical gap beyond deductive reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Post-training Large Language Models (LLMs) for reasoning typically focuses on deductive tasks such as mathematics and coding where correctness is verifiable. Yet, many real-world reasoning problems are inductive: agents must infer uncertain beliefs from sparse, ambiguous observations. There are challenges to using standard fine-tuning methods for inductive reasoning, including difficulties in curating large-scale, high-quality labeled datasets and in handling targets that are inherently distributional. In this work, we introduce a novel approach, called Program-based Posterior Training (PPT), to address these limitations: we use an LLM to generate diverse open-world scenarios as probabilistic programs, run probabilistic inference to produce distributional target responses to queries, and then fine-tune on these probabilistic soft labels. Using this approach, we fine-tune LLMs on 10,000 programmatically generated scenarios and evaluate on held-out motifs, human-labeled judgments, and external benchmarks. Overall, PPT substantially improves estimation accuracy on held-out inductive tasks, increases alignment with human judgments, and transfers to external benchmarks for estimation and calibration. Additionally, the gains in raw calibration are not subsumed by post-hoc temperature scaling, showing that the models have more deeply internalized uncertainty compared to output rescaling. Together, these results suggest that probabilistic-program-mediated fine-tuning is a promising approach for post-training LLMs to reliably perform approximate inductive inference.

</details>


### [4] [Less Context, More Accuracy: A Bi-Temporal Memory Engine for LLM Agents Where a Lean Retrieved Context Beats the Full History](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09900)
*Liuyin Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Engram is an open-source memory system for LLM agents that uses a bi-temporal knowledge graph with dual write paths (lossless episode storage + asynchronous fact extraction) and a hybrid retrieval mechanism, achieving 83.6% accuracy on LongMemEval_S versus 73.2% for full-context replay, while using ~8× fewer tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents lack persistent long-term memory across sessions. The common workaround of replaying full conversation history into the prompt is expensive, slow, and degrades in accuracy as distractors accumulate. Existing memory systems sacrifice accuracy for cost/latency, and benchmark results are inconsistent and non-reproducible due to flawed evaluation harnesses.

Method: Engram employs a dual-process architecture: (1) a fast write path that appends lossless episode storage without LLM involvement; (2) an asynchronous path that extracts atomic (subject, predicate, object) facts into a bi-temporal knowledge graph with automatic contradiction resolution via invalidation (never deletion), preserving provenance and supersession chains; (3) a hybrid read path that fuses dense, lexical, graph, and recency/salience signals with point-in-time filtering to assemble compact, provenance-tagged context. The authors also contribute a reproducible evaluation harness with the official judge and full-context baseline.

Result: On the 500-question LongMemEval_S benchmark, Engram's lean configuration scores 83.6% vs. 73.2% for full-context replay (+10.4 points, McNemar p < 10⁻⁶), using ~9.6k tokens versus ~79k (~8× reduction), with zero errors. Ablation shows the hybrid read path is essential: facts alone lose recall, while facts plus retrieved chunks recover detail.

Conclusion: Engram demonstrates that a structured, bi-temporal memory engine with hybrid retrieval can outperform full-context replay on both accuracy and efficiency. The paper also highlights and addresses measurement-integrity pitfalls (truncation, home-grown judges, full-history leaks) that distort memory benchmarks, and provides fully reproducible results.

Abstract: Long-term memory is the missing layer for LLM agents: across sessions they forget, and the common workaround -- replaying the whole history into the prompt -- is expensive, slow, and, as distractors accumulate, less accurate. Most memory systems win on cost or latency but still lose to the full-context baseline on accuracy, and benchmark numbers are reported on inconsistent, non-reproducible harnesses, so one system appears at wildly different scores across sources. We present Engram, an open-source, dual-process memory engine on a bi-temporal data model. A fast write path appends lossless episodes with no LLM on the critical path; an asynchronous path extracts atomic (subject, predicate, object) facts, builds a bi-temporal knowledge graph, and resolves contradictions without an LLM call per fact -- invalidating, never deleting, so every fact keeps provenance and a supersession chain. A hybrid read path fuses dense, lexical, graph, and recency/salience signals, applies a point-in-time ("as-of") filter, and assembles a compact, provenance-tagged context. On the full 500-question LongMemEval_S, graded by the official category-specific judge, Engram's lean configuration -- answering from a ~9.6k-token retrieved slice, never the full history -- scores 83.6% vs. 73.2% for full-context (+10.4 points, McNemar p < 10^-6) at ~8x fewer tokens (9.6k vs. 79k), with 0/500 errored. The gain needs a hybrid read path: facts alone lose recall, while facts plus retrieved chunks recover detail. We also contribute a neutral, in-repo evaluation harness with the official judge baked in and the full-context baseline in every table, publish the raw per-question logs, and document the measurement-integrity pitfalls (truncation, home-grown judges, full-history leaks) that silently distort memory benchmarks. Every number ships with a command to reproduce it.

</details>


### [5] [BenSyc: Benchmarking Conversational Sycophancy and Human Alignment in LLMs for Bengali Contexts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10061)
*Kazi Noshin,Sajib Acharjee Dip,Ranat Das Prangon,Fardin Hassan Tamim,Syed Ishtiaque Ahmed,Liqing Zhang,Sharifa Sultana*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: BenSyc is the first benchmark for conversational sycophancy in Bengali, built from Reddit data with a five-level taxonomy (Invalidation to Escalation). Evaluations on 15+ LLMs reveal that distinguishing empathetic support from excessive validation remains difficult, with the best model achieving only ~61.8 Macro-F1.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing sycophancy research focuses on factual agreement and instruction-following, neglecting culturally grounded conversational sycophancy — particularly in non-English, emotionally sensitive social contexts. Bengali, spoken by ~300 million people, lacks any benchmark for this phenomenon.

Method: The authors collected 11,840 Reddit posts and ~170k comments from Bengali-speaking communities (Bangladesh and West Bengal). They constructed a human-validated benchmark with (a) binary labels for sycophancy detection and (b) a fine-grained five-level taxonomy: Invalidation, Neutral, Support, Validation, and Escalation. Over 15 open and proprietary LLMs were evaluated on both classification and response generation tasks.

Result: The best-performing model achieved only 61.8 Macro-F1 on binary sycophancy detection and 61.7 Macro-F1 on five-class classification. In generation tasks, several models frequently produced strongly validating or escalatory responses in emotionally charged scenarios. Substantial variation was observed across model families and conversational behaviors.

Conclusion: Distinguishing empathetic support from reinforcement-oriented validation remains a significant challenge for frontier LLMs in Bengali conversational contexts. The findings underscore the critical need for culturally grounded multilingual benchmarks to evaluate and improve socially aligned conversational AI systems.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly participate in emotionally sensitive social conversations, where responses may shift from balanced support toward excessive validation or escalatory alignment. Existing sycophancy research primarily focuses on factual agreement and instruction-following settings, leaving culturally grounded conversational sycophancy underexplored. We introduce BenSyc, the first benchmark for studying conversational sycophancy in Bengali social contexts. Starting from 11,840 Reddit posts and 170k comments collected from communities across Bangladesh and West Bengal, we construct a human-validated benchmark with binary labels and a fine-grained five-level taxonomy spanning Invalidation, Neutral, Support, Validation, and Escalation. We evaluate more than 15 open and proprietary LLMs on conversational alignment classification and response generation tasks. Results show that distinguishing empathetic support from reinforcement-oriented validation remains challenging even for frontier instruction-tuned models: the best system achieves only 61.8 Macro-F1 on binary detection and 61.7 Macro-F1 on five-class classification. In generation settings, several models frequently produce strongly validating or escalatory responses in emotionally charged situations. Our findings highlight substantial variation across model families and conversational behaviors, underscoring the importance of culturally grounded multilingual benchmarks for evaluating socially aligned conversational AI systems.

</details>


### [6] [CodeAlchemy: Synthetic Code Rewriting at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10087)
*Ankit Gupta,Aditya Prasad,Rameswar Panda*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: CodeAlchemy introduces a synthetic data generation framework with five strategies that transforms public source code into 500B+ tokens of semantically-rich training data. A 3B model trained on this data outperforms frontier models 10x its size on code benchmarks, while new evaluation benchmarks (DevEval, TraceEval) reveal critical semantic understanding gaps in even the largest models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pre-training on raw code primarily teaches syntax but provides sparse signal for diverse real-world programming tasks. While synthetic data has revolutionized language model training, its application to code remains underexplored beyond modest quality improvements. There is a need for semantically-rich, diverse training data that captures the full spectrum of software development tasks.

Method: CodeAlchemy employs five synthetic data generation strategies: (1) CodeEnhance — quality-aware rewriting of existing code; (2) CodeQA — template-based problem generation; (3) CodeDev — developer-oriented task synthesis; (4) CodeDialogue — multi-turn conversational data; and (5) CodeTrace — instrumentation and execution of 1.3M+ files across 14 languages and 5K libraries to capture control flow, state tracking, and library knowledge. Three corpora across 15 languages are processed, yielding 500B+ synthetic tokens and 350B reasoning tokens. Two new benchmarks are introduced: DevEval (developer tasks) and TraceEval (execution prediction).

Result: A 3B-parameter model trained with CodeAlchemy data achieves: 83.5% on HumanEval, 63.2% on MBPP, 8.09% win rate on DevEval, and 15.36 ROUGE-2 on TraceEval. These results surpass frontier models 10x larger, including Gemma-3 (27B) and Granite-4.0 (32B). Claude Sonnet 4.5 achieves only 5.6% exact match on TraceEval, highlighting significant semantic understanding gaps.

Conclusion: Synthetic data generation via CodeAlchemy enables small models to achieve competitive or superior performance on code tasks compared to much larger frontier models. The poor performance of even state-of-the-art models on TraceEval indicates that current models lack deep semantic understanding of program execution, and execution-trace-based training data offers a promising direction for improvement.

Abstract: Pre-training on raw code teaches syntax but provides sparse signal for diverse real-world task formats. While synthetic data has proven transformative for language models, code remains largely unexplored beyond limited quality improvements. We present CodeAlchemy, a synthetic data generation framework that transforms publicly sourced code into semantically-rich training data through 5 strategies: CodeEnhance (quality-aware rewriting), CodeQA (template-based problems), CodeDev (developer tasks), CodeDialogue (multi-turn conversations), and CodeTrace (execution traces). We process 3 corpora across 15 languages to generate 500B+ tokens of synthetic data plus 350B reasoning tokens, orders of magnitude more than prior efforts. CodeTrace instruments and executes 1.3M+ files across 14 languages and 5K libraries, capturing control flow, state tracking, and library knowledge. We introduce DevEval (developer tasks) and TraceEval (execution prediction) benchmarks; frontier models like Claude Sonnet 4.5 achieve only 5.6% exact match on TraceEval, revealing critical gaps in semantic understanding. Our 3B models achieve 83.5% on HumanEval, 63.2% on MBPP, 8.09% win rate on DevEval, and 15.36 ROUGE-2 on TraceEval, outperforming frontier models 10x the size including 27B Gemma-3 and 32B Granite-4.0.

</details>


### [7] [Emotion Profiling in LLM-Based Literary Translation: Systematic Shifts Across MT and Post-Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10113)
*Antonio Castaldo,Johanna Monti,Sheila Castilho*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper examines whether LLM translations carry distinct emotional signatures and how human post-editing shifts them toward human-like emotional norms, using translations of Margaret Atwood's Oryx and Crake as a case study. Findings reveal that MT systems leave statistically significant, model-specific emotional fingerprints, resulting in limited preservation of the author's original voice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study is motivated by the growing use of LLMs in literary translation and the concern that machine translation may distort the emotional texture and authorial voice of literary works. There is a need to empirically assess whether different MT systems impose identifiable emotional biases and whether post-editing can restore human-like emotional expression.

Method: A comparative corpus-based approach is employed: LLM-generated translations of Oryx and Crake are compared against their post-edited versions and a published human translation. A large-scale corpus of contemporary Italian science-fiction serves as a reference baseline. Emotion is quantified through both lexicon-based emotion analysis and multilingual emotion modeling, enabling fine-grained comparison of emotional variation across translation systems.

Result: MT systems produce translations with statistically significant, model-specific emotional fingerprints that deviate from human translation norms. Post-editing partially realigns these emotional profiles toward human-like patterns, but the author's emotional voice remains only partially preserved across machine translation pipelines.

Conclusion: LLM-based translation introduces systematic emotional distortions that are both identifiable and model-dependent. Even with post-editing, full preservation of an author's emotional voice is not guaranteed, raising important questions for the use of MT in literary contexts where stylistic and affective fidelity are critical.

Abstract: This paper investigates whether LLM translations exhibit identifiable emotional profiles and how post-editing reshapes them toward human-like norms. We compare LLM translations of Margaret Atwood's Oryx and Crake with their post-edited versions and a human translation, using a large-scale corpus of contemporary Italian science-fiction as a baseline. We examine emotion through lexicon-based and multilingual modeling, conducting a fine-grained analysis of emotional variation across systems. We find that MT systems introduce model-specific and statistically significant emotional fingerprints across translations, leading to a limited preservation of an author's voice.

</details>


### [8] [Pareto-Guided Teacher Alignment for Fair Personalized Text Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10126)
*Tunazzina Islam*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper formalizes fairness in personalized persuasive text generation as a constrained multi-objective alignment problem, proposes a Pareto-guided teacher alignment framework, and demonstrates via a dual-domain evaluation that no single method dominates — instead, methods trade off along a fairness-personalization Pareto frontier, motivating bounded-regression multi-audit model selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Personalized persuasive text generation can improve relevance and engagement, but conditioning on demographics risks introducing unequal framing across groups (e.g., persuasion bias, formality disparity, emotional and lexical association disparities). Existing approaches lack a principled framework that simultaneously reduces demographic disparities while preserving personalization fidelity, and there is no systematic understanding of how different fairness mitigation strategies trade off across multiple audit dimensions.

Method: The authors propose a Pareto-guided teacher alignment framework with four components: (1) revision-based candidate generation — a teacher model produces multiple candidate revisions of a base persuasive text; (2) pair-aware feasibility gating — filters candidates against feasibility constraints using demographic pair information; (3) Pareto-style candidate selection — selects candidates that lie on or near the multi-objective Pareto frontier trading off fairness metrics and personalization fidelity; and (4) optional preference optimization — applies supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and direct preference optimization (DPO) on the Pareto-selected outputs. Evaluation uses a controlled demographic grid with matched gender and age pairs across climate change and vaccination persuasion tasks, with a unified five-audit suite: persuasion bias, formality disparity, emotional framing disparity, lexical association disparity, and personalization fidelity. Cross-family transfer is also tested.

Result: No single alignment strategy dominates all five audit objectives simultaneously across either domain. Methods occupy different regions of a fairness-personalization Pareto frontier: some achieve stronger disparity reductions (e.g., larger drops in formality or emotional framing gaps), while others better preserve personalization fidelity or demographic stability. Fairness mitigation effects are objective-dependent and transfer inconsistently across domains (climate vs. vaccination) and model families, meaning a method strong in one setting may underperform in another.

Conclusion: Fairness mitigation in personalized generation cannot be reduced to single-metric optimization. The findings motivate bounded-regression, multi-audit model selection, where practitioners define acceptable regression bounds on personalization fidelity and then select methods that maximize fairness gains within those bounds, using a Pareto-informed framework tailored to the specific domain, model family, and audit objectives of interest.

Abstract: Personalized persuasive text generation can improve relevance and engagement, but demographic conditioning may also introduce unequal framing across groups. We study fairness mitigation in personalized generation as a constrained multi-objective alignment problem: reduce demographic disparities while preserving personalization fidelity. We propose a Pareto-guided teacher alignment framework that combines revision-based candidate generation, pair-aware feasibility gating, Pareto-style candidate selection, and optional preference optimization through supervised fine-tuning and direct preference optimization. We evaluate the framework on climate change and vaccination persuasion tasks using a controlled context-rich demographic grid with matched gender and age pairs and a unified five-audit evaluation suite spanning persuasion bias, formality disparity, emotional framing disparity, lexical association disparity, and personalization fidelity. Across both domains and cross-family transfer settings, no single alignment strategy dominates all objectives simultaneously. Instead, methods occupy different regions of a fairness-personalization Pareto frontier: some achieve stronger disparity reductions, while others better preserve personalization or demographic stability. Our results show that fairness mitigation effects are objective-dependent and transfer inconsistently across domains and model families, motivating bounded-regression, multi-audit model selection over single-metric optimization for fairness-sensitive personalized generation.

</details>


### [9] [Gaming AI-Assisted Peer Reviews Poses New Risks to the Scientific Community](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10159)
*Lin Li,Qi Zhang,Xander Davies,Jianing Qiu,Yarin Gal*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: AI-based peer review systems are vulnerable to adversarial abstract rephrasing, which can inflate review scores by up to +1.31 on a 10-point scale with a 38% attack success rate, rising above 50% for initially rejected papers. The attack costs ~$1 and 5 minutes, is hard to detect, and could bias editorial decisions toward acceptance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI is increasingly deployed in scientific peer review, yet the robustness of these systems to strategic manipulation is unknown. The authors aim to expose vulnerabilities before widespread real-world deployment leads to compromised scientific integrity.

Method: Adversarial attacks on AI reviewers (Gemini 3 Flash, GPT 5.4 Mini) via superficial abstract rephrasing that preserves scientific content. Attacks were tested across multiple disciplines, venues, and for both human-written and AI-generated papers. Metrics include attack success rate, score inflation, confidence shifts, and sub-criterion impact (soundness, significance, contribution).

Result: Adversarial rephrasing substantially improves AI review outcomes: strongest attack achieves ~38% success rate, +1.31 score increase for Gemini, +0.88 for GPT; success rate exceeds 50% for initially rejected papers. Effects extend to review confidence and core scientific criteria. Attack costs ~$1 and 5 minutes per paper, and is indistinguishable from normal editing.

Conclusion: AI tools should not be treated as neutral evaluators in high-stakes peer review without systematic robustness testing, transparent safeguards, and careful human oversight. Otherwise, authors may be incentivized to optimize manuscripts for AI judgment rather than scientific merit.

Abstract: AI is increasingly used to support scientific peer review, from manuscript screening, reviewer assistance to editorial triage. Although such systems promise to reduce reviewer burden and accelerate publication, their robustness to strategic manipulation remains poorly understood. Here we show that AI-mediated peer review is vulnerable to a simple, low-cost manipulation: superficial rephrasing of the manuscript abstract. Without changing the underlying scientific content and communication, and even without knowledge of the reviewing model, adversarially rewritten abstracts substantially improve AI review outcomes. We see this across disciplines and publication venues, for both human-written and AI-generated papers. Our strongest attack achieves an attack-success-rate of about 38%, increasing acceptance ratings by +1.31 for Gemini 3 Flash reviewers and by +0.88 for GPT 5.4 Mini reviewers on a 10-point scale. When the original AI review suggests 'reject', the success rate rises to more than 50%. This effect extends beyond overall score inflation, increasing review confidence and scores on core scientific criteria such as soundness, significance and perceived contribution. The attack is practical, requiring only about 5 minutes and $1 for a 10-page AI conference submission, and is hard to distinguish from ordinary scientific editing. Inflated AI reviews could bias downstream human decision-making, shifting editorial recommendations from rejection towards acceptance. These findings reveal a general vulnerability in AI-assisted scientific evaluation: when AI-generated review influence editorial decisions, authors may be incentivized to optimize manuscripts for AI judgment rather than scientific merit. Our results suggest that AI tools should not be treated as neutral evaluators in high-stakes peer review without systematic robustness testing, transparent safeguards and careful human oversight.

</details>


### [10] [OpenRTLSet: A Fully Open-Source Dataset for Large Language Model-based Verilog Module Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10285)
*Jinghua Wang,Lily Jiaxin Wan,Sanjana Pingali,Scott Smith,Manvi Jha,Shalini Sivakumar,Xing Zhao,Kaiwen Cao,Deming Chen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: OpenRTLSet is a large-scale, fully open-source dataset of 131K+ Verilog code samples paired with natural language descriptions, enabling fine-tuning of LLMs for hardware design. It demonstrates that open-source approaches can achieve superior performance in Verilog code generation across various model families, sizes, and quantization settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hardware design research has been constrained by the lack of large, high-quality, openly accessible datasets for training and fine-tuning language models. Existing datasets are often proprietary, limited in size, or lack paired natural language descriptions. OpenRTLSet aims to democratize hardware design AI by providing the largest fully open-source Verilog dataset to the research community and industry.

Method: The dataset is constructed from three sources: (1) 102K Verilog modules from GitHub repositories, (2) 5K modules from VHDL translations, and (3) 24K modules from synthesizable C/C++ translations. The reasoning model DeepSeek-R1 was used to generate paired natural language descriptions for each code sample. Verilator-generated C++ files were explored as additional context during labeling. The dataset was used to fine-tune multiple LLM families (Qwen, Granite) across model sizes (7B–32B parameters) and quantization schemes (INT4 vs. BF16), with systematic performance comparisons.

Result: Open-source approaches using OpenRTLSet achieved superior performance in Verilog code generation tasks. The paper reports performance differences across model sizes (7B–32B), compares INT4 vs. BF16 quantization, and evaluates the impact of using Verilator-generated C++ as auxiliary context during training.

Conclusion: OpenRTLSet establishes that fully open-source datasets can rival or surpass proprietary alternatives for hardware design tasks. By releasing 131K+ diverse, freely accessible Verilog samples with natural language pairs, it provides a new foundation for both research and commercial applications in AI-assisted hardware design.

Abstract: OpenRTLSet introduces the largest fully open-source dataset for hardware design, offering over 131,000 diverse Verilog code samples to the research community and industry. Our dataset uniquely combines Verilog code from GitHub repositories (102k modules), VHDL translations (5k modules), and synthesizable C/C++ translations (24k modules), all freely accessible without proprietary restrictions. Using the reasoning model DeepSeek-R1, we generated paired natural language descriptions for each code sample, enabling fine-tuning of various language model families (e.g., Qwen and Granite) for Verilog code generation. Our dataset explores multiple options, including Verilator-generated C++ files as additional context during labeling, quantization techniques (INT4 vs. BF16), and performance differences across model sizes (7B-32B parameters). OpenRTLSet demonstrates that open-source approaches can achieve superior performance in hardware design tasks, establishing a new foundation for accessible research and commercial use in this domain.

</details>


### [11] [The Confident Liar: Diagnosing Multi-Agent Debate with Log-Probabilities and LLM-as-Judge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10296)
*Ali Keramati,Justin Cheok,Jacob Horne,Mark Warschauer*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether internal token-level confidence signals in multi-agent debate systems align with externally judged reasoning quality and final task accuracy. Using a two-agent (Constructor-Auditor) debate framework with LLM-as-judge scoring, it finds a consistent four-phase confidence trajectory, a strong role asymmetry where Constructor confidence aligns with reasoning quality roughly twice as well as Auditor confidence, and more reliable failure detection for the Constructor (AUROC 0.804 vs 0.634).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work evaluates multi-agent debate systems solely on final answer correctness, neglecting the quality of intermediate reasoning that debate is explicitly designed to improve. The authors aim to bridge this gap by examining whether internal confidence signals (token-level log-probabilities) can serve as proxies for externally evaluated reasoning quality and task success.

Method: The authors propose a two-agent debate framework: a Constructor generates reasoning, an Auditor critiques it, and an LLM-as-judge scores each agent's reasoning along three dimensions — instruction following, justification quality, and evidence grounding — plus a critical-failure flag. They then analyze correlations between token-level log-probability distributions, judge-assigned rubric scores, and final task accuracy across three domains: rubric-based scoring, mathematical reasoning, and factual question answering.

Result: In the rubric-scoring domain, a consistent four-phase confidence trajectory emerges during debate. A substantial role asymmetry is observed: Constructor confidence aligns with judged reasoning quality roughly twice as strongly as Auditor confidence. Confidence-based detection of critical reasoning failures is markedly more reliable for the Constructor (AUROC 0.804) than for the Auditor (AUROC 0.634).

Conclusion: Internal confidence signals can serve as effective proxies for reasoning quality in multi-agent debate, but their reliability is highly role-dependent. The Constructor's confidence is a substantially stronger indicator of reasoning quality and failure detection than the Auditor's, warranting broader cross-domain investigation.

Abstract: Multi-agent debate systems are typically evaluated only on whether the final answer is correct, overlooking the quality of the intermediate reasoning that debate is designed to produce. This paper studies the relationship between three signals in multi-agent debate: token-level log-probability distributions over reasoning tokens, LLM-as-judge rubric scores assigned to those tokens, and final task accuracy. We examine whether internal confidence signals predict externally evaluated reasoning quality, and whether either signal aligns with task correctness, across three domains: rubric-based scoring, mathematical reasoning, and factual question answering. Our framework pairs a two-agent debate architecture -- a Constructor and an Auditor -- with an LLM-as-judge that scores each agent's reasoning along instruction following, justification quality, and evidence grounding, together with a critical-failure flag. Experiments in the rubric-scoring domain reveal a consistent four-phase confidence trajectory and a substantial role asymmetry: confidence aligns with judged reasoning quality roughly twice as strongly for the Constructor as for the Auditor, and confidence-based detection of critical reasoning failures is markedly more reliable for the Constructor (AUROC 0.804) than for the Auditor (0.634). These findings motivate the broader cross-domain investigation proposed in this paper.

</details>


### [12] [Where You Inject Diversity Matters: A Unified Framework for Diverse Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10302)
*Cheng Zhang,Rui Xin,Chudi Zhong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a framework for analyzing test-time diverse generation methods by characterizing diversity sources and measuring their transmission to outputs. The authors propose specification-level generation, which creates diverse intermediate specifications then conditions on them, demonstrating improved output diversity across five tasks and four models while maintaining quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-ended generation tasks require meaningfully different outputs, but LLMs tend to produce similar generations. Existing diversity methods operate at different stages with unclear design principles for achieving meaningful diversity.

Method: The authors introduce (1) a characterization framework that classifies diversity methods by the source of variation introduced during generation, (2) a transmission score that quantifies how effectively source variation propagates to final output, and (3) specification-level generation methods that first generate diverse intermediate specifications (e.g., outlines, key points) and then condition final output generation on these specifications.

Result: Specification-level injection improves output diversity over test-time baselines across five open-ended tasks and four backbone models, while maintaining comparable output quality. Analysis reveals that both source diversity and transmission fidelity are critical for successful diversity injection.

Conclusion: Source design and source-to-output realization are two key levers for building more diverse generation systems. The proposed framework provides principled guidance for developing effective test-time diversity methods.

Abstract: Open-ended generation tasks often require a set of meaningfully different outputs, yet large language models often produce similar generations. Existing test-time diversity methods operate at different stages of generation with varying effectiveness, but it remains unclear what design choices lead to meaningful diversity in the output. We introduce a framework that characterizes test-time diverse generation methods by the diversity source introduced during generation and provide a transmission score for measuring how effectively variation in the source reaches the final output. Guided by this framework, we propose fully automated specification-level generation methods that first generate diverse intermediate specifications and then condition on them to produce final responses. Across five open-ended tasks and four backbone models, specification-level injection improves output diversity over test-time baselines while maintaining comparable quality. Our analysis shows that successful diversity injection depends on both the diversity of the sources and their transmission to the output, highlighting source design and source-to-output realization as two key levers for building more diverse generation systems.

</details>


### [13] [MIRAGE: A Polarity-Flipping Encoding Subspace in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10304)
*Pratibha Revankar,Kargi Chauhan,Jihye Kim,Sadiba Nusrat Nur,Vincent Siu,Chenguang Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Covertly encoded sensitive data evades output-side detection but leaves detectable traces in LLM residual streams. A shared low-dimensional encoding subspace generalizes across nine encoding families and eight models. The same direction exhibits a planning-token polarity flip distinguishing inline encoding from tool-call outsourcing. MIRAGE, a two-channel real-time monitor exploiting both signals, achieves AUC 0.918 on agentic exfiltration vs. 0.518 for output-only detection. Monitor performance depends on host model geometry; attacks suppressing the subspace also destroy encoding fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents can be coerced into covertly encoding sensitive data using methods like Base64, ROT13, acrostics, and synonym chains. While these encodings evade output-side content filters, the authors hypothesize that the underlying neural computation remains detectable within the model's internal representations, and that this signal can be exploited for robust exfiltration monitoring.

Method: The authors systematically test nine encoding families across eight models from five architecture families. They use logistic-regression probes trained on residual stream activations to detect an encoding subspace. A probe trained on eight encoding families is evaluated on the held-out ninth (cross-encoding generalization). They also identify a planning-token signature: the encoding direction's polarity flips positively for inline simulated encoding and negatively for tool-call outsourcing, before any encoded text is generated. MIRAGE combines both the encoding-execution signal and the planning-token signal into a two-channel real-time monitor, tested across 126 agentic exfiltration scenarios. Adversarial budgets are explored by attempting to suppress the subspace.

Result: The encoding subspace generalizes strongly: probes trained on eight families recover the held-out ninth at AUC 0.975–1.000. The planning-token polarity flip reliably distinguishes inline encoding from tool outsourcing. MIRAGE achieves AUC 0.918 vs. 0.518 for output-only detection. Monitor performance is geometry-dependent: benign-encoding false-positive rates range from 0% (Qwen-7B) to 100% (Phi-3.5). Across all tested adversarial budgets, attacks that suppress the encoding subspace also destroy encoding fidelity — reported as an empirical regularity, not a proven impossibility.

Conclusion: Internal model representations contain a robust, generalizable signal for detecting covert encoding that output-side filters miss. The planning-token polarity flip adds a second, anticipatory detection channel. MIRAGE demonstrates practical superiority over output-only monitoring, but its effectiveness hinges on the host model's representational geometry. The coupling between encoding fidelity and subspace detectability suggests a fundamental tension for adversaries, though the authors caution this is an empirical observation rather than a structural guarantee.

Abstract: When LLM agents are coerced into covertly encoding sensitive data (Base64, ROT13, acrostic, synonym chains, and beyond), the resulting outputs evade output-side detection but the underlying computation does not. Across nine encoding families and eight models from five architecture families, that computation is supported by a shared low-dimensional encoding subspace in the residual stream. A logistic-regression probe trained on eight encoding families recovers the held-out ninth at AUC 0.975-1.000, reading the computation rather than surface features. The same direction exhibits a second mechanistic signature at the planning token, flipping polarity to activate positively when the model will simulate the encoding inline and negatively when it will outsource it to a tool call, distinguishing two execution strategies before the encoded text exists. We build MIRAGE (Model-Internal Readout of Agentic Generation Exfiltration), a two-channel real-time monitor exploiting both signals. On 126 agentic exfiltration scenarios, it reaches AUC = 0.918, substantially outperforming output-only detection (AUC = 0.518). Monitor performance is fundamentally a property of the host model's geometry: benign-encoding false-positive rate ranges from 0% on Qwen-7B to 100% on Phi-3.5, revealing that the probe faithfully reads whether a model's geometry separates covert from overt encoding. Across all tested adversarial budgets, every attack suppressing the subspace also destroyed encoding fidelity, reported as an empirical regularity on the evaluated budgets, not a structural impossibility claim.

</details>


### [14] [Early-Token Confidence Predicts Reasoning Quality in Multi-Agent LLM Debate](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10307)
*Ali Keramati,Justin Cheok,Jacob Horne,Mark Warschauer*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Early-token confidence signals (log-probabilities from the first few generated tokens) reliably predict reasoning quality in multi-agent LLM systems, outperforming full-sequence statistics, with stronger alignment for supportive reasoning than adversarial critique.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating reasoning quality in multi-agent LLM systems is difficult, especially for open-ended tasks lacking reference answers. The authors investigate whether lightweight, intrinsic confidence signals (token-level log-probabilities) can serve as proxies for reasoning quality, avoiding costly LLM-as-judge evaluations.

Method: Using a debate-based essay scoring framework with two ASAP essay sets, the authors compare confidence proxies (token-level log-probabilities during decoding) against rubric-based LLM-as-judge scores. They analyze log-probability trajectories across generation phases and examine alignment between confidence and quality across different agent roles (supportive reasoning vs. adversarial critique).

Result: Early-token confidence (first few tokens) is the strongest predictor of reasoning quality, surpassing full-sequence statistics. The opening generation phase is the most heterogeneous and informative. A systematic asymmetry exists: confidence-quality alignment is stronger for supportive reasoning than adversarial critique.

Conclusion: Early decoding dynamics offer a lightweight, effective signal for estimating reasoning reliability in multi-agent LLM systems, enabling confidence-based quality estimation without reference answers.

Abstract: Evaluating reasoning quality in multi-agent LLM systems is challenging, especially for open-ended tasks without reference answers. We investigate whether intrinsic confidence signals, token-level log-probabilities from decoding, can predict reasoning quality as assessed by LLM-as-judge evaluation. Using a debate-based essay scoring framework, we compare confidence proxies against rubric-based judge scores across two ASAP essay sets. We find that early-token confidence, particularly within the first few generated tokens, is consistently the strongest predictor of reasoning quality, outperforming full-sequence statistics. Analysis of log-probability trajectories shows that the opening phase of generation is the most heterogeneous and therefore most informative. We also observe a systematic asymmetry between agent roles, with stronger alignment between confidence and quality for supportive reasoning than for adversarial critique. These results suggest that early decoding dynamics provide a lightweight and effective signal for estimating reasoning reliability in multi-agent LLM systems.

</details>


### [15] [Catching One in Five: LLM-as-Judge Blind Spots in Production Multi-Turn Transaction Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10315)
*Sawyer Zhang,Alexander Wang,Sophie Lei*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LLM-as-judge for conversational agents misses most real defects (catching <25% of issues) because its rubric lacks behavioral dimensions like state-tracking and guardrails. The failure is a routing problem — judge notes describe defects correctly but misclassify them under "brand voice" — leading to 3-6x undercounting of true defect rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current reliance on LLM-as-judge for evaluating conversational agents is validated only by inter-rater agreement with humans, not by recall of actual defects. The authors aim to measure genuine defect-detection capability of a deployed LLM judge against exhaustive human review ground truth in a production multi-turn ordering agent.

Method: The study analyzes a deployed multi-turn food-and-beverage ordering agent across three batches. Human reviewers exhaustively reviewed transcripts to identify all genuine quality problems (defects and systematic patterns). The built-in LLM judge's outputs — both raw notes and operational gate decisions — were compared against this ground truth. A blind-spot taxonomy was constructed, and the Rogan-Gladen estimator was applied to assess prevalence estimation consequences.

Result: The LLM judge caught only 2 of 9 human-confirmed systematic patterns (22%) in one batch and flagged 0 of 100 rounds in another batch where humans found 23 distinct defects and 7 cross-cutting patterns. The failure is structured: the judge catches turn-local issues (fabricated statistics, wrong language) but misses cross-turn state issues (confirm-gate lockout, cart hallucination, escalation lockout). The rubric has only three coarse axes (intent, brand-voice, personalization) with no behavioral categories. Critically, 113 of 114 rounds where raw judge notes describe a confirm-gate or cart-state defect are scored as "brand voice" — a routing failure, not a perception failure. The Rogan-Gladen correction degenerates when apparent defect rate is zero, and where nonzero, it implies a 3-6x undercount given measured sensitivity.

Conclusion: For production multi-turn agents, automated LLM judging functions as a regression floor rather than a substitute for human review. The fundamental problem is rubric design (missing behavioral dimensions) and routing/wiring of judge observations to operational decisions, not the judge's perceptual capacity.

Abstract: LLM-as-judge is the default instrument for evaluating conversational agents, yet its reliability is almost always reported as agreement with human ratings, not recall of real defects. We study a deployed multi-turn food-and-beverage ordering agent and measure how many genuine quality problems its built-in LLM judge catches, using exhaustive human transcript review as ground truth. Across three batches the judge surfaces well under a quarter of human-confirmed systematic problems -- 2 of 9 patterns (22%) in one batch, and its operational gate flagged zero of 100 rounds in a batch where humans confirmed 23 distinct defects and 7 new cross-cutting patterns. Our blind-spot taxonomy shows the failure is structured, not random: the judge catches turn-local issues (a fabricated statistic, a wrong language) but misses cross-turn state issues (confirm-gate lockout, cart hallucination, escalation lockout, stale referents). The mechanism: the scoring rubric exposes only three coarse axes (intent, brand-voice, personalization) and has no category for the behavioural dimensions -- state-tracking, guardrails, recovery -- where most defects cluster. The failure is routing, not perception: 113 of 114 rounds whose raw judge note describes a confirm-gate or cart-state defect are scored "brand voice", and none reach an operational failure -- the gate is wired to hangs and hard assertions, not the rubric -- so the 0% is a routing-and-wiring failure, not blindness. The consequence for prevalence estimation is sharp: when the apparent defect rate is zero the Rogan-Gladen correction degenerates -- no signal can recover the true rate -- while where the gate reports a nonzero rate the same estimator implies a 3-6x undercount under our measured sensitivity. For production multi-turn agents, automated judging is a regression floor, not a substitute for human review.

</details>


### [16] [TabClaw: An Interactive and Self-Evolving Agent for Spreadsheet Manipulation and Table Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10316)
*Mingyue Cheng,Shuo Yu,Daoyu Wang,Qingchuan Li,Xiaoyu Tao,Qingyang Mao,Yitong Zhou,Qi Liu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: TabClaw is an open-source interactive AI agent for spreadsheet manipulation and table reasoning that combines transparent ReAct-style analysis loops, parallel multi-table reasoning with consensus markers, and persistent user memory with skill distillation to personalize recurring data-analysis workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM agents for spreadsheet analysis suffer from limited transparency into intermediate decisions, implicit assumptions, poor multi-table comparison capabilities, and an inability to adapt to user preferences across repeated workflows. There is a need for an inspectable, personalizing agent that addresses these gaps.

Method: TabClaw operates through: (1) clarifying ambiguous user intent and exposing editable execution plans; (2) streaming a ReAct-style tool-using analysis loop for transparency; (3) dispatching specialist agents for parallel multi-table reasoning; (4) synthesizing findings with explicit consensus and uncertainty markers; (5) recording completed workflows and extracting persistent user memory; (6) distilling reusable skills from repeated tool-use patterns with package-style import; and (7) upgrading skills from negative feedback.

Result: Experiments on spreadsheet manipulation and table reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that TabClaw improves executable task completion and reasoning performance while preserving an inspectable user workflow.

Conclusion: TabClaw successfully transforms spreadsheets and tables into inspectable analytical workflows while gradually personalizing itself to recurring data-analysis tasks, addressing key limitations of prior LLM-based approaches.

Abstract: Spreadsheets and tables are widely used representations for structured data analysis, but effective analysis still requires substantial manual effort and domain expertise. Recent large language model (LLM) agents can automate parts of this process, but they often provide limited transparency into intermediate decisions, rely on implicit assumptions, struggle with multi-table comparison, and repeat similar workflows without adapting to a user's preferences. This paper presents TabClaw, an open-source interactive AI agent for spreadsheet manipulation and table reasoning. Users upload CSV or Excel files and issue natural-language requests; TabClaw clarifies ambiguous intent, exposes an editable execution plan, streams a ReAct-style tool-using analysis loop, dispatches specialist agents for parallel multi-table reasoning, and synthesizes findings with explicit consensus and uncertainty markers. Beyond one-off analysis, TabClaw records completed workflows, extracts persistent user memory, distills reusable skills from repeated tool-use patterns, supports package-style skill import, and upgrades skills from negative feedback. Experiments on spreadsheet manipulation and table reasoning benchmarks show that TabClaw improves executable task completion and reasoning performance while preserving an inspectable user workflow. This paper shows how TabClaw turns spreadsheets and tables into inspectable analytical workflows while gradually personalizing itself to recurring data-analysis tasks. Our code is available.

</details>


### [17] [The Order Matters: Sequential Fine-Tuning of LLaMA for Coherent Automated Essay Scoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10327)
*Ali Keramati,Mark Warschauer*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Investigates curriculum-based LoRA fine-tuning of LLaMA-3.1-8B for AES, finding that sequential training aligned with discourse structure outperforms independent and randomized curricula, even surpassing LLaMA-70B on conclusion scoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most AES systems treat interdependent discourse elements (lead, claim, evidence, conclusion) in isolation, harming coherence and generalization. The authors hypothesize that curriculum design respecting discourse dependencies can improve performance.

Method: Task-aware fine-tuning of LLaMA-3.1-8B using parameter-efficient LoRA with 4-bit quantization. Three curricula compared: (i) Sequential (lead → position → claim → evidence → conclusion), (ii) Independent task-specific models, (iii) Randomized multi-task. Evaluated on PERSUADE 2.0 corpus.

Result: Sequential fine-tuning achieves best overall results: 65% F1 (evidence), 87% F1 (conclusion), with 63% and 85% accuracy respectively. It surpasses independent training and outperforms LLaMA-70B on conclusion scoring. Randomized training improves position scoring (57% F1) but is less consistent elsewhere.

Conclusion: Curriculum design aligned with discourse structure materially improves AES. Small, task-optimized models can compete with much larger LLMs, offering a scalable, cost-effective path for assessment. Templates and implementation details are released for reproducibility.

Abstract: Automated Essay Scoring (AES) systems must judge interdependent discourse elements (e.g., lead, claim, evidence, conclusion), yet most approaches treat these in isolation, harming coherence and generalization. We investigate task-aware fine-tuning of LLaMA-3.1-8B for AES using parameter-efficient LoRA with 4-bit quantization and compare three training curricula: (i) Sequential (progressively fine-tuning on lead, then position, then claim, then evidence, then conclusion), (ii) Independent (task-specific models), and (iii) Randomized (shuffled multi-task). Experiments on the PERSUADE~2.0 corpus show that modeling task dependencies matters: Sequential fine-tuning yields the strongest overall results, including F1 scores of 65% (evidence) and 87% (conclusion) and corresponding accuracies of 63% and 85%, surpassing Independent training and outperforming a general-purpose LLaMA-70B baseline on conclusion despite its far larger capacity. Randomized training improves position scoring (57% F1) but is less consistent elsewhere. These findings indicate that (1) curriculum design aligned with discourse structure can materially improve AES, and (2) small, task-optimized models can be competitive with substantially larger Large Language Models (LLM), offering a practical path to scalable, cost-effective assessment. We release templates and implementation details to facilitate reproduction and future work on curriculum design for educational NLP.

</details>


### [18] [Routing-Aware Expert Calibration for Machine Unlearning in Mixture-of-Experts Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10338)
*Jingyi Xie,Yijun Lin,Yinjiang Xiong,Zhikun Zhang,Sai Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: TRACE addresses machine unlearning in Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLMs by identifying experts disproportionately activated by forget data and reweighting retain losses so that retain-side activation matches forget-side activation, improving the forget-utility trade-off by 9% over the strongest baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MoE architectures route tokens to sparse expert subsets, creating a mismatch where forget data strongly activates certain experts while retain data only weakly activates them. This mismatch causes forget-critical experts to be under-regularized during unlearning, degrading model utility on retained knowledge.

Method: TRACE operates in two stages: (1) offline detection of forget-critical experts using activation statistics from forget vs. retain data, and (2) targeted calibration where token-level retain losses are reweighted so that each forget-critical expert's retain-side activation frequency matches its forget-side counterpart, ensuring proper regularization during the unlearning process.

Result: On WMDP and MUSE-BOOKS benchmarks across multiple MoE LLMs, TRACE consistently improves the forget-utility trade-off, achieving a 9% relative utility gain over the strongest baseline at comparable forgetting quality, and attaining best performance on three of four MUSE-BOOKS metrics.

Conclusion: TRACE demonstrates that explicitly accounting for MoE routing dynamics during unlearning yields significant improvements in the forget-utility trade-off, highlighting the importance of architecture-aware unlearning strategies.

Abstract: Machine unlearning is increasingly important for large language models, yet unlearning in Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures remains underexplored. Unlike dense models, MoE architectures employ a router at each layer to assign each token to a sparse subset of experts. In this work, we observe that forget data often activates a small subset of experts disproportionately, while these experts may receive much weaker activation from retain data. This forget--retain routing mismatch can leave forget-critical experts under-regularized during unlearning. To address this, we propose \textbf{TRACE}, Targeted Routing-Aware Calibration of Experts, for MoE unlearning. TRACE first detects forget-critical experts from offline activation statistics, and then calibrates retain regularization by reweighting token-level retain losses so that each selected expert's retain-side activation frequency better matches its forget-side counterpart. Experiments on WMDP and MUSE-BOOKS across multiple MoE LLMs show that TRACE consistently improves the forget-utility trade-off, yielding a 9\% relative utility improvement over the strongest baseline under comparable forgetting quality and the best performance on three out of four MUSE-BOOKS metrics.

</details>


### [19] [PADD: Path-Aligned Decompression Distillation for Non-Router Teacher to Guide MoE Student Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10369)
*Xinyue Peng,Yi Qian,Jiaojiao Lin,Wenjian Shao,Yanming Liu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: PADD is a four-stage, two-phase framework that distills dense LLMs into MoE architectures without explicit routing supervision, achieving MoE students that match or surpass their dense teachers on mathematical reasoning benchmarks at equal inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling LLMs under fixed compute budgets is increasingly difficult. The authors aim to address the challenge of transferring knowledge from a dense teacher model to a mixture-of-experts (MoE) student while simultaneously learning high-quality routing policies—without requiring explicit routing signals from the teacher.

Method: PADD consists of two phases spanning four stages. Phase 1 (Initialization): Stage I builds diverse expert functionality via teacher neuron clustering and student-expert warmup. Phase 2 (Training): Stages II–IV jointly perform online adaptive distillation, path-refined policy optimization, and reward-augmented load balancing in a unified training pipeline.

Result: On mathematical reasoning benchmarks, PADD achieves substantial gains over strong baselines at the same inference cost, and the resulting MoE student can match or outperform its dense teacher. The method also demonstrates effective knowledge transfer and stable routing dynamics.

Conclusion: PADD provides an effective recipe for distilling dense LLMs into MoE architectures, learning routing endogenously while preserving or exceeding teacher performance, all within a fixed inference budget.

Abstract: As large language models (LLMs) continue to scale, it becomes increasingly challenging to grow model capacity under fixed computation budgets. We propose Path-Aligned Decompression Distillation (PADD), a framework for distilling knowledge from dense teachers without explicit routing into mixture-of-experts (MoE) students while learning high-quality routing policies. PADD organizes knowledge distillation into four stages in two phases: an initialization phase (Stage I) that builds diverse functionality in the student's experts through teacher neuron clustering and student-expert warmup, and a training phase (Stages II--IV) that integrates online adaptive distillation, path-refined policy optimization, and reward-augmented load balancing in a single training pipeline. Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that PADD yields substantial gains over strong baselines at the same inference cost and that the MoE student can match or surpass its dense teacher. They also demonstrate effective teacher-to-student knowledge distillation and stable routing behavior.

</details>


### [20] [ConvMemory v2: A Recall-Preserving Top-10 Evidence Reranker for Conversational Memory Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10842)
*Taiheng Pan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ConvMemory v2 is a fine-tuned cross-encoder reranker applied to ConvMemory v1's top-10 candidates, boosting conversational memory retrieval MRR from 0.5824 to 0.6560 while preserving recall. It nearly matches a costlier full-pool reranker and surpasses it on specific hard slices, with ablations confirming candidate-specific memory text as the key mechanism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve ranking quality over the lightweight ConvMemory v1 reranker without sacrificing recall, using an opt-in token-evidence cross-encoder that operates only on v1's protected top-10 candidate set, aiming to approach the performance of a far more expensive full-pool reranker at lower computational cost.

Method: A fine-tuned ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2 cross-encoder (22.7M parameters) is applied as a second-stage reranker over ConvMemory v1's top-10 (query, memory) pairs. This is a recall-preserving cascade: v2 reorders but does not alter the candidate set, so Recall@10 and Hit@10 are identical to v1 by construction. Evaluation uses the LoCoMo benchmark (5 seeds, n=4,955), with paired bootstrap confidence intervals. A four-arm load-bearing ablation (removing, shuffling, or replacing candidate-specific memory text) tests the mechanism. Comparisons are made against a full-pool mxbai-rerank-large-v1 over top-500.

Result: v2 lifts FULL MRR from 0.5824 (v1) to 0.6560 (+0.0734, 95% CI [+0.0645, +0.0827]) and H@1 from 0.4440 to 0.5474. It closes most of the gap to mxbai-rerank-large-v1 (MRR 0.6688), sitting only 0.013 below; on two raw-dense-hard slices, v2 exceeds mxbai_top500. The ablation shows that removing, shuffling, or replacing candidate-specific memory text collapses MRR below raw dense retrieval, confirming this text as the operative mechanism.

Conclusion: ConvMemory v2 is a standard recall-preserving cascade with LoCoMo-specific fine-tuning, an explicit anti-shortcut inference contract, and rigorous load-bearing analysis. Its advantage over mxbai is slice-specific rather than general, and the core mechanism is candidate-specific memory text re-ranking.

Abstract: We describe ConvMemory v2, an opt-in token-evidence reranker that sits after the lightweight ConvMemory v1 reranker and reorders only v1's protected top-10 candidate set. v2 is a fine-tuned ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2 cross-encoder (22,713,601 parameters, measured from the released checkpoint) applied to the ten (query, memory) pairs that v1 has already selected; it does not change which ten memories are returned, so Recall@10 and Hit@10 are identical to v1 by construction, not by statistical coincidence. On the LoCoMo conversational memory benchmark (5 seeds, n = 4955 test rows), v2 raises FULL MRR from v1's 0.5824 to 0.6560 (paired bootstrap +0.0734, 95% CI [+0.0645, +0.0827]) and H@1 from 0.4440 to 0.5474. v2 closes most but not all of the gap to a much more expensive full-pool cross-encoder reference (mxbai-rerank-large-v1 over the top-500, MRR 0.6688): on FULL MRR v2 sits 0.013 below mxbai_top500, but on two raw-dense-hard slices (where v1's protected top-10 has higher recall than mxbai's own top-10) v2 exceeds mxbai_top500. A four-arm load-bearing ablation shows candidate-specific memory text is the mechanism: removing, shuffling, or replacing it collapses MRR below raw dense retrieval. v2 is best understood as a standard recall-preserving cascade pattern with LoCoMo-specific fine-tuning, an explicit anti-shortcut inference contract, and disciplined load-bearing analysis; its advantage over mxbai is slice-specific rather than a general dominance claim. This report extends the v1 technical report (arXiv:2605.28062).

</details>


### [21] [Expert-Level Crisis Detection in Mental Health Conversations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10380)
*Grace Byun,Abigail Lott,Rebecca Lipschutz,Sean T. Minton,Elizabeth A. Stinson,Jinho D. Choi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: CRADLE-Dialogue is a clinician-annotated benchmark for turn-level crisis detection in multi-turn dialogues, featuring an Alert-Confirm evaluation protocol that distinguishes early warning signals from explicit crisis identification. Models struggle at this task (mid-40% to high-60% Micro F1), and the authors release a synthetic training corpus plus a 32B-parameter model that competes with proprietary systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing crisis intervention research focuses on static, single-turn texts, ignoring the inherently conversational nature of real-world crisis scenarios. When applied to multi-turn dialogues, current models degrade significantly and fail to track evolving risk signals across conversational context. There is a need for benchmarks and methods that address turn-level crisis detection in dialogues.

Method: The authors introduce CRADLE-Dialogue, a dataset of 600 clinician-annotated dialogues with multi-label annotations at the turn level, covering suicide ideation, self-harm, and child abuse (with past vs. ongoing risk distinctions). They propose an Alert-Confirm evaluation protocol: Alert identifies early warning signals, while Confirm marks turns where the crisis becomes explicitly identifiable. A synthetic training corpus is generated, and a 32B-parameter model is fine-tuned for the task, evaluated against both open-source and proprietary models.

Result: Turn-level crisis detection proves substantially harder than dialogue-level recognition — models achieve only mid-40% to high-60% Micro F1. The proposed 32B-parameter model significantly outperforms existing open-source models and achieves competitive or superior results against proprietary models across turn-level, dialogue-level, and confirm-only evaluation settings.

Conclusion: Identifying when a crisis emerges in a conversation is far more challenging than simply recognizing its existence. CRADLE-Dialogue and the Alert-Confirm protocol provide a clinically grounded framework for advancing turn-level crisis detection, and the released model and synthetic corpus offer strong baselines for future work in conversational crisis intervention.

Abstract: Real-world crisis intervention is inherently conversational, yet existing research largely focuses on static texts.Real-world crisis intervention is inherently conversational, yet existing research largely focuses on static texts. When applied to multi-turn dialogues, current models exhibit significant performance degradation, struggling to track risk signals that emerge as context evolves. To address this gap, we introduce CRADLE-Dialogue, a clinician-annotated benchmark for turn-level crisis detection in conversational settings. The dataset features 600 dialogues with multi-label annotations across clinically grounded risks, including suicide ideation, self-harm, and child abuse, distinguishing past from ongoing risk. We further propose an Alert-Confirm evaluation protocol that distinguishes early warning signals (Alert) from turns where a specific crisis becomes explicitly identifiable (Confirm), reflecting the clinical need to intervene before risk becomes explicit. Experiments show that identifying when risk emerges is much harder than recognizing that it exists: models achieve only mid-40% to high-60% Micro F1. Additionally, we release a synthetic training corpus and a 32B-parameter model that substantially outperforms existing open-source models and achieves competitive or superior results against proprietary models across turn-level, dialogue-level, and confirm-only evaluation settings.

</details>


### [22] [Do Vision-Language Models See or Guess? Measuring and Reducing Textual-Prior Reliance with a Phrasing-Controlled Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10400)
*Pratham Singla,Shivank Garg,Vihan Singh,Paras Chopra*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces a benchmark to measure how much VLMs rely on textual priors (memorized world knowledge and question phrasing) rather than actual image content. Using 540 images with four controlled question variants—including a hardest variant that minimizes text leakage—the authors show all 11 tested VLMs degrade, especially open-weight models. A no-image ablation reveals open models collapse to near-zero accuracy, confirming textual dependence. GRPO post-training and in-context exemplars help mitigate this reliance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs increasingly produce answers based on textual priors and memorized knowledge rather than genuine visual grounding, inflating benchmark scores with confident but ungrounded responses. Existing benchmarks fail to isolate this behavior because they pair each image with a single fixed question, conflating visual and textual cues.

Method: The authors construct a 540-image benchmark spanning six reasoning categories, generating four question variants per image where phrasing—not image content—is the controlled variable. The hardest variant is written directly from the image to minimize text leakage. They benchmark 11 VLMs (open-weight to closed-source). The central diagnostic is a no-image ablation. Supplementary analyses include LLM-rated difficulty, textual similarity metrics, and human re-annotation. They also test in-context exemplars matched to variant construction and apply GRPO post-training to a small VLM.

Result: Every VLM degrades on the hardest variant; open-weight models fall furthest. The no-image ablation collapses open-weight models to their text-only floor (1–9% accuracy). LLM difficulty ratings, low base-to-final textual similarity, and human re-annotation all corroborate genuine image-dependence. In-context exemplars matching variant construction recover the most accuracy. GRPO post-training yields consistent gains across all four variants, transferring to a held-out out-of-distribution set.

Conclusion: Textual-prior reliance in VLMs is measurable and partly trainable away. The benchmark and diagnostic methods provide tools for evaluating and improving visual grounding in vision-language models.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly deployed where answers must follow from what is in the image, yet they often answer from textual priors, the question's phrasing together with memorized world knowledge, rather than from the image itself, which inflates benchmark scores and yields confident but ungrounded answers.
