Existing benchmarks rarely isolate this behavior, since each image is usually paired with a single fixed question. To measure the reliance, we build a 540-image benchmark across six reasoning categories and generate four question variants over the same images, so that phrasing rather than image content is the controlled variable. The hardest variant is written directly from the image to minimize text leakage. We benchmark eleven VLMs spanning small open-weight models to large closed-source systems: every model degrades on the hardest variant, and open models fall furthest. Our central diagnostic is a no-image ablation, which collapses the open-weight models to their text-only floor (1 to 9 percent). Three further analyses, LLM-rated difficulty, low base-to-final textual similarity, and human re-annotation, corroborate genuine image-dependence. In-context exemplars that match how a variant was built recover the most accuracy, and GRPO post-training of a small VLM yields consistent gains across all four variants that transfer to a held-out out-of-distribution set. Textual-prior reliance is measurable and partly trainable away.

</details>


### [23] [Harnessing the Collective Intelligence of AI Agents in the Wild for New Discoveries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10402)
*Federico Bianchi,Yongchan Kwon,Aneesh Pappu,James Zou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: EinsteinArena is an open platform where AI agents collaboratively tackle mathematical problems with verifiable solutions. Over time, agents share ideas, build on each other's work, and have achieved 12 new state-of-the-art results, including improving the kissing number lower bound in dimension 11 from 593 to 604 — illustrating emergent collective scientific discovery through decentralized agent interaction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scientific discovery is inherently collective, yet existing AI research agents typically operate in isolation. The authors aim to create a platform that mirrors real-world collaborative science, where agents can share partial results, discuss failures, and iteratively build on each other's contributions over extended periods.

Method: The authors built EinsteinArena, an agent-native platform featuring: (1) a live set of open mathematical problems, (2) solid verifiers for unambiguous progress measurement, (3) public leaderboards, and (4) problem-specific discussion forums enabling agent-to-agent communication. Agents submit solutions, engage in public discussion, refine verifiers, and borrow ideas from one another in an open, decentralized fashion.

Result: As of May 2026, agents on EinsteinArena have produced 12 new state-of-the-art results surpassing all prior human and AI solutions. The flagship result is the kissing number problem in dimension 11, where the lower bound was improved from 593 to 604. Crucially, this advance emerged not from a single agent but through a sequence of submissions, forum discussions, verifier refinement, and cross-agent idea borrowing.

Conclusion: The results demonstrate that decentralized scientific discovery can emerge from open interaction among autonomous agents, establishing a new paradigm for collective AI-driven research where progress arises from community dynamics rather than isolated efforts.

Abstract: Scientific discovery is often a collective process: researchers share partial results, inspect failed attempts, and build on each other's ideas over long time horizons. Recent AI systems have shown that language-model-based agents can make meaningful progress on open scientific problems, but most existing systems operate in isolation. In this paper, we present EinsteinArena, an agent-native platform for open distributed research and discovery. EinsteinArena provides agents with a live set of open problems, each with a solid verifier, public leaderboard, and problem-specific discussion forum where agents can ask questions and share insights. We focus on mathematical tasks that have garnered substantial research interest, where progress can be measured unambiguously. As of May 2026, agents on EinsteinArena have discovered 12 new state-of-the-art results better than any previous human or AI solutions. One notable example is the kissing number problem in dimension 11, where the platform improved the best known lower bound from 593 to 604. This advance did not come from a single agent or isolated run. Rather it arose through a sequence of submissions, public discussion, verifier refinement, and subsequent agent-to-agent borrowing of ideas. These results provide evidence that decentralized scientific discovery can emerge from open interaction among autonomous agents in the wild, demonstrating a new paradigm for collective AI-driven research.

</details>


### [24] [KCSAT-ML: Probing Reasoning Models with Nationwide-Cohort Human Difficulty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10403)
*Sanghee Park,Geewook Kim,Kee-Eung Kim*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: KCSAT-ML introduces a mathematics benchmark from Korean college entrance exams (2014–2025) with official per-item difficulty from hundreds of thousands of test-takers, paired with a new metric (DRG) that reveals how model errors align with human difficulty—exposing that TTS produces anti-scaling on hard items and overthinking on easy ones, while similarly accurate models can show opposite DRG values.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most math reasoning benchmarks lack per-item difficulty signals grounded in actual human performance data. Aggregate accuracy masks crucial behavioral differences: models with identical scores can fail on entirely different subsets of problems. The authors aim to expose how model errors relate to human-perceived difficulty and how test-time scaling interacts with item hardness.

Method: The authors curated KCSAT-ML, a dataset of 664 mathematics problems (339 core items) from 2014–2025 Korean CSAT exams, each tagged with official per-item error rates from nationwide cohorts (hundreds of thousands of examinees). They introduced Difficulty-aligned Reasoning Gain (DRG), a score-orthogonal metric measuring whether model errors concentrate on items humans found hard or easy. They evaluated a wide range of VLMs and LLMs (via OCR) and analyzed the interaction between test-time scaling (TTS) and item difficulty.

Result: Three key findings: (1) Low-budget accuracy collapses on high-human-error items across all model sizes. (2) Test-time scaling increases token usage roughly linearly with cohort error rate, but accuracy gains follow a non-monotonic curve. (3) Within a single model family, TTS exhibits anti-scaling (worse performance with more compute) on the hardest items and overthinking on easier items—two manifestations of the same alignment failure. DRG reveals that models with near-identical accuracy can have near-opposite DRG values, highlighting behavioral differences that aggregate metrics conceal.

Conclusion: Per-item human difficulty signals and difficulty-aligned evaluation metrics like DRG are essential for understanding model reasoning behavior. The findings reveal fundamental alignment failures in how current models distribute errors relative to human difficulty, particularly under test-time scaling. KCSAT-ML provides a principled, ecologically valid resource for benchmarking and diagnosing math reasoning in VLMs and LLMs.

Abstract: Math reasoning benchmarks have proliferated, yet most lack a per-item difficulty signal grounded in actual human performance. We introduce KCSAT-ML, a decade (2014-2025) of Korean College Scholastic Ability Test (KCSAT; Suneung) mathematics: 664 problems with a 339-item core set carrying official per-item error rates from nationwide cohorts of hundreds of thousands of examinees. We pair the benchmark with Difficulty-aligned Reasoning Gain (DRG): a score-orthogonal metric that asks whether a model's mistakes concentrate on the items humans found hard, or on items humans found easy. Together they expose, across a wide range of VLMs (and LLMs via OCR), three patterns: (i) low-budget accuracy collapses on the high-human-error tail at every model size; (ii) test-time scaling (TTS) raises token use roughly linearly with cohort error rate, while accuracy gains follow a non-monotonic curve; (iii) within a single family, TTS flips between anti-scaling on the hardest items and overthinking on easier ones -- two faces of the same alignment failure. On DRG, models with near-identical accuracy can sit at near-opposite values: one model gets wrong what humans also find hard, while another solves the hardest items yet fails on items humans find easy -- a contrast that aggregate accuracy hides. Our code and dataset builder will be open-sourced at https://github.com/naver-ai/KCSAT-ML.

</details>


### [25] [WebChallenger: A Reliable and Efficient Generalist Web Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10423)
*Jayoo Hwang,Xiaowen Zhang,Vedant Padwal*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: WebChallenger introduces a web agent framework that mimics human cognitive strategies—selective attention, persistent memory, and procedural fluency—via a structured DOM representation called PageMem. Without fine-tuning, it achieves competitive benchmarks using open-weight models at low cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based web agents rely on expensive proprietary reasoning models because existing architectures fail to replicate human-like cognitive advantages: selective attention to relevant page regions, persistent memory of website structure, and procedural fluency with common interactions. The authors aim to close this performance gap through architecture design rather than model scale.

Method: The framework is built on PageMem, a deterministic DOM-derived structured page representation that organizes each page into a hierarchy of semantic sections with short summaries. Three mechanisms operate over PageMem: (1) a divide-and-conquer observation pipeline for skimming section summaries and extracting task-relevant details; (2) a lightweight exploration-and-memory system that traverses each website once to build a reusable map of pages and element behaviors; and (3) compound action workflows that collapse multi-step interactions into single agent actions with automatic partial state handling.

Result: Using off-the-shelf open-weight models without fine-tuning, WebChallenger achieves 56.3% on WebArena, 48.7% on VisualWebArena, 51.0% on Online-Mind2Web, and 70.9% on WorkArena, approaching frontier proprietary systems at a fraction of the inference cost.

Conclusion: Architecture-level design—not model scale—can substantially close the gap between open-weight and proprietary web agents. The PageMem substrate enables the three cognitive-inspired mechanisms to generalize across websites without site-specific adapters, making autonomous web navigation both effective and cost-efficient.

Abstract: Autonomous web navigation remains challenging for LLM agents, and the strongest generalist systems rely on proprietary reasoning models whose inference cost is prohibitive for the repetitive tasks where such agents would be most useful. We argue this gap stems not from insufficient model capability but from agent architectures that fail to replicate three human cognitive advantages: selective attention to relevant page regions, persistent memory of website structure, and procedural fluency with common interaction patterns. We introduce WebChallenger, a web agent framework that addresses each gap through architecture design rather than model scale, built around PageMem: a structured page representation deterministically constructed from the DOM that exposes each page as a hierarchy of semantic sections with short summaries. On this shared substrate we build three mechanisms that mirror the three cognitive advantages: a divide-and-conquer observation pipeline that lets the agent skim section summaries and extract details only from task-relevant regions; a lightweight exploration and memory system that traverses each website once to build a reusable map of pages and element behaviors; and compound action workflows that collapse common multi-step interactions into single agent actions, handling partial state changes automatically. Because all three operate over PageMem, the framework generalizes across websites without site-specific adapters. Using off-the-shelf open-weight models without fine-tuning, our system achieves 56.3% on WebArena, 48.7% on VisualWebArena, 51.0% on Online-Mind2Web, and 70.9% on WorkArena, approaching frontier proprietary systems at a fraction of the cost. Our code is released at https://github.com/jayoohwang1/webchallenger

</details>


### [26] [Which LoRA? An Empirical Study on the Effectiveness of LoRA Techniques During Multilingual Instruction Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10428)
*Thamali Wijewardhana,Napoleon H. Reyes,Surangika Ranathunga*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether complex LoRA variants outperform basic LoRA for multilingual instruction tuning. Experiments across multiple languages reveal no significant advantage of advanced LoRA variants over basic LoRA in balancing cross-lingual transfer and knowledge retention, with hidden embedding analysis showing similar layer-wise language representations across different LoRA techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While numerous LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) variants with architectural innovations have been proposed for parameter-efficient fine-tuning, it remains unclear whether these more complex variants provide tangible benefits over basic LoRA specifically in the context of multilingual instruction tuning, where both cross-lingual transfer and knowledge retention must be balanced.

Method: The authors conduct a systematic comparison of basic LoRA against four other LoRA variants on two multilingual datasets spanning diverse target languages. They evaluate performance along two dimensions: cross-lingual transfer capability and knowledge retention. Additionally, they analyze hidden embeddings to examine layer-wise language representations across LLMs fine-tuned with different LoRA techniques.

Result: No significant advantage was found for any of the more complex LoRA variants over basic LoRA in multilingual instruction tuning tasks. The analysis of hidden embeddings further revealed that layer-wise language representations remain largely similar regardless of which LoRA variant is used, suggesting that architectural differences among LoRA techniques do not lead to meaningfully different cross-lingual adaptation.

Conclusion: The architectural novelty of advanced LoRA variants does not translate into improved cross-lingual adaptation. For multilingual instruction tuning, practitioners may confidently use basic LoRA without sacrificing performance, as more complex variants offer no demonstrable benefit in this domain.

Abstract: We investigate whether commonly available LoRA variants have an advantage over basic LoRA in multilingual instruction tuning. Experiments involving LoRA and four other variants on two datasets across diverse target languages show that there is no significant advantage in using more complex LoRA variants instead of basic LoRA, with respect to balancing cross-lingual transfer and knowledge retention. An analysis of hidden embeddings reveal that layer-wise language representation remains largely similar across LLMs fine-tuned with different LoRA techniques, suggesting that architectural novelty of LoRA techniques may not translate into better cross-lingual adaptation.

</details>


### [27] [LakeQA: An Exploratory QA Benchmark over a Million-Scale Data Lake](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10460)
*Haonan Wang,Jiaxiang Liu,Yurong Liu,Austin Senna Wijaya,Tianle Zhou,Eden Wu,Yijia Chen,Wanting You,Reya Vir,Daniela Pinto,Grace Fan,Yusen Zhang,Juliana Freire,Eugene Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LakeQA is a large-scale benchmark (~9.5 TB) for evaluating LLMs on search-centric QA over heterogeneous data lakes, requiring both multi-hop retrieval and reasoning. GPT-5.2 achieves only 18.37% exact match, highlighting the challenge of discovering and synthesizing evidence across unstructured (Wikipedia) and structured (government) sources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing reading-based QA benchmarks assume evidence is explicitly provided or trivially retrievable. Real-world QA requires searching over massive, heterogeneous data lakes where relevant documents are not pre-identified. No comprehensive benchmark jointly tests both searching and reasoning over such large-scale, multi-source data environments.

Method: The authors construct LakeQA from ~9.5 TB of Wikipedia and open-source government data, spanning both structured and unstructured formats. Each sample is annotated by at least one Ph.D.-level expert and requires long-horizon multi-hop reasoning with implicit intermediate steps — agents must discover correct documents and compose evidence across sources. Seven frontier LLMs are evaluated using exact-match scoring.

Result: LakeQA proves highly challenging for frontier LLMs: GPT-5.2 achieves only 18.37% exact-match score. The benchmark effectively exposes the difficulty of search-centric QA where retrieval and multi-hop reasoning must be jointly performed over heterogeneous data lakes.

Conclusion: LakeQA provides a realistic, challenging testbed for developing LLM agents capable of both finding and analyzing data in modern data lakes, filling a critical gap in QA benchmark design.

Abstract: Recent large language models (LLMs) have shown rapid progress in reading-based question answering (QA), where evidence is explicitly provided or can be trivially retrieved. In contrast, real-world questions are often not paired with accurate evidence documents. The useful evidence resides in massive data lakes, making search a prerequisite for answering. However, there is a lack of comprehensive benchmarks that require both searching and reasoning over large data lakes. To this end, we introduce LakeQA, a comprehensive benchmark for search-centric question answering over data lakes that jointly emphasizes searching and reasoning capabilities. LakeQA is built on a heterogeneous collection of approximately 9.5 TB of text resources from Wikipedia and open-source government data, spanning structured and unstructured data. To ensure task quality, each sample is annotated by at least one Ph.D.-level expert. Each task requires long-horizon multi-hop reasoning with implicit intermediate steps: agents need to discover the correct documents and then compose evidence across sources to produce the answer. Experimental results on seven frontier LLMs demonstrate that LakeQA is challenging. For instance, GPT-5.2 achieves only an exact-match score of 18.37% on LakeQA. Overall, LakeQA provides a realistic testbed for developing LLM agents that can both find and analyze data in modern data lakes.

</details>


### [28] [Large Language Models as Modal Models in Linguistics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10467)
*Haruto Suzuki,Saku Sugawara*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper applies modal modeling from philosophy of science to the LLM-linguistic theory debate, arguing that LLMs serve as minimal models providing how-possibly explanations (HPEs) but not yet how-actually explanations (HAEs) of human language, with their explanatory power existing on a continuum between the two.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To clarify the polarized debate about LLMs' relevance to linguistic theory by providing a philosophically rigorous framework that avoids both overstating and understating their explanatory significance.

Method: Application of the modal modeling framework from philosophy of science, combined with the mechanistic account of scientific explanation, to evaluate LLMs as minimal models capable of generating how-possibly explanations and to assess the conditions for how-actually explanations.

Result: LLMs possess genuine epistemic value as minimal models for testing modal claims about language acquisition and competence, providing how-possibly explanations. However, current LLMs fail to meet the mechanistic requirements for how-actually explanations of human language.

Conclusion: LLMs' explanatory power should be understood on a continuum between HPEs and HAEs, offering a balanced middle-ground that supports conciliationism while avoiding the extremes of insulationism and eliminativism.

Abstract: The rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs) has intensified debates about their significance for linguistic theory. These debates are commonly divided into three positions: insulationism, which regards LLMs as irrelevant to human language; eliminativism, which claims that LLMs can replace traditional linguistic theories; and conciliationism, which views them as useful tools for linguistic research. To clarify these positions, this paper applies the framework of modal modeling from the philosophy of science. We argue that LLMs possess genuine epistemic value as minimal models, even without structural correspondence to human cognition. In particular, they can provide how-possibly explanations (HPEs) by testing modal claims about language acquisition and linguistic competence. We then examine the conditions under which LLMs could qualify as how-actually explanations (HAEs) of human language, drawing on the mechanistic account of scientific explanation. We argue that current LLMs do not yet satisfy these requirements. On the basis of this analysis, we propose understanding the explanatory power of LLMs as lying on a continuum between HPEs and HAEs. This framework avoids both overstating and understating their explanatory significance and offers a more precise basis for evaluating the role of LLMs in the scientific study of language.

</details>


### [29] [Detecting Speculative Language in Biomedical Texts using Recurrent Neural Tensor Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10471)
*Dhruv Dixit*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper compares distributed sentence representation methods (Paragraph Vector and Recursive Neural Tensor Network) against traditional baselines (SVM, Naive Bayes, pattern matching) for detecting speculative language in biomedical articles. RNTN achieves a marginal improvement (F1=0.885) over the best baseline, linear bigram SVM (F1=0.881), while Paragraph Vector performs poorly (F1=0.368) despite extensive unsupervised pre-training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated detection of speculative language (hedging) in biomedical literature is critical for improving information retrieval, multi-document summarization, and knowledge discovery. The authors seek to determine whether modern distributed sentence representation methods can outperform established shallow baselines on this task.

Method: Two distributed representation approaches are evaluated: (1) the Paragraph Vector (PV) model, an unsupervised method that learns fixed-length sentence embeddings, and (2) the Recursive Neural Tensor Network (RNTN), which constructs compositional sentence representations via parse trees. These are benchmarked against three baselines: linear bigram SVM, Naive Bayes, and pattern matching. The PV model is also trained on a large unlabeled biomedical corpus to assess whether additional data improves performance.

Result: The RNTN achieves the highest F1 score of 0.885, only marginally surpassing the linear bigram SVM (F1=0.881). The Paragraph Vector model substantially underperforms with an F1 of 0.368, and extensive unsupervised pre-training on a large unlabeled dataset fails to close this gap.

Conclusion: Deep compositional models like RNTN offer only slight gains over well-tuned linear classifiers for speculation detection, while simpler distributed representations (Paragraph Vector) are ineffective. The authors discuss reasons for these disparities and provide guidance for future research in applying representation learning to biomedical hedge detection.

Abstract: In this investigation, we delve into the automated detection of speculative language within biomedical articles by utilizing distributed sentence representations and advanced deep learning techniques. The implications of such identification extend to information retrieval, multi-document summarization, and the exploration of new knowledge. Our exploration encompasses two distinct approaches for acquiring distributed sentence representations: the Paragraph Vector model and the Recursive Neural Tensor Network. These methodologies are then rigorously compared against three foundational baseline algorithms: Support Vector Machines, Naive Bayes, and pattern matching. Our findings reveal that the Recursive Neural Tensor Network (RNTN) demonstrates a slight performance edge (F1 = 0.885) over the top-performing baseline, the linear bigram SVM (F1 = 0.881). Meanwhile, the Paragraph Vector model proves less effective (F1 = 0.368), even after extensive training using an expansive, unlabeled dataset. We engage in a comprehensive discourse on the factors influencing these performance disparities and provide insightful recommendations for future research directions.

</details>


### [30] [UniSVQ: 2-bit Unified Scalar-Vector Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10520)
*Haoyu Wang,Haiyan Zhao,Xingyu Yu,Zhangyang Yao,Xu Han,Zhiyuan Liu,Maosong Sun*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: UniSVQ proposes a unified 2-bit quantization framework that bridges scalar and vector quantization by parameterizing codewords as affine transforms of integer lattices, achieving VQ-comparable performance with SQ-like inference throughput via data-driven block-wise fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 2-bit post-training quantization enables low-cost LLM deployment, but scalar quantization (SQ) suffers severe accuracy degradation while vector quantization (VQ) incurs high computational and storage overhead. A method combining the strengths of both is needed.

Method: UniSVQ parameterizes codewords as an affine transform of integer lattices, unifying SQ and VQ. This structure maintains compatibility with optimized integer kernels while preserving VQ flexibility. A data-driven block-wise fine-tuning strategy directly minimizes quantization reconstruction error.

Result: Extensive experiments across multiple LLM families and zero-shot benchmarks show UniSVQ consistently outperforms state-of-the-art SQ methods and achieves performance comparable to advanced VQ methods, while providing higher inference throughput.

Conclusion: UniSVQ effectively bridges the gap between scalar and vector quantization at 2-bit precision, delivering a practical solution that balances accuracy and inference efficiency for LLM deployment.

Abstract: Post-training quantization at the 2-bit level enables low-cost deployment and inference acceleration for large language models (LLMs). Scalar quantization (SQ) and vector quantization (VQ) are two primary quantization methods, however, the former suffers from significant performance degradation, and the latter incurs computational and storage overhead. We propose UniSVQ, a unified 2-bit quantization framework that bridges scalar and vector quantization by parameterizing codewords as an affine transform of integer lattices. This structure preserves compatibility with optimized integer kernels while retaining much of VQ's flexibility. We further introduce a data-driven block-wise fine-tuning strategy to directly minimize quantization reconstruction error. Extensive experiments across multiple LLM families and zero-shot benchmarks demonstrate that UniSVQ consistently outperforms state-of-the-art SQ methods and achieves performance comparable to advanced VQ methods, while providing higher inference throughput.

</details>


### [31] [LC-QAT: Data-Efficient 2-Bit QAT for LLMs via Linear-Constrained Vector Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10531)
*Haoyu Wang,Xingyu Yu,Haiyan Zhao,Fengxiang Wang,Xu Han*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LC-QAT introduces a 2-bit vector quantization-aware training framework for LLMs that uses learned affine mappings over discrete vectors, enabling fully differentiable end-to-end training with strong PTQ initialization and exceptional data efficiency (0.1%-10% of typical training data).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scalar quantization (SQ)-based QAT methods suffer severe performance degradation at 2-bit precision, while vector quantization (VQ) offers higher representational capacity but its discrete codebook lookup blocks end-to-end training. There is a need for a method that combines VQ's representational power with differentiable optimization.

Method: LC-QAT represents quantized weights via a learned affine mapping over discrete vectors. It generates a high-quality post-training quantization (PTQ) initialization, then enables fully differentiable end-to-end optimization that avoids explicit codebook lookup during the training forward pass, making the entire pipeline trainable.

Result: LC-QAT consistently outperforms state-of-the-art QAT methods across diverse LLMs while using only 0.1% to 10% of the training data required by competing approaches.

Conclusion: LC-QAT establishes a practical and scalable solution for extreme low-bit (2-bit) LLM deployment, combining VQ's representational capacity with the optimization benefits of differentiable training.

Abstract: Quantization-aware training (QAT) is essential for extremely low-bit large language models (LLMs). Current QAT methods are mainly based on scalar quantization (SQ), which enables efficient optimization but suffers from severe performance degradation at 2-bit precision. On the other hand, vector quantization (VQ) provides substantially higher representational capacity, but its discrete codebook lookup prevents end-to-end training. We propose LC-QAT, a 2-bit weight-only VQ-QAT framework that represents quantized weights via a learned affine mapping over discrete vectors, which yields a high-quality PTQ initialization and enables fully differentiable end-to-end optimization without explicit codebook lookup in the training forward pass. This strong post-training initialization makes LC-QAT highly data-efficient. Experiments across diverse LLMs demonstrate that LC-QAT consistently outperforms state-of-the-art QAT methods while using only 0.1%--10% of the training data. Our results establish LC-QAT as a practical and scalable solution for extreme low-bit model deployment.

</details>


### [32] [Prefilling-dLLM: Predictive Prefilling for Long-Context Inference in Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10537)
*Jing Xiong,Qi Han,Shansan Gong,Yunta Hsieh,Chengyue Wu,Chaofan Tao,Chenyang Zhao,Ngai Wong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Prefilling-dLLM introduces a training-free framework that disaggregates prefill and decode phases for diffusion LLMs, caching chunked prefix KV representations and using sparse top-K chunk retrieval to reduce per-step complexity from quadratic in full context to quadratic only in decode length, achieving significant speedups and state-of-the-art quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion LLMs recompute the entire prefix at every denoising step, leading to quadratic scaling with context length that becomes prohibitive for long-context scenarios. Existing solutions are insufficient, motivating a method to reduce this computational burden without retraining.

Method: The proposed framework partitions the prefix into N chunks, caches their KV representations once during a prefilling phase, and during decoding selects only the top-K most relevant chunks using intra-chunk token sparsity. This disaggregates prefill from decode. Additionally, beginning-of-sequence tokens are prepended to each chunk as periodic attention anchors to eliminate the lost-in-the-middle phenomenon. A custom attention kernel enables parallelized decoding over non-contiguously cached chunk KVs.

Result: On LongBench and InfiniteBench, Prefilling-dLLM achieves state-of-the-art quality among dLLM acceleration methods. The attention kernel yields 9.1–28.0× speedup at 8K–32K context lengths. The periodic attention anchors effectively eliminate the lost-in-the-middle phenomenon.

Conclusion: Prefilling-dLLM demonstrates that sparse prefilling with chunk-level KV caching and top-K retrieval can outperform dense attention in diffusion LLMs, dramatically reducing long-context inference cost while maintaining or improving generation quality.

Abstract: Diffusion large language models (dLLMs) re-encode the entire prefix at every denoising step, causing recomputation that scales
  quadratically with context length and becomes prohibitive for long-context scenarios. We propose Prefilling-dLLM, a training-free
  prefill-decode disaggregation framework for dLLMs that partitions the prefix into N chunks, caches their KV representations once,
  and selects the top-K most relevant chunks with intra-chunk token sparsity for decoding, showing that sparse prefilling can
  outperform dense attention while reducing per-step complexity from quadratic in the full sequence length to quadratic only in the
  decode length. On LongBench and InfiniteBench, Prefilling-dLLM achieves state-of-the-art quality among dLLM acceleration methods,
  and an attention kernel that parallelizes decoding over the non-contiguously cached chunk KV yields 9.1--28.0x speedup at 8K--32K
  contexts. We further show that beginning-of-sequence tokens prepended to each chunk act as periodic attention anchors that eliminate
  the lost-in-the-middle phenomenon. Code is available at https://github.com/menik1126/Prefilling-dLLM.

</details>


### [33] [Benchmarking Knowledge Editing using Logical Rules](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10554)
*Tatiana Moteu Ngoli,NDah Jean Kouagou,Hamada M. Zahera,Axel-Cyrille Ngonga Ngomo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces a new benchmark for evaluating knowledge editing methods in LLMs, focusing not only on direct fact recall but also on whether edited facts correctly propagate to their logical consequences. Using knowledge graph rules to generate multi-hop questions, the authors show that popular methods like ROME and FT suffer up to a 24% performance drop on entailed knowledge versus directly edited facts, highlighting the need for semantics-aware evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing knowledge editing benchmarks only test recall of directly edited facts, ignoring whether the model can infer logical consequences of those edits. This creates a blind spot: a method might appear successful at inserting a fact while failing to propagate its entailments, undermining real-world reliability.

Method: The authors construct a benchmark by (1) extracting relevant logical rules from a knowledge graph for a given edit, (2) generating multi-hop questions based on these rules to probe entailments, and (3) evaluating popular knowledge editing methods (ROME, FT) on both directly edited facts and their logical consequences, measuring the performance gap.

Result: Existing methods like ROME and FT show a performance gap of up to 24% between directly edited knowledge and entailed knowledge, demonstrating that current approaches fail to reliably inject the downstream logical consequences of a fact edit.

Conclusion: There is a critical need for semantics-aware evaluation frameworks in knowledge editing. Current methods can insert isolated facts but cannot ensure logical coherence, which is essential for real-world deployment of LLMs requiring up-to-date knowledge.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in real-world applications that require access to up-to-date knowledge. However, retraining LLMs is computationally expensive. Therefore, knowledge editing techniques are crucial for maintaining current information and correcting erroneous assertions within pre-trained models. Current benchmarks for knowledge editing primarily focus on recalling edited facts, often neglecting their logical consequences. To address this limitation, we introduce a new benchmark designed to evaluate how knowledge editing methods handle the logical consequences of a single fact edit. Our benchmark extracts relevant logical rules from a knowledge graph for a given edit. Then, it generates multi-hop questions based on these rules to assess the impact on logical consequences. Our findings indicate that while existing knowledge editing approaches can accurately insert direct assertions into LLMs, they frequently fail to inject entailed knowledge. Specifically, experiments with popular methods like ROME and FT reveal a substantial performance gap, up to 24%, between evaluations on directly edited knowledge and on entailed knowledge. This highlights the critical need for semantics-aware evaluation frameworks in knowledge editing.

</details>


### [34] [Hidden Consensus:Preference-Validity Compression in Human Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10569)
*Dorcas Chia Ern Chua,Karen Myn Hui Lee,Jia Yue Tan,Zhen Xue Gue,Norzalena Abdul Hamid,Azima Binti Azmi,Keat Mei Yeong,Aizat Izyani binti Mujab,Hafsah Noor Azam,Chee Guo Khoo,Han Ying Lim,Chee Seng Chan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Standard RLHF reduces heterogeneous human judgments to a single scalar reward, which the authors argue misrepresents alignment in pluralistic societies. Studying Malaysia as a test case, they find 79% of prompts have multiple majority-supported responses discarded by single-winner aggregation, demonstrating "Preference-Validity Compression" — a measurement-validity failure where plural-valid options are collapsed into one target.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RLHF pipelines aggregate diverse human preferences into a single reward signal, implicitly treating disagreement as noise. In structurally plural societies — where divergent judgments stem from legitimate cultural, linguistic, regional, historical, or normative differences — this reduction systematically mis-measures alignment, discarding valid response options that reflect coherent interpretive frames.

Method: The authors use Malaysia as a diagnostic setting, collecting 321 preference events from 20 participants across 107 trio-annotated prompts. They analyze RLHF-style feedback aggregation by examining preference events that link prompts, responses, and acceptability judgments across different interpretive frames. They compare single-winner aggregation outcomes against the full set of majority-supported responses to quantify how many valid options are discarded.

Result: In 79% of prompts, more than one response receives majority support — options that single-winner aggregation would discard. Apparent dominance gaps between top responses shrink substantially when all majority-supported responses are considered. Participants routinely select multiple acceptable responses, and discarded responses reflect coherent local, practical, or cultural frames rather than noise. Single-winner aggregation thus captures argmax acceptability, not genuine plural alignment.

Conclusion: The authors identify this as a measurement-validity problem and propose that future alignment methods should satisfy "Validity-Preserving Consistency" — remaining stable across plural-valid interpretive frames rather than collapsing them into a single optimization target.

Abstract: Standard RLHF pipelines often reduce heterogeneous human judgments into a single scalar reward target. We argue that this reduction can mis-measure alignment in structurally plural societies, where disagreement may reflect culturally, historically, linguistically, regionally, or normatively grounded interpretations rather than annotation noise. We call this failure Preference-Validity Compression, the collapse of multiple plural-valid response options into a single optimization target. Using Malaysia as a diagnostic setting, we analyze RLHF-style feedback aggregation through preference events linking prompts, responses, and acceptability judgments across interpretive frames. Across 321 preference events from 20 participants and 107 trio-annotated prompts, 79% of prompts contain more than one majority-supported response that single-winner aggregation would discard, and apparent dominance gaps between top responses diminish when all majority-supported options are considered. Participants frequently select multiple acceptable responses, and discarded responses demonstrably reflect coherent local, practical, or cultural frames. These findings show that majority aggregation in this corpus measures argmax acceptability rather than plural alignment. We treat this as a measurement-validity issue and argue that future alignment methods should satisfy Validity-Preserving Consistency, remaining stable across plural-valid interpretive frames rather than collapsing them into a single reward target.

</details>


### [35] [ParaBridge: Bridging Paralinguistic Perception and Dialogue Behavior in Speech Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10581)
*Yuxiang Wang,Qinke Ni,Shengbo Cai,Wan Lin,Liqiang Zhang,Zhizheng Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Current SLMs detect paralinguistic cues but fail to act on them in dialogue. The authors propose ParaBridge, an on-policy self-distillation method that converts a brittle inference-time scaffold into stable model behavior, using the scaffold's predictions as dense supervision targets. This significantly improves paralinguistic responsiveness while preserving general capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SLMs exhibit a perception-behavior gap: they can recognize paralinguistic cues (tone, background noise, etc.) but often ignore them during open-ended dialogue. Simple inference-time scaffolds can prompt the model to use these cues, but they are brittle under multi-turn context and competing instructions. The goal is to internalize this paralinguistic sensitivity into the model's native behavior.

Method: ParaBridge is an on-policy self-distillation method. During training, a scaffolded version of the model (with access to paralinguistic instructions) generates next-token targets along its trajectory. The scaffold-free model rolls out its own responses and receives supervision from these scaffolded targets, using full-vocabulary dense supervision. This teaches the model when non-lexical cues should influence replies, without requiring curated dialogues, human labels, or external reward models. The scaffold is a temporary privileged view used only during training.

Result: On Qwen3-Omni-thinking, ParaBridge raises scaffold-free VoxSafeBench SAR from 14.6% to 40.3% and improves EchoMind average rating from 3.27 to 3.92. General ability is preserved: MMAU-Pro, VoiceBench, and GPQA all remain within 0.4 points of the original model. The method also generalizes to unseen paralinguistic cues, transfers from safety-oriented training to empathy-oriented dialogue, and works on a different SLM backbone.

Conclusion: ParaBridge effectively closes the perception-behavior gap in SLMs by distilling paralinguistic sensitivity from a brittle inference scaffold into stable, scaffold-free model behavior. The approach is data-efficient (no curated dialogues or human labels), preserves general capabilities, and shows strong generalization across unseen cues, tasks, and model architectures.

Abstract: Speech carries more information than just words: a child's voice, a fearful tone, or a noisy background should all lead a sufficiently competent spoken-dialogue assistant to different replies. Current Speech Language Models (SLMs) can recognize such paralinguistic cues but often ignore them in open-ended dialogue. We observe that a simple paralinguistic instruction scaffold at the inference stage narrows this perception-behavior gap, suggesting that the relevant cues are already latent in the model. Such scaffolds, however, remain brittle under multi-turn context and competing instructions. Therefore, we propose \textbf{ParaBridge}, an on-policy self-distillation method that turns a brittle inference-time scaffold into stable model behavior. During training, the scaffold serves only as a temporary privileged view; the scaffold-free model rolls out its own response, while the scaffolded view supplies dense, full-vocabulary next-token targets along its trajectory. This supervision teaches when non-lexical cues should affect the reply without the need for curated dialogues, human labels, or external reward models. On Qwen3-Omni-thinking, ParaBridge raises scaffold-free VoxSafeBench SAR from $14.6\%$ to $40.3\%$ and improves EchoMind average rating from $3.27$ to $3.92$. It also preserves general ability, with MMAU-Pro, VoiceBench, and GPQA all within $0.4$ points of the original model. Beyond the training distribution, ParaBridge generalizes to unseen paralinguistic cues, transfers from safety-oriented training to empathy-oriented dialogue, and works on a different SLM backbone.

</details>


### [36] [Small Data, Big Noise: Adversarial Training for Robust Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10610)
*Eitan Cohen,Idan Simai,Uri Shaham*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SDBN is a PEFT framework that integrates adversarial training to improve robustness and generalization, especially in low-resource and noisy settings, using character-level gradient-based edits (SDBN-h) or LLM-generated variants (SDBN-p).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current PEFT methods lack robustness to noise and suffer performance degradation with limited training data. The intersection of adversarial training and PEFT remains underexplored despite their complementary strengths.

Method: SDBN introduces adversarial training into PEFT via two discrete uncertainty set variants: SDBN-h enumerates character-level edits (insertions, deletions, substitutions) and selects worst-case variants using gradients; SDBN-p leverages LLM-generated paraphrases for robust optimization in generative tasks. The framework adds no extra parameters and incurs only modest computational overhead.

Result: Experiments across multiple benchmarks show substantial improvements, particularly in low-resource settings and under both word-level and character-level corruptions, outperforming alternative approaches.

Conclusion: SDBN demonstrates that adversarial training can be effectively integrated into PEFT, making deployments more reliable in real-world scenarios where data scarcity and linguistic variability coexist, without significant computational cost.

Abstract: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) has become essential for adapting foundation models to downstream NLP tasks. However, current PEFT methods often struggle with robustness to noise and performance degradation on limited training data. We propose SDBN (Small Data Big Noise), a unified framework that brings adversarial training to PEFT - a combination that remains less studied in the PEFT setting despite its complementary strengths - to enhance model robustness and generalization, outperforming alternative approaches. We also introduce two variants of the method that use discrete uncertainty sets: SDBN-h, which enumerates character-level edits and selects worst-case variants using gradients, and SDBN-p, which uses LLM-generated variants for robust optimization in generative tasks. Experiments across multiple benchmarks reveal substantial improvements, particularly in low-resource settings and under both word-level and character-level corruptions. This framework addresses the less explored intersection of adversarial training and parameter-efficient adaptation, without introducing additional parameters or only modest computational overhead, making PEFT deployments more reliable in real-world scenarios where data scarcity and linguistic variability often coexist

</details>


### [37] [Dynamic Linear Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10650)
*Xin Wang,Hui Shen,Boyuan Zheng,Xueshen Liu,Minkyoung Cho,Zhongwei Wan,Zesen Zhao,Zhuoqing Mao,Shen Yan,Mi Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: DLA proposes a dynamic memory framework for multi-state linear attention in LLMs, replacing fixed state merging with information-aware adaptive merging and capacity-bounded memory management, achieving superior long-context performance over existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard attention's quadratic complexity limits LLM scalability to long contexts. While linear attention reduces this cost, existing multi-state linear attention methods use rigid, fixed merging policies that fail to adapt to dynamic token importance, causing irreversible loss of critical information and error accumulation over long sequences.

Method: DLA introduces two components: (1) Information-Aware Dynamic State Merging, which adaptively determines state boundaries by detecting token-level information variation — preserving fine-grained representations near semantic transitions and aggressively summarizing stable regions; (2) Capacity-Bounded Memory Modeling, which maintains a fixed-size chronological state cache by selectively merging adjacent low-information states to bound memory growth with minimal information loss. The framework is pre-trained on two different linear attention architectures.

Result: DLA was evaluated on 16 datasets across three categories and demonstrated superior performance over state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: Dynamic, information-aware memory management significantly improves multi-state linear attention for long-context LLMs by preventing error accumulation and preserving semantically critical information, outperforming fixed merging strategies across diverse benchmarks.

Abstract: The scalability of Large Language Models (LLMs) to long contexts is fundamentally constrained by the quadratic complexity of standard attention, motivating the adoption of linear attention mechanisms with sub-quadratic cost. To improve representation capacity under long contexts, recent approaches organize memory in a multi-state manner. However, existing multi-state linear attention methods rely on fixed state merging policies that cannot adapt to dynamically varying token importance, irreversibly obscuring critical tokens and causing severe error accumulation over long sequences. To address this limitation, we propose DLA, a dynamic memory modeling framework for multi-state linear attention. DLA introduces (i) Information-Aware Dynamic State Merging, which adaptively determines state boundaries based on token-level information variation, preserving high-resolution representations around semantic transitions while aggressively summarizing stable regions, and (ii) Capacity-Bounded Memory Modeling, which maintains a fixed-size, chronologically ordered state cache by selectively merging adjacent low-information states to control memory growth with minimal information loss. We pre-train DLA on two different linear attention models and evaluate on 16 datasets across three categories. Experimental results demonstrate the superiority of DLA over state-of-the-art.

</details>


### [38] [Speaker Group Encoding in Self-supervised Speech Recognition Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10654)
*Felix Herron,Solange Rossato Alexandre Allauzen,Benoit Favre,François Portet*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Analyzes what self-supervised speech models (S3Ms) encode about speaker groups (gender, age, dialect, ethnicity, native speaker status). Finds that ASR finetuning discards phonetically-variant speaker info but retains semantically-variant info; SID finetuning does the opposite. Fairness-enhancing ASR algorithms alter encoding of phonetic SGCs more than semantic ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding what speaker group information is encoded in self-supervised speech recognition models, and how different finetuning objectives (ASR, SID, fairness-oriented ASR) affect this encoding, in order to inform the design of fairer ASR systems.

Method: The authors examine S3Ms across several states: pretrained, finetuned on speaker identification (SID), finetuned on automatic speech recognition (ASR), and ASR-finetuned with a fairness-enhancing algorithm. They probe the models for encoded speaker group categories (SGCs) — gender, age, dialect, ethnicity, native speaker status — and distinguish between phonetically-variant and semantically-variant SGCs. Layer-wise analysis and embedding subdimension identification are also performed.

Result: Pretrained S3Ms encode multiple SGCs. SID finetuning amplifies phonetically-variant SGCs but not semantically-variant ones. ASR finetuning discards phonetically-variant SGI while retaining semantically-variant SGI. Fairness-enhancing ASR algorithms primarily affect encoding of phonetically-variant SGCs, with less impact on semantically-variant SGCs. Layer-wise encoding patterns and specific embedding subdimensions responsible for different SGCs were identified.

Conclusion: Different finetuning objectives differentially affect speaker group information encoding in S3Ms along a phonetic-vs-semantic axis. These insights can guide the development of fairer ASR algorithms by targeting how and where specific types of speaker group information are encoded.

Abstract: We investigate what self-supervised speech recognition models (S3Ms) learn about speaker groups (SGs). We examine several states of S3Ms: pretrained, finetuned on speaker identification (SID), finetuned on automatic speech recognition (ASR), and ASR-finetuned using a fairness enhancing algorithm. We find that S3Ms encode information about several speaker group categories (SGCs), including their gender, age, dialect, ethnicity, and whether they are a native speaker. We find that finetuning for SID amplifies certain SGCs, namely those whose variance is more phonetic in nature, though it does not amplify other SGCs, namely those whose variance is more semantic in nature. On the other hand, finetuning for ASR discards phonetically variant speaker group information (SGI) but retains semantically variant SGI. We find that ASR algorithms designed for fairness improvement change to what extent SGI is encoded in S3Ms; however, this is primarily true for for phonetically variant SGCs, and less true for semantically variant SGCs. We discuss how SGI is encoded by each layer, and identify subdimensions of embeddings responsible for encoding different SGCs. Finally, we discuss how our findings could be beneficial in designing fairer ASR algorithms.

</details>


### [39] [Are We Evaluating Knowledge or Phrasing? Mitigating MCQA Sensitivity with ParaEval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10657)
*João Maria Janeiro,Mathurin Videau,Andrea Caciolai,Benjamin Piwowarski,Patrick Gallinari,Loic Barrault*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Standard log-likelihood scoring in MCQA benchmarks is unreliable because it confounds surface-form familiarity with true capability. The authors propose ParaEval, which uses multiple paraphrases per answer option and scores based on the most favorable phrasing, reducing false performance gaps from >2 points to <1 point.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard MCQA evaluation using log-likelihood scoring produces artifacts: models with identical knowledge can show spurious performance gaps (>2 points) simply due to sensitivity to exact answer phrasing. This undermines the reliability of MCQA benchmarks for assessing true language model capabilities.

Method: The authors use a controlled testbed of 1B-8B parameter models trained on identical knowledge to isolate the surface-form effect. They propose ParaEval, an evaluation framework that generates multiple paraphrases for each answer option and scores each model using its most favorable (highest likelihood) paraphrase, thereby disentangling surface-form familiarity from underlying knowledge.

Result: Under standard scoring, models with identical knowledge exhibit a false performance gap exceeding 2 points. ParaEval reduces this gap to below 1 point. The evaluation artifact and the effectiveness of ParaEval are confirmed to persist in larger frontier models (70B and 120B open-source).

Conclusion: ParaEval provides a robust, efficient method for evaluating true underlying capability in LLMs, mitigating the confounding effect of surface-form familiarity inherent in standard log-likelihood MCQA scoring.

Abstract: Multiple-choice (MCQA) benchmarks are the standard for evaluating pretrained large language models, but their reliance on log-likelihood scoring makes them unreliable. Specifically, standard scores are highly sensitive to the exact phrasing (surface form) of the answers, conflating a model's familiarity with a specific phrase with its actual capability. We demonstrate this flaw using a controlled testbed of 1B-8B models trained on the same knowledge. Despite having identical knowledge, standard metrics falsely report a performance gap of over 2 points. To solve this, we propose ParaEval, an evaluation framework that queries models using multiple paraphrases per answer option. By scoring each model based on its most favorable phrasing, ParaEval successfully reduces the false performance gap to below 1 point. We confirm that these evaluation artifacts, and the improvements from ParaEval, persist in frontier 70B and 120B open-source models. Ultimately, ParaEval provides a robust and efficient way to evaluate true underlying capability rather than surface-form familiarity.

</details>


### [40] [Multilingual Word-Level Forced Alignment with Self-Supervised Representations and Learned Dynamic Programming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10675)
*Roy Weber,Meidan Zehavi,Rotem Rousso,Joseph Keshet*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A novel multilingual word-level forced alignment method combining MMS representations with a self-supervised phoneme boundary detector, using a learned alignment decoder, outperforms existing tools on English and generalizes to unseen languages without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing forced alignment tools like MFA require language-specific training and phonetic resources, limiting scalability to low-resource languages. The authors aim to leverage multilingual speech models (MMS) to create an alignment system that works accurately across 1100+ languages without per-language training.

Method: A two-stage architecture: (1) An alignment encoder that fuses MMS representations with UnSupSeg (self-supervised phoneme boundary detector) outputs to estimate word-boundary probabilities over long temporal contexts. (2) A learned alignment decoder employing learned dynamic programming that integrates encoder outputs with segmental features from both representations to infer final word boundaries. The model is trained iteratively on TIMIT and Buckeye corpora.

Result: The proposed method outperforms Montreal Forced Aligner (MFA) and MMS-based alignment on both TIMIT and Buckeye datasets. On unseen languages (Dutch, German, Hebrew), it achieves performance consistently better than or on par with existing alignment approaches.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates effective cross-lingual generalization, suggesting it can scale to over 1100 languages supported by MMS without requiring further language-specific training, offering a practical solution for building forced alignment systems for low-resource languages.

Abstract: We present a method for accurate multilingual word-level forced alignment, consisting of an alignment encoder and a learned alignment decoder. The encoder integrates two representations: one from the Massively Multilingual Speech (MMS) model and another from a self-supervised phoneme boundary detector (UnSupSeg). It learns to fuse them and to estimate word-boundary probabilities over long temporal contexts. The alignment decoder is a learned dynamic programming that combines encoder outputs with segmental features over the MMS and UnSupSeg representations to infer final word boundaries. Trained iteratively on TIMIT and Buckeye, the proposed approach outperforms Montreal Forced Aligner (MFA) and MMS-based alignment on both datasets. On unseen languages (Dutch, German, and Hebrew), the proposed model achieves performance consistently better than or on par with existing alignment approaches, indicating its potential to scale to 1100+ languages supported by MMS without further training.

</details>


### [41] [REAL: A Reasoning-Enhanced Graph Framework for Long-Term Memory Management of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10694)
*Keer Lu,Liwei Chen,Guoqing Jiang,Zhiheng Qin,Yunhuai Liu,Wentao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: REAL is a long-term conversational memory system that represents facts as a temporal, confidence-aware directed property graph with non-destructive updates and a query-driven hybrid beam search retrieval mechanism augmented by counterfactual inference, achieving 22.72% average improvement over existing baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs face inherent context-window limitations for long-horizon interactions. Existing memory systems suffer from three critical shortcomings: (1) flat text-based memory fails to capture explicit inter-memory relations, (2) structured memory systems destructively overwrite evolving facts, and (3) retrieval mechanisms are query-agnostic and passive when evidence is incomplete.

Method: REAL models long-term conversational memory as a temporal and confidence-aware directed property graph, where each atomic fact comprises entities, relations, valid-time intervals, confidence scores, and exploration intent labels. It employs a non-destructive temporal update strategy that preserves parallel fact versions with their validity intervals. For retrieval, REAL anchors query-relevant root entities, decouples their exploration intents, and performs semantic evaluator-guided hybrid beam search to extract compact memory subgraphs. A counterfactual inference component repairs unreliable retrieval states and recovers missing evidence via implicit logical relations.

Result: REAL achieves an average improvement of 22.72% over flat-text, graph-based, and other existing memory baselines in long-term memory performance across comprehensive experiments.

Conclusion: REAL demonstrates that a temporal, confidence-aware graph-based memory architecture with non-destructive updates and semantically guided retrieval with counterfactual inference significantly advances long-term conversational memory for LLMs.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly expected to interact with users over long time horizons. However, due to their finite context window, LLMs cannot retain all past interactions, making long-term memory management essential for storing, updating, and retrieving historical information beyond the context limit. Although recent memory systems attempt to address this issue by storing historical information externally, existing approaches suffer from three key limitations: flat text-based memory organizations fail to capture explicit relations among memories, structured memory systems often destructively overwrite evolving facts, and current retrieval mechanisms remain query-agnostic and passive when evidence is incomplete. REAL constructs long-term conversational memory as a temporal and confidence-aware directed property graph, where each atomic fact is represented with entities, relations, valid-time intervals, confidence scores, and exploration intent labels. During memory construction, REAL adopts a non-destructive temporal update strategy that preserves parallel fact versions and their validity intervals, enabling faithful tracking of fact evolution. During retrieval, REAL anchors query-relevant root entities, decouples their exploration intents, and performs semantic evaluator-guided hybrid beam search to extract compact memory subgraphs. It further incorporates counterfactual inference to repair unreliable retrieval states and recover missing memory evidence through implicit logical relations. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that REAL substantially improves long-term memory performance over flat-text, graph-based, and existing memory baselines, achieving an average improvement of 22.72\%.

</details>


### [42] [Attention Expansion: Enhancing Keyphrase Extraction from Long Documents with Attention-Augmented Contextualized Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10716)
*Roberto Martínez-Cruz,Alvaro J. López-López,José Portela*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper proposes an attention expansion mechanism that augments PLM token representations with information from out-of-context chunks via pre-trained word embeddings, enabling efficient long-document keyphrase extraction without requiring full-document attention or expensive LLM inference. The method consistently improves F1 scores across five PLM backbones and five benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-document keyphrase extraction is challenging because salient keyphrase evidence is often scattered across distant sections that exceed the limited context window of most PLMs. While long-context LLMs can handle broader contexts, their computational cost makes them impractical for efficient, high-throughput KPE.

Method: The authors propose an attention expansion mechanism that augments PLM token representations with information from surrounding out-of-context chunks using pre-trained word embeddings. This expands the effective contextual scope without requiring full-document attention or LLM inference.

Result: The approach was evaluated across five PLM backbones (general-purpose, scientific, task-specific, and long-context encoders), two training regimes, and five benchmark corpora. Attention expansion consistently enhanced KPE performance across all settings, outperforming SOTA models with notable F1 improvements, including on domain-specific, task-specialized, and native long-context models.

Conclusion: Attention expansion is established as an efficient and effective strategy for long-document KPE, providing complementary information rather than merely compensating for limited input length.

Abstract: Pre-trained language models (PLMs) have achieved strong performance in keyphrase extraction (KPE), largely due to their ability to generate rich contextualized representations. However, long-document KPE remains challenging because salient keyphrase evidence may be scattered across distant document sections that cannot be jointly captured within the limited context window of most PLMs. Although long-context large language models (LLMs) can process broader textual contexts, their computational cost limits their practicality for efficient and high-throughput KPE. To overcome this limitation, we propose an attention expansion mechanism that augments PLM token representations with information from surrounding out-of-context chunks using pre-trained word embeddings. The proposed mechanism expands the effective contextual scope of PLM-based KPE models without requiring full-document attention or expensive LLM-based inference. We evaluate our approach across five PLM backbones, including general-purpose, scientific, task-specific, and long-context encoders, using two training regimes and five benchmark corpora from scientific and news domains. Experimental results demonstrate that attention expansion consistently enhances KPE performance across all evaluation settings, outperforming state-of-the-art models and yielding notable improvements in F1 score. The improvements extend to domain-specific, task-specialized, and native long-context models, showing that the proposed mechanism provides complementary information rather than merely compensating for limited input length. These results establish attention expansion as an efficient and effective strategy for long-document KPE.

</details>


### [43] [Continual LLM Upcycling: A Predictor-Gated Bank-Wise Sparsity Training Recipe for Dense-to-Sparse LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10722)
*Ruixuan Huang,Jinyuan Shi,Hantao Huang,Yifan Huang,Ziyi Guan,Hao Zeng,Ian En-Hsu Yen,Minghui Yu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Proposes dense-to-sparse continual training to convert a dense Qwen2.5-8B into a channel-sparse LLM using predictor-gated sparse SwiGLU FFNs with bank-wise top-k routing (4x sparsity), and identifies/fixes a layer-local long-context failure mode on RULER-CWE.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To construct hardware-efficient channel-sparse large language models from existing dense pretrained checkpoints via continual training, avoiding the inference overhead of post-hoc sparse methods by integrating the routing mechanism directly into the training path.

Method: Starting from Qwen2.5-8B at 32K context length, replaces standard SwiGLU FFN with a predictor-gated sparse variant: a low-rank predictor produces routing logits per token/layer, and a bank-wise top-k rule selects 16 out of 64 channels per bank (4x sparsity). The entire routing module is trained during continual training. A single-layer repair algorithm is proposed to fix layer-local degradation on long-context tasks.

Result: Demonstrates successful upcycling of a dense 8B model into a 4x channel-sparse variant. Reports benchmark performance and training insights. Identifies a layer-local failure mode on RULER-CWE and shows the proposed single-layer repair substantially improves performance in the affected length range.

Conclusion: Dense-to-sparse continual training with predictor-gated sparse FFNs is a viable path to hardware-oriented sparse LLMs. The identified layer-local long-context failure can be effectively mitigated with a targeted repair algorithm.

Abstract: We study dense-to-sparse continual training as a way to construct channel-sparse large language models from dense checkpoints. Starting from a Qwen2.5-8B dense backbone, we continue training at 32K context and introduce a predictor-gated sparse SwiGLU FFN in the 32K stage. For each token and layer, we use a low-rank predictor to produce FFN-channel routing logits. We then apply a bank-wise top-k rule to retain 16 channels in every 64-channel bank, yielding 4x sparsity in the FFN intermediate activation. Unlike post-hoc sparse inference methods, the routing module is placed on the main language modeling path and optimized during continual training, enabling the dense model to be upcycled into a hardware-oriented sparse model. We report the architecture, training recipe, benchmark performance, and training lessons. We also identify a layer-local long-context failure mode on RULER-CWE and propose a single-layer repair algorithm that substantially improves the affected length range.

</details>


### [44] [Detecting Knowledge Gaps from Conversational AI Interactions Using Curriculum Prerequisite Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10736)
*Youssef Medhat,Junsoo Park,Ploy Thajchayapong,Ashok K. Goel*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Summary generation failed


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivation analysis unavailable

Method: Method extraction failed

Result: Result analysis unavailable

Conclusion: Conclusion extraction failed

Abstract: Large online courses generate thousands of student questions directed at conversational AI teaching assistants, yet these interaction logs remain largely untapped as diagnostic signals. We present a pipeline that maps student questions from a conversational AI teaching assistant to curriculum topics using a few-shot text classifier, grounded in a GPT-4-extracted prerequisite knowledge graph of course concepts. Evaluated on 1,340 question events from 164 students in a graduate-level AI course, our classifier achieves 80.0% accuracy across 43 labels (42 curriculum topics plus an "unknown" abstention class). Topic-level question volume correlates significantly with student self-reported difficulty from an independent mid-semester survey (rho = 0.491, p = 0.008, n = 28 topics), providing convergent evidence that the classified question stream reflects genuine topic difficulty. These results demonstrate that conversational AI interaction logs, mapped onto curriculum structure, carry actionable signals about topic-level knowledge gaps and provide instructors with a curriculum-grounded view of which topics warrant attention.

</details>


### [45] [ArabiGEE: A Hierarchical Taxonomy for Arabic Grammatical Error Explanation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10765)
*Khaled Elhady,Omar Kallas,Nizar Habash,Bashar Alhafni*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ArabiGEE introduces the first structured Arabic grammatical error explanation (GEE) taxonomy with 27 error types, 140 correction types, and 324 explanations across four linguistic dimensions, enabling structured evaluation of LLMs on Arabic GEE tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GEE approaches treat explanation generation as free-form text without explicit error type grounding. There is no comprehensive, structured taxonomy for Arabic grammatical error explanations, limiting both human annotation consistency and automatic evaluation of LLMs on Arabic GEE.

Method: The authors construct ArabiGEE, a hierarchical taxonomy organizing grammatical explanations across four dimensions: orthographic, morphological, syntactic, and lexical. The taxonomy defines 27 error types, 140 correction types, and 324 associated explanations. They manually annotate portions of existing Arabic grammatical error correction corpora using this taxonomy and demonstrate its use for automatic evaluation of LLM-generated grammatical explanations.

Result: The taxonomy yields 27 error types, 140 correction types, and 324 explanations, covering orthographic, morphological, syntactic, and lexical dimensions. Manual annotation of existing Arabic GEC corpora demonstrates the taxonomy's applicability, and structured explanations enable automatic evaluation of LLMs on Arabic GEE.

Conclusion: ArabiGEE provides a principled, structured framework for Arabic grammatical error explanation, moving beyond free-form generation. It supports both consistent human annotation and automated LLM evaluation, with code and data released publicly to facilitate further research.

Abstract: We introduce ArabiGEE, the first comprehensive Arabic grammatical error explanation (GEE) taxonomy grounded in explicit error types. Unlike existing GEE approaches that treat explanation generation as free-form text, ArabiGEE organizes grammatical explanations through a hierarchical structure spanning orthographic, morphological, syntactic, and lexical dimensions. The taxonomy consists of 27 error types, 140 correction types, and 324 associated explanations. We apply ArabiGEE to manually annotate portions of existing Arabic grammatical error correction corpora and demonstrate how structured grammatical explanations can support automatic evaluation of LLMs on Arabic GEE. Our code and data are publicly available.

</details>


### [46] [Dep-LLM: Training-Free Depression Diagnosis via Evidence-Guided Structured Multi-factor with Reliable LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10796)
*Yiqing Lyu,Xianbing Zhao,Buzhou Tang,Ronghuan Jiang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Dep-LLM is a training-free, three-stage framework using frozen foundation LLMs for automatic depression detection from clinical interviews. It decomposes dialogues into five clinical themes via Chain-of-Thought, modulates confidence via token-level entropy analysis, and collaboratively fuses multi-factor predictions. It outperforms zero-shot baselines, supervised domain-specific LLMs, and commercial LLMs on DAIC-WOZ and E-DAIC without any training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Two core challenges: (1) depression-relevant cues are complex yet sparsely distributed across lengthy, multi-topic clinical interviews, causing superficial reasoning; (2) labeled clinical data is scarce due to privacy, and training/fine-tuning costs are high, hindering supervised ADD deployment.

Method: Dep-LLM is a training-free framework with three stages: (1) CoT Depression Multi-factor Analysis — structurally decomposes long dialogues into five clinically aligned themes and generates evidence-grounded rationales; (2) Confidence Analysis and Modulation — quantifies epistemic reliability via token-level entropy, then applies intra-label and inter-theme modulation to amplify trustworthy signals and suppress uncertain ones without training; (3) Collaborative Multi-factor Prediction — dynamically integrates multi-factor signals weighted by confidence into the final diagnosis. All stages operate on frozen off-the-shelf LLMs.

Result: Dep-LLM surpasses the zero-shot baseline on nearly all 21 foundation LLMs across 9 metrics (accuracy, macro F1, weighted-average F1, etc.) and outperforms state-of-the-art supervised domain-specific LLMs and the latest closed-source commercial LLMs on DAIC-WOZ and E-DAIC datasets.

Conclusion: A training-free, clinically inspired CoT framework with confidence modulation can effectively overcome long-context dependency and data scarcity challenges in ADD, achieving superior performance without extra training and demonstrating strong generalizability across diverse foundation LLMs.

Abstract: Automatic Depression Detection (ADD) from clinical interviews is a pivotal task in computational mental health, yet it remains challenging due to two critical obstacles: 1) difficulty in modeling complex but sparsely distributed depression clues within lengthy, multi-topic clinical interviews, leading to superficial and unreliable reasoning; 2) scarcity of labeled data due to clinical privacy, together with high cost of training and fine-tuning, limiting the deployment of supervised ADD systems. To jointly address these challenges, we propose Dep-LLM, a training-free framework that mirrors the step-by-step reasoning of clinical psychiatrists and operates entirely on frozen off-the-shelf foundation LLMs. Dep-LLM comprises three stages. First, a Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Depression Multi-factor Analysis module structurally decomposes the long dialogue into five clinically aligned themes and produces evidence-grounded rationales, effectively handling long-context dependencies. Second, we introduce Confidence Analysis and Modulation module that quantifies the epistemic reliability from token-level entropy of each rationale and applies an intra-label and inter-theme modulation that amplifies trustworthy signals while suppressing uncertain ones without extra training. Third, a Collaborative Multi-factor Prediction module dynamically integrates multi-factor signals weighted by confidence into the final diagnosis. Extensive experiments on the DAIC-WOZ and E-DAIC datasets demonstrate the effectiveness and generalizability of Dep-LLM: it surpasses zero-shot baseline on nearly all 21 foundation LLMs across 9 metrics such as accuracy, macro F1 and weighted-average F1, and further outperforms state-of-the-art supervised domain-specific LLMs as well as the latest closed-source commercial LLMs, while requiring no extra training.

</details>


### [47] [Beyond APIs: Probing the Limits of MLLMs in Physical Tool Use](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10803)
*Zhixin Ma,Yutong Zhou,Yongqi Li,Chong-Wah Ngo,Wenjie Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces PhysTool-Bench, a benchmark with 2,510 queries over 2,678 physical tools across domains (manufacturing, electrical, agriculture, healthcare) to evaluate MLLMs on tool recognition and task planning.
