The best model (Gemini-3.1-Pro) identifies only 58.7% of tools and completes 21.0% of queries end-to-end, revealing a two-level deficit in perception and functional commonsense reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite MLLMs increasingly serving as the "brain" of embodied AI, their proficiency in physical tool use remains unexplored. Existing benchmarks focus on digital API use, leaving a critical gap in evaluating whether MLLMs can perceive, identify, and plan with physical tools in real-world embodied settings.

Method: The authors construct PhysTool-Bench, a benchmark with 2,510 queries spanning 2,678 real-world physical tools across diverse domains. Evaluation proceeds along two dimensions: (1) recognizing all physical tools present in a visual scene, and (2) planning the correct tool selection and sequential use based on instructions and visual context. Thirteen leading MLLMs are benchmarked.

Result: Even the strongest model, Gemini-3.1-Pro, identifies only 58.7% of tools in a scene and completes merely 21.0% of queries end-to-end. The analysis reveals a two-level deficit: MLLMs struggle with perceptual grounding in realistic scenes, and suffer an even larger performance drop at the planning stage, indicating a lack of functional commonsense for mapping perceived tools onto task semantics.

Conclusion: The results pinpoint a critical bottleneck for practical embodied AI: MLLMs lack both robust tool perception and the functional commonsense needed for task-oriented tool planning. PhysTool-Bench provides a foundation for future research aimed at bridging this perception-to-planning gap in physical tool use.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) excel at utilizing digital APIs and increasingly serve as the "brain" of embodied AI, instructing robots to interact with the physical world. In such embodied settings, a central capability is the use of physical tools, which underpins MLLMs' ability to assist humans in real-world tasks. Despite the importance, MLLMs' proficiency in physical tool use remains largely unexplored. To address this gap, we introduce PhysTool-Bench, the first physical tool-use benchmark designed to evaluate MLLMs' ability to comprehend real-world scenarios, identify physical tools, and plan their use. PhysTool-Bench comprises 2,510 queries over 2,678 real-world physical tools spanning diverse domains, including manufacturing, electrical work, agriculture, and healthcare. Concretely, models are evaluated along two primary dimensions: 1) recognizing all physical tools present in the scene, and 2) planning the tool selection and use sequence based on the instruction and visual context. Across 13 leading MLLMs, even the strongest model (Gemini-3.1-Pro) identifies only 58.7% of tools in a scene and completes merely 21.0% of queries end-to-end. Our analysis reveals a two-level deficit: MLLMs struggle to perceive tools in realistic scenes, and the much larger drop at the planning stage further indicates a lack of functional commonsense for mapping perceived tools onto task semantics, pinpointing a critical bottleneck for the development of practical embodied AI.

</details>


### [48] [Attention-Discounted Adaptive Sampler for Masked Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10829)
*Yusuf Sahin,Ahmed Rockey Saikia,Volkan Cevher,Paolo Favaro*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ADAS is a training-free reranking method for parallel masked diffusion decoding that uses soft attention-based penalty to discount candidates whose predictions are coupled with uncertain already-selected tokens, improving low-NFE performance by ~9-11 percentage points with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing parallel samplers for masked diffusion language models (Top-k, Fast-dLLM, EB-Sampler) select tokens based on individual confidence scores, ignoring interdependencies among jointly revealed tokens. This causes fragile parallelism where individually confident tokens become unsafe when committed together due to coupled predictions.

Method: ADAS is a training-free reranking rule that modifies only the subset construction step of existing samplers while leaving stopping rules unchanged. It employs a greedy approach: when building the set of tokens to reveal, it discounts a candidate token's score if that token attends strongly (via attention weights) to already-selected positions whose predictions remain uncertain. This uses continuous attention values as a soft marginal penalty rather than hard compatibility constraints.

Result: Plugging ADAS into Top-k, Fast-dLLM, and EB-Sampler on LLaDA-8B-Base and Dream-7B-Base across GSM8K, MATH500, HumanEval, and MBPP yields average improvements of 9.11 and 10.46 percentage points at matched denoiser evaluations, with only 3.1% per-forward runtime overhead.

Conclusion: Soft attention-discounted reranking is a simple, modular, and effective mechanism to improve quality in highly parallel decoding for masked diffusion language models without requiring additional training or architectural changes.

Abstract: Masked diffusion language models can reduce inference steps by revealing multiple tokens per denoising iteration, but this parallelism is fragile: positions that are individually confident may be unsafe to commit together when their predictions are coupled. Existing training-free samplers such as Top-\(k\), Fast-dLLM, and EB-Sampler mainly control how many tokens to reveal, while often ranking candidates by token-wise scores that ignore interactions within the selected set. We propose ADAS, a training-free reranking rule for parallel masked diffusion decoding. ADAS leaves the base sampler's stopping rule unchanged and modifies only subset construction: it greedily discounts a candidate when it attends strongly to already selected positions whose predictions remain uncertain. Unlike graph-constrained methods that turn attention into hard compatibility constraints, ADAS keeps attention continuous and uses it as a soft marginal penalty. Across LLaDA-8B-Base and Dream-7B-Base on GSM8K, MATH500, HumanEval, and MBPP, plugging ADAS into Top-\(k\), Fast-dLLM, and EB-Sampler improves low-NFE performance at matched denoiser evaluations by \(9.11\) and \(10.46\) percentage points on average, respectively, with \(3.1\%\) per-forward runtime overhead. These results show that soft attention-discounted reranking is a simple and modular way to improve quality in highly parallel decoding for masked diffusion language models.

</details>


### [49] [Janus: A Benchmark for Goal-Conditioned Information Distortion in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10852)
*Polydoros Giannouris,Mohsinul Kabir,Sophia Ananiadou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: JANUS is a benchmark that evaluates how LLMs produce pragmatically distorted yet factually grounded outputs when conditioned on goals like increasing adoption or approval, revealing consistent goal-conditioned misleading behavior across 12 models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM deception benchmarks focus on explicit falsehoods (lies, fabrication, concealment) but miss a subtler and more dangerous failure mode: selective treatment of true facts—omitting adverse evidence, softening unfavorable details, emphasizing favorable information, or using vague qualifiers. Real-world misleading communication often relies on these pragmatic distortions rather than outright false statements.

Method: The authors introduce JANUS, a benchmark with 160 scenarios across 8 domains. Each scenario provides a fixed pool of favorable and adverse facts and compares a neutral condition against a goal-directed condition (e.g., increasing adoption, enrollment, approval, or support despite potential harm). By constraining all outputs to the same fact pool, JANUS isolates misleading net impressions from hallucination and fabrication. Scenarios are paired with neutral and goal-conditioned prompts along with annotated material facts.

Result: Extensive experiments across 12 LLMs reveal consistent goal-conditioned distortions. Models remain sensitive to incentive and framing objectives, producing selectively misleading communications even when grounded in the same fact pool, and lack robust safeguards against this form of pragmatic distortion.

Conclusion: Current LLMs are vulnerable to goal-conditioned pragmatic distortion—a failure mode distinct from hallucination or explicit lying. The publicly released JANUS corpus and code provide a foundation for future research on detecting and mitigating this subtle but dangerous form of misleading communication.

Abstract: LLM deception is often evaluated through direct markers such as fabricated claims, explicit lies, or strategic concealment. However, many real-world misleading communications do not depend on false statements, rather, they arise from selective treatment of true material facts: omitting adverse evidence, softening unfavorable details, emphasizing favorable details, or replacing precise qualifications with vague language. Existing benchmarks largely miss this subtler and arguably more dangerous failure mode. We introduce JANUS, a benchmark for measuring goal-conditioned pragmatic distortion in fact-grounded LLM outputs. Each scenario in our benchmark provides a fixed pool of favorable and adverse facts and compares a neutral condition against a goal-directed condition, such as increasing adoption, enrollment, approval, or support, despite potential harm to directly affected individuals or groups. Because all outputs are constrained to use the same fact pool, JANUS isolates misleading net impressions from hallucination and fabrication. JANUS contains 160 scenarios across 8 domains, with each scenario paired with neutral and goal-conditioned prompts and annotated material facts. Extensive experiments across 12 LLMs reveal consistent goal-conditioned distortions, demonstrating that current models remain sensitive to incentive and framing objectives and lack robust safeguards against selectively misleading communication. We publicly release our corpus and code for future research.

</details>


### [50] [Pushing the Limits of LLM Tool Calling via Experiential Knowledge Integration and Activation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10875)
*Yupu Hao,Zhuoran Jin,Huanxuan Liao,Kang Liu,Jun Zhao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper presents a systematic study of how experiential knowledge influences LLM tool-use across three stages—acquisition, activation, and internalization—and proposes KATE, a framework that integrates instance-level knowledge with reasoning-width expansion and knowledge-aware training to improve multi-step tool execution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs frequently fail at multi-step tool execution due to insufficient tool-related knowledge and ineffective activation of existing knowledge. The authors aim to systematically understand how knowledge impacts tool-use performance and develop a principled framework to address these shortcomings.

Method: The study is structured around three stages: (1) Knowledge Acquisition — comparing instance-level experiential knowledge (specific tool-use examples) against abstract intent-level knowledge, evaluated via retrieval-based augmentation; (2) Knowledge Activation — contrasting depth-expansion (longer reasoning chains) with width-expansion (parallel sampling with aggregation, e.g., majority voting) for activating latent knowledge at inference; (3) Knowledge Internalization — comparing supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL) for post-training on knowledge-augmented data. The resulting KATE framework combines instance-level knowledge, width-expanded inference, and RL-based internalization.

Result: Simple instance-level knowledge yields strong and reliable gains, while abstract intent-level knowledge provides limited benefits. Expanding reasoning depth produces diminishing returns, whereas width expansion via parallel sampling with aggregation more effectively activates latent knowledge. For internalization, RL outperforms SFT. KATE achieves consistent and substantial improvements over strong baselines on BFCL-V3 and AppWorld across model scales.

Conclusion: The authors conclude that effective tool use in LLMs depends critically on acquiring granular instance-level experiential knowledge, activating it through reasoning-width expansion rather than depth, and internalizing it via RL-based post-training. KATE operationalizes these findings into a unified framework that significantly advances autonomous agent capabilities.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) rely on tool use to act as autonomous agents, yet often fail in multi-step execution due to insufficient tool-related knowledge and ineffective knowledge activation. Therefore, we present a systematic study on how knowledge influences tool-use performance, covering the stages of knowledge acquisition, activation, and internalization. In the knowledge acquisition stage, we acquire and evaluate various forms of experiential knowledge, and our analysis shows that simple instance-level knowledge can already provide strong and reliable gains, while abstract intent-level knowledge offers limited benefits. At inference time, to activate knowledge, we find that prompting LLM to expand the depth of reasoning yields diminishing returns, whereas expanding the width of reasoning by parallel sampling with aggregation more effectively activates latent experiential knowledge. At training time, for knowledge internalization, post-training with knowledge-augmented data further improves performance, with reinforcement learning outperforming supervised fine-tuning. Based on these insights, we propose the Knowledge-Augmented Tool Execution (KATE), a knowledge-augmented tool execution framework that integrates experiential knowledge with reasoning-width-expanded inference and knowledge-aware training. Experiments on BFCL-V3 and AppWorld demonstrate consistent and substantial improvements over strong baselines across model scales. Our Code is available at https://github.com/hypasd-art/KATE.

</details>


### [51] [Trace Only What You Need: Structure-Aware On-Demand Hypergraph Memory for Long-Document Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10921)
*Xiangjun Zai,Xingyu Tan,Chen Chen,Xiaoyang Wang,Wenjie Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: DocTrace is a multi-agent RAG framework for long-document QA that introduces query-triggered knowledge organization, document-structure-aware reasoning via a lightweight structural tree index, and experience-guided reasoning using graph-structured memory, achieving state-of-the-art performance with significantly lower computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing structured RAG methods for long-document QA suffer from three key limitations: (1) costly query-agnostic knowledge organization done upfront regardless of the query, (2) insufficient use of the original document structure (hierarchy, sections, event order), and (3) inability to reuse historical reasoning experience across related questions. These gaps motivate the need for a more adaptive, structure-aware, and experience-driven approach.

Method: DocTrace employs a multi-agent RAG framework with three core components: (1) a lightweight document structural tree index that preserves document hierarchy, (2) an agent-shared hypergraph-structured working memory constructed on demand during reasoning (query-triggered), and (3) a graph-structured experience memory that stores successful reasoning plans for future reuse, enabling adaptive exploration across related long-document questions.

Result: DocTrace achieves the best performance on three out of four long-document QA datasets, surpassing the strongest baseline (ComoRAG) by up to 8.85% in F1 and 4.40% in Exact Match (EM), while simultaneously reducing overall computational cost by 53.32%.

Conclusion: DocTrace demonstrates that query-triggered knowledge organization, combined with document-structure-aware and experience-guided multi-agent reasoning, yields both superior accuracy and efficiency in long-document QA, validating the framework's design principles.

Abstract: Long-document question answering (QA) requires large language models (LLMs) to reason over evidence scattered across lengthy documents, where answers often depend on event order, section-level context, and cross-part evidence connections. Although retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) reduces the input context by retrieving relevant evidence, existing structured RAG methods still face three limitations: costly query-agnostic knowledge organization, insufficient use of original document structure, and no reuse of historical reasoning experience. To address these limitations, we propose DocTrace, a multi-agent RAG framework for long-document QA that supports query-triggered knowledge organization, document-structure-aware and experience-guided reasoning. DocTrace preserves document hierarchy with a lightweight document structural tree index, constructs agent-shared hypergraph-structured working memory on demand during reasoning, and stores successful reasoning plans in graph-structured experience memory for future reuse, enabling adaptive exploration across related long-document questions. Experiments on four long-document QA datasets show that DocTrace achieves the best performance on three datasets, surpassing the strongest baseline, ComoRAG, by up to 8.85% in F1 and 4.40% in EM, while reducing the overall computational cost by 53.32%

</details>


### [52] [It Takes One to Bias Them All: Breaking Bad with One-Shot GRPO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10931)
*Naihao Deng,Yilun Zhu,Naichen Shi,Clayton Scott,Rada Mihalcea*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A single biased training example with GRPO is sufficient to break LLM alignment, inducing systematic stereotype-driven reasoning that generalizes broadly. Susceptibility varies with the model's initial likelihood of producing biased outputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate the fragility of post-training alignment in LLMs by testing whether guardrails can be easily subverted through minimal targeted intervention — specifically, whether a single biased example under GRPO can induce widespread, generalizable bias.

Method: The authors apply one-shot Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) training on a single biased example to aligned LLMs. They then evaluate the resulting models for systematic bias across multiple attributes, categories, and benchmarks to assess generalization. They also analyze the relationship between initial likelihood of biased outputs and susceptibility to the attack.

Result: One-shot GRPO on a single biased example suffices to break alignment and induce systematic bias. The induced stereotype-driven reasoning generalizes across attributes, categories, and benchmarks. Model susceptibility correlates with the initial likelihood of producing biased outputs.

Conclusion: Post-training alignment in LLMs exhibits a critical vulnerability: guardrails can be overridden by a single biased example during GRPO fine-tuning, revealing that current safety measures are surprisingly brittle.

Abstract: Warning: This paper contains several toxic and offensive statements.
  Modern large language models (LLMs) are typically aligned through large-scale post-training to ensure fair and reliable behavior. In this work, we investigate how easily such guardrails can be broken by Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). We show that one-shot GRPO training on a single biased example is sufficient to induce systematic bias, with stereotype-driven reasoning generalizing across attributes, categories, and benchmarks. We further find that models differ in their susceptibility based on the initial likelihood of producing biased outputs. Our results reveal a critical vulnerability in post-training: alignment can be overridden by a single example.

</details>


### [53] [Density Field State Space Models: 1-Bit Distillation, Efficient Inference, and Knowledge Organization in Mamba-2](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10932)
*Chirag Shinde*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: DF-SSM compresses Mamba-2 SSMs to 1-bit with int8 low-rank correction, achieving 9.7× size reduction and 21.4× GPU inference speedup while maintaining performance within 2-4 points of a 1.58-bit baseline. The authors also discover three distinct processing phases in the compressed model's internal representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: State Space Models (SSMs) like Mamba-2 are powerful but large and expensive to deploy. Existing compression methods either degrade quality too much or require expensive training from scratch. The authors seek an efficient, post-training compression pipeline that preserves downstream performance while enabling fast GPU/CPU inference.

Method: DF-SSM uses a 1-bit binarized scaffold with an int8 low-rank correction factor applied to Mamba-2's weights, combined with knowledge distillation using only 32M tokens. An optimized inference pipeline leverages cuBLAS INT8 tensor cores for scaffold matrix multiplication, custom CUDA kernels for stateful SSM and convolution operations, and an AVX-512 CPU backend for cross-platform deployment.

Result: For Mamba-2 1.3B, DF-SSM produces a 278 MB model (9.7× smaller than FP16) with 21.4× faster GPU inference (batch=1). Downstream task performance stays within 2-4 percentage points of BitMamba-2 (a 1.58-bit model trained from scratch on 150B tokens). Distillation takes only 6 hours on one A100. The authors identify three internal processing phases: intent classification (layers 0-3, abstract, no vocabulary alignment), knowledge retrieval (layers 25-35, factual associations in a 5-layer window), and output formatting (layers 36-47, category structure dissolves). Analysis of 445 factual prompts shows early classification is syntactic (template-driven) not semantic, and representational structure is well-organized despite weak factual recall.

Conclusion: Extreme compression of SSMs to ~1-bit with low-rank correction is feasible and practical, enabling dramatic speedups with minimal quality loss. The phase-based analysis of internal representations reveals that knowledge organization emerges structurally before factual recall strength, suggesting representational structure may precede factual competence in these models.

Abstract: We present Density Field State Space Models (DF-SSM), a framework for compressing SSMs to a 1-bit scaffold with int8 low-rank correction. Applied to Mamba-2 1.3B, we achieve a 278 MB model (9.7x smaller than the 2.7 GB FP16 teacher) that runs at 21.4x faster inference on GPU (batch=1, relative to the mamba-ssm reference implementation) while maintaining downstream task performance within 2-4 percentage points of BitMamba-2, a 1.58-bit model trained from scratch on 150B tokens. The distillation itself requires only 32M tokens and 6 hours on a single A100 GPU, though it presupposes a pretrained FP16 teacher. We develop an optimized inference pipeline combining cuBLAS INT8 tensor cores for the scaffold matmul, custom CUDA kernels for stateful SSM and convolution operations, and an AVX-512 CPU backend for efficient deployment on both GPU and CPU. Beyond compression, we investigate the internal knowledge organization of the resulting model, discovering three distinct processing phases: intent classification (layers 0-3, operating in an abstract space with no vocabulary alignment), knowledge retrieval (layers 25-35, where factual associations localize to a 5-layer window), and output formatting (layers 36-47, where category structure dissolves). Through systematic analysis of 445 factual prompts across 19 categories, we find that early-layer classification is syntactic (driven by template structure) rather than semantic, and that the model exhibits well-organized knowledge representations despite weak factual recall--suggesting that representational structure may precede factual strength.

</details>


### [54] [Who Brought Easter Eggs to Eid? Auditing Cultural Translation of Math Word Problems Across Diverse Languages and Regions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11009)
*Parisa Suchdev,Juniper Lovato*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Analysis of 6,489 entity transformations across three LLMs adapting math word problems into seven languages reveals low inter-model agreement (33.5% on specific substitutions), entropy collapse compressing cultural diversity, and systematic biases including surface-marker prioritization and regional misattribution. Corpus-level failures remain hidden beneath surface plausibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly used to adapt math word problems for personalized learning at scale, but it is unknown whether adaptations are consistent across models, preserve cultural diversity, or reveal which cultural entities models treat as salient.

Method: Three LLMs (Claude Opus 4, GPT-4.1, Gemini 2.5 Pro) adapted 60 English math word problems into seven languages spanning high-resource (Italian, Hindi) to under-studied (Sindhi, Sicilian, Punjabi) varieties. Researchers annotated 6,489 entity transformations across five coding categories: preserve, localize, generalize, omit, or change. Agreement was measured both on transformation type and specific substitutions. Entropy was used to quantify diversity collapse.

Result: Models agreed on transformation type in only 62.5% of cases and on specific substitutions in 33.5%. All 21 language-model combinations exhibited entropy collapse, compressing rather than expanding cultural diversity. Models prioritized surface markers (names, foods, currencies) while preserving deeper structural features like grade-level systems. Regional misattribution occurred (e.g., Bangladeshi taka for Indian Bengali) and cross-cultural contamination emerged (e.g., Easter egg hunts recast as Eid activities). Deeper failures were only detectable through corpus-level analysis.

Conclusion: Surface plausibility of LLM-adapted math problems masks deeper, systematic failures—diversity collapse, regional misattribution, and cultural contamination—that escape detection in individual translations. Reliable deployment requires corpus-level evaluation, not single-instance inspection.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly used to adapt math word problems for personalized learning at scale, but it remains an open question whether those adaptations are consistent across models, preserve cultural diversity at scale, and reveal which cultural entities models treat as most salient. We analyze how Claude Opus 4, GPT-4.1, and Gemini 2.5 Pro adapt 60 English math word problems into Bengali, Hindi, Punjabi (India), Urdu, Sindhi (Pakistan), Italian, and Sicilian (Italy), a language set spanning the full resource spectrum, from high-resource Italian and Hindi to under-studied Sindhi, Sicilian, and Punjabi. We annotate 6,489 entity transformations, coding whether models preserve, localize, generalize, omit, or change entities such as names, foods, and places. Models agree on transformation type in 62.5% of cases and on specific substitutions in only 33.5%, meaning model choice directly shapes which cultural world students encounter. All 21 language-model combinations show entropy collapse, with adaptation compressing rather than expanding cultural diversity. Models prioritize surface markers such as names, foods, and currencies while preserving deeper structural features such as grade-level systems that embed culturally specific assumptions. Despite prompts specifying target countries, models misattribute regional context by using Bangladeshi taka for Indian Bengali students and produce cross-cultural contamination, such as adapting egg hunts as Eid activities. Some failures are visible in individual translations. Others, including diversity collapse, systematic preference for surface markers, and consistent regional misattribution, emerge only through corpus-level analysis. The surface plausibility that makes adapted problems look correct is precisely what makes deeper failures easy to overlook.

</details>


### [55] [Measuring Human Value Expression in Social Media Texts: Calibrated LLM Annotation and Encoder Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11018)
*Maria Milkova,Maksim Rudnev*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper investigates how LLMs can be used to annotate non-English social media text for Schwartz's basic human values. It examines prompt engineering, language effects, and structural alignment with expert annotations, then demonstrates that LLM annotations can be transferred to encoder models via soft-label training while preserving uncertainty information.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Measuring subjective constructs like human values in social media text requires theoretically grounded annotation that scales. Manual expert annotation is costly and slow. The paper seeks to determine whether LLMs can operationalize theory-based value definitions reliably across languages, and whether LLM-generated annotations can be distilled into efficient encoder models without losing theoretical fidelity or uncertainty information.

Method: The authors annotate non-English social media posts using Schwartz's theory of basic human values as the theoretical framework. They systematically compare different LLMs, prompt designs, and instruction languages. Evaluation goes beyond standard metrics (precision, recall, F1) to include structural alignment of value structures, error structure analysis, confidence-ambiguity relationships, and annotation stability. They employ iterative prompt calibration guided by error analysis to reduce misattributions, derive expert verification rules from recurrent errors, and transfer LLM annotations to an encoder model using soft-label training.

Result: Different LLMs produce divergent value interpretations from the same texts. Iterative prompt calibration through error analysis successfully reduces misattributions and improves alignment with expert annotations. Recurrent error structures yield targeted expert verification rules useful during corpus annotation. Soft-label training successfully transfers LLM annotations to an encoder model, preserving both theory-based value interpretations and uncertainty information.

Conclusion: Theory-based value definitions can constrain LLM interpretations and reduce spurious attributions. LLM-based annotation, when carefully calibrated, can serve as a scalable alternative for measuring subjective constructs in social media text, and the resulting annotations can be efficiently transferred to encoder models without loss of theoretical grounding or uncertainty representation.

Abstract: Measuring subjective constructs in naturally occurring social media text requires annotation procedures that are theoretically grounded, empirically validated, and transferable to an encoder model for scalable prediction. Using non-English social media posts annotated according to Schwartz's theory of basic human values, we investigate how different LLMs, prompts, and instruction languages operationalize the expression of values in text. We argue that although texts may permit multiple plausible interpretations, theory-based value definitions can constrain interpretations and reduce spurious value attributions. Beyond precision, recall, and F1, we evaluate structural alignment between values, error structure, confidence-ambiguity relations, and annotation stability. We show that different LLMs produce different value interpretations. Iterative prompt calibration through error analysis reduces misattributions and improves alignment with expert annotations. We also derive targeted expert verification rules from recurrent error structures and use them during corpus annotation. Finally, we show that LLM annotations can be transferred to an encoder model through soft-label training, retaining theory-based value interpretations and information about uncertainty in value expression.

</details>


### [56] [Does Reasoning Preserve Alignment? On the Trustworthiness of Large Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11046)
*Prajakta Kini,Avinash Reddy,Souradip Chakraborty,Satya Sai Srinath Namburi GNVV,Furong Huang,Amrit Singh Bedi,Alvaro Velasquez*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Converting instruction-tuned LLMs into reasoning models via post-training often improves reasoning accuracy but introduces alignment regressions across safety, toxicity, stereotyping, ethics, privacy, and robustness dimensions. The authors audit these trustworthiness failures and argue that such metrics must be reported alongside reasoning gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-training methods for reasoning (SFT, RL, distillation) are optimized for accuracy without explicitly preserving the alignment properties (safe refusal, bias avoidance, privacy) of the original instruction-tuned model. The authors investigate whether this conversion inadvertently degrades trustworthiness.

Method: A systematic trustworthiness audit comparing reasoning models (produced via supervised fine-tuning, RL-based post-training, and distillation) against matched instruction-tuned baselines. Evaluation spans six dimensions: safety, toxicity, stereotyping/bias, machine ethics, privacy, and out-of-distribution robustness. Behavioral drift is quantified via KL divergence from the instruction-tuned baseline.

Result: Reasoning models exhibit alignment regressions: increased toxicity, amplified stereotyping, miscalibrated refusal, and contextual privacy leakage. These regressions correlate with behavioral drift measured by KL divergence from the instruction-tuned baseline, indicating the conversion is not behavior-preserving by default.

Conclusion: Trustworthiness metrics are essential for evaluating reasoning models and should be reported alongside reasoning capability gains. The conversion from instruction-tuned to reasoning models requires explicit mechanisms to preserve alignment.

Abstract: Instruction-tuned LLMs are increasingly converted into reasoning models through post-training to improve multi-step task performance. This conversion is usually optimized for reasoning accuracy, without explicitly preserving the alignment behavior of the instruction-tuned model, such as safe refusal, bias avoidance, and privacy protection. We ask: does this conversion preserve alignment? We study this question through a trustworthiness audit and find that it is not behavior-preserving by default. For a systematic analysis, we compare reasoning models produced via supervised fine-tuning, RL-based post-training, and distillation against matched instruction-tuned baselines across six trustworthiness dimensions: safety, toxicity, stereotyping and bias, machine ethics, privacy, and out-of-distribution robustness. We observe that reasoning models often improve on reasoning benchmarks but exhibit alignment regressions, including increased toxicity, amplified stereotyping, miscalibrated refusal, and contextual privacy leakage. These regressions are consistent with behavioral drift from the instruction-tuned baseline, measured by KL divergence. Overall, our results point to the broader conclusion that trustworthiness metrics are essential for evaluating reasoning models and should be reported alongside gains in reasoning capability.

</details>


### [57] [Attention Amnesia in Hybrid LLMs: When CoT Fine-Tuning Breaks Long-Range Recall, and How to Fix It](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11052)
*Xinyu Zhou,Boyu Zhu,Yi Xu,Zhiwei Li,Yingfa Chen,Huiming Wang,Zhijiang Guo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: CoT-SFT degrades long-context recall in hybrid linear-attention models by biasing attention toward short-range patterns. The authors propose QK-Restore, a training-free method that reverts query-key projections to pre-SFT weights, successfully restoring long-context retrieval while preserving reasoning gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While CoT supervised fine-tuning is widely used to enhance reasoning, the authors discovered it systematically damages long-context retrieval capabilities in hybrid linear-attention models. This degradation worsens with harder retrieval settings and longer contexts, motivating the need for a fix that preserves both reasoning and retrieval abilities.

Method: The authors propose QK-Restore, a training-free method that restores only the query and key projection matrices (W_Q, W_K) from the pre-SFT checkpoint while keeping all other parameters at their post-SFT state. A Procrustes variant is introduced to better balance routing preservation with reasoning adaptation by aligning the restored projections via orthogonal transformation.

Result: QK-Restore consistently recovers long-context retrieval performance at zero training cost across architectures. On HypeNet-5B, S3@256K improves from 65.4% to 76.4%. The degradation pattern is stark: HypeNet-9B drops from 67.2% to 9.4% on NIAH-S2@256K after CoT-SFT, and QK-Restore substantially reverses this collapse.

Conclusion: CoT-SFT introduces a harmful attention bias toward short-range patterns by disrupting W_Q and W_K projections responsible for long-range routing. QK-Restore offers a simple, zero-cost solution to recover long-context capabilities without sacrificing reasoning performance, highlighting a critical tension between reasoning fine-tuning and retrieval in linear-attention architectures.

Abstract: Chain-of-thought (CoT) supervised fine-tuning (SFT) is widely adopted to improve reasoning ability, yet we find that it systematically degrades long-context recall in hybrid linear-attention models. Across architectures including HypeNet and Jet-Nemotron, retrieval performance on Needle-In-A-Haystack (NIAH) deteriorates substantially after CoT-SFT, and the degradation becomes more severe under harder retrieval settings and longer context windows. For example, HypeNet-9B on NIAH-S2@256K decreases from $67.2\%$ to $9.4\%$. We attribute this to CoT-SFT biasing attention gradients toward short-range patterns, disrupting query-key projections ($W_Q, W_K$) that are responsible for long-range routing. Motivated by this observation, we propose QK-Restore, a training-free method that restores only $W_Q$ and $W_K$ from the pre-SFT checkpoint while preserving all other post-SFT parameters. We further introduce a Procrustes variant to balance routing preservation and reasoning adaptation. Across architectures, QK-Restore consistently restores long-context capability at zero training cost while preserving reasoning performance; for instance, on HypeNet-5B it improves S3@256K from $65.4\%$ to $76.4\%$ while maintaining strong reasoning performance.

</details>


### [58] [T1-Bench: Benchmarking Multi-Scenario Agents in Real-World Domains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11070)
*Genta Indra Winata,Amartya Chakraborty,Yuzhen Lin,Swasthi P Rao,Shikhhar Siingh,Houhan Lu,Nadia Bathaee,Sriharsha Hatwar,Paresh Dashore,Anmol Jain,Kshitij Tayal,Xiuzhu Lin,Anirban Das,Sambit Sahu,Shi-Xiong Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: T1-Bench introduces a high-fidelity benchmark for evaluating LLM-based agents across 25 domains in realistic, multi-turn, multi-domain customer-facing scenarios. Evaluated on 12 models, it advances prior benchmarks through increased task complexity, interaction depth, and domain diversity, complemented by human evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing agent benchmarks suffer from limited task complexity, insufficient realism, narrow domain diversity, and failure to capture cross-domain interactions. They inadequately assess agents in realistic multi-step settings requiring sustained reasoning and coordination across multiple domains.

Method: The authors construct T1-Bench, a benchmark featuring interleaved multi-domain scenarios with multi-turn user-assistant interactions across 25 domains of varying difficulty. Evaluation uses both automatic metrics and human judgments, tested on 12 proprietary and open-weight LLMs to assess agent behavior, tool utilization, and conversational quality.

Result: T1-Bench substantially advances prior benchmarks by increasing task complexity, interaction depth, and domain coverage in simulated multi-domain environments. Both automatic and human evaluations are employed, with full data and code to be open-sourced.

Conclusion: T1-Bench provides a more rigorous, realistic, and reproducible framework for evaluating agentic LLM systems, highlighting the need for sustained reasoning and cross-domain coordination. The public release of data and evaluation code aims to facilitate future research.

Abstract: Recent advances in reasoning and tool-calling capabilities of large language models (LLMs) have enabled increasingly capable agentic systems. However, existing benchmarks remain limited in task complexity, realism, and domain diversity, and often fail to capture interactions that span multiple domains, limiting their ability to evaluate agents in realistic multi-step settings that require sustained reasoning and coordination. To address these limitations, we introduce T1-Bench, a high-fidelity, comprehensive benchmark for evaluating agentic systems in realistic customer-facing, multi-domain environments, featuring interleaved scenarios that require structured reasoning across multi-turn user-assistant interactions and substantially increasing both compositional complexity and evaluative rigor across 25 domains of varying difficulty. We evaluate T1-Bench using 12 proprietary and open-weight models, providing a reproducible and standardized framework for assessing agent behavior, tool utilization, and conversational quality in complex, multi-step environments. We further complement automatic evaluation with human judgments to strengthen the assessment of qualitative performance. Overall, T1-Bench substantially advances prior benchmarks by increasing task complexity, interaction depth, and domain coverage in simulated multi-domain environments. To facilitate future research on agentic systems, we will publicly release data and evaluation code as open source.

</details>


### [59] [Modeling Complex Behaviors: Multi-Personality Composition and Dynamic Switching in Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11074)
*Peiqi Jia,Haonan Jia,Ziqi Miao,Linkang Du,Yuntao Wang,Zhou Su*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper investigates explicit personality conditioning in MLLMs, establishing an evaluation framework for single/multi-personality induction and switching. Personality induction boosts captioning but degrades VQA reasoning. It reveals co-modulation effects from prior and current personality constraints, showing that prompt-based methods transfer poorly to multimodal settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs are increasingly deployed in social contexts, yet understanding how they behave under complex personality conditions remains underexplored. There is a need for systematic methods to induce, compose, and switch personalities, and to evaluate the downstream effects on multimodal task performance.

Method: The authors introduce explicit personality conditioning and design a systematic evaluation framework with three paradigms: (1) single-personality induction, (2) multi-personality induction (trait composition), and (3) personality switching. Experiments apply prompt-based personality induction to MLLMs and assess performance on image captioning and visual question answering (VQA).

Result: Personality induction improves image captioning but degrades VQA performance requiring precise reasoning. Multi-trait composition reveals balancing effects, while dynamic switching shows residual effects where prior personality constraints continue to influence behavior alongside current constraints. Existing prompt-based methods exhibit limited transferability from text-only to multimodal settings.

Conclusion: Personality modeling in MLLMs is dynamic and complex, with task-dependent benefits and trade-offs. The findings highlight the need for robust, MLLM-tailored personality induction and evaluation methods rather than naive adaptation of text-only approaches.

Abstract: With the widespread deployment of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) in social interaction, understanding and controlling their behavior under complex personality conditions is essential. This paper introduces explicit personality conditioning and establishes a systematic evaluation framework encompassing single-personality induction, multi-personality induction, and personality switching. Experiments show that personality induction improves image captioning performance but can impair performance on tasks requiring precise reasoning, such as visual question answering (VQA). Balancing and residual effects are observed during multi-trait composition and dynamic switching, indicating that model behavior is co-modulated by both previous and current personality constraints. Existing prompt-based personality induction methods show limited transferability to multimodal settings. Our work reveals the dynamic and complex nature of personality modeling in MLLMs and underscores the need for robust, tailored methods for personality induction and evaluation. The code will be released when the paper is accepted.

</details>


### [60] [VISTA: A Versatile Interactive User Simulation Toolkit for Agent Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11079)
*Yunan Lu,Ryan Shea,Yusen Zhang,Zhou Yu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: VISTA is a toolkit for evaluating interactive agents via user simulation, addressing the lack of realism and coverage metrics with six evaluation metrics and a hybrid UI+API simulator, validated in e-commerce and education settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static benchmarks fail to capture dynamic, multi-step agent behavior and miss meaningful failure modes. Existing user-simulation frameworks lack metrics for simulation quality/comprehensiveness and are restricted to either UI-only or API-only actions, limiting realistic user behavior modeling.

Method: VISTA introduces: (1) a suite of six metrics measuring realism, capability coverage, and interaction effectiveness of simulated interactions; and (2) a hybrid user simulator integrating both UI-based and API-based interactions for comprehensive evaluation across diverse environments.

Result: Evaluation in e-commerce shopping and education customer service domains shows VISTA produces more realistic and comprehensive evaluations compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: VISTA advances interactive agent evaluation by combining hybrid simulation (UI+API) with a principled metric suite, yielding more thorough and realistic assessments than existing frameworks.

Abstract: Evaluation remains a critical bottleneck for interactive agent development. Existing evaluation methods often rely on static benchmarks, which fail to capture the dynamic, multi-step nature of agentic behavior and struggle to expose meaningful failure modes. While user-simulation-based evaluation offers a promising alternative, existing simulation frameworks suffer from two major limitations. First, they provide limited mechanisms for evaluating the quality and comprehensiveness of simulated interactions, making it difficult to assess whether a simulator sufficiently explores an agent's capabilities and failure modes. Second, most frameworks are restricted to either UI-only actions or API-only actions, limiting their ability to model the full range of realistic user behaviors. To address these limitations, we propose VISTA, a Versatile Interactive user Simulation Toolkit for Agent evaluation. Our toolkit includes a suite of six metrics for measuring the realism, capability coverage, and interaction effectiveness of simulated interactions. In addition, we develop a hybrid user simulator that integrates both UI-based interactions and API-based interactions, enabling more realistic and comprehensive evaluation across diverse interactive environments. We evaluate VISTA in e-commerce shopping and education customer service settings and demonstrate that it produces more realistic and comprehensive evaluations than existing methods.

</details>


### [61] [The Shibboleth Effect: Auditing the Cross-Lingual Distributional Skew of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11082)
*Hakan Mehmetcik*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A multi-agent wargame experiment reveals that language of play (English vs. Turkish) causes divergent behavioral shifts in frontier LLMs during a simulated maritime crisis—some models become more coercive, others less, and some show no effect—indicating that cross-lingual bias is architecture-dependent rather than universal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether frontier LLMs exhibit cross-lingual distributional skew (the Shibboleth Effect) under adversarial geopolitical conditions, and whether such skew is a universal property of Western-origin models or contingent on specific architectures and training regimes.

Method: A between-groups experiment using a synthetic maritime territorial dispute wargame (Cerulean Sea Crisis) with six frontier LLMs (GPT-4o, Llama-4, Mistral-Large, Gemini-3.1-Pro, Qwen3.6-Plus, DeepSeek-R1). The sole manipulation was language (English vs. Turkish), with N=10 games per arm, K=5 rounds per game, yielding 586 validated statements. A zero-shot classifier assessed Concession Rate and Coercive Rhetoric continuously, with Holm-corrected significance testing.

Result: Results were heterogeneous: Llama-4 showed a significant increase in coercive rhetoric in Turkish (delta=+0.800, p=.002); Gemini-3.1-Pro showed a large decrease (delta=-0.750, p=.005); DeepSeek-R1 similarly decreased (delta=-0.860, p=.006) with chain-of-thought evidence of a buffering mechanism; GPT-4o showed no significant effect (delta=+0.130, p=.614). Two buffering mechanisms were identified: chain-of-thought institutional anchoring and multilingual RLHF alignment.

Conclusion: Cross-lingual behavioral skew in LLMs is not a universal feature but depends on model architecture and training. The identification of distinct buffering mechanisms has practical implications for safely deploying LLMs in diplomatic and crisis-management contexts.

Abstract: This study investigates cross-lingual distributional skew (the Shibboleth Effect) in frontier large language models (LLMs) subjected to sustained adversarial conditions. We develop a multi-agent geopolitical wargame, the Cerulean Sea Crisis, a synthetic maritime territorial dispute designed to mirror the structural dynamics of Eastern Mediterranean conflicts. Six frontier models (GPT-4o, Llama-4, Mistral-Large, Gemini-3.1-Pro, Qwen3.6-Plus, and DeepSeek-R1) participate in a between-groups experiment (N = 10 games per arm, K = 5 rounds per game) in which the sole manipulation is the language of play (English versus Turkish), producing 586 validated statements. A zero-shot classifier assesses behavioral dispositions along two continuous dimensions: Concession Rate and Coercive Rhetoric. The results are heterogeneous. Llama-4 shows a substantial, Holm-corrected increase in coercive rhetoric under Turkish (delta = +0.800, p = .002), whereas Gemini-3.1-Pro displays an equally large decrease (delta = -0.750, p = .005). DeepSeek-R1 exhibits a similar negative shift (delta = -0.860, p = .006) and provides chain-of-thought evidence consistent with a buffering mechanism. GPT-4o shows no detectable effect (delta = +0.130, p = .614). These findings indicate that cross-lingual behavioral skew is contingent on model architecture and training regime rather than a universal property of Western-origin LLMs. We identify two distinct buffering mechanisms, chain-of-thought institutional anchoring and multilingual RLHF alignment, and discuss their implications for integrating LLMs safely into diplomatic and crisis-management settings.

</details>


### [62] [PhantomBench: Benchmarking the Non-existential Threat of Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11105)
*Haeji Jung,Hila Gonen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: PhantomBench introduces a large-scale benchmark of 60K+ non-existent terms to evaluate hallucination rates in language models, revealing alarming failure rates (up to 86.7%) even among frontier models when presented with fabricated concepts, and provides a pipeline for scalable benchmark generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hallucinations in LMs pose serious risks, especially in high-stakes domains where users blindly rely on model outputs. Despite progress in understanding hallucinations, it remains unclear how reliably models can recognize the limits of their own knowledge — i.e., abstain when probed on non-existent concepts. No prior large-scale benchmark systematically tests this capability across diverse domains and model types.

Method: The authors construct PhantomBench, a benchmark of 60K+ non-existent terms and entities derived from real concepts across diverse domains. They evaluate 21 models of varying types and sizes by presenting inputs that either presume or do not presume the existence of these fabricated terms. They further demonstrate PhantomBench's utility as a proxy for studying hallucination on rare concepts, and provide a pipeline for scalable, customizable generation of non-existent concepts.

Result: Staggering hallucination rates were observed across all model categories, with average rates reaching as high as 86.7%. Even frontier models failed to abstain on non-existent concepts, particularly when the input framing presumed their existence. PhantomBench also proved effective as a proxy for studying model behavior on rare concepts where hallucination is more prevalent.

Conclusion: Current language models, including frontier systems, exhibit severe deficiencies in recognizing the boundaries of their knowledge. PhantomBench provides a robust, scalable tool for benchmarking and probing this limitation. The pipeline enables researchers and practitioners to generate domain-specific non-existent concept benchmarks for targeted evaluation.

Abstract: Hallucinations, where language models (LMs) generate factually ungrounded responses, pose serious risks, as users tend to blindly rely on them. This is particularly concerning in high-stakes domains, where consequences of such model behavior can lead to significant harms. Despite notable progress in understanding hallucinations, it remains unclear how reliably these models can recognize the limits of their knowledge. We introduce PhantomBench, the first large-scale benchmark of its kind, comprising more than 60K non-existent terms and entities derived from real concepts across diverse domains. Using our benchmark, we evaluate a total of 21 models of various types and sizes. We show staggering hallucination rates across the board (with average rates as high as 86.7% in some cases), and note that even frontier models surprisingly fail to abstain on non-existent concepts, especially when the input presumes their existence. We then show that PhantomBench can serve as a proxy for studying model behavior on rare concepts for which models are more prone to hallucinate. We also provide a pipeline to construct PhantomBench, enabling scalable generation of non-existent concepts tailored to the specific needs of researchers and practitioners.

</details>


### [63] [Provenance-Grounded Gating and Adaptive Recovery in Synthetic Post-Training Data Curation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11127)
*Soham Bhattacharjee,Karun Sharma,Vinay Kumar Sankarapu,Pratinav Seth*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper examines two underexplored aspects of synthetic post-training pipelines: (1) whether filtering signals are grounded in source evidence (provenance), and (2) whether rejected samples can be systematically recovered. Using adversarially injected corpora for ground-truth labels, the authors find that source provenance improves faithfulness gating, hallucination and reward gates reject disjoint populations, and an adaptive recovery pipeline combining failure diagnosis with targeted regeneration outperforms naive resampling. Generator scale is the primary driver of downstream fine-tuning quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Synthetic post-training pipelines commonly use reward models or LLM judges to filter generated samples, but two critical questions remain unexamined: whether filtering is grounded in the source evidence that induced each generation, and whether rejected samples can be systematically recovered rather than permanently discarded. Addressing these gaps could improve both sample yield and fine-tuning quality.

Method: The authors conduct a controlled study using adversarially injected corpora to obtain ground-truth failure labels. They systematically compare: (1) gate configurations — hallucination gates, reward gates, and source-provenance-based faithfulness gates; (2) recovery strategies including naive resampling versus an adaptive pipeline that combines failure diagnosis (classifying why a sample was rejected) with targeted regeneration; and (3) multiple generator scales. Downstream fine-tuning quality is evaluated across these conditions.

Result: Key findings: (1) exact source provenance improves faithfulness gating, particularly for stronger judges; (2) hallucination and reward gates reject largely disjoint sample populations, indicating both are necessary; (3) the adaptive recovery pipeline achieves higher yield, recovery rate, and injection recall than naive resampling; (4) downstream fine-tuning quality is primarily driven by generator scale, with filtration and recovery conditions contributing meaningfully but secondarily.

Conclusion: Effective synthetic post-training pipelines should incorporate both hallucination and reward-based gates (since they filter different failure modes), ground filtering in source provenance for improved faithfulness, and adopt adaptive recovery strategies over naive resampling to maximize sample yield. However, generator scale remains the dominant factor in downstream model quality.

Abstract: Synthetic post-training pipelines commonly filter generated samples with reward models or holistic LLM judges, yet two practices remain rarely examined together: whether the filtering signal is grounded in the source evidence that induced each generation, and whether rejected samples can be systematically recovered rather than permanently discarded. We present a controlled study of both questions across gate configurations, recovery strategies, and generator scales, using adversarially injected corpora to provide ground-truth failure labels. We find that exact source provenance improves faithfulness gating for stronger judges, that hallucination and reward gates reject largely disjoint sample populations making both necessary, and that an adaptive recovery pipeline combining failure diagnosis with targeted regeneration achieves higher yield, recovery rate, and injection recall than naive resampling. Downstream fine-tuning quality is driven primarily by generator scale, with filtration and recovery conditions contributing meaningfully but secondarily.

</details>


### [64] [Multi-Faceted Interactivity Alignment in Full-Duplex Speech Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11167)
*Atsumoto Ohashi,Neil Zeghidour,Alexandre Défossez,Eugene Kharitonov*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a post-training alignment method using reinforcement learning to comprehensively improve interactivity in full-duplex spoken dialogue models across four axes: pause handling, turn-taking, backchanneling, and user interruption, using axis-specific reward functions and an LLM-based quality reward to prevent semantic degradation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current full-duplex spoken dialogue models are trained with supervised token-level likelihood maximization, which fails to optimize interaction-level behaviors. This leads to interactivity issues such as excessive silence and ill-timed turn-taking. While recent RL-based approaches exist, they address only limited interactive behaviors in their rewards.

Method: The authors propose RL-based post-training alignment targeting four canonical axes of interactivity: (1) pause handling, (2) turn-taking, (3) backchanneling, and (4) user interruption. For each axis, short audio segments are extracted from human conversation corpora, and axis-specific reward functions are designed. An additional LLM-based reward is used to evaluate response quality and prevent semantic degradation. The method is applied to two open-source full-duplex models: Moshi and PersonaPlex.

Result: The method demonstrates consistent improvements in interactivity across all four axes, validated through both offline evaluation with pre-recorded audio and real-time multi-turn dialogue evaluation on Moshi and PersonaPlex.

Conclusion: Comprehensive RL-based post-training alignment with axis-specific rewards and an LLM-based quality reward effectively improves the naturalness and interactivity of full-duplex spoken dialogue systems without degrading response quality.

Abstract: Full-duplex spoken dialogue models can listen and speak simultaneously, making them a promising architecture for natural conversation. However, current models are trained solely with supervised learning through token-level likelihood maximization, which does not directly optimize interaction-level behaviors, causing interactivity issues such as excessive silence and ill-timed turn-taking. Recent work has applied reinforcement learning (RL) to improve interactivity, but existing methods address only a limited set of interactive behaviors in their rewards. In this work, we propose a post-training alignment method that comprehensively improves the interactivity of full-duplex spoken dialogue models through RL. We address the four canonical axes of interactivity: pause handling, turn-taking, backchanneling, and user interruption. For each axis, we extract short audio segments from human conversation corpora and optimize the model with axis-specific reward functions. An extra LLM-based reward for response quality prevents semantic degradation. We apply our method to two open-source models, Moshi and PersonaPlex, demonstrating consistent improvements in interactivity on both offline evaluation with pre-recorded audio and real-time multi-turn dialogue evaluation.

</details>


### [65] [Gradient-Guided Reward Optimization for Inference-time Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09635)
*Hankun Lin,Ruqi Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: GGRO is a lightweight inference-time alignment method that uses gradient signals from a reward model to inject nudging tokens at high-uncertainty decoding steps, improving LLM alignment across safety, helpfulness, and reasoning without the sampling overhead of Best-of-N or the reward hacking vulnerability of rejection sampling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing inference-time alignment methods like Best-of-N and rejection sampling suffer from two key limitations: (1) performance is bounded by the base model's generation quality, and (2) reliance on imperfect reward models makes them susceptible to reward hacking. There is a need for a method that actively steers generation rather than merely re-ranking sampled outputs.

Method: GGRO operates during autoregressive decoding by monitoring token-level entropy to detect high-uncertainty regions indicative of distribution drift or misalignment. When such regions are detected, it computes gradient signals from an off-the-shelf reward model and uses them to generate nudging tokens that are injected into the decoding stream, steering the generation trajectory toward higher-reward outputs with minimal intervention.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that GGRO consistently improves inference-time alignment across safety, helpfulness, and reasoning benchmarks. It increases coverage of high-quality responses and exhibits greater robustness to reward hacking compared to Best-of-N and rejection sampling, while incurring only minimal computational overhead.

Conclusion: GGRO offers an effective and efficient alternative to sampling-based inference-time alignment methods by using gradient-guided token injection for targeted trajectory steering, overcoming the base model quality ceiling and reward hacking vulnerabilities of prior approaches. Code is publicly available.

Abstract: Ensuring the reliability of Large Language Models (LLMs) under distribution drift requires inference-time adaptation. While inference-time alignment methods such as Best-of-$N$ and rejection sampling are widely used, they frame the task as a sampling-intensive, reward-guided search, leading to two key limitations: their performance is bounded by the base model's generation quality, and their reliance on imperfect reward models makes them vulnerable to reward hacking. To address these challenges, we introduce Gradient-Guided Reward Optimization (GGRO), a lightweight inference-time method that performs targeted, minimal intervention during decoding via gradient guidance. Specifically, GGRO monitors token-level entropy to identify high-uncertainty regions indicative of drift or misalignment. Upon detection, it responds by injecting nudging tokens, generated using gradient signals from an off-the-shelf reward model, to steer the generation trajectory rather than merely re-ranking samples. Experiments show that GGRO consistently improves inference-time alignment across safety, helpfulness, and reasoning benchmarks. It also increases coverage of high-quality responses and robustness to reward hacking, with minimal computational overhead. Code is available at https://github.com/lhk2004/GGRO.

</details>


<div id='cs.SE'></div>

# cs.SE [[Back]](#toc)

### [66] [Anomaly Detection and Root Cause Analysis for Microservice Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09942)
*Luan Pham*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This thesis addresses five key limitations in microservice anomaly detection and RCA: decoupled detection/RCA, neglect of event data, dependency on service call graphs, lack of standardized benchmarks, and unclear effectiveness of causal inference methods. Contributions include BARO, EventADL, and TORAI frameworks, plus RCAEval—a benchmarking suite with systematic evaluation of existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Microservice failures are inevitable due to system complexity, causing degraded user experience and economic loss. Existing automated anomaly detection and RCA techniques suffer from five critical limitations: (1) treating detection and RCA separately, causing cascading errors; (2) focusing narrowly on metrics/logs/traces while ignoring event data (API calls, config changes); (3) requiring pre-existing service call graphs; (4) lacking standardized datasets and evaluation frameworks for fair comparison; and (5) unexamined effectiveness, efficiency, and robustness of causal inference-based RCA methods despite their dominance.

Method: The thesis contributes two groups: (1) Three RCA frameworks—BARO (end-to-end metric-based anomaly detection and RCA), EventADL (end-to-end event-data-based framework), and TORAI (multimodal RCA without requiring a service call graph)—validated on real microservice systems. (2) RCAEval, a comprehensive benchmark with ready-to-use datasets and reproducible baselines, plus a systematic evaluation of existing RCA methods, particularly causal inference-based approaches.

Result: Extensive experiments on real microservice systems demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of BARO, EventADL, and TORAI. The systematic evaluation of existing RCA methods, especially causal inference-based approaches, yields insights to guide future research directions in incident mitigation and remediation.

Conclusion: The thesis advances automated anomaly detection and RCA for microservice failures by delivering both novel end-to-end/multimodal frameworks and the RCAEval benchmarking infrastructure. These contributions address the identified five limitations and lay groundwork for future research on incident mitigation and remediation.

Abstract: Microservice systems are widely used to build cloud applications, yet their complexity makes failures inevitable, degrading user experience and causing economic loss. Automated anomaly detection and root cause analysis (RCA) are now active research areas, but existing techniques share five limitations. First, most treat anomaly detection and RCA separately, assuming anomalies are detected correctly, and falter when detection is imprecise due to noise or delay. Second, they focus on metrics, logs, and traces, leaving event data such as API calls and configuration changes underexplored. Third, many require a given service call graph and cannot diagnose without one. Fourth, the field lacks standardised datasets and evaluation frameworks, so methods are hard to compare fairly. Fifth, although causal inference-based RCA has become dominant, its effectiveness, efficiency, and robustness remain unclear.
  This thesis addresses these limitations through two groups of contributions. The first introduces methods that exploit observability data both independently and collectively. BARO is an end-to-end anomaly detection and RCA approach for metric data. EventADL is an end-to-end framework for event data. TORAI is a multimodal RCA framework that requires no service call graph. Extensive experiments on real microservice systems demonstrate their effectiveness and robustness. The second group delivers benchmarking datasets, an evaluation framework, and systematic evaluation efforts. RCAEval is a comprehensive benchmark providing ready-to-use datasets and reproducible baselines for future research. A systematic evaluation of existing RCA methods, especially causal inference-based approaches, offers insights that guide future directions. This thesis thereby advances automated anomaly detection and RCA for microservice failures, enabling future research on incident mitigation and remediation.

</details>


### [67] [Multi-task LLMs for Bug Classification: Efficient Inference with Auxiliary Decoding Heads](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09956)
*Nikolai Rozanov*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper presents a lightweight, line-level bug localization method using a multi-task LLM that achieves state-of-the-art performance with minimal inference cost—requiring only a single generated token per file—while matching costly agentic approaches on standard benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing bug localization techniques suffer from two extremes: agentic reasoning methods are accurate but prohibitively expensive (minutes per file, thousands of tokens), while lightweight approaches either operate at coarse function-level granularity or have limited performance and context size at line-level. A fast, precise, line-level method is needed.

Method: Three contributions: (1) a token alignment algorithm resolving tokenization mismatches that plagued prior work; (2) MLC, a lightweight multi-task LLM fine-tuned for line-level bug classification; (3) an optimized training recipe tailored for multi-line bug prediction. The model processes full-file context and outputs a single token per file for classification.

Result: State-of-the-art performance among comparable line-level setups with full-file context. Matches agentic approaches on Defects4J and PypiBugs benchmarks while reducing inference latency by orders of magnitude. Strong generalization is demonstrated on an out-of-domain Python evaluation dataset introduced by the authors.

Conclusion: Line-level bug localization can be made both highly efficient and accurate without agentic reasoning. The MLC approach offers a practical, scalable alternative for real-world debugging, with code, models, and datasets to be open-sourced upon acceptance.

Abstract: The rapid adoption of LLM-powered code generation has dramatically accelerated software development, yet effective verification methods remain severely underdeveloped. Existing bug localization techniques are either prohibitively expensive, requiring minutes of agentic reasoning and thousands of generated tokens per file, and/or operate at coarse function-level granularity unsuitable for precise debugging. While works that focus on line-level granularity and are more light-weight are often limited in their performance or context size. We introduce a novel line-level bug localization approach that addresses these limitations through three key contributions: (1) a token alignment algorithm that overcomes fundamental tokenization challenges in previous work, (2) a lightweight multi-task LLM for bug localization (MLC) enabling efficient line-level bug classification, and (3) an optimized training recipe for multi-line prediction. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance among similar setups on line-level bug localization with full-file context. At the same time we reach comparable performance to agentic approaches on Defects4J and PypiBugs benchmarks while reducing inference latency by orders of magnitudes, requiring only a single generated token per file. We further demonstrate strong generalization by introducing and evaluating on a small out-of-domain evaluation datasets in Python. We will open source our code, models, and datasets upon acceptance.

</details>


### [68] [Data-aware Static Analysis: Improving Detection of Semantic Faults in Machine Learning Code Using Data Characteristics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09957)
*Willem Meijer,Kristian Sandahl,Dániel Varró*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper proposes a data-aware static analysis method to detect semantic faults in ML code before training, using combined data/control flow analysis and API contracts. The approach is validated on real-world ML notebooks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Machine learning developers frequently introduce semantic faults—such as using unscaled data with scale-sensitive models—that lead to suboptimal predictions, high computational cost, or incorrect outputs. Current practice relies on post-training manual result analysis, which is inefficient. There is a need for early detection during coding.

Method: A data-aware static analysis approach combining data flow analysis, control flow analysis, and API contracts. This enables reasoning about ML code at a high abstraction level by tracking data properties and API preconditions/postconditions, allowing detection of semantic faults without executing or training models.

Result: The approach was evaluated on a sample of real-world machine learning notebooks and successfully detected faults requiring data-aware reasoning, demonstrating the potential of the technique.

Conclusion: Data-aware static analysis can effectively identify semantic faults in ML code during development rather than after training, improving developer productivity and model correctness. The combined use of data/control flow analysis with API contracts proves promising for this domain.

Abstract: Semantic faults specific to the use of machine learning models are a common problem for machine learning developers, causing suboptimal predictions, high computational cost, or incorrect outputs. For example, one may erroneously use unscaled data to train a scale-sensitive model. Machine learning developers detect these faults after training their models and manually analyzing the results, making it an inefficient process. We propose a novel data-aware static analysis approach to detect semantic faults in machine learning code, allowing developers to reveal these bugs while writing code instead of after training the model. Our approach uses combined data and control flow analysis, and API contracts, enabling data-aware reasoning about machine learning code at a high level of abstraction. We highlight the potential of our solution by analyzing a sample of real-world machine learning notebooks, finding that we can detect faults that require a data-aware approach.

</details>


### [69] [What makes a harness a harness: necessary and sufficient conditions for an agent harness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10106)
*Sanderson Oliveira de Macedo*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper proposes a rigorous, operational definition of "agent harness" in software engineering, distinguishing it from related concepts like agent frameworks, SDKs, IDEs, and orchestrators. Through conceptual analysis and genealogical tracing, the authors establish necessary and sufficient conditions, validate the definition against six real systems and edge cases, and outline a research agenda.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The term "agent harness" is widely but loosely used in software engineering with generative AI, leading to polysemy and conflation with related terms (framework, SDK, IDE plugin, orchestrator, eval harness). A precise, reference definition is needed to enable consistent engineering practice and scientific comparison of agentic coding systems.

Method: Conceptual analysis combining scholarly works with persistent identifiers and primary grey-literature sources (official documentation, glossaries, engineering reports). The method includes: (1) genealogical reconstruction from "horse's tack" → "test harness" → "ML evaluation harness" → "agent harness"; (2) formulation of a constitutive definition with necessary and sufficient conditions; (3) operationalization as an inclusion/exclusion test; (4) boundary drawing against five adjacent concepts; (5) application to six real harnesses (Claude Code, Codex CLI, Aider, Cline, OpenHands, SWE-agent) and edge cases.

Result: The constitutive definition successfully includes the six real agent harnesses and excludes non-harnesses consistently. Deliberate edge cases are also handled correctly by the inclusion/exclusion test. The analysis yields clear boundaries distinguishing agent harness from agent framework, SDK, IDE plugin, eval harness, and orchestrator.

Conclusion: The paper delivers an operational definition of "agent harness" with a shared vocabulary that can guide engineering practice and scientific comparison of agentic systems. It closes with a research agenda organized by design tension axes, providing a foundation for systematic study of the design space.

Abstract: The term agent harness now circulates widely in software engineering with generative artificial intelligence. It names the layer that wraps a language model and turns it into a coding agent able to act on a repository. The usage is loose and polysemous. Sometimes the term denotes the whole product (Claude Code, Codex CLI); sometimes it denotes the evaluation scaffold that runs an agent against tasks (the SWE-bench harness); sometimes it gets conflated with an agent framework, an SDK, an IDE plugin, or an orchestrator. What is missing is a reference definition that works as an instrument, one that includes and excludes cases consistently. We build that definition through a conceptual analysis that combines works with persistent identifiers and primary grey-literature sources, such as official documentation, glossaries, and engineering reports. We reconstruct the genealogy of the term, from the horse's tack to the classic test harness, to the machine-learning evaluation harness, and finally to the agent harness.
