We then propose a constitutive definition that states the necessary and sufficient conditions for a system to be an agent harness, we operationalize it as an inclusion and exclusion test, and we draw the boundary of the concept against an agent framework, an agent SDK, an IDE plugin, an eval harness, and an orchestrator. We apply the definition to six real harnesses (Claude Code, Codex CLI, Aider, Cline, OpenHands, and SWE-agent) and to deliberate edge cases; the test includes and excludes consistently. We close with a research agenda organized by design tension axes. The contribution is an operational definition of agent harness, with a shared vocabulary, able to guide engineering practice and the scientific comparison of agentic systems.

</details>


### [70] [TestMap: Evidence Infrastructure for Foundation-Model-Assisted Test Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10211)
*Hunter Leary,Luke Hanuska,Chris Brown*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: TestMap is an open-source infrastructure for C#/.NET that automates the full lifecycle of foundation-model-assisted test generation, from repository analysis through validation and repair, with comprehensive evidence tracking of all test outcomes—not just passing ones—to enable reproducible, comparable evaluation across models and strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generated unit tests from foundation models are both code artifacts and validation artifacts requiring their own validation before they can be trusted. Current validation is fragmented across disparate tools (build systems, test runners, coverage, mutation, static analysis), and existing approaches report only final passing tests, obscuring intermediate outcomes that reveal model limitations, repair costs, and toolchain inefficiencies.

Method: TestMap provides an integrated pipeline covering: repository analysis, source-test mapping, baseline execution, code metric collection, test smell detection, coverage measurement, mutation testing, model-guided test generation, validation, repair, and experiment tracking. It records the full lifecycle of each test candidate—including failed, repaired, low-impact, and evidence-positive outcomes—rather than only final passing results.

Result: As a design case, the paper describes the architecture and evidence model needed to make generated tests observable, repeatable, and comparable across repositories, models, prompts, and generation strategies. It demonstrates that tracking intermediate outcomes reveals insights invisible in pass-only reporting.

Conclusion: The authors identify open challenges including oracle and assertion quality, metric attribution, test maintainability, flakiness, execution cost, and developer acceptance, positioning TestMap as a foundation for addressing these in future research.

Abstract: Foundation models (FMs) can generate plausible unit tests, but determining whether those tests are correct, useful, maintainable, and worth integrating remains difficult. Generated tests must be mapped to the code they target, inserted into real projects, built, executed, measured against the baseline suite, repaired when necessary, and compared across models and generation strategies. This validation process is fragmented across build systems, test runners, coverage tools, mutation tools, static analyzers, and experiment scripts. The problem is especially important because generated tests are both code artifacts and validation artifacts: they must themselves be validated before they can be trusted as evidence about the system under test. This paper presents TestMap, an open-source infrastructure prototype that automates evidence-backed foundation-model-assisted test generation for C#/.NET repositories. TestMap supports repository analysis, source-test mapping, baseline execution, code metric collection, test smell detection, coverage measurement, mutation testing, model-guided test generation, validation, repair, and repository-specific experiment tracking. Rather than reporting only final passing tests, TestMap records the lifecycle of each generated candidate, including failed, repaired, low-impact, and evidence positive outcomes. These intermediate outcomes can reveal model limitations, missing context, repair cost, toolchain inefficiencies, or possible faults in the system under test. Using TestMap as a design case, we describe the architecture and evidence model needed to make generated tests observable, repeatable, and comparable across repositories, models, prompts, and generation strategies. We conclude with lessons learned and open challenges, including oracle and assertion quality, metric attribution, test maintainability, flakiness, execution cost, and developer acceptance.

</details>


### [71] [From Awareness to Action: How Developers Engage with Accessibility Innovation in LLM-Assisted Development](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10311)
*Thayssa Águila da Rocha,Luciane Silva,Ana Duarte,Marcelle Pereira Mota,Gustavo Pinto*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: A study at a Brazilian tech company shows that when People with Disabilities (PWD) lead accessibility initiatives, compliance-driven accessibility transforms into inclusive innovation, driving both technological excellence and cultural change.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Developers in corporate settings treat accessibility as a compliance checkbox rather than a source of innovation. The authors seek to understand how this dynamic can be shifted toward inclusive innovation.

Method: The study analyzed 14 LLM-based accessibility project proposals and conducted focus group discussions with 9 participants at a Brazilian tech company. A participatory, qualitative approach was employed to examine how innovation emerges from accessibility efforts.

Result: Inclusive innovation emerges most effectively when PWD themselves lead the initiatives. Participatory approaches enable accessibility to evolve beyond a compliance afterthought into a driver of technological and cultural transformation.

Conclusion: Organizations should adopt participatory approaches that empower PWD to lead accessibility efforts. This reframes accessibility from a reactive compliance requirement into a proactive force for innovation and cultural change.

Abstract: Developers often struggle to design truly accessible digital solutions in corporate environments. In these environments, accessibility is usually treated as a compliance requirement rather than an innovation opportunity. By analyzing 14 LLM-based accessibility project proposals and focus group discussions with 9 participants at a Brazilian tech company, we found that inclusive innovation can emerge particularly when initiatives are led by People with Disabilities (PWD) themselves. If organizations adopt similar participatory approaches, accessibility would evolve from an afterthought into a driving force for technological excellence and cultural transformation.

</details>


### [72] [Communication Skills in Software Engineering: A Multivocal Review](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10320)
*Dannilo Rabelo,Deisy Peres,Emmanuel Dias,Thayssa Rocha,Enne Rebeca de Freitas,Kiev Gama,Gustavo Pinto*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: A multivocal literature review reveals strong convergence between academic and gray literature on communication as a core software engineering competency, but with differing emphases: academia prioritizes conceptualization and empirical evidence, while gray literature stresses practical consequences and industry trends.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Communication skills are increasingly recognized as essential in software engineering, but discussions remain fragmented across academic and gray literature, limiting a holistic understanding of how communication is valued, taught, and applied in educational and professional contexts.

Method: The authors conducted a multivocal literature review (MLR), systematically synthesizing both academic (peer-reviewed) and gray literature (e.g., industry reports, practitioner blogs, white papers) to capture a comprehensive picture of communication in software engineering.

Result: Strong convergence was found between academic and gray sources in treating communication as a core competency. Key differences emerged in emphasis: academic literature focuses on conceptualization and empirical evidence, whereas gray literature highlights practical consequences and emerging industry practices.

Conclusion: The fragmentation of communication discourse across literature types obscures a unified understanding; bridging academic rigor with industry pragmatism can strengthen both software engineering education and professional practice regarding communication skills.

Abstract: Communication skills are increasingly recognized as essential in Software Engineering, yet discussions about them remain fragmented across academic and gray literature. This fragmentation is problematic because it limits a broader understanding of how communication is valued, taught, and applied in both educational and professional settings. Through a multivocal literature review, we found strong convergence between academic and gray sources in treating communication as a core competency, while also identifying differences in emphasis, with academia focusing on conceptualization and empirical evidence and gray literature stressing practical consequences and emerging industry practices.

</details>


### [73] [Beyond Coverage and Kill Scores: Empirically Measuring Test Suite Behavioural Gaps](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10417)
*Partha Protim Paul,Reid Holmes*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper introduces a novel approach to detect "behavioural gaps" — discrepancies between what software is expected to do (as documented) and what is actually tested. Analysing 10 Java libraries, the authors find 17.5% of expected behaviours are untested, and these gaps are not captured by traditional structural coverage metrics like line coverage or mutation testing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional test adequacy metrics (e.g., code coverage, mutation testing) are implementation-centric: they measure whether the code as written is exercised, not whether the system adheres to its expected behaviour. Developers lack automated means to detect gaps between documented expectations and validated behaviours, leaving a critical blind spot in test quality assessment.

Method: The authors propose an automated proof-of-concept approach that: (1) extracts expected method-level behaviours from natural language documentation (Javadoc) and source code; (2) maps these extracted behaviours to existing test cases; and (3) identifies gaps where expected behaviours lack corresponding test validation. The approach is evaluated on 10 open-source Java libraries (8,922 methods, 20,729 extracted behaviours). Additionally, they compare behavioural gaps against structural metrics and evaluate whether automated test generators (EVOSUITE, ASTER) can close these gaps.

Result: Behaviour extraction achieves 93.1% precision. Conservatively, 17.5% of detected expected behaviours are entirely untested (the "behavioural gap"). Automated test generators EVOSUITE and ASTER fail to validate at least 20.6% and 27.1% of expected behaviours, respectively. Crucially, the majority of untested behaviours occur in methods with high line coverage, and over half persist in methods with high mutation kill scores — indicating behavioural coverage is an independent dimension of test adequacy.

Conclusion: Behavioural coverage constitutes an independent, complementary dimension of test suite adequacy that is not captured by traditional structural metrics. The findings highlight a significant limitation in current testing practices and automated test generation tools, and motivate the integration of behavioural adequacy assessment into developer workflows.

Abstract: Traditional test adequacy metrics measure a system's implementation, not whether it adheres to its expected behaviour. While developers rely heavily on code coverage and mutation testing to assess test suite quality, these metrics are fundamentally implementation-centric and cannot detect gaps between what the code is expected to do and what it actually does. Unfortunately, there has been no way to reliably detect these discrepancies; in this paper we introduce an automated proof-of-concept approach to investigate these gaps. The approach extracts expected method-level behaviours from natural language documentation and source code, maps them to existing test cases, and identifies gaps between expected and validated behaviours. We evaluate the approach across ten popular open-source Java libraries comprising 8,922 methods, extracting 20,729 behaviours with 93.1% precision. Our empirical analysis conservatively estimates that 17.5% of detected expected behaviours remain entirely untested, which we term as the test suite's behavioural gap. To determine if these gaps are merely an artifact of human-driven testing, we evaluate state-of-the-art automated test generators (EVOSUITE / ASTER), finding that they similarly fail to validate at least 20.6% / 27.1% of detected expected behaviours. We further demonstrate that behavioural gaps are not predicted by traditional structural metrics: the majority of untested behaviours occur in methods that already have high line coverage, and over half persist in methods with high mutation kill score. These results suggest behavioural coverage acts as an independent dimension of test suite adequacy that can complement traditional structural metrics.

</details>


### [74] [MASTOR: A Multi-Agent Approach to Semantic Test Oracle Generation for RESTful APIs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10465)
*Sida Deng,Rubing Huang,Zhenzhen Yang,Man Zhang,Xuan Xie,Rongcun Wang*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: MASTOR is a multi-agent system that generates semantic test oracles for RESTful APIs by analyzing implementation source code. It combines single-operation oracles (status/field checks) with multi-operation behavioral consistency oracles, uses a challenger-agent review mechanism, and achieved a 75.4% mutation score across 13 projects, outperforming Direct Prompting and SATORI by 30+ percentage points.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing automated RESTful API testing approaches rely on superficial checks (HTTP status codes, schema conformance) that fail to detect semantic faults, business logic violations, and state-dependent inconsistencies. There is a need for test oracles that capture deeper behavioral and semantic properties derived from the actual implementation source code.

Method: MASTOR operates in two phases: (1) Source Analysis — a source extraction agent builds a source context for each endpoint by analyzing the transitive import closure of relevant source files; (2) Oracle Generation — two parallel paths run over these contexts: a single-operation path producing status and field oracles per endpoint, and a multi-operation path generating behavioral consistency oracles across operation sequences using cross-operation semantic associations. Both paths employ a challenger-agent review mechanism where a dedicated reviewer identifies weaknesses and issues improvement hints to guide targeted regeneration, followed by oracle normalization to filter structurally invalid results.

Result: Evaluated on 13 open-source RESTful API projects (296 operations, 251,303 LOC) from WFD and PRAB datasets, MASTOR achieved an average mutation score of 75.4% and generated 10,022 oracles. Oracles were translated into executable assertions via ToJUnit and ToPostmanAssertify, and into human-readable descriptions via ToReadable. On 50 selected operations, MASTOR (69.9%) outperformed Direct Prompting (39.8%) by 30.1 pp and SATORI (20.5%) by 49.4 pp.

Conclusion: MASTOR demonstrates that combining multi-agent source code analysis with dual-path oracle generation and challenger-agent review significantly improves the detection of semantic and behavioral faults in RESTful APIs. The approach substantially outperforms baseline methods and produces oracles that can be translated into both executable assertions and human-readable formats for practical use.

Abstract: Existing automated RESTful API testing approaches commonly rely on simple checks (e.g., HTTP status codes, schema conformance), which are insufficient for detecting semantic faults, business logic violations, and state-dependent inconsistencies. To address this, we propose MASTOR, a Multi-Agent approach for generating Semantic Test Oracles for RESTful APIs based on implementation source code. MASTOR consists of two phases: source analysis and oracle generation. The former employs a source extraction agent to construct a source context for each endpoint operation by analyzing a transitive import closure of relevant source files. The latter employs two parallel oracle-generation paths over the collected contexts: a single-operation path producing status and field oracles per operation, and a multi-operation path generating behavioral consistency oracles for operation sequences by leveraging cross-operation semantic associations. Both paths apply a challenger-agent review, where a dedicated reviewer identifies weaknesses and issues improvement hints to guide targeted regeneration, followed by oracle normalization to filter out structurally invalid oracles. We evaluated MASTOR on a benchmark of 13 open-source RESTful API projects (296 operations, 251,303 lines of code) from the WFD and PRAB datasets. MASTOR achieved an average mutation score of 75.4%, generating 10,022 oracles. These oracles were translated into executable assertions via ToJUnit and ToPostmanAssertify, and into human-readable descriptions via ToReadable. In a baseline comparison on 50 selected operations, MASTOR outperformed Direct Prompting by 30.1 percentage points (69.9% vs. 39.8%) and SATORI by 49.4 percentage points (69.9% vs. 20.5%).

</details>


### [75] [Exploring and Complementing End Users' Requirements in IoT enabled System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10598)
*Haotian Li,Xiaohong Chen,Zhi Jin,Shuyuan Xiao,Chenxu Wang,Haoxiang Yan,Xiaoyi Chen*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: Proposes an intent-driven requirements completion approach for IoT trigger-action programming that reconstructs user intent from fragmented rules, then regenerates complete, safe rules using a Bidirectional Requirements Traceability Tree and a multi-agent LLM framework, achieving 43% better completion and 21% fewer logical conflicts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End users writing IoT automation rules via trigger-action programming often produce fragmented expressions that capture low-level device operations rather than high-level intents, leading to missing conditions, logical conflicts, and overlooked safety constraints that risk hazardous behaviors.

Method: The approach reframes rule completion as a dual process: (1) reconstructing intent from fragmented rules, then (2) regenerating rules from that intent with safety embedded. It introduces a Bidirectional Requirements Traceability Tree — a three-layer model linking rules, intents, and quality concerns — and a multi-agent framework combining LLM reasoning with structured traceability for functionally complete, inherently safe, traceable, and explainable completions.

Result: The method significantly outperforms baselines, improving rule completion rate by 43% and reducing logical conflicts by over 21%.

Conclusion: By grounding completion in intent understanding, the work shifts the paradigm from user responsibility to system responsibility, and from functional correctness to holistic trustworthiness in IoT automation.

Abstract: End users create IoT automation rules via trigger action programming, but their expressions are often fragmented, capturing device operations rather than high level intents. This gap leads to missing conditions, logical conflicts, and overlooked safety constraints, risking hazardous behaviors. To address this, we propose an intent driven requirements completion approach that reframes rule completion as a dual process: reconstructing intent from fragmented rules, then regenerating rules from that intent, with safety embedded throughout. We introduce a Bidirectional Requirements Traceability Tree, a three layer model linking rules, intents, and quality concerns, and design a multiagent framework that combines LLM reasoning with structured traceability. This enables completions that are both functionally complete and inherently safe, while remaining traceable and explainable. Evaluation shows our method significantly outperforms the baselines, improving the rule completion rate by 43% and reducing logical conflicts by over 21%. By grounding completion in intent understanding, we shift the paradigm from user to system responsibility, and from functional correctness to holistic trustworthiness.

</details>


### [76] [Watts and Debts of Agentic Frameworks: An Empirical Study (Registered Report)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10702)
*Aneetta Sara Shany,Chandrasekar S,Karthik Vaidhyanathan*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper proposes an empirical study correlating Self-Admitted Technical Debt (SATD) in agentic AI frameworks with their hardware-level runtime energy consumption, aiming to determine whether code quality analysis can serve as an early-warning proxy for energy-efficient framework selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic AI systems in production carry hidden risks of accumulated Technical Debt (TD) and unmonitored runtime energy costs. While functional benchmarking is common, the empirical relationship between internal code quality (TD) and dynamic energy consumption remains unexplored, creating a blind spot for practitioners managing both sustainability and operational budgets.

Method: A confirmatory empirical study evaluating five open-source agentic frameworks. A standardized task suite will be executed in a controlled environment. SATD will be extracted via automated Python-based comment mining and classified using LLM-based classification with fine-tuned prompts. Runtime energy will be measured at the hardware level. Three research questions are investigated: (RQ1) presence of TD; (RQ2) variance in runtime energy consumption across architectures; (RQ3) statistical correlation between framework TD and task-level energy consumption.

Result: The paper does not report results, as it describes a planned confirmatory study design rather than completed experimental findings.

Conclusion: The study aims to establish whether automated source code analysis can reliably predict energy-efficient framework selection, advancing both green software engineering and agentic AI quality research. Results remain pending.

Abstract: Context: Every agentic AI system shipped to production carries two hidden risks: accumulated Technical Debt (TD) and unmonitored runtime energy costs. While functional benchmarking is common, the empirical link between internal structural quality (specifically TD) and dynamic energy consumption during execution remains unexplored, creating a blind spot for practitioners and organizations managing sustainability and operational budgets at scale. Goal: We propose a confirmatory empirical study correlating Self-Admitted Technical Debt (SATD) with hardware-level runtime energy consumption across agentic frameworks, to determine whether code quality can drive energy-aware design decisions. Method: We will evaluate five open-source agentic frameworks by executing a standardized task suite in a strictly controlled environment. SATD will be extracted via automated Python-based comment mining and categorized via LLM-based classification using fine-tuned prompt, while runtime energy will be measured at the hardware level. Our study will investigate three core research questions: (RQ1) the presence of TD within these frameworks; (RQ2) the variance in runtime energy consumption across architectures; and (RQ3) the statistical correlation between a framework's TD and its task-level energy consumption. Conclusion: The findings will establish whether automated source code analysis can serve as a reliable, early-warning proxy for energy-efficient framework selection, thereby advancing both green software engineering and agentic AI quality research.

</details>


### [77] [DeNovoSWE: Scaling Long-Horizon Environments for Generating Entire Repositories from Scratch](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10728)
*Jiale Zhao,Guoxin Chen,Fanzhe Meng,Wayne Xin Zhao,Ruihua Song,Ji-Rong Wen,Kai Jia*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: DeNovoSWE introduces a large-scale dataset of 4,818 instances for training LLMs to generate complete software repositories from documentation, achieving a dramatic performance improvement on the BeyondSWE-Doc2Repo benchmark (5.8% → 47.2%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based code agents are evolving from local bug fixing to whole-repository generation from high-level specifications, but training for such long-horizon tasks is hindered by the lack of large-scale, verifiable whole-repository generation data.

Method: DeNovoSWE is automatically constructed via a sandboxed agentic workflow without human annotation. The construction follows "divide and conquer" and critic-repair philosophies, with a difficulty-aware trajectory filtering strategy to balance data quality and diversity.

Result: Fine-tuning Qwen3-30B-A3B on DeNovoSWE raises its score on the BeyondSWE-Doc2Repo benchmark from 5.8% to 47.2%, a substantial improvement in long-horizon software engineering performance.

Conclusion: DeNovoSWE demonstrates that scalable, automatically curated datasets can effectively train agents for complex, long-horizon software engineering tasks like whole-repository generation from documentation.

Abstract: As the capabilities of LLM-based code agents continue to advance, their expected role is expanding beyond localized bug fixing in existing codebases toward architecting and implementing complete software repositories from high-level specifications. However, training agents for such long-horizon software engineering tasks remains difficult due to the scarcity of large-scale, verifiable whole-repository generation data. In this paper, we introduce \textbf{DeNovoSWE}, a large-scale dataset for whole-repository generation. DeNovoSWE comprises 4,818 high-quality instances, where each instance requires generating a complete repository from documentation. Our dataset is automatically constructed through a carefully designed sandboxed agentic workflow, enabling scalable curation without human annotation. DeNovoSWE is constructed with "divide and conquer" and critic-repair philosophy. To balance data quality and diversity, we further introduce a difficulty-aware trajectory filtering strategy. Fine-tuning Qwen3-30B-A3B on DeNovoSWE substantially improves long-horizon SWE performance, raising its score on the challenging BeyondSWE-Doc2Repo benchmark from 5.8% to 47.2%.

</details>


### [78] [Modular2Simple: A Tool for Modular Scenario Creation Based on the OpenSCENARIO Format](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10851)
*Nikolai Khriapov,Mohamed Taha Drif,Renjue Li,Cas Widdershoven*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: Modular2Simple is a tool that simplifies complex autonomous driving scenario creation by combining existing simple OpenSCENARIO scenarios into modular, reusable components, integrating with CARLA and reducing development time and code complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous driving systems (ADS) require realistic and complex scenarios for testing and validation, but traditional scenario creation methods are time-consuming, effort-intensive, and suffer from high code complexity with limited reusability.

Method: The tool leverages existing simple scenarios in OpenSCENARIO format and enables developers to compose complex scenarios by combining multiple simple or modular scenarios in a customizable manner. It integrates with the CARLA simulator and works with any OpenSCENARIO-compatible software.

Result: Modular2Simple significantly reduces development time and effort, promotes scenario reuse and customization, reduces code complexity, and enhances efficiency in scenario design and testing compared to traditional methods.

Conclusion: Modular2Simple provides an effective approach for building complex ADS testing scenarios through modular composition, addressing key bottlenecks in autonomous driving validation workflows.

Abstract: The rapid advancement of autonomous driving systems (ADS) has introduced significant challenges, particularly in the creation of realistic and complex scenarios for testing and validation. This paper introduces Modular2Simple, a tool designed to address these challenges by simplifying and enhancing the process of creating complex ADS scenarios. Modular2Simple seamlessly integrates with the CARLA simulator and is applicable to any software that supports the OpenSCENARIO format. By leveraging existing simple scenarios in the OpenSCENARIO format, the tool enables developers to create easily customizable modular scenarios through the combination of multiple simple or modular scenarios, significantly simplifying the scenario creation process while maintaining flexibility in scenario design. This approach not only facilitates the development of complex scenarios, reducing both development time and effort, but also promotes scenario reuse and customization, which leads to a significant reduction in code complexity and enhanced efficiency in scenario design and testing compared to traditional scenario development methods.

</details>


### [79] [From Perception to Action: Can UI Interventions Foster Sustainable LLM Chatbot](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10861)
*Nitish Patkar,Pooja Rani,Jack Glässer,Simon Lüscher,Martin Kropp*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper explores sustainability-oriented UI interventions in LLM chatbots, finding that features like an energy-efficiency mode toggle, per-response energy feedback, and usage dashboards can increase energy awareness and shift user behavior toward more energy-responsible usage patterns without sacrificing usability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-powered chatbots are increasingly prevalent, raising concerns about their energy consumption. While most mitigation efforts focus on model or infrastructure efficiency, the UI layer—which directly shapes user interaction behavior—remains underexplored as a lever for promoting energy-responsible use.

Method: A two-phase approach: (1) a baseline survey of 77 participants to gauge awareness of AI energy use, accuracy of consumption estimates, environmental concern, and willingness to accept performance trade-offs; (2) design and implementation of a web-based chatbot prototype featuring a three-mode switch (Energy-efficient, Balanced, Performance), per-response energy feedback, pre-send energy estimates, a usage metrics dashboard, and energy analogies, followed by a five-day field study with 11 participants to evaluate behavioral effects.

Result: Survey: 94.8% had some awareness of AI energy use, but 88.3% misestimated actual consumption; only 39.0% willing to trade performance for lower energy. Field study: Energy-efficient mode accounted for 55.8% of logged prompts; 90.9% self-reported actively choosing Eco-mode when high accuracy wasn't needed; no reduction in prompt length, indicating mode switching as the primary behavioral mechanism.

Conclusion: Sustainability-oriented UI interventions can improve energy awareness and promote more energy-responsible interaction patterns in LLM chatbots. These behavioral and model-based estimates complement backend efficiency work, and the open-source prototype and replication package enable further research on energy-aware conversational AI design.

Abstract: LLM-powered chatbots are increasingly embedded in everyday workflows, raising sustainability concerns due to their energy use. Most mitigation strategies emphasize model or infrastructure efficiency, while the user-interface (UI) layer remains underexplored despite its potential to shape interaction behavior. We investigate whether sustainability-oriented UI interventions can increase users' energy awareness and encourage more energy-responsible chatbot use without reducing usability. We first conducted a baseline survey with 77 participants to assess awareness and receptiveness to intervention concepts. Guided by prior work on persuasive technology and choice architecture, we implemented a web-based chatbot prototype with a three-mode switch (Energy-efficient, Balanced, Performance), per-response energy feedback, pre-send energy estimates, a usage metrics dashboard, and energy analogies. We then evaluated the prototype in a five-day field study with 11 participants. In the baseline survey, 94.8% of respondents reported at least some awareness of AI energy use, yet 88.3% misestimated actual consumption. Although concern about environmental impact was high, only 39.0% indicated willingness to accept a performance trade-off for lower energy use. In the field study, Energy-efficient mode accounted for 55.8% of logged prompts, while 90.9% self-reported actively choosing Eco-mode when high accuracy was not required. Participants did not reduce prompt length, suggesting mode switching as the primary behavioral mechanism. Sustainability-oriented UI interventions can improve awareness and support more energy-responsible interaction patterns in LLM chatbots. These effects are best interpreted as behavioral and model-based estimates that complement backend efficiency work, and the provided prototype and replication package support further research on energy-aware conversational AI design.

</details>


### [80] [Writing Better Software Explanations: A Guideline-Based Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10880)
*Martin Obaidi,Jean-Carl Kremser,Hannah Deters,Jakob Droste,Marc Herrmann,Kurt Schneider*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: A guideline-based tool combining LLM assistance with quality criteria helps developers formulate software explanations faster (24.4% time reduction) and with significantly higher perceived quality compared to manual writing, as shown in a two-phase study with practitioners and controlled survey participants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Software systems increasingly depend on natural-language explanations for requirements communication, but purely automatic LLM generation suffers from unreliable grounding and uncontrollable output quality. There is a need for tool support that balances automation with developer control and empirical quality standards.

Method: The authors developed a guideline-based formulation support tool that structures explanation writing into three stages: LLM-assisted generation, automated quality checking against empirically derived guidelines, and iterative revision—all under developer control. Evaluation proceeded in two phases: (1) an interview-based experiment with six industry practitioners comparing manual vs. tool-supported formulation across real explanation needs, and (2) a controlled user survey with 17 participants performing 204 paired comparisons of manual vs. tool-generated explanations.

Result: Phase 1: Tool-supported formulation was on average 24.4% faster than manual formulation, though inferential analyses showed only a trend toward efficiency. Phase 2: Tool-supported explanations were rated significantly higher in overall satisfaction (p=0.003, rank-biserial correlation=0.86).

Conclusion: Guideline-driven LLM assistance yields potential efficiency gains and demonstrably higher perceived formulation quality for software explanations. The authors recommend future investigation into long-term industrial deployment and integration with existing development workflows.

Abstract: As software systems increasingly rely on natural-language explanations to address user-reported explanation needs in requirements communication and support, ensuring that such explanations are consistent, relevant, and well formulated remains a major challenge. Purely automatic large language model (LLM) generation often lacks reliable grounding and controllable output quality. In this paper, we present a guideline-based formulation support tool for software explanations that combines LLM-assisted text generation with an empirically derived quality guideline. The tool structures the writing process into generation, quality checking, and iterative revision, while keeping domain control with developers. We evaluated the approach in a two-phase study consisting of an interview-based developer experiment and a controlled user survey. Six industry practitioners with software development or DevOps experience formulated explanations for real explanation needs in a human-only manual condition and in a human-with-LLM-support condition. In this small-scale evaluation, tool-supported formulation was on average 24.4% faster, although inferential analyses indicated only a trend for efficiency. In a subsequent user study with 17 participants and 204 paired comparisons, tool-supported explanations were rated significantly higher in overall satisfaction than manual explanations (p=0.003, rank-biserial correlation=0.86). Our findings suggest potential efficiency gains and higher perceived formulation quality through guideline-driven LLM assistance. Future work should examine long-term industrial use and integration into existing development workflows.

</details>


### [81] [From Quality Properties to Practice: A Guideline and Workflow for Explainability Requirements](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10882)
*Martin Obaidi,Jakob Droste,Hannah Deters,Marc Herrmann,Michel Krahl,Kurt Schneider*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: A guideline-driven workflow with lightweight LLM support reduces the effort of writing explainability requirements by 23.5% while producing outputs comparable in quality to manually written alternatives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explainability requirements for AI-enabled systems are often crafted ad hoc, and unguided LLM assistance can produce vague, inconsistent, or incomplete statements. A structured, evidence-based approach is needed to improve both efficiency and quality.

Method: The authors conducted a structured literature review and developer interviews to elicit candidate quality properties, then prioritized them via a practitioner survey (n=20) to derive a guideline of ten core properties. This guideline was operationalized in a web-based tool supporting an iterative draft-check-revise workflow. Evaluation comprised a workshop with requirements engineers (n=6) measuring formulation time, and an online study with developers (n=18) comparing implementability and quality ratings between tool-supported and manually written requirements.

Result: Tool support reduced formulation time by 23.5% on average (Wilcoxon p=0.021). In the independent online study, tool-supported and manually written requirements received comparable ratings for implementability and formulation quality, with a descriptive but non-significant preference trend toward tool-supported versions.

Conclusion: Combining a prioritized quality guideline with lightweight LLM support can significantly reduce formulation effort while maintaining perceived quality, offering a practical approach for generating explainability requirements in AI-enabled systems.

Abstract: Explainability is increasingly required in AI-enabled software systems to support transparency, user trust, and compliance. Yet, explainability requirements are often written ad hoc, and unguided large language model support can yield vague, inconsistent, or incomplete statements. This paper presents a sequential, guideline-driven workflow for formulating explainability requirements and evaluates its tool-based operationalization. We first elicited candidate quality properties through a structured literature review and developer interviews. We then prioritized these properties in an online survey with practitioners (n=20) and derived a concise guideline of ten core properties with actionable formulation instructions. Next, we operationalized the guideline in a web-based tool that supports an iterative workflow of drafting, property-based checks, and revision. We evaluated the workflow in two complementary studies. In a workshop with requirements engineers (n=6), tool support reduced formulation time by 23.5% on average (Wilcoxon p=0.021). In an independent online study with software developers (n=18), tool-supported and manually written requirements received comparable ratings for implementability and formulation quality, with a descriptive slight preference tendency toward the tool-supported versions. Overall, our results suggest that combining a prioritized quality guideline with lightweight LLM support can reduce formulation effort while producing requirements that are perceived comparably to manually written ones.

</details>


### [82] [Early Comparative Evaluation of Transformer Models for Multilingual Software Vulnerability Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10925)
*Fiza Naseer,Javad Khan,Muhammad Yaqoob,Alexios Mylonas*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: A comparative evaluation of BERT, RoBERTa, and CodeBERT for binary vulnerability detection across four programming languages (HTML, Python, JavaScript, PHP) using the CVEFixes dataset, revealing significant cross-language performance variation and highlighting the need for language-aware multilingual vulnerability detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern software applications integrate multiple programming languages, making cross-language vulnerability detection critical. Existing transformer-based models have not been systematically evaluated for their language-wise effectiveness in this multilingual security context.

Method: The authors benchmark three pre-trained transformer models — BERT, RoBERTa, and CodeBERT — on the CVEFixes dataset for binary vulnerability classification. Evaluation is conducted separately across HTML, Python, JavaScript, and PHP using language-wise three-fold stratified cross-validation to ensure balanced class distribution per fold.

Result: Performance varies substantially across programming languages for all three models, with no single model dominating across all languages. This demonstrates that current transformer architectures lack consistent multilingual robustness for vulnerability detection.

Conclusion: Multilingual vulnerability detection demands more language-aware modelling strategies tailored to the linguistic and structural properties of individual programming languages, rather than relying on a one-size-fits-all transformer approach.

Abstract: Software vulnerability detection is increasingly important as modern applications combine multiple programming languages. This paper presents an early comparative evaluation of BERT, RoBERTa, and CodeBERT for binary vulnerability detection across HTML, Python, JavaScript, and PHP using the CVEFixes dataset and language-wise three-fold stratified cross-validation. The results show clear performance differences across languages, indicating that multilingual vulnerability detection requires more language-aware and robust transformer-based modelling strategies.

</details>


### [83] [GapFuzz: Cross-Plane Divergence Fuzzing for Distributed SDN Controllers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11035)
*Moustapha Awwalou Diouf,Samuel Ouya,Jacques Klein,Tegawendé F. Bissyandé*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: GapFuzz is a concurrency fuzzer targeting distributed SDN clusters that exposes a replication race window where contradictory rules from non-master nodes can be serialized into the kernel datapath, achieving 81.7% divergent verdicts on ONOS 2.7. It uses controlled inter-injection delays, cross-plane state reconstruction, and a two-phase timing search to bound the race window, revealing that prior control-plane-only fuzzers miss a significant fraction of faults.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Distributed SDN clusters asynchronously replicate flow state between master and backup nodes, creating a vulnerability window where two backups can commit contradictory rules. The master can serialize both into the data plane, and the kernel datapath can latch onto an action that no node considers authoritative. Existing SDN fuzzers fail to detect this fault because they restrict their oracle to the control plane, target only a single controller, or lack concurrency steering to provoke replication races.

Method: GapFuzz injects pairs of contradictory Northbound requests on two non-master nodes with a controlled inter-injection delay Δt. It reconstructs the global cross-plane state by querying every replica and the kernel-datapath action via ovs-appctl ofproto/trace. A two-phase timing search first detects whether a divergence exists, then doubles and bisects on Δt to bound the injection-time window. A lifetime probe labels each verdict as transient or persistent and maps it to one of four cross-plane state classes derived from the ONOS 2.7 source code.

Result: On a three-node ONOS 2.7 cluster, GapFuzz produces a divergent verdict in 81.7% of attempts (N=50, Wilson 95% CI [77.3, 85.4]%). All divergences occur between the cluster's authoritative state and the kernel datapath. Phase 2 isolates a 5 ms race window for one template and a doubling-cap regime (Δt_max = 10.24 s) for six others; 99.4% of divergences persist past 30 s. Replacing the kernel-datapath probe with the OpenFlow user-space probe used by prior fuzzers reduces detection by 26.6 percentage points overall and by 46.5 points after excluding canonicalization-forced verdicts.

Conclusion: Distributed SDN controllers exhibit a previously unaddressed replication-induced consistency fault between the control plane and kernel datapath. GapFuzz demonstrates that kernel-datapath probing is essential for detecting such cross-plane divergences, as control-plane-only oracles miss a substantial portion of faults, and that the majority of these divergences are persistent rather than transient.

Abstract: Distributed Software-Defined Networking (SDN) clusters replicate flow state asynchronously between a master node and its backups, leaving a window during which two backup nodes can each commit a contradictory rule, the master can serialize both into the data plane, and the kernel datapath can latch onto an action that no node believes authoritative. Existing SDN fuzzers miss this fault: they confine their oracle to the control plane, target a single controller, or do not steer concurrency to provoke replication races.
  We present GapFuzz, a stateful concurrency fuzzer for distributed SDN clusters. GapFuzz injects pairs of contradictory Northbound requests on two non-master nodes with controlled inter-injection delay $Δt$, and reconstructs the global cross-plane state by querying every replica and the kernel-datapath action through ovs-appctl ofproto/trace. A two-phase timing search detects whether a divergence exists, then doubles and bisects on $Δt$ to bound the injection-time window; a lifetime probe labels each verdict transient or persistent and assigns it to one of four cross-plane state classes derived from the ONOS 2.7 source.
  On a three-node ONOS 2.7 cluster, GapFuzz produces a divergent verdict in 81.7% of attempts ($N=50$, Wilson 95% CI $[77.3, 85.4]$%); every divergence sits between the cluster's authoritative state and the kernel datapath. Phase 2 separates a 5 ms race window for one template from a doubling-cap regime ($Δt_{\max}=10.24$ s) for six others, and 99.4% of divergences persist past 30 s. Replacing the kernel-datapath probe with the OpenFlow user-space probe used by prior fuzzers drops detection by 26.6 percentage points overall and by 46.5 points after excluding canonicalization-forced verdicts.

</details>


### [84] [Making Software Meaningful](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11051)
*Eagon Meng,Abutalib Namazov,Carmel Schare,Alcino Cunha,Daniel Jackson*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper argues that adopting a single, explicit, shared representation of software behavior—grounded in domain phenomena (individuals, actions, facts)—improves usability, modularity, and accountability. It partitions actions into concepts and demonstrates benefits across three areas: aligning user-designer meaning for usability, mapping meaning units to modular LLM-generated code, and enforcing agent conduct codes for accountability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current software development suffers from fragmented representations of behavior across stakeholders, artifacts, and activities, leading to misalignment, poor modularity, and lack of accountability. The paper motivates a unified commitment to explicit meaning grounded in the application domain as a remedy.

Method: The method constructs a shared behavioral vocabulary from domain phenomena: individuals, the actions they participate in, and resulting facts. Actions are partitioned into concepts to form larger semantic units. This vocabulary then serves as the single grounding representation for all discourse, artifacts, and activities across all stakeholders.

Result: Three demonstrated applications: (1) usability improves when user and designer share a single meaning; (2) LLMs generate modular, legible code by mapping semantic units to code units; (3) agents become accountable by adhering to a behavioral code of conduct derived from the shared meaning.

Conclusion: A commitment to explicit, domain-grounded meaning—formalized as individuals, actions, facts, and concepts—provides a unifying foundation that simultaneously enhances software usability, modularity, and accountability.

Abstract: Adopting a single measure can improve the usability, modularity and accountability of software: a commitment to explicit meaning. This entails constructing and agreeing upon a representation of the behavior of the software, as observed in the domain of application. The phenomena comprising this behavior become a vocabulary that grounds all discourse about the software, among all stakeholders, and for all artifacts and activities. These phenomena are individuals; actions they participate in; and facts that result from actions. They can be organized, by partitioning the set of actions, into concepts, offering larger units of meaning. Examples of exploiting meaning are given in three areas: designing for usability (by aligning user and designer on a single shared meaning); generating modular code with LLMs (by mapping units of meaning to units of code, achieving not only modularity but also legibility); and making agents accountable (by having them adhere to a code of conduct that defines their intended behavior).

</details>


### [85] [Operationalizing Property-Based Testing for Data-Intensive Scalable Computing Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11132)
*Yaoxuan Wu,Ingrid Lee,Ahmad Humayun,Muhammad Ali Gulzar,Miryung Kim*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: DiscPBT is a property-based testing engine for Apache Spark that defines eight reusable meta-properties and provides generators plus an instantiation framework to synthesize valid workloads. It achieves higher branch coverage and plan diversity than CometFuzz, revealing semantic drifts and corner-case errors missed by crash-based fuzzing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Crash-based fuzzing for DISC frameworks misses semantic drifts and optimization-related errors because its oracles are shallow. Property-based testing can address this by checking general semantic invariants, but operationalizing PBT for DISC systems is difficult due to the need for reusable property definitions and effective instantiation into valid workloads and data.

Method: DiscPBT introduces eight reusable meta-properties across four categories: equivalence rewriting, data decomposition, computation decomposition, and operator-local semantic relations. It provides reusable generators for synthesizing valid workload skeletons and input data, along with an instantiation framework that realizes each meta-property in schema-compatible contexts using compatible operators, expressions, and UDFs.

Result: On PySpark, DiscPBT achieves 1.2× higher branch coverage and 1153× greater plan diversity than CometFuzz. Across 66 concrete properties, it reveals cross-version semantic drift and subtle corner-case pitfalls involving NaN and empty inputs that crash-based fuzzing alone cannot capture.

Conclusion: Systematic property-based testing is demonstrably valuable for uncovering semantic issues in DISC frameworks, going beyond what crash-based fuzzing can achieve.

Abstract: While fuzzing effectively catches crashes, its shallow oracles often miss semantic drifts and optimization-related errors in data-intensive scalable computing (DISC) frameworks. Property-based testing (PBT) addresses this limitation by checking general semantic invariants across diverse workloads and inputs, rather than relying on specific expected outputs. However, systematically operationalizing PBT for DISC systems remains difficult because it requires both reusable property definitions and effective instantiation into valid workloads and data.
  We present DiscPBT, a property-based testing engine for Apache Spark. DiscPBT introduces eight reusable meta-properties for DISC semantic testing, spanning equivalence rewriting, data decomposition, computation decomposition, and operator-local semantic relations. To operationalize these meta-properties, DiscPBT provides reusable generators for synthesizing valid workload skeletons and input data, together with an instantiation framework that realizes each meta-property in schema-compatible contexts through compatible operators, expressions, and UDFs.
  Our evaluation on PySpark shows that DiscPBT achieves 1.2$\times$ higher branch coverage and 1153$\times$ greater plan diversity than CometFuzz. Across 66 concrete properties, DiscPBT reveals cross-version semantic drift as well as subtle corner-case pitfalls involving NaN and empty inputs, that are not captured by crash-based fuzzing alone. These results demonstrate the value of systematic PBT for uncovering semantic issues in DISC frameworks.

</details>


<div id='cs.IR'></div>

# cs.IR [[Back]](#toc)

### [86] [Mult-DPO: Multinomial Direct Preference Optimization for Recommender Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10078)
*Yaochen Zhu,Harald Steck,James McInerney,Aditya Sinha,Yinhan He,Nathan Kallus,Jundong Li*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: Mult-DPO extends DPO from pairwise to set-wise preferences for LLM-based recommender systems by using a tractable multinomial surrogate likelihood that upper-bounds the marginalized Plackett-Luce DPO loss, enabling efficient alignment with multiple positive/negative items.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DPO relies on pairwise (Bradley-Terry) preferences, but real-world recommender systems involve set-wise feedback: multiple positive items should all outrank negatives, with no internal ordering among positives or negatives. The Plackett-Luce model naturally handles rankings but requires marginalizing over all positive orderings, which is combinatorially intractable. The authors aim to bridge this gap with a tractable alternative.

Method: The authors propose Mult-DPO, which replaces the intractable marginalized PL likelihood with a multinomial surrogate defined over the same reward-induced weight space. This yields a closed-form, classification-style DPO objective for set-wise preferences. They prove the multinomial loss is a tractable upper bound on the marginalized PL DPO loss and characterize bound tightness via the relative total weight of positives versus negatives. They also extend Mult-DPO to handle multiple preference levels.

Result: The multinomial DPO loss is proven to be a tight upper bound on the marginalized Plackett-Luce DPO loss, with tightness governed by the relative total weight of positive vs. negative items, suggesting that harder/richer negatives can tighten the bound. The method scales efficiently to set-wise preferences.

Conclusion: Mult-DPO provides a principled and computationally efficient way to align LLM-based recommenders with set-wise user preferences, overcoming the combinatorial intractability of the PL model while retaining theoretical guarantees via the upper-bound relationship.

Abstract: Direct preference optimization (DPO) is a simple and effective alignment strategy for large language models (LLMs) based on pairwise preferences. In recommender systems, however, user feedback is rarely pairwise. For a given context, e.g., a user, a session, or a conversation, we typically observe set-wise preferences with multiple positive items, where every positive item should outrank every unobserved or explicitly negative item, with no prescribed order among the positives or the negatives themselves. A natural generalization is to use the Plackett-Luce (PL) reward model, which extends the Bradley-Terry reward model underlying vanilla DPO from pairwise preferences to full rankings of candidates. However, we show that adapting the PL model to set-wise preferences requires marginalizing over all positive orderings, where the resulting expression is combinatorial in complexity. To address this fundamental challenge, we propose Mult-DPO, a novel DPO objective with a tractable multinomial surrogate likelihood over set-wise preference events for the user-preference alignment of LLM-based recommender systems. The multinomial construction is not itself a ranking distribution, but it is defined on the same reward-induced weight space and admits a closed-form DPO-style objective, enabling direct alignment of LLMs with multiple candidates through a classification-style objective. In addition, we prove that the multinomial DPO loss is a tractable upper bound on the marginalized PL DPO loss when optimizing against the set-wise preference data. We further characterize the tightness of this bound in terms of the relative total weight of positives versus negatives, which provides insights into tightening the bound with richer or harder negatives. Finally, we extend Mult-DPO to the alignment of LLMs with multiple preference levels. Code is available at https://github.com/yaochenzhu/Mult_DPO

</details>


### [87] [MetaPlate: Counterfactual-Guided RAG-LLM Tool for Personalized Food Recommendation and Hyperglycemia Prevention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10120)
*Asiful Arefeen,Carol Johnston,Hassan Ghasemzadeh*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: MetaPlate is a decision-support framework that uses counterfactual explanation-guided optimization and LLM-based retrieval-augmented generation to produce personalized, actionable meal recommendations for mitigating postprandial hyperglycemia, validated through expert dietitian evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dietary guidance for postprandial hyperglycemia is static, impractical, and insufficiently personalized. Current CGM and ML-based approaches are primarily predictive rather than prescriptive, lacking actionable, context-aware recommendations aligned with user goals.

Method: MetaPlate integrates multimodal data (CGM, wearable physiological signals, meal inputs) from 25 individuals to model pre-meal context. An ML model predicts glucose response, and a counterfactual optimization module adjusts macronutrient amounts to keep glucose ≤140 mg/dL. An LLM-based retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) layer with constrained USDA database search produces human-readable recommendations. Evaluation uses a structured expert-in-the-loop assessment with registered dietitians, comparing pre- and post-prompt refinement performance.

Result: Prompt refinement led to improvements in meal realism, portion suitability, and recommendation likelihood. Expert feedback showed a shift from clinically implausible outputs to actionable, contextually appropriate recommendations.

Conclusion: Domain knowledge and structured constraints are critical for LLM-driven dietary recommendation systems. MetaPlate demonstrates potential as a real-time personalized dietary decision-support tool for healthy adults.

Abstract: Postprandial hyperglycemia is a key risk factor for metabolic disorders; however, existing dietary guidance is often static, impractical, and insufficiently personalized, providing recommendations that are difficult to follow or not impactful. While recent advances leverage continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) and machine learning to predict glycemic responses, these approaches are largely predictive and lack actionable guidance. Moreover, recommendation systems are often misaligned with user goals and require extensive input. We present MetaPlate, a counterfactual explanation (CF) guided, context-aware decision-support framework that generates personalized meal recommendations to mitigate postprandial glucose excursions in healthy adults. MetaPlate integrates multimodal data, including CGM readings, wearable-derived physiological signals, and user-provided meal inputs from $25$ individuals to model pre-meal context. A machine learning model predicts glucose response, while a CF optimization module adjusts meal composition modifying macronutrient amounts to maintain glucose levels within a target range ($\leq 140$ mg/dL). An LLM-based retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) layer enhances interpretability by producing human-readable recommendations using constrained search of the USDA food database. We evaluate MetaPlate via a structured expert-in-the-loop assessment with registered dietitians (RDs), comparing performance before and after prompt refinement. Results show improvements in meal realism, portion suitability, and recommendation likelihood, with expert feedback indicating a shift from clinically implausible outputs to actionable, contextually appropriate recommendations. Our findings emphasize the importance of domain knowledge and structured constraints in LLM-driven systems and highlight the potential of MetaPlate as a real-time personalized dietary decision-support tool.

</details>


### [88] [$τ$-Rec: A Verifiable Benchmark for Agentic Recommender Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10156)
*Bharath Sivaram Narasimhan,Karthik R Narasimhan*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: τ-Rec is a benchmark for agentic recommender systems that replaces subjective LLM-as-a-judge evaluations with verifiable rewards and a reveal-tagged elicitation mechanism, revealing that even the best models achieve only ~57% pass@1 and ~38% pass@4, highlighting a critical reliability gap in conversational agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation paradigms for conversational recommender systems have not kept pace with the shift toward agentic, multi-turn interfaces. Existing benchmarks rely heavily on "LLM-as-a-judge" evaluations, which suffer from subjectivity, high operational costs, and inconsistency. There is a need for an objective, systematic, and reproducible evaluation framework.

Method: The authors introduce τ-Rec, a benchmark featuring: (1) verifiable rewards based on structured catalog predicates that can be objectively checked, eliminating subjective judgment; (2) a reveal-tagged elicitation (RTE) mechanism that controls how and when task constraints are revealed during multi-turn dialogue; and (3) a pass^k reliability metric that measures an agent's ability to reason consistently across multiple attempts. The benchmark was tested on nine configurations across six model families.

Result: Evaluation across nine configurations from six model families (GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 2.5 Flash, DeepSeek V4 Flash, Qwen3-32B, and GPT-5 mini) reveals a steep reliability cliff. The best-performing model achieves only ~57% at pass@1 and ~38% at pass@4, indicating that even top-tier conversational agents fail to consistently satisfy task constraints under repeated evaluation.

Conclusion: There exists a critical reliability gap in current conversational agent deployment for recommendation tasks. The τ-Rec benchmark provides a systematic, objective framework for measuring and improving agentic recommender system reliability. All code and data are open-sourced.

Abstract: As recommender systems transition toward agentic, multi-turn conversational interfaces, evaluation paradigms have struggled to keep pace. Current benchmarks often rely on "LLM-as-a-judge" evaluations, which introduce subjectivity, high costs and inconsistency. We present $τ$-Rec, a benchmark for agentic recommender systems that replaces subjective evaluation with verifiable rewards and a reveal-tagged elicitation (RTE) mechanism that controls how task constraints surface during dialogue. By testing agents against structured catalog predicates and employing a pass^k reliability metric, $τ$-Rec provides a systematic test for consistent reasoning. Our evaluation of nine configurations across five model families -- GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 2.5 Flash, DeepSeek V4 Flash, Qwen3-32B and GPT-5 mini -- reveals a steep reliability cliff, where even the best model achieves only ~57% at pass^1 and ~38% at pass^4, highlighting a critical gap in current conversational agent deployment. All code and data are publicly available at https://github.com/nbharaths/tau-rec.

</details>


### [89] [Atomic Intent Reasoning: Bringing LLM Semantics to Industrial Cross-Domain Recommendations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10357)
*Zhuohang Jiang,Yuxin Chen,Shijie Wang,Haohao Qu,Zhou Jindong,Wenqi Fan,Li Qing,Dongxu Liang,Jun Wang*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: AIR (Atomic Intent Reasoning) is an LLM-driven cross-domain recommendation framework that shifts LLM inference offline and uses efficient retrieval/composition online, achieving ~400× inference acceleration. It demonstrates SOTA on public datasets and +3.446% GMV lift in Kuaishou E-commerce A/B tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-domain recommendation from content to e-commerce faces three key industrial challenges: (1) significant semantic gaps between content and e-commerce domains, (2) massive, noisy user cross-domain behavior sequences, and (3) LLMs' strong reasoning capabilities are unusable online due to millisecond-level inference latency constraints.

Method: AIR migrates LLM inference entirely to the offline phase, where it reasons about atomic user intents from cross-domain behaviors. During online serving, user intent representations are dynamically constructed via efficient retrieval and composition mechanisms, avoiding any LLM inference on the critical path while preserving semantic consistency.

Result: The method achieves ~400× inference acceleration. On multiple public datasets, AIR reaches state-of-the-art performance. In large-scale online A/B testing at Kuaishou E-commerce, it delivers a +3.446% GMV increase along with stable, significant improvements across multiple core business metrics.

Conclusion: AIR successfully bridges the gap between LLM reasoning capabilities and industrial latency requirements for cross-domain recommendation. The framework's offline-online decoupled design proves both effective and practically deployable at scale, validated through rigorous offline benchmarks and real-world A/B testing.

Abstract: Cross-domain recommendation is a core problem in content-to-e-commerce platforms. Its objective is to leverage user interactions with content to infer potential purchasing intent on the e-commerce side, thereby enhancing conversion rates and commercial value. However, in real industrial scenarios, cross-domain recommendation faces multiple challenges: significant semantic gaps exist between different domains, and user cross-domain behavior sequences are often massive in scale and rich in noise. Although large language models (LLMs) possess powerful semantic understanding and reasoning capabilities, their millisecond-level inference latency makes direct application in online recommendation systems difficult. To address these issues, this paper introduces AIR (Atomic Intent Reasoning), an LLM-driven cross-domain recommendation framework designed for industrial-grade deployment. By migrating LLM inference to the offline phase and dynamically constructing user intent representations through efficient retrieval and composition during online operations, it achieves approximately 400* inference acceleration while maintaining semantic consistency. Experimental results across multiple public datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in cross-domain recommendation tasks. Furthermore, large-scale online A/B testing conducted in Kuaishou E-commerce's real-world business scenarios shows that our approach delivers stable and significant improvements across multiple core business metrics, including a +3.446% increase in GMV, fully validating its effectiveness and practical value in industrial-scale recommendation systems.

</details>


### [90] [SIDInspector: A Mapping-First Diagnostic Resource for Semantic-ID Tokenizers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10375)
*Jiandong Ding,Heng Chang,Huijie Qin,Tianying Liu*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces a diagnostic tool for inspecting Semantic-ID tokenizer artifacts used in generative recommendation, revealing that exported item-to-code mappings often harbor hidden issues like aliasing and weak prefix alignment that only surface after downstream training. A key finding is that behaviorally meaningful prefix alignment does not automatically follow from learned tokenization—a deterministic category-prefix baseline outperformed learned exports—and should be inspected separately from final ranking quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Semantic-ID tokenizers are increasingly reused as standalone artifacts in generative recommendation pipelines, but they lack a common inspection interface. Critical problems—coverage gaps, full-code aliasing, behaviorally weak prefixes, tail compression, and prefix fan-out—are typically discovered only after expensive downstream training. The authors aim to provide a mapping-first diagnostic resource that surfaces these issues early, before committing to full model training and leaderboard evaluation.

Method: The proposed tool, called "Tool" (name redacted), defines a small adapter contract over five components: item mappings, metadata, interactions, and optional generator traces. It validates this contract and reports mapping-level probes covering utilization, aliasing, neighborhood alignment, popularity allocation, and structural cost, with additional hooks for temporal churn and generator traces. The diagnostic is demonstrated across four tokenizer artifact lines: a same-item GRID/RQ-KMeans-style and ReSID/GAOQ contrast on 23,742 Musical items, plus released LETTER and LC-Rec item-index artifacts.

Result: In the Musical domain contrast, the GRID-style feature-text export yielded 3,749 unique full codes with a 0.977 full-code aliasing rate, while ReSID/GAOQ was aliasing-free. However, the strongest prefix co-occurrence alignment (0.447) came from a deterministic category-prefix control, considerably exceeding both the GRID-style (0.154) and ReSID/GAOQ (0.055–0.080) learned exports. Cross-domain, fixed-reranker, and mechanism-probe checks confirmed that prefix alignment functions as a candidate-exposure signal, while final ranking quality remains a separate downstream model concern.

Conclusion: Addressability (code uniqueness) and behaviorally meaningful prefix semantics should be inspected as distinct properties in SID tokenizer artifacts. The tool enables such pre-training diagnostics, separating concerns around prefix alignment (a candidate-exposure signal) from final ranking quality (a downstream model question), thereby saving computational resources and guiding tokenizer design before full-scale training.

Abstract: Semantic-ID (\sid) tokenizers are increasingly reused as standalone artifacts in generative recommendation: an exported item-to-code mapping becomes the address space that a later sequence generator must use. These mappings rarely come with a common inspection interface, so coverage gaps, full-code aliasing, behaviorally weak prefixes, tail compression, and prefix fan-out are often found only after downstream training. We present \tool, a mapping-first diagnostic resource for \sid tokenizer artifacts. \tool defines a small adapter contract over item mappings, metadata, interactions, and optional generator traces; validates the contract; and reports mapping-level probes for utilization, aliasing, neighborhood alignment, popularity allocation, and structural cost, with hooks for temporal churn and generator traces.
  \tool reports inspectable artifact profiles before downstream leaderboard scores. The released resource covers four tokenizer artifact lines: a same-item GRID/RQ-KMeans-style and ReSID/GAOQ contrast on 23,742 Musical items, plus released LETTER and LC-Rec item-index artifacts. In the Musical contrast, the GRID-style feature-text export has 3,749 unique full codes and a 0.977 full-code aliasing rate, while ReSID/GAOQ is aliasing-free in its exported mapping. Yet the strongest prefix--co-occurrence alignment comes from a deterministic category-prefix control, not from either learned export row (0.447 versus 0.154 and 0.055--0.080), showing that addressability and behaviorally meaningful prefixes should be inspected separately. Cross-domain, fixed-reranker, and mechanism-probe checks support the same diagnostic direction: prefix alignment is a candidate-exposure signal, while final ranking quality remains a downstream model question.

</details>


### [91] [SkillResolve-Bench: Measuring and Resolving Same-Capability Ambiguity in Agent Skill Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10388)
*Jiandong Ding*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: Agent skill retrieval suffers from "same-capability execution-risk retrieval" where a retriever finds the right capability family but exposes a risky sibling skill (stale resources, missing preconditions, wrong procedures). The authors introduce SkillResolve-Bench 1.0 with 661 helpful/risky pairs and a reference method SkillResolve that resolves families and selects one safe representative, achieving Recall@3 0.766 and NDCG@3 0.699 while eliminating harmful sibling exposure (HSR@3=0).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agent skill libraries are becoming routable software assets where retrieved skills contribute not just instructions but scripts, resource bindings, and execution assumptions. This makes retrieval failure modes more dangerous than simple relevance mismatch — returning a skill from the right capability family but with the wrong execution assumptions (stale resources, missing preconditions, wrong procedures) can actively harm downstream execution. Existing retrieval benchmarks focus on broad relevance matching and do not capture this same-capability execution-risk failure mode.

Method: The authors construct SkillResolve-Bench 1.0 with 661 query-skill pairs, each pairing a helpful skill with a query-specific risky sibling sharing the same capability family. The benchmark includes source-role and admission evidence, cue/leakage checks, query-disjoint splits, and a 7,982-candidate pool (including 6,660 public SkillRet candidates). The SkillResolve reference method operates in three stages: (1) resolve active candidate families to identify capability groupings, (2) score query-conditioned utility using confusable library negatives and contract-profile cues, and (3) select exactly one representative from each family before constructing the final top-K list, thereby preventing multiple same-family skills from occupying ranking slots.

Result: Under the released family relation, SkillResolve achieves Recall@3 0.766 and NDCG@3 0.699 with HSR@3=0 (no harmful sibling exposure in top-3). Compared to SkillRouter, it improves Recall@3 by 0.112 and NDCG@3 by 0.165 while reducing HSR@3 from 0.693 to 0. Ablation reveals that without representative selection, HSR@3 rises to 0.236 under the same scorer, demonstrating that within-family representative choice is the key mechanism converting capability retrieval into safer procedural exposure.

Conclusion: Same-capability execution-risk retrieval is a distinct and practically important failure mode for agent skill retrieval. Representative selection within capability families is the critical mechanism for safe retrieval — it eliminates harmful sibling exposure while preserving strong relevance metrics. The SkillResolve-Bench and SkillResolve method together provide a foundation for developing and evaluating retrieval systems that are both relevant and execution-safe for agent workflows.

Abstract: Agent skill libraries are becoming routable software assets: a retrieved skill can contribute instructions, scripts, resource bindings, and execution assumptions to an agent. This makes skill retrieval more than broad relevance matching. A retriever can find the right capability family yet expose the wrong same-capability representative. We study this failure as same-capability execution-risk retrieval. Each query pairs a helpful skill with a query-specific risky sibling that shares the capability family but can lead execution toward a stale resource, missing precondition, or wrong procedure. We introduce SkillResolve-Bench 1.0, an auditable benchmark for this setting with 661 helpful/risky pairs, source-role and admission evidence, cue/leakage checks, query-disjoint splits, and a 7,982-candidate pool that includes 6,660 public SkillRet candidates. The benchmark reports helpful ranking together with harmful sibling rate (HSR@K), the top-K exposure of the risky sibling. We also provide SkillResolve, a reference method that resolves active candidate families, scores query-conditioned utility from confusable library negatives and contract-profile cues, and selects one representative from each family before the final top-K list. Under the released family relation, SkillResolve reaches Recall@3 0.766 and NDCG@3 0.699 while keeping HSR@3=0. It improves over SkillRouter by 0.112 Recall@3 and 0.165 NDCG@3 while reducing HSR@3 from 0.693 to 0. Without representative selection, HSR@3 rises to 0.236 under the same scorer, identifying within-family representative choice as the mechanism that turns capability retrieval into safer procedural exposure.

</details>


### [92] [Selection, Not Salience: The Shape and Limits of Personalization in Social Highlighting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10398)
*Kazuki Nakayashiki,Keisuke Watanabe*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: This paper investigates the extent and limits of personalization in reading behavior across different "altitudes" (document selection, span selection, sentence highlighting). Using a social web highlighter with a co-readership identity control, it finds a modest but significant personalization signal at the document/selection level (~+0.13) that is largely topic-driven, but no reliable personalization gain at the salience (sentence highlighting) level — even frontier LLMs fail to improve on a lead baseline, and personal re-ranking underperforms salience ordering. The authors also uncover a control-in-negatives bias that artificially inflated earlier results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
