Motivation: To rigorously quantify where and how much personalization actually helps in reading contexts, and to identify its limits — moving beyond upper-bound estimates from next-document prediction to a clean, identity-controlled, leakage-free measurement across multiple granularities of reading.

Method: Using a social web highlighter platform, the authors employ a co-readership identity control: the same document highlighted by multiple users, holding document and topic fixed, to test whether a person's own history predicts their marks better than another reader's. They measure personalization at three altitudes: (1) document selection — own-vs-other classification with community and topic-matched hard negatives; (2) span selection — replicating prior work; (3) sentence highlighting — a two-stage pipeline where an impersonal model proposes candidates and a personal model re-ranks, tested against zero-shot LLMs (including a frontier model) and a lead baseline. They also audit a control-in-negatives bias.

Result: At the document altitude, the own-versus-other gap is +0.169 (community negatives) and +0.119 (topic-matched hard negatives), both highly significant, and largely thematic rather than title-driven. The selection signal is comparably modest across altitudes (+0.12 to +0.17). At the sentence altitude, two-stage personalized auto-highlight yields no improvement over the impersonal baseline; zero-shot LLMs predict highlights worse than a lead baseline; personal re-ranking is beaten by salience ordering even on high-recall candidate pools. A control-in-negatives bias was discovered that had inflated the document gap to a spurious +0.227.

Conclusion: Measurable personalization is modest (~+0.13), topic-dominated, and appears primarily at the selection layer, with no reliable gain at the salience layer. The authors suggest that going beyond the shared salience layer may be better approached by aggregating individuals rather than personalizing harder.

Abstract: Does personalizing what a reader sees pay off, and where does it stop? Using a social web highlighter and a co-readership identity control (the same document highlighted by many users, which holds document and topic fixed and asks whether a person's own history predicts their marks better than another reader's does), we map the shape and limits of personalization across reading altitudes. At the document altitude we give the clean, leakage-free, identity-controlled measurement that prior next-document evaluations could only upper-bound: a person's history identifies which documents in a co-reading neighborhood are theirs, with an own-versus-other gap of +0.169 against community negatives and +0.119 against topic-matched hard negatives (both highly significant); a content-based arm suggests the signal is not purely title-driven but is largely thematic. This is comparable to the span-level selection signal (+0.14) from our prior work: the selection signal is of comparable magnitude across altitudes (+0.12 to +0.17), most of it stable topic preference. At the sentence altitude, a two-stage personalized auto-highlight (an impersonal model proposes candidates, a personal model re-ranks them) does not improve on its impersonal baseline: two off-the-shelf zero-shot LLMs, including a frontier model, predict highlight locations worse than a lead baseline, and personal re-ranking is beaten by the salience order even on the highest-recall candidate pool, so the null is not merely a Stage-1 ceiling artifact. Measurable personalization appears primarily at the selection layer: modest (~+0.13), topic-dominated, with no reliable gain at the salience layer. We also surface a control-in-negatives bias that inflated our document gap to a spurious +0.227 until audited. Going beyond the shared salience layer may be better approached by aggregating individuals than by personalizing them harder.

</details>


### [93] [STORM: Stepwise Token Optimization with Reward-Guided Beam Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10621)
*Arthur Satouf,Giulio D'Erasmo,Yuxuan Zong,Habiboulaye Amadou Boubacar,Pablo Piantanida,Benjamin Piwowarski*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: STORM introduces stepwise token optimization with beam search for training LLM-based query rewriters via token-level retrieval rewards, enabling lexical retrievers to match dense neural models while maintaining BM25-level speed and infrastructure simplicity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dense and learned-sparse neural retrievers require costly index rebuilding when models change, while lexical retrievers (BM25) operate on stable inverted indices but suffer from vocabulary mismatch. Existing LLM query rewriting either produces retrieval-ineffective terms (prompted) or receives only delayed, sequence-level supervision (RL-trained), obscuring which terms actually improve retrieval.

Method: STORM is a self-supervised framework that trains a query rewriter using stepwise token generation guided by retrieval metrics. At each decoding step, candidate expansion tokens are scored against a BM25 index, low-reward branches are pruned via beam search, and the retrieval reward is decomposed into token-level signals. This concentrates exploration on retrieval-effective vocabulary without requiring human annotations.

Result: On TREC DL and BEIR benchmarks, STORM-tuned 0.6B–8B backbone models match or surpass competitive LLM rewriters while maintaining BM25's retrieval speed. The 8B variant rivals far larger proprietary rewriters. STORM also transfers zero-shot to 18 languages on MIRACL, outperforming dedicated multilingual dense retrievers on average.

Conclusion: STORM offers a competitive, infrastructure-light alternative to dense neural retrieval by enabling lexical retrievers to achieve near-dense performance through effective query expansion, without requiring specialized indices or model-specific reindexing.

Abstract: Modern retrieval increasingly relies on dense and learned-sparse neural models that are effective but require encoding the entire corpus into a specialized index, rebuilt whenever the model changes. Lexical retrievers like BM25 stay efficient and transparent on a standard inverted index that need not change as models evolve, but suffer from vocabulary mismatch. LLM query rewriting can help, yet prompted rewriters emit well-formed but retrieval-ineffective or harmful-terms, and training against a retrieval reward gives only delayed, sequence-level supervision that obscures which terms helped. We introduce STORM (Stepwise Token Optimization with Reward-guided beaM search), a self-supervised framework for lexical query expansion. STORM trains the rewriter through generation guided by retrieval metrics: at each step, candidate expansions are scored against the BM25 index and low-reward continuations pruned, turning the retrieval reward into a token-level signal that concentrates exploration on retrieval-effective vocabulary. Across TREC DL and BEIR, STORM lets 0.6B-8B backbones match or surpass competitive LLM rewriters while retrieving as fast as plain BM25; at 8B it rivals far larger proprietary rewriters. It further transfers zero-shot to 18 languages (MIRACL), beating dedicated multilingual dense retrievers on average, making STORM a competitive, infrastructure-light alternative to dense neural retrieval.

</details>


### [94] [Beyond Patches: Superpixel Token-based Transformers for Attribute-Specific Fashion Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10697)
*Shuili Zhang,Hongzhang Mu,Wenyuan Zhang,Duohe Ma,Tingwen Liu*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: SuperFashion introduces superpixel tokens into a Transformer architecture for attribute-specific fashion retrieval, using attribute-guided attention to crop relevant regions, segmenting them into superpixel tokens, and fusing them via modality-specific embeddings — achieving significant MAP improvements over prior SOTA on FashionAI, DARN, and DeepFashion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing patch-based attention and Transformer methods for ASFR suffer from misalignment with irregular attribute regions and sensitivity to background noise, which hampers their ability to capture subtle, pixel-level microstructures critical for fine-grained fashion retrieval.

Method: SuperFashion is a Transformer-based framework that uses attribute-guided attention to extract attribute-relevant features for cropping semantically meaningful image regions. These regions undergo superpixel segmentation to produce compact, semantically coherent superpixel tokens. Modality-specific embeddings are assigned to both attribute tokens and superpixel tokens, and the Transformer facilitates adaptive cross-modal interaction and fusion to enhance attribute localization and discrimination.

Result: On FashionAI, DARN, and DeepFashion datasets, SuperFashion achieves relative overall MAP improvements of 1.84%, 9.27%, and 9.35% respectively over prior state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: SuperFashion presents a novel solution for attribute-specific fashion retrieval by leveraging superpixel tokens within a Transformer, effectively addressing the limitations of patch-based methods, and demonstrates strong potential for web-based image retrieval applications.

Abstract: Attribute-Specific Fashion Retrieval (ASFR) aims to improve fine-grained image retrieval by focusing on specific attributes. However, existing patch-based attention and Transformer methods often misalign with irregular attribute regions and are prone to background noise, limiting their ability to capture subtle, pixel-level microstructures. To tackle these challenges, we propose SuperFashion, the first ASFR framework that adopts superpixel tokens within a Transformer architecture. SuperFashion initially employs an attribute-guided attention mechanism to extract attribute-related features, which in turn guide the cropping of semantically meaningful image regions. Superpixel segmentation is then leveraged on these regions to generate compact, semantically coherent superpixel tokens. By incorporating modality-specific embeddings for both attribute and superpixel tokens, the superpixel token-based Transformer facilitates adaptive interaction and fusion, thereby enhancing attribute localization and discrimination. Extensive experiments on FashionAI, DARN, and DeepFashion demonstrate relative overall MAP improvements of 1.84%, 9.27%, and 9.35% over prior SOTA. SuperFashion offers a new solution for web-based image retrieval.

</details>


### [95] [Effective Reinforcement Learning for Agentic Search by Recycling Zero-Variance Queries During Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10709)
*João Coelho,João Magalhães,Bruno Martins,Chenyan Xiong*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: GRPO-style training wastes rollouts on zero-variance groups (all-correct or all-incorrect). The paper shows queries dynamically flip between zero-variance and signal-bearing states during training, and proposes "query recycling" — returning zero-variance groups to a mutable pool for later resampling. A 1.7B model trained this way matches or beats 7B models on multi-hop QA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In GRPO-style LLM search agent training with outcome-only rewards, only rollout groups with mixed successes and failures provide a meaningful gradient signal. All-correct (too-easy) and all-incorrect (too-hard) groups are zero-variance and waste compute. Existing solutions treat zero-variance as static, discarding or pre-filtering such queries, but this ignores the dynamic nature of the policy during training.

Method: The authors propose "query recycling": instead of discarding zero-variance rollout groups, they are returned to a mutable pool for future resampling. This allows the effective training distribution to co-evolve with the policy. As the policy improves or drifts, previously zero-variance queries can become signal-bearing, making them valuable again.

Result: A 1.7B parameter model trained on synthetic data using query recycling achieves 66.0 average Pass@1 across seven multi-hop QA benchmarks, matching or surpassing systems with up to 7B parameters trained on benchmark-derived supervision. Analysis reveals that by end of training, recycled queries supply roughly three quarters of the effective batch, with contributions from both policy improvement recovery and policy drift.

Conclusion: Zero-variance is a dynamic, not static, property. Query recycling is a simple, effective technique that significantly improves sample efficiency in GRPO-style training by allowing the training distribution to co-evolve with the policy, enabling smaller models to compete with much larger ones.

Abstract: The use of GRPO-style algorithms has become the standard strategy for training LLM search agents under outcome-only rewards. With these algorithms, a query contributes to parameter updates only when its rollout group mixes successes and failures; all-correct (too-easy) and all-incorrect (too-hard) groups are zero-variance and waste rollout cost. Existing approaches treat zero-variance as a static property and either discard or pre-filter such groups. We hypothesize and empirically validate that queries flip between zero-variance and signal-bearing states as the policy evolves during training. Building on this intuition, we propose query recycling, which returns zero-variance groups to a mutable pool for future resampling, so that the effective training distribution co-evolves with the policy. With the proposed technique, a 1.7B parameter model trained on synthetic data can reach 66.0 average Pass@1 accross seven multi-hop QA benchmarks, matching or surpassing systems with up to 7B parameters trained on benchmark-derived supervision. Analysis of recycling patterns shows that recycled queries supply roughly three quarters of the effective batch by the end of training, with contributions split between recovery from policy improvement and policy drift.

</details>


### [96] [miniReranker: Efficient Multimodal Reranking through Visual Cache Reuse and Interaction Sparsity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10759)
*Yingqi Fan,Xuan Lu,Anhao Zhao,Junlong Tong,Ping Nie,Kai Zou,Yunpu Ma,Wei Zhang,Xiaoyu Shen*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: miniReranker proposes a vision-first multimodal reranking formulation that drastically reduces computational cost through early exit, narrow cross-segment attention, and visual token pruning, achieving <1% runtime while retaining >96% performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Point-wise MLLM rerankers suffer from repeated computation across query-document pairs, and the causal transformer architecture only allows prefix reuse via pre-caching, which existing query-first and document-first formats fail to fully exploit.

Method: A vision-first formulation is proposed to improve cache reuse and reranking. Three efficiency mechanisms are introduced: (1) early exit to reduce active model depth; (2) narrow cross-segment attention bands across limited layers to constrain inter-segment computation; and (3) embedder-guided visual token pruning to reduce token count.

Result: miniReranker reduces reranking runtime to less than 1% of the dense implementation under high-reuse settings for a single query, while preserving over 96% of the dense model's performance.

Conclusion: The vision-first formulation combined with the three efficiency designs enables near-lossless reranking performance at a fraction of the original computational cost, making MLLM-based reranking significantly more practical.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have recently shown strong potential as point-wise rerankers by directly modeling query--document relevance through next-token prediction. However, point-wise reranking suffers from substantial repeated computation across query--document pairs, while the causal structure of transformers allows only prefix segments to be reused via pre-caching. To address the misalignment of existing query-first and document-first formats with both VQA-style prompting and computation-aware reuse, we propose a \textit{vision-first} formulation that improves both cache reuse efficiency and reranking performance. However, the remaining cost is still considerable and stems from three main sources: (1) \textit{model depth}, for which we reduce active parameters via early exit; (2) \textit{cross-segment attention}, which we restrict to a narrow interaction band across a few layers; and (3) \textit{visual tokens}, where we reduce the number of tokens via embedder-guided pruning. Together, these designs form miniReranker, which reduces reranking runtime to <1% of the dense implementation under high-reuse settings for a single query, while preserving >96% of the dense model performance.

</details>


### [97] [Generative Archetype-Grounded Item Representations for Sequential Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11023)
*Yifan Li,Jiahong Liu,Xinni Zhang,Hao Chen,Yankai Chen,Wenhao Yu,Jianting Chen,Irwin King*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: GenAIR proposes a framework that uses LLMs to generate item representations based on "archetypes" (descriptions of ideal target audiences) rather than static attributes, and then aligns these semantic representations with actual user behavior via a behavioral calibration objective, achieving SOTA in sequential recommendation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current sequential recommendation models suffer from poor item representation quality. LLMs offer rich semantics but existing methods use static attribute encoding and ignore the target audience's role in defining item identity. Furthermore, there is a gap between semantic representations and actual behavioral patterns, limiting recommendation performance.

Method: GenAIR operates in two stages: (1) Generative Archetype construction — an LLM analyzes item metadata to infer a textual "Archetype" describing the item's ideal target audience, from which embeddings are extracted in a single forward pass; (2) Behavioral Calibration — a training objective that explicitly incorporates real interaction signals to adjust the embedding space structure so that semantic representations align with empirical user behavior patterns. The framework is model-agnostic and integrates with most existing sequential recommenders.

Result: Comprehensive experiments on three real-world datasets show GenAIR significantly improves the performance of various sequential recommendation models and consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: Grounding LLM-generated item representations in audience-centric archetypes and calibrating them with behavioral data effectively bridges the semantic-behavioral gap, yielding superior sequential recommendation performance with plug-and-play efficiency.

Abstract: Sequential recommendation aims to predict users' next interaction with items by analyzing their historical behavior. However, the limited quality of item representations remains a critical bottleneck. While pre-trained large language models (LLMs) can provide rich semantic representations, existing approaches only rely on static encoding of fixed attributes, overlooking the crucial role of target audiences in defining item identity. Moreover, the semantic space struggles to reflect actual user behavior, resulting in a significant gap between semantic representations and behavioral patterns. To address these limitations, we propose GenAIR, a general framework that empowers sequential recommendation with Generative Archetype-grounded Item Representations. Specifically, we first leverage an LLM to analyze item metadata and infer textual description of the Archetype, which represents the conceptual profile of the item's ideal target audience. We then extract the corresponding embeddings in a single forward pass. Further, to ground these generative archetypes in real-world behavior, we introduce a behavioral calibration objective, which explicitly incorporates behavioral signals from actual interactions. This objective adjusts the structure of the embedding space to reflect empirical patterns. GenAIR enables seamless integration with most existing models while maintaining high efficiency. Comprehensive experiments conducted on three real-world datasets demonstrate that GenAIR significantly improves the performance of various sequential recommendation models and consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baseline approaches. Implementation codes are available at https://github.com/AI-Santiago/GenAIR.

</details>


<div id='cs.CR'></div>

# cs.CR [[Back]](#toc)

### [98] [Safecloud: A Distributed, Encrypted Storage Cloud for Streaming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09870)
*Gregory Magarshak*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Safecloud is a distributed, encrypted storage and streaming network where servers never access plaintext or keys. It introduces a one-root key hierarchy, convergent content addressing for deduplication, three parallel Merkle/key/access trees, a key tree doubling as a streaming index, and a cheap proof-of-storage mechanism called Proof-of-Corruption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing distributed storage networks (e.g., Filecoin) either lack confidentiality (nodes see plaintext) or use costly proof-of-replication that is slow and provides no encryption. The authors aim to build a system where storage and routing nodes are fully untrusted—never seeing plaintext or holding keys—while still supporting deduplication, integrity verification by keyless nodes, efficient streaming with per-track/per-segment access control, and economically sound storage proofs.

Method: The system architecture comprises Drops (browser tabs storing ciphertext chunks in IndexedDB) and Jets (federated routing servers). Five core constructions are presented: (1) A one-root HKDF-based key hierarchy enabling owner–grantee derivation agreement and delegation containment; (2) Convergent content addressing producing identical ciphertext and identifiers for identical plaintext, with authenticated ciphertext binding for blind verifiability by keyless Drops; (3) Three parallel trees (Merkle hash tree, key-derivation tree, access tree) over one navigation path with Merkle-verified retrieval; (4) The key tree serving as a streaming index with O(1) per-segment key derivation, supporting independent per-track/per-segment subtrees; (5) A proof-of-storage challenge using a single signature under "chilling-effect Proof-of-Corruption," forming a zero-sum economy cheaper than Filecoin's sealing.

Result: The paper provides the full architecture, cryptographic construction, threat model, and an open-source reference implementation, clearly distinguishing implemented components from those only designed.

Conclusion: Safecloud demonstrates that a fully untrusted, encrypted, distributed storage and streaming network is feasible, combining confidentiality, integrity, deduplication, fine-grained streaming access control, and efficient economic incentives without the cost of proof-of-replication.

Abstract: We present Safecloud, a distributed, encrypted, self-pricing storage and streaming network whose storage and routing nodes never see plaintext and never hold keys. Each file is split into chunks, encrypted on the owner's device, and distributed across Drops (browser tabs storing ciphertext in IndexedDB) and Jets (federated routing servers). Only the owner, or an authorised grantee, can decrypt. We make five contributions: (1) A one-root key hierarchy: every key derives deterministically from a single root via HKDF, and owner and range-scoped grantee derive identical chunk keys (derivation agreement); a subtree key derives its range and nothing else (delegation containment). (2) Convergent content addressing: identical content yields identical ciphertext and identifiers, enabling deduplication without plaintext exposure, with identifiers binding authenticated ciphertext so a keyless Drop verifies integrity (blind verifiability). (3) Three parallel trees over one navigation path (Merkle for integrity, key-derivation for confidentiality, access for authorisation), with sound Merkle-verified retrieval. (4) The key tree doubles as a streaming index: a player derives each segment key in O(1), seeking by derivation, while parallel tracks (video, audio, captions) are independent subtrees unlockable per-track and per-segment, a combination we believe no prior encrypted-storage network offers. (5) Jets and Drops earn Safebux verifiably, kept honest by a one-signature proof-of-storage challenge under chilling-effect Proof-of-Corruption, a zero-sum economy that is significantly cheaper than Filecoin's proof-of-replication sealing (which is slow and provides no confidentiality). We give the architecture, cryptographic construction, a threat model, and an open-source reference implementation, stating precisely what is implemented versus designed.

</details>


### [99] [IDP-Bench: Benchmarking ability of LLMs to protect personal information in interdependent privacy contexts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09908)
*Ayana Hussain,Soumya Sharma,Golnoosh Farnadi,Nicholas Vincent,Héber Hwang Arcolezi,Ulrich Aïvodji*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Introduces IDP-Bench, the first benchmark for evaluating LLMs on interdependent privacy scenarios. Eight open-source LLMs are tested: most recognize co-ownership of data well (>90% for 6/8 models) but struggle with nuanced contextual integrity parameters (secondary subjects, information attributes) and judging sharing appropriateness, with performance declining in smaller models and high prompt sensitivity on IDP-specific tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly deployed as personal AI assistants with access to sensitive user data. Prior privacy research focuses on individual-level risks, neglecting interdependent privacy (IDP) — where one person's data can be exposed by another's actions without consent. This gap motivates a systematic benchmark grounded in the Contextual Integrity framework.

Method: Authors construct IDP-Bench using the Contextual Integrity (CI) framework, evaluating eight open-source LLMs across three levels of IDP reasoning. Two LLM judges assess model performance on: (1) recognizing co-ownership of information, (2) identifying CI parameters (information attribute, primary subject, secondary subjects), and (3) judging the appropriateness of information sharing in IDP scenarios.

Result: Six of eight models exceed 90% on co-ownership recognition. However, 7/8 models score below 74% on identifying secondary subjects and other CI parameters. Five of eight models score below 77% on judging sharing appropriateness. Performance on appropriateness judgments improves with model scale but declines in smaller models. Prompt sensitivity remains high on IDP-specific questions.

Conclusion: Current LLMs exhibit a significant gap between recognizing data co-ownership and performing nuanced IDP reasoning (parameter identification, appropriateness judgments). Scaling helps only partially. The findings call for targeted research on interdependent privacy in LLMs, particularly around CI-aware reasoning and robustness to prompt variation.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are becoming widely deployed as personal AI assistants with access to sensitive user data, making privacy a major challenge for their design and evaluation. Prior work focuses mainly on individual-level risks, overlooking \textbf{interdependent privacy (IDP)}--where one person's data may be revealed by others without their knowledge or consent. We address this gap by introducing \textbf{IDP-Bench}: the first LLM benchmark for IDP scenarios, grounded in the Contextual Integrity (CI) framework. We evaluate eight open-source LLMs on their understanding of IDP scenarios across three levels of IDP reasoning using two LLM judges. Results show strong co-ownership recognition (6/8 models exceed 90\%) but persistent weaknesses in identifying CI parameters (information attribute, primary subject) and IDP-specific parameters such as secondary subjects, where 7/8 models score below 74\%. Models also struggle to judge sharing appropriateness (5/8 scoring below 77\%). While the ability to judge the appropriateness of sharing improves with scale, performance tends to decline in smaller models, and prompt sensitivity remains high on IDP-specific questions--highlighting the need for more targeted study of IDP in LLM privacy research. Data \& code available \href{https://github.com/tisl-lab/Interdependent_Privacy_Bench}{here}.

</details>


### [100] [Bypassing Copyright Protection in Diffusion-based Customization via Two-Stage Latent Feature Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09909)
*Ziang Xu,Wenbo Yu,Hongyao Yu,Hao Fang,Jiawei Kong,Bin Chen,Hao Wu,Shu-Tao Xia,Zhiyong Wu*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: TS-LFO is a two-stage attack that restores the broken image-to-latent mapping in protected LDMs, effectively bypassing adversarial copyright defenses and outperforming existing attacks like DiffPure, GrIDPure, and IMPRESS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing copyright defenses for diffusion-based customization introduce persistent latent-space perturbations that disrupt the input-to-latent mapping. However, these defenses remain vulnerable to adaptive attacks that restore this mapping. The authors aim to demonstrate that current defenses are insufficient by proposing a stronger attack.

Method: TS-LFO employs a two-stage optimization: (1) Latent Denoising Stage — jointly minimizes a Latent-Image Alignment Loss and a Latent Diffusion Loss with timestep-dependent weights to suppress high-frequency defensive noise while enhancing semantic consistency; (2) Latent Reconstruction Stage — applies pixel-level constraints to recover low-frequency semantic information and refine latent features.

Result: TS-LFO consistently bypasses state-of-the-art copyright defenses and outperforms SOTA attacks (DiffPure, GrIDPure, IMPRESS) across diverse experimental settings.

Conclusion: Current adversarial perturbation-based copyright defenses for diffusion customization are insufficient against adaptive two-stage optimization attacks like TS-LFO, highlighting the need for more robust protection mechanisms.

Abstract: With the growing concerns over copyright infringement in diffusion-based customization, adversarial attacks have emerged as a prominent defense strategy to prevent malicious content forgery in personalized image generation. However, current defenses typically introduce persistent perturbations in the latent space of Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs), which remain susceptible to adaptive bypasses by adversaries. In this paper, we introduce Two-Stage Latent Feature Optimization (TS-LFO), an efficient and effective copyright-stealing attack against protected diffusion-based customization. We begin by observing that existing defenses primarily disrupt the mapping between input images and their latent representations, thereby degrading the model's ability to produce personalized outputs. To counteract this, TS-LFO restores the broken mapping through a two-stage optimization process. In the Latent Denoising Stage, we enhance semantic consistency between latent codes and input images by jointly minimizing a Latent-Image Alignment Loss and a Latent Diffusion Loss with timestep-dependent weights, effectively suppressing the high-frequency noise introduced by defenses. In the Latent Reconstruction Stage, we recover low-frequency semantic information using pixel-level constraints to refine the latent features. Extensive experiments show that TS-LFO consistently bypasses state-of-the-art (SOTA) copyright defenses and outperforms SOTA copyright attacks such as DiffPure, GrIDPure and IMPRESS across diverse settings.

</details>


### [101] [GitInject: Real-World Prompt Injection Attacks in AI-Powered CI/CD Pipelines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09935)
*Jafar Isbarov,Umid Suleymanov,Ilia Shumailov,Murat Kantarcioglu*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: GitInject is an open-source framework for evaluating prompt injection vulnerabilities in AI agents within real GitHub CI/CD workflows. It provisions ephemeral repositories to test actual production behavior, revealing that all four tested AI providers are susceptible to at least one attack class, with the most critical vulnerabilities being structural (CI/CD infrastructure) rather than model-specific.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI agents in CI/CD pipelines ingest untrusted content (PRs, issues) while holding elevated repository permissions, creating a high-risk attack surface for prompt injection with supply chain implications. Existing agent security benchmarks rely on simulated tool calls rather than real production environments, failing to capture sandbox constraints, credential handling, and permission boundaries accurately.

Method: GitInject provisions ephemeral GitHub repositories and triggers actual CI/CD workflow runs to test prompt injection vulnerabilities in live environments. The framework was used to study workflow configurations across four AI providers, systematically documenting eleven named attack classes including config-file injection, credential exfiltration, judgment manipulation, and availability attacks.

Result: All four tested AI providers were susceptible to at least one attack class under default configurations. The most critical vulnerabilities were structural — stemming from CI/CD infrastructure's handling of credentials and configuration files — not from any specific model's behavior. For each confirmed attack class, minimum-cost workflow-level countermeasures were identified along with their coverage and limitations.

Conclusion: The structural nature of the discovered vulnerabilities indicates that securing AI agents in CI/CD requires infrastructure-level hardening rather than solely model-level defenses. GitInject is publicly released to enable further research into prompt injection vulnerabilities in production CI/CD environments.

Abstract: AI-powered agents are increasingly embedded in continuous integration and continuous delivery/deployment (CI/CD) pipelines to autonomously review pull requests (PRs), triage issues, and maintain codebases. These agents ingest untrusted content while operating with elevated repository permissions, making them a natural target for prompt injection attacks with supply chain consequences. We present GitInject, an open-source framework for evaluating prompt injection vulnerabilities in real, live GitHub workflows, a widely deployed instance of CI/CD pipelines. Unlike prior agent security benchmarks that simulate tool calls, GitInject provisions ephemeral repositories and triggers actual workflow runs, so that sandbox constraints, credential handling, and permission boundaries behave exactly as in production. Using GitInject, we study workflow configurations across four AI providers and document eleven named attacks spanning config-file injection, credential exfiltration, judgment manipulation, and availability. We find that all tested providers are susceptible to at least one attack class in their default configuration, and that the most critical vulnerabilities are structural: they arise from how CI/CD infrastructure handles credentials and configuration files, not from any specific model's behavior. For each confirmed attack class, we identify the minimum-cost workflow-level countermeasure and analyze its coverage and limitations. GitInject is released publicly to facilitate further research in this direction.

</details>


### [102] [The Chronicles of Radio Frequency Fingerprinting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10031)
*Abdul Aziz,Ingrid Huso,Savio Sciancalepore,Gabriele Oligeri*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: A critical historical analysis of Radio Frequency Fingerprinting (RFF) from 1993–2026, organized around major conceptual paradigm shifts: transient-based approaches, digital communications/steady-state features, machine learning standardization, and deep learning with raw IQ samples. The paper examines changing assumptions, persistent limitations (channel dependence, dataset realism, generalization, open-set recognition, adversarial robustness), and outlines directions for real-world deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rather than a conventional survey, the authors aim to provide a critical historical analysis that reveals the field's conceptual paradigm shifts, changing assumptions, and persistent limitations that continue to impede dependable real-world adoption of RFF technology.

Method: The paper organizes over three decades of RFF research (1993–2026) into four distinct methodological phases: (1) early transient-based approaches exploiting turn-on behavior and hardware nonlinearities; (2) transition to digital communications with steady-state impairments and engineered features; (3) the Machine Learning period standardizing feature extraction, dimensionality reduction, and supervised classification; and (4) the Deep Learning period enabling representation learning directly from raw IQ samples. The analysis critically examines assumptions and limitations across these phases rather than merely cataloging methods.

Result: The analysis identifies persistent challenges including channel dependence, receiver sensitivity, limited dataset realism, poor cross-domain generalization, open-set recognition difficulties, and adversarial robustness vulnerabilities. It clarifies how assumptions have shifted across methodological phases and why certain limitations have resisted resolution.

Conclusion: RFF has matured through distinct paradigm shifts, but central challenges remain unresolved. Moving toward dependable real-world adoption requires addressing the identified persistent limitations. The paper provides a coherent narrative that clarifies the field's evolution and outlines key future research directions.

Abstract: Radio Frequency Fingerprinting (RFF) has evolved from an early idea for radar emitter identification into a broad research field for wireless device identification and spectrum monitoring for security. Rather than presenting a conventional literature survey, this work provides a critical historical analysis of RFF organized around the field's major conceptual paradigm shifts from 1993 to 2026. We discuss the evolution of RFF across its fundamental methodological phases, beginning with early transient-based approaches, in which transmitter turn-on behavior, unintentional modulation, and hardware nonlinearities were treated as the primary fingerprint sources. We then examine the transition to digital communications, during which attention shifted to steady-state impairments and to engineered features extracted from signals. Next, we discuss the Machine Learning period, which standardized the RFF workflow around feature extraction, dimensionality reduction, and supervised classification, followed by the Deep Learning period, in which representation learning from raw IQ samples significantly improved performance and expanded the application space. Beyond a chronological list of methods and best practices, this paper critically examines the changing assumptions and persistent limitations that have driven these transitions. We highlight the central challenges that continue to shape the field, including channel dependence, receiver sensitivity, limited dataset realism, poor cross-domain generalization, open-set recognition, and adversarial robustness. By organizing more than three decades of work into a coherent narrative, this paper clarifies the evolution of RFF, identifies persistent limitations, and outlines the key research directions required to move the field toward dependable real-world adoption.

</details>


### [103] [The Human Vulnerabilities & Exploits (HVE) Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10083)
*Avichai Ben,Tom Rahav,Daniel Illaev,Aviv Nahon,Avi Grushka*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Proposes the Human Vulnerabilities & Exploits (HVE) Framework — a standardized taxonomy, scoring system (HVSS), and remediation approach (HVP) for behavioral and psychological vulnerabilities exploited in scams and social engineering, analogous to CVE/CVSS for software flaws.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite decades of investment in software vulnerability frameworks (CVE, CVSS, CWE), most successful cyberattacks exploit human behavioral and psychological vulnerabilities through social engineering, fraud, and scams. No equivalent standardized framework exists for classifying and mitigating these human-centric attack vectors.

Method: Synthesizes literature across cybersecurity standardization, behavioral science (dual-process theory, prospect theory, social influence frameworks, visceral state models), and fraud defense to establish theoretical foundations for the HVE Framework, which introduces structured identifiers, multi-dimensional severity scoring (HVSS), and actionable remediation guidance (HVP).

Result: The paper delivers the conceptual architecture of the HVE Framework as a machine-readable taxonomy with structured identifiers, a Human Vulnerability Severity Score (HVSS) for multi-dimensional severity assessment, and Human Vulnerability Patches (HVPs) for remediation — mirroring the CVE/CVSS ecosystem for human vulnerabilities.

Conclusion: A structured, standardized framework for human vulnerabilities is both theoretically grounded in established behavioral science and practically necessary to address the dominant attack vector in modern cybersecurity. The HVE Framework provides this missing infrastructure.

Abstract: The cybersecurity community has invested over two decades in building standardized frameworks, the Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE) system, the Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS), and the Common Weakness Enumeration (CWE) to identify, classify, and remediate threats to digital infrastructure. However, an emerging body of research reveals that a vast majority of successful cyberattacks exploit not software flaws, but human behavioral and psychological vulnerabilities. Social engineering, fraud, and scam attacks, which manipulate human cognition, emotion, and trust, do not have an equivalent standardized framework. Meanwhile, behavioral science and psychology research has established robust theoretical foundations, such as dual-process theory, prospect theory, social influence frameworks, and visceral state models, which explain precisely why and how these attacks succeed. This paper introduces the Human Vulnerabilities & Exploits (HVE) Framework, a structured approach for identifying, classifying, and mitigating the behavioral and psychological vulnerabilities exploited in scams, social engineering, and other human-centric fraud and attacks, analogous in concept to how CVE helps classify software vulnerabilities: it provides a shared, machine-readable taxonomy with structured identifiers, multi-dimensional severity scoring via the Human Vulnerability Severity Score (HVSS), and actionable remediation guidance through Human Vulnerability Patches (HVPs). This introduction synthesizes the relevant literature across cybersecurity standardization, behavioral science, and fraud defense to establish the theoretical and practical foundations for the HVE framework, whose architecture and technical specifications are detailed in subsequent sections.

</details>


### [104] [SoK: Colluding Adversaries in Machine Learning Pipelines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10091)
*Vasisht Duddu,Lipeng He,Asim Waheed,N. Asokan*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper presents a systematic framework for analyzing collusion among adversaries in machine learning, categorizing collusion types between train-time and inference-time adversaries and among inference-time adversaries, and introducing enabling factors to predict and validate unexplored attack collusions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ML models face diverse security, privacy, and fairness threats, yet existing research lacks a systematic framework to understand how adversaries with different objectives, knowledge, and capabilities can collude to amplify attacks. The absence of structured analysis limits the ability to anticipate and defend against compound threats.

Method: The authors propose a taxonomy-based framework that categorizes collusion into two main types: (a) between train-time and inference-time adversaries, and (b) among inference-time adversaries. They define enabling factors that facilitate collusion and develop a guideline to conjecture potential collusions based on these factors. The framework is validated by explaining prior work, hypothesizing unexplored collusions, and empirically testing five novel cases.

Result: The framework successfully explains existing collusion attacks in the literature and enables the conjecture of previously unexplored collusions. Empirical validation of five such conjectured cases confirms the framework's predictive utility. The analysis also reveals how specific adversary characteristics (objectives, knowledge, capabilities) influence collusion potential.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a structured lens to systematically reason about adversary collusion in ML systems. Understanding enabling factors and adversary characteristics is critical for anticipating compound attacks and designing robust defenses against coordinated adversarial strategies.

Abstract: Machine learning (ML) models are susceptible to various security, privacy, and fairness risks. Adversaries with different characteristics (i.e., objectives, knowledge, and capabilities) can collude by executing one attack to amplify others. Existing work lacks a systematic framework to explore collusion among adversaries, and to study the implications of the adversaries' characteristics. We present a framework covering collusion (a) between train- and inference-time adversaries, and (b) among inference-time adversaries. Our framework accounts for factors enabling collusion between adversaries. We propose a guideline to conjecture about the potential for collusion using enabling factors. We use it to explain prior work, conjecture about unexplored collusions, and empirically validate five such cases. Finally, we discuss how adversaries' characteristics influence the potential for collusion.

</details>


### [105] [Secrets Best Not Shared: DNS Privacy Enhancements for the Constrained IoT](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10097)
*Martine S. Lenders,Thomas C. Schmidt,Matthias Wählisch*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper empirically analyzes DNS traffic obfuscation for IoT devices using DNS over CoAP (with optional onion routing), comparing it against DNS over HTTPS. Using Random Forest classifiers and header field analysis, the authors find that DNS over CoAP with equalized packet lengths, block-wise transfer, and header/payload compression reduces DNS frame identification accuracy to 77–86%, outperforming DNS over HTTPS where classifiers always succeed based on IP addresses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Attackers can identify and target DNS traffic to disrupt Internet services. While prior work encrypts DNS queries via DoT, DoH, or DoQ, encryption alone may not suffice against traffic analysis. IoT devices, being resource-constrained, require IETF protocols like CoAP, yet the potential for obfuscating DNS traffic in such settings remains unexplored. The paper aims to empirically evaluate whether DNS over CoAP can obscure DNS traffic patterns better than DNS over HTTPS.

Method: The authors create a dataset of machine-to-machine-compatible data objects paired with DNS resolution processes. They evaluate 296 deployment scenarios involving DNS over CoAP (and an onion-routing variant of CoAP) under varying link-layer conditions, comparing them to DNS over HTTPS. They apply Random Forest classifiers and header field analysis to identify which packet fields leak the most identifying information.

Result: DNS over CoAP with equalized packet lengths, block-wise transfer, and header compression reduces DNS frame classification accuracy to 86%, and further to 77% when payload compression is added. In contrast, DNS over HTTPS frames are always identified by classifiers based on IP addresses.

Conclusion: DNS over CoAP offers superior obfuscation compared to DNS over HTTPS in IoT scenarios when combined with length equalization, block-wise transfer, and compression techniques. The dataset is made publicly available to support further research.

Abstract: Attackers often identify DNS traffic to disrupt or compromise Internet services. While prior work has focused on encrypting queries using DNS over TLS, HTTPS, or QUIC to counter such attacks, we consider IETF protocols designed for resource-constrained IoT devices and empirically analyze the potential of obfuscating DNS traffic in addition to encryption. We create a dataset of machine-to-machine-compatible data objects along with the corresponding DNS resolution processes, evaluating 296 deployment scenarios of resolving host names, including DNS over the Constrained Application Layer Protocol (CoAP) and an onion routing flavor of CoAP under varying link-layer conditions. We compare them to DNS over HTTPS. Using Random Forest and a header field analysis, we identify fields that leak most information. Our findings show that DNS over CoAP with equalized packet lengths, block-wise transfer, and header compression reduces the accuracy of identifying DNS frames to 86% and further to 77% with payload compression. Our approach outperforms DNS over HTTPS, where classifiers always identify DNS frames based on IP addresses. The dataset is publicly available.

</details>


### [106] [RadKey: An LLM-Guided RF Backscatter System for Through-Wall Keystroke Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10148)
*Qijun Wang,Chunqi Qian,Huacheng Zeng*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: RadKey is an RF backscatter system enabling covert, long-range, through-wall keystroke eavesdropping using a batteryless tag that captures vibrations/acoustics and an RF reader with an LLM-enhanced classification pipeline, achieving robust, user-independent keystroke inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing keystroke side-channel attacks rely on conspicuous short-range sensors (acoustic, vibration) and require victim-specific training, limiting practicality, scalability, and stealth for real-world eavesdropping scenarios.

Method: RadKey uses a batteryless backscatter tag with two magnetically-coupled LC resonators to capture keystroke-induced vibrations and acoustics, modulating them onto the frequency shift of backscattered RF signals. Spectral separation between excitation and backscatter mitigates self-interference, extending range. The RF reader employs time-frequency domain feature extraction for user/keyboard-independent classification, plus an LLM-based online adaptation mechanism that uses LLM outputs as pseudo ground-truth labels for runtime classifier refinement.

Result: Extensive over-the-air experiments demonstrate accurate and robust keystroke inference across diverse users in real-world settings.

Conclusion: RadKey demonstrates a practical, stealthy, and scalable keystroke eavesdropping attack combining RF backscatter with LLM-driven adaptation, highlighting both a novel threat vector and the need for countermeasures against such long-range through-wall side-channel attacks.

Abstract: In today's digitally connected world, keyboards remain the primary interface for inputting sensitive information, making them a persistent target for eavesdropping attacks. While prior keystroke inference techniques have exploited side-channel signals such as acoustics and vibrations, they typically rely on conspicuous, short-range sensors and require victim-specific data for model training, limiting their practicality, scalability, and stealth. In this paper, we present RadKey, an RF backscatter system for covert, long-range, through-wall keystroke eavesdropping. RadKey comprises two components: a compact batteryless backscatter tag and an RF reader. The tag captures keystroke-induced vibrations and acoustic signals, modulating them onto the frequency shift of its backscattered RF signal using two magnetically-coupled LC resonators. This design also enables spectral separation between the excitation and backscatter signals, mitigating self-interference for the RF reader and thus extending eavesdropping range. The RF reader demodulates the backscattered RF signal to infer typed content. It employs a dedicated signal processing pipeline that extracts user- and keyboard-independent keystroke features across time and frequency domains, enabling strong generalizability. To further enhance adaptability, RadKey integrates an LLM for online adaptation, leveraging LLM outputs as pseudo ground-truth labels to refine the classifier during runtime. We have built a prototype of the full RadKey system and evaluated it through extensive over-the-air experiments. Results show that RadKey achieves accurate and robust keystroke inference across diverse users in real-world settings. A demo video is available at: https://radkey-submission.github.io/RadKey/

</details>


### [107] [GRAFT: Graphlet-Triggered Backdoor Attack on GNN-Based Hardware Security Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10163)
*Sanaz Kazemi Abharian,Sai Manoj Pudukotai Dinakarrao*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: GRAFT is a backdoor attack on GNN-based hardware security that uses graphlet-based triggers at RTL or gate level, preserving circuit functionality while achieving up to 100% attack success rate against HT detection and IP piracy detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GNNs are increasingly used for hardware security (HT detection, IP piracy detection), but they are vulnerable to backdoor attacks. Existing backdoor triggers (random subgraphs, gradient-guided generative subgraphs) are impractical for hardware security because they fail to preserve circuit functionality — a non-negotiable constraint in IC design.

Method: The authors propose GRAFT, which embeds graphlet-based backdoor triggers at either the register-transfer level (RTL) or gate level of circuit designs. The key innovation is that these graphlet triggers are designed to preserve the original circuit functionality, making them stealthy in the hardware security context. Evaluation is conducted on ISCAS-85 and TrustHub benchmark datasets.

Result: GRAFT effectively evades both hardware Trojan (HT) detection and IP piracy detection. It achieves an attack success rate (ASR) of up to 100% on the evaluated benchmarks.

Conclusion: GRAFT demonstrates a practical and highly effective backdoor attack vector for GNN-based hardware security systems, highlighting a critical vulnerability where trigger stealthiness is achieved through circuit functionality preservation. This motivates the need for robust defenses in hardware security GNNs.

Abstract: The globalization of the integrated circuit (IC) supply chain increases the risk of security threats, such as hardware Trojans (HTs) and the theft of intellectual property (IP). Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), among the most powerful deep learning methods for processing graph-structured data, have been widely adopted to detect such threats. However, GNNs are susceptible to backdoor attacks that can maliciously manipulate output predictions toward an adversarial target. These attacks are not only difficult to detect but also compromise the integrity of GNN-based security systems. Most prior work embeds backdoor triggers using randomly generated subgraphs or gradient-guided generative subgraphs. However, such triggers are impractical for GNN-based hardware security applications as they do not guarantee the preservation of circuit functionality. In this paper, we propose GRAFT, a graph let-triggered backdoor attack targeting GNN-based hardware security. GRAFT embeds graphlet-based triggers at either the register-transfer level (RTL) or gate level of the design while preserving the circuit 's original function. We evaluate GRAFT on the ISCAS-85 and TrustHub datasets. Our experimental results demonstrate that GRAFT can effectively evade HT detection and IP piracy detection, achieving an attack success rate (ASR) of up to 100%.

</details>


### [108] [Proof of Source of Funds: Efficient On-chain Provenance of Cryptoassets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10172)
*Alireza Kavousi,István András Seres,Zhipeng Wang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: A cryptographic framework called Proof of Source of Funds (PoSoF) that shifts regulatory compliance burden from platforms to users via zero-knowledge proofs, enabling private, O(1)-verification provenance of funds without revealing transaction topology or addresses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current regulatory compliance in DeFi relies on centralized blockchain intelligence firms performing retroactive graph analysis or binary inclusion/exclusion lists, which destroys financial privacy for honest users, produces false positives/negatives, and burdens decentralized platforms with on-chain monitoring duties.

Method: PoSoF introduces a unified temporal DAG abstraction covering both UTXO and account-based ledger histories. Users locally extract a compliant sub-DAG of their transaction history and apply Incrementally Verifiable Computation (IVC) with zero-knowledge proofs to prove state-transition predicates, demonstrating funds originate exclusively from compliant sources without revealing intermediate topology, addresses, or specific origins.

Result: An Ethereum-compatible prototype shows incremental PoSoF updates take ~1.8 seconds per new transaction, with constant-time on-chain EVM verification requiring ~1.5 ms (~800k gas).

Conclusion: Fully private, proactive compliance is highly practical. PoSoF enables a paradigm shift from platform-side surveillance to user-side cryptographic provenance, preserving privacy while satisfying regulatory requirements.

Abstract: Regulatory compliance is increasingly mandatory for decentralized finance and privacy-enhancing technologies. Current approaches rely on binary inclusion/exclusion lists or retroactive graph analysis by centralized blockchain intelligence firms. This approach strips honest users of their financial privacy, leads to false positives and negatives, and forces decentralized platforms to bear the burden of on-chain transaction monitoring. In this work, we propose a paradigm shift: moving from platform-side surveillance to user-side provenance. We introduce Proof of Source of Funds (PoSoF), a novel cryptographic framework that shifts the burden to the user. Rather than the platform tracing funds, the user locally generates a zero-knowledge proof demonstrating that their deposit originates exclusively from a set of compliant sources. The platform is thus relieved of chain-analysis duties, requiring a constant-time, O(1) verification to enforce admission control.
  We formulate a unified temporal Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) abstraction that formalizes both UTXO and account-based ledger histories within a generalized value-flow model. Users extract a compliant sub-DAG of their transaction history and utilize Incrementally Verifiable Computation (IVC) to prove rigorous state-transition predicates that protect against various attack vectors. Crucially, PoSoF provides verifiable cryptographic provenance; it guarantees the legitimacy of the funds without leaking the intermediate transaction topology, intermediary addresses, or the specific origins utilized. We formally define the security properties of PoSoF and evaluate an Ethereum-compatible prototype. Our benchmarks demonstrate that fully private, proactive compliance is highly practical, requiring only ~1.8 s to incrementally update a user's PoSoF per new transaction, and a constant-time ~1.5 ms (~800k gas) for final on-chain EVM verification.

</details>


### [109] [Game-Theoretic Multi-Agent Control for Robust Contextual Reasoning in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10322)
*Saeid Jamshidi,Amin Nikanjam,Arghavan Moradi Dakhel,Kawser Wazed Nafi,Foutse Khomh*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: GT-MCP is a controller-driven multi-agent framework that treats context management in multi-turn LLM interactions as a closed-loop dynamical process, using game-theoretic selection, causal consistency checks, and rollback-based self-healing to bound contextual drift to 99.6% of turns under adversarial attack.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM defenses focus on filtering individual outputs and ignore how locally plausible adversarial fragments can gradually poison reasoning across multi-turn interactions. While MCP standardizes context exchange, it is a passive routing layer with no enforcement of contextual stability. A proactive, dynamics-aware defense is needed for long-horizon reasoning safety.

Method: GT-MCP coordinates three heterogeneous LLM agents via a controller that selects outputs using a trust function evaluating: (1) causal consistency against a validated context graph, (2) semantic agreement among agents, and (3) distributional drift over time. When instability is detected, a rollback-based self-healing mechanism restores the validated context, preventing unsupported fragment propagation.

Result: Over 500 interaction turns under an adaptive adversarial threat model: contextual drift bounded in 99.6% of turns, recovery required in only 0.4% of turns. Median per-turn utility = -0.19, P05 = -0.72, P95 = 0.30; severe degradation (< -1) in 0.4% of cases. No injection attempt succeeded at the controller level. Win rates above 98%, per-token latency = 1.63e-3 s.

Conclusion: GT-MCP demonstrates that game-theoretic, dynamics-aware context management with heterogeneous agents and self-healing rollbacks can effectively neutralize prompt-injection and context-poisoning attacks in multi-turn LLM interactions, with bounded drift, high utility retention, and predictable computational cost.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) in multi-turn interactions maintain evolving context rather than generating isolated responses, making them vulnerable to prompt-injection and context-poisoning attacks in which locally plausible adversarial fragments gradually distort reasoning trajectories. Existing defenses mainly filter individual outputs and often ignore context evolution across turns, leaving long-horizon reasoning exposed. Although the Model Context Protocol (MCP) standardizes context exchange and tool invocation, it functions as a passive routing layer and does not enforce contextual stability. To address these limitations, we introduce the Game-Theoretic Secure Model Context Protocol (GT-MCP), a controller-driven multi-agent method that treats context management as a closed-loop dynamical process. GT-MCP coordinates three heterogeneous LLM agents and selects outputs through a trust function that jointly evaluates causal consistency against a validated context graph, semantic agreement among agents, and distributional drift over time. When instability is detected, a rollback-based self-healing mechanism restores the validated context and prevents unsupported fragments from propagating. Empirical evaluation over 500 interaction turns under an adaptive adversarial threat model shows that contextual drift remains bounded in 99.6% of turns, with recovery required in only 0.4%. Per-turn utility remains tightly concentrated, with median = -0.19, P05 = -0.72, and P95 = 0.30; severe degradation below -1 occurs in only 0.4% of cases, and no injection attempt succeeds at the controller level. Selected outputs maintain stable win rates above 98%, and computational overhead remains predictable, with latency per token = 1.63e-3 s.

</details>


### [110] [Local Is Not a Sufficient Privacy Boundary: Governing OS-Integrated On-Device AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10173)
*Jonghyun Chung,Sanket Badhe*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper argues that local AI execution alone is insufficient for privacy. It proposes an OS-centered privacy framework treating privacy as institutional accountability, featuring a threat model, taxonomy, controls, and an audit rubric. The framework is demonstrated by comparing Apple Intelligence, Android AICore, and Microsoft Recall, concluding that meaningful on-device AI privacy requires constrained information flow, bounded authority, visible user control, and auditable governance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing privacy discourse around on-device AI focuses narrowly on whether inference runs locally versus in the cloud. This binary framing ignores broader systemic risks: local assistants aggregate cross-application data (email, calendar, files, notifications), retain derived state (embeddings, summaries), invoke tools with ambient authority, emit telemetry, and may route sensitive requests to cloud infrastructure. The authors argue that privacy must be reframed as an institutional accountability problem spanning the entire OS lifecycle, not merely a deployment-location attribute.

Method: The paper develops a four-component framework: (1) a threat model for on-device AI assistants at the OS level; (2) a six-part privacy risk taxonomy; (3) privacy-by-architecture controls embedded in OS design; and (4) a four-level audit rubric for evaluating AI-OS privacy posture. The rubric is applied through a documentation-bounded comparative analysis of three real-world systems: Apple Intelligence/Foundation Models, Android AICore/Gemini Nano, and Microsoft Recall.

Result: The documentation-bounded comparison reveals substantial variation in privacy posture across the three platforms. Key findings include that local execution does not guarantee privacy; data assembly breadth, persistence of derived state, tool invocation authority, and cloud routing policies are critical differentiators. The rubric surfaces gaps in each system's controls, such as unclear retention policies for embeddings, ambiguous user consent for cross-app data fusion, and insufficient auditability of automated actions taken by on-device models.

Conclusion: Meaningful privacy in on-device AI cannot be reduced to a local-vs-cloud decision. It requires constrained information flow across application boundaries, bounded authority for model-invoked actions, visible and granular user control, and auditable governance mechanisms that persist through OS updates and model changes. The proposed framework provides a systematic lens for evaluating and designing privacy-respecting AI-augmented operating systems.

Abstract: As AI systems move into operating systems, privacy no longer turns only on whether a model runs locally. A local assistant may assemble email, calendar entries, files, screenshots, notifications, and app intents; retain embeddings or summaries; invoke tools; emit telemetry; or route difficult requests to cloud infrastructure. Local inference reduces some exposure, but it answers only one question: where computation occurs. It does not answer who may assemble context, what derived state persists, which actions are authorized, or how updates change the system's authority. We develop an OS-centered privacy framework for on-device AI that treats privacy as an institutional accountability problem rather than a deployment attribute. The framework specifies a threat model, a six-part privacy risk taxonomy, privacy-by-architecture controls, and a four-level audit rubric. We demonstrate the rubric through a documentation-bounded comparison of Apple Intelligence/Foundation Models, Android AICore/Gemini Nano, and Microsoft Recall. Meaningful privacy in on-device AI depends on constrained information flow, bounded authority, visible user control, and auditable governance across the operating-system lifecycle.

</details>


### [111] [RECON: An LLM-Enhanced Backward Constraint Analysis Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10264)
*Babangida Bappah,Lamine Noureddine,Umar Farooq,Aisha Ali-Gombe*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: RECON is an LLM-enhanced backward constraint analysis framework that combines static program analysis with LLM semantic reasoning to extract precise execution constraints from Android bytecode. It achieves 5.8× speedup over traditional symbolic execution with 100% success rate, and demonstrates 84% success in malware analysis for generating semantic constraints leading to dangerous API execution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional symbolic execution struggles to scale to modern software systems due to path explosion, the need for function modeling, and loss of semantic intent at low-level representations. These issues are amplified in Android environments with their extensive framework interactions and event-driven behavior. There is a need for an approach that combines the precision of static analysis with the semantic understanding capabilities of LLMs to overcome these limitations.

Method: RECON performs backward path discovery from target methods to application entry points, discovers method-level control-flow constraints, and leverages LLM reasoning to transform bytecode conditions into interpretable specifications. The framework combines static program analysis precision with the semantic understanding of large language models.

Result: Evaluated with five LLMs across 78 Android constraint-extraction scenarios: 5.8× faster than traditional symbolic execution, 100% success rate, with logically equivalent and more precise/interpretable output. In malware analysis on 100 samples: 84% success rate in generating semantic constraints that trigger dangerous API behaviors and detecting complex multi-path constraints.

Conclusion: RECON demonstrates that augmenting static backward constraint analysis with LLM-based semantic reasoning effectively overcomes the scalability limitations of symbolic execution in complex environments like Android, offering a fast, reliable, and interpretable alternative for constraint extraction with strong applicability to malware analysis.

Abstract: While traditional techniques, such as symbolic execution, provide a principled foundation for precise constraint reasoning in program analysis, they struggle to scale to modern software systems mainly due to path explosion, the need for function modeling, and the loss of semantic intent at low-level program representations. In complex execution environments such as Android, characterized by extensive framework interactions and event-driven behavior, these limitations are even more amplified. Thus, in this paper, we present a novel large language model (LLM)-enhanced backward constraint analysis framework that combines the precision of static program analysis with LLM's semantic understanding to extract precise execution constraints from Android bytecode. Our approach, titled RECON, performs backward path discovery from target method(s) to the application entry point(s), discovers method-level control-flow constraints, and leverages LLM reasoning to transform bytecode conditions into interpretable specifications. We evaluated RECON using five LLMs across 78 Android constraint-extraction scenarios and compared it with traditional symbolic execution on real-world applications. Results demonstrate that our approach operates 5.8X faster than traditional symbolic execution, with a 100% success rate, while maintaining logical equivalence and providing significantly more precise and interpretable output. We further evaluated RECON for malware analysis on 100 samples. The results indicate an 84% success rate in generating semantic constraints that lead to the execution of dangerous API behaviors and in detecting complex constraints across multiple execution paths.

</details>


### [112] [Benchmarking and Exploring the Capabilities of LLMs for Attack Investigations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10281)
*Aniket Anand,Yiwei Hou,Daniel Fields,Alex Kantchelian,David Tao,Kurt Thomas,Grant Ho*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: AuditBench is a new benchmark for evaluating LLMs on security audit log investigation, covering 50+ scenarios across Linux and Windows. It assesses five frontier LLMs on four incident response tasks, revealing how model size, data representation, prompts, and task type affect performance and error profiles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is growing interest in using LLMs for security operations, but no standardized benchmark exists to systematically evaluate their ability to investigate system audit logs for attack detection and incident response. Existing evaluations lack coverage of realistic, multi-platform audit log analysis tasks.

Method: The authors construct AuditBench, a dataset of system audit logs from Linux and Windows machines spanning 50+ investigation scenarios (malicious and benign). They define four common incident response tasks (e.g., alert triage, persistence mechanism identification) and evaluate five frontier LLMs under varying conditions: different model sizes, data representations, prompt constructions, and task formulations. Both quantitative performance metrics and qualitative error/explanation analyses are conducted.

Result: LLM performance and error profiles vary significantly with design choices — model size, log representation format, prompt structure, and task type all meaningfully affect outcomes. The paper characterizes the types of errors LLMs make and the quality of their explanations across benchmark scenarios.

Conclusion: AuditBench provides a foundational resource for assessing LLM capabilities in security log investigation. The findings offer actionable insights for practitioners deploying LLMs in SOC environments and highlight key directions for future research, including improving LLM reasoning over structured audit logs and reducing task-specific error modes.

Abstract: This paper presents AuditBench, a new benchmark dataset for evaluating the capabilities of LLMs at investigating security-related system audit logs. We design and use this benchmark to explore the performance of LLMs on four log-investigation tasks that incident response teams commonly perform, ranging from triaging alerts generated by detectors to identifying persistence mechanisms on compromised systems. AuditBench consists of system audit logs collected from Linux and Windows machines, and spans over 50 different security investigation scenarios, including both malicious and benign activity. Using our benchmark, we evaluate and analyze the performance of five frontier LLMs at analyzing audit logs for attack investigations. Our analysis illuminates how LLM performance and error profiles vary according to different design choices, such as differences in model size, data representation, prompt construction, and specific investigation tasks. Additionally, we characterize the quality of the explanations produced by LLMs and the types of errors that models make across our benchmark. Collectively, our work provides a foundation for assessing the capabilities of LLMs for investigating security logs, novel insights for practitioners using LLMs in security operations, and important directions for future research.

</details>


### [113] [The Linux IOCTL Census: A Source-Derived Database of the Linux Kernel Control-Code Surface](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10290)
*Michael J. Bommarito*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper presents an automated, source-code-based census of the Linux kernel's ioctl attack surface, recovering dispatch points, command codes, input sinks, and permission gates to enable systematic security analysis, and releases the data as open, queryable, cross-OS schema.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ioctl is Linux's universal device-control interface with thousands of driver-defined commands parsed in kernel context, making it one of the largest and least uniform local attack surfaces. Handlers often trust unvalidated argument lengths, enabling out-of-bounds kernel memory access, yet no centralized catalog of this surface exists.

Method: An allmodconfig build compiles 878 modules across 169 subtrees. A deterministic libclang pass over the kernel source automatically recovers: (1) ioctl dispatch entry points, (2) decoded _IOC command codes, (3) controlled-input sinks, and (4) permission gates. A second pass encodes the kernel's threat model to separate the capability-ungated surface (upper bound on unprivileged reach) from capability-gated portions.

Result: The census recovers 586 ioctl dispatch entry points, 1,289 decoded _IOC command codes, 3,583 controlled-input sinks, and 1,298 permission gates. Backtesting against 22 recent in-tree ioctl CVEs validates the approach. The structural tier is released as open data on a schema shared with the companion Windows IOCTL Census.

Conclusion: The Linux IOCTL Census provides the first systematic, queryable inventory of the kernel's ioctl attack surface, enabling cross-OS comparison with Windows and offering a foundation for targeted hardening and auditing of this broad, non-uniform interface.

Abstract: The ioctl system call is Linux's catch-all device-control interface. A userspace program opens a device node and hands the driver a numeric command code and an argument buffer, and the driver does whatever that code means, whether configuring hardware, reading back state, or moving data into and out of the kernel. Drivers define these commands themselves, by the thousand, and parse their arguments in kernel context, which makes ioctl handlers one of the broadest and least uniform local attack surfaces in the kernel. A handler that trusts an argument length it never validates can read or write kernel memory out of bounds, and the command space is catalogued in no central place. We present the Linux IOCTL Census, a source-derived and queryable inventory of that surface. An allmodconfig build compiles 878 modules across 169 subtrees, and over them a single deterministic libclang pass over the kernel source recovers 586 ioctl dispatch entry points, 1,289 decoded _IOC command codes, 3,583 controlled-input sinks, and 1,298 permission gates. A second pass encodes the kernel's own documented threat model as a queryable column, separating the capability-ungated ioctl surface, an upper bound on unprivileged reach rather than proven reach, from the part a hard capability gate puts out of scope. We backtest the census against 22 recent in-tree ioctl CVEs and release the structural tier as open data, on a schema shared with the companion Windows IOCTL Census so a single query spans both operating systems.

</details>


### [114] [Semantic Multi-Agent Intrusion Detection for IoT:Zero-Day and Adversarial Threats with Risk-Aware Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10323)
*Saeid Jamshidi*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: A multi-agent IDS using four specialized semantic agents (Scout, Mutator, Auditor, Arbiter) achieves 95.9% accuracy, 6.8% false-positive rate, and 87.9% zero-day detection on IoT datasets while remaining computationally efficient for edge deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IoT networks face sophisticated cyber threats (zero-day, adversarial attacks), but traditional IDS solutions struggle with generalization to unseen attacks, computational cost, and lack of interpretability — especially in resource-constrained, heterogeneous IoT environments.
