Existing DL, open-set, and LLM-based approaches address only partial aspects and rarely combine semantic reasoning with multi-agent architectures.

Method: A semantic multi-agent architecture with four specialized agents: (1) Scout — induces structured hypotheses from semantic embeddings; (2) Mutator — generates adversarially constrained variants; (3) Auditor — evaluates consistency and filters unreliable outputs; (4) Arbiter — produces interpretable, risk-aware alerts via multi-stage probabilistic decision fusion.

Result: On multiple real-world IoT datasets: 95.9% overall detection accuracy, 6.8% false-positive rate, 87.9% zero-day detection rate, with computational efficiency suitable for edge deployment.

Conclusion: The proposed semantic multi-agent IDS effectively addresses key IDS limitations — generalization, interpretability, and resource efficiency — demonstrating strong performance across conventional and zero-day attacks while remaining lightweight enough for edge IoT deployment.

Abstract: The rapid proliferation of Internet of Things (IoT) devices has enabled unprecedented automation and connectivity, but it has also substantially increased the attack surface, exposing networks to sophisticated cyber threats, including zero-day and adversarial intrusions. Traditional Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) struggle to generalize to unseen attacks, often require substantial computational resources, and lack interpretability, particularly in resource-constrained and heterogeneous IoT networks. Recent advances, including Deep Learning (DL), open-set detection, and Large Language Model (LLM)-based semantic reasoning, address some of these challenges but typically focus on zero-day and adversarial threats and rarely combine semantic reasoning with multi-agent systems. To overcome these limitations, we propose a semantic multi-agent ID that integrates four specialized agents (e.g., Scout, Mutator, Auditor, and Arbiter) that leverage semantic embeddings and multi-stage probabilistic decision fusion. The Scout induces structured hypotheses from semantic embeddings; the Mutator generates adversarially constrained variants; the Auditor evaluates consistency and filters unreliable outputs; and the Arbiter produces interpretable, risk-aware alerts. Extensive experiments across multiple real-world IoT datasets demonstrate that the proposed system achieves 95.9% overall detection accuracy, reduces false-positive rates to 6.8%, improves zero-day detection to 87.9%, and maintains computational efficiency suitable for edge deployment.

</details>


### [115] [The Distributed Detectability Band Against Marginal-Preserving Attacks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10456)
*Zhang Qinqin,Gao Yuze*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: A distributed-sabotage attack is constructed that perfectly preserves per-step score marginals (defeating threshold/tail monitors) while encoding harm in temporal correlation structure. Temporal-correlation monitors (CUSUM, SPRT, etc.) can detect the attack (AUC 0.79–0.97), establishing a non-empty detectability band between marginal-based and sequence-based monitors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world AI sabotage can distribute harm across many individually benign-looking steps, each falling below per-step alarm thresholds. Existing per-step monitors (Monitor A: mean, max, top-k tail, threshold-based) are thus structurally vulnerable. The paper seeks to formally characterize this vulnerability, construct a provably stealthy attack, and determine whether temporal-correlation monitors (Monitor B) can close the detection gap.

Method: The authors construct a Gaussian-copula AR(1) attack that exactly preserves the marginal distribution of per-step monitor scores (identical to benign), while encoding harm in the temporal autocorrelation structure. They evaluate across three gates: (1) realizability via KS-distance between attack and benign distributions; (2) formal proof of marginal-preservation under a different Monitor B score function; (3) empirical comparison of Monitor A (threshold/tail) vs. Monitor B (CUSUM, SPRT, HMM-LR, runs test, autocorrelation, windowed logistic regression) via AUC at 1% FPR.

Result: The attack achieves KS-distance of 0.013 to benign across harm levels up to 3.0, confirming realizability. Monitor A yields AUC ~0.52 (chance), while Monitor B achieves AUC 0.79–0.97 at 1% FPR. As harm is amortized over more steps, Monitor A collapses to chance while Monitor B maintains AUC ~0.95, demonstrating a robust non-empty detectability band.

Conclusion: There exists a non-empty detectability band: distribution-shape (marginal) monitors fail by construction against correlation-encoded sabotage, while temporal-correlation monitors can detect but are not trivially optimal. This characterizes the sub-threshold sabotage frontier and implies that safety monitoring must incorporate sequence-level temporal signatures.

Abstract: AI-control monitors score individual agent actions to detect misbehavior, but real harm can be distributed across many benign-looking steps, each individually below any per-step alarm. We construct a marginal-preserving, correlation-encoded distributed-sabotage attack using a Gaussian-copula AR(1) construction: the per-step monitor-score marginal is held exactly equal to benign, so mean, max, top-k tail, and threshold monitors (Monitor A) are defeated by construction, while harm is encoded in the temporal correlation structure. We sequence the paper around three reviewer-mandated gates. (1) Realizability gate: the stealthy attack achieves KS-distance to benign of 0.013 (effectively zero) at all tested harm levels up to 3.0, confirming that harm is fully decoupled from the per-step marginal and realizability is not harm-limited. (2) Monitor-A-vs-B reconciliation: we show formally that the attack, built against Monitor A's score marginal, remains marginal-preserving under a different-score Monitor B (the correlation/sequence family: CUSUM, SPRT, HMM-LR, runs test, autocorrelation, windowed logistic), and scope worst-case claims to score functions that admit a temporal signature. (3) Non-empty detectability band: Monitor A achieves AUC 0.52 (chance); Monitor B spans AUC 0.79-0.97 at the same 1% FPR target, and as harm is amortized over more steps Monitor A collapses to chance while Monitor B holds at AUC ~0.95. These results demonstrate a non-empty detectability band and characterize the sub-threshold sabotage frontier: distribution-shape monitors fail by construction; temporal-correlation monitors can detect but are not trivially optimal.

</details>


### [116] [HE-DAP: Homomorphic Encryption-based Dynamic Adaptive Parameter Optimization for Statistical Computation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10477)
*Yun-Soo Park,Hyunmin Choi,Hyoungshick Kim,Mun-Kyu Lee*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: HE-DAP is a cross-platform optimization framework that automatically tunes the trade-off between high-degree Chebyshev polynomial evaluation and bootstrapping to accelerate the encrypted inverse square root computation in homomorphic encryption, achieving up to 2.35x speedup over fixed configurations while maintaining high numerical accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The inverse square root—a critical primitive in statistical and ML workloads—exhibits inconsistent, suboptimal performance across HE libraries and hardware due to a fundamental trade-off between Chebyshev polynomial degree and bootstrapping. Since relative costs vary up to 6x across environments, any fixed configuration is inherently inefficient, motivating an adaptive solution.

Method: HE-DAP profiles an execution environment's unique performance characteristics and automatically determines the optimal balance between polynomial degree and Newton iteration count to minimize inverse square root computation time for a given accuracy target, effectively navigating the polynomial-degree vs. bootstrapping trade-off.

Result: Evaluated on Lattigo, HEaaN-CPU, and HEaaN-GPU, HE-DAP accelerates the inverse square root by up to 2.35x over the fixed configuration in PP-STAT while maintaining high accuracy (MRE ≤ 3.1 × 10⁻⁸). End-to-end complex statistical analyses also benefit from this optimization.

Conclusion: Principled, environment-aware parameter optimization via HE-DAP demonstrates that automated adaptation to heterogeneous execution environments can make privacy-preserving statistical analytics practical at scale.

Abstract: Homomorphic encryption (HE) enables privacy-preserving analytics but remains hindered by high computational overhead. We find that the inverse square root-a key primitive in many statistical and machine learning workloads-exhibits inconsistent and often suboptimal performance across HE libraries and hardware. This stems from a core trade-off between two costly HE operations: evaluating high-degree Chebyshev polynomials to speed up Newton's method versus performing bootstrapping to manage ciphertext noise. Because their relative costs vary by up to 6x across environments, any fixed configuration proves inherently inefficient.
  To address this challenge, we present HE-DAP, a cross-platform optimization framework that automatically navigates this trade-off. By profiling an environment's unique performance characteristics, HE-DAP finds the optimal balance between polynomial degree and iteration count to accelerate the encrypted inverse square root computation for a given accuracy target. Our evaluation on Lattigo, HEaaN-CPU, and HEaaN-GPU shows that HE-DAP's adaptive approach yields significant performance gains. It accelerates the core inverse square root computation by up to 2.35x over the fixed configuration in PP-STAT while maintaining high numerical accuracy (MRE <= 3.1 x 10^-8). We further demonstrate that optimizing this fundamental building block directly enhances the end-to-end performance of complex statistical analyses, confirming the practical benefits of our environment-aware approach. By automatically adapting to heterogeneous execution environments, HE-DAP demonstrates that principled parameter optimization can make privacy-preserving statistical analytics practical at scale.

</details>


### [117] [AgentCanary: A Security Evaluation Framework for Autonomous AI Agents in Real Executable Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10484)
*Peiyang Li,Songping Wang,Yi Huang,Yanhua Shi,Chenhao Zhang,Qi Li,Yueming Lyu,Caifeng Shan,Fengting Li,Chao Feng,Chuanqun Zhu,Liang Chen*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: AgentCanary is a comprehensive security evaluation framework for autonomous AI agents featuring an orthogonal Entry×Impact risk taxonomy, a high-fidelity real executable environment with persistent state, and trajectory-grounded multi-dimensional evaluation. Evaluations reveal current agents fail to recognize attacks, especially under compromised skills, persistent state, and long-horizon execution scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous AI agents shift security failures from textual deception to system compromise, yet existing security evaluations suffer from three critical bottlenecks: fragmented risk coverage, static/low-fidelity execution environments, and single-dimensional/coarse-grained metrics. A systematic, realistic, and multi-dimensional evaluation framework is needed for proactive risk prevention.

Method: The framework comprises three components: (1) An orthogonal Entry×Impact risk taxonomy that decouples adversarial entry vectors (how influence enters) from impact types (what harm results), instantiated as scenario-aligned task suites; (2) A real executable environment where agents interact with real tools, dynamically provisioned artifacts, and persistent state across multi-step interactions for long-horizon attack evaluation; (3) Trajectory-grounded evaluation that scores the full agent trajectory along three orthogonal dimensions: Outcome Safety, Security Awareness, and Task Utility.

Result: Evaluations across frontier models, multiple adversarial attack methods, and three agent frameworks show that current agents frequently fail to recognize attacks, especially under compromised skills, persistent state, and long-horizon execution attacks. The framework provides a systematic baseline for developing more reliable and secure agent systems.

Conclusion: AgentCanary establishes a more realistic and systematic security evaluation paradigm for autonomous AI agents, exposing critical vulnerabilities in current systems and providing a foundation for building more secure and reliable agent architectures.

Abstract: Autonomous AI agents have driven the transition from conversation to task execution, shifting security failures from textual deception to system compromise. Although security evaluation is crucial for proactive risk prevention, prior work is constrained by fundamental bottlenecks, including fragmented risk coverage, static or low-fidelity execution environments, and single-dimensional and coarse-grained assessment metrics. To address these challenges, we propose AgentCanary, a comprehensive security evaluation framework for autonomous AI agents. AgentCanary provides a systematic solution along three contributions. First, comprehensive risk coverage: we introduce an orthogonal Entry $\times$ Impact risk taxonomy that decouples how adversarial influence enters the agent from what harm it ultimately causes, and instantiate it as a scenario-aligned task suite spanning realistic deployment workflows. Second, a high-fidelity real executable environment: rather than static Q&A or mocked tool responses, agents interact with real tools against dynamically provisioned task artifacts, with persistent state across multi-step interactions that naturally supports long-horizon attack evaluation. Third, trajectory-grounded multi-dimensional evaluation: evaluation consumes the full agent trajectory rather than the reply text or a single tool call, enabling decomposed scoring along three orthogonal dimensions, Outcome Safety, Security Awareness, and Task Utility. We evaluate a broad set of frontier models on AgentCanary against multiple established adversarial attack methods across three agent frameworks. The results reveal that current agents often fail to recognize the attacks they face, particularly under compromised skills, persistent state, and long-horizon execution attacks, and provide a systematic baseline for developing more reliable and secure agent systems.

</details>


### [118] [When VR Meets BCI: (Un)Observable Brainwave-aware Privacy Reconstruction in the Metaverse via Unrestricted Inbuilt Motion Sensors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10502)
*Tao Ni,Zehua Sun,Qingchuan Zhao,Wei-Bin Lee,Cong Wang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: BraVeSpy exploits motion sensors in VR headsets to reconstruct brain EEG-correlated signals via pupillary-induced vibrations, enabling inference of unobservable brain perceptions (perceptive images at 52-67% accuracy) and observable behaviors (activity fingerprinting, de-anonymization, gaze tracking, keystroke inference at 85-96%+ accuracy).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior VR privacy research was limited to inferring observable behaviors (e.g., what users see). This work explores a deeper, unaddressed threat: reconstructing unobservable neural representations (EEG-correlated brain signals) through overlooked motion sensor side channels in commodity VR headsets.

Method: The authors designed and implemented BraVeSpy, a system that captures subtle headset vibrations from pupillary responses using inbuilt motion sensors (accelerometers). Pupillary dynamics are correlated with visual stimuli and brain activity. BraVeSpy reconstructs EEG-correlated representations from these motion signals and applies classification models to infer both unobservable perceptive images and observable behavioral information (websites, apps, videos, gaze, keystrokes, identity).

Result: BraVeSpy achieved 52.0%–67.2% accuracy in reconstructing perceptive images from brain representations. For observable behavior inference, it achieved >85% accuracy for website/app/video fingerprinting, >96% for user de-anonymization, gaze tracking, and virtual keystroke inference. It outperformed prior coarse-grained approaches limited to observable behaviors.

Conclusion: VR motion sensors constitute a severe and previously neglected privacy vector capable of exposing both unobservable neural-level perceptions and fine-grained behavioral data. This represents the first demonstration of brain EEG-correlated privacy leakage in the Metaverse.

Abstract: Metaverse devices, such as virtual reality (VR), have seen substantial development and widespread applications in numerous areas. Although recent studies have revealed privacy leakages in VR, these vulnerabilities were limited in the scope of observable behaviors in virtual scenes (e.g., what a user is seeing). In this work, we uncover the feasibility of going beyond the scope of observable user behaviors to unobservable brain EEG-correlated representations (e.g., what a user is perceiving) by leveraging unrestricted motion sensors in VR headsets to reconstruct brain EEG signals, a seemingly neglected but promising vector. The insight is that the inbuilt motion sensors (e.g., accelerometers) in the VR headset can capture subtle vibrations induced by pupillary responses, which are highly correlated with users' visual stimuli and in-brain perceptions.
  Therefore, we design and implement BraVeSpy to systematically investigate and demonstrate the feasibility of this severe privacy leakage originating from brain EEG-correlated representations reconstructed from variations of inbuilt motion sensors. Our extensive evaluation results from different VR devices show that BraVeSpy, for the first time in the Metaverse, can reveal unobservable privacy, where we successfully unveiled perceptive images in the brain with 52.0%-67.2% accuracy. In particular, we also find that BraVeSpy outperforms the current approaches that are limited to coarse-grained inference of observable behaviors and achieves over 85.0% accuracy in inferring user activity-related sensitive information, such as fingerprinting websites, apps, and streaming videos, and over 96.0% accuracy in user de-anonymization, gaze movement tracking, and virtual keystroke inference.

</details>


### [119] [A Deployment-Oriented Framework for Explainable AI-Assisted eBPF/XDP Mitigation at the IoT Edge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10508)
*Abdurrahman Tolay*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Proposes a conceptual framework for IoT edge security that combines AI-assisted risk scoring with eBPF/XDP-based mitigation, featuring reversible time-limited actions, explainable decisions, and a hardware-aware evaluation methodology — without new experimental results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IoT deployments face heterogeneous resource-constrained devices, weak security, limited logging, and long lifecycles. Signature/threshold-based controls and offline AI benchmarks alone are insufficient for dynamic IoT networks; there is a need for an operational, resource-aware, and explainable security architecture deployable at the edge.

Method: Presents a conceptual framework and research agenda (no experiments): a Linux-based IoT edge gateway architecture separating user-space complex reasoning (AI-assisted flow-level risk scoring with event-level explainability) from kernel-space packet decisions via eBPF/XDP. Mitigation is bounded, reversible, and time-limited with critical-device safeguards. Also defines a hardware-aware evaluation pathway across detection quality, resource cost, response timing, rollback behavior, and legitimate-traffic preservation.

Result: No new experimental results are reported. The contribution is purely architectural and agenda-setting, defining the framework components and evaluation criteria for future implementation and validation.

Conclusion: A research agenda is established for a resource-aware, explainable AI-assisted IoT edge security architecture using eBPF/XDP for bounded, reversible mitigation. Future work should implement and evaluate the framework along the defined hardware-aware metrics.

Abstract: Internet of Things (IoT) deployments combine heterogeneous, resource-constrained devices with weak security configurations, exposed services, limited logging, patching constraints, and long lifecycles. Signature- and threshold-based controls remain useful baselines, but they are insufficient as standalone mechanisms in dynamic IoT networks. Likewise, offline artificial intelligence (AI) benchmark performance alone does not establish operational deployability. This article presents a conceptual framework and research agenda for a Linux-based IoT edge gateway that combines resource-aware flow-level AI-assisted risk scoring, event-level explainability, and bounded mitigation through eBPF/XDP. The controller applies reversible, time-limited actions subject to critical-device safeguards, updates packet-level enforcement state, and records structured logs. The architecture separates complex reasoning and policy control in user space from concise packet-handling decisions in the kernel. It also defines a future hardware-aware evaluation pathway covering detection quality, resource cost, response timing, rollback behaviour, and legitimate-traffic preservation. The paper does not report new experimental meas

</details>


### [120] [Assessing Automated Prompt Injection Attacks in Agentic Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10525)
*David Hofer,Edoardo Debenedetti,Florian Tramèr*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Empirical evaluation of automated prompt injection attacks against LLM agents reveals black-box methods (TAP) significantly outperform white-box approaches (GCG). Attack success is attacker-model-dependent, with task-universal attacks transferring across unseen tasks but failing to transfer from small open-source models to frontier models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Indirect prompt injection threatens LLM agents processing untrusted external data, yet automated attack methods proven effective for jailbreaking remain largely unexplored in realistic agentic settings. The authors aim to systematically evaluate whether automated optimization-based injection attacks pose a credible threat in practical agent environments.

Method: The authors adapt white-box (Greedy Coordinate Gradient, GCG) and black-box (Tree of Attacks with Pruning, TAP) automated attack methods to the agentic setting. Evaluation is conducted within the AgentDojo framework across 80 task pairs spanning four domains and multiple LLM models, assessing attack success rates, transferability to unseen tasks and out-of-distribution domains, and the effect of attacker model capability and safety tuning.

Result: Black-box optimization (TAP) substantially outperforms gradient-based methods (GCG), attributed to GCG's optimization instability under reasonable compute budgets. TAP's effectiveness depends critically on the attacker model: stronger general-capability models produce more effective injections, but safety-tuned attackers may refuse to generate adversarial prompts. Task-universal attacks transfer effectively to unseen tasks and out-of-distribution domains, but attacks optimized on smaller open-source models fail to transfer to frontier models like GPT-5.

Conclusion: Automated prompt injection represents a credible but model-dependent threat to LLM agents. Significant barriers remain for model-agnostic exploitation, as attack transferability breaks down when moving from smaller open-source models to frontier systems, limiting the practical risk of universal, cross-model attacks.

Abstract: Indirect prompt injection poses a critical threat to LLM agents that interact with untrusted external data, yet automated attack methods--proven effective for jailbreaking--remain underexplored in realistic agentic settings. We present a comprehensive empirical evaluation of automated prompt injection attacks against LLM agents, adapting both white-box (GCG) and black-box (TAP) methods to the agentic setting within the AgentDojo framework. We evaluate across 80 task pairs spanning four domains and multiple models, and find that black-box optimization substantially outperforms gradient-based methods, a gap we attribute to GCG's optimization instability under reasonable compute budgets. We also find that TAP's effectiveness depends on the attacker model, as both general capability and safety tuning affect attack success--stronger models produce more effective injections, while safety-tuned attackers can refuse to generate adversarial prompts. Task-universal attacks transfer effectively to unseen tasks and out-of-distribution domains, but attacks optimized on smaller open-source models do not transfer to frontier models like GPT-5. These findings highlight automated prompt injection as a credible but model-dependent threat, with significant barriers remaining for model-agnostic exploitation.

</details>


### [121] [A Hybrid Edge-Cloud Architecture for Low-Latency Entitlement Verification in Resource-Constrained Devices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10536)
*Pravin Nagare,Aditya Sabbineni,Devendra Dahiphale,Faiz Gouri,Pratik Thantharate*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: A Hybrid Edge-Cloud Entitlement Framework with an Adaptive Entitlement Cache and Proactive Refresh algorithm reduces OTT platform authorization latency from 422.8ms to 18.4ms (95.6% reduction) using local cryptographic verification and TEE isolation on ARM hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current OTT platform entitlement/identity verification relies on synchronous cloud-tethered flows that introduce significant latency, degrading user experience, particularly on resource-constrained consumer devices. The authors aim to decouple user interaction from backend network variability.

Method: The authors propose a Hybrid Edge-Cloud Entitlement Framework featuring a secure local caching layer in device middleware, governed by an Adaptive Entitlement Cache with Proactive Refresh (AEC-PR) algorithm. Security is hardened through deterministic Ed25519 arithmetic and Trusted Execution Environment (TEE) isolation. Evaluation is performed on ARM Cortex-A series hardware.

Result: Localized cryptographic verification reduced mean authorization latency from 422.8ms to 18.4ms, representing a 95.6% reduction. The approach also mitigated implementation-level side-channel risks.

Conclusion: Edge-based entitlement caching with proactive refresh and hardware-backed isolation is a viable and effective strategy for eliminating user-perceived latency in large-scale OTT platforms without compromising security.

Abstract: As digital media consumption shifts toward large-scale Over-the-Top (OTT) platforms, the efficiency of the control plane, specifically entitlement and identity verification, has become a critical factor in user experience. Current architectures often rely on synchronous cloud-tethered validation flows that introduce significant latency, especially on resource-constrained consumer electronics. This paper proposes a Hybrid Edge-Cloud Entitlement Framework designed to minimize user-perceived friction. By implementing a secure, local caching layer within device middleware and utilizing an Adaptive Entitlement Cache with Proactive Refresh (AEC-PR) algorithm, we decouple the user interaction from backend network variability. We evaluate the performance on ARM Cortex-A series hardware, demonstrating that localized cryptographic verification reduces authorization latency from a mean of 422.8ms to 18.4ms (a 95.6% reduction) while mitigating implementation-level side-channel risks through deterministic Ed25519 arithmetic and TEE isolation.

</details>


### [122] [Securing Code Understanding: Detecting Natural Backdoor Vulnerability in Code Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10846)
*Yuchen Chen,Weisong Sun,Haocheng Huang,Yuan Xiao,Chunrong Fang,Yiran Zhang,Tingting Xu,Zhenpeng Chen,An Guo,Peizhuo Lv,Xiaofang Zhang,Zhenyu Chen,Yang Liu,Baowen Xu*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper presents the first comprehensive empirical study of natural backdoor vulnerabilities in Code Language Models, examining 44 scenarios across different architectures and tasks. The authors find natural backdoors are prevalent and intrinsic to CodeLMs, analyze their differences from injected backdoors, investigate their transferability and causes, evaluate existing defenses, and propose a novel detection method called ScanNBT.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While backdoor attacks via data poisoning are well-studied in CodeLMs, naturally occurring backdoors in normally trained models remain poorly understood despite posing comparably serious security threats. This gap motivates a systematic investigation into the prevalence, characteristics, causes, and mitigation of natural backdoor vulnerabilities in CodeLMs.

Method: The authors conduct an empirical study across 44 scenarios spanning various model architectures and code intelligence tasks. They analyze natural backdoors at model and parameter levels, examine transferability across datasets/architectures/knowledge, investigate root causes (training data and training procedures), evaluate pre-training/in-training/post-training defenses, and propose ScanNBT, a novel detection method for natural backdoor vulnerabilities.

Result: Natural backdoors are found to be prevalent and intrinsic to CodeLMs across all 44 scenarios. Key findings include: (1) natural backdoors differ from injected backdoors at both model and parameter levels; (2) these vulnerabilities exhibit transferability across datasets, model architectures, and shared knowledge; (3) both training datasets and training procedures contribute to their emergence; (4) existing defense techniques show limitations in mitigating natural backdoors, motivating the need for ScanNBT.

Conclusion: Natural backdoor vulnerabilities represent a significant and underappreciated security threat in CodeLMs. The proposed ScanNBT method aims to improve detection comprehensiveness. The findings provide foundational insights for strengthening CodeLM security and call for further research into effective defenses against these intrinsic vulnerabilities.

Abstract: Code Language Models (CodeLMs) have become integral to software engineering, significantly advancing code intelligence tasks. However, their widespread adoption has raised critical security concerns, particularly regarding susceptibility to backdoor attacks. Recent studies have uncovered naturally occurring backdoors, referred to as natural backdoors, in normally trained deep learning models. Despite posing threats as serious as those introduced through data poisoning, security implications of natural backdoor vulnerabilities in CodeLMs remain poorly understood.
  In this paper, we conduct a thorough empirical study of natural backdoor vulnerabilities in CodeLMs across various model architectures and code intelligence tasks. Specifically, we examine potential natural backdoor vulnerabilities across 44 scenarios, demonstrating that natural backdoors are prevalent and intrinsic to CodeLMs. We reveal differences between injected and natural backdoor vulnerabilities at both the model and parameter levels. We then analyze the transferability of natural backdoor vulnerabilities from three perspectives: datasets, model architectures, and shared knowledge. We further investigate the causes of natural backdoors from two aspects: training datasets and the model training procedure. We evaluate existing backdoor defense techniques, including pre-training, in-training, and post-training defenses, in mitigating natural backdoors. Finally, we propose ScanNBT, a novel detection method designed to improve comprehensive detection of natural backdoor vulnerabilities in CodeLMs. We aim for our findings to enhance understanding of these vulnerabilities and provide insights for strengthening CodeLM security against backdoor threats.

</details>


### [123] [From Data Heterogeneity to Convergence: A Data-Centric Review of Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10595)
*Huong Nguyen,Mickaël Bettinelli,Amirhossein Ghaffari,Alexandre Benoit,Hong-Tri Nguyen,Susanna Pirttikangas,Lauri Lovén*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: A survey that analyzes federated learning from a data-centric perspective, categorizing non-IID traits by their impact on convergence, linking experimental data-splitting protocols to real-world phenomena, and making explicit the trade-off between convergence and robustness under data-related attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing FL surveys cover general foundations, security, and applications but lack a systematic, data-lens synthesis connecting concrete data properties, splitting protocols, and defenses to convergence speed and stability. This gap leaves practitioners without actionable guidance on how data characteristics govern FL training outcomes.

Method: The survey makes three contributions: (1) it decomposes non-IIDness into measurable traits and ranks their influence on convergence (strong/medium/light), explaining underlying mechanisms across images, text, and graph modalities; (2) it maps experimental data-splitting practices to real phenomena, identifies introduced artifacts, and quantifies their effect on target accuracy; (3) it systematically evaluates data-related vulnerabilities and defenses, reporting performance under both clean and adversarial conditions to expose the convergence-robustness trade-off.

Result: The survey reconciles evidence across multiple data modalities, provides a ranked taxonomy of non-IID traits by convergence impact, exposes artifacts from common splitting protocols, and makes the convergence-robustness trade-off explicit for data-related attacks and defenses. It distills clear, actionable takeaways for each concern.

Conclusion: This is the first survey to deliver a complete, data-centric understanding of FL challenges, serving as actionable guidance for practitioners to design FL systems with predictable convergence and stability by accounting for data properties, splitting choices, and robustness needs.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) has emerged as a promising solution for data hunger in centralized learning. This paradigm enables privacy with multiple clients to train a shared-task model collaboratively without exposing their local data. While being a key component in any learning system, data is also a primary source of vulnerabilities and challenges, and a major determinant of a stable and well-converged training. Existing FL reviews describe general foundations, security practices, opportunities, challenges, and applications, without delving into diverse aspects of data and considering problems from the data perspective. They rarely provide a data-lens synthesis that links concrete data properties, split protocols, and defenses to convergence speed and stability. This survey fills that gap with three advances. First, we analyze non-IID into measurable traits and rank their influence on convergence as strong, medium, or light, explaining the mechanisms behind each and reconciling evidence across images, texts, and graphs. Second, we connect experimental splitting practices to the real phenomena they emulate, expose the artifacts they introduce, and show how those artifacts affect target accuracy. Third, we analyze how data-related vulnerabilities and their proposed defenses affect convergence, reporting performance under clean and adversarial conditions to make the convergence-robustness trade-off explicit. To our knowledge, this is the first survey to provide a complete understanding of data-related challenges that govern FL. With clear takeaways distilled for each concern, our work serves as actionable guidance, helping practitioners design their system with predictable convergence and stability.

</details>


### [124] [Context-Based Adversarial Attacks on AI Code Generators: Vulnerability Analysis and Implications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10945)
*Walther A. Del Orbe,John D. Hastings,Varghese Vaidyan*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Strategic contextual inputs (comments, variable names, documentation) can manipulate AI code generation models into producing vulnerable code. Experiments across 2,800 trials show adversarial contexts increase vulnerability generation 10.7x, with cross-model transferability of 60-100%, while a dual-layer defense achieves 89.1% detection with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI-powered code generation systems (e.g., Copilot, CodeLlama) are increasingly adopted in software development, yet their susceptibility to adversarial contextual manipulation at inference time remains poorly understood. The authors aim to systematically characterize how seemingly benign contextual cues—comments, variable names, documentation—can be weaponized to induce generation of exploitable, security-vulnerable code.

Method: The study conducted 2,800 controlled experiments spanning four models: CodeT5+, CodeLlama, GPT-3.5-Turbo, and GPT-4. Adversarial attacks were crafted through strategically manipulated contextual inputs (direct instruction attacks, comment-based injection, variable naming, and documentation poisoning). Attack effectiveness was quantified by measuring vulnerability generation rates under normal vs. adversarial conditions. Cross-model transferability was assessed to determine whether vulnerabilities are model-specific or systemic. A dual-layer defense framework was also proposed and evaluated on detection rate, false positive rate, and latency.

Result: Adversarial contexts increased vulnerability generation from 3.5% (baseline) to 37.4% — a 10.7x increase. Direct instruction attacks achieved 100% success rate on GPT-3.5-Turbo. Cross-model transferability ranged from 60–100%, indicating systemic architectural vulnerabilities rather than isolated model flaws. The proposed dual-layer defense achieved 89.1% detection rate, 0.3% false positive rate, and 520ms latency.

Conclusion: Context-based adversarial attacks pose a significant, systematic threat to AI code generation systems. The high cross-model transferability reveals fundamental architectural vulnerabilities. The dual-layer defense demonstrates practical feasibility for real-time deployment, with strong detection performance and acceptable latency, offering a viable mitigation strategy for development environments.

Abstract: AI-powered code generation systems have transformed software development but introduce critical inference-time security vulnerabilities. This research presents a systematic investigation of context-based adversarial attacks, where strategically crafted contextual inputs, including comments, documentation, variable names, bias large language models toward generating exploitable code. Through 2,800 controlled experiments across CodeT5+, CodeLlama, GPT-3.5-Turbo, and GPT-4, we quantify attack effectiveness and defense mechanisms. Results demonstrate that adversarial conditions increase vulnerability generation 10.7x (from 3.5% to 37.4%), with direct instruction attacks achieving 100% success on GPT-3.5-Turbo. Cross-model transferability reaches 60-100%, indicating systemic architectural vulnerabilities rather than model-specific flaws. Our dual-layer defense framework achieves 89.1% detection rate with 0.3% false positives and 520ms latency, demonstrating practical feasibility for real-time deployment in development environments.

</details>


### [125] [Two-Way Confidential VMs (2cVM): Collaborative Confidential Computing for Mutually Distrustful Parties](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10615)
*Jordi Thijsman,Merlijn Sebrechts,Stefan Lefever,Filip De Turck,Bruno Volckaert*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: 2cVM is a two-layer confidential computing architecture combining hardware TEE (AMD SEV-SNP) with WebAssembly sandboxing to enable mutually distrustful parties to collaboratively compute on sensitive data/code, governed by a verifiable Commitment Manifest. Overhead is workload-dependent (negligible to ~2x), and the two isolation layers do not accumulate linearly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Collaborative computation across organizations requires processing sensitive data and proprietary code without exposing them to untrusted infrastructure or other participants. Existing cryptographic solutions (FHE, MPC) are computationally impractical for general workloads. Regular Confidential VMs protect against the infrastructure provider but lack intra-workload isolation between mutually distrustful participants.

Method: A two-layer architecture: (1) hardware TEE (AMD SEV-SNP) for protecting the entire VM from the host/infrastructure, and (2) WebAssembly Component Model for fine-grained sandboxing between participant workloads. A Commitment Manifest specifies participants, component composition, permitted data channels, and authorized outputs; it is locked to the VM and embedded in attestation evidence for immutable, verifiable policy enforcement throughout the VM's lifetime.

Result: The two isolation layers do not accumulate linearly — once inside the Wasm sandbox, the marginal cost of enabling hardware memory encryption is small. Overhead is primarily governed by memory access patterns: negligible for sequential workloads, up to ~2x for irregular pointer-chasing patterns. Four benchmark classes on commodity hardware were used.

Conclusion: 2cVM provides a practical and verifiable foundation for privacy-preserving collaborative computation, demonstrating that combining hardware TEEs with software sandboxing is feasible with acceptable overhead for real-world workloads.

Abstract: Collaborative computation across organizations is often constrained by the need to process sensitive data and proprietary code without exposing them to untrusted infrastructure or participants. Cryptographic approaches such as fully homomorphic encryption and secure multi-party computation provide strong confidentiality but remain impractical for general workloads due to their extreme computational cost. We present the Two-Way Confidential Virtual Machine (2cVM), a two-layer architecture that pairs a hardware trusted execution environment with an intra-workload isolation layer. Unlike regular Confidential Virtual Machines, 2cVM enforces mutual isolation between co-resident workloads, ensuring that participants retain control over their data and code. All computation in 2cVM is governed by a Commitment Manifest that enumerates participants, component composition, permitted data channels, and authorized outputs; the manifest is locked to the VM and incorporated into attestation evidence, making the policy immutable and independently verifiable throughout the VM's lifetime. A proof-of-concept realization combines AMD SEV-SNP for hardware protection with the WebAssembly Component Model for fine-grained sandboxing of participant code. Evaluation on commodity hardware across four benchmark classes shows that the two isolation layers do not accumulate linearly: once a workload executes inside the WebAssembly sandbox, the marginal cost of enabling hardware memory protection is small. Overhead is workload-dependent, governed primarily by memory access pattern, ranging from negligible for sequential workloads to approximately 2x for irregular, pointer-chasing access patterns. These results indicate that 2cVM provides a practical and verifiable foundation for privacy-preserving collaborative computation.

</details>


### [126] [Understanding and mitigating the risks of OpenClaw for non-technical users: A practical guide with Skill](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11007)
*Junchang Zheng,Junfeng Tan,Jialiang Lin*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper addresses the security risks faced by non-technical users of the OpenClaw AI agent framework. The authors identify seven core risks explained in plain language, provide actionable defensive strategies for each, and offer an automated OpenClaw Skill for security configuration, demonstrating that non-experts can meaningfully protect themselves against AI agent threats.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing research on OpenClaw's security risks predominantly targets technically sophisticated audiences, leaving non-technical users—a rapidly growing demographic—without accessible guidance. These users urgently need practical, straightforward advice to mitigate the risks of autonomous multi-step AI agents.

Method: The authors employ three interconnected strategies: (1) identifying and categorizing seven core risks with plain-language explanations, (2) distilling clear, actionable defensive steps for each risk, and (3) developing a companion OpenClaw Skill that automates key security configurations to minimize manual intervention.

Result: The paper delivers a taxonomy of seven core risks, a set of corresponding defensive strategies expressed as simple operational steps, and a functional OpenClaw Skill for automated security hardening.

Conclusion: Safeguarding against intelligent agent risks should not be restricted to security experts. Non-technical users can meaningfully reduce their exposure through simple, practical actions when provided with accessible guidance and automation tools.

Abstract: OpenClaw has rapidly emerged as a transformative artificial intelligence (AI) agent framework, and its ability to autonomously execute complex, multi-step tasks has attracted an ever-growing and diverse user base. However, this capability comes with significant risks. While existing research has made important strides in characterizing these threats, such work is predominantly directed at technically sophisticated audiences. It remains largely inaccessible to non-technical users. This demographic now makes up an increasingly large and underserved portion of the community, yet it is these very users who most urgently need practical and straightforward guidance. In response, we bridge this gap through a series of interconnected efforts designed to lower the risk barrier for non-technical OpenClaw users. First, we identify and categorize seven core risks that OpenClaw users may encounter in daily usage, explaining each in plain language so that non-technical users can readily grasp the nature and potential consequences of these threats. Second, for each identified risk, we distill a set of corresponding defensive strategies into clear and actionable operational steps that are easy to follow. Third, to make protection even easier, we provide a companion OpenClaw Skill that automates key security configurations, enabling users to safeguard their systems with minimal manual intervention. Through this work, we demonstrate that safeguarding against the risks of intelligent agents need not be the exclusive domain of security experts, and that non-technical users can meaningfully participate in reducing these risks through simple, practical actions.

</details>


### [127] [snaproot: Decentralized File Integrity Verification Using Blockchain-Anchored Cryptographic Hashing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10625)
*Arslan Brömme,Tarkan Yavas*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: snaproot is a lightweight file integrity verification system that stores SHA-256 file hashes on Solana, distinguishing between existence proofs (key-independent) and authorship proofs (key-dependent), with a four-tier trust architecture for long-term persistence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing integrity verification solutions either rely on centralized authorities (creating trust dependencies and single points of failure) or decentralized storage (incurring prohibitive overhead). A lightweight, decentralized alternative is needed that preserves integrity guarantees without excessive resource costs.

Method: snaproot implements the Haber-Stornetta hash-anchoring paradigm on Solana: it generates a SHA-256 hash of a file, stores it immutably on-chain as a permanent reference record, and verifies integrity by recomputing and comparing hashes. The system uses a four-tier trust architecture (three realized, one prospective) and formally separates existence proof from authorship proof. Security is grounded in SHA-256 second-preimage resistance, evaluated empirically on Solana Devnet with files from 1 KB to 500 MB.

Result: The paper provides a formal threat model, a security analysis based on SHA-256 second-preimage resistance, and empirical evaluation across file sizes from 1 KB to 500 MB on Solana Devnet. The explicit decoupling of existence and authorship proofs allows existence guarantees to survive key loss.

Conclusion: snaproot offers a practical, lightweight decentralized integrity verification solution. The separation of existence and authorship proofs is a key contribution. Limitations include pre-registration manipulation and challenges with AI-generated content. Positioned against OpenTimestamps, OriginStamp, and Chainpoint.

Abstract: The rapid growth of digital content has made reliable integrity verification increasingly important. Existing solutions rely either on centralized authorities, which introduce trust dependencies and single points of failure, or on decentralized storage systems that incur prohibitive resource overhead. In this paper, we present snaproot, a lightweight system that implements the hash-anchoring paradigm of Haber and Stornetta on the Solana blockchain to provide efficient, decentralized file integrity verification. snaproot generates a SHA-256 hash of a file and stores it immutably on-chain as a permanent reference record. Verification is performed by recomputing the hash and comparing it to the stored value, yielding a deterministic binary outcome. We describe a four-tier trust architecture comprising three realized tiers and one prospective tier for long-term persistence beyond the lifetime of any single blockchain. We present a formal threat model, a security analysis grounded in the second-preimage resistance of SHA-256, and an empirical evaluation on Solana Devnet across file sizes from 1 KB to 500 MB. A central conceptual contribution is the explicit separation between existence proof, the key-independent claim that a file existed at a given time, and authorship proof, the key-dependent binding between a record and a specific wallet identity. This separation allows existence guarantees to survive key loss while preserving stronger authorship claims where keys are retained. We position snaproot against OpenTimestamps, OriginStamp, and Chainpoint and discuss limitations with respect to pre-registration manipulation and AI-generated content.

</details>


### [128] [Layer Order Semantics for Automata-Based Cybersecurity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10649)
*Faruk Alpay,Taylan Alpay*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper formalizes how the ordering of transformations in layered cybersecurity pipelines determines which security-relevant evidence is visible at each layer. Using HTTP request desynchronization as a case study, it develops an automata-theoretic framework (layer-order automata, security transducers, evidence markers) to characterize when layered systems can faithfully enforce policies, distinguishing recognizable from deployable enforcers under causal visibility constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In layered cybersecurity architectures (e.g., front-end/back-end HTTP processors), the sequential ordering of evidence transformations determines whether an attack is detected or missed. HTTP request desynchronization exemplifies this: incompatible request boundary computations between layers cause the same trace to be classified differently depending on whether framing evidence arrives before a parser-differential layer commits. No existing formal framework provides a compositional semantics for how layer ordering affects security outcomes.

Method: The authors construct a finite-state framework comprising: (1) a layer-order automaton that sequences deterministic security transducers, (2) evidence markers that track which facts survive or are born across transformations, and (3) a final decision automaton. They formalize four operational regimes: completed-trace recognition, online editing, decision synthesis, and faithful enforcement. The framework is shown monolithically equivalent to finite-output deterministic edit automata. A concrete parser-pair semantics for HTTP desynchronization identifies forbidden marker factors (CL.TE, TE.CL, TE.TE, HTTP/2 downgrade), and a contextual reorder congruence classifies which component permutations preserve the decision language.

Result: The framework cleanly separates completed-trace recognition, online editing, decision synthesis, and faithful enforcement. Faithful online enforcement is characterized as precisely the regular prefix-closed case under causal visibility. Regular policies beyond that boundary remain recognizable but cannot be deployed as faithful online enforcers. The layer-order automaton preserves layer-local invariants (marker birth, marker survival, reorder-sensitive visibility). The contextual reorder congruence identifies which layer permutations induce identical decision languages, enabling compositional auditing and comparison of layered pipelines.

Conclusion: The paper delivers an automata-theoretic account of order-sensitive security failures in layered enforcement pipelines and provides a compositional vocabulary for auditing, synthesizing, and comparing such systems. The framework bridges the gap between monolithic edit automata and layer-local invariants, offering both theoretical grounding and practical tools for analyzing real-world desynchronization vulnerabilities.

Abstract: Layered cybersecurity pipelines transform evidence before they decide on it, and the order of those transformations determines which security facts become visible to which layer. This paper gives layer order a finite-state semantics built from a layer-order automaton, deterministic sequential security transducers, evidence markers, and a final decision automaton. The worked case is HTTP request desynchronization: front-end and back-end processors compute incompatible request boundaries, and the same trace is detected or missed according to whether framing evidence reaches the parser-differential layer before it commits. The results separate completed-trace recognition, online editing, decision synthesis, and faithful enforcement; characterize faithful online enforcement as the regular prefix-closed case under causal visibility; and show that regular policies beyond that boundary remain recognizable without becoming deployable enforcers. The framework is monolithically equivalent to finite-output deterministic edit automata, while preserving layer-local invariants such as marker birth, marker survival, and reorder-sensitive visibility. A concrete parser-pair semantics identifies the forbidden marker factor with CL.TE, TE.CL, TE.TE, and HTTP/2-downgrade boundary disagreement under the stated abstraction, and a contextual reorder congruence classifies which component permutations induce the same decision language. The result is an automata-theoretic account of order-sensitive security failures and a compositional vocabulary for auditing, synthesizing, and comparing layered enforcement pipelines.

</details>


### [129] [Post-Quantum Secure Federated DeFi for Inclusive Banking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10658)
*Swati Sachan,Dale Fickett,Richard Buchinger,Theo Miller*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: A post-quantum secure federated DeFi framework uses lattice-based FHE and NASA-IBM geospatial verification to enable privacy-preserving inter-bank lending for underserved rural borrowers, demonstrated on agricultural loans in Virginia.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantum computing threatens current cryptographic systems. Simultaneously, many individuals (e.g., rural borrowers) are excluded from lending due to limited financial histories. Inter-bank data sharing could improve credit assessment, but raises privacy and security concerns that require post-quantum solutions.

Method: Banks contribute encrypted data batches to a virtual server using lattice-based Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE), enabling end-to-end homomorphic computation on encrypted data. The server fuses probabilistic assessments from local data, expert beliefs, and verifiable evidence from the NASA-IBM Prithvi Geospatial Foundation Model (GFM). Decentralized technologies ensure tamper-proof evidence and auditable accountability for all encrypted data exchanges.

Result: The framework is empirically tested on agricultural lending decisions for rural borrowers in Virginia.

Conclusion: The paper demonstrates a viable post-quantum secure federated DeFi architecture that balances privacy, security, and financial inclusivity, with practical validation in agricultural lending contexts.

Abstract: Recent advances in error-corrected qubits have accelerated the timeline for practical quantum computing. It poses a threat to cryptographic primitives used to secure financial systems, government infrastructure, communication networks, and DeFi (Decentralized Finance) ecosystems. This paper introduces a post-quantum secure federated DeFi framework that enables inter-bank collaboration to improve the inclusivity of individuals underserved by local lenders due to limited financial histories. Multiple banks contribute encrypted information batches to a virtual server, where lattice-based Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) enables end-to-end homomorphic computation. The server fuses local data-driven probabilistic assessments, expert beliefs, and verifiable evidence generated by the NASA-IBM Prithvi Geospatial Foundation Model (GFM), in encrypted format. Decentralized technologies are employed to ensure tamper-proof evidence and auditable accountability for all encrypted data exchanges between institutions and the server. The framework is tested on agricultural lending decisions for rural borrowers in Virginia.

</details>


### [130] [Do LLMsMakeNeural Distinguishers Wise?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10692)
*Tatsuya Sakagami,Masashi Hisai,Naoto Yanai*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper investigates whether large language models (LLMs) can improve neural distinguishers for symmetric-key cryptanalysis. Experiments on SPECK-32/64 show LLMs offer no observable improvement over ResNet baselines, and only incorporating XOR operation results in prompts significantly boosts LLM-based distinguisher performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: No existing work has explored using LLMs for neural distinguishers in symmetric-key cryptanalysis. The authors aim to fill this gap by investigating whether LLMs can strengthen neural distinguishers compared to conventional architectures like ResNet.

Method: The authors propose LLM-based neural distinguishers using prompt design, where pairs of plaintexts and ciphertexts with specific differences are fed to LLMs. Extensive experiments are conducted on the SPECK-32/64 block cipher, comparing LLM-based distinguishers against ResNet baselines. They also explore prompt design variations, particularly incorporating XOR operation results.

Result: Three key findings: (1) LLMs provide no observable improvement over ResNet for neural distinguishers; (2) at high rounds, the choice of input differences becomes ineffective for both LLM-based and ResNet distinguishers; (3) incorporating only XOR operation results in the prompt design significantly improves LLM-based distinguisher performance.

Conclusion: LLMs do not inherently strengthen neural distinguishers beyond existing architectures like ResNet. However, careful prompt engineering — specifically leveraging XOR operation results — can significantly enhance LLM-based distinguisher performance, suggesting that representation design is critical when applying LLMs to cryptanalysis tasks.

Abstract: Neural distinguishers are a cryptanalysis method for symmetric-key cryptography that trains machine learning models on pairs of plaintexts and ciphertexts with specific differences in order to recover a secret key. To the best of our knowledge, no existing work has explored the use of large language models (LLMs) for neural distinguishers. In this paper, we propose LLM-based neural distinguishers through a prompt design and conduct extensive experiments with them on SPECK-32/64 to investigate whether LLMs can strengthen neural distinguishers. We then found three key insights. First, by comparing the results of LLM-based neural distinguishers with ResNet in the existing work, we demonstrate that LLMs provide no observable improvement in the performance of neural distinguishers. Second, we confirm that, at high rounds, the choice of differences is no longer effective for LLM-based neural distinguishers as well as ResNet. Third, we show that the performance of LLM-based neural distinguishers can be significantly improved by incorporating only the XOR operation results as a prompt design.

</details>


### [131] [Fingerprinting All AI Cluster I/O Without Mutually Trusted Processors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10724)
*Naci Cankaya,Jakub Kryś,Jonathan Ng,Luke Marks,Felix Krückel*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Proposes a cryptographic verification infrastructure for AI data centres using network taps to hash all inbound/outbound data, combined with a Secure Gateway Device that eliminates covert channels (side-channels, steganography, timing leaks) without requiring processors trusted by both parties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In anticipation of international AI governance agreements, there is a need for verifiable infrastructure that can assure compliance of AI data centres — specifically, preventing the covert exfiltration of results from undisclosed training runs — without relying on mutually trusted hardware components.

Method: The authors propose: (1) cryptographic commitment via hashing of all traffic using passive optical fibre tap-based network taps on all wires crossing the cluster perimeter; (2) retroactive challenge-response auditing where preimage data is sent to a privacy-preserving verification facility; (3) a Secure Gateway Device architecture that erases covert channels (analogue/timing side-channels, protocol header steganography) using passive optical splitters and coin-flip protocols for random number generation, eliminating any processor trusted by both Prover and Verifier.

Result: The architecture makes covert exfiltration through tapped wires infeasible. The design eliminates dual-trust processor requirements. Development costs for a demonstration device are estimated at a small engineering team over a few months with a modest bill of materials.

Conclusion: Verification infrastructure for AI data centres is feasible at relatively low cost using the proposed cryptographic commitment and covert-channel elimination architecture, providing a practical path toward compliance monitoring under future international AI agreements.

Abstract: In preparation for potential international agreements on artificial intelligence, the development of verification infrastructure for AI data centres is vital. We propose a method for cryptographically committing all information entering and leaving a data centre: Hashes are computed by network taps placed on all the information-carrying wires between the cluster and the outside world, enabling an auditor to retroactively challenge the preimage data to be sent to a privacy-preserving verification facility performing compliance checks. Our goal is to make it infeasible to covertly exfiltrate the results of undisclosed workloads in the cluster through the tapped wires. To this end, we specify the architecture of a ``Secure Gateway Device'', which handles the erasure of covert channels that post-hoc verification on hashed data cannot address: analogue and timing side-channels, as well as steganography in network protocol headers. The architecture eliminates the need for any processors trusted by both the Prover and the Verifier, leveraging passive optical fibre splitters and coin-flip protocols for random number generation where needed. We expect development costs of a demonstration device to be roughly equivalent to the cost of a small team of engineers for a few months, with a comparatively small bill of materials.

</details>


### [132] [MemVenom: Triggered Poisoning of Multimodal Memories in Web Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10742)
*Yv Zhang,Hao Sun,Hao Fang,Kuofeng Gao,Fan Mo,Bin Chen,Shu-Tao Xia,Yaowei Wang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: MemVenom is a black-box attack framework that poisons graph-structured external memory in web agents using coordinated text-image evidence, achieving persistent, reusable attacks with up to 99.15% success on GPT-5-family agents while preserving benign performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: External memory enables long-horizon reasoning in web agents but introduces a critical and overlooked vulnerability: malicious content injected into memory can be persistently recalled, repeatedly influencing agent behavior. Prior attacks focus on prompts or text-only memory, failing to exploit the multimodal, graph-structured memory used in modern web agents.

Method: MemVenom employs a two-stage design: (1) a trigger-conditioned retrieval attack that ensures high-probability recall of poisoned memory by exploiting the retrieval mechanism, and (2) a post-retrieval attack induction stage using adversarial perturbations and stealthy OCR injection to override the original user objective. The framework is black-box, requiring no model parameter modification or task re-optimization, and coordinates text and image evidence within graph-structured memory.

Result: Experiments across multiple web-agent frameworks and vision-language models show MemVenom achieves up to 99.15% attack success rate on GPT-5-family web agents, with minimal impact on benign performance. The attack transfers effectively across different architectures and model scales.

Conclusion: Multimodal memory poisoning represents a practical and severe attack surface in web-agent systems. MemVenom demonstrates that persistent, goal-agnostic, black-box attacks on graph-structured external memory are both feasible and highly effective, highlighting an urgent need for defenses in memory-augmented agent systems.

Abstract: External memory has become a core component of modern web agents, enabling long-horizon reasoning through the retrieval of past experiences. However, this paradigm introduces a critical vulnerability: malicious content injected into memory can be persistently recalled and repeatedly influence agent behavior. In this work, we identify and systematically study multimodal memory poisoning, an overlooked yet practical attack surface in web-agent systems. We propose MemVenom, a unified black-box attack framework that poisons graph-structured external memory with coordinated text-image evidence. Our method consists of a two-stage design: (1) a trigger-conditioned retrieval attack that ensures high-probability recall of malicious memory, and (2) a post-retrieval attack induction that leverages adversarial perturbations and stealthy OCR injection to override the original user objective. Unlike prior attacks that operate on prompts or text-only memory, our approach enables persistent, reusable, and goal-agnostic attacks without modifying model parameters or re-optimizing malicious tasks. Experiments across multiple web-agent frameworks and vision-language models demonstrate that MemVenom achieves strong end-to-end attack success with minimal impact on benign performance, reaching up to 99.15% on GPT-5-family web agents, while transferring effectively across architectures and model scales.

</details>


### [133] [Toward Secure LLM Agents: Threat Surfaces, Attacks, Defenses, and Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10749)
*Yuchen Ling,Shengcheng Yu,Zhenyu Chen,Chunrong Fang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: A survey of 247 papers on LLM agent security, organizing findings around a lifecycle-based framework. Key threats include prompt injection and tool-mediated control-flow hijacking, with emerging risks in persistent state corruption and multi-agent propagation. Current defenses are weakly compositional, and benchmarks underrepresent long-horizon, stateful risks. The paper argues for explicit trust boundaries, principled privilege control, and provenance-aware state management.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents are evolving from conversational interfaces into software components that plan, invoke tools, and act on external environments. This shift changes the security risk landscape beyond mere unsafe text generation to include control-flow hijacking, state corruption, and harmful external actions. Research remains fragmented across attack families, defense layers, and evaluation settings, motivating a unified synthesis.

Method: The authors conduct a systematic literature survey of 247 papers, synthesizing them through a lifecycle-based, systems-oriented framework. The framework models agent security around three core concepts: information flow, delegated authority, and persistent state. The literature is organized around four guiding questions addressing security modeling, threat surfaces/attack families, proposed defenses and their tradeoffs, and evaluation practices.

Result: Prompt injection and tool-mediated control-flow hijacking remain the dominant threats. Persistent state corruption and multi-agent propagation are emerging as central concerns. Current defenses provide useful building blocks but lack compositional strength. Existing benchmarks underrepresent long-horizon, stateful, and deployment-sensitive risks.

Conclusion: Securing LLM agents requires explicit trust boundaries, principled privilege control, provenance-aware state management, and evaluation practices that align with realistic operational settings. The paper calls for a systems-oriented security mindset rather than treating agent security as a narrow extension of LLM safety.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents are rapidly moving from conversational interfaces to software components that plan, invoke tools, maintain memory, and act on external environments. This transition changes the nature of security risk. In agentic settings, failures are no longer limited to unsafe text generation. Untrusted content may redirect control flow, misuse tool privileges, corrupt persistent state, leak sensitive information, or trigger harmful external actions. At the same time, research on LLM agent security is expanding quickly but remains fragmented across attack families, defense layers, application domains, and evaluation settings. This paper synthesizes 247 papers through a lifecycle-based, systems-oriented framework that models agent security around the interaction of information flow, delegated authority, and persistent state. We organize the literature around four questions: how LLM agent security should be modeled, which threat surfaces and attack families dominate, what defenses have been proposed and with what tradeoffs, and how security claims are evaluated. We find that prompt injection and tool-mediated control-flow hijacking still dominate the field, while persistent state corruption and multi-agent propagation are becoming central emerging concerns. We further find that current defenses provide useful building blocks but remain weakly compositional, and that existing benchmarks still underrepresent long-horizon, stateful, and deployment-sensitive risks. We argue that secure LLM agents require explicit trust boundaries, principled privilege control, provenance-aware state management, and evaluation practices aligned with realistic operational settings.

</details>


### [134] [A Bayesian Network Approach for Enhancing Security-Focused Decision Support Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10782)
*Carolina Fernández-Martínez,Shuaib Siddiqui,Vanesa Daza*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Proposes a Decision Support System (DSS) using Bayesian Networks to guide infrastructure operators in selecting appropriate security tools for heterogeneous open-source networks, capturing high-level security triad requirements and providing ranked recommendations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The growing complexity of heterogeneous, multi-component open-source network stacks demands interdisciplinary expertise across diverse tools and domains. Operators face significant cognitive burden in selecting appropriate security mechanisms. A structured, automated decision-support approach is needed to alleviate this and ensure proper security posture.

Method: A Bayesian Network-based DSS framework is designed. The system captures end-user high-level requirements across the CIA security triad (confidentiality, integrity, availability) for different domains, then runs inference on BN models to identify and rank suitable security mechanisms/tools. The architecture is presented alongside its theoretical underpinnings.

Result: The DSS framework is evaluated on performance metrics of inference time and prediction accuracy, demonstrating its viability as an understandable and extensible solution for security tool selection in heterogeneous network environments.

Conclusion: The proposed DSS effectively reduces the knowledge burden on operators by automating security mechanism selection via Bayesian inference, offering an extensible framework adaptable to varying requirements and models.

Abstract: The adoption and integration of heterogeneous stacks in most of today's open-source based networks brings clear benefits like interoperability and availability of advanced features. Yet, on the other hand the increasing number of interconnecting components and moving parts requires maintaining an ever increasing base of interdisciplinary knowledge of different tools in different domains to ensure proper operation. To alleviate such efforts, this work proposes a Decision Support System (DSS) to guide infrastructure operators through the selection of security approaches (e.g. tools) to adopt in their environments. This framework easily captures the end-user high-level requirements on the security triad for different domains and runs inference on the designated models to provide the identified tools (security mechanisms) that better serve such needs. The presented DSS aims at delivering an understandable and extensible framework to accommodate varying requirements and Bayesian Network (BN) models. The architecture and modelling of the system are proposed, aligned with its theoretical framework. Its performance is evaluated in terms of time and prediction accuracy.

</details>


### [135] [RedAct: Redacting Agent Capability Traces for Procedural Skill Protection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10813)
*Shuwen Xu,Zhitao He,Yi R.,Fung*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces CapTraceBench to quantify skill leakage from public agent execution traces and proposes RedAct, a framework that redacts protected information while preserving auditability, reducing skill transfer to near-zero and embedding robust behavioral watermarks for provenance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Public agent execution traces expose sensitive procedural skills (formulas, thresholds, strategies), enabling downstream skill recovery without access to model weights or skill files. There is a need to quantify this leakage risk and develop protection mechanisms that maintain accountability.

Method: The authors construct CapTraceBench (75 tasks, 154 skills across 7 domains) and propose RedAct, a three-component framework: (1) localization of protected key information, (2) trace rewriting that preserves verifier-critical evidence, and (3) embedding behavioral watermarks for downstream provenance analysis.

Result: RedAct reduces normalized skill transfer (NST) from 44.7–67.1% on raw traces to below the no-skill baseline. Behavioral watermarks achieve 93.6–100.0% true detection rate with ≤1.9% false alarm rate.

Conclusion: Public agent traces should be treated as security interfaces. Selective redaction can effectively mitigate procedural capability leakage while preserving necessary audit evidence.

Abstract: Users rely on execution traces to observe agent behavior, diagnose failures, and ensure accountability. These traces contain rich procedural detail, including tool invocations, intermediate decisions, and error-recovery logic. Yet this detail can expose private procedural skills, allowing downstream methods to recover key formulas, thresholds, and strategies without access to model weights or skill files. To quantify this risk and evaluate protection, we construct \textsc{CapTraceBench}, a benchmark of 75 specialized long-horizon tasks and 154 curated skills across seven domains. We also introduce \textsc{RedAct} https://github.com/XuShuwenn/RedAct, a protected trace release framework that localizes protected key information, rewrites traces while preserving verifier-critical evidence, and embeds behavioral watermarks for downstream provenance analysis. Across representative trace reuse methods, \textsc{RedAct} reduces normalized skill transfer (NST) from 44.7--67.1\% on raw traces to below the no-skill baseline, while preserving audit evidence. Its standalone behavioral watermarks reach 93.6--100.0\% true detection with a false alarm rate of at most 1.9\%. These results frame public agent traces as security interfaces and show that selective redaction can reduce procedural capability leakage without removing audit evidence.

</details>


### [136] [Training LLMs to Enforce Multi-Level Instruction Hierarchies via Gravity-Weighted Direct Preference Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10860)
*Lena S. Bolliger,Lena A. Jäger*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper addresses the structural vulnerability of LLMs treating all input tokens with uniform privilege, enabling prompt injection. It formalizes a 5-level instruction hierarchy (10 pairwise priority relations), then proposes Gravity-Weighted DPO (GW-DPO) with bilateral scheduling that weights violation severity by both privilege gap and victim level. Combined with hierarchy-specific delimiters and Instructional Segment Embeddings, GW-DPO Pareto-improves priority adherence over standard DPO while halving over-refusal rates on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Production LLMs lack a principled mechanism for resolving conflicts between instructions from sources with differing trust levels. Training-based instruction hierarchy approaches are limited: they formalize only 3–4 levels, treat all hierarchy violations as equally severe, and rarely evaluate the full pairwise interaction space. The authors aim to address these gaps with a richer hierarchy and a severity-aware optimization objective.

Method: The authors formalize a k-level instruction hierarchy problem (instantiated at k=5, yielding 10 pairwise priority relations). They introduce Gravity-Weighted DPO (GW-DPO), a preference-optimization objective where each sample's offset scales with structural distance between conflicting levels, using either a linear schedule (gap only) or a bilateral schedule (weighting severity by both the privilege gap and the privilege of the victim).
