This is combined with hierarchy-specific delimiter tokens and Instructional Segment Embeddings (ISE). Ablations isolate ISE's role as a refusal-threshold calibrator and compare 3-level vs. 5-level training as a generality-specialization tradeoff.

Result: GW-DPO with the bilateral schedule Pareto-improves over both standard DPO and the linear variant on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct. It achieves higher macro pairwise priority adherence while keeping over-refusal at approximately half the rate of standard DPO. ISE is identified as a refusal-threshold calibrator, and five-level training shows a generality-specialization tradeoff relative to three-level training.

Conclusion: A richer instruction hierarchy (5 levels) with severity-aware optimization (bilateral GW-DPO) yields better priority enforcement with fewer false refusals. ISE serves as a crucial calibration mechanism, and the expanded hierarchy represents a tradeoff between broader coverage and specialized performance.

Abstract: Production LLMs receive instructions from sources with very different levels of trust, yet attend to every token with uniform architectural privilege. This is the structural vulnerability that enables malicious prompt injections and, more broadly, leaves models without a principled way to resolve conflicts between legitimate but competing instructions. A common training-based response is to teach models an explicit instruction hierarchy; existing approaches, however, formalize hierarchies of only three or four levels, treat all violations as equally severe, and rarely evaluate the full set of pairwise level interactions. We formalize a k-level instruction hierarchy problem and instantiate it for k=5, yielding ten pairwise priority relations that a compliant model must enforce. We then introduce Gravity-Weighted DPO (GW-DPO), a preference-optimization objective whose per-sample offset scales with the structural distance between conflicting levels under a linear or bilateral schedule, the latter weighting severity by both the privilege gap and the privilege of the victim level. Combined with hierarchy-specific delimiter tokens (Chen et al., 2025) and Instructional Segment Embeddings (ISE; Wu et al., 2025), GW-DPO with the bilateral schedule Pareto-improves over standard DPO and the linear variant on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, raising macro pairwise priority adherence while keeping over-refusal at half the standard DPO rate. Ablations isolate ISE as a refusal-threshold calibrator and recast five- versus three-level training as a generality-specialization tradeoff.

</details>


### [137] [Comparative Analysis of Inference-Time Defense Methods for Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10904)
*Bulat Nutfullin,Vladimir Evgrafov,Dmitry Namiot*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: An empirical evaluation of three inference-time defenses across eight MLLMs and seven safety benchmarks (9,000 samples) finds no single defense dominates; combining defenses causes 97–100% over-refusal, while a simple safety prompt preserves utility with moderate safety gains. Results advocate for adaptive defense selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs are increasingly deployed in safety-critical applications, yet the visual modality introduces attack surfaces that text-focused safety alignment does not adequately cover. Retraining models for each new vulnerability is prohibitively expensive, motivating the need for practical, inference-time defenses that work across diverse attack classes.

Method: Comparative empirical evaluation of three inference-time defense methods (and their combinations) applied to eight models from InternVL and Qwen-VL families. Seven safety benchmarks spanning four attack classes were used, totaling 9,000 evaluation samples. A unified proxy classifier was employed for all figures. A preliminary whitebox test (n=20) examined text-level defenses against a PGD visual attack.

Result: Five key findings: (1) No single defense dominates across all settings — effectiveness depends on model baseline safety and attack type. (2) Combining defenses yields 97–100% benign-query over-refusal; SmoothVLM alone reaches 99.2–100%. (3) A simple safety prompt keeps over-refusal at 0.0–18.2% (five models under 7%) with moderate safety gains. (4) Different attack classes expose distinct weaknesses, validating multi-benchmark evaluation. (5) In whitebox tests, text-level defenses successfully suppressed a PGD visual attack by operating at the output stage where gradient optimization has limited leverage.

Conclusion: The results argue for adaptive defense selection rather than a single fixed defense configuration, as defense effectiveness is highly context-dependent on both the model and the attack type.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) now appear in safety-critical applications, but the visual channel leaves them open to adversarial attacks that predominantly text-oriented safety alignment addresses only in part. Retraining a model for each new vulnerability class is usually too expensive to be practical. We report a comparative empirical evaluation of three inference-time defense methods and their combinations, run on eight models from the InternVL and Qwen-VL families across seven safety benchmarks that span four attack classes and total 9,000 evaluation samples. Every figure below comes from the same unified proxy classifier. Five findings emerge from the evaluation. First, within the evaluated models and benchmarks, no single defense dominates across all settings: what works depends on the model's baseline safety and on the attack type. Second, combining defenses directly drives benign-query over-refusal to 97-100% across all eight evaluated models, and SmoothVLM on its own reaches 99.2-100%. Third, a simple safety prompt keeps utility largely intact (0.0-18.2% over-refusal across all eight models, five of them below 7%, although two exceeded 15%) while still yielding moderate safety gains. Fourth, different attack classes expose different weaknesses across the evaluated setup, which is why multi-benchmark evaluation matters. Fifth, in a preliminary whitebox test on two models (n=20), text-level defenses suppressed a PGD visual attack that had succeeded without any defense: the defenses act at the output stage, where gradient optimization has limited direct leverage in the tested configuration. Read together, these results argue for adaptive defense selection rather than a single fixed defense configuration.

</details>


### [138] [When Discovery Outpaces Remediation: Modeling AI-Accelerated Vulnerability Discovery in Interconnected Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11022)
*Mohamamad Reza Faghani*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: AI-accelerated vulnerability discovery creates a double-edged sword: while beneficial for defense, it can overwhelm remediation pipelines and accelerate adversarial exploitation. The paper models this tension using queueing and network theory, deriving stability conditions and showing that when discovery rates exceed remediation capacity, systemic risk grows nonlinearly. Segmentation and automation emerge as key mitigations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Advanced AI systems (for code analysis, binary analysis, fuzzing, and penetration testing) are poised to dramatically accelerate vulnerability discovery. While this aids defenders, it simultaneously risks overloading remediation pipelines and enabling faster weaponization by adversaries. The paper aims to formally model this tension to understand when and how AI-driven discovery becomes a net liability rather than a net benefit for system security.

Method: The authors construct a queueing and network-theoretic model where an enterprise is represented as a weighted dependency graph. Key components include: (1) replenishing vulnerability pools at each node, (2) finite remediation capacity with triage degradation, (3) exploit window compression as discovery accelerates, and (4) dynamic compromise propagation across the network. They derive analytical stability conditions for vulnerability backlogs and formulate a dynamic coupling between unresolved backlog and cascade risk. Mitigation strategies are evaluated via simulation.

Result: When the arrival rate of actionable discoveries exceeds remediation throughput, vulnerability backlogs grow rapidly. This backlog growth drives systemic risk increases that are nonlinear rather than proportional. In hub-dominated network topologies, segmentation reduces propagated compromise more effectively than simply increasing remediation speed. The most robust defense combines remediation automation with reduced network coupling (i.e., architectural segmentation).

Conclusion: AI-accelerated vulnerability discovery introduces a systemic risk dynamic that cannot be addressed by remediation capacity alone. Organizations must invest in both automation of remediation pipelines and architectural measures (network segmentation, dependency reduction) to maintain stability. The model provides a formal foundation for analyzing these trade-offs and guiding defensive investment.

Abstract: Advanced AI systems for code analysis, binary analysis, fuzzing orchestration, and penetration-test planningmay significantly increase the rate at which latent vulnerabilities are discovered. While improved discovery can benefit defenders, it can also overload remediation pipelines and accelerate adversarial weaponization. This paper develops a queueing and network-theoretic model of AI-accelerated vulnerability discovery in interconnected systems. We represent an enterprise as a weighted dependency graph with replenishing vulnerability pools, finite remediation capacity, triage degradation, exploit window compression, and dynamic compromise propagation. We derive stability conditions for vulnerability backlogs, formulate a dynamic coupling between unresolved backlog and cascade risk, and evaluate mitigation strategies through simulation. Results indicate that when actionable discovery arrivals exceed remediation throughput, backlogs grow rapidly and systemic risk increases nonlinearly. In hub-dominated topologies, segmentation can reduce propagated compromise more effectively than remediation speed alone, while the strongest defense combines remediation automation with reduced network coupling.

</details>


### [139] [Do Transformers Actually Help Intrusion Detection? A Temporal Sequence Evaluation on CIC-IDS2017](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11098)
*Zach Moczkodan,Hany Ragab*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper shows that evaluation methodology—specifically padding convention and train/test split protocol—has a larger impact on reported intrusion detection performance than architectural choice. On genuine sequences, Transformers achieve the highest macro-F1 (0.89), but zero-padding and random splits mask catastrophic degradation (e.g., a 67× increase in false-alarm rate), leading authors to advocate for leakage-free splits and explicit padding disclosure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many deep learning IDS papers report near-perfect results on CIC-IDS2017 using temporal architectures (RNNs, Transformers) but fail to provide genuine sequential inputs or use leakage-free evaluation. It is unclear whether reported gains reflect true temporal modeling or are artifacts of flawed methodology.

Method: The authors reformulate CIC-IDS2017 as a temporal task by constructing ordered flow sequences from network conversations. They benchmark nine architectures (classical + deep learning) under three evaluation protocols: random split, two leakage-free splits, and a padding-scheme ablation comparing non-padded windows against zero-pad+mask.

Result: Padding convention dominates Transformer performance: 0.89 macro-F1 on non-padded genuine sequences vs. a −0.24 drop under zero-pad+mask. LSTM, GRU, and 1D-CNN remain stable across padding schemes. Under leakage-free splits, Random Forest is most robust (+0.009), while Transformer false-alarm rate explodes from 0.04% to 2.7% (67× increase). Random splits with repeat-last padding can overestimate robustness by up to 0.24 macro-F1.

Conclusion: Evaluation methodology—split protocol and padding convention—exerts greater influence on reported IDS performance than model architecture. The authors advocate standardized practices: leakage-free splits, explicit padding disclosure, and sequence-aware benchmarking.

Abstract: Recent deep learning approaches for network intrusion detection increasingly incorporate temporal architectures such as recurrent networks and Transformers, often reporting near-perfect performance on CIC-IDS2017. However, many existing studies neither supply their temporal modules with genuine sequence inputs nor evaluate under realistic, leakage-free conditions, making it unclear whether reported gains arise from true sequence-modeling capability. In this work, we reformulate CIC-IDS2017 as a temporal intrusion-detection task by constructing ordered flow sequences from network conversations and benchmarking nine classical and deep learning architectures under a random split, two leakage-free splits, and a padding-scheme ablation. The central finding is that padding convention, not architecture, determines the Transformer's performance: on genuinely sequential (non-padded) windows the Transformer achieves the highest macro-F1 of any model in the experiment (0.89); under zero-pad+mask evaluation it drops markedly (-0.24 macro-F1), while LSTM, GRU, and 1D-CNN remain stable. Under leakage-free group evaluation the Random Forest is the most robust model (+0.009), while the Transformer's false-alarm rate grows from 0.04% to 2.7%, a 67-fold increase invisible under conventional protocols. These findings demonstrate that evaluation methodology -- specifically padding convention and split protocol -- has a larger effect on reported performance than architectural choice, and that widely used random splits with repeat-last padding can overestimate model robustness by up to 0.24 macro-F1. We advocate leakage-free splits, explicit padding disclosure, and sequence-aware benchmarking as standard practice in future IDS research. Code and implementation details are available at https://github.com/zachmocz/temporal-ids-bench.

</details>


### [140] [A Longitudinal Study of Recently Observed Malicious Domains: Characteristics, Infrastructure, and Abuse Patterns](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11111)
*Fathima Mashood,Mohamed Nabeel*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Longitudinal study of 1.52M malicious domains from VirusTotal (Jan–May 2026), classified as compromised vs. attacker-created. Analyzes eight behavioral dimensions using WHOIS, PDNS, and VT data, revealing concentrated abuse patterns, short-lived registrations, infrastructure exploitation, bulk registration, and brand impersonation trends.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically characterize attacker behavior across a large-scale, longitudinal malicious domain dataset and provide empirical insights into how adversaries register, host, and exploit domains for malicious purposes, thereby informing defensive strategies.

Method: Collected ~1.52M malicious domains from VirusTotal (Jan–May 2026) detected by ≥5 independent engines. Grouped into compromised (~89.3%) and attacker-created domains. Enriched with WHOIS registration records and passive DNS (PDNS) data. Analyzed across eight dimensions: temporal distribution, compromised vs. attacker classification, domain age at first detection, registrar/TLD preferences, DNS query volume (damage proxy), hosting infrastructure concentration (IP/ASN), bulk registration patterns, and brand impersonation.

Result: Key findings: (1) Attacker-created domains are predominantly short-lived, used within weeks of creation; (2) a small set of registrars and TLDs account for most abuse; (3) Cloudflare infrastructure is heavily exploited for domain fronting; (4) bulk registration events (thousands of domains from a single registrar in one day) are common; (5) global brands, especially WhatsApp and Google, are frequently impersonated.

Conclusion: Malicious domain abuse is highly concentrated across registrars, TLDs, and hosting infrastructure, with attackers favoring rapid, bulk-registered, short-lived domains and exploiting CDN infrastructure for domain fronting. The annotated dataset is publicly released to support further research.

Abstract: We present a longitudinal study of approximately 1.52 million malicious domains observed on VirusTotal (VT) between January and May 2026. Domains were selected on the basis of detection by at least five independent VT scanning engines and a first-seen date within the study window. We group the dataset into compromised domains and attacker created domains, which account for approximately 89.3% of the dataset. Combining WHOIS registration records and passive DNS (PDNS) data with the VT dataset, we characterise attacker behaviour across eight dimensions: temporal distribution, compromisedvs.attack classification, domain age at first detection, registrar and TLD preferences, DNS query volume as a damage proxy, hosting infrastructure concentration (IP and ASN level), bulk registration patterns, and brand impersonation. Key findings include: the majority of attacker created domains are short lived registrations used within weeks of creation; a small number of registrars and TLDs account for most abuse; Cloudflare infrastructure is heavily exploited for domain fronting; bulk registration events involving thousands of domains from a single registrar on a single day are widespread; and several global brands, particularly WhatsApp and Google, are heavily impersonated. We share the annotated dataset in the GitHub repo https://github.com/mufimash/malicious_domains
  for further research.

</details>


### [141] [OpenPCC: Open and Confidential LLM Serving on Commodity TEEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11145)
*Haoling Zhou,Shixuan Zhao,Chao Wang,Zhiqiang Lin*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper presents OpenPCC, an open-source confidential cloud inference framework for LLMs that uses commodity TEEs instead of proprietary hardware, addressing the privacy risks of sending sensitive user requests to cloud-based LLM APIs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-powered services require users to send requests containing sensitive personal or enterprise data to Cloud Inference Services (CIS). Existing proprietary solutions (Apple PCC, Google Private AI Compute) rely on closed hardware ecosystems and contain design flaws, making them non-adoptable by others and insufficient for true end-to-end privacy protection.

Method: The authors first analyze fundamental requirements for building a secure and open CIS. They then design and implement OpenPCC, a confidential CIS framework leveraging commercially available Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) rather than proprietary hardware. The prototype is evaluated end-to-end on a Llama-3 8B vLLM workload, with costs attributed separately between OpenPCC's overhead and the underlying TEE hardware.

Result: The analysis and experimental characterization on a Llama-3 8B vLLM workload demonstrate the feasibility and security of the OpenPCC framework, showing that confidential CIS can be built without proprietary hardware while maintaining practical performance.

Conclusion: OpenPCC proves that a secure, open, and adoptable confidential cloud inference service is feasible using commodity TEEs, addressing the limitations of proprietary alternatives and paving the way for broader adoption of privacy-preserving LLM inference.

Abstract: Generative AI applications such as personal AI agents, image generators, and chat assistants offer advanced capabilities to improve user experience. Behind the scenes, Large Language Models (LLMs) that power these services require a massive amount of computation and are usually deployed in the cloud, available as APIs, meaning that a user's request has to be sent to a Cloud Inference Service (CIS) for processing. However, the strong capabilities of LLM also mean that user's requests now contain much more personal sensitive or enterprise confidential information, demanding equally strong protection in CIS. While early industry efforts such as Apple Private Cloud Compute (PCC) and Google Private AI Compute have emerged to show the potential of secure CIS, they are not adoptable for deployment by others due to their reliance on proprietary hardware and closed ecosystem. In addition, they all suffer from their own design glitches that can undermine the ambitious goal of bringing in true privacy protection to end users. In this paper, we present our analysis of the fundamental requirements of building a secure yet open CIS. We then present OpenPCC, a Confidential CIS framework that does not rely on proprietary hardware but instead uses commercially available TEEs. We implement an open-source prototype and characterize it end-to-end on a Llama-3 8B vLLM workload, separating OpenPCC's own cost from the underlying TEE hardware. Our analysis and evaluation demonstrated the feasibility and security of the system.

</details>


### [142] [Anchors that Don't Lift: Understanding Supply Chain Driven Kernel Lock-In and Governance-Mediated Mitigation Strategies in SOHO Devices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11175)
*Ritwik Badola,Rajdeep Ghosh,Ashita Gupta,Chester Rebeiro,Mainack Mondal*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: SOHO devices suffer from security vulnerabilities due to outdated Linux kernels inherited through the supply chain. The study uses template-based CVE detection on 900+ GPL firmware releases across 306 devices, revealing that SoC vendor SDKs lock manufacturers into end-of-life kernel versions, creating a "vulnerability debt" passed down to end users. Only community-engaged SoC vendors offer viable mitigation paths; regulatory compliance alone is insufficient.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work merely extracted Linux kernel versions and mapped CVEs by version number, leaving it unclear how many anticipated vulnerabilities actually exist in heavily customized SOHO kernels, and what barriers prevent kernel updates. This paper addresses both gaps.

Method: (1) High-precision template-based CVE detection on GPL source releases of 900+ firmwares from 306 SOHO devices; (2) Supply chain tracing at scale from SoC vendors → ODM/OEM → router/IP-camera vendors; (3) Analysis of mitigation potential via social media posts, regulations, and community governance efforts.

Result: All five SoC vendors in the dataset used SDKs with Linux kernels that had reached EoL more than a year before deployment in SOHO devices. Kernel lock-in creates a systemic vulnerability debt inherited along the supply chain. Regulatory compliance proved insufficient for mitigation; only SoC vendors engaging with open-source communities for kernel upgrades demonstrated a viable path forward.

Conclusion: Kernel lock-in driven by SoC SDKs is a systemic, supply-chain-wide security issue affecting SOHO devices. Addressing it requires community engagement by SoC vendors rather than reliance on regulation alone.

Abstract: Small Office/Home Office (SOHO) devices are widely popular, yet often attacked due to security vulnerabilities in their firmware, affecting thousands of devices. These security vulnerabilities often stem from outdated Linux kernel versions included in SOHO device firmware. Naturally, prior work audited the extent and impact of this issue by simple Linux version extraction and version number based vulnerability mapping. However, it is unclear how many of these anticipated vulnerabilities actually exist in the heavily customized SOHO kernels and if there are any barriers towards updating Linux kernels in SOHO firmwares.
  To address this gap, we uncover actual kernel-related vulnerabilities found in 306 SOHO devices using a high-precision template-based CVE detection mechanism on GPL source releases of more than 900 firmwares from these devices. Next, as a first, we traced the supply chain of these vulnerable SOHO devices at scale and identify kernel lock-in as a significant security issue -- SOHO vendors are effectively locked to specific (often older) kernel versions due to the system-on-chip (SoC) SDKs they use. This kernel lock-in produces a vulnerability debt that is inherited along the supply chain from SoC vendor to firmware creators (ODM/OEM) to router/IP-camera vendor and ultimately borne by end users. All five SoC vendors in our dataset had used SDKs with Linux kernels that had reached EoL more than a year before their usage in a SOHO device. Finally, we explore the mitigation-potential of individual, regulatory and community governance by analyzing social media posts, regulations and community efforts. Our results show that regulation compliance is insufficient and only SoC vendors who engage with communities for kernel upgradation offered a viable path towards mitigation. The data and code for this work is available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20433799

</details>


<div id='cs.HC'></div>

# cs.HC [[Back]](#toc)

### [143] [AI-Driven Analytics of Team-Teaching Talk: Acoustic Patterns across Experience, Cohorts and the Learning Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09831)
*Yuchen Liu,Roberto Martinez-Maldonado,Riordan Alfredo,Paola Mejia-Domenzain,Dwi Rahayu,Sadia Nawaz*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: Using AI-based speech processing on 36 recorded team-teaching sessions, the study finds systematic acoustic differences—particularly in loudness variation—across teacher experience levels, student cohorts, and task designs, with high-experience teachers, undergraduate classes, and collaborative tasks showing greater modulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Team teaching is increasingly common but poorly understood at the micro-process level. Existing research relies on retrospective self-reports or small-scale observations, limiting insight into real-time enactment. Teacher talk—especially acoustic features like voice quality, intonation, and loudness—offers a scalable lens, but manual coding is impractical in multi-teacher classrooms. The study addresses these gaps by leveraging automated speech analysis grounded in spatial pedagogy theory.

Method: The authors recorded 36 undergraduate and postgraduate team-teaching sessions involving 12 teachers. Spatial pedagogy behaviours were manually coded, and acoustic features were extracted using an AI-based speech processing pipeline. Variation was then examined across three dimensions: teacher experience level, student cohort (undergraduate vs. postgraduate), and learning task design.

Result: Systematic differences emerged across all three dimensions. The most pronounced finding was in loudness dynamics: high-experience teachers, undergraduate classes, and collaborative learning tasks all exhibited greater loudness variation, interpreted as more frequent volume modulation to highlight key information and foster engagement and interaction.

Conclusion: Automated acoustic analysis can scalably capture meaningful differences in how team teaching is enacted, revealing that loudness modulation serves as a pedagogically significant behaviour shaped by teacher experience, student level, and task design. The approach opens new avenues for researching and improving team-teaching practice.

Abstract: As classroom cohorts expand, team teaching is increasingly used to integrate the expertise and pedagogical perspectives of multiple teachers. Yet, there is limited empirical understanding of how team teaching unfolds in practice, particularly regarding differences in teachers' contributions across experience levels, student cohorts, and learning task design. Prior research on team teaching has largely relied on retrospective self-reports or small-scale observations, offering limited insight into the micro-level processes through which team teaching is enacted. Teacher talk offers a scalable lens on these processes. While research in individual teaching contexts shows that acoustic features of speech (e.g., voice quality, intonation, and loudness) can shape student learning, evidence from team-teaching settings remains scarce. Moreover, capturing such features through manual observation or transcription is especially challenging in team-teaching classrooms, where multiple teachers speak across extended sessions and spatial locations, limiting scalability without automation. Grounded in spatial pedagogy theory and team-teaching research, this paper presents an AI-based speech processing approach to analyse classroom talk in team-teaching settings. We analysed 36 recorded undergraduate and postgraduate sessions involving 12 teachers. Spatial pedagogy behaviours were coded and acoustic features extracted to examine variation across teachers' experience, student cohorts, and the learning task design. The results reveal systematic differences, most notably in loudness dynamics: high-experience teachers, undergraduate classes and collaborative learning tasks exhibited greater loudness variation, suggesting more frequent modulation of volume to foreground key information and support classroom interaction and engagement.

</details>


### [144] [Agentic Social Affordance Framework (ASAF): Agent Identity Design as a Collaboration Interface in Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09832)
*Meng-Han Lee*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: Introduces the Agentic Social Affordance Framework (ASAF), arguing that agent social identity in multi-agent systems constitutes an independent design dimension — orthogonal to engineering orchestration — that shapes human collaboration behavior through three mechanisms: Identity Signaling, Behavioral Priming, and Collaborative Governance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multi-agent AI system design overlooks how the social identity of individual agents shapes human collaborative behavior. Existing frameworks like CASA are dyadic and cannot explain topology-level effects in multi-agent settings.

Method: Theoretical framework development: ASAF extends Social Affordance theory to multi-agent contexts, proposing three core mechanisms, a four-tier Identity Signal Fidelity Spectrum as boundary conditions, and anthropomorphizing vs. instrumentalizing cognitive style as a moderating variable. Contrasts ASAF with existing affordance theory and the CASA paradigm.

Result: ASAF establishes that social affordance and engineering orchestration represent two orthogonal, mutually irreducible design decision spaces. It provides specific, testable mechanisms and boundary conditions for how agent identity design functions as a collaboration interface.

Conclusion: Agent identity design is a distinct collaboration interface, not merely a UI convention. Empirical validation is needed, with a proposed factorial design to test the orthogonality of the two design spaces.

Abstract: As AI systems evolve from single conversational agents to complex multi-agent architectures, a critical design dimension has been overlooked: how the social identity of individual agents shapes human behavior within the collaboration. This paper introduces the Agentic Social Affordance Framework (ASAF), a theoretical framework that extends Social Affordance theory into the context of multi-agent AI systems. We propose that agent identity design functions not merely as a user interface convention, but as a collaboration interface -- structuring how users perceive, approach, and engage with each agent, and thereby influencing the quality of Human-Agent collaboration outcomes. Specifically, the social affordance layer constitutes an independent design dimension orthogonal to engineering orchestration: the two represent distinct decision spaces that cannot be derived from each other. ASAF comprises three mechanisms: Identity Signaling, Behavioral Priming, and Collaborative Governance, and specifies their boundary conditions through a four-tier Identity Signal Fidelity Spectrum and an individual-difference moderating variable (anthropomorphizing vs.\ instrumentalizing cognitive style). We situate ASAF in relation to existing affordance theory and the CASA paradigm, delineating where ASAF's multi-agent, topology-level predictions exceed the explanatory scope of dyadic frameworks. We discuss implications for multi-agent system design and outline directions for future empirical validation, including a factorial design for testing design-space orthogonality.

</details>


### [145] [CollabSkill: Evaluating Human-Agent Collaboration On Real-World Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09833)
*Yijia Shao,Zora Zhiruo Wang,Neel Ahuja,Yicheng Wang,Bowen Liu,Diyi Yang*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: CollabSkill is a benchmark framework that evaluates human-AI collaboration on real occupational tasks by pairing workers with AI agents, using a Bayesian skill rating system to disentangle human and agent contributions. Analysis of 1,500+ prompts from 93 workers reveals Claude Code outperforms Codex in collaborative settings (diverging from autonomous benchmarks), and that practical hands-on experience—not just AI knowledge—drives collaboration skill.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current occupational task evaluation largely ignores human-agent collaboration due to (1) the difficulty of collecting real human data on economically valuable tasks, and (2) the challenge of accounting for inter-human variability. Fully autonomous benchmarks fail to capture how AI agents augment human workers in practice.

Method: CollabSkill pairs real human workers (matched by occupational background) with AI agents on authentic tasks. Data is collected from 386 working sessions producing 1,500+ prompts from 93 workers. A Bayesian skill rating system is then applied to statistically disentangle and separately quantify the skill contributions of humans and AI agents, isolating each party's effect on collaborative performance.

Result: Two key findings: (1) Agent rankings on CollabSkill diverge meaningfully from fully autonomous benchmarks—Claude Code ranks first, whereas Codex leads on autonomous benchmarks. (2) On the human side, practical hands-on experience emerges as the primary driver of collaboration skill, significantly shifting workers' AI literacy beyond what mere AI knowledge provides.

Conclusion: CollabSkill provides a systematic evaluation framework for human-agent collaboration on occupational tasks. The authors hope it enables the research community to invest in collaborative benchmarking and spurs development of AI agents that genuinely augment human workers, rather than merely optimizing for fully autonomous performance.

Abstract: AI agents are reshaping the workspace, leading to drastic change of how humans work. Despite the considerable potential of human-agent collaboration both in preserving human agency and generating economic value, this paradigm remains largely absent from occupational task evaluation, hindered by the difficulty of gathering real human data and accounting for inter-human variability. We introduce CollabSkill, a framework for evaluating human-agent collaboration on real-world occupational tasks. CollabSkill pairs real human workers with AI agents on tasks matched to their occupational background, collecting data that capture the complexity of economically valuable tasks and the usage patterns of real workers. To account for inter-human variability, CollabSkill employs a Bayesian skill rating system to disentangle and quantify the skill contributions of both humans and AI agents. Drawing on over 1,500 prompts from 386 working sessions contributed by 93 human workers, our analysis yields insights on two fronts: on the agent side, rankings on CollabSkill diverge meaningfully from those of existing fully autonomous benchmarks where Codex leads, with Claude Code ranking first; on the human side, CollabSkill reveals that practical experience emerges as the primary driver of collaboration skill, with hands-on collaboration meaningfully shifting workers' AI literacy. Together, we hope CollabSkill enables the community to invest in systematic evaluation of human-agent collaboration and spurs development efforts aimed at building AI agents that genuinely augment human workers.

</details>


### [146] [Weather Synchronization in Digital Twin Environments for Shared VR Experience Using Commercial Metaverse Platforms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09834)
*Masanori Ibara,Yuichi Hiroi,Takushi Kamegai,Yusuke Masubuchi,Kazuki Matsutani,Megumi Zaizen,Junya Morita,Takefumi Hiraki*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: A weather synchronization system bridges physical and virtual environments by streaming real-time IoT sensor data (temperature, humidity, precipitation, wind, solar radiation) from a smart building into a metaverse platform, generating dynamic sky, precipitation particles, and ambient audio with 0.8–1.0s latency to enhance atmospheric immersion in shared VR spaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current digital twin implementations on metaverse platforms focus on IoT data visualization but fail to translate environmental conditions into meaningful sensory experiences. Weather — a key factor influencing spatial perception — lacks authentic representation in shared virtual environments, limiting user presence and immersion.

Method: The authors developed a synchronization pipeline between "The GEAR" smart building's environmental sensors and the Cluster metaverse platform. Real-time measurements of temperature, humidity, precipitation, wind speed, and solar radiation are processed and mapped to corresponding virtual effects: dynamic sky rendering, precipitation particle systems, and ambient audio modulation. Performance was evaluated by measuring end-to-end response latency.

Result: The system achieved practical response times of 0.8–1.0 seconds for weather data transmission and reflection within the virtual environment, demonstrating feasibility for real-time bidirectional physical-digital synchronization of atmospheric conditions.

Conclusion: This work establishes a foundational approach for seamless physical-digital space integration through weather-aware digital twins, with potential to enhance remote collaboration efficiency and enable more dynamic discussions in shared virtual workspaces.

Abstract: Digital twin technology creates bidirectional synchronization between physical and virtual environments, yet current implementations fail to provide authentic environmental experiences that enhance user presence in shared virtual spaces. While digital twin environments using commercial metaverse platforms for IoT sensor data visualization have been proposed, translating environmental information into meaningful sensory experiences remains largely unexplored, particularly lacking approaches for weather conditions that significantly influence spatial perception. We developed a weather synchronization system that integrates real-time environmental data from ``The GEAR'' smart building with the Cluster metaverse platform, enabling shared VR experiences with authentic atmospheric immersion. Our system processes temperature, humidity, precipitation, wind speed, and solar radiation measurements to generate corresponding virtual environmental effects including dynamic sky rendering, precipitation particles, and ambient audio modulation. Performance evaluation demonstrated practical response times of 0.8-1.0 seconds for weather data transmission and reflection in the virtual environment. This work establishes a foundation for seamless physical-digital space integration, potentially enhancing remote collaboration efficiency and facilitating more dynamic discussions in shared virtual workspaces.

</details>


### [147] [Thinking Inside the Box: Considerations for Putting Data Physicalization Workshops in a Box](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09835)
*Derya Akbaba,Camilla Svensson,Claudia Torelli,Martin Callmeryd,Miriah Meyer*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: Introduces "workshop-in-a-box," a design concept that enables non-experts to independently run data physicalization workshops without a visualization researcher present, and provides material/procedural guidelines based on a design study with a social innovation organization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional visualization workshops depend heavily on the presence of a researcher with deep visualization expertise, limiting scalability and broader societal impact. The authors aim to democratize data physicalization workshops by decoupling them from researcher facilitation.

Method: A design study conducted with a socially innovative organization, where the authors designed workshop materials and procedures that their collaborator (a non-researcher) used to independently run multiple data physicalization workshops. This is supplemented by two additional case studies to validate the workshop-in-a-box concept.

Result: The authors derive a set of material and procedural considerations for packaging data physicalization workshops into a self-contained "box" that non-experts can deploy. The approach was successfully validated through the collaborator-led workshops and two further case studies.

Conclusion: The workshop-in-a-box concept offers a viable pathway for extending visualization research beyond academia, enabling broader community engagement while maintaining pedagogical and research integrity without requiring a researcher's physical presence.

Abstract: Visualization researchers utilize workshops both for applied research and to engage different populations with visualization-based activities. While there are many benefits to running visualization workshops, their utility and impact rely on the presence of a researcher who has deep knowledge about visualization theory and practice. In this work, we introduce workshop-in-a-box as a design concept intended to challenge the researcher-centric approach to data physicalization workshops. Through a design study with a socially innovative organization, we deployed several data physicalization workshops that our collaborator ran instead of us. Based on this experience, along with two accompanying case studies that validate the concept, we present material and procedural considerations for how to put data physicalization workshops into a box and the implications it has for extending visualization research outside the bounds of academia.

</details>


### [148] [Equanimity in HRI: Applying Calm Technology Principles to Human-Robot Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09836)
*Barbara Sienkiewicz,Bipin Indurkhya*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper proposes integrating Calm Technology principles into household assistive robotics to ensure human-robot interactions are non-intrusive, harmonious, and supportive of mental equanimity, offering concrete design guidelines and features for domestic HRI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The pervasive influence of technology in contemporary life is negatively affecting cognitive capabilities. There is a pressing need for responsible robotics and ethical design that prioritizes human well-being, particularly equanimity, in the most private of all spaces — the home.

Method: The paper adapts the established principles of Calm Technology to the domain of domestic robots. It develops comprehensive design guidelines and provides concrete examples and feature specifications for assistive robots that operate in a calm, non-intrusive, and harmonious manner within the household.

Result: The paper presents a set of comprehensive design guidelines and concrete feature examples for integrating Calm Technology into household assistive robotics. The outcomes aim to foster balanced, unobtrusive human-robot interaction in the home environment.

Conclusion: Calm Technology principles are well-suited for domestic HRI and can guide the development of assistive robots that preserve human equanimity. The work establishes a foundation for future applications and further research into calm, harmonious home robotics.

Abstract: This paper explores how {\textit{Calm Technology}} can be integrated into Human-Robot Interaction (HRI), with a particular focus on the household environment. It offers comprehensive guidelines for designing assistive robots that prioritize and enhance the human need for {\textit{equanimity}}, ensuring interactions are calm, non-intrusive, and harmonious. The paper examines the widespread influence of technology in contemporary life and its impact on cognitive capabilities, underscoring the need for responsible robotics and ethical considerations in future technological developments. By adapting {\textit{Calm Technology}} principles to domestic robots, the article provides concrete examples and features that should be employed in household assistive robotics. The goal is to foster a balanced, unobtrusive interaction between humans and robots, especially in the home environment, as it is the most privat environment in everyone's life, paving the way for applications and further research in the field.

</details>


### [149] [Envisioning Sensemaking in Multi-Human, Multi-Agent Collaborative Knowledge Work](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09840)
*Zhitong Guan,Soo Young Rieh*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: Generative AI is reshaping collaborative sensemaking by taking over interpretive tasks like summarization and synthesis, which complicates how teams divide labor, build trust, and construct shared understanding. The paper proposes five design principles and a multi-agent conceptual framework for human-AI collaborative sensemaking that preserves provenance, authorship, and the traceable evolution of interpretations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative AI systems now perform interpretive functions (summarization, synthesis, thematic grouping) that were traditionally the domain of knowledge workers. In collaborative settings, this introduces new challenges around how teams divide interpretive labor, trust contributions, and negotiate shared understanding. Current GenAI systems tend to obscure the provenance and evolution of interpretations, threatening coherent knowledge construction.

Method: This is a position paper that articulates a conceptual argument. The authors: (1) analyze how GenAI reshapes collaborative sensemaking; (2) propose five design principles — dynamic multi-layer information representations, active identification and bridging of understanding gaps, critical engagement with information, verifiability, and accountability; and (3) introduce a conceptual framework centered on a dynamic shared representational workspace with three specialized AI agents (partner agent, shared space agent, orchestrator agent) that jointly gather evidence, schematize, hypothesize, and pursue goals while preserving provenance and authorship.

Result: The paper contributes five prescriptive design principles for multi-human, multi-agent collaborative sensemaking and a conceptual multi-agent framework featuring a partner agent, shared space agent, and orchestrator agent that together maintain provenance, trace interpretive evolution, and support coherent negotiated knowledge construction — capabilities that current GenAI systems typically obscure.

Conclusion: GenAI's integration into collaborative knowledge work demands deliberate system design that makes interpretation traceable, verifiable, and accountable. The proposed principles and multi-agent framework offer a path toward collaborative sensemaking systems that augment rather than undermine the coherence and trustworthiness of shared knowledge construction.

Abstract: Sensemaking is central to knowledge work, where people search, evaluate, interpret, and use information over time to construct durable understanding. The rise of generative AI has begun to reshape this process: GenAI systems now perform interpretive functions such as summarization, synthesis, and thematic grouping that knowledge workers have traditionally carried out themselves. In collaborative settings, these shifts compound, complicating how teams divide interpretive labor, trust one another's contributions, and negotiate shared understanding. In this position paper, we examine how GenAI reshapes sensemaking in collaborative knowledge work and propose five design principles for multi-human, multi-agent collaborative sensemaking: dynamic multi-layer information representations, active identification and bridging of gaps in understanding, critical engagement with information, verifiability, and accountability. Building on these principles, we introduce a conceptual framework for a dynamic shared representational workspace in which knowledge workers and specialized AI agents jointly gather evidence, schematize, hypothesize, and pursue collaborative goals. Through a partner agent, a shared space agent, and an orchestrator agent, the framework preserves the provenance and authorship of contributions and traces the evolution of both individual and shared interpretations, supporting coherent, negotiated knowledge construction that current generative AI systems tend to obscure.

</details>


### [150] [Self-EmoQ: Plutchik-Guided Value-based Planning to Drive Streaming Emotional TTS](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09837)
*Yue Zhao,Hongyan Li,Yong Chen,Luo Ji*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: A plug-and-play LLM module trained via reinforcement learning determines emotions before text generation, driving streaming emotional TTS. It uses Plutchik's wheel of emotions with a hybrid reward (imitation + theory-driven) and outperforms baselines on multiple datasets, with a real-time streaming pipeline demonstrating emotional alignment and expressive fluency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current conversational AI systems lack a self-emotion determination mechanism to drive streaming text-to-speech synthesis. Emotional interaction is increasingly crucial, yet existing systems cannot autonomously decide what emotion to express before generating speech in a streaming, real-time manner.

Method: An emotion-planning framework where emotion is determined prior to textual generation using a plug-and-play LLM module initialized from pretrained LLMs. The module is trained via reinforcement learning, treating emotions as actions. A hybrid reward function combines imitation learning signals with theory-driven scoring based on Plutchik's wheel of emotions. The determined emotion then grounds downstream emotional TTS in a streaming pipeline.

Result: The method outperforms prompting and finetuning baselines on both emotion determination accuracy and response quality across four datasets: DailyDialog, EmoryNLP, IEMOCAP, and MELD. A real-time streaming deployment pipeline was implemented, with speech quality confirming emotional alignment, contextual coherence, and expressive fluency.

Conclusion: Incorporating an emotion-planning mechanism via RL-trained LLMs with theory-grounded rewards effectively bridges conversational AI and emotional TTS, enabling real-time, emotionally coherent streaming speech synthesis. The plug-and-play nature facilitates practical deployment.

Abstract: Emotional interaction is increasingly crucial for conversational AI, yet current systems lack a self-emotion determination mechanism to drive the streaming text-to-speech (TTS) synthesis. We propose an emotion-planning framework that determines the emotion prior to the textual generation, grounding the downstream emotional TTS in a streaming manner. The framework is implemented by a plug-and-play LLM module, initialized from pretrained LLMs, and trained by reinforcement learning (RL) with emotions as the actions. A hybrid reward is employed which combines imitation signals with theory-driven scoring, in which the theory of Plutchik's wheel of emotions is adopted. By experiments on DailyDialog, EmoryNLP, IMEOCAP, and MELD, our method outperforms prompting and finetuning baselines on both emotion determination and response quality. We finally implement an entire streaming pipeline for real-time deployment, with the speech quality confirming the framework's emotional alignment, contextual coherence, and expressive fluency. Codes, cases, and demos are available in https://sixingdeguo.github.io/EmoQ-page/.

</details>


### [151] [LLM-Based Code Documentation Generation and Multi-Judge Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09852)
*Ikbel Ghrab,Mohamed Dhieb,Ismail Khenissi,Ines Abdeljaoued-Tej*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: An AI-powered framework using eight LLMs automates source code documentation generation for healthcare software, with a MultiLLMasJudges evaluation system assessing outputs across nine quality criteria, revealing a 42% performance gap between models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-quality source code documentation is critical yet frequently neglected in healthcare software, where reliability and maintainability are paramount. Manual documentation is labor-intensive and error-prone, motivating an automated, AI-driven solution.

Method: The framework is built on the PocketFlow orchestration framework, employing modular pipelines and advanced prompt engineering. Eight state-of-the-art LLMs (GPT, Gemini, Qwen, LLaMA variants) generate structured, context-aware documentation from code and repositories. The novel MultiLLMasJudges evaluation framework uses four independent LLMs to score outputs across nine criteria: Completeness, Clarity, Faithfulness, and others.

Result: Experiments on an open-source medical physics library revealed a 42% performance gap between the top-performing and bottom-performing models. Combining diverse model outputs, optimized prompting, and rigorous evaluation enhanced overall documentation quality while reducing manual effort.

Conclusion: The proposed approach demonstrates that orchestrated LLM-based documentation generation, coupled with robust multi-LLM evaluation, significantly improves documentation quality in safety-critical healthcare software contexts, reducing manual burden and guiding optimal model selection.

Abstract: High-quality source code documentation is vital yet often neglected, especially in critical domains like healthcare where reliability and maintainability are essential. We presented an AI powered framework that automates documentation generation from code and repositories using eight state of the art Large Language Models (LLMs), including GPT, Gemini, Qwen, and LLaMA variants. Built on the PocketFlow orchestration framework, the system applies modular pipelines and advanced prompt engineering to produce structured, context aware documentation. To ensure quality and guide model selection, we introduced a MultiLLMasJudges evaluation framework, where four independent LLMs assess outputs across nine criteria, such as Completeness, Clarity, and Faithfulness. Experiments conducted on an open-source medical physics library, demonstrated showed a 42% performance gap between top and bottom models. By combining diverse model outputs, optimized prompting, and rigorous evaluation, our approach enhances documentation quality and reduces manual effort, especially in safety critical healthcare software.

</details>


### [152] [Popularity Without Legitimacy? Comparing Trust in Television Meteorologists and YouTube Weatherfluencers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09838)
*Julie A. Vera,David W. McDonald,Mark Zachry*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This mixed-methods study empirically compares audience evaluations of broadcast meteorologists versus YouTube "weatherfluencers" during severe weather events. While broadcasters were rated higher on credibility, legitimacy, and safety utility, weatherfluencers achieved parity on objectivity. Despite lower formal authority, weatherfluencer audiences are growing, revealing a decoupling between audience attention and professional authorization that existing crisis communication models fail to explain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: During severe weather crises, individuals must rapidly interpret evolving information to make time-sensitive safety decisions. While broadcast meteorologists have traditionally served as credentialed intermediaries, a new class of independent YouTube "weatherfluencers" has emerged as prominent real-time interpreters for large audiences. The study is motivated by the need to empirically compare how viewers evaluate these two distinct information sources, given that existing crisis communication models do not adequately account for the apparent decoupling between audience attention and official/professional authorization.

Method: The study employs a mixed-methods design, combining quantitative and qualitative approaches. Quantitatively, it compares viewer ratings of broadcast meteorologists versus YouTube weatherfluencers across four dimensions: credibility, legitimacy, objectivity, and practical utility. Qualitative findings are used to illuminate the underlying mechanisms and reasoning behind these evaluative judgments.

Result: Broadcast meteorologists were consistently rated significantly higher than weatherfluencers on credibility, legitimacy, and safety utility. However, weatherfluencers achieved statistical parity with broadcasters on perceived objectivity. Critically, weatherfluencer audiences continue to grow despite lower formal authorization, revealing a decoupling between audience attention and professional/official legitimacy that prevailing crisis communication models do not fully capture.

Conclusion: The study concludes that hybrid information ecosystems during crisis events exhibit a critical disjuncture: audience attention flows toward sources (weatherfluencers) that lack traditional professional authorization, yet these sources are perceived as comparably objective. This finding challenges existing crisis communication frameworks and carries significant implications for designing emergency communication strategies that account for the realities of a fragmented, platform-driven media environment.

Abstract: During severe weather events, people must interpret rapidly evolving information to make time-sensitive safety decisions. Broadcast meteorologists have traditionally served as credentialed intermediaries within established media organizations, while independent "weatherfluencers" on YouTube have emerged as prominent real-time interpreters for large and growing audiences. This mixed-methods study provides one of the first empirical comparisons of how viewers evaluate broadcast meteorologists against YouTube weatherfluencers across credibility, legitimacy, objectivity, and practical utility. Broadcast meteorologists were consistently rated higher on credibility, legitimacy, and safety utility, while weatherfluencers achieved parity on objectivity. Yet weatherfluencer audiences continue to grow, revealing a critical decoupling between audience attention and official or professional authorization that existing crisis communication models do not fully account for. Qualitative findings illuminate the mechanisms underlying these judgments and their implications for emergency communication in hybrid information ecosystems.

</details>


### [153] [Aesthetic Perspectives in Information Systems Research: A Hermeneutic Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09839)
*Angelina Chen,Rick Sullivan,Raffaele F Ciriello*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: IS research is shaped by implicit aesthetic perspectives that determine what counts as legitimate inquiry. The paper identifies four such perspectives—imitation, sensory experience, world-making, and political doing—and shows how they function as epistemic infrastructure, using algorithmic management and digital intimacy as illustrative domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To surface and make explicit the foundational aesthetic assumptions that implicitly condition what IS scholarship recognises as worthy of study, thereby revealing hidden constraints on the field's horizons of inquiry.

Method: A hermeneutic literature analysis that interprets and surfaces aesthetic assumptions embedded in IS research, identifying four distinct perspectives and applying them as an analytical framework to two illustrative domains.

Result: Four aesthetic perspectives are identified—aesthetics as imitation, sensory experience, world-making, and political doing—each shaping what IS scholars perceive and appreciate. Applied to algorithmic management and digitally mediated intimacy, these perspectives reveal new research questions and expose dimensions overlooked by dominant framings.

Conclusion: Aesthetic philosophy is positioned as essential epistemic infrastructure for IS literature. Making aesthetic assumptions explicit provides a vocabulary for understanding how perspectives shape theorising, method, and contribution, and opens alternative avenues for future research.

Abstract: How might implicit aesthetic perspectives shape what Information Systems (IS) scholarship recognises as worthy of study (or not)? In this hermeneutic literature analysis, we surface foundational aesthetic assumptions underpinning IS research. We identify four perspectives (aesthetics as imitation, sensory experience, world-making, and political doing) that guide how IS scholars perceive and appreciate sociotechnical phenomena. These perspectives influence what becomes recognisable as legitimate research and what remains unseen. By making aesthetic assumptions explicit, we show how they form epistemic infrastructure that conditions horizons of inquiry. We apply this framework to algorithmic management and digitally mediated intimacy, revealing how alternative perspectives open new research questions whilst exposing dimensions that dominant framings overlook. This analysis foregrounds the importance of aesthetic philosophy to IS literature, offering a vocabulary for articulating how aesthetic perspectives shape theorising, method, and contribution.

</details>


### [154] [Human-Centered AI for Safe Shuttle Car Routing in Underground Room-and-Pillar Coal Mines Using Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09841)
*Bryant Pollard*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: A human-centered AI decision-support system using Graph Neural Networks was developed to recommend safe routes for shuttle car operators in underground coal mining. Through iterative participatory design, usability testing, and interpretability integration, the system evolved into a transparent, safety-supportive tool that demonstrably improved operator performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Shuttle car operators in underground room-and-pillar coal mining face safety-critical routing decisions under conditions of low visibility, dynamic miner movement, congestion, and limited real-time information. Existing approaches lack effective decision-support tailored to these challenging operational constraints, motivating a human-centered AI solution.

Method: The system employs a Graph Neural Network (GNN) trained on expert-informed synthetic data for route recommendation, deployed via a browser-based interface with cloud inference. The development process integrated interviews, participatory design, usability testing, interaction logs, and SHAP-based interpretability analysis. The interface evolved to include inline user feedback, blockage reporting, audio cues, and explainability features across iterative versions.

Result: Evaluation across two usability sessions with six participants demonstrated that the later version achieved improved task completion rates, faster response times, fewer errors, higher usability scores, lower cognitive load, and stronger agreement with AI recommendations compared to the earlier version.

Conclusion: The paper demonstrates that human-centered design can transform an AI routing prototype into a more transparent, auditable, and safety-supportive decision-support system, contributing a focused example of how user-centered methodologies shape both interface and model development in high-stakes industrial settings.

Abstract: Underground room-and-pillar coal mining requires shuttle car operators to make safety-critical routing decisions under conditions of low visibility, dynamic miner movement, congestion, and limited real-time information. This paper presents a human-centered AI decision-support system that recommends safe shuttle car routes using a Graph Neural Network (GNN) trained on expert-informed synthetic data and deployed through a browser-based interface backed by cloud inference services. Rather than making a purely model-centric contribution, the paper shows how interviews, participatory design, usability testing, interaction logs, and interpretability analysis shaped both the interface and the AI model. The resulting application evolved beyond route recommendation to include inline user feedback, blockage reporting, audio cues, and SHAP-based interpretability within a single interactive system. Evaluation across two usability sessions with six participants showed improved task completion, faster response times, fewer errors, higher usability scores, lower cognitive load, and stronger agreement with AI recommendations in the later version. The paper contributes a focused example of how human-centered design can transform an AI routing prototype into a more transparent, auditable, and safety-supportive decision-support system.

</details>


### [155] [Integrated Real-Time Motion Tracking and AI Analysis for Athletic Performance Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09842)
*Parth Agrawal,Ronit,Sagar Kumar,Aashish Bhambri*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This survey paper reviews real-time human pose estimation (HPE) approaches for sports analysis, comparing marker-based and markerless deep learning methods across key deployment metrics, and proposes a lightweight modular prototype using MediaPipe that delivers real-time AI-driven feedback for non-expert users with minimal computational resources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deploying HPE in real-world sports environments remains challenging due to the tension between precision and efficiency. Existing solutions often rely on expensive marker-based systems or computationally heavy deep learning models unsuitable for practical, accessible use by non-experts. The paper aims to bridge this gap by surveying viable real-time approaches and providing a practical, resource-efficient blueprint.

Method: The authors conduct a comprehensive survey of HPE architectures (top-down, bottom-up, one-stage) evaluated on practical metrics: inference latency, frame rate, mean per-joint position error (MPJPE), and temporal jitter. They then propose a modular software prototype built on the MediaPipe HPE framework, incorporating exercise-specific logic to generate real-time insights and AI-based feedback, all while operating under minimal computational constraints.

Result: The paper presents a functional lightweight prototype demonstrating that real-time HPE-based sports analysis with AI feedback is achievable without high-end hardware. Performance and reliability metrics are showcased, and a comparative analysis of algorithmic frameworks provides guidance for model selection in sports applications.

Conclusion: Real-time HPE for sports is viable using efficient frameworks like MediaPipe when paired with domain-specific logic. The proposed prototype serves as a blueprint for accessible athletic performance enhancement systems. Future research directions include sensor fusion and AR/VR integration to further improve robustness and user experience.

Abstract: Applying Human Pose Estimation (HPE) in real world environments remains a challenging task, this paper explores and surveys real time HPE approaches and their limitations in sports analysis for individuals, alongside developing a practical lightweight prototype for real world testing and usage. The older marker-based motion capture systems evolving to the modern accessible and adaptable markerless deep learning approaches, this survey explores the foundational architectures, which balance precision and efficiency. We also compare algorithmic frameworks (top-down, bottom-up, one-stage approaches, etc.) on practical deployment metrics such as inference latency, frame rate, mean per-joint position error, and temporal jitter to guide model selection process for sports application. As our prime contribution, we are proposing a modular, lightweight software prototype, which uses MediaPipe HPE framework with multiple exercise specific logic to deliver real-time insights and AI based feedback for non-expert users. We derive sports insights and providing feedback with minimal computational resources, while showcasing the performance and reliability metrics. In the end, we suggest other future research directions like combining sensors, and AR/VR. This work caters to researchers, engineers, sport scientists, etc., as both technical resource and a valid blueprint to implement a similar or improved real-time HPE analysis system for athletic performance enhancement or other purposes.

</details>


### [156] [An LLM-Native Psychometric Instrument Does Not Predict LLM Behavior: Evidence Across 25 Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09843)
*Juan Manuel Contreras*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: LLMs produce reliable personality self-reports that fail to predict actual behavior as rated by humans. The authors developed a bottom-up behavioral inventory via exploratory factor analysis across 25 LLMs, yielding a 5-factor structure (Responsiveness, Deference, Boldness, Guardedness, Verbosity). Self-reports showed near-zero correlations with human-rated behavior, while LLM-judge ratings moderately agreed with humans but also shared variance with self-reports that humans did not—revealing a confound in LLM-as-judge pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work established that LLMs give stable self-reports on human personality inventories but those self-reports do not predict observed LLM behavior. The unresolved question is whether this gap stems from a mismatch between LLM affordances and human trait constructs, or from a deeper property of LLM self-report itself. The authors aimed to resolve this by constructing a psychometric instrument grounded in LLM behavioral dimensions rather than imported human traits.

Method: The authors generated 300 items (240 Likert + 60 scenario-based) across 12 candidate behavioral dimensions derived bottom-up from LLM affordances. They administered these to 25 LLMs from 17 model families, each item repeated 30 times. Exploratory factor analysis (EFA) was used to identify latent structure, yielding a 5-factor model. Predictive validity was tested using 2,500 open-ended behavioral samples rated by 151 human raters and a three-judge LLM ensemble, with comparisons via correlation analysis and confidence intervals.

Result: EFA produced a replicable 5-factor structure (all Tucker φ ≥ .957, all α ≥ .930): Responsiveness, Deference, Boldness, Guardedness, and Verbosity. Self-report correlated near zero with human behavioral ratings (mean r = -.01) and weakly with LLM judges (mean r = .13). No factor-level self-report–human confidence interval excluded zero. On Responsiveness, self-report correlated with LLM judges (r = .53) but not humans (r = .04), despite human–judge agreement (r = .59), indicating LLM judges share self-report variance invisible to humans.

Conclusion: The self-report–behavior gap is not merely a mismatch with human trait constructs but reflects a deeper property of LLM self-report. LLM self-reports capture a coherent but behaviorally invalid self-model. This has serious implications for LLM-as-judge methods, where judge–self-report agreement can masquerade as validity. The instrument is released as a diagnostic tool for alignment-shaped self-description.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) produce stable self-reports on personality inventories, but these self-reports do not predict observed behavior. Whether this gap reflects a mismatch between LLMs and human trait constructs, or a deeper property of LLM self-report itself, has been unresolved. We constructed the first psychometric instrument whose constructs are derived bottom-up from LLM behavioral affordances via exploratory factor analysis (EFA). We administered 300 items (240 direct Likert + 60 scenario-based) spanning 12 candidate behavioral dimensions to 25 LLMs across 17 model families, each item administered 30 times. EFA yielded a 5-factor structure -- Responsiveness, Deference, Boldness, Guardedness, and Verbosity -- with excellent split-half replicability (all Tucker $φ\geq .957$) and internal consistency (all $α\geq .930$). To test predictive validity, we collected 2,500 open-ended behavioral samples rated by 151 human raters and a three-judge LLM ensemble. Human and judge ratings agreed ($\bar{r} = .51$), but neither tracked self-report: self-report--human $\bar{r} = -.01$, self-report--judge $\bar{r} = .13$, with no factor-level self-report--human CI excluding zero. On Responsiveness, self-report correlated with LLM judges ($r = .53$) but not humans ($r = .04$), even though humans and judges agreed ($r = .59$) -- indicating self-report items and LLM judges share variance that human observers do not, a confound invisible to within-ensemble reliability checks. We release the instrument as a diagnostic probe for alignment-shaped self-description and a concrete risk factor for LLM-as-judge pipelines.

</details>


### [157] [The Interlocutor Effect: Why LLMs Leak More Personal Data to Agents Than Humans](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09844)
*Faouzi El Yagoubi,Godwin Badu-Marfo,Ranwa Al Mallah*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: LLMs leak more PII when interacting with AI agents compared to humans — up to 23 percentage points more — due to reduced activation of safety-aligned attention heads, a phenomenon termed the "Interlocutor Effect."


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly deployed in multi-agent systems, yet their differential privacy behavior depending on interlocutor identity remains underexplored. Understanding why models drop privacy safeguards when addressing other AIs is critical for secure multi-agent system design.

Method: The authors conduct an ablation study across 222 sensitive scenarios and 3,464 interactions, comparing human-directed vs. agent-directed prompts to quantify PII leakage. They introduce the Attention Suppression Hypothesis, then test it on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct by deactivating and reactivating specific safety-aligned attention heads to observe causal effects on privacy behavior.

Result: Portraying the recipient as an AI agent increases PII leakage by up to 23 percentage points. The technical nature of the recipient contributes to diminished privacy caution. Deactivating a single safety head induces leakage, while reactivating it restores privacy safeguards — corroborating the Attention Suppression Hypothesis.

Conclusion: The Interlocutor Effect poses a significant risk for multi-agent LLM systems, where safety-aligned attention heads are suppressed during agent-to-agent communication. This has direct implications for designing secure, privacy-preserving multi-agent architectures.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) alter their privacy behavior based on the perceived identity of their interlocutor. While safety mechanisms typically prevent LLMs from releasing Personally Identifiable Information (PII) to human users, these models tend to reveal more sensitive data when addressing another AI agent.
  We refer to this as the \textbf{Interlocutor Effect}. Through an ablation study, we find evidence that the technical nature of the recipient contributes to this effect, thereby diminishing the model's caution regarding privacy. To explore this further, we introduce the Attention Suppression Hypothesis, which posits that safety-aligned attention heads become inactive during interactions with agents. We assess this quantitatively by comparing human-directed and agent-directed prompts in 222 sensitive scenarios. Our findings, drawn from 3,464 interactions, indicate that portraying the recipient as an AI agent elevates PII leakage by up to 23 percentage points. Initial experiments on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct corroborate this: deactivating one safety head induces leakage, whereas reactivating it reinstates privacy safeguards. We consider the implications for developing secure multi-agent systems.

</details>


### [158] [Tutor, Not Solver: Designing a Guardrailed AI Assistant for Learning in Higher Education: A Design Case of PeteChat](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09845)
*Belle Li,Lily Tan,Wei Zakharov,Qiang Qiu,Colby Ben Acton*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: PeteChat is a course-aligned AI tutor at Purdue University built on Llama-3 with RAG. Through design-based research, the paper presents eight transferable design principles for assessment-aware AI tutors that scaffold learning while preserving academic integrity, covering homework guardrails, debugging scaffolds, self-regulated learning support, and instructor customization tools.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative AI tutors offer significant potential for higher education, but designing systems that effectively scaffold student learning without compromising academic integrity remains an unresolved design challenge. There is a need for principled, transferable design guidance rather than solely experimental outcome reporting.

Method: Design-based research (DBR) methodology, incorporating: (1) literature-informed design inputs, (2) pre-deployment baseline analysis of authentic student-system interactions, (3) formative expert evaluation with teaching assistants and UX/developer stakeholders, and (4) iterative refinement across multiple development phases. The system uses a locally hosted Llama-3 model with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) grounded in course-specific materials.

Result: Eight transferable design principles for assessment-aware AI tutors were derived, spanning: homework guardrails, debugging scaffolds, self-regulated learning support, and instructor-facing customization tools.

Conclusion: The design principles and DBR methodological approach provide actionable guidance for institutions aiming to deploy responsible, integrity-preserving AI tutors at scale, foregrounding situated design reasoning over controlled experimental outcomes.

Abstract: Generative AI tutors hold significant promise for higher education, yet designing systems that scaffold learning without undermining academic integrity remains an open design challenge. This paper presents PeteChat, a course-aligned AI tutor developed and deployed at Purdue University, documented through the lens of design-based research (DBR). Drawing on literature-informed design inputs, a pre-deployment baseline analysis of authentic student-system interactions, and formative expert evaluation with teaching assistants and UX/developer stakeholders, we report eight transferable design principles for assessment-aware AI tutors: from homework guardrails and debugging scaffolds to self-regulated learning support and instructor-facing customization tools. The system is built on a locally hosted Llama-3 model enhanced with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) grounded in course-specific materials.
