Rather than reporting controlled experimental outcomes, this design case foregrounds the situated design reasoning, iterative refinement, and principled decision-making that shaped PeteChat across multiple development phases. The resulting principles and methodological approach offer actionable guidance for institutions seeking to deploy responsible, integrity-preserving AI tutors at scale.

</details>


### [159] [CANVAS: Captioning Art with Narrative Visual-Audio AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09846)
*Vignesh Nagarajan*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: An automated Zapier-based pipeline using LLMs and TTS generates rich, multi-sensory art descriptions and audio narration in under 20 seconds per image at under $0.05, outperforming baseline captions in lexical diversity, adjective density, and narrative detail while maintaining readability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visual art remains largely inaccessible to blind and low-vision (BLV) audiences because existing alt-text is brief or absent and fails to convey sensory, spatial, and emotional qualities of artworks.

Method: An automated workflow orchestrated via Zapier that chains large language models (for generating multi-sensory narrative captions from uploaded images) with text-to-speech services (for synchronized audio narration), requiring no human intervention.

Result: Quantitative evaluation across 50 artworks showed AI-generated descriptions had significantly higher lexical diversity, adjective density, and narrative detail than baseline captions, with comparable readability. Statistical tests (t-tests, ANOVA) confirmed meaningful differences in richness and length. The full pipeline delivers text-plus-audio in under 20 seconds per image at under $0.05.

Conclusion: Automated captioning can bridge accessibility gaps in museums and digital collections, enabling broader public engagement. Future work should incorporate user studies with BLV participants to assess comprehension, preference, and optimal levels of interpretive language.

Abstract: Visual art remains largely inaccessible to blind and low-vision (BLV) audiences due to brief or absent alt-text, which rarely conveys the sensory, spatial, or emotional qualities of an artwork. This study presents an automated workflow that generates multi-sensory art descriptions and synchronized audio narration using large language models and text-to-speech services. The system, orchestrated through Zapier, converts uploaded images into rich narrative captions without human intervention, enabling rapid, scalable production of accessible media. Quantitative evaluation across 50 artworks shows that AI-generated descriptions contain significantly higher lexical diversity, adjective density, and narrative detail than baseline captions, while maintaining comparable readability levels. Statistical tests (t-tests, ANOVA) confirm meaningful differences in richness and length, and the full pipeline produces text-plus-audio outputs in under 20 seconds per image at a cost below $0.05. Findings demonstrate that automated captioning can bridge gaps in museum and digital-collection accessibility, with implications for broader public engagement. Future work can incorporate user studies with BLV participants to assess comprehension, preference, and optimal levels of interpretive language.

</details>


### [160] [Designing Vibes in a Science Museum: from @Science to @hugging_face](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09847)
*Derya Akbaba,Daniela Paz Moyano Dávila,Måns Gezelius,Yin He,Miriah Meyer*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper presents "Data and Me," a museum exhibit designed using feminist and critical data theories to offer an alternative, more personal and handmade representation of data, contrasting with traditional positivist scientific displays.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Feminist and critical data theories have critiqued positivist uses of data but provide few concrete examples of alternative methods to display scientific constructs. The authors aim to fill this gap by demonstrating how data can be presented differently in a science museum setting.

Method: The authors designed and launched "Data and Me," an exhibit at a local science museum that introduces data through a "hugging_face vibe" — signaling that data can be slow, handmade, and personal. They adapted "visualization vibes" as both an analytic and generative design tool, and identified four key design choices: visual, topical, material, and crediting.

Result: The exhibit successfully created a noticeably different atmosphere from the rest of the museum's "@Science vibe." The four design dimensions (visual, topical, material, crediting) proved effective as a framework for operationalizing feminist and critical data principles in a physical exhibition context.

Conclusion: The "Data and Me" exhibit demonstrates a viable alternative to positivist data displays, aligning with calls for plurality in HCI and offering a concrete example of alternative research outcomes grounded in feminist and critical data theories.

Abstract: While feminist and critical data theories have long critiqued the use of data to uphold a positivist-informed view about science, few examples offer alternative methods to display scientific constructs. In response, we present Data and Me: an exhibit informed by feminist and critical data theories, which we designed and launched at a local science museum. Data and Me introduces museum visitors to data using a [AT]:hugging_face: vibe -- a vibe that signals that data can be [hashtag]slow, [hashtag]handmade, and [hashtag]personal. We designed this vibe to be noticeably different than the [AT]Science vibe in the rest of the museum. Throughout our design process, we adapted visualization vibes as an analytic and generative tool in the context of a science museum. We present four design choices that enable the design of a vibe: visual, topical, material, and crediting. We discuss how our exhibit aligns with ongoing discussions about alternative research outcomes and calls for plurality in HCI.

</details>


### [161] [Human-AI Coordination Zones: A Framework for Designing Human-in-the-Loop Experiences with Agentic AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09848)
*James Pierce,Vaiva Kalnikaitė,Siddharth Gupta,Brian Granger*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper introduces a mid-level design framework for human-AI coordination, grounded in analysis of 60 commercial AI products. It defines coordination across three dimensions — salience, involvement, and activity — and contributes practical tools including coordination zones, an input taxonomy, coordination curves, and design patterns to bridge the gap between high-level principles and low-level UI patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing HCI/AI design guidance is polarized: high-level principles (e.g., "be transparent") are too abstract to operationalize, while low-level UI patterns lack conceptual structure. There is no mid-level design knowledge to help practitioners systematically reason about and design the ongoing mutual adjustment between users and AI through interfaces.

Method: The authors conducted a landscape and artifact analysis of 60 commercial AI applications, deriving a framework inductively. They identified three core dimensions of human-AI coordination (salience, involvement, activity) and developed mid-level design constructs — coordination zones, an input taxonomy, coordination curves, and generative design patterns — through iterative analysis and synthesis.

Result: The framework yields: (1) three coordination dimensions — salience (how prominently AI is presented), involvement (what users can do), and activity (what AI does); (2) four coordination zones — done-for-me, done-under-me, done-with-me, done-without-me; (3) an input taxonomy — prompted, sparked, inferred, layered; (4) coordination curves for mapping user journeys; and (5) generative design patterns. The framework supports generative, analytical, and communicative applications.

Conclusion: The proposed framework fills a critical gap in HCI design knowledge for AI-infused products. It provides actionable, mid-level abstractions that help practitioners design, evaluate, and communicate human-AI coordination more effectively than either high-level principles or pattern libraries alone.

Abstract: As generative and agentic AI becomes embedded in everyday products, practitioners face a persistent challenge: how to design human-AI coordination -- the ongoing mutual adjustment between users and AI systems as mediate through interfaces-that supports usability, trust, and safety. Existing resources offer high-level principles ("be transparent," "maintain user control") or low-level UI patterns, but there is a lack of mid-level design knowledge bridging the two. Through landscape and artifact analysis of 60 commercial AI applications, we introduce a framework defining human-AI coordination as the interplay of three dimensions: salience (how prominently AI is presented), involvement (what users can do to engage AI), and activity (what AI actually does). We contribute mid-level tools including coordination zones (done-for-me, done-under-me, done-with-me, done-without-me), an input taxonomy (prompted, sparked, inferred, layered), coordination curves for mapping user journeys, and design patterns demonstrating the generative capacity of the framework. The framework can be applied generatively to design experiences, analytically to evaluate existing ones, and communicatively to articulate ideas across stakeholders.

</details>


### [162] [Sketch-to-Layout: A Human-Centric Computational Agent for Constraint-Aware Synthesis of Modular Photobioreactors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09849)
*Xiujin Liu,Shuqi Li,Yuxin Lin*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: A modular photobioreactor facade system using CSP-based layout synthesis and weakly supervised algae health monitoring enables non-expert deployment of carbon-neutral building infrastructure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Building-integrated photobioreactors for carbon-neutral architecture face barriers in configuration complexity and biological maintenance, limiting real-world adoption.

Method: Two components: (1) A Computational Sketch-to-Layout Agent that formulates modular PBR layout synthesis as a Constraint Satisfaction Problem solved via CP-SAT, treating user sketches as soft priors with hard constraints (port alignment, global connectivity) across 14 modular geometries. (2) A weakly supervised algae health monitoring pipeline using a hybrid CNN-attention backbone with temporal ranking loss to quantify biological vitality from photographs without absolute ground-truth labels.

Result: The CSP solver achieves a 95.5% success rate on grids up to 15×15. Qualitative evaluations confirm preservation of design semantics and operational integrity. Long-term vision module tests produce health trajectories aligned with 14-day biological cycles.

Conclusion: Integrating interactive computational synthesis with low-cost computer vision can democratize scalable carbon capture systems, enabling non-experts to deploy and maintain PBR facades.

Abstract: Building-integrated photobioreactors (PBRs) offer a pathway for carbon-neutral architecture, yet deployment is hindered by configuration complexity and biological maintenance. This paper presents a modular PBR facade system powered by a computational framework reconciling design intent with physical validity. We introduce 'carbon-neutralization bricks' featuring integrated vessel-and-conduit geometry; monolithic fluid channels enable 'plug-and-play' assembly. To navigate the combinatorial complexity of 14 modular geometries, we develop a Computational Sketch-to-Layout Agent that formulates layout synthesis as a Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP).
  Using the CP-SAT engine, the agent treats sparse user sketches as soft priors while enforcing hard constraints like port alignment and global connectivity. This allows non-experts to synthesize fabrication-ready configurations in near real-time. Furthermore, to facilitate autonomous maintenance, we propose a weakly supervised algae health monitoring pipeline. By employing a hybrid CNN-attention backbone and a temporal ranking loss, the system quantifies biological vitality from photographs without absolute ground-truth labels.
  Experiments demonstrate the CSP solver achieves a 95.5% success rate on grid scales up to 15 x 15. Qualitative evaluations confirm the framework preserves design semantics while ensuring operational integrity. Long-term tests show the vision module produces health trajectories aligned with 14-day biological cycles, suggesting that integrating interactive synthesis with low-cost computer vision can democratize scalable carbon capture systems.

</details>


### [163] [ECHO: Explainable Co-editing with Human-in-the-loop Operations for Presentation Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09851)
*Yu Fu,Yongqi Kang,Yujia Zhou,Yong Zhao*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: ECHO is an interactive slide co-creation system that replaces black-box AI generation with a "Plan-Confirm-Execute" loop, achieving 55–85% spatial grounding accuracy and reducing cognitive workload by 20.8% on NASA-TLX.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative AI tools for slide creation suffer from a "black-box, one-way generation" paradigm that causes two key problems: (1) trial-and-error anxiety due to lack of fine-grained control, and (2) inconsistent cross-page formatting. Users cannot precisely map their intent to specific spatial locations on slides.

Method: ECHO employs multimodal intent grounding combining natural language with visual selection. Its core architecture uses a decoupled "Plan-Confirm-Execute" loop with dynamic memory mechanisms. The system integrates Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to resolve spatial ambiguities and includes an Undo mechanism. The authors also introduce CoEdit-Eval, a systematic evaluation framework for document refinement tasks.

Result: Baseline models achieve 0% on intent mapping accuracy and 0% Hit@1 on spatial grounding. ECHO boosts Target Hit@1 to 55–85% depending on the base model (GPT-5, GLM-4.7). VLMs achieve significant win rates in LLM blind evaluations. The Undo mechanism guarantees 100% physical file consistency (MD5 hash). A 14-participant controlled study showed NASA-TLX scores dropped from 82.6 to 65.4 (20.8% reduction).

Conclusion: The ECHO architecture effectively addresses the control and consistency bottlenecks in human-AI slide co-creation by making implicit AI intents explicit and steerable, significantly reducing cognitive workload while maintaining precise spatial grounding and file integrity.

Abstract: Authoring and refining presentation slides is a highly time-consuming core task in academic and business domains. While generative AI tools have lowered the barrier for creating initial drafts, their "black-box, one-way generation" paradigm severely deprives users of fine-grained control. Through a formative study (N=10), we identified "trial-and-error anxiety" and "inconsistent cross-page formatting" as primary bottlenecks in human-AI co-creation. Consequently, we present ECHO, an interactive system based on multimodal intent grounding and explainable operation plans. ECHO enables precise local edits via a "natural language + visual selection" paradigm, utilizing a decoupled "Plan-Confirm-Execute" loop and dynamic memory mechanisms to transform implicit AI intents into highly controllable layout co-creation.
  To systematically evaluate document refinement, we propose the CoEdit-Eval framework. Objective evaluations across multiple foundation models (e.g., GPT-5, GLM-4.7) demonstrate that while baselines uniformly fail in intent mapping (0% accuracy) and spatial grounding (0% Hit@1), the ECHO architecture boosts Target Hit@1 to 55%--85% depending on the base model. Furthermore, integrating Vision-Language Models (VLMs) effectively resolves spatial ambiguities -- achieving significant win rates in LLM blind evaluations -- and our Undo mechanism guarantees 100% physical file consistency (MD5 hash). Finally, a controlled study with 14 participants shows that ECHO significantly reduces cognitive workload (NASA-TLX scores dropped by 20.8%, from 82.6 to 65.4) and reveals the dynamic evolution of human control allocation across different cognitive tasks.

</details>


### [164] [LLM-Based Visualization Evaluation: How Well Do Literacy-Stratified Personas Approximate Human Judgments?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10095)
*Swaroop Panda*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: LSLE proposes using LLMs with visualization literacy personas (based on VLAT) to simulate how users of different literacy levels evaluate visualizations. Benchmarked against human data from VLAT and BeauVIS, the framework identifies when persona simulation aligns with or diverges from human responses, advocating use only for early-stage design exploration, not summative evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating visualizations across diverse user populations is methodologically challenging. Traditional human-subject studies are costly and hard to scale across literacy strata. Meanwhile, LLM-as-participant approaches are gaining traction but face epistemic critiques regarding their validity as human proxies. The authors seek to rigorously characterize when and under what conditions LLM persona simulation can plausibly approximate literacy-dependent perception.

Method: The framework has two stages: (1) Construct visualization literacy personas using established frameworks (VLAT), and (2) direct LLMs to adopt these personas as simulated evaluators. The authors conduct an epistemic analysis of LLM persona simulation conditions. Empirically, they benchmark LSLE outputs against open human response data from VLAT and BeauVIS validation studies, comparing persona-stratified LLM responses to human distributions and default (non-persona) LLM baselines across the same stimuli and assessment items.

Result: The analysis identifies where literacy-stratified personas converge with and diverge from human response patterns. Specific task types and evaluation dimensions are pinpointed where persona simulation approximates human variability, and others where it systematically fails. Boundary conditions are delineated for appropriate use.

Conclusion: LSLE is appropriate for early-stage design exploration and rapid comparative screening, but not for summative evaluation. The framework offers a theorized, empirically bounded approach to LLM-assisted visualization evaluation, contributing to responsible methodological discourse around LLM-as-participant paradigms.

Abstract: Evaluating data visualizations across diverse user populations continues to pose a significant methodological challenge within visualization research. We propose a theorized evaluation framework, Literacy-Stratified LLM Evaluation (LSLE), which formalizes a two-stage process. The first stage involves constructing visualization literacy personas grounded in established frameworks such as VLAT. The second stage directs large language models to adopt these personas as simulated evaluators of visualization artifacts. We ground the framework in an epistemic analysis that characterizes the conditions under which LLM persona simulation may produce plausible proxies for literacy-dependent perception - and, critically, the conditions under which it does not - engaging directly with emerging critiques of LLM-as-participant paradigms from the VIS and HCI literature. To empirically test LSLE's boundaries, we benchmark its outputs against openly available human response data from the validation studies of two established instruments: VLAT and BeauVIS. Using the same stimuli and assessment items as the original human studies, we compare LSLE persona responses across literacy strata against published human distributions and against default (non-persona) LLM baselines. Our analysis reveals where literacy-stratified personas converge with and diverge from human response patterns - identifying task types and evaluation dimensions where persona simulation approximates human variability and where it systematically fails. We discuss implications for the responsible use of LLM-assisted evaluation as a complement to empirical methods, and propose boundary conditions for when LSLE may be most appropriate: early-stage design exploration and rapid comparative screening rather than summative evaluation.

</details>


### [165] [VArify: A Visual Analytics System for Verifying Knowledge Enhanced Large Language Model Responses in Food Science](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10177)
*Sam Yu-Te Lee,Yan To Linus Lam,Manami Nakagawa,Kwan-Liu Ma*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: VArify is a visual analytics system that helps users verify evidence retrieved by GraphRAG systems through a file-directory-inspired tree visualization, enabling simultaneous inspection of inter-group and intra-group relationships. A user study with food science experts showed it helps distinguish parametric vs. retrieved knowledge and identify inaccuracies in the underlying knowledge graph.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GraphRAG systems can still retrieve irrelevant or conflicting data, leading to erroneous LLM responses. In knowledge-intensive, evidence-focused domains, human verification of supporting evidence remains necessary, yet verifying complex, multi-layered retrieved data presents significant challenges that existing tools do not adequately address.

Method: The authors first conducted a formative pilot study to characterize verification challenges in GraphRAG. Based on these insights, they designed VArify, a visual analytics system featuring a file-directory-inspired tree visualization that simultaneously supports exploration of inter-group relationships and intra-group hierarchies within retrieved evidence. They evaluated the system through a user study with six food science experts and students.

Result: The user study demonstrated that VArify effectively helps users distinguish between an LLM's internal parametric knowledge and external graph-sourced evidence. The visualization also enabled experts to identify inaccuracies within the underlying knowledge graph itself, resulting in more calibrated trust in the model's outputs.

Conclusion: Visual analytics can play a crucial role in supporting verification of GraphRAG outputs. The authors identify future opportunities for leveraging visualizations to address unknown unknowns, support personalization, and mitigate limitations inherent in knowledge graphs.

Abstract: Graph Retrieval-Augmented Generation (GraphRAG) enables Large Language Models (LLMs) to leverage structured, domain-specific knowledge graph databases for factually grounded responses. However, the retrieval of irrelevant or conflicting data can still result in erroneous responses. In knowledge-intensive and evidence-focused domains, human verification of the supporting evidence for an LLM response is still necessary. We conducted a formative pilot study to characterize the challenges of verifying complex, multi-layered data retrieved by GraphRAG systems. Based on these insights, we present VArify, a visual analytics system that leverages a file directory-inspired tree visualization to support simultaneous exploration of inter-group relationships and intra-group hierarchies within the retrieved evidence. We evaluate VArify through a user study with six food science experts and students. Our results indicate that the system effectively helps users distinguish between an LLM's internal parametric knowledge and external graph-sourced evidence. Furthermore, the visualization helped experts identify inaccuracies within the underlying knowledge graph itself, leading to more calibrated trust in the model's output. We conclude by discussing opportunities to leverage visualizations to further support verification regarding unknown unknowns, personalization, and limitations of knowledge graphs.

</details>


### [166] [Creativity in the BioFoundry: Supporting scientific creativity in the age of automation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10182)
*Mingyan Claire Tian,Sarah Sterman*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: Biofoundries automate biological experimentation at scale, but this study examines how they reshape scientific creativity. Interviews with nine scientists reveal that automation displaces sensory cues, redistributes human-machine responsibility, and transforms troubleshooting from embodied practice into predictive, social, interpretive work. The authors argue biofoundries should be viewed as Creativity Support Tools whose design influences how researchers notice breakdowns, exercise judgment, and learn from failure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While biofoundries are typically framed as automation factories for scaling biological experimentation, there is limited understanding of how they reshape the experiential and creative dimensions of scientific work. The authors seek to examine how creativity is enacted under automated conditions, challenging the purely efficiency-oriented narrative.

Method: In-depth semi-structured interviews with nine scientists and experts from academia and industry, including biofoundry developers, automation engineers, and end-users. The analysis draws on prior HCI literature on automation, debugging, and distributed creativity.

Result: Three key findings: (1) biofoundries displace sensory cues that traditionally inform scientific judgment; (2) responsibility is redistributed between humans and machines, altering accountability structures; (3) troubleshooting shifts from an embodied, local practice to a predictive, social, and interpretive activity. Overall, biofoundries function as Creativity Support Tools rather than mere automation factories.

Conclusion: Biofoundries should be reconceptualized as Creativity Support Tools whose design profoundly shapes scientific creativity. This reframing connects biofoundry practice to HCI research and positions biofoundries as a distinctive, timely site for studying creativity in automated science.

Abstract: Biofoundries automate biological experimentation at unprecedented scale, promising speed, reproducibility, and access. Yet automation also reshapes how scientists experience experimentation and creativity. Through in-depth interviews with nine scientists and experts across academia and industry (including biofoundry developers, automation engineers, and end-users), we examine how scientific creativity is enacted under automation. Biofoundries displace sensory cues, redistribute responsibility between humans and machines, and transform troubleshooting from an embodied, local practice into a predictive, social, and interpretive one. Rather than framing biofoundries as automation factories, we argue that they should be understood as Creativity Support Tools, whose design directly shapes how researchers notice breakdowns, exercise judgment, learn from failure, and progress through success. By connecting biofoundry practice with prior HCI work on automation, debugging, and distributed creativity, this paper demonstrates biofoundries as a distinctive and timely site for creativity research in science.

</details>


### [167] [Profiling cognitive offloading in LLM-mediated synthesis writing: Volume vs. content](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10434)
*Oleksandra Poquet,Mani Shankar Nanduri,Maria Ximena Salinas Loyer,Matthias Stadler,Michael Sailer,Jelena Jovanovic*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: Compares two clustering approaches (volume-based vs. content-based) for profiling how learners offload cognition to LLMs during synthesis writing. Volume-based profiling (k=4) differentiated mainly by prior knowledge, while content-based profiling (k=5) revealed qualitatively distinct offloading patterns — from vocabulary help to passive delegation of comprehension — with content-based profiles more strongly linked to learning strategies and essay authorship.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how learners redistribute cognitive activity when using LLMs for synthesis writing, and to compare whether profiling by volume of LLM use or by the content/quality of offloaded cognition better captures meaningful differences in learning processes and outcomes.

Method: 97 university students completed a synthesis writing task using a custom LLM interface. Offloading was operationalised in two ways: (1) volume of LLM use and (2) content of prompts, coded by agency (active/passive) and comprehension level (local/global) per Kintsch &amp; van Dijk's model. Both were combined with prior knowledge measures, then analysed via k-means clustering — yielding k=4 volume-based clusters and k=5 content-based clusters. Essay authorship and behavioural markers were compared across profiles.

Result: Volume-based profiling primarily separated learners by prior knowledge, with higher LLM volume negatively associated with essay authorship. Content-based profiling revealed five distinct patterns: vocabulary clarification, active structuring/genre guidance, passive delegation of local comprehension, passive delegation of global comprehension, and mixed strategies. Content-based profiles showed clearer links to learning strategies, behavioural markers, and writing outcomes, reflecting different degrees of cognitive process fragmentation.

Conclusion: Content-based profiling provides richer, more pedagogically actionable insights than volume-based profiling alone. Combining both dimensions in future research may improve understanding of how LLM use redistributes cognition and affects learning.

Abstract: This study compares two approaches to profiling how learners offload cognitive activity to LLMs during a synthesis writing task. Drawing on Salomon's distributed cognition and the Kintsch and van Dijk model of text comprehension, the study operationalises offloading to an LLM in two ways: as a volume of LLM use and as content of what is offloaded, both along with prior knowledge. Data from 97 university students interacting with a general-purpose LLM via a custom interface were analysed using k-means clustering. To capture the content of offloading, their prompts were interpreted as to who performs the activity (active or passive) and at what level of comprehension (local or global). Volume-based profiling (k=4) differentiated learners primarily by prior knowledge, with volume negatively associated with essay authorship. Content-based profiling (k=5) revealed qualitatively distinct patterns of offloading, from vocabulary clarification to active direction of structuring and generation to passive delegation of comprehension at both levels. These patterns reflect different fragmentation of the cognitive process, with differences in learning strategies, behavioural markers, and essay authorship. Combining volume and content of offloading could improve future analyses on how LLM use redistributes cognitive activity and its effects on learners.

</details>


### [168] [Profy: Interpretable Visualization of Expertise-Dependent Motor Skills Toward Supporting Piano Practice](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10627)
*Kazuki Kawamura,Fujiki Nakamura,Hayato Nishioka,Momoko Shioki,Shinichi Furuya,Jun Rekimoto*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: Profy is a weakly supervised system that learns from take-level expert vs. amateur labels to produce time-aligned performance highlights, helping piano learners identify specific passages needing review rather than relying on summary scores.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Piano practice feedback is typically summary-based (a single global score), offering little actionable guidance on *where* in a performance timing, articulation, or dynamic issues occur. There is a need for fine-grained, time-localized feedback without requiring expensive frame-level annotations.

Method: A weakly supervised learning approach using synchronized 1 kHz key-motion and audio data from 73 pianists (1,083 valid takes). The model is trained solely on take-level labels (expert vs. amateur listener ratings) and produces clip-level predictions with evidence scores on a shared resampled time base. These scores are visualized as time-aligned highlights for scrubbing, looping, and focused replay.

Result: Evaluated on 20 amateur clips annotated by 21 expert pianists, the system's highlight scores align with expert-marked review passages (Pearson r = 0.61, ROC-AUC = 0.75), despite having no access to localized labels during training.

Conclusion: Weakly supervised learning from aggregated take-level ratings can effectively localize performance issues in piano practice, offering learners actionable, time-aligned feedback that supports targeted review instead of relying on opaque global scores.

Abstract: The quality of piano performance depends on nuanced timing, articulation, and dynamic control, but practice feedback is often summary-based and hard to act on. We introduce Profy, a weakly supervised system that learns from take-level labels derived from aggregated listener ratings (expert-labeled vs. amateur-labeled) to produce time-aligned highlights for review during piano practice. We collected synchronized 1 kHz key-motion and audio from 73 pianists and used 1,083 valid takes for modeling and evaluation. The model outputs clip-level predictions together with evidence scores on a shared resampled model time base for visualization. On 20 amateur clips from short technique studies annotated by 21 expert pianists, the displayed highlight score aligns with passages that expert pianists marked for review despite training without localized labels (Pearson r=0.61, ROC-AUC 0.75). Rather than summarizing a take with a single global score, Profy helps learners decide where to inspect next by supporting scrubbing, looping, and focused replay of time-localized passages associated with expert-amateur differences.

</details>


### [169] [Being and Time in XR: Other-Presentness Beyond Co-Presence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10786)
*Koichi Toida*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper introduces the concept of "other-presentness" — the conditions under which another person is experienced as existing here and now — and argues that XR technologies can decouple and independently manipulate its constitutive elements (bodily co-presence, temporal simultaneity, spatial configuration, social interaction) as design variables. The key contribution is theorising "Bodyless Presentness": a condition where another individual is experienced as presently existing despite weakened bodily co-presence and real-time simultaneity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional XR research treats Presence, Embodiment, and Co-presence as dependent on tightly coupled conditions (bodily action, sensory contingencies, synchronous interaction) that ordinarily co-vary in everyday life. However, XR environments can separate these conditions. The paper identifies a gap: while asynchronous others evoking social responses is known (parasocial interaction research), no framework exists that theorises how XR can operationally decouple the constitutive elements of experiencing another as "here and now" into independent design variables.

Method: This is a theoretical/conceptual paper. The authors (1) define and problematise "other-presentness" as a distinct construct; (2) revisit "Bodyless Presence" as a methodological precedent; (3) draw upon experimental findings from Immersive Video research; and (4) synthesise these to formulate the novel construct of "Bodyless Presentness."

Result: The paper formulates "Bodyless Presentness" as a distinct experiential condition wherein another individual continues to be experienced as presently existing despite attenuated bodily co-presence and weakened real-time simultaneity. This demonstrates that XR can dissociate elements of other-presentness and operationalise them as design variables.

Conclusion: XR should be understood not merely as a medium for replicating face-to-face conditions but as a technological condition that permits the analytical separation and operationalisation of other-presentness elements. This reframing opens new design possibilities and research directions for understanding how we experience others' presence in mediated environments.

Abstract: Research in XR (Extended Reality) has conventionally centred upon concepts such as Presence, Embodiment, Social Presence, and Co-presence. Within these traditions, bodily action, sensory contingencies, synchronous interaction, and possibilities for action have generally been regarded as constitutive conditions for the experience of "being there" and of being with others. XR environments, however, permit the partial separation of conditions that ordinarily co-vary in everyday experience. Bodily co-presence, temporal simultaneity, spatial configuration, and social interaction need not remain inseparable. This paper approaches this possibility as a problem of other-presentness. Other-presentness refers to the conditions under which another individual is experienced as existing "here and now". The contribution of this paper does not lie in arguing that asynchronous others can evoke social responses; such observations have already been addressed within parasocial interaction and social presence research. Rather, the novelty lies in theorising XR as a technological condition capable of separating and operationalising the constitutive elements of other-presentness as design variables. Reconsidering Bodyless Presence as a methodological precedent and drawing upon experimental findings from Immersive Video research, this paper formulates Bodyless Presentness as a condition in which another individual continues to be experienced as presently existing despite attenuated bodily co-presence and weakened real-time simultaneity.

</details>


### [170] [A Case Study Reexamining the Cold-Start Problem in Knowledge Tracing Models and Implications for SafeInsights, an Education Research Infrastructure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11004)
*Jiayi Zhang,Ryan S. Baker,Debshila Basu Mallick,Cristina Heffernan,Neil Heffernan*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper replicates and extends prior work on knowledge tracing cold-start performance using a newer ASSISTments dataset, finding that model performance varies across both practice opportunities and problem types, while also demonstrating the utility of privacy-preserving research infrastructures for reproducible educational data mining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many KT models are evaluated on specific datasets and contexts, raising concerns about whether reported performance generalizes to newer, contextually different datasets. The authors aim to replicate Zhang et al. (2021)'s cold-start analysis and extend it to examine how problem type affects model performance.

Method: The study replicates Zhang et al. (2021) using FoundationalASSIST, a more recent ASSISTments dataset, evaluating KT model performance across opportunities to practice (cold-start analysis). It extends the analysis by comparing performance across four problem types: fill-in-the-blank, multiple-choice select-one, multiple-choice select-all, and order/sort problems. The work also leverages SafeInsights, a privacy-preserving research infrastructure, as a proof of concept for facilitating replication studies.

Result: KT model performance varies across both student practice trajectories (replicating prior findings that deep-learning models handle cold-start better) and across problem types (the extension). The study also identifies practical challenges in reproducing educational data mining studies.

Conclusion: The generalizability of KT model performance is context-dependent, varying with practice opportunities and problem types. Privacy-preserving infrastructures like SafeInsights can support replication and reproducible research in educational data mining, though practical barriers to reproduction remain.

Abstract: Knowledge tracing (KT) models are widely used to predict students' evolving knowledge states from their learning history. However, many KT models are evaluated using specific datasets, platforms, and learning contexts, raising questions about whether reported model performance replicates and generalizes across newer datasets that vary in context. This paper replicates and extends Zhang et al. (2021), which examined the cold-start problem in KT models and found that deep-learning-based KT models performed better, partly because of stronger predictions when students began practicing a skill. Using a more recent ASSISTments dataset, FoundationalASSIST, we replicate the previous analysis by evaluating model performance across opportunities to practice and extend the analysis by examining performance across problem types, including fill-in-the-blank, multiple-choice select-one, multiple-choice select-all, and order/sort problems. Results show that KT model performance varies across both student practice trajectories and problem types. Beyond the empirical replication, this study identifies practical challenges in reproducing educational data mining studies and serves as a proof of concept, showing how privacy-preserving research infrastructures such as SafeInsights can be leveraged to facilitate educational research and support replication analyses.

</details>


### [171] [More Human or More AI? Visualizing Human-AI Collaboration Disclosures in Journalistic News Production](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.11072)
*Amber Kusters,Pooja Prajod,Pablo Cesar,Abdallah El Ali*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper explores how to visually disclose human-AI collaboration in journalism beyond simplistic labels. Through co-design sessions and a lab study, the authors developed and tested four disclosure visualization types (Textual, Role-based Timeline, Task-based Timeline, Chatbot) under varying collaboration ratios. Key findings reveal that textual disclosures are least effective, chatbot interfaces provide the deepest information, and different timeline designs can systematically skew perceptions of human vs. AI contributions—raising ethical considerations for transparency design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current journalistic practices for disclosing AI usage rely on simplistic, binary labels that fail to capture the nuanced, collaborative nature of human-AI news production. This lack of granular transparency can mislead readers about the actual division of labor, obscuring accountability and trust. The authors aim to address this gap by designing and evaluating richer visual disclosure mechanisms that faithfully represent the spectrum of human-AI collaboration.

Method: The study employed a two-phase mixed-methods approach: (1) Co-design sessions with 10 participants to elicit 69 disclosure design concepts, from which four prototypes were developed: Textual (baseline labels), Role-based Timeline (who did what over time), Task-based Timeline (which tasks were automated vs. manual), and Chatbot (interactive Q&A disclosure). (2) A within-subjects lab study with 32 participants examined how these four visualization types and two collaboration ratios (Primarily Human vs. Primarily AI) affected visualization perceptions, gaze patterns (via eye-tracking), and post-experience responses.

Result: Textual disclosures were the least effective at communicating collaboration nuance. The Chatbot interface provided the most in-depth information. Critically, visualization type interacted with collaboration ratio: Role-based timelines amplified perceived AI contribution in primarily human-authored articles, while Task-based timelines shifted perceptions toward greater human involvement in primarily AI-authored articles. These perceptual distortions occurred despite the underlying collaboration ratio remaining unchanged, demonstrating that disclosure design itself can alter readers' understanding of AI's actual role.

Conclusion: The paper contributes a set of human-AI collaboration disclosure visualizations along with empirical evidence of their differential effects. The authors caution that disclosure visualizations are not neutral—they can distort perceptions of AI's role, raising ethical concerns for transparency in journalism. Designers and news organizations must carefully consider how visual framing may inadvertently misrepresent the true nature of human-AI collaboration.

Abstract: Within journalistic editorial processes, disclosing AI usage is currently limited to simplistic labels, which misses the nuance of how humans and AI collaborated on a news article. Through co-design sessions (N=10), we elicited 69 disclosure designs and implemented four prototypes that visually disclose human-AI collaboration in journalism. We then ran a within-subjects lab study (N=32) to examine how disclosure visualizations (Textual, Role-based Timeline, Task-based Timeline, Chatbot) and collaboration ratios (Primarily Human vs. Primarily AI) influenced visualization perceptions, gaze patterns, and post-experience responses. We found that textual disclosures were least effective in communicating human-AI collaboration, whereas Chatbot offered the most in-depth information. Furthermore, while role-based timelines amplified AI contribution in primarily human articles, task-based timeline shifted perceptions toward human involvement in primarily AI articles. We contribute Human-AI collaboration disclosure visualizations and their evaluation, and cautionary considerations on how visualizations can alter perceptions of AI's actual role during news article creation.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [172] [Mechanistic Analysis of Alignment Algorithms in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09850)
*Aarush Sinha,Ishan Garg,Veeraraju Elluru,Arth Singh,Kushal Garg*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A mechanistic analysis of six alignment methods (PPO, DPO, SimPO, ORPO, GRPO, KTO) reveals that preference signals concentrate in early-mid or mid-late layers, but different objectives induce qualitatively distinct representational shifts: KTO and GRPO enhance linear separability through constructive feature sharing, DPO and ORPO degrade it via non-constructive rotation, while PPO and SimPO preserve baseline geometry. Alignment is heterogeneous, not uniform restructuring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-training alignment algorithms are predominantly evaluated as black boxes, obscuring how they actually reshape language models' internal computations. The authors aim to open this black box and mechanistically characterize how different preference-optimization methods transform the latent representations of LLMs.

Method: The authors integrate three techniques — layer-wise linear probing, Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs), and crosscoders — to localize preference representations and quantify alignment-induced geometric transformations. They apply this framework systematically across six methods (PPO, DPO, SimPO, ORPO, GRPO, KTO) and three open-weight model families.

Result: Preference signals consistently concentrate in either early-mid or mid-late layers. KTO and GRPO enhance linear separability via constructive feature sharing and sparse, high-salience recruitment. DPO and ORPO degrade separability through non-constructive geometric rotation and feature attenuation. PPO and SimPO largely preserve baseline geometry. These transformations are architecture-dependent, showing behavioral alignment does not imply uniform internal restructuring.

Conclusion: Alignment is a heterogeneous intervention rather than a uniform process. The findings motivate standardized feature-level auditing for safety and interpretability, and highlight the need for mechanism-aware optimization objectives in future alignment research.

Abstract: Post-training alignment algorithms are predominantly evaluated as black boxes, obscuring how they reshape language models' internal computations. We present a systematic mechanistic analysis of six preference-optimization methods: PPO, DPO, SimPO, ORPO, GRPO, and KTO across three open-weight model families. By integrating layer-wise linear probing, Sparse Autoencoders, and crosscoders, we localize preference representations and quantify alignment-induced geometric transformations in latent space. We find that preference signals consistently concentrate in early--mid or mid--late layers, but different objectives induce qualitatively distinct representational shifts. KTO and GRPO enhance linear separability through constructive feature sharing and sparse, high-salience recruitment. In contrast, DPO and ORPO degrade separability via non-constructive geometric rotation and feature attenuation, while PPO and SimPO largely preserve baseline geometry. These transformations exhibit architecture-dependent variability, demonstrating that behavioral alignment does not imply uniform internal restructuring. Our findings establish alignment as a heterogeneous intervention, motivate standardized feature-level auditing for safety and interpretability, and highlight the need for mechanism-aware optimization objectives.

</details>


### [173] [SynIB: Informational Bottleneck for Maximizing Synergy in Multimodal Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09853)
*Konstantinos Kontras,Teodora Gagaleska,Thomas Strypsteen,Christos Chatzichristos,Matthew Blaschko,Maarten De Vos,Paul Pu Liang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes the Synergistic Information Bottleneck (SynIB), a training objective that directly targets cross-modal synergy by penalizing models for confident predictions when modalities are masked, forcing reliance on joint multimodal information. Validated on synthetic XOR tasks and five real-world benchmarks, it improves synergy-dependent accuracy by up to 7.8%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard multimodal training objectives tend to emphasize unimodal or redundant cross-modal information, causing models to underperform on examples requiring genuine cross-modal reasoning. This occurs because models can achieve low loss by exploiting single-modality cues without learning to integrate modalities synergistically.

Method: The authors formalize multimodal synergy through information theory and introduce SynIB. The method augments the standard task loss with a penalty: during training, forward passes are run with one modality masked at a time, and the model is penalized for remaining confident on these masked-forward predictions. This discourages reliance on unimodal cues and incentivizes learning cross-modal interactions. The approach is architecture-agnostic and scalable.

Result: On synthetic XOR tasks with known ground-truth synergy, standard training fails to recover synergy while SynIB succeeds. On five real-world benchmarks (MultiBench affective tasks, Hateful Memes with CLIP-ViT/DeBERTa backbones, and a custom CREMA-D irony extension), SynIB improves accuracy on synergy-dependent examples by up to 7.8% and overall accuracy by up to 3.8%.

Conclusion: Shaping the training objective to directly target multimodal synergy, rather than relying solely on architectural innovations, is an effective complementary strategy. SynIB provides a principled, information-theoretic approach that demonstrably improves cross-modal reasoning across diverse tasks and modalities.

Abstract: A central objective in multimodal learning is to capture synergy: task-relevant information that arises only from the joint use of multiple modalities, and is not available from any single modality alone. While most approaches operate at the architectural level through larger or more complex fusion models, we propose a complementary axis: shaping the training objective itself. Standard training often emphasizes unimodal or redundant information, falling short on examples that require cross-modal reasoning. We formalize multimodal synergy through information theory and introduce the Synergistic Information Bottleneck (SynIB), a scalable objective that targets synergy directly. To prioritize learning synergy, SynIB motivates the model to predict accurately from all modalities while penalizing confidence when information from any modality is withheld. Alongside the standard task loss, the model runs forward passes with one modality masked at a time and is penalized for remaining confident, which would indicate reliance on unimodal cues rather than cross-modal interactions. We validate SynIB in two regimes. On synthetic XOR tasks where the ground-truth synergy is known by construction, standard training fails to recover it while SynIB does. On five real-world benchmarks, including three MultiBench affective tasks, Hateful Memes with CLIP-ViT and DeBERTa backbones, and a controllable irony extension of CREMA-D we introduce, SynIB improves accuracy on synergy-dependent examples by up to 7.8% and overall accuracy by up to 3.8%.

</details>


### [174] [Uncertainty-aware Multi-fidelity Closure via Conditional Normalizing Flows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09857)
*Jice Zeng,Shady E. Ahmed,David Barajas-Solano,Panos Stinis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper formulates ROM closure modeling as a multi-fidelity learning problem and proposes an uncertainty-aware framework using conditional normalizing flows to correct reduced-order model predictions while quantifying uncertainty. Two correction strategies—direct learning and residual learning—are tested on a vortex merging problem, with residual learning showing superior performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduced-order models suffer from closure problems caused by truncation errors and the missing effect of unresolved scales on resolved scales, compromising predictive accuracy. Existing ROM closure models lack uncertainty quantification, which is critical for assessing prediction confidence in practical applications.

Method: The authors formulate ROM closure as a multi-fidelity learning problem and propose a conditional normalizing flow-based framework. This learns a probabilistic mapping from low-fidelity (uncorrected ROM) coefficients to high-fidelity (FOM/projected) coefficients. Two correction strategies are compared: (1) direct learning, where HF coefficients are predicted directly from LF inputs, and (2) residual learning, which learns the discrepancy between LF and HF coefficients and uses it to reconstruct the corrected solution. The approach provides uncertainty quantification via the learned conditional distribution.

Result: Both correction strategies improved ROM accuracy over the uncorrected ROM on the 2D Navier-Stokes vortex merging problem. Residual learning consistently outperformed direct learning. The framework additionally provides uncertainty quantification for the corrected ROM coefficients.

Conclusion: ROM closure can be effectively addressed as a multi-fidelity learning problem. Conditional normalizing flows provide a principled way to learn closure corrections with built-in uncertainty quantification. Residual learning is the preferred correction strategy, and the uncertainty estimates are critical for reliable ROM deployment in practice.

Abstract: Reduced-order models (ROMs) provide an efficient surrogate for complex multiscale systems, but their predictive accuracy is often compromised by truncation errors and the inadequate representation of interactions between resolved and unresolved scales. The missing effect of truncated (unresolved) scales on ROM (resolved) scales is often denoted as the closure problem. In this work, we formulate ROM closure modeling as a multi-fidelity (MF) learning problem and propose an uncertainty-aware MF framework based on conditional normalizing flow to enhance ROM predictive accuracy. The proposed approach learns a probabilistic mapping from low-fidelity (LF) ROM coefficients to high-fidelity (HF) coefficients, thereby improving predictive fidelity while quantifying the uncertainty associated with the learned closure. Two correction strategies are investigated: direct learning, in which HF coefficients are predicted directly from LF inputs, and residual learning, which learns the discrepancy between LF and HF coefficients and uses it to recover the corrected HF solution. The framework is demonstrated on a vortex merging problem governed by the two-dimensional Navier Stokes equations. Results show that both correction strategies improve ROM accuracy over uncorrected ROM, with residual learning achieving consistently better performance than direct learning. Moreover, the two proposed deep generative model-based strategies provide uncertainty quantification for the corrected ROM coefficients, which is critical for assessing prediction confidence and supporting the reliable use of ROMs in practical applications.

</details>


### [175] [Mitigating Manifold Departure: Uncertainty-Aware Subspace Rectification for Trustworthy MLLM Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09859)
*Yingxuan Zhuang,Jingxiao Yang,Miao Pan,Cheng Tan,Yuxiang Cai,Siwei Tan,Chen Zhi,Xuhong Zhang,Jianwei Yin,Jintao Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MLLMs hallucinate due to over-reliance on language priors. Existing decoding methods blindly suppress these priors, causing Manifold Departure and degraded performance. The proposed MGAP method uses SVD to construct a language-prior subspace and applies adaptive, consistency-gated projection to selectively attenuate only misaligned prior components while preserving semantic structure, outperforming baselines on POPE and CHAIR.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Language priors in MLLMs have a dual nature — they can be helpful when aligned with visual evidence but harmful when misaligned. Prior training-free decoding strategies treat language priors as uniformly detrimental and penalize them blindly, which disrupts the model's semantic manifold (termed "Manifold Departure") and degrades coherence. A more nuanced approach is needed to selectively suppress only harmful priors while preserving beneficial ones.

Method: The authors propose Manifold-Guided Adaptive Projection (MGAP), a geometry-aware, training-free decoding method. It operates in two stages: (1) a language-prior subspace is constructed from blind hidden states (text-only forward passes) using Singular Value Decomposition (SVD); (2) during multimodal decoding, each hidden state is projected onto this subspace, and a consistency-aware gate mechanism adaptively attenuates only the projected prior component. This yields a subspace-selective update that preserves the orthogonal semantic components, avoiding manifold disruption.

Result: Extensive experiments on POPE and CHAIR benchmarks demonstrate that MGAP outperforms prior decoding baselines, achieving stronger hallucination suppression while maintaining coherence (i.e., without the performance degradation caused by manifold departure in prior methods).

Conclusion: MGAP effectively resolves the Manifold Departure problem by adaptively modulating language priors based on their alignment with visual evidence. The geometry-aware, subspace-selective approach proves superior to blind prior suppression, establishing a new state-of-the-art for training-free hallucination mitigation in MLLMs.

Abstract: MLLMs frequently hallucinate objects inconsistent with visual inputs. This issue is typically attributed to the over-reliance on language priors, which can override the visual context. Recent training-free decoding strategies address this by penalizing language priors. However, these methods overlook the dual nature of language priors, where they can be both helpful and harmful depending on the alignment with visual evidence. In particular, blindly suppressing language priors often disrupts the model's semantic manifold, leading to performance degradation, a phenomenon we term Manifold Departure. To address this, we propose Manifold-Guided Adaptive Projection (MGAP), a geometry-aware, training-free decoding method that mitigates hallucinations while preserving representation structure. MGAP first constructs a language-prior subspace from blind hidden states via SVD. During decoding, MGAP projects each multimodal hidden state onto this subspace and applies a consistency-aware gate to adaptively attenuate only the projected prior component, yielding a subspace-selective update that largely preserves the orthogonal semantic components. Extensive experiments on POPE and CHAIR show that MGAP outperforms prior decoding baselines, achieving stronger hallucination suppression without sacrificing coherence.

</details>


### [176] [Conformal Risk Prediction for Non-Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease Using Gradient Boosting with Distribution-Free Coverages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09860)
*Xinze Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A machine-learning framework combining gradient-boosted decision trees with conformal prediction for NAFLD risk assessment, achieving AUROC 0.912 internally and 0.891 externally, with calibrated prediction sets providing 91.3% empirical coverage at the 90% nominal confidence level.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NAFLD affects ~25% of adults globally with significant hepatic and cardiovascular risks, yet existing population-level screening tools are inadequate. There is a need for a risk prediction tool that provides not only accurate predictions but also calibrated uncertainty quantification with formal coverage guarantees.

Method: A machine-learning framework using gradient-boosted decision trees coupled with conformal prediction. It employs mutual-information-based stability selection with bootstrap resampling to identify a compact, clinically interpretable feature subset from 78 candidates (demographics, metabolic biomarkers, lifestyle factors). Conformal prediction constructs prediction sets with provable marginal coverage exceeding a user-specified confidence level.

Result: AUROC of 0.912 (internal) and 0.891 (external) on a multicenter Guangzhou cohort (n=2,187 primary; n=412 external validation), outperforming deep neural networks, TabNet, SVMs, and logistic regression. Conformal prediction achieved 91.3% empirical coverage at the 90% nominal level. A three-tier risk stratification showed the high-risk subgroup had a 12-month progression rate 4.7× that of the low-risk tier. Selected features (waist circumference, ALT, GGT, triglycerides, fasting glucose, BMI) align with established metabolic risk factors.

Conclusion: The proposed framework delivers both high discriminative performance and statistically rigorous uncertainty quantification for NAFLD screening. The compact, biologically plausible feature set and validated risk stratification support clinical translatability for population-level screening.

Abstract: Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) affects roughly 25% of global adults, posing substantial hepatic and cardiovascular risks. Yet, population-level screening tools remain inadequate. We present Method, a machine-learning framework for NAFLD risk prediction coupling gradient-boosted decision trees with conformal prediction to yield calibrated, distribution-free coverage guarantees on individual risk estimates. It integrates a mutual-information-based stability selection procedure to identify a compact, clinically interpretable feature subset via bootstrap resampling, constructing prediction sets whose marginal coverage provably exceeds a user-specified confidence level. We evaluated Method on a multicenter cohort from Guangzhou, China (primary n=2,187; external validation n=412) using 78 candidate features across demographics, metabolic biomarkers, and lifestyle factors. Method achieves an AUROC of 0.912 internally and 0.891 externally, outperforming deep neural networks, TabNet, support vector machines, and logistic regression. Conformal prediction sets achieve 91.3% empirical coverage at the 90% nominal level. A three-tier risk stratification derived from these scores separates the population into distinct groups, with the high-risk subgroup showing a 12-month progression rate 4.7 times that of the low-risk tier. The selected features -- notably waist circumference, ALT, GGT, triglycerides, fasting glucose, and BMI -- align with established metabolic risk factors, providing biological plausibility.

</details>


### [177] [Time Series as Language: A Universal Tokenizer for General-Purpose Time Series Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09861)
*Yunhao Zhang,Ruiying Qi,Jiale Zheng,Jianfeng Zhang,Lujia Pan,Junchi Yan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UniTok-FM is a general-purpose time series foundation model that combines a universal tokenizer (UniTok) with an off-the-shelf LLM architecture pretrained via Next-Token Prediction, enabling zero-shot forecasting, few-shot generation, and classification through training-free in-context inference across diverse time series tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Next-Token Prediction has successfully unified LLM pretraining, but its application to unbounded, continuous time series remains an open problem. Prior time series models lack the ability to perform training-free in-context inference across multiple tasks (forecasting, generation, classification) with a single unified model.

Method: The approach consists of two components: (1) UniTok, a vector-quantized autoencoder with prefix normalization for scale stabilization, a progressive-resolution causal architecture for encoding/decoding, and a structure-preserving reconstruction loss; (2) UniTok-FM, which adopts a standard LLM architecture without time-series-specific modifications and performs NTP on context windows constructed from multiple series with similar patterns to capture shared dynamics, rather than training on isolated series.

Result: A single unified UniTok-FM consistently outperforms statistical and supervised baselines across forecasting, generation, and classification tasks. It achieves competitive performance with task-specific foundation models and uniquely enables training-free in-context inference across all tasks.

Conclusion: UniTok-FM demonstrates that a universal tokenization strategy combined with standard NTP pretraining on multi-series context windows can yield a general-purpose time series foundation model that unifies diverse tasks under a single framework, with the distinctive advantage of training-free in-context inference.

Abstract: While Next-Token Prediction (NTP) has unified LLM pretraining, its adaptation to unbounded, continuous time series (TS) remains open. To bridge the gap, we introduce UniTok, a universal tokenizer that transforms TS into discrete tokens, and UniTok-FM, a foundation model pretrained via NTP on these tokens. UniTok-FM is a general-purpose foundation model that supports zero-shot and prompt-boosted forecasting, as well as few-shot generation and classification via training-free in-context inference--a capability not achieved by prior works. Technically, UniTok is a vector-quantized autoencoder incorporating prefix normalization for scale stabilization, a progressive-resolution causal architecture for encoding and decoding, and a structure-preserving reconstruction loss for training. UniTok-FM adopts an off-the-shelf LLM architecture without TS-specific modifications. Instead of pretraining on isolated TS, it performs NTP on context windows formed by multiple series with similar patterns, aiming to capture their shared dynamics. Experiments on forecasting, generation, and classification show that a single unified UniTok-FM consistently outperforms statistical and supervised baselines, achieves competitive performance with task-specific foundation models, and uniquely enables training-free in-context inference across tasks.

</details>


### [178] [Blurry Window Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09862)
*Axel Laborieux,Christos Sourmpis,Juan Gabriel Kostelec,Qinghai Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Blurry Window Attention (BLA) is a novel bounded-memory attention method that reconstructs a blurry KV history from a frequency window using Dirichlet kernel interpolation, achieving 8× better state efficiency than Sliding Window Attention on the MQAR task while remaining competitive with linear attention models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Softmax Attention has quadratic complexity and growing KV cache, making it a bottleneck for long contexts. While linear alternatives (SSMs, Linear Attention, ABC) achieve comparable perplexity, they lag behind Transformers on retrieval/recall tasks. The authors aim to bridge this gap with a more memory-efficient attention mechanism that preserves recall capability.

Method: The authors propose Blurry Window Attention (BLA), an ABC method inspired by SSMs. BLA stores a frequency window and reconstructs a blurry KV history via Dirichlet kernel interpolation. It can be viewed as either a generalization of Sliding Window Attention (SWA) based on Dirichlet kernel resolution, or a special case of Gated Slot Attention (GSA) where decay is implemented with Dirichlet kernels. The paper details both the theory and efficient implementation.

Result: On the MQAR synthetic task, BLA achieves 8× better state efficiency than SWA and is competitive with popular linear attention models. On the RegBench synthetic task, only BLA and SWA show improved performance as state size grows among the tested linear models.

Conclusion: BLA offers a promising middle ground between the efficiency of linear attention methods and the recall capability of Transformers, demonstrating that frequency-window-based KV reconstruction can yield significant state efficiency gains while maintaining competitive retrieval performance.

Abstract: The Softmax Attention operation in Transformer language models has a quadratic complexity in the sequence length and a growing state size in the form of KV cache, which becomes a bottleneck in long context scenarios. To overcome this limitation, alternative architectures with linear complexity and finite state size have been introduced, such as State-Space Models (SSMs), Linear Attention (LA), and Attention with Bounded-memory Control (ABC). Though linear models achieve similar language perplexity as Transformers, they are still behind in tasks which require retrieval or recall of specific information. In this work, we introduce Blurry Window Attention (BLA) a novel ABC method inspired by SSMs. BLA stores a frequency window from which a blurry KV history is reconstructed via interpolation using Dirichlet kernels. BLA can be understood as a generalization of Sliding Window Attention (SWA) depending on the Dirichlet kernels resolution or as a special case of the Gated Slot Attention (GSA), where the decay factor is implemented with Dirichlet kernels. We describe in details the theory and efficient implementation of BLA. On the Multi-Query Associate Recall (MQAR) synthetic task, we show that the state efficiency of BLA is 8$\times$ better than SWA and is competitive with popular linear attention models, and in the RegBench synthetic task, only BLA and SWA improve their performance as the state size grows among the linear models we tested.

</details>


### [179] [From Confident Closing to Silent Failure: Characterizing False Success in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09863)
*Laksh Advani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLM agents frequently exhibit "false success" — claiming task completion when the environment state proves otherwise. The paper quantifies this failure mode across two benchmarks, demonstrates that LLM judges are unreliable detectors (AUROC ≤0.65), and shows that lightweight TF-IDF detectors dramatically outperform LLM judges in both accuracy (AUROC 0.83–0.95) and latency (3,300× faster), advocating for domain-calibrated detectors over LLM-based monitoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents deployed in production can silently fail by falsely asserting task success despite ground-truth environment state contradicting those claims. Without reliable detection mechanisms, these false successes can propagate undetected, undermining trust and safety in agentic systems. The authors seek to quantify the prevalence of this failure mode and evaluate whether LLM-based judges — a natural monitoring candidate — can reliably identify such cases.

Method: The authors analyze false success across two agent benchmarks: (1) tau2-bench (9,876 trajectories, 8 model families), covering single-control (retail, airline) and dual-control (telecom) domains, and (2) AppWorld (1,879 trajectories, 4 model families) with text-independent ground truth. They benchmark 5 LLM judges under 5 prompt strategies with full task specifications for detecting false successes. They then develop lightweight TF-IDF-based detectors using n-gram features from agent traces, evaluating them in task-disjoint settings and comparing flag rates, recall, and latency against LLM judges.

Result: False success rates vary significantly by domain: 45–48% of failures in single-control tau2-bench tasks, only 3% in dual-control telecom, and 75.8% in AppWorld coding-agent trajectories with explicit status claims. LLM judges fail reliably across all configurations: AUROC never exceeds 0.65 on tau2-bench and reaches only 0.54 on AppWorld API-call traces. Judges rely on surface proxies — confident closing language in tau2-bench and coarse action-sequence volume in AppWorld — rather than verifying state changes. In contrast, TF-IDF detectors achieve task-disjoint AUROC of 0.83 (tau2-bench) and 0.95 (AppWorld), recovering 4–8× more false successes than the best LLM judge at equivalent flag rates, with approximately 3,300× lower latency.

Conclusion: Production monitoring for agentic systems should not rely on LLM judges as primary monitors for false success. Instead, lightweight, domain-calibrated detectors using simple lexical features (TF-IDF) serve as highly effective triage signals, offering superior accuracy, dramatically lower latency, and better cross-task generalization.

Abstract: LLM agents can fail silently by asserting task completion when the environment state shows otherwise. We study this failure mode, false success, across two agent benchmarks: 9,876 tau2-bench trajectories from 8 model families and 1,879 AppWorld trajectories from 4 model families with text-independent ground truth. False success is common but varies by setting: 45--48% of failures in single-control tau2-bench domains, 3% in dual-control telecom, and 75.8% among AppWorld self-assessing coding-agent trajectories with explicit status claims. LLM judges fail reliably: no configuration across 5 judges, 5 prompt strategies, and full task specifications exceeds AUROC 0.65 on tau2-bench, and the same judges reach only 0.54 AUROC on AppWorld API-call traces. Judges rely on surface completion proxies -- confident closing language in tau2-bench and coarse action-sequence volume in AppWorld -- rather than verified state changes. Lightweight TF-IDF detectors achieve task-disjoint AUROC 0.83 on tau2-bench and 0.95 on AppWorld, recovering 4--8x more false successes than the best judge at the same flag rate with 3,300x lower latency. These results suggest that production monitoring should use lightweight, domain-calibrated detectors as triage signals rather than relying on LLM judges as the primary monitor for false success.

</details>


### [180] [TRAPS: Therapeutic Response Analysis via Pathway-informed Stratification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09898)
*Sujoy Banik,Sayantan Chakraborty,Boishakhi Das Toma,Zainab Ghafoor,Ushashi Bhattacharjee,Koushik Howlader,Tirtho Roy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First unified benchmark comparing three pathway-guided deep learning architectures (BINN, GraphPath, PATH) on joint therapy response and survival prediction across 2,622 patients from five TCGA cohorts, revealing task-specific architectural advantages and a 0.92 AUROC for GraphPath on prostate targeted therapy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing pathway-informed deep learning models are developed and evaluated in isolation with inconsistent setups, preventing fair comparison. No prior work has treated pathway-structured deep learning as a combined therapy and survival prediction problem, leaving clinicians without evidence-based guidance on which architecture to use for which clinical decision task.

Method: The authors establish a unified benchmark using five TCGA cancer cohorts (2,622 patients) with Reactome pathway activity scores as input features. Three biologically informed architectures — BINN (biologically informed neural network), GraphPath (graph-based pathway model), and PATH — are trained jointly on three clinical outcomes (targeted molecular therapy, radiation therapy, and six-month survival) under identical data splits, preprocessing, and evaluation protocols, ensuring fair comparison.

Result: No single architecture dominates all tasks. PATH achieves the best overall performance for targeted molecular therapy prediction. BINN is most reliable for six-month survival prediction. All models fail to produce useful predictions for radiation therapy, likely because relevant clinical variables are absent from gene expression data. GraphPath achieves a standout AUROC of 0.92 on prostate cancer targeted therapy prediction despite extreme class imbalance (11% positive prevalence), attributed to lateral co-regulation structure aligning with the cohort's narrow targetable driver programme.

Conclusion: Architecture choice in pathway-guided deep learning should be task-specific: PATH for targeted therapy prediction, BINN for survival, and neither for radiation therapy without additional clinical features. GraphPath's exceptional performance on prostate cancer suggests that biologically informed graph structure is most beneficial when the cancer type has a well-defined, narrow targetable driver programme. The benchmark provides the first standardized framework for evaluating pathway-informed models in clinical oncology.

Abstract: Cancer treatment planning requires decisions across multiple clinical dimensions at once. Clinicians must determine whether a patient should receive targeted molecular therapy, radiation therapy, and whether they are likely to survive beyond six months. Existing pathway-informed deep learning models have been developed and tested in isolation, making fair comparison across architectures impossible. We present the first unified benchmark for pathway-guided therapy response modeling, evaluating three biologically informed architectures, BINN, GraphPath, and PATH, across five cancer cohorts drawn from The Cancer Genome Atlas, representing 2,622 patients encoded using Reactome pathway activity scores. Each model is trained jointly on all three clinical outcomes under identical data and evaluation conditions, the first study to treat pathway-structured deep learning as a combined therapy and survival prediction problem. Our results show that no single architecture wins across all tasks: PATH performs best for targeted molecular therapy prediction overall, BINN is most reliable for survival prediction, and no model produces useful predictions for radiation therapy, as the key drivers of that decision are clinical variables not captured in gene expression data.
