Most strikingly, GraphPath achieves an AUROC of 0.92 on prostate targeted molecular therapy prediction, the highest score in the entire benchmark, demonstrating that lateral co-regulation structure produces exceptional discriminative power when matched to a cohort with a narrow targetable driver programme, even under conditions of extreme class imbalance at only 11\% positive prevalence.

</details>


### [181] [Alignment Collapse Under KV Cache Quantization: Diagnosis and Mitigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09864)
*Bruce Changlong Xu,Adarsh Kumarappan,Mu Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KV cache quantization can silently destroy LLM safety alignment at low bit-widths, even when perplexity appears normal. The authors identify that safety features occupy a low-dimensional subspace highly vulnerable to quantization noise and propose PCR, a diagnostic that classifies failure modes and enables training-free recovery of up to 97% alignment loss with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing KV cache quantization evaluations only measure perplexity and accuracy, completely ignoring safety alignment. The authors demonstrate that this is a critical blind spot: safety can be destroyed without visible degradation in standard metrics, and there is no universal safe bit-width across models.

Method: The authors first benchmark 11 instruction-tuned models (3.8B–72B) across 5 safety benchmarks (1,894 prompts) under KV cache quantization. They then identify the geometric root cause: safety features occupy a low-dimensional subspace 100–1000× more vulnerable to quantization noise than the full representation space. Based on this, they propose Per-Channel Reduction (PCR), which uses 20 calibration prompts to classify each model into one of three failure modes: (1) outlier-crushes-safety, (2) outlier-as-safety, or (3) multi-layer dilution. Each mode prescribes a specific mitigation strategy, enabling training-free recovery.

Result: Mistral-7B loses 15.2% of refusals at only 1.03× perplexity increase. Safety subspace vulnerability is 10²–10³× greater than the full space. PCR correctly predicts mitigation direction on all 9 primary models and 1 held-out model from an independent family. The method achieves up to 97.2% recovery of lost alignment (including with KIVI quantizer), with approximately 35 GPU-minutes of calibration. Vulnerabilities were confirmed in production vLLM serving with FP8 KV cache on NVIDIA GPUs.

Conclusion: Safety alignment must be treated as a first-class evaluation criterion for KV cache quantization. Standard perplexity/accuracy metrics are insufficient. PCR provides a practical, training-free diagnostic and mitigation protocol that recovers nearly all lost alignment at minimal cost, and attention-based allocation methods are insufficient for this problem.

Abstract: Key-value (KV) cache quantization is widely used to reduce Large Language Model (LLM) inference memory, yet existing evaluations solely focus on measuring perplexity and accuracy without assessing the safety impact. In this study, we explore alignment preservation under KV cache quantization. Across eleven instruction-tuned models (3.8B-72B) and five benchmarks (1,894 prompts), we find that low-bit quantization can silently destroy safety alignment: Mistral-7B loses 15.2% of its refusals at only 1.03x perplexity, and no universal safe bit-width exists, with sharp model-specific phase transitions invisible to standard metrics. We identify that the root cause is geometric: safety features occupy a low-dimensional activation subspace 10^2-10^3x more vulnerable to quantization noise than the full representation space perplexity averages over. Inspired by this observation, we propose Per-Channel Reduction (PCR), a diagnostic that classifies each model into one of three mechanistic failure modes: outlier-crushes-safety, where safety lives in non-outlier channels collaterally damaged by outlier-driven scale factors; outlier-as-safety, where safety overlaps outlier channels and finer granularity cannot rescue it; and multi-layer dilution, where safety is distributed across many layers and per-layer fixes fail. PCR predicts the correct mitigation direction on all nine primary models and one held-out model from an independent family using 20 calibration prompts. PCR generalizes across unseen prompts, models, and production quantizers, including KIVI with up to 97.2% recovery, succeeding where attention-based allocation methods fail. The resulting training-free protocol, requiring approximately 35 GPU-minutes, recovers up to 97% of lost alignment at minimal memory overhead, addressing vulnerabilities confirmed in production vLLM serving with FP8 KV cache on NVIDIA GPUs.

</details>


### [182] [Co-GLANCE: Uncertainty-Aware Active Perception for Heterogeneous Robot Teaming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09919)
*Michal P. Podolinsky,Neel P. Bhatt,Pranay Samineni,Rohan Siva,Christian Ellis,Ufuk Topcu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Co-GLANCE is a real-time onboard system that distills a vision-language model into an end-to-end architecture for occlusion segmentation and robot allocation in heterogeneous teams. It uses conformal prediction with selective abstention for calibrated uncertainty quantification, triggering active perception. It outperforms cloud-based VLM baselines by 25–36% in accuracy with 350× lower latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Heterogeneous robot teams in unstructured outdoor environments face perceptual uncertainty (e.g., occlusions) that varies by viewpoint and requires both scene-based reasoning and capability-aware robot allocation. Vision-language models (VLMs) offer strong semantic priors but are too computationally expensive for onboard use and lack calibrated uncertainty quantification.

Method: Co-GLANCE distills a VLM's semantic reasoning into an end-to-end model for occlusion segmentation and robot allocation, eliminating cloud dependency. It integrates conformal prediction with selective abstention to produce statistically valid uncertainty estimates (coverage guarantees) for segmentation, allocation, and detection outputs. These calibrated uncertainties drive active perception by dispatching the best-suited robot to acquire informative viewpoints.

Result: Co-GLANCE outperforms cloud-based VLM baselines by 25% in occlusion segmentation accuracy and 36% in robot allocation accuracy, while reducing per-frame inference latency by a factor of 350×.

Conclusion: Co-GLANCE demonstrates that distilling VLM reasoning into an efficient onboard system with calibrated uncertainty quantification enables effective, real-time active perception for heterogeneous robot teams. The released air-ground dataset supports further research in this direction.

Abstract: Perceptual uncertainty is a central challenge for heterogeneous robot teams operating in unstructured outdoor environments, where no single viewpoint affords reliable scene understanding. Perceptual uncertainty, arising from sources such as occlusions, manifests differently across robot viewpoints depending on scene structure. Detecting and resolving sources of perceptual uncertainty requires both scene-based contextual reasoning and capability-aware robot allocation. While vision-language models provide strong semantic priors for both, they are computationally prohibitive for onboard inference and lack calibrated uncertainty quantification. We introduce Co-GLANCE, a real-time onboard perception and decision-making system for uncertainty resolution in heterogeneous robot teams. Co-GLANCE distills the semantic reasoning capabilities of a vision-language model into an end-to-end model for occlusion segmentation and robot allocation, eliminating the need for cloud-based inference. To quantify perceptual uncertainty, Co-GLANCE combines conformal prediction with selective abstention to provide statistically valid coverage guarantees for segmentation, robot allocation, and detection outputs. These calibrated uncertainty estimates directly trigger active perception, dispatching the most appropriate robot to acquire informative viewpoints and resolve uncertainty. Across real-world scenarios, Co-GLANCE outperforms cloud-based vision-language model baselines in occlusion segmentation and robot allocation accuracy by 25% and 36%, respectively, while reducing per-frame inference latency 350x. We also release an air-ground dataset for future research. Code, videos, and dataset available at https://co-glance.github.io/ .

</details>


### [183] [LLM-as-a-Discriminator: When Synthetic Tables Still Look Real](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09865)
*Manel Slokom,Malek Slokom,Thierno Kante*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes using LLMs as discriminators to audit privacy in synthetic tabular data, testing whether LLMs can distinguish real from synthetic samples. Experiments with LLaMA and Gemini across multiple synthesis models and datasets show that LLM-based discrimination is a viable privacy audit signal when model choice and data encoding are carefully managed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Auditing privacy for synthetic tabular data remains challenging — even humans often cannot reliably tell real from synthetic tables. Existing approaches are limited, motivating the exploration of whether LLMs can serve as effective discriminators for privacy auditing.

Method: The authors prompt an LLM to classify each tabular sample as REAL or SYNTHETIC under two settings: (C1) table data only, and (C2) table data plus distributional metadata. They use LLaMA (open model) and Gemini (reference), testing against three synthesis models (CTGAN, TVAE, Gaussian Copula) on UCI Adult and ACS Census datasets. They collect 451 valid trials and compare against classifier two-sample tests (C2ST), record linkage baselines, and a human pilot study (2 annotators, 240 trials). Performance is measured via DRS (Detection Rate of Synthetic).

Result: LLaMA achieves DRS=0% on Adult (failing to detect synthetic data), while Gemini reaches DRS=100% for CTGAN and TVAE. On Census, LLaMA heavily biases toward SYNTHETIC predictions; Gemini performs well in C1 but drops for CTGAN and TVAE in C2. Results show clear model-dependent and dataset-dependent variation, with Gemini generally outperforming LLaMA. The method shows promise compared to C2ST and record linkage baselines.

Conclusion: LLM-based discrimination constitutes a practical and effective privacy audit signal for synthetic tabular data, provided that model selection, per-provider reporting practices, and data encoding are handled with care. The approach surfaces vulnerabilities that traditional methods may miss.

Abstract: Privacy and data sharing are often in tension. Many organizations use synthetic data to reduce privacy risk and still share useful data. For tabular data, auditing privacy remains hard. In many cases, even humans cannot easily tell if a table is real or synthetic. In this paper, we propose a method based on LLM discrimination. We ask an LLM to classify each table sample as REAL or SYNTHETIC. We test two settings: C1 with table only, and C2 with table plus distributional metadata. We use LLaMA as an open model and Gemini as a reference model. In our experiments, we run three synthesis models, CTGAN, TVAE, and Gaussian Copula, on two public datasets, UCI Adult and ACS Census. We collect 451 valid trials. Our results show clear differences between models. On Adult, LLaMA reaches DRS=0% in reported cells, while Gemini reaches DRS=100% for CTGAN and TVAE. On Census, LLaMA predicts SYNTHETIC for most samples, while Gemini stays high in C1 but drops for CTGAN and TVAE in C2. We also compare with a classifier two-sample test (C2ST) and record linkage as distributional baselines, and with a human pilot of 2 annotators and 240 trials. Our results show that LLM discrimination is a practical privacy audit signal when model choice, per provider reporting, and data encoding are handled with care. For reproducibility, code and experiment scripts are available at https://github.com/SlokomManel/LLM-as-a-Discriminator.

</details>


### [184] [Two to Tango: Coupled Task-Reference Selection for Safe LLM Fine-tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09866)
*Xinrui Chen,Jianhao Zhang,Ou Wu,Di Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DualSelect is a coupled task-and-reference selection framework that preserves LLM safety during fine-tuning by jointly selecting safety references with high preservation loss and compatible task samples, outperforming baselines by 5.10+ Safety Avg. points with moderate overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-tuning safety-aligned LLMs on downstream tasks often erodes safety behaviors. Existing methods rely on fixed safety examples, global constraints, or one-sided filtering, which fail because different task updates expose different safety constraints. A joint selection approach is needed.

Method: DualSelect jointly selects task samples and safety references: (1) safety references are chosen based on high preservation loss and task conflict under a minimax framework; (2) task samples compatible with the induced reference direction are filtered in. Key techniques include entropy-regularized scoring surrogates, lazy reference refresh, and gradient correction to manage computational cost.

Result: On 1B–8B LLMs, DualSelect preserves safety without sacrificing task utility. Using the REDORCA judge, it improves Safety Avg. over the strongest baseline by at least 5.10 points and achieves the highest Safety Avg. across multiple judges with only moderate overhead.

Conclusion: Joint task-and-reference selection is an effective strategy for safety-preserving fine-tuning, and the minimax selection view extends naturally to retention-focused continual learning settings.

Abstract: Fine-tuning safety aligned large language models (LLMs) on downstream data improves adaptation but may erode learned safety behavior. Existing methods use fixed safety examples, global constraints, or one-sided task filtering. Our diagnostics show task updates expose different safety constraints, motivating joint selection of relevant references and compatible task samples. We propose DualSelect, a coupled framework for task and reference selection that refreshes task conditioned safety references before filtering whole task samples compatible with the induced reference direction. Under a minimax view, DualSelect selects safety references with high preservation loss and task conflict, together with compatible task samples, through entropy-regularized scoring surrogates, lazy reference refresh, and gradient correction. On 1B-8B LLMs, DualSelect preserves safety without losing task utility; using the REDORCA judge, it improves Safety Avg. over the strongest baseline by at least 5.10 points and remains highest in Safety Avg. across judges with moderate overhead. This view extends to retention focused continual learning.

</details>


### [185] [SPACE: Source-free Proxy Anchor Concept Erasure for MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09868)
*Zhijing Zhang,Jiaqi Ding,Qianshan Wei,Nan Zhou,Jiaqi Li,Yongliang Wu,Tongxin Zhu,Xiaolin Fang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SPACE is the first source-free machine unlearning framework for MLLMs that erases target concepts without requiring access to the original visual data, using text-guided proxy anchor selection and dual-constraint semantic isolation. It achieves performance comparable to data-dependent methods across six datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs face growing privacy risks and regulatory constraints, creating a need for machine unlearning to remove sensitive data. However, existing MU methods require access to the target visual data, which is often unavailable due to strict data retention policies. This motivates the need for source-free unlearning approaches.

Method: SPACE consists of two stages: (1) Text-Guided Proxy Anchor Selection (TPAS), which retrieves semantically aligned proxy anchors from a shared feature space without accessing target visual data. (2) Dual-Constraint Semantic Isolation (DCSI), which optimizes these proxy anchors to indirectly erase target concepts while confining updates to the null space of retained knowledge. The method is theoretically proven to bound perturbation on retained knowledge and maximize feature spectral entropy.

Result: Extensive experiments across six datasets demonstrate that SPACE achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art data-dependent methods, validating its effectiveness in source-free machine unlearning scenarios.

Conclusion: SPACE provides an effective and theoretically grounded solution for source-free machine unlearning in MLLMs, eliminating the dependency on target visual data while preserving model performance through null-space-confined updates and spectral entropy maximization.

Abstract: As Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) face growing privacy risks and regulatory constraints, machine unlearning (MU) has emerged as a crucial solution for removing sensitive data while preserving model performance. However, existing MU methods typically rely on visual data of the target concepts, which is often unavailable due to strict data retention policies, thus creating a demand for source-free unlearning approaches that operate without access to the target data. In this work, we propose Source-free Proxy Anchor Concept Erasure (SPACE), the first source-free unlearning framework specialized for MLLMs. SPACE consists of two stages: (1) Text-Guided Proxy Anchor Selection (TPAS), which retrieves semantically aligned proxy anchors from the shared feature space. (2) Dual-Constraint Semantic Isolation (DCSI), which optimizes these anchors to indirectly erase target concepts. DCSI confines updates to the null space of retained knowledge, ensuring structural integrity. We theoretically prove that SPACE strictly bounds the perturbation on retained knowledge and maximizes feature spectral entropy, thereby maintaining the model's performance. Furthermore, extensive experiments across six datasets show that SPACE achieves performance comparable to that of state-of-the-art data-dependent methods, validating its effectiveness in source-free MU scenarios. The source code will be released.

</details>


### [186] [QSplitFL: Capability Aware Deep Q-Learning for Optimal Split Point Selection in Split Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09869)
*Nazmus Shakib Shadin,Xinyue Zhang,Jingyi Wang,Miao Pan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QSplitFL introduces a DQN-based framework that dynamically selects optimal model split points in Split Federated Learning by using lightweight client hardware metrics (CPU, memory, battery, latency) instead of high-dimensional model features, achieving faster convergence and higher accuracy across heterogeneous devices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In Split Federated Learning, fixed split points fail to accommodate heterogeneous client hardware, overloading weaker devices and increasing communication/server costs, which degrades convergence speed and stability. Existing split-point selection methods rely on high-dimensional model weight representations that are computationally expensive and impractical for resource-constrained clients.

Method: QSplitFL proposes: (1) a lightweight state representation using direct client hardware metrics (CPU utilization, memory, battery, network latency) rather than model weights; (2) a decayed loss-drop reward function that prioritizes early convergence in the DQN training; (3) a committee-based DQN architecture with majority voting among multiple DQN agents to mitigate reward hacking and improve robustness. The framework is evaluated across MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100 with CNN, ResNet50, MobileNetV4, and ConvNeXt architectures.

Result: QSplitFL achieves better convergence and higher accuracy compared to existing split-point selection methods. The approach effectively adapts to heterogeneous device resources across all tested datasets and model architectures.

Conclusion: The capability-aware DQN framework with hardware-metric-based state representation and committee voting provides an efficient, scalable solution for optimal split point selection in SFL, enabling robust performance in real-world heterogeneous edge computing environments.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) combined with Split Learning (SL) is a privacy preserving paradigm that enables training deep neural networks (DNNs) on resource constrained devices while reducing overall training cost. However, determining the optimal split point, meaning the layer where the model is divided still remains a critical challenge, especially when clients have heterogeneous hardware capabilities. Fixed split points can overload weak devices and increase the communication and server load, which slows convergence and reduces stability. This paper introduces QSplitFL, a novel capability-aware Deep Q-Network (DQN) framework for optimal split point selection in Split learning based Federated Learning (SFL) environments. Unlike existing approaches that rely on high-dimensional model weight representations, QSplitFL employs a lightweight state representation derived directly from client hardware metrics, including CPU utilization, memory, battery level, and network latency. The proposed framework incorporates a decayed loss-drop reward function that prioritizes early convergence, and a committee-based DQN architecture with majority voting to mitigate reward hacking. Extensive experiments on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100 datasets using CNN, ResNet50, MobileNetV4, and ConvNeXt architectures demonstrate that our approach achieves better convergence and higher accuracy compared to existing methods, while effectively adapting to heterogeneous device resources. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/AIPO-Lab/QSplitFL.

</details>


### [187] [PatchSTG: Scalable Spatiotemporal Graph Transformers for Traffic Forecasting on Irregular Sensor Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09872)
*Jichao Li,Xuanming Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PatchSTG introduces a hierarchical patch-based spatial partitioning scheme and a dual attention encoder for traffic forecasting on irregular sensor networks, achieving competitive accuracy with near-linear computational complexity instead of quadratic scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph-based and attention-based traffic forecasting models struggle with irregular sensor distributions (non-uniform spatial structures) and high computational cost when modeling large-scale spatiotemporal dependencies. A scalable, efficient solution for real-world uneven sensor deployments is needed.

Method: PatchSTG partitions sensors into balanced, locality-preserving patches using geographic information. A dual attention encoder alternates between intra-patch attention (local interactions) and inter-patch attention (global dependencies), reducing complexity from quadratic to near-linear. The architecture is a patch-based spatiotemporal graph Transformer.

Result: On real-world Rhode Island traffic data and additional large-scale datasets, PatchSTG achieves stable and competitive forecasting performance across multiple horizons while significantly improving computational efficiency. Ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of spatial partitioning and dual attention.

Conclusion: Patch-based spatiotemporal modeling offers a scalable and effective framework for traffic forecasting under irregular spatial settings, balancing accuracy with efficiency.

Abstract: Traffic forecasting is a fundamental component of intelligent transportation systems, yet remains challenging in real-world settings due to irregular sensor distributions and the high computational cost of modeling large-scale spatiotemporal dependencies. In practical traffic networks, sensors are unevenly distributed across regions, leading to non-uniform spatial structures that limit the effectiveness and scalability of existing graph-based and attention-based models. To address these challenges, we propose PatchSTG, a patch-based spatiotemporal graph Transformer designed for efficient forecasting on irregular sensor networks. The key idea is to introduce a hierarchical spatial representation that partitions sensors into balanced, locality-preserving patches based on geographic information. On top of this structure, a dual attention encoder alternates between intra-patch attention for capturing local interactions and inter-patch attention for modeling global dependencies, reducing computational complexity from quadratic to near-linear scaling. We evaluate PatchSTG on real-world traffic data from Rhode Island and additional large-scale datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves stable and competitive forecasting performance across multiple horizons, while significantly improving computational efficiency. Ablation studies further validate the effectiveness of spatial partitioning and dual attention in capturing both local and long-range traffic dynamics. These results suggest that patch-based spatiotemporal modeling provides a scalable and effective framework for traffic forecasting under irregular spatial settings.

</details>


### [188] [Representation Curriculum: Stagewise Training for Robust Ranking and Allocation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09891)
*Ehsan Ebrahimzadeh,Sina Baharlouei,Abraham Bagherjeiran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Representation Curriculum (RC) is a training-time intervention that stages feature utilization — foregrounding content-based merit signals early, then phasing in exposure-dependent belief signals — to curb shortcut learning on historical popularity signals in ranking systems. This improves cold-start generalization and robustness under distribution shift, with formal guarantees and empirical validation on public benchmarks and a large-scale e-commerce search system.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern ranking systems rely heavily on exposure-confounded signals (popularity, CTR, ID embeddings) because they are highly predictive under stationary demand. However, this creates a learning shortcut where models over-rely on historical exposure-dependent signals at the expense of exposure-independent merit signals (content features, semantic affinity). This entrenches incumbents, degrades cold-start generalization, and reduces robustness under distribution shift. The core problem is that early access to "belief" signals starves gradient flow to "merit" signal pathways.

Method: Representation Curriculum (RC) is a training-time intervention with two phases: (1) an initial phase where only content-based merit signals are used, learning a strong merit representation; (2) a subsequent phase where exposure-dependent belief signals are introduced while anchoring the content pathway near the learned merit representation to prevent regression. The method is formalized independently of task and hypothesis class. For ranking, specific instantiations are provided. In a Gaussian linear ridge setting, closed-form solutions are derived along with sufficient conditions for strict cold-start risk reduction, including a quantified Pareto tradeoff against source-distribution performance.

Result: In the Gaussian linear ridge setting, closed-form solutions establish sufficient conditions under which RC strictly reduces population risk on cold-start target distributions, with a quantified Pareto tradeoff. Experiments on public learning-to-rank and recommendation benchmarks, plus randomized online experiments in a large-scale e-commerce search system, demonstrate that RC measurably shifts reliance from historical belief signals to content-based merit signals and yields consistent gains on cold populations while maintaining controlled trade-offs on head performance.

Conclusion: RC effectively mitigates shortcut learning in ranking systems by temporally staging feature utilization, breaking the feedback loop where exposure-confounded signals dominate optimization. It provides a principled, task-agnostic framework with formal guarantees, and delivers practical cold-start gains in both offline benchmarks and live e-commerce systems with manageable head-performance trade-offs.

Abstract: Ranking in digital marketplaces is a dynamic exposure-allocation mechanism: displayed items shape discovery trajectories and success events logged by the platform to update future allocation policies. Modern ranking systems rely heavily on exposure-confounded signals (e.g. popularity estimates, CTR/CVR aggregates, and ID-based representation), because they are highly predictive under stationary demand. Yet this predictive power can become a learning shortcut: early access to exposure-dependent belief signals steers optimization toward over-reliance on them and away from exposure-independent merit signals (e.g., content-based competitiveness and semantic affinity). Consequently, the learned policy tends to entrench incumbents and degrade cold-start generalization and robustness under distribution shift. We propose Representation Curriculum (RC), a training-time intervention that temporally stages feature utilization. RC foregrounds content-based merit signals initially, then introduces exposure-dependent belief signals while anchoring the content pathway near the learned merit representation, curbing shortcut reliance on historical signals and mitigating gradient starvation on content signals. We formalize RC independently of task and hypothesis class and provide ranking-specific instantiations. In a Gaussian linear ridge setting, we derive closed-form solutions and sufficient conditions under which RC strictly reduces population risk on a cold-start target distribution, with a quantified Pareto tradeoff against source performance. Experiments on public learning-to-rank and recommendation benchmarks, and randomized online experiments in a large-scale e-commerce search system, show that RC measurably shifts reliance from historical belief signals toward content-based merit signals and yields consistent gains on cold populations with a controlled trade-off in head performance.

</details>


### [189] [Rotate2Think: Geometric Priming via Orthogonal Rotation to Improve Language Model Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09873)
*Aditya Sharma,Christopher J. Pal,Amal Zouaq*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reasoning models' hidden states during thinking occupy a geometrically distinct region from input embeddings. The authors propose Rotate2Think, a training-free method using orthogonal Procrustes analysis to rotate input embeddings toward the "thinking" direction, injecting this synthetic vector at inference time to prime reasoning. The method improves accuracy in 30/32 model-benchmark configurations across math, science, and code tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The internal representational geometry of reasoning models during thinking is poorly understood. The authors aim to characterize how hidden representations differ during reasoning versus input encoding, and whether this geometric structure can be exploited to elicit stronger reasoning at inference time without additional training.

Method: The authors first analyze the geometry of input and thinking embeddings (mean-pooled last-layer hidden states), finding both exhibit high conicity (tight clustering around a mean direction) but with non-collinear mean directions occupying distinct regions. They then cast the input-to-thinking transition as a rotation problem solvable via orthogonal Procrustes analysis. Rotate2Think estimates this rotation matrix from a small set of correctly solved examples (computing the optimal orthogonal mapping between paired input and thinking embeddings), then at inference time applies the rotation to the input embedding and injects the resulting "synthetic thinking vector" between thinking delimiters before the reasoning trace begins.

Result: Rotate2Think improves accuracy in 30 out of 32 model-benchmark configurations spanning mathematics, science, and code tasks. The method also generalizes zero-shot to multimodal reasoning on MATH-Vision, demonstrating cross-modal transfer of the learned rotation.

Conclusion: The distinct geometric structure of thinking versus input embeddings is a robust phenomenon across models and tasks. Exploiting this via a simple training-free rotation (orthogonal Procrustes) provides a lightweight, effective inference-time intervention that primes models for improved reasoning, with strong empirical gains and zero-shot multimodal generalization.

Abstract: Reasoning models achieve strong performance on challenging tasks by generating explicit intermediate reasoning traces before producing a final answer. Yet the internal structure of representation space when reasoning remains poorly understood: how do a model's hidden representations differ during thinking versus the embeddings of the input prompt, and can this structure be exploited to elicit stronger reasoning at inference time? We show that both input embeddings and thinking embeddings (mean-pooled last-layer hidden states over the prompt and reasoning trace, respectively) exhibit extremely high conicity, with all vectors clustering tightly around a single mean direction. Crucially, these mean input and thinking directions are non-collinear, with thinking embeddings occupying a geometrically distinct region of embedding space across many different models and benchmark tasks. This observation motivates casting the input-to-thinking transition as a rotation problem admitting a closed-form solution via orthogonal Procrustes analysis. We propose Rotate2Think, a training-free method that estimates this rotation from a small set of correctly solved examples and injects the resulting synthetic thinking vector between thinking delimiters at inference time, providing a geometric primer at the onset of the reasoning trace. Evaluated across multiple benchmarks and model families, Rotate2Think improves accuracy in 30 of 32 model-benchmark configurations across mathematics, science, and code tasks, and generalizes zero-shot to multimodal reasoning on MATH-Vision.

</details>


### [190] [Disjoint or Overlapping? Inference Windowing for Reconstruction-Based Time Series Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09874)
*Guillaume Coulaud,Reza Akbarinia,Florent Masseglia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper revisits reconstruction-based anomaly detection for univariate time series, showing that overlapping inference windows (stride < window size) consistently improve performance (up to +28% gain) and can change method rankings. The authors propose a unified training/tuning/evaluation protocol on the TSB-AD benchmark and extend evaluation to the UCR archive, demonstrating that reconstruction-based baselines are competitive, practical approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reconstruction-based anomaly detection methods suffer from heterogeneous evaluation practices and underspecified inference procedures, making reported results hard to compare. The key overlooked factor is inference stride — whether subsequences are processed as disjoint or overlapping windows during inference.

Method: The authors establish a unified protocol covering training, hyperparameter tuning, and multi-seed evaluation on the curated TSB-AD benchmark. They systematically vary the inference stride across six model types (PCA-based baselines, DLinear, AutoEncoder, TimesNet, Transformer variants) and measure its impact on anomaly detection performance. They further evaluate on the full UCR archive using localization criteria aligned with sliding-window reconstruction.

Result: Overlapping windows yield consistent improvements across all models, with average relative gain up to +28% and can alter method rankings. The study also analyzes variability across datasets, random seeds, and hyperparameter configurations. Reconstruction-based baselines achieve strong performance on both TSB-AD and UCR benchmarks.

Conclusion: Reconstruction-based anomaly detection performance depends critically on inference choices (stride), not just model architecture and training. Overlapping inference is a simple yet impactful design choice. Reconstruction-based methods are competitive and practical for univariate time series anomaly detection when evaluated under a clear, reproducible protocol.

Abstract: Reconstruction-based methods are widely used for time series anomaly detection, where models are trained to reconstruct subsequences, and anomalies are identified through reconstruction errors. However, reported results are often hard to compare due to heterogeneous evaluation practices and underspecified inference procedures. In this paper, we revisit reconstruction-based anomaly detection in the univariate offline setting and study the role of the inference stride, which controls whether subsequences are processed as disjoint windows or with overlap. We propose a unified training, tuning, and multi-seed evaluation protocol on the curated TSB-AD benchmark, and study how overlapping inference affects anomaly detection performance for a range of reconstruction models, including PCA-based baselines, DLinear, an AutoEncoder, TimesNet, and Transformer variants. The results show that across all models, overlapping windows yield consistent improvements, with average relative gain up to +28%, and can alter method rankings. We further analyze variability across datasets, random seeds, and hyperparameter configurations. Finally, we complement the benchmark study with an evaluation on the full UCR archive using localization criteria aligned with sliding-window reconstruction. Overall, our results highlight that reconstruction-based anomaly detection performance depends not only on model architecture and training, but also on inference choices, motivating a clear and reproducible protocol. Our results show that reconstructionbased baselines achieve strong performance on both TSB-AD and UCR benchmarks, supporting them as competitive and practical approaches for univariate time series anomaly detection.

</details>


### [191] [Integrating Local and Global Entropy for Uncertainty Quantification in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09875)
*Johanne Medina,Tianyi Zhou,Keivin Isufaj,Aristides Gionis,Sanjay Chawla*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hidden-state geometric complexity (global uncertainty) and token-level entropy (local uncertainty) are near-orthogonal signals that capture distinct failure modes. The proposed Global-Local Uncertainty (GLU) score fuses both via a multiplicative gate, matching or outperforming all unsupervised baselines in a single forward pass.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing UQ methods rely on token-level signals and neglect the geometric structure of intermediate hidden states. Token-level signals systematically miss the "confident-but-wrong" failure mode, motivating a complementary global geometric approach.

Method: GLU combines hidden-state geometric entropy (global uncertainty, measured via the geometric complexity of hidden-state matrices) with token-level entropy (local uncertainty) through a multiplicative gating mechanism. It is unsupervised, single-pass, length-normalized, and architecture-agnostic.

Result: Global and local uncertainty are statistically near-orthogonal and capture distinct failure regimes. GLU matches or outperforms all unsupervised baselines across three model families and six benchmarks.

Conclusion: Fusing global geometric structure with local token-level signals substantially improves uncertainty quantification. Global geometry recovers the confident-but-wrong failures that local signals miss, and GLU provides an efficient, strong baseline for reliable LLM deployment.

Abstract: Large language models hallucinate confidently, making uncertainty quantification (UQ) essential for reliable deployment. Existing methods rely predominantly on token-level signals, leaving the geometric structure of intermediate hidden states underused. In this paper, we take the geometric complexity of hidden-state matrices as a measure of the global uncertainty of LLMs, while treating token-level uncertainty estimation as a local metric. We show that hidden-state geometric entropy (global uncertainty) and token-level entropy (local uncertainty) are statistically near-orthogonal, capturing distinct failure regimes for reliability prediction. In particular, global geometry recovers the confident-but-wrong failure mode that local signals systematically miss. Building on this, we propose Global-Local Uncertainty (GLU), an unsupervised, single-pass score that fuses the two signals via a multiplicative gate. Across three model families and six benchmarks, GLU matches or outperforms all unsupervised baselines while requiring only a single forward pass and remaining length-normalized and architecture-agnostic.

</details>


### [192] [Flash-GMM: A Memory-Efficient Kernel for Scalable Soft Clustering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10896)
*Gal Bloch,Ariel Gera,Matan Orbach,Ohad Eytan,Assaf Toledo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Flash-GMM is a fused GPU kernel for Gaussian Mixture Models that avoids materializing the full responsibility matrix, achieving 20× speedup and enabling 100× larger datasets. Applied to IVF-based ANN search, it makes soft GMM clustering a viable replacement for k-means, reducing distance computations by up to 1.7× or improving recall@10 by 2–12 points at equal cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GMM implementations on GPU suffer from two bottlenecks: (1) materializing the full N×K responsibility matrix in global memory is prohibitively expensive for large-scale data, and (2) standard expectation-maximization requires multiple passes over data. These limitations prevent GMMs from being practical on large datasets and from being used as alternatives to k-means in ANN search pipelines.

Method: Flash-GMM is a fused Triton kernel that performs the entire GMM computation in a single GPU pass. It decomposes the responsibility computation into tile-level operations using an online softmax scheme, aggregating sufficient statistics (weighted sums, weighted squared sums, and soft counts) on-the-fly without ever storing the full responsibility matrix. The kernel employs intra-GMM parallelism across clusters and fuses the E-step and M-step partial aggregation, using a block-sparse-like tiling strategy to maintain numerical stability and memory efficiency.

Result: Flash-GMM achieves a 20× speedup over baseline GMM implementations and scales to datasets over 100× larger than previously feasible on a single GPU. When integrated into IVF coarse quantization for ANN search, soft GMM clustering with multi-cluster assignment via responsibility thresholds reaches fixed recall targets with up to 1.7× fewer distance computations compared to k-means, or equivalently yields +2 to +12 recall@10 at matched computational cost.

Conclusion: Flash-GMM demonstrates that GMMs can now be efficiently computed at scale on a single GPU, making soft clustering a practical drop-in replacement for k-means in vector search pipelines. The open-source kernel enables broader adoption of probabilistic clustering in large-scale ANN systems.

Abstract: We present \textbf{Flash-GMM}, a fused Triton kernel for efficient computation of Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs) over large-scale data in a single GPU pass. By eliminating the need to materialize the full responsibility matrix in GPU memory, Flash-GMM achieves a \textbf{20$\times$} speedup over existing implementations and enables training on datasets more than \textbf{100$\times$} larger than previously feasible on one device. To demonstrate its impact, we integrate Flash-GMM into the IVF coarse quantizer for approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) search. We show that soft GMM clustering is now a viable drop-in replacement for $k$-means, and that GMM responsibilities can be leveraged to assign border vectors to multiple clusters. Our approach reaches fixed recall targets with up to $1.7\times$ fewer distance computations, or equivalently, yields $+2$--$12$ recall@10 at matched computational cost. We release the kernel as an open-source project.

</details>


### [193] [Calibrating Overconfidence Without Sacrificing Confidence: Probe-Conditioned Head Intervention for LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09876)
*Ke Li,Chongzhe Zhang,Zifan Zeng,Feng Liu,Qunli Zhang,Zheng Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PCHI is an inference-time method that uses a frozen probe to detect likely wrong-but-confident LLM responses and conditionally rescales attention-head outputs, selectively reducing verbalized overconfidence while largely preserving warranted confidence on correct answers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs frequently express high confidence in incorrect answers. Standard calibration methods (temperature scaling, etc.) operate globally and indiscriminately suppress confidence on both wrong and correct answers, trading off reduced overconfidence against damaged warranted confidence. The authors seek to decouple these two effects.

Method: Probe-Conditioned Head Intervention (PCHI): (1) Train a linear probe on internal activations to classify whether the model will produce a wrong-but-confident ("wrong-yes") response. (2) At inference time, when the probe flags a likely wrong-yes response, conditionally rescale the outputs of specific attention heads during the confidence-generation phase. Interventions target either the readout token alone or jointly across upstream confidence-template tokens.

Result: On Qwen3-4B-Instruct with OpenMathInstruct: readout-token PCHI converts 82.2% of wrong-yes confidence readouts to "no." Joint upstream intervention reduces ECE from 21.9% to 9.2%, damaging only 5.1% of originally correct-yes readouts. On Gemma3-4B, readout-token effects replicate but upstream interventions are weaker and mask-dependent.

Conclusion: Verbalized overconfidence can be selectively reduced via conditional internal intervention, partially decoupling suppression of unwarranted confidence from loss of warranted confidence — a capability that global calibration methods lack.

Abstract: Large language models often express high confidence in answers that are wrong. Standard calibration remedies typically act globally or at the score level, reducing unwarranted confidence but also risking erosion of warranted confidence on correct answers. We introduce Probe-Conditioned Head Intervention (PCHI), an inference-time method that uses a frozen probe to detect likely wrong-but-confident responses and conditionally rescales downstream attention-head outputs during confidence generation. On Qwen3-4B-Instruct solving OpenMathInstruct problems with a structured binary confidence field, readout-token PCHI converts 82.2% of originally wrong-yes confidence readouts to $\texttt{no}$, while a joint intervention across upstream confidence-template tokens reduces ECE from 21.9% to 9.2% and damages only 5.1% of originally correct-yes readouts. The readout-token effect also appears on Gemma3-4B, though upstream interventions are weaker and more mask-dependent. These results show that verbalized overconfidence can be selectively reduced through conditionally applied internal intervention, partially decoupling the suppression of unwarranted confidence from the loss of warranted confidence.

</details>


### [194] [Streaming Knowledge Compilation: Proactive Materiality-Scored Pinning for Time-Evolving LLM Wikis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09877)
*Juan M. Huerta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper formalizes Streaming Knowledge Compilation — maintaining a dynamic wiki from a document stream under a token budget to minimize regret against an offline oracle. The key insight is a materiality signal φ_t that predicts document importance before queries arrive, yielding an O(√(T log K)) regret bound. The framework is instantiated in finance (via LLM-predicted abnormal volatility) and Wikipedia (via Abnormal Edit Ratio), with cumulative regret analysis shown to be a more reliable evaluation metric than absolute QA scores due to a pervasive LLM-as-judge confound on post-training knowledge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM wiki systems assume a static corpus, which fails when the underlying information landscape evolves. The authors address the need for a principled online algorithm that compiles streaming documents into a fixed-size KV cache to support future, unknown queries with minimal regret relative to an oracle.

Method: The authors formalize Streaming Knowledge Compilation as an online optimization problem with a token budget constraint. They introduce a materiality signal φ_t(k,n) ∈ [0,1] that scores document importance for an entity k at time t, serving as a query-relevance surrogate for proactive cache pinning. They prove an O(√(T log K)) regret bound where ε = E[|φ_t − φ̂_t|] is the key domain-specific quantity. The algorithm is instantiated in two domains: (1) finance, using a frozen Llama 3.1 8B classification head predicting abnormal stock volatility (AUROC=0.728), and (2) Wikipedia, using the Abnormal Edit Ratio (AER), a cross-sectionally normalized edit velocity. End-to-end QA evaluation uses 173 finance and 119 Wikipedia matched pairs, with cumulative regret as the primary metric.

Result: Finance cumulative regret converges to -20.0 (-0.12/step); Wikipedia to +16.0 (+0.13/step). The positive Wikipedia regret confirms that edit content is genuinely post-training — richer context consistently improves scores (No Wiki 3.80 vs. Oracle 4.74). A pervasive LLM-as-judge confound on post-training knowledge is identified, establishing that regret analysis, not absolute QA scores, is the reliable evaluation metric. The finance materiality predictor achieves AUROC 0.728 with 1.49× higher realized forward volatility for predicted-material articles.

Conclusion: The O(√(T log K)) regret guarantee applies to any domain where knowledge gaps can be predicted from streaming signals. Regret analysis is established as the definitive evaluation framework for compiled knowledge systems, resolving the LLM-as-judge post-training confound. The approach generalizes across finance and Wikipedia domains via domain-specific materiality signal design.

Abstract: LLM wiki systems compile knowledge into pre-filled KV caches for efficient inference, but assume a static corpus -- an assumption that fails whenever the underlying information landscape evolves. We formalize Streaming Knowledge Compilation: given a document stream, a fixed token budget, and future queries unknown at ingestion time, maintain a compiled wiki that minimizes cumulative regret against an offline oracle with perfect foresight. The enabling insight is a materiality signal $φ_t(k,n)\in[0,1]$ that scores document importance for entity $k$ at time $t$, acting as a query-relevance surrogate for proactive pinning before queries arrive; we prove an $O(\sqrt{T\log K})$ regret bound where $\varepsilon=\mathbb{E}[|φ_t-\hatφ_t|]$ is the only domain-specific quantity. We instantiate in two domains: finance, where $φ_t$ is abnormal stock volatility predicted by frozen Llama 3.1 8B classification head (AUROC = 0.728 on 76K articles, strict temporal split; $1.49\times$ higher realized forward volatility for predicted-material articles); and Wikipedia, where $φ_t$ is the Abnormal Edit Ratio (AER), a cross-sectionally normalized edit velocity -- showing the same algorithm generalizes beyond the finance domain. End-to-end QA evaluation on 173 matched pairs (finance) and 119 (Wikipedia) reveals a pervasive LLM-as-judge confound on post-training knowledge, establishing that regret analysis -- not absolute QA scores -- is the reliable evaluation metric for compiled knowledge systems. Finance cumulative regret converges to -20.0 (-0.12/step); Wikipedia to +16.0 (+0.13/step), with the positive sign confirming that Wikipedia edit content is genuinely post-training -- richer context consistently improves scores (No Wiki 3.80 vs. Oracle 4.74) -- and eliminates this confound. The $O(\sqrt{T\log K})$ guarantee applies to any domain where knowledge gaps can be predicted from streaming signals.

</details>


### [195] [FailureScope: Cross-Regime Behavioral Diagnosis of Language Model Weaknesses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09878)
*Nicholas Saban*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FailureScope introduces LOMO (leave-one-model-out) behavioral clustering to diagnose model failures across single-turn benchmarks, multi-turn dialogue, and adversarial attacks, achieving high-fidelity failure prediction and exposing systematic gaps like LLM-judge vs. real-execution disparities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Aggregate accuracy metrics obscure which specific capabilities models lack. Practitioners need fine-grained, interpretable failure taxonomies to understand model weaknesses, yet existing benchmarks lack a portable diagnosis primitive that generalizes across evaluation regimes.

Method: FailureScope clusters evaluation probes by their cross-model pass/fail patterns using LOMO (leave-one-model-out). On 2,664 single-turn tasks (18 models), taxonomy-conditioned sampling is evaluated via Kendall's tau; cross-model failure prediction via AUC. The same clustering primitive is applied to a 363-task multi-turn corpus and 630 adversarial agent traces to test portability.

Result: Taxonomy-conditioned sampling achieves Kendall's tau = 0.81 at 50 tasks (vs. 0.34 random). Cross-model failure prediction reaches AUC 0.88. On adversarial traces, FailureScope reveals a 73–100 pp gap between LLM-judge ASR and real execution. Cluster cohesion remains strong across all three regimes.

Conclusion: Behavioral clustering via LOMO is a portable diagnosis primitive that yields stable, interpretable failure taxonomies generalizing beyond any single benchmark. The authors release the pipeline, annotated corpora, and cross-regime taxonomies.

Abstract: Standard benchmarks report aggregate accuracy, but practitioners need to know which specific capabilities a model lacks. We introduce FailureScope, a behavioral-diagnosis method that clusters evaluation probes by their cross-model pass/fail patterns (leave-one-model-out, LOMO), and show it yields stable, interpretable failure taxonomies across three regimes usually studied separately: single-turn benchmarks, multi-turn dialogue, and adversarial agent attacks. On 2,664 single-turn tasks across 18 models, taxonomy-conditioned sampling reaches Kendall's tau = 0.81 at 50 tasks (versus 0.34 for random selection), and cross-model failure prediction reaches AUC 0.88. The same primitive recovers interpretable clusters on a 363-task multi-turn corpus and on 630 adversarial agent traces, where it exposes a meta-failure mode: a 73-100 percentage-point gap between LLM-judge ASR and real execution. Cluster cohesion remains strong across all three regimes, which we take as evidence that behavioral clustering is a portable diagnosis primitive that generalizes beyond any single benchmark. We release the pipeline, three annotated corpora, and the cross-regime taxonomies.

</details>


### [196] [Operator Fusion for LLM Inference on the Tensix Architecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09879)
*Qingbo Wu,Ke Li,Wenzhu Wang,Jie Yu,Ruian Zhang,Lili Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes operator fusion of RMSNorm with matrix multiplications on Tenstorrent's Tensix architecture, leveraging on-chip SRAM and NoC multicast, achieving up to 37.44% attention latency reduction on Transformer inference with minimal numerical degradation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: On-device Transformer inference on Tenstorrent's Tensix architecture suffers from bottlenecks caused by memory-bound normalization operations interspersed with compute-bound matrix multiplications, leading to excessive off-chip DRAM traffic and scheduling overhead that limit throughput and energy efficiency.

Method: The authors fuse RMSNorm with adjacent matrix multiplications in both self-attention and FFN sub-layers, enabling back-to-back execution of memory-bound and compute-bound operators within on-chip SRAM. A NoC-based multicast mechanism is employed for multi-core parallelism, where row/column master nodes distribute inputs and weights across the core mesh to reduce DRAM bandwidth contention. Evaluated on the Wormhole platform using Qwen2.5-0.5B, Qwen3-0.6B, and Qwen3-4B models.

Result: Up to 37.44% latency reduction for attention, 15.89% for MLP, and up to 7.91% per decoder layer. Pearson Correlation Coefficient remains above 98.75%, confirming strong numerical consistency with the unfused baseline.

Conclusion: The proposed operator fusion strategy effectively alleviates memory bottlenecks in Transformer inference on the Tensix architecture, delivering significant end-to-end efficiency gains while preserving numerical fidelity, and demonstrates the value of co-optimizing operator scheduling with on-chip memory hierarchy and NoC communication patterns.

Abstract: This study addresses on-device inference bottlenecks of Transformer models on Tenstorrent's Tensix architecture and proposes an operator fusion strategy that enhances data locality. RMSNorm is fused with matrix multiplication in self-attention and in the FFN, enabling back-to-back execution of memory-bound and compute-bound operators in on-chip SRAM to significantly reduce DRAM reads/writes of intermediate results and scheduling overhead. To support multi-core parallelism, a NoC-based multicast mechanism is leveraged in which row/column master nodes efficiently distribute inputs and weights across the core mesh, alleviating DRAM bandwidth contention. Experiments on the Wormhole platform with Qwen2.5-0.5B, Qwen3-0.6B, and Qwen3-4B show up to 37.44% latency reduction for attention and 15.89% for MLP, with up to 7.91% reduction per decoder layer, while Pearson Correlation Coefficient (PCC) remains above 98.75%, confirming significant end-to-end efficiency gains under numerical consistency.

</details>


### [197] [Hyperparameter Learning for Latent Factorization of Tensors for Representation Learning to Large-scale Dynamic Weighted Directed Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09880)
*Yaqian Zhan,Jialan He,Tianzhu Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes DE-LFT, a framework that uses Differential Evolution to automatically optimize the three regularization hyperparameters (λ₁, λ₂, λ₃) in Latent Factorization of Tensors for dynamic weighted directed networks, achieving lower prediction error than manually tuned baselines on four real-world datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LFT model performance is highly sensitive to hyperparameter selection (λ₁, λ₂, λ₃), yet current practice relies on manual tuning or grid search, which is computationally expensive and labor-intensive. An automated, efficient hyperparameter optimization method is needed.

Method: Integrates Differential Evolution (DE) — a population-based metaheuristic optimization algorithm — into the LFT training process. DE automatically searches the hyperparameter space to find optimal values for the three regularization coefficients λ₁, λ₂, and λ₃, enabling adaptive optimization without exhaustive manual tuning.

Result: On four real-world DWDN datasets, DE-LFT achieves lower MAE and RMSE compared to manually tuned baseline LFT models, while substantially reducing the need for extensive parameter tuning.

Conclusion: DE-based hyperparameter optimization is an effective and practical solution for automating LFT model tuning, improving both prediction accuracy and training efficiency for large-scale dynamic weighted directed network analysis.

Abstract: Large-scale dynamic weighted directed networks (DWDNs) are widely used to model time-varying interactions among nodes. Latent factorization of tensors (LFT) extracts target knowledge from DWDNs via low-rank embedding. However, similar to many machine learning models, the performance of LFT heavily depends on the selection of hyperparameters. In practice, these parameters are often tuned manually or through grid search, which requires significant computational resources and human effort. Motivated by this challenge, this paper proposes an automated hyperparameter optimization framework based on Differential Evolution (DE) for LFT (DE-LFT). The proposed method integrates DE into the training process of the LFT model to automatically learn optimal regularization parameters $λ_1$, $λ_2$ and $λ_3$. As a result, the model can adaptively search the hyperparameter space and improve prediction accuracy. Experimental results on four real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves lower MAE and RMSE compared with manually tuned baselines while reducing the need for extensive parameter tuning.

</details>


### [198] [Toward Calibrated, Fair, and accurate Deepfake Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09881)
*Ryan Brown,Chris Russell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Face-Fairness (FF), a plug-and-play bias mitigation framework for deepfake detection. The core method, Face-Feature Tuning (FFT), performs demographic label-free logit remapping using frozen face embeddings. Two variants address scenarios where demographics are known (FF-Max) or discovered via embeddings (FF-Discover). FF consistently narrows fairness gaps and improves worst-group accuracy without sacrificing overall performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deepfake detectors exhibit significant performance disparities across demographic groups. Existing fairness interventions are limited because they require demographic labels (often unavailable or privacy-sensitive), necessitate retraining, or degrade overall accuracy. A label-free, detector-agnostic, plug-and-play solution is needed.

Method: The Face-Fairness framework centers on Face-Feature Tuning (FFT): a lightweight calibrator that remaps logits conditioned on frozen, pre-computed face embeddings, requiring no demographic labels. Two extensions are provided: (1) FF-Max — maximizes worst-group accuracy using known demographic labels; (2) FF-Discover — achieves the same objective using clusters discovered from face embeddings when labels are absent. The framework operates post-hoc on any pre-trained detector.

Result: Across both in-domain and cross-dataset evaluations, FF consistently reduces False Positive Rate (FPR) and True Positive Rate (TPR) gaps between groups, improves minimum group accuracy, and maintains or even improves overall accuracy. The method adds negligible inference overhead.

Conclusion: Face-Fairness provides an effective, detector-agnostic fairness solution for deepfake detection that eliminates the need for demographic labels or retraining. FFT in particular enables practical, privacy-preserving bias mitigation without accuracy trade-offs, establishing the first demographic label-free fairness method in this domain.

Abstract: Deepfake detectors show large performance gaps across demographic groups. Existing fairness approaches require demographic labels, retraining, or sacrifice accuracy. We introduce Face-Fairness (FF), a plug-and-play framework for bias mitigation. Our primary contribution, Face-Feature Tuning (FFT), is the first demographic label-free fairness method demonstrated for deepfake detection: a lightweight calibrator that performs a logit remapping conditioned on frozen face embeddings. We complement FFT with two variants: FF-Max, which maximizes worst-group accuracy when demographics are available, and FF-Discover, which does the same with embedding-discovered groups. Across in-domain and cross-dataset test settings, FF consistently reduces FPR/TPR gaps and improves minimum group accuracy while maintaining (often improving) overall accuracy. The approach is detector-agnostic, adds negligible runtime overhead, and requires no access to identity attributes.

</details>


### [199] [TD-Grokking: Learning from Zero-Reward Problems by Training-Time Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09883)
*Ningyuan Xi,Hao Xu,Hongsheng Xin,Ning Miao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TD-Grokking addresses the zero-reward problem in RLVR by recursively decomposing intractable problems into verifiable subproblems, forming hierarchical trees where solvable leaves provide training signals, outperforming GRPO and other baselines on mathematical and medical tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RLVR-based post-training of LLMs fails on zero-reward problems where all sampled trajectories yield failures, providing no optimization signal. Existing solutions (dense process supervision, partial rewards, prefix-guided exploration) are limited by task constraints or insufficiently equip the model to solve the original hard problems.

Method: TD-Grokking is a training-time decomposition framework that recursively breaks intractable root problems into self-contained, verifiable subproblems, constructing hierarchical decomposition trees. Solvable leaf subproblems yield non-zero rewards, converting previously useless zero-reward examples into effective training signals. The framework integrates with existing RLVR pipelines like GRPO.

Result: TD-Grokking outperforms vanilla GRPO and all baseline approaches on mathematical and medical reasoning tasks. Detailed analysis confirms that training-time decomposition effectively converts zero-reward examples into usable training signals, enabling consistent performance gains.

Conclusion: Training-time decomposition via TD-Grokking successfully mitigates the zero-reward bottleneck in RLVR, enabling policy models to learn from previously intractable problems and achieve consistent improvement on challenging reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have made remarkable progress in reasoning tasks, largely driven by post-training paradigms, especially reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR). However, a critical bottleneck persists: RLVR fails on highly challenging zero-reward problems, where all sampled reasoning trajectories yield uniformly failed outcomes, providing no optimization signal to drive model improvement. Prior efforts to address this limitation, such as dense process supervision, partial reward assignment, or prefix-guided exploration, suffer from inherent task constraints or do not fully equip the policy model with the capabilities necessary to solve the original intractable problems. To address this, we propose TD-Grokking, a training-time decomposition framework for zero-reward problems. It recursively decomposes intractable root problems into self-contained, verifiable subproblems, forming hierarchical trees where solvable leaves provide non-zero rewards. Evaluations on mathematical and medical tasks show that TD-Grokking outperforms vanilla GRPO as well as all baseline approaches. Together with detailed analysis, these results confirm that training-time decomposition effectively converts zero-reward examples into usable training signals, enabling consistent performance gains. Our code and datasets are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/TD-Grokking-6567/.

</details>


### [200] [TENP: Trapezoidal Expert Neuron Pruning For Mixture-of-Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09885)
*Jiangyang He,Shaolin Zhu,Deyi Xiong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TENP is a structured pruning framework for MoE LLMs that uses trapezoidal expert-neuron pruning: retaining important experts fully, applying neuron-level pruning to less important ones, with depth-dependent sparsity. It jointly evaluates expert importance via output magnitude and directional influence on inputs. At 40% routing sparsity and ~64% activated parameters, DeepSeek drops only 1 accuracy point and gains 10% on code tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MoE LLMs face deployment constraints due to large static parameter footprints. Existing solutions either remove entire experts (disrupting routing) or use unstructured pruning (limited practical efficiency). A structured approach is needed that preserves routing topology while achieving meaningful compression.

Method: TENP (Trapezoidal Expert-Neuron Pruning): (1) Uses few-shot samples to identify important experts via a joint criterion combining expert output magnitude and the expert's ability to change input vector direction. (2) Applies Expert Neuron Pruning (ENP) to less important experts, measuring each neuron's projected contribution to expert output to retain salient neurons. (3) Adopts a trapezoidal sparsity pattern — shallower layers retain more parameters, deeper layers are pruned more aggressively.

Result: On DeepSeek, with 40% routing expert sparsity and averaged 63.76% activated expert parameters: only 1-point accuracy drop versus the full-parameter model. Additionally, TENP outperforms the full-parameter model by 10% on code generation tasks.

Conclusion: TENP enables efficient structured compression of MoE LLMs without disrupting routing topology. The trapezoidal depth-aware pruning strategy, combined with the dual-metric expert importance criterion and neuron-level contribution measurement, achieves near-lossless accuracy while even improving performance on specialized tasks like code generation.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts large language models (LLMs) scale efficiently through sparse activation, yet their deployment is fundamentally constrained by the large static parameter footprint of experts. Existing compression approaches either remove entire experts, disrupting routing topology and harming performance, or rely on unstructured weight pruning with limited practical efficiency. To address the limitations, we propose TENP, a structured Trapezoidal ExpertNeuron Pruning framework. Using a few samples, we identify and retain important experts, while applying expert neuron pruning (ENP) to less important experts, reserving model parameters in a trapezoidal pattern from shallow to deep layers. When evaluating expert importance, we jointly consider both the magnitude of the expert output and its ability to change the direction of the input vector. For ENP, we measure each neuron's projected contribution to the expert output to identify and retain important neurons. We conduct extensive experiments on the Qwen and DeepSeek models. Under a routing expert sparsity of 40% and an average of 63.76% activated expert parameters, the DeepSeek model suffers only a 1-point drop in accuracy compared to the full-parameter model. Moreover, it outperforms the full-parameter model by 10% on code generation tasks.

</details>


### [201] [SHAPE: Coalition-Aware Expert Pruning for Sparse Mixture-of-Experts LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09886)
*Yuhao Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SHAPE is a task-driven pruning framework for sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLMs that models intra-layer expert cooperation using Shapley values from cooperative game theory, enabling smarter expert removal that preserves high-utility collaborations rather than just frequent ones, achieving robust accuracy under 20-40% pruning without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MoE models face a memory wall during deployment because all experts must remain resident in memory. Existing pruning methods score experts independently, ignoring that MoE inference is coalitional — outputs depend on top-k expert combinations. Independent scoring fails to capture which experts are essential for high-utility collaborations.

Method: SHAPE operates in three steps: (1) It collects routing traces on a small calibration set and formulates them as a cooperative game. (2) It computes Shapley-style interaction-aware values for each expert based on their marginal contributions across observed top-k coalitions. (3) A quality-coverage selection rule retains, per layer, the minimal expert subset covering an α fraction of non-negative Shapley mass, using bisection to match a global target keep rate.

Result: Experiments on Qwen3-30B-A3B, GPT-OSS-20B, and DeepSeek-V2-Lite across diverse benchmarks show SHAPE consistently improves robustness over global and layer-wise pruning variants, maintaining competitive accuracy under 20% and 40% expert pruning without any additional training, with clear reductions in peak GPU memory footprint.

Conclusion: Modeling expert cooperation via Shapley values yields substantially better pruning decisions than independent expert scoring. SHAPE demonstrates that coalition-aware pruning is a practical, training-free approach to reduce MoE memory requirements while preserving model quality.

Abstract: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) large language models achieve strong quality with low per-token compute, yet their deployment is often limited by the memory wall: the full expert pool must remain resident to support token-dependent routing. Expert pruning is a direct remedy, but prior criteria typically score experts independently and overlook that MoE inference is inherently \emph{coalitional}, where outputs arise from routed top-$k$ expert combinations. We propose \textbf{SHAPE}, a task-driven pruning framework that explicitly models \emph{intra-layer} expert cooperation. SHAPE formulates routing traces on a small calibration set as an empirical cooperative game and assigns interaction-aware expert values via a Shapley-style attribution over observed top-$k$ coalitions, enabling the identification of experts that are essential for high-utility collaborations rather than merely frequent. To preserve MoE topology under a global pruning budget, SHAPE further introduces a \emph{quality-coverage} selection rule that retains, in each layer, the minimal expert subset covering an $α$ fraction of non-negative Shapley mass, while using bisection to match a target keep rate. Experiments on three modern MoE backbones (Qwen3-30B-A3B, GPT-OSS-20B, and DeepSeek-V2-Lite) across diverse benchmarks show that SHAPE consistently improves robustness over global and layer-wise pruning variants, maintaining competitive accuracy under 20\% and 40\% expert pruning without additional training and delivering clear reductions in peak GPU memory footprint. The open-source code is available at https://github.com/Alizen-1009/Shapley-Moe.

</details>


### [202] [SocraticPO: Policy Optimization via Interactive Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09887)
*Zirui Liu,Jie Ouyang,Qi Liu,Xianquan Wang,Jiayu Liu,Tingyue Pan,Qingchuan Li,Jing Sha,Zhenya Huang,Shijin Wang,Enhong Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SocraticPO augments RL for LLMs by providing Socratic-style text guidance during rollouts when the student errs, paired with reward decay to prevent dependency on teacher assistance, improving scientific reasoning over standard RL baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard RL for LLMs uses scalar outcome rewards (e.g., binary correctness) that indicate whether an answer is right but don't explain how to fix flawed reasoning, enabling shortcut learning and brittle policies. The authors aim to integrate corrective natural-language feedback into the RL loop without requiring teacher logit access or altering the policy-gradient objective.

Method: SocraticPO modifies the rollout process: the student generates an answer independently; if incorrect, a teacher provides concise Socratic corrective guidance; the student then continues generation under the expanded context. Reward decay is applied — correct answers after teacher intervention receive lower rewards — to prevent treating teacher help as a shortcut. The framework is backend-agnostic (e.g., Reinforce++) and only requires black-box teacher text output.

Result: On SciKnowEval undergraduate-level scientific reasoning benchmarks, SocraticPO outperforms strong RL and self-distillation baselines. Ablations confirm that both the targeted Socratic guidance and reward decay are necessary components, with reward decay specifically reducing over-reliance on assisted corrections.

Conclusion: SocraticPO effectively integrates corrective textual guidance into policy optimization without altering the standard RL objective, improving reasoning performance while mitigating dependency on teacher intervention through reward decay.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) for large language models usually supervises reasoning with scalar outcome rewards, such as binary correctness. Such rewards provide an optimization direction but rarely explain how a model should revise its mistaken reasoning, which can encourage shortcut learning and brittle policies. We propose \textbf{SocraticPO} (Socratic Policy Optimization), a policy-optimization framework that augments RL rollouts with Socratic-style natural-language guidance. During rollout, the student first answers independently; if the answer is incorrect, a teacher diagnoses the attempt and provides concise corrective guidance, after which the student continues under the expanded context. Crucially, this guidance is paired with reward decay: correct answers obtained after teacher intervention only receive decayed rewards, preventing the policy from treating teacher help as a free path to reward. Since SocraticPO only modifies the rollout process while leaving the standard expected-reward objective intact, it can be plugged into existing policy-gradient backends such as Reinforce++. Moreover, because the teacher provides only text-level guidance, SocraticPO can leverage stronger black-box teacher models without requiring access to logits or distribution matching. On undergraduate-level scientific reasoning benchmarks from SciKnowEval, SocraticPO improves over strong RL and self-distillation baselines. Ablations show that both targeted guidance and reward decay are necessary, with reward decay mitigating reliance on assisted correction.

</details>


### [203] [SinkRec: Mitigating Semantic State Sink in Long Sequence Recommendation with Memory-Conditioned Gated Delta Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09888)
*Zhuang Zhuang,Zhipeng Wei,Ji Dai,Jie Chen,Fei Pan,Peng Jiang,Kun Gai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Linear attention for long-sequence recommendation suffers from "semantic state sink," where repetitive behaviors dominate the recurrent state. SinkRec solves this via a hybrid architecture that externalizes recurring patterns into a learnable conditional memory (using residual vector quantization) and employs Temporal-Aware State-Relation Differential Gated DeltaNet (TDGD) to purify recurrent state updates, achieving linear-time efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Linear attention enables efficient long-sequence modeling by avoiding quadratic complexity, but its compressed recurrent state becomes overwhelmed by repetitive user behavior patterns. The authors identify this as "semantic state sink" — recurring semantics monopolize the limited state capacity, biasing subsequent readouts and degrading recommendation performance on dynamic, non-repetitive behaviors.

Method: SinkRec is a hybrid memory-transition looped architecture with two key components: (1) A learnable conditional memory that externalizes recurring local patterns via residual vector quantization, storing them as discrete codes and reinjecting retrieved code representations along with memory key-value pairs into the attention block.
