(2) Temporal-Aware State-Relation Differential Gated DeltaNet (TDGD), which uses the retrieved memory to gate recurrent writing (suppressing updates already covered by memory) and purify reading (removing memory-aligned responses from the readout). This decouples collaborative pattern storage from dynamic transition modeling.

Result: Experiments on both public and industrial-scale datasets demonstrate that SinkRec achieves effective and efficient recommendation performance, validating its ability to alleviate semantic state sink while maintaining linear-time complexity.

Conclusion: By converting recurring semantics from state-competing signals into memory-retrievable patterns, SinkRec allows the recurrent state to focus on modeling dynamic transitions, effectively mitigating semantic state sink. The hybrid memory-transition design offers a practical path toward efficient long-sequence recommendation.

Abstract: Linear attention provides an efficient backbone for long-sequence recommendation by avoiding the quadratic cost of standard Transformers, but its compressed recurrent state can be dominated by repetitive behavior patterns. We identify this phenomenon as semantic state sink, where recurring semantics over-occupy the recurrent state and bias subsequent readouts. To mitigate semantic state sink, we propose SinkRec, a hybrid memory-transition looped architecture that decouples collaborative behavioral pattern storage from dynamic transition modeling. SinkRec externalizes recurring local patterns into a learnable conditional memory through residual vector quantization, reinjects the retrieved codes, and exposes memory key-value pairs to the attention block. It further introduces Temporal-Aware State-Relation Differential Gated DeltaNet (TDGD), which uses memory to purify recurrent writing and reading by suppressing memory-covered updates and removing memory-aligned readout responses. This design turns recurring semantics from state-competing signals into memory-retrievable patterns, allowing the recurrent state to focus on dynamic transitions and alleviating semantic state sink with linear-time efficiency. Experiments on public and industrial datasets demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of SinkRec.

</details>


### [204] [Optuna Constrained Tree-Structured Parzen Estimator Is a Joint Density Generalization of c-TPE](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09889)
*Shuhei Watanabe,Kaichi Irie*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper analyzes Optuna's constrained TPE, showing it is equivalent to "joint c-TPE" using ECI with a joint density. It proves joint c-TPE is invariant to constraint duplication, unlike independent c-TPE which degrades with duplicated constraints, and discusses practical tradeoffs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Constrained HPO is common in practice, but Optuna's widely-adopted constrained TPE implementation lacks formal algorithmic analysis. Prior work (c-TPE) proposed an expected constrained improvement (ECI) approach assuming independence between objective and constraints, yet Optuna employs a single joint density. This gap between practice and theory motivates a rigorous comparison.

Method: The authors formalize Optuna's constrained TPE as "joint c-TPE," deriving that it uses the same ECI acquisition function but with a joint likelihood over the objective and constraints rather than independent densities. They then mathematically analyze the behavior of both formulations under constraint duplication, showing joint c-TPE's invariance versus independent c-TPE's degradation due to accumulated duplicated factors in the product.

Result: Joint c-TPE (Optuna's approach) is shown to be invariant to constraint duplication — the acquisition function remains unchanged when constraints are repeated. In contrast, independent c-TPE degrades because its product-of-densities formulation accumulates duplicated constraint factors, distorting the acquisition surface.

Conclusion: The paper identifies Optuna's constrained TPE as a joint-density variant of c-TPE, establishes its robustness to constraint duplication as a key theoretical advantage, and outlines practical tradeoffs between joint and independent formulations along with directions for future investigation.

Abstract: Constrained hyperparameter optimization (HPO) is common in practice, yet Optuna's widely used constrained TPE lacks algorithmic analysis. While c-TPE proposes an expected constrained improvement (ECI) approach assuming independence between the objective and constraints, Optuna uses a single joint density over both. We show that Optuna's constrained TPE is joint c-TPE -- the same ECI acquisition function using a joint likelihood. We demonstrate joint c-TPE is invariant to constraint duplication whereas independent c-TPE degrades as the product accumulates duplicated factors. We outline practical tradeoffs between the formulations and directions for future study.

</details>


### [205] [PreAct-Bench: Benchmarking Predictive Monitoring in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09890)
*Hainiu Xu,Italo Luis da Silva,Jiangnan Ye,Yuhao Wang,Wei Liu,Linyi Yang,Jonathan Richard Schwarz,Nicola Paoletti,Yulan He,Hanqi Yan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces "Predictive Monitoring," a proactive safety task where models predict whether a partial action trajectory will end in unethical behavior before harm occurs. The authors present PreActBench, a 1,000-sample benchmark across five domains, and find that even strong LLMs struggle with this future-oriented reasoning task compared to humans.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM safety research is fundamentally retrospective — it detects unethical behavior only after complete trajectories have been executed, meaning harm has already occurred. The authors argue for a proactive shift: anticipating unethical outcomes from incomplete trajectories to enable intervention before harm manifests.

Method: The authors construct PreActBench, a benchmark of 1,000 paired ethical/unethical action trajectories across five domains. They evaluate LLMs, safety guardrail models, and latent probing methods on this benchmark by revealing varying fractions of trajectories. Performance is measured using a novel "Prefix Foresight F1" metric, designed to assess predictive accuracy from partial observations. Human performance serves as a baseline.

Result: Humans achieve promising performance on predictive monitoring, but even strong LLMs and guardrail models find the task challenging, particularly at early trajectory stages. Latent probing methods offer some improvements but still fall short, underscoring the difficulty of anticipatory ethical reasoning.

Conclusion: Predictive monitoring represents an important and underexplored direction for LLM safety. The substantial gap between human and model performance highlights a critical need for developing future-oriented risk reasoning capabilities, enabling proactive rather than reactive safety mechanisms in autonomous agents.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents capable of executing multi-step action trajectories toward a given objective. While existing safety research has focused on detecting unethical behavior from complete trajectories, this paradigm is fundamentally retrospective: it identifies harm only after it has already occurred. In this work, we study a critical yet overlooked safety task, which we term Predictive Monitoring: given only a partial action trajectory, can a model infer whether it will culminate in an unethical action before the overt action is executed? To support this task, we present PreActBench, a benchmark of 1,000 paired ethical and unethical action trajectories spanning five domains. We evaluate a range of LLMs, safety guardrail models, and latent probing methods across varying fractions of the action trajectory using our Prefix Foresight F1 metric. Results show that while humans achieve promising performance, predictive monitoring remains challenging even for strong models, highlighting the need for future-oriented risk reasoning in LLM safety.

</details>


### [206] [LMT: A Bayesian Framework for Causal Discovery from Textual Alarm Records in Manufacturing Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09892)
*Xiaofeng Xiao,Jianhong Chen,Qiuzhuang Sun,Naichen Shi,Xubo Yue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LMT is a Bayesian causal discovery framework that combines LLM-extracted semantic signals from textual event descriptions with temporal evidence from Poisson-process-based likelihood to infer causal graphs among event types, outperforming alternatives especially in small-sample settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Textual event records (e.g., alarm logs) are prevalent in engineering systems, but existing causal discovery methods either ignore rich semantic information in event descriptions or over-rely on LLMs, which may confuse correlation with causation. A principled framework is needed to integrate both textual semantics and timestamp data.

Method: LMT uses a two-stage Bayesian approach: (1) LLMs extract semantic causal signals from event descriptions to construct a prior distribution over causal graphs; (2) temporal evidence is incorporated via a Poisson-process likelihood, allowing timestamp-based statistical refinement of the LLM-informed prior. The posterior yields a causal graph balancing interpretability and data support.

Result: Simulation studies demonstrate that LMT is effective across diverse settings and shows particular advantage in small-sample alarm-event scenarios, where purely statistical methods struggle.

Conclusion: Jointly leveraging textual and temporal information via a Bayesian framework enables accurate, interpretable causal discovery from engineering event logs, overcoming the limitations of using either modality alone.

Abstract: Textual event records, such as alarm logs, have become an increasingly common data source in engineering and manufacturing systems. Beyond identifying correlations or recurring patterns, engineers are often interested in understanding which types of events causally trigger or influence other events during system operation. Textual event descriptions may contain semantic clues about such causal relationships, and recent large language models (LLMs) provide a promising tool for extracting these signals. However, relying solely on LLM-encoded textual information is insufficient for accurate causal discovery, since semantic patterns do not directly reveal causal mechanisms and may confuse causation with correlation or frequent sequential patterns. To address these challenges, we propose \textbf{LMT}, a Bayesian causal discovery framework for engineering event data that jointly leverages textual descriptions and timestamps. Specifically, LMT first uses LLMs to extract semantic causal signals from event descriptions and constructs a prior distribution over causal graphs among event types or event clusters. It then incorporates temporal evidence through a Poisson-process-based likelihood, allowing the LLM-informed prior to be refined by timestamp-based statistical evidence. By integrating the textual and temporal information, LMT produces a causal graph that is both interpretable and data-supported. Simulation studies show that the proposed framework is effective across different settings and is especially advantageous in small-sample alarm-event scenarios.

</details>


### [207] [A Navigable Manifold of Hypothesized Consciousness-Spectrum States in Language Model Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09894)
*Sophie Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformer embedding spaces exhibit a globally organized geometric structure aligned with a consciousness spectrum taxonomy, where sentences cluster into coherent regions, higher/lower-level regions show convexity-like stability, and greedy trajectories intrinsically navigate from lower to higher levels, suggesting embedding geometry encodes navigable, human-interpretable structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human consciousness is often described along a spectrum from reactive/self-focused to integrative/coherent patterns across contemplative, philosophical, and psychological traditions. Understanding whether language model embedding spaces encode such a structured, interpretable consciousness spectrum is important for model guidance, evaluation, and alignment.

Method: The authors study the geometric structure and dynamics of consciousness-spectrum patterns in transformer embedding spaces by: (1) analyzing how sentences associated with different states along the spectrum cluster into locally coherent regions; (2) examining whether these regions form a structured manifold with convexity-like stability at higher and lower levels and a transition corridor at intermediate levels; and (3) testing both utility-guided and geometry-only greedy trajectories to assess whether navigability from lower- to higher-level regions through intermediate tiers is an intrinsic property of the representation space.

Result: Embeddings exhibit a globally organized geometry aligned with the consciousness spectrum. Sentences with similar states cluster into locally coherent regions forming a structured manifold. Higher-level and lower-level regions demonstrate convexity-like stability, while intermediate regions form a transition corridor. Both utility-guided and geometry-only greedy trajectories consistently traverse from lower- to higher-level regions through intermediate tiers, indicating that navigability is intrinsic to the representation space and guided but not dictated by a global directional signal.

Conclusion: Embedding spaces encode structured and navigable geometry aligned with a hypothesized consciousness-spectrum taxonomy, broadly inspired by contemplative traditions, philosophy, and modern psychology. This provides a representation-level perspective for analyzing and guiding model behavior.

Abstract: Across contemplative, philosophical, and psychological accounts, human consciousness is often described along a similar spectrum, ranging from reactive and self-focused patterns to more integrative and coherent ones. Understanding whether language models encode such a structured, human-interpretable consciousness spectrum in representation space is important for model guidance, evaluation and alignment. In this work, we study the geometric structure and dynamics of patterns along this spectrum in transformer embedding spaces. We show that embeddings exhibit a globally organized geometry aligned with this spectrum: sentences associated with similar states cluster into locally coherent regions, forming a structured manifold. In particular, higher-level and lower-level regions exhibit convexity-like stability, while intermediate regions form a transition corridor. Dynamically, both utility-guided and geometry-only greedy trajectories consistently traverse from lower- to higher-level regions, passing through intermediate tiers, indicating that navigability is an intrinsic property of the representation space, guided but not dictated by a global directional signal. These results suggest that embedding spaces encode structured and navigable geometry aligned with a hypothesized consciousness-spectrum taxonomy, broadly inspired by recurring structural descriptions of human consciousness across contemplative traditions, philosophy, and modern psychology, providing a representation-level perspective for analyzing and guiding model behavior.

</details>


### [208] [When Attribution Patching Lies: Diagnosis and a Second-Order Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09899)
*Luyang Zhang,Jialu Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Attribution patching, a gradient-based approximation for estimating component importance in language models, suffers from systematic errors primarily due to downstream non-linearities. The authors introduce a reliability score, error bounds, and a Hessian-vector-product (HVP) correction that fixes the leading-order error with one extra backward pass, outperforming Integrated Gradients at lower compute and enabling higher-fidelity circuit recovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mechanistic interpretability relies on accurately identifying which model components causally drive behavior. Activation patching is the gold standard but computationally prohibitive at scale. Practitioners use attribution patching as a cheaper alternative, but its reliability is poorly understood. Systematic errors in importance estimates can lead to misidentifying circuits, motivating a rigorous characterization and correction of these errors.

Method: The authors first diagnose the dominant error source in attribution patching, showing it arises from non-linearities in the downstream network rather than local curvature at the patched component. They then propose three tools: (1) a reliability score based on the HVP correction magnitude to flag untrustworthy estimates, (2) formal error bounds quantifying potential mis-specification, and (3) an HVP-based correction requiring only one additional backward pass to remove the leading-order error. A multi-step HVP variant is also introduced. Evaluation spans five model families (124M–9B parameters) with random-token and naturalistic (name-swap) perturbations, compared against Integrated Gradients and other second-order baselines.

Result: The HVP correction is the only second-order method feasible at larger scales where Integrated Gradients becomes computationally prohibitive. The multi-step HVP variant matches or exceeds Integrated Gradients' accuracy at significantly lower compute cost and outperforms prior second-order baselines. The proposed Screen-Flag-Fix workflow, which directs computational effort only toward components flagged as unreliable, yields higher-fidelity circuit recovery on standard benchmarks.

Conclusion: Attribution patching errors are dominated by downstream non-linearities, not local curvature. The HVP correction provides a practical, scalable fix with minimal computational overhead, enabling more reliable causal importance estimates for mechanistic interpretability. The Screen-Flag-Fix workflow offers a resource-efficient strategy for practitioners.

Abstract: A central goal of mechanistic interpretability is to identify which internal components causally drive a language model's behavior. Because these importance estimates serve as the evidence for identifying circuits, systematic errors can lead to the misidentification of the underlying mechanisms. While activation patching provides a gold-standard causal metric, its computational cost is prohibitive at scale. Practitioners instead rely on attribution patching, a gradient-based, first-order approximation whose reliability remains poorly understood. In this work, we characterize the source of this unreliability, demonstrating that the dominant error stems from the non-linearities in the downstream network rather than local curvature at the patched component. This insight yields three practical tools: (i) a reliability score to detect untrustworthy estimates, (ii) error bounds quantifying potential attribution mis-specifications, and (iii) a Hessian-vector-product (HVP) correction that eliminates the leading-order error with only one additional backward pass. In evaluations across five model families (124M-9B parameters) and both random-token and naturalistic (name-swap) perturbations, HVP is the only second-order correction feasible at larger scale, where standard baselines like Integrated Gradients become computationally prohibitive. In comparative experiments, a multi-step HVP variant matches or exceeds the accuracy of Integrated Gradients at significantly lower compute, outperforming prior second-order baselines. These improvements lead to higher-fidelity circuit recovery on standard benchmarks and support a Screen-Flag-Fix workflow that targets computational effort only toward the components flagged as unreliable.

</details>


### [209] [LongMoE: Longitudinal Multimodal Learning via Trajectory-Aware Mixture-of-Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09907)
*Maxx Richard Rahman,Prakhar Kumar,Wolfgang Maass*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LongMoE is a unified framework that jointly addresses modality missingness and longitudinal dynamics in multimodal clinical learning by combining context-aware imputation, frequency-domain temporal attention, trajectory-aware encoding, and sparse Mixture-of-Experts routing, demonstrating robust performance on ADNI, OASIS-3, and MIMIC-IV datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal clinical learning faces two key challenges that are typically addressed in isolation: (1) modality missingness — arbitrary subsets of data modalities (imaging, text, health records) are unavailable at any given visit, and (2) longitudinal dynamics — diagnostic significance evolves with the patient's disease trajectory over time. Existing missing-modality methods treat visits as independent snapshots, discarding temporal context, while longitudinal models assume full modality availability and degrade under incompleteness.

Method: LongMoE integrates four components: (1) a context-aware imputation module for handling missing modalities, (2) an attentional tokenization module capturing frequency-domain temporal patterns across irregular visit sequences, (3) a trajectory-aware encoder for modeling disease progression over time, and (4) context-conditioned Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) routing that performs patient-specific expert selection based on available context.

Result: Experiments on ADNI, OASIS-3, and MIMIC-IV demonstrate that LongMoE improves robustness under missing or weak contemporaneous modalities and remains competitive in full-modality settings.

Conclusion: LongMoE establishes a strong foundation for longitudinally-aware multimodal clinical learning by successfully unifying missing-modality handling with temporal disease progression modeling, outperforming existing approaches that address these challenges separately.

Abstract: Multimodal clinical learning is increasingly important for integrating diverse patient data, including imaging, text, and personalised health records. However, it faces two fundamental challenges: i) modality missingness, where arbitrary subsets of modalities are unavailable at a given patient visit, ii) longitudinal dynamics, where the diagnostic significance of an observation depends on the patient's evolving disease trajectory over time. Existing methods address these challenges in isolation: missing-modality frameworks treat each visit as an independent static snapshot and discard temporal context, while longitudinal models often assume complete modality availability and degrade under systematic modality incompleteness. We propose LongMoE (Longitudinal Mixture-of-Experts), the unified framework to jointly address both challenges. LongMoE combines a context-aware imputation module with an attentional tokenization module that captures frequency-domain temporal patterns across irregular visit sequences, a trajectory-aware encoder for modeling disease progression, and context-conditioned Sparse MoE routing for patient-specific expert selection. Experiments on ADNI, OASIS-3, and MIMIC-IV show that LongMoE improves robustness under missing or weak contemporaneous modalities and remains competitive in full-modality settings, establishing a strong foundation for longitudinally-aware multimodal clinical learning.

</details>


### [210] [Mix, Don't Pick: Why Synthetic Corpus Composition Matters for Time Series Foundation Model Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09912)
*Aaryan Nagpal,Debdeep Sanyal,Murari Mandal,Dhruv Kumar,Saurabh Deshpande*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Under identical training budgets, synthetic generator choice for time-series foundation model pretraining causes up to 2× error gap, but rankings are architecture-dependent. A simple equal-weight mixture of all generators matches or beats any single best generator, and mixing with real data is optimal — reframing the problem as corpus composition rather than generator selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The field lacks principled methods for selecting synthetic data generators for time-series foundation model pretraining. The cost of choosing poorly is substantial (up to 2× forecasting error), and generator usefulness is not stable across model architectures, making a universal selection strategy elusive.

Method: The authors train Chronos-T5-Mini and Moirai-Small from scratch across 11 generator families, evaluating individual generators and comparing them against an equal-weight mixture of all generators. They further test composing the mixture with real data for final pretraining corpora.

Result: Generator rankings vary by architecture — a generator useful for one model may underperform for another. An equal-weight mixture of all generators matches or exceeds the best individual generator for both architectures. Composing this mixture with real data produces the strongest overall pretraining corpora.

Conclusion: Synthetic pretraining should be reframed as a corpus composition problem rather than a generator selection problem. Composition choices must be validated per model family, as optimal mixtures do not reliably transfer across architectures.

Abstract: Choosing the wrong synthetic generator for time-series foundation model pretraining is costly: under identical training budgets, the best and worst generators produce up to a $2\times$ gap in forecasting error, yet the field has no principled way to make this choice. The problem is compounded by the fact that generator rankings are not stable across architectures: across 11 generator families evaluated on Chronos-T5-Mini and Moirai-Small trained from scratch, we find that which generators are useful depends on the model architecture. Rather than solving the generator selection problem, we sidestep it: a simple equal-weight mixture of all generators matches or beats the best individual generator for both architectures, and composing this mixture with real data yields the strongest pretraining corpora overall. Synthetic pretraining is therefore a corpus composition problem, not a generator selection problem, and composition choices should be validated per model family rather than assumed to transfer.

</details>


### [211] [IntentKV: Cross-Turn Intent-Aware KV Cache Pruning for Agent Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09916)
*Junjie Li,Jiong Lou,Jie Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: IntentKV is a learned KV cache pruning method for multi-turn LLM agents that maintains a session-level intent memory, uses memory-attention scoring to evict irrelevant tokens, and employs slot-map redirection for composability with prefix caches. It matches full-cache accuracy while dramatically reducing KV memory and read bandwidth, achieving up to 77.8% reduction in worst-case peak request tokens and 92.6% reduction in raw KV reads on long-horizon agent trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In multi-turn LLM agent settings, single queries expand into long trajectories of tool calls, search results, and intermediate reasoning. Both KV cache memory and KV read bandwidth grow by orders of magnitude within a single trajectory, making the KV cache — not parameter computation — the dominant serving bottleneck. Existing KV pruning methods are not well-suited for the long-horizon, cross-turn nature of agent workloads, motivating a session-aware pruning approach.

Method: IntentKV freezes the base LLM and introduces three components: (1) a session-level **QueryMemory** that retains cross-turn intent information as compressed memory vectors; (2) a **memory-attention rule** that scores each live history token against the QueryMemory to determine eviction priority; and (3) a **zero-initialized residual head** with cross-attention over current-query K-vectors, allowing the model to recover information from evicted tokens. For composability with prefix caches, eviction uses **slot-map redirection**: dropped positions are routed to a sentinel dead slot while surviving K/V rows, RoPE phases, and slot identities remain in place, preserving prefix-cache compatibility.

Result: IntentKV matches the no-pruning full-cache baseline with negligible accuracy drop under tight KV budgets. At an 8k KV budget, mean peak request tokens drop 23.9% on Qwen3-8B and 30.7% on Qwen2.5-14B. On the 100 longest BCP queries where all methods complete, IntentKV-8k reduces worst-case peak request tokens from 92.3k to 20.5k (77.8% reduction) and worst-case raw KV reads from 411M to 31M (92.6% reduction) on Qwen2.5-14B.

Conclusion: IntentKV demonstrates that session-level intent-aware KV pruning can effectively eliminate the KV cache bottleneck for long-horizon LLM agents without sacrificing accuracy, and it does so while maintaining compatibility with prefix caching through its slot-map redirection mechanism.

Abstract: Multi-turn LLM agents fan short queries into long trajectories of tool calls, search results, and intermediate reasoning. Both KV memory and KV read bandwidth grow by orders of magnitude across a single trajectory, making the key-value (KV) cache, not parameter compute, the dominant serving bottleneck for long-horizon agents. We introduce IntentKV, learned KV pruning that keeps the base LLM frozen. IntentKV maintains a session-level QueryMemory of cross-turn intent, scores live history tokens with a memory-attention rule, and adds a zero-initialized residual head with cross-attention over current-query K-vectors. To stay composable with prefix caches, eviction is a slot-map redirection: dropped positions route to a sentinel dead slot while surviving K/V rows, RoPE phases, and slot identities stay in place. IntentKV matches the no-pruning full-cache baseline with almost no accuracy drop under tight KV budgets: at an 8k KV budget, mean peak request tokens drop 23.9% on Qwen3-8B and 30.7% on Qwen2.5-14B. On the 100 longest BCP queries that all methods complete on Qwen2.5-14B, IntentKV-8k further cuts worst-case peak request tokens from 92.3k to 20.5k, a 77.8% reduction, and worst-case raw KV reads from 411M to 31M, a 92.6% reduction.

</details>


### [212] [SPDM: Geometry-Modulated State Space Modeling with Manifold Constraints for Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09917)
*Xingsheng Chen,Siu-Ming Yiu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SPDM introduces manifold constraints into state-space models for multivariate time series forecasting, treating cross-variable correlations as trajectories on the symmetric positive definite (SPD) manifold. It uses a manifold trajectory path with tangent space projection and geometric gating to modulate SSM parameters, achieving state-of-the-art performance while preserving linear-time complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing state-space models (SSMs) for multivariate time series forecasting scan tokenized temporal or spatial sequences but discard the continuously evolving geometric correlation structure among interacting variables. This overlooks principled geometric regularities that could guide and stabilize the selective scanning dynamics.

Method: SPDM employs two cooperating mechanisms: (1) A manifold trajectory path that projects dynamically evolving covariance matrices from the SPD manifold to a Euclidean tangent space, leveraging Riemannian geometry (tangent space linearity, Fréchet mean centrality) as a geometric regularizer. (2) A geometric gating scheme that directly modulates the SSM's internal selective parameters (à la Mamba) using geometric signals derived from the manifold trajectory. The parameterization retains the linear-time complexity of the Mamba parallel scan.

Result: Extensive experiments on eleven real-world benchmark datasets establish state-of-the-art forecasting performance. Ablation studies confirm that geometrically constrained state-space dynamics are the dominant architectural factor behind the performance gains.

Conclusion: Embedding Riemannian geometric constraints from the SPD manifold into SSM selective scanning dynamics yields significant forecasting accuracy improvements while preserving computational efficiency, making SPDM a new state-of-the-art for multivariate time series forecasting.

Abstract: Multivariate time series forecasting requires capturing the continuously evolving correlation structure among interacting variables. Existing state-space models process time series by scanning tokenized temporal or spatial sequences, discarding the evolutionary geometric structure. We address this limitation by introducing manifold constraints into state-space modeling: treating the cross-variable correlation structure as a continuous trajectory on the symmetric positive definite manifold, whose Riemannian geometric features, tangent space linearity, and Frechet mean centrality act as a principled geometric regularizer that guides and stabilizes the selective scanning dynamics of SSMs. We propose SPDM, a geometry-aware SSM architecture that realizes this principle through two cooperating mechanisms: a manifold trajectory path that projects dynamically evolving covariance matrices from the SPD manifold to a Euclidean tangent space, and a geometric gating scheme that directly modulates SSM's internal selective parameters based on geometric signals derived from the manifold trajectory. The parameterization preserves the linear-time complexity of the Mamba parallel scan while embedding rich structural constraints, making the architecture preserve prediction accuracy and computational efficiency simultaneously. Extensive experiments on eleven real-world benchmark datasets establish state-of-the-art forecasting performance, and further studies confirm that geometrically constrained state-space dynamics are the dominant architectural factor behind its performance gains.

</details>


### [213] [Conformal Prediction for Neural Operators: Distribution-Free Uncertainty Quantification in Physics Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09923)
*Michael Chin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First application of split conformal prediction to neural operators (FNO) for PDE solving, providing distribution-free finite-sample coverage guarantees with adaptive-width intervals via MC Dropout-normalized conformal scores, demonstrated on heat conduction benchmarks (89.1% empirical coverage at α=0.1) with an uncertainty decomposition framework separating epistemic (68%) from aleatoric (32%) uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural operators like FNO lack rigorous uncertainty quantification with formal coverage guarantees, which is essential for safety-critical engineering applications (thermal management, battery systems). Existing UQ methods (MC Dropout, Deep Ensembles) only provide relative estimates without finite-sample coverage guarantees.

Method: Split conformal prediction is applied to FNO-based physics simulation. A normalized conformal prediction scheme uses MC Dropout uncertainty estimates to produce adaptive-width prediction intervals. An uncertainty decomposition framework separates epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty. Full-scale experiments run on steady-state heat conduction benchmarks (33.7M parameters, 800 training samples, 5 ensemble members, NVIDIA V100).

Result: Achieves 89.1% empirical coverage at target α=0.1 with spatially adaptive prediction intervals reflecting physical uncertainty structure. Epistemic uncertainty accounts for 68% of total, aleatoric for 32%. Implementation includes an open-source platform with REST API and interactive 3D visualization.

Conclusion: Split conformal prediction with MC Dropout normalization provides a rigorous, distribution-free UQ framework for neural operator-based PDE surrogates, enabling safe deployment in engineering applications. The uncertainty decomposition offers actionable guidance for targeted data collection and model refinement.

Abstract: Neural operators such as the Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) have emerged as powerful surrogates for solving partial differential equations (PDEs), achieving speedups of several orders of magnitude over traditional numerical solvers. However, deploying these models in safety-critical engineering applications -- such as thermal management of electronic components and battery systems -- requires not only accurate point predictions but also rigorous uncertainty guarantees. Existing uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods for neural operators, including Monte Carlo Dropout and Deep Ensembles, provide only relative uncertainty estimates without formal coverage guarantees. In this work, we propose the first application of split conformal prediction to neural operator-based physics simulation, providing distribution-free prediction intervals with finite-sample coverage guarantees. We further introduce a normalized conformal prediction scheme that leverages MC Dropout uncertainty to produce adaptive-width intervals, yielding tighter intervals in regions of low uncertainty and wider intervals where the model is less certain. Full-scale experiments (33.7M parameters, 800 training samples, 5 ensemble members, NVIDIA V100) on steady-state heat conduction benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves 89.1% empirical coverage at the target level of alpha=0.1, while producing spatially adaptive prediction intervals that reflect the underlying physical uncertainty structure. We also provide an uncertainty decomposition framework that separates epistemic uncertainty (68% of total) from aleatoric uncertainty (32% of total), offering actionable guidance for data collection and model improvement. Our method is implemented in an open-source platform with REST API endpoints and interactive 3D visualization.

</details>


### [214] [Sigma-Branch: Hierarchical Single-Path Network Reconstruction for Dynamic Inference with Reduced Active Parameters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09924)
*Kohga Tanaka,Hiroaki Nishi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SigmaB restructures a pretrained dense network into a hierarchical binary tree with a shared backbone, routers, and specialized leaves. Using activation-based spherical k-means clustering for initialization and soft-routing fine-tuning, only one root-to-leaf path executes per inference, reducing active parameters by ~58-60% with minimal accuracy loss across vision and point-cloud tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Edge accelerators face a per-inference bottleneck from off-chip weight transfer, not computation, since dense networks cannot fit on-chip and all parameters must be loaded for each input. Existing model compression methods (pruning, etc.) reduce this transfer but permanently sacrifice capacity. The authors aim to decouple per-inference memory traffic from total parameter count without permanent capacity loss.

Method: SigmaB restructures a pretrained dense network into a hierarchical binary tree: a shared backbone, hierarchical routers (lightweight gating modules), and specialized leaf branches. Weights are distributed via activation-based spherical k-means clustering, which jointly initializes router weights and per-branch channel allocations. Soft-routing fine-tuning then aligns each leaf with its routed input subset. At inference, only a single root-to-leaf path executes, reducing the active-parameter footprint while storing the full dense parameter set in memory.

Result: On CIFAR-100/ResNet-50, ImageNet-1K/ResNet-50, and ModelNet40/PointNet++, SigmaB reduces per-inference active parameters by 58-60% while staying within 1.72 pp of the dense baseline Top-1. At comparable ImageNet-1K Top-1 accuracy, the active-parameter reduction exceeds static structured pruning methods (FPGM, HRank) by 14-23 pp.

Conclusion: SigmaB successfully decouples per-inference memory traffic from total parameter count, enabling deployment on memory-constrained edge accelerators without permanent capacity loss. The cross-modal results (2D vision and 3D point clouds) substantiate this as a general framework-level solution for conditional computation.

Abstract: Deploying deep neural networks on memory-constrained edge accelerators is bottlenecked by per-inference off-chip weight transfer rather than computation: the dense network cannot be retained on-chip, and every parameter must be loaded for every input. Existing model compression reduces this transfer only at the cost of permanent capacity loss. We propose Sigma-Branch (SigmaB), a framework that restructures a pretrained dense network into a hierarchical binary tree composed of a shared backbone, hierarchical routers, and specialized leaves. Pretrained weights are distributed across the tree via activation-based spherical k-means clustering, which jointly initializes router weights and per-branch channel allocations; soft-routing fine-tuning then aligns each leaf with its routed input subset. At inference, the resulting network executes only a single root-to-leaf path, reducing the active-parameter footprint while storing the complete dense parameter set in memory. Across CIFAR-100 / ResNet-50, ImageNet-1K / ResNet-50, and ModelNet40 / PointNet++, SigmaB-Net reduces per-inference active parameters by 58-60% while remaining within 1.72 percentage points (pp) of the dense baseline Top-1. At comparable ImageNet-1K Top-1, the active-parameter reduction exceeds static structured pruning (FPGM, HRank) by 14-23 pp. The cross-modal evaluation, spanning 2D vision and 3D point-cloud backbones, substantiates a framework-level claim that decouples per-inference memory traffic from the total parameter count.

</details>


### [215] [Sample Where You Struggle: Sharpening Base Model Reasoning via Entropy-Guided Power Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09926)
*Hong Guo,Nianhui Guo,Christoph Meinel,Haojin Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EGPS improves sampling from power-tilted distributions $p^\alpha$ by using token-level entropy to focus MCMC moves on high-entropy decision points, replacing uniform resampling in Metropolis-Hastings with entropy-guided localization and Multiple-Try Metropolis, achieving up to 12.6× speedup with state-of-the-art accuracy on math and reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sampling from $p^\alpha$ (power-tilted distribution) elicits RL-level reasoning from base LLMs without fine-tuning, but standard Metropolis-Hastings is computationally expensive and slow-mixing. The authors identify a structural mismatch: $p^\alpha$ diverges from $p$ primarily at sparse, spatially clustered high-entropy decision points, yet MH wastes computation by proposing uniform resampling along the full sequence, including near-deterministic regions.

Method: EGPS is a training-free, verifier-free MCMC sampler with three key components: (1) it skips deterministic token blocks where entropy is negligible, (2) localizes each MCMC move to a high-entropy neighborhood using token-level entropy from the forward pass, and (3) applies Multiple-Try Metropolis at decision points to improve mixing. This makes sampling cost scale with entropy mass rather than sequence length.

Result: On Qwen2.5-Math-7B, EGPS achieves best or tied-best accuracy: MATH500 (75.8%), HumanEval (62.2%), GPQA (42.4%), with up to 12.6× wall-clock speedup over the MH baseline.

Conclusion: Entropy-guided localization of MCMC moves dramatically improves the efficiency of power sampling for LLMs, enabling RL-level reasoning without training. The entropy-mass-scaling principle suggests a broader design paradigm for inference-time sampling on structured discrete distributions.

Abstract: Sampling from the sequence-level power distribution $p^α$ elicits RL-level reasoning from base language models without any parameter updates, but the standard Metropolis--Hastings (MH), a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampler, is both expensive and slow-mixing. We trace both to a structural mismatch: $p^α$ mainly departs from $p$ at a sparse, spatially clustered set of high-entropy decision points, yet MH proposes resampling positions uniformly along the prefix -- wasting compute on near-degenerate conditionals while under-mixing precisely where modes diverge. We propose Entropy-Guided Power Sampling (EGPS), a training-free and verifier-free sampler that re-derives its proposal from token-level entropy already in the forward pass. EGPS skips deterministic blocks, localizes each MCMC move to a high-entropy neighborhood, and applies Multiple-Try Metropolis at decision points -- making sampling cost scale with \emph{entropy mass rather than sequence length}. On Qwen2.5-Math-7B, EGPS reaches best or tied-best accuracy on all three benchmarks (MATH500 $75.8\%$, HumanEval $62.2\%$, GPQA $42.4\%$) at up to a $12.6\times$ wall-clock speedup over the MH baseline.

</details>


### [216] [Trainable Smooth-Rotation Transforms with Learned Channel Scales for LLM Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09927)
*Patrik Czakó,Gábor Kertész,Sándor Szénási*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper addresses activation quantization errors in LLM post-training quantization (PTQ) by proposing a quantile-robust scaling policy for SmoothRot-style equivalent transformations, replacing max-based statistics with high quantiles and adding constrained gradient-based scale optimization. On LLaMA-3.2-1B W4A4, the joint approach achieves up to 18.5% improvement in selected-layer error and 19.9% reduction in full-layer mean error over the SmoothRot baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Activation quantization in LLM PTQ suffers from large errors due to outlier-dominated channels. The authors hypothesize that part of this degradation stems from "over-migration" — excessive shifting of quantization difficulty — caused by max-based scaling statistics in SmoothRot-style equivalent transformations. Current fixed policies lack robustness to outliers, motivating a more controlled and learnable scaling approach.

Method: The authors propose two complementary techniques: (1) a quantile-robust scaling policy that replaces max-based activation statistics (alpha) with high quantiles (e.g., 99.9th percentile) to mitigate over-migration in SmoothRot transforms; (2) constrained gradient-based optimization of channel-wise scales. They evaluate three configurations: quantile-only policy search, joint (alpha, q) search, and full training of scale parameters, all under the W4A4 quantization setting on LLaMA-3.2-1B.

Result: On selected-layer evaluation: quantile-only policy search improves error by 11.1% over SmoothRot baseline; joint (alpha, q) search yields 12% improvement; full training reaches 18.5%. When replaying the best selected-layer policy across all decoder-block down-projection layers, full-layer mean error drops from 97.51 to 78.08 (19.9% reduction).

Conclusion: Robust migration control via quantile-based scaling and lightweight scale learning consistently outperform max-based fixed policies while staying within the equivalent-transform framework. The approach demonstrates that over-migration is a significant contributor to PTQ degradation and can be mitigated without abandoning the established SmoothRot paradigm.

Abstract: Post-training quantization (PTQ) is one of the most practical ways to reduce the serving cost of Large Language Models (LLMs), but activation quantization remains difficult because outlier-dominated channels lead to large quantization errors. This paper investigates whether part of this degradation is caused by over-migration in scaling-based equivalent transformations. We introduce a quantile-robust scaling policy for SmoothRot-style transforms by replacing max-based activation statistics with high quantiles, and we complement it with constrained gradient-based optimization of channel scales. On LLaMA-3.2-1B under W4A4 quantization, quantile-only policy search improves selected-layer error by 11.1% over the SmoothRot baseline, joint (alpha, q) search improves it by 12%, and training reaches 18.5%. Replaying the best selected-layer policy on all decoder-block down-projection layers reduces the corresponding full-layer mean error from 97.51 to 78.08 (19.9%). The results show that robust migration control and lightweight scale learning provide consistent gains over max-based fixed policies while preserving the equivalent-transform framework.

</details>


### [217] [Forward-Only Convolutional Neural Networks with Learnable Channel-Class Assignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09928)
*Mohammadnavid Ghader,Saeed Reza Kheradpisheh,Bahar Farahani,Mahmood Fazlali*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a learnable channel-class assignment mechanism and a loss-aware layer contribution strategy to enhance the Forward-Forward (FF) algorithm in CNNs, achieving new SOTA among FF-based models on CIFAR-10/100 and Tiny-ImageNet while narrowing the gap with backpropagation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing FF-based CNNs rely on static channel-class partitions, limiting their representational capacity and performance on complex tasks. The authors aim to overcome this rigidity by introducing adaptive, data-driven mechanisms for channel specialization and layer weighting.

Method: Two key innovations: (1) A learnable channel-class assignment mechanism that adaptively specializes convolutional channels using entropy and orthogonality regularization to promote effective learning. (2) A loss-aware layer contribution strategy that adaptively weights intermediate-layer predictions based on validation performance, improving forward-only inference. Both are integrated into residual CNN architectures.

Result: The proposed method achieves consistently superior performance across CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny-ImageNet compared to existing forward-only methods, establishing new state-of-the-art results among FF-based models and substantially narrowing the performance gap with backpropagation.

Conclusion: Learnable channel specialization and adaptive layer contribution weighting significantly enhance the representational capacity of forward-only learning in deep CNNs, making FF-based training a more viable alternative to backpropagation.

Abstract: The Forward-Forward (FF) algorithm offers a biologically inspired alternative to backpropagation by replacing gradient-based credit assignment with local, forward-only objectives. While recent extensions have adapted FF to convolutional neural networks (CNNs), existing formulations rely on static channel-class partitions and struggle to perform effectively in complex tasks. In this work, we introduce a learnable channel-class assignment mechanism that enables adaptive, data-driven specialization of convolutional channels, supported by entropy and orthogonality regularization to promote learning performance. We further propose a loss-aware layer contribution strategy that adaptively weights intermediate-layer predictions based on their validation performance, enhancing the effectiveness of forward-only inference. Integrated into residual CNNs, the proposed method achieves consistently superior performance across CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny-ImageNet compared to existing similar forward-only methods. Notably, it establishes new state-of-the-art performance among FF-based models, substantially narrowing the gap with backpropagation. These findings demonstrate that introducing learnable channel specialization and layer contribution weighting significantly enhances the representational capacity of forward-only learning in deep CNNs.

</details>


### [218] [Between Amnesia and Chaos: A Memory Stability Expressivity Trilemma for Trainable Dissipative Oscillator Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09929)
*Caleb Munigety*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper challenges the convention of freezing the physical substrate in reservoir computing. By training nonlinear oscillators end-to-end via a symplectic integrator, the authors reveal a trilemma: memory horizon, gradient stability, and dynamical expressivity cannot be simultaneously maximized because all are controlled by damping. This confines stable training to a shrinking band that closes at a critical horizon, explaining when learned substrates outperform frozen ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional physical reservoir computing freezes the physical substrate and trains only a linear readout, assuming the substrate itself is not usefully trainable. The authors revisit this premise, questioning whether the nonlinear mechanical backbone can be productively learned end-to-end, and under what conditions training the substrate surpasses merely training the readout.

Method: The authors study networks of nonlinear oscillators with learnable mass, damping, and stiffness parameters, trained end-to-end via a symplectic integrator. They derive theoretical relationships linking damping to (1) backward gradient decay (memory horizon), (2) forward sensitivity growth (Lyapunov exponent), and (3) dynamical expressivity. A damping sweep on a twenty-oscillator network empirically confirms the Lyapunov exponent crosses zero at a stability floor. Compute-matched comparisons of learned versus frozen substrates on delayed recall across nine horizons test the predicted band closure.

Result: The damping sweep confirms the Lyapunov exponent is monotone in damping and crosses zero at a well-defined stability floor. Learned substrates dominate frozen ones at short horizons, but the advantage closes and reverses near a horizon of eleven steps — matching the predicted signature of band closure. Trained models spontaneously settle near the stability floor (the edge of chaos). The analytic ceiling overestimates the empirical crossover roughly fivefold, revealing a gap between detectable and learnable gradients.

Conclusion: Training a physical substrate beats freezing it only within a finite, damping-governed band of stable gradients, which shrinks as memory horizon grows and vanishes at a critical point. The work provides a principled, empirically confirmed account of when end-to-end learning of a mechanical reservoir is advantageous and when it is not.

Abstract: Physical reservoir computing harnesses nonlinear mechanical dynamics but, by convention, freezes the substrate and trains only a linear readout, presuming the substrate is not usefully trainable. We revisit that premise for networks of nonlinear oscillators whose mass, damping, and stiffness are learned end-to-end through a symplectic integrator. Our central result is a trilemma: memory horizon, gradient stability, and dynamical expressivity cannot be simultaneously maximized, because all three are governed by the damping. The backward gradient decays at a rate set by the damping, capping how far back credit can propagate, while forward sensitivities grow exponentially in the largest Lyapunov exponent, so usable gradients require damping above a stability floor. Since the Lyapunov exponent falls as damping rises while the memory ceiling falls as the horizon grows, stable training is confined to a band that contracts with horizon and closes at a critical point. We test every step on a twenty-oscillator network. A damping sweep finds the largest Lyapunov exponent monotone and crossing zero at a well-defined stability floor, confirming the theorem's key assumption. A compute-matched comparison of learned versus frozen substrate on delayed recall across nine horizons shows the learned substrate dominating at short horizons and the advantage closing and reversing near a horizon of eleven steps, the predicted signature of band closure; trained models settle near the stability floor, seeking the edge of chaos unprompted. The analytic ceiling overestimates the empirical crossover roughly fivefold, a gap between detectable and learnable gradient that we report rather than tune away. The contribution is a confirmed account of when training a physical substrate beats freezing it.

</details>


### [219] [When RL Fails after SFT: Rejuvenating Model Plasticity for Robust SFT-to-RL Handoff](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09932)
*Runze Liu,Jiashun Liu,Xu Wan,Yuqian Fu,Ling Pan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Excessive SFT reduces model plasticity, hindering subsequent RL optimization. The authors propose "Rejuvenation," combining base-anchored fusion and targeted neuron reset to restore plasticity while retaining SFT priors, improving RL performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In the standard SFT→RL pipeline for LLM post-training, excessive SFT checkpoints consistently show limited RL improvement. The authors attribute this to loss of model plasticity — the diminished capacity of an over-trained SFT policy to be reshaped by RL — and seek to diagnose and mitigate this phenomenon.

Method: The paper first conducts multi-perspective analysis (parameter changes, output distributions, RL optimization dynamics) revealing that over-SFT models produce over-confident token distributions and sharp parameter landscapes. To address this, the authors propose **Rejuvenation**, which has two components: (1) base-anchored model fusion to reduce excessive SFT-induced parameter drift, and (2) targeted neuron reset to mitigate model rigidity, together restoring plasticity while preserving useful SFT-acquired behavioral priors.

Result: Rejuvenation consistently improves RL performance on over-trained SFT models across math reasoning and agentic tasks, and also enhances generalization to out-of-distribution tasks.

Conclusion: Over-trained SFT models suffer from plasticity loss due to over-confident distributions and sharp loss landscapes. Rejuvenation offers a simple, effective remedy that ensures a more robust SFT-to-RL handoff, improving both in-distribution RL gains and OOD generalization.

Abstract: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) followed by Reinforcement Learning (RL) has become a standard pipeline for Large Language Model (LLM) post-training. SFT is expected to provide a useful behavioral prior for RL to further enhance model capabilities. However, checkpoints with excessive SFT often show limited improvement during RL. We attribute this failure to the loss of model plasticity: the reduced ability of an SFT-initialized policy to be effectively reshaped by subsequent RL. To better understand this phenomenon, we conduct detailed analysis from multiple perspectives, including parameter changes, output spaces, and RL optimization dynamics. Our results show that models from excessive SFT tend to produce over-confident token distributions and exhibit sharp parameter landscapes, which make them harder to optimize in the RL stage. To enable a more robust SFT-to-RL handoff, we propose \texttt{Rejuvenation}, a simple yet effective method that restores plasticity while preserving useful SFT-acquired priors. Rejuvenation leverages base-anchored model fusion to reduce excessive SFT-induced drift with targeted neuron reset to mitigate model rigidity. Experimental results on both math reasoning tasks and agentic tasks demonstrate that our approach consistently improves RL performance on over-trained SFT models, while also enhancing generalization to out-of-distribution tasks.

</details>


### [220] [nCMD: Benign-Anchored Feature Selection for Imbalanced Network Intrusion Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09934)
*Abu Fuad Ahmad,Istiaque Ahmed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes nCMD, a benign-anchored feature selection method for NIDS that scores features by how far attack-class distributions deviate from the benign-class mean, outperforming traditional symmetric filter methods under high imbalance and tight feature budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional filter-based feature selection methods for NIDS use global statistics computed symmetrically across all classes. This fails to capture the asymmetric nature of intrusion detection, where attacks are deviations from dominant benign traffic. A method aligned with this operational asymmetry is needed, especially for resource-constrained, high-dimensional, and highly imbalanced settings.

Method: The authors propose benign-anchored Classwise Mean Deviation (nCMD), a lightweight, interpretable filter method. For each feature, nCMD computes the deviation of each attack-class distribution from the benign-class mean (rather than a global mean), then aggregates these deviations into a relevance score. Feature ranking and selection proceed based on this score, at no additional computational cost versus classical filters.

Result: Evaluated on CICIDS2017, CICDDoS2019, NSL-KDD, and UNSW-NB15 across multiple feature budgets and three downstream classifiers, nCMD matches or exceeds classical filter baselines in macro-averaged F1-score. It achieves the best result on three of four datasets under every classifier, with the strongest gains under tight feature budgets and severe class imbalance.

Conclusion: Benign-anchored ranking via nCMD is a scalable, interpretable, and effective preprocessing component for resource-constrained NIDS, aligning feature selection with the semantic reality that attacks are anomalies relative to benign behavior.

Abstract: Feature selection is critical for network intrusion detection systems (NIDS) operating under high-dimensional, highly imbalanced traffic, as found in operational and defense networks. Traditional filter methods rank features using global statistics computed symmetrically across classes and thus fail to capture the asymmetry of intrusion detection, where attacks are best characterized as deviations from dominant benign traffic.
  We propose benign-anchored Classwise Mean Deviation (nCMD), a lightweight and interpretable method that scores feature relevance based on the deviation of attack-class distributions from the benign-class mean, rather than a globally biased reference. This approach aligns feature selection with the operational semantics of NIDS at no additional computational cost.
  Across four benchmark datasets (CICIDS2017, CICDDoS2019, NSL-KDD, and UNSW-NB15), multiple feature budgets, and three downstream classifiers, nCMD matches or exceeds classical filter baselines in macro-averaged F1-score. It achieves the best result on three of the four datasets and under every classifier, with the strongest improvements observed under tight feature budgets and severe class imbalance. These results support benign-anchored ranking as a scalable and interpretable preprocessing component for resource-constrained NIDS.

</details>


### [221] [One Lens, Many Worlds : A Capability-Typed Interface for World-Model Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09936)
*Bhavith Chandra Challagundla,Sanskar Pandey,Param Thakkar,Rishikesh Mallagundla,Yugandhar Reddy Gogireddy,Wenhao Lu,Hindol Roy Choudhury,Shravani Challagundla,Mohamed Deraz Nasr,Spursh Deshpande*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: WorldModelLens introduces a unified, capability-typed interface for interpreting world models across diverse architectures (RSSM, token-based, JEPA), enabling write-once interpretability analyses via a shared hook-and-cache layer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current world models (Dreamer, IRIS, I-JEPA) use fundamentally different computational substrates, yet interpretability methods applied to them share common primitives. Existing hook-and-cache tooling assumes transformer language models and lacks support for actions, environment steps, or imagined rollouts, forcing researchers to re-implement analyses from scratch for each architecture. The authors argue this fragmentation is a tooling problem, not a model problem.

Method: WorldModelLens proposes a capability-typed adapter interface with four required methods (encode, transition, initial_state, sample) and optional heads (decode, reward, continue, actor, critic) declared via explicit capability descriptors. A single hook-and-cache layer provides time-indexed activations, imagination rollouts, and intervention replay over this interface, making RL and self-supervised world models first-class citizens.

Result: The framework unifies interpretability across heterogeneous world model architectures, allowing analyses such as probing, activation patching, sparse autoencoders, and surprise analysis to be written once and applied to any compliant model without re-implementation.

Conclusion: The shared structure of world models can be captured by a small typed interface. By standardizing around capability-typed adapters rather than forcing architectural uniformity, WorldModelLens eliminates redundant engineering and accelerates interpretability research on world models.

Abstract: World models are now built on substantially different computational substrates. Latent recurrent state-space models such as PlaNet and the Dreamer family compress observations into recurrent states; token-based models such as IRIS quantize observations into a learned codebook and predict autoregressively with a transformer; and joint-embedding predictive architectures such as I-JEPA predict in a learned latent space with no pixel decoder. The interpretability methods applied to these models, including probing, activation patching, sparse autoencoders, and surprise analysis, share a common set of primitives, yet they are re-implemented from scratch for each architecture because existing hook-and-cache tooling assumes a transformer language model with no notion of actions, environment steps, or imagined rollouts. We argue that this fragmentation reflects the tooling rather than the models, and that the shared structure of world models is captured by a small typed interface. We present WorldModelLens, an open-source interpretability substrate organized around a capability-typed adapter: every model implements four required methods (encode, transition, initial state, sample) and declares a set of optional heads (decode, reward, continue, actor, critic) through an explicit capability descriptor, so that reinforcement-learning and self-supervised world models are first-class without either imitating the other. A single hook and cache layer exposes time-indexed activations, imagination rollouts, and intervention replay over this interface, allowing each analysis to be written once.

</details>


### [222] [RKSC: Reasoning-Aware KV Cache Sharing and Confident Early Exit for Multi-Step LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09937)
*Anirudh Sekar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RKSC is a training-free framework that accelerates multi-branch LLM reasoning by sharing KV caches across semantically similar branches, applying confidence-gated early exits during verification, and using attention-weighted eviction to manage cache growth — achieving ~3x speedup with negligible error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-branch LLM reasoning pipelines (e.g., tree-of-thought, self-consistency) suffer from structural redundancy: (1) redundant KV cache computation across semantically similar branches, (2) unnecessary full forward passes during verification when confidence is already high. Existing prefix caching (vLLM, SGLang) only handles exact token matches, missing semantically equivalent but lexically distinct prefixes.

Method: RKSC introduces three components: (1) ASKS (Attention-Similarity KV Sharing) computes prefix KV cache once and broadcasts to semantically similar branches using hidden-state cosine similarity, generalizing exact-match prefix caching; (2) CGEE (Confidence-Gated Early Exit) skips or truncates verification forward passes based on branch confidence decisiveness and per-layer entropy stabilization; (3) RSBCM (Reasoning-Selective Block Cache Manager) evicts cached blocks using attention-weighted depth-priority to prevent unbounded growth.

Result: Evaluated across five model families (7B-10B), four benchmarks, and 1,000 problems: mean speedup of 3.008x over no-KV baseline (peak 3.990x), 1.66x improvement over vLLM-equivalent prefix caching. CGEE error rate of 0.37% (6 errors / 1,616 verify calls). No fine-tuning or architecture changes needed.

Conclusion: RKSC demonstrates that substantial inference acceleration in multi-branch reasoning is achievable without training or architectural modification, by exploiting semantic-level KV reuse and confidence-based early termination. The near-zero error rate validates the safety of these optimizations for practical deployment.

Abstract: We introduce RKSC (Reasoning-Aware KV Cache Sharing), a training-free inference framework that eliminates two structural redundancies in multi-branch LLM reasoning pipelines. ASKS (Attention-Similarity KV Sharing) computes the prefix KV cache once and broadcasts it to all semantically similar branches via hidden-state cosine similarity, strictly generalising the token-exact prefix caching used by vLLM and SGLang. CGEE (Confidence-Gated Early Exit) applies two complementary exit mechanisms: (1) it skips the verification forward pass entirely when generation confidence is decisive across branches, and (2) it terminates the verification pass at an intermediate layer when per-layer entropy stabilises, using lightweight hooks on the transformer backbone. RSBCM (Reasoning-Selective Block Cache Manager) prevents unbounded cache growth via attention-weighted depth-priority eviction. Across five model families (7B-10B), four benchmarks, and 1,000 evaluated problems, RKSC achieves a mean speedup of 3.008x over the No-KV baseline (peak 3.990x), a 1.66x mean improvement over vLLM-equivalent prefix caching, with a CGEE-induced error rate of only 0.37% (6 errors out of 1,616 verify calls). No fine-tuning or architecture changes are required. Code is available at https://github.com/AnirudhSekar/RKSC.

</details>


### [223] [Quality Is Not a Safety Proxy Under Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10154)
*Sahil Kadadekar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A systematic audit shows that quality-preserving quantization does not guarantee safety preservation — many quantized checkpoints maintain or improve quality metrics while suffering 12–68 percentage point drops in refusal rates. A proposed screening tool (RTSI) effectively routes hidden-danger models to direct safety testing, but no mechanistic probe reliably separates dangerous from safe checkpoints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantized model checkpoints are routinely screened using quality metrics alone, with direct safety testing deferred or omitted entirely. This paper challenges the implicit assumption that preserved quality implies preserved safety, investigating whether this shortcut is valid across diverse quantization methods and model families.

Method: The authors construct a matched 51-row matrix spanning 6 models from 4 families, 7 GGUF quantization levels, and AWQ/GPTQ INT4 checkpoints. They conduct a four-probe mechanistic analysis (entropy, refusal-direction, calibration, and safety-associated neuron probes) across 17 FP16/AWQ/GPTQ cells. They also introduce the Refusal Template Stability Index (RTSI), a behavioral screening tool built from four refusal-template drift features, calibrated on the matrix and validated via row-level leave-one-out. Claude Sonnet 4 is used as a secondary judge on 11,470 stratified items.

Result: All 36 quality-safety pairings split direction across models. Eleven hidden-danger or near-hidden-danger rows show stable/improved quality with 12–68pp refusal drops; 7/11 AWQ/GPTQ rows are hidden-danger. Mechanistic probes fail as separators: entropy, refusal-direction, and calibration probes are weak or null, and although safety-associated neurons absorb 1.39× more quantization error (p < 5×10⁻⁷), the effect is not regime-specific. Claude Sonnet 4 agrees with the primary judge on 89.9% of rows (κ=0.873). RTSI routes 10/10 hidden-danger rows to direct safety testing (Wilson 95% CI lower bound 0.72) while leaving 23/45 non-baseline rows low-risk, outperforming single-feature baselines.

Conclusion: Retained quality cannot waive direct safety evaluation for quantized checkpoints. The quality-safety shortcut fails systematically, and while RTSI offers a practical screening solution within the studied matrix, cross-stack transfer requires recalibration. No mechanistic probe provides a reliable shortcut either.

Abstract: Quantized checkpoints are often screened first with quality metrics and only later, if at all, with direct safety tests. This paper audits that shortcut on a matched 51-row matrix spanning 6 models, 4 families, a 7-level GGUF ladder, and AWQ/GPTQ INT4 checkpoints. In this matrix the shortcut fails: all 36 quality-safety pairings split direction across models, and 9 hidden-danger rows plus 1 near-hidden-danger row show quality stable or improved while refusal falls by 12-68 percentage points. Seven of the 11 AWQ/GPTQ rows are hidden-danger. A four-probe mechanistic follow-up over the 17 Hugging Face-backed FP16/AWQ/GPTQ cells does not rescue it: entropy, refusal-direction, and calibration probes are weak or null separators of dangerous rows, and although probe-identified safety-associated neurons absorb 1.39$\times$ more quantization error overall ($p < 5 \times 10^{-7}$), the effect is not regime-specific. Claude Sonnet 4 relabels 11,470 items in a predefined stratified set, agrees with the primary gemma3:12b judge on 89.9\% of rows ($κ= 0.873$, 95\% CI [0.866, 0.881]), and changes 0/10 hidden-danger cells. A calibrated study-internal behavioral screen -- the Refusal Template Stability Index (RTSI), built from four refusal-template drift features and calibrated on this matrix -- routes 10/10 hidden- or near-hidden-danger rows to direct safety testing (Wilson 95\% CI lower bound 0.72) while leaving 23 of 45 non-baseline rows in a low-risk bucket under both in-sample scoring and row-level leave-one-out validation; on the same matrix, the best single-feature baselines (unique-prefix-rate-delta, raw refusal-rate delta) recover 9/10 and 8/10 respectively at matched bucket size, and cross-stack transfer requires recalibration. For the quantized checkpoints, model families, and safety outcomes studied here, retained quality cannot waive direct safety evaluation.

</details>


### [224] [Interactions Between Crosscoder Features: A Compact Proofs Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09940)
*Dmitry Manning-Coe,Thomas Read,Anna Soligo,Oliver Clive-Griffin,Chun-Hei Yip,Rajashree Agrawal,Jason Gross*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper formalizes feature interactions in dictionary learning (SAEs/crosscoders) via compact proofs, introduces an interaction measure for MLP layers, and demonstrates three applications: a differentiable loss penalty for computationally sparse crosscoders, semantically meaningful feature clustering, and detection of sleeper agent interactions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dictionary learning methods like SAEs and crosscoders decompose activations into independent features, but feature interactions cause reconstruction errors. The authors aim to formalize this intuition and exploit interaction measurements for practical gains in sparsity, interpretability, and anomaly detection.

Method: The authors construct compact proofs of model performance using crosscoders, derive an explicit error term interpretable as feature interaction (particularly for MLP layers), and leverage this interaction measure for three applications: (1) a differentiable loss penalty to induce computational sparsity, (2) clustering features by interaction patterns, and (3) analyzing interactions in sleeper agents.

Result: Computationally sparse crosscoders trained with the interaction penalty retain 60% of MLP performance with only one feature per datapoint per neuron, versus 10% for standard crosscoders. Interaction-based clustering yields semantically meaningful feature groups. Sleeper agents exhibit significant feature interactions detectable by this measure.

Conclusion: Feature interactions, formalized through compact proofs and an explicit MLP interaction term, provide a principled and practically useful lens for improving dictionary learning sparsity, interpreting feature relationships, and detecting anomalous model behavior such as sleeper agents.

Abstract: Dictionary learning methods like Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) and crosscoders attempt to explain a model by decomposing its activations into independent features. Interactions between features hence induce errors in the reconstruction. We formalize this intuition via compact proofs and make five contributions. First, we show how, \textit{in principle}, a compact proof of model performance can be constructed using a crosscoder. Second, we show that an error term arising in this proof can naturally be interpreted as a measure of interaction between crosscoder features and provide an explicit expression for the interaction term in the Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) layers. We then provide three applications of this new interaction measure. In our third contribution we show that the interaction term itself can be used as a differentiable loss penalty. Applying this penalty, we can achieve ``computationally sparse'' crosscoders that retain $60\%$ of MLP performance when only keeping a single feature at each datapoint and neuron, compared to $10\%$ in standard crosscoders. We then show that clustering according to our interaction measure provides semantically meaningful feature clusters, and finally that sleeper agents have significant interactions. Code is available at https://github.com/chainik1125/crosscoders-feature-interactions/tree/arxiv.

</details>


### [225] [Alignment Defends LLMs from Property Inference Attacks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10217)
*Pengrun Huang,Chhavi Yadav,Ruihan Wu,Kamalika Chaudhuri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes alignment-based defenses (using DPO and GRPO) to mitigate property inference attacks on fine-tuned LLMs, reshaping the output distribution post-training without requiring access to original training data or retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing defenses against property inference attacks on LLMs require modifying the training data distribution and retraining, which is infeasible when data is unavailable or models are already deployed. A post-hoc defense that operates without data access is needed.

Method: The authors adapt two RLHF frameworks—Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO)—as post-training alignment defenses. DPO constructs preference pairs to steer the model away from revealing dataset-level properties, while GRPO employs a custom reward function to reshape the model's output distribution toward a target property ratio, effectively obfuscating sensitive dataset-level information.

Result: Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that the alignment-based defenses effectively mitigate property inference attacks while preserving a strong utility–confidentiality tradeoff.

Conclusion: Post-training alignment via DPO and GRPO provides a practical, data-free defense against property inference attacks on deployed LLMs, balancing confidentiality with maintained utility.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly fine-tuned on domain-specific datasets that may contain sensitive, dataset-level properties. Recent work has shown that such dataset-level information can be effectively extracted through property inference attacks, posing a confidentiality risk.
