Existing defenses against these attacks primarily operate by modifying the training data distribution and hence require access to the original data and retraining the model, limiting their applicability to settings where data is unavailable or models are already deployed. In this work, we propose alignment-based defenses for mitigating property inference attacks in LLMs. Our approach reshapes the model's output distribution towards a target property ratio via post-training alignment, without modifying the training data. In particular, we adapt two widely used RLHF frameworks--Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO)--as our defenses by constructing preference pairs and defining a specific reward function respectively. Through comprehensive experiments, we show that our alignment based defenses effectively mitigate property inference attacks while maintaining a strong utility confidentiality tradeoff.

</details>


### [226] [Learning Where to Simulate: Generative Active Sampling for Online PDE Surrogate Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09949)
*Pierre Cesar,Sofya Dymchenko,Abhishek Purandare,Bruno Raffin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OGAS is an online active learning method that trains a diffusion model to conditionally sample PDE configuration parameters toward challenging regimes, using surrogate-derived difficulty signals to steer data generation, consistently improving tail error statistics with negligible overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uniform sampling of PDE configuration parameters (initial conditions, physical coefficients) for training surrogate models under-represents challenging dynamical regimes, leading to high prediction errors and large error variance. Online training couples data generation with surrogate training, enabling on-the-fly steering of solver parameters, but requires an efficient mechanism to exploit this capability.

Method: OGAS trains a fast diffusion model in parallel with the surrogate to serve as a conditional sampler that maps a surrogate-derived difficulty signal (e.g., loss or uncertainty) to configuration parameters. Target difficulty signals are actively drawn from a prior biased toward high difficulty, continuously steering data generation toward challenging regimes without delaying training. The method is evaluated on 2D PDEs (Kuramoto-Sivashinsky, Navier-Stokes, Gray-Scott) with up to 308 parameters using multiple surrogate architectures.

Result: Across all settings, OGAS consistently improves tail statistics, yielding substantial reductions in errors above the 99th percentile and overall error dispersion compared to uniform sampling. A trade-off with average error exists, but OGAS effectively ensures worst-case reliability of trained surrogates.

Conclusion: OGAS provides an efficient online active sampling strategy that significantly enhances worst-case surrogate reliability with negligible wall-time overhead, making it a practical method for training PDE surrogates that must generalize across diverse configurations.

Abstract: Data-driven PDE surrogates are trained with data produced by numerical PDE solvers. However, when the surrogate's goal is to generalize across a wide range of PDE configurations (e.g., initial conditions and physical coefficients), generating a representative training set is non-trivial. Uniform sampling of configuration parameters often under-represents trajectories exhibiting challenging dynamics, leading to high prediction errors and large error variance in the trained surrogate. Online training, where data generation and surrogate training are coupled, offers a natural advantage by allowing solver parameters to be steered on-the-fly. To efficiently exploit this capability, we introduce Online Generative Active Sampling (OGAS), an active learning method that reactively learns the relationship between configuration parameters and surrogate performance to control the sampling distribution. OGAS trains a fast diffusion model in parallel to the surrogate to act as a conditional sampler, mapping a surrogate-derived difficulty signal (e.g., loss or uncertainty) to configuration parameters. By actively drawing target signals from a prior biased toward high difficulty, OGAS continuously steers data generation toward challenging regimes without delaying the training workflow. We evaluate OGAS across 2D PDEs with distinct challenging dynamics (Kuramoto-Sivashinsky, Navier-Stokes, Gray-Scott) and up to 308 parameters, using multiple surrogate architectures. Across all settings, OGAS consistently improves tail statistics, yielding substantial reductions in errors above the 99th percentile and overall error dispersion compared to uniform sampling. While prioritizing challenging trajectories introduces a trade-off with average error, OGAS effectively ensures worst-case reliability of trained surrogates with negligible wall-time overhead.

</details>


### [227] [Privacy-Preserving Credit Risk Prediction with Alternative Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10333)
*Hongzhe Zhang,Jiarong Xu,Jing He,Xiao Fang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces PrivacyCredit, a privacy-preserving machine learning method that enables financial institutions to leverage alternative data (e.g., mobile phone records) for credit risk prediction without exposing consumer privacy, while maintaining centralized model ownership and lossless predictive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Alternative data held by external entities can significantly improve credit risk prediction, but direct data sharing violates consumer privacy. Existing credit risk prediction research largely ignores this privacy constraint, creating a gap between predictive potential and practical deployability.

Method: The authors define a novel problem with three constraints — privacy-preserving, model-confidentiality (centralized model at the financial institution), and lossless performance — and develop PrivacyCredit, a privacy-preserving ML method. They provide theoretical proofs of these three properties and validate the approach through experiments on a real-world credit dataset linked with alternative data.

Result: PrivacyCredit achieves identical predictive performance to a model trained on the insecure plaintext combination of traditional and alternative data, while satisfying all three constraints. The method's model-confidentiality and computational efficiency are also empirically validated.

Conclusion: PrivacyCredit successfully bridges the gap between leveraging alternative data for credit risk prediction and preserving consumer privacy, offering a practical, lossless, and secure solution deployable by financial institutions.

Abstract: Credit risk prediction is a critical problem in the consumer credit industry. Traditionally, financial institutions construct credit risk prediction models using borrowers' demographic, financial, and credit history data, collectively referred to as traditional data. Recent studies have demonstrated that alternative data, such as borrowers' mobile phone communication data, enable lenders to acquire fuller and more accurate profiles of borrowers' creditworthiness, thereby improving credit risk prediction performance. Nevertheless, alternative data are held by external entities independent of financial institutions. Directly sharing alternative data with financial institutions infringe on consumer privacy, yet existing credit risk prediction studies largely overlook this issue. To address this gap, we define a new problem, namely privacy-preserving credit risk prediction with alternative data, which simultaneously considers three practical constraints: the privacy-preserving constraint that protects consumer privacy, the model-confidentiality constraint that learns and stores the model centrally at the financial institution, and the lossless constraint that maintains the performance of the learned model. To solve this problem, we develop PrivacyCredit, a novel privacy-preserving machine learning method. We then theoretically demonstrate the privacy-preserving, model-confidential, and lossless properties of PrivacyCredit. Through extensive experiments using a real-world credit dataset linked with alternative data, we demonstrate the predictive value of securely incorporating alternative data into credit risk prediction and show that PrivacyCredit achieves the same predictive performance as the model learned from the insecure plaintext combination of traditional and alternative data. We further evaluate its model-confidentiality property and computational efficiency.

</details>


### [228] [Integrating Out, Twice:The Open-System Case That Neural-Network Ensemble Theory Is Missing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09950)
*Jin Lei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This note establishes a correspondence between neural network ensembles (closed case) and nuclear reaction theory's optical model (open case), revealing that standard deep learning lacks the continuous-spectrum, wave-like dynamics needed for the open case's distinctive uncertainty contributions. The main finding is a negative result: operationally useful uncertainty remains epistemic and resides in the closed half.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore whether the open-system formalism from nuclear reaction theory—which exactly accounts for probability flux lost to a continuum—can be meaningfully exported to machine learning architectures, potentially providing a principled source of uncertainty quantification beyond standard ensembling.

Method: The author derives the closed-case dictionary (neural tangent kernel = Fisher sensitivity kernel, infinite-width limit = Gaussian process emulator, lazy-to-feature transition = reduced-basis validity boundary) using only moments, Gaussian algebra, and block inversion. The open case is framed via the Schur complement and non-Hermitian effective generators from nuclear optical model theory. Empirical tests are conducted on a truncated attention map, a token-level transfer operator, and a sparse expert router.

Result: The open export yields a mostly negative result: the conserved flux ledger appears only where openness is genuinely present, but its distinctive content is absent—it is either an artifact of the partition or pinned near a floor by the training objective. The structurally missing ingredient is an eliminated sector with continuous spectrum and wave-like (not relaxational) dynamics.

Conclusion: The correspondence is established, but its practical value is limited by a structural mismatch: mainstream ML systems employ finite, dissipative, relaxational dynamics rather than the continuous-spectrum wave dynamics required for the open case. The note's primary contribution is the precise location of this negative finding within the correspondence map.

Abstract: Averaging a neural network over its random parameters and marginalizing a Gaussian sector are the same operation, the Schur complement of the eliminated block, and when that block is closed it returns a covariance and its inverse. That is all a network ensemble produces, the closed case. The open case is missing, and nuclear reaction theory has it worked out. Projecting a scattering problem onto a chosen set of channels, with the rest carrying probability irreversibly to a continuum, leaves a non-Hermitian effective generator that conserves and itemizes exactly what it loses: the nuclear optical model and its generalized optical theorem. I set the two cases side by side using only the moments of a distribution, the algebra of Gaussians, and block inversion, no field theory, and give the closed-case dictionary in full: the neural tangent kernel is the Fisher sensitivity kernel, the infinite-width Gaussian limit is the Gaussian-process emulator, and the lazy-to-feature transition is the validity boundary of a reduced-basis emulator. I then test the open export on a truncated attention map, a token-level transfer operator, and a sparse expert router, and report a mostly negative result. The conserved flux ledger ports wherever openness is genuinely present, but its distinctive content is absent, an artifact of the chosen partition, or pinned near a floor by the training objective, and the operationally useful uncertainty turns out to be epistemic, living in the closed half of the correspondence, not the open one. The negative has a structural reason this note makes precise: the open case needs an eliminated sector with a continuous spectrum and wave-like, not relaxational, dynamics, which mainstream learning's finite or dissipative objects do not supply. This is a note, not a result; its main finding is that negative one, and its value is the map that locates it.

</details>


### [229] [Advancing the State-of-the-Art in Empirical Privacy Auditing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10481)
*Nicole Mitchell,Galen Andrew,Arun Ganesh,Brendan McMahan,Peter Kairouz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes using synthetic canaries generated via high-temperature LLM sampling for privacy auditing of fine-tuned models and synthetic data, and systematically studies how model capacity and canary entropy interact to affect memorization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning of LLMs on sensitive data risks memorizing training examples. Existing privacy auditing methods struggle with designing effective canary examples that are both highly identifiable and non-private (inspectable). Additionally, synthetic data generated from fine-tuned models introduces its own privacy leakage that needs auditing.

Method: The authors propose: (1) Generating synthetic canaries via high-temperature sampling (T ≥ 0.8) from LLMs, with prompts tailored to the sensitive training data, producing high-influence outliers for strong membership inference audits. (2) A synthetic data audit where an auxiliary model is fine-tuned on the synthetic data, and privacy leakage is estimated by auditing this auxiliary model for the original canaries. (3) A systematic empirical study varying model capacity and canary entropy to examine their interacting effects on memorization.

Result: High-temperature synthetic canaries serve as effective high-influence outliers, enabling strong privacy audits. The synthetic data audit using auxiliary model fine-tuning provides a robust estimate of privacy leakage through generated synthetic data. The systematic investigation reveals how model capacity and canary entropy jointly influence memorization behavior.

Conclusion: Synthetic canaries generated via high-temperature sampling are an effective, inspectable tool for empirical privacy auditing of fine-tuned LLMs and their synthetic data outputs. The interplay between model capacity and canary entropy is critical for understanding and quantifying memorization risks.

Abstract: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large language models (LLMs) can exhibit problematic memorization of individual training examples. Empirical privacy auditing (EPA) quantifies this risk by measuring realistic data leakage on membership inference (MI) or reconstruction attacks. A key challenge in EPA is designing ``canary'' examples that are mixed with the privacy-sensitive training data. We propose generating synthetic canaries via high-temperature sampling ($T \geq 0.8$) from LLMs, using prompts tailored to the privacy-sensitive training data. These canaries act as high-influence outliers, ensuring high identifiability and hence strong audits. Further, since the canaries are themselves non-private, they are inspectable and can be inserted with repetition without jeopardizing the privacy of the real data. An important use of models fine-tuned on privacy-sensitive data is the generation of synthetic data. This also comes with privacy risk. We introduce a powerful synthetic data audit based on fine-tuning an auxiliary model on the synthetic data. Auditing the auxiliary model for the original canaries then provides a strong estimate of the privacy leakage through the synthetic data. Finally, leveraging our strong auditing methodologies, we perform a systematic investigation into the interacting effects of model capacity and canary entropy on memorization.

</details>


### [230] [Hasse Diagrams for Attention: A Partial Order Framework for Designing Transformer Masks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09951)
*Chentao Li,Han Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a theoretical framework analyzing how attention masks in multi-layer Transformers induce information flow that converges to a Hasse diagram (a partial-order DAG). The authors recast parallel training task design as finding minimal common supergraphs of Hasse diagrams, yielding a constructive method to derive attention masks from task families. Two novel masks are designed: Block Two-Stream Attention and Butterfly Attention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite the proliferation of attention mask variants and operators like FlexAttention that support arbitrary masks, there has been no systematic formal analysis of the information-flow structure induced by arbitrary attention masks in Transformers. The authors aim to fill this gap with a rigorous theoretical framework.

Method: The authors develop a complete theoretical framework based on partial order theory. They prove that with sufficient depth, information flow in a multi-layer Transformer converges to a Hasse diagram — a directed acyclic graph representing a partial order. They then reformulate parallel training task design as the problem of finding a minimal common supergraph of Hasse diagrams and establish a criterion for it, enabling constructive derivation of attention masks directly from task families.

Result: The framework yields a constructive method to derive attention masks from arbitrary families of tasks. Applying this, the authors design two novel masks: (1) Block Two-Stream Attention, a block-generation mask ensuring training-inference consistency, and (2) Butterfly Attention, a fully supervised bidirectional attention mask. Both demonstrate the framework's capacity to discover new attention structures.

Conclusion: The proposed theoretical framework provides a principled, constructive approach to designing attention masks for parallel training of Transformers, unifying existing mask variants and enabling the discovery of novel structures with desirable properties.

Abstract: During the training of large Transformer models, attention masks regulate the scope and direction of information flow across a sequence. Numerous mask variants exist, and operators such as FlexAttention already support arbitrary attention masks. Nevertheless, a systematic formal analysis of the information-flow structure induced by arbitrary masks has been missing. This paper develops a complete theoretical framework. We prove that, with sufficient depth, the information flow of a multi-layer Transformer converges to a Hasse diagram -- a directed acyclic graph representing a partial order. Building on this, we recast the design of parallel training tasks as the problem of finding a minimal common supergraph of Hasse diagrams, and we establish a criterion for the minimal common supergraph. This yields a constructive method to derive attention masks directly from a family of tasks. Applying the framework, we design two novel masks: a block-generation attention mask that ensures training-inference consistency (Block Two-Stream Attention), and a fully supervised bidirectional attention mask (Butterfly Attention). These results demonstrate the framework's capacity to discover new structures.

</details>


### [231] [In Defense of Information Leakage in Concept-based Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10669)
*Mateo Espinosa Zarlenga*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper challenges the conventional view that information leakage in concept-based models is always harmful, arguing that some leakage ("benign leakage") is necessary for accuracy and intervenability under real-world concept incompleteness, and proposes a reformulated training objective to exploit this.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing narrative holds that concept-based models leaking concept-irrelevant information is undesirable and harms interpretability. The authors argue this view is ill-posed—evidence linking leakage to reduced interpretability is inconclusive—and impractical, since real-world settings inevitably suffer from concept incompleteness, making some leakage necessary for accurate, intervenable models.

Method: The authors reframe the typical concept-based model training objective to explicitly accommodate and encourage "benign leakage." Rather than attempting to eliminate all leakage, they optimize a modified objective that allows the model to leverage leakage that does not compromise accuracy or intervenability.

Result: By optimizing the reframed training objective, concept-based models can encourage and exploit benign leakage without sacrificing accuracy or intervenability, demonstrating that leakage is not categorically harmful.

Conclusion: The conventional blanket condemnation of leakage in concept-based models is misguided. Under real-world constraints of concept incompleteness, benign leakage exists and is practically necessary; CMs should be designed to harness rather than eradicate it.

Abstract: Concept-based models (CMs), deep neural networks that ground their predictions on representations aligned with human-understandable concepts (e.g., "round", "stripes", etc.), have been shown to learn representations that leak concept-irrelevant information. As the traditional narrative goes, this leakage is undesirable and should be eradicated as it leads to uninterpretable models. In this paper, we posit that this conventional view of leakage in CMs is not only ill-posed, as the evidence of how leakage makes a model less interpretable is often inconclusive, but also bound to lead to impractical CMs under common real-world constraints. Specifically, we argue that in real-world settings where concept incompleteness is the norm, some leakage is often necessary for constructing accurate and intervenable CMs. To this end, we propose that there is such a thing as benign leakage and show that, by optimizing a reframing of the typical CM training objective, CMs can encourage and exploit this form of leakage without sacrificing accuracy or intervenability.

</details>


### [232] [Does Normalization Choice Matter for Causal Large Time-Series Models?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09954)
*Samy-Melwan Vilhes,Gilles Gasso,Mokhtar Z Alaya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper evaluates normalization strategies in causal autoregressive large time-series models, revealing that normalization choices significantly affect training convergence and forecasting performance, and that common approaches may inadvertently leak future information during training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large time-series forecasting models use causal autoregressive architectures where normalization is needed to handle non-stationarities. However, standard normalization in efficient causal settings can leak future information during training. Recent alternatives (causal normalization, statistics from initial observations) exist but their practical implications are poorly understood.

Method: The authors systematically evaluate various normalization strategies applied to transformer-based large time-series models that use patching and efficient causal training strategies. They compare different normalization approaches in terms of training dynamics and forecasting accuracy.

Result: The choice of normalization strategy significantly impacts both training convergence (stability, speed) and final forecasting performance. The study quantifies the trade-offs between different normalization methods and highlights the practical consequences of information leakage in naive normalization schemes.

Conclusion: Normalization is not merely a preprocessing detail but a critical architectural choice in causal large time-series models. Practitioners should carefully select normalization strategies to avoid information leakage while maintaining effective handling of non-stationarities.

Abstract: Large models for time-series forecasting have been emerged as a promising paradigm for training models on heterogeneous collections of signals. These models typically rely on causal autoregressive architectures, where each observation is sequentially predicted from past. In practice, real-world time-series exhibit non-stationarities, which significantly influence predictive performance. To mitigate this, normalization is commonly employed. However, in efficient causal settings it might induce information leakage from future observations during training. Recent alternatives, including causal normalization and statistics computed from initial observations, have been proposed to address this issue, but their practical implications remain insufficiently understood. In this work, we evaluate normalization strategies for transformer-based large time-series models trained with patching and efficient causal strategy. We showcase that normalization choice significantly influences both training convergence and forecasting performance.

</details>


### [233] [Temporal Context Conditioning for Seasonality-Aware Precipitation Nowcasting of High-Intensity Rainfall](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09959)
*Gijs van Nieuwkoop,Siamak Mehrkanoon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TA-SmaAt-UNet enhances radar precipitation nowcasting by adding lightweight cyclical time-of-day and time-of-year encodings to SmaAt-UNet, yielding improved performance on rare high-intensity rainfall events and better seasonal distribution representation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning radar nowcasting models excel at short-term precipitation motion but lack broader meteorological context (e.g., diurnal cycles, seasonal patterns) that influence rainfall development, especially for high-intensity events.

Method: The authors propose TA-SmaAt-UNet, extending SmaAt-UNet with temporal conditioning layers that inject cyclical (sinusoidal) encodings of time-of-day and time-of-year to modulate intermediate feature representations. Layer conductance analysis is used to verify that temporal layers are actively utilized.

Result: Temporal conditioning yields the greatest benefits for rare, high-intensity precipitation events; improves seasonal variability and rainfall-intensity distribution alignment; and the low-parameter temporal layers are confirmed to be actively used by the model.

Conclusion: Simple, physically motivated temporal context is a cost-effective way to improve the realism and reliability of deep learning precipitation nowcasts, particularly for impactful high-intensity events.

Abstract: Precipitation nowcasting is increasingly being approached with deep learning models that learn directly from recent radar observations. Although such models can efficiently capture short-term precipitation motion, they often lack broader contextual information about the meteorological conditions under which rainfall develops. This paper investigates whether lightweight temporal context can improve radar-based nowcasting, particularly for high-intensity rainfall. We propose the Time-Aware Small-Attention U-Net (TA-SmaAt-UNet), which extends the core SmaAt-UNet model with temporal conditioning layers that use cyclical encodings of time-of-day and time-of-year to modulate intermediate feature representations. Experiments on KNMI radar precipitation data show that temporal conditioning is most beneficial for rare, high-intensity precipitation events, while also improving the representation of seasonal variability and predicted rainfall-intensity distributions. A layer conductance analysis further indicates that the added temporal conditioning layers are actively used by the model despite their small parameter cost. These findings suggest that simple, physically motivated temporal context can improve the realism and reliability of deep learning-based precipitation nowcasts. The implementation of our models and training setup is available on \href{https://github.com/gijsvn/TA-SmaAt-UNet}{GitHub}.

</details>


### [234] [HydraCIL: Decoupled Class-Incremental Learning through Prototype-Guided Multi-Head Classifiers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09960)
*Daniel Vila-Cruz,Laura Morán-Fernández,Verónica Bolón-Canedo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HydraCIL is a decoupled continual learning model that freezes the backbone and uses prototype-guided multi-head classifiers, enabling efficient class-incremental learning on resource-constrained devices without retraining the feature extractor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most CIL methods demand powerful hardware and long retraining cycles, making them impractical for embedded/edge AI systems (e.g., robots) that require rapid, energy-efficient adaptation with limited resources.

Method: HydraCIL freezes the backbone after initial training and decouples feature extraction from learning. For each new task, features are extracted once and a lightweight, task-specific classifier head is created using prototype-guided representations. At inference, the appropriate head is selected via similarity comparison with stored prototypes, avoiding any backbone retraining.

Result: On CIFAR-100, ImageNet-100, CoRe50, and Flowers102, HydraCIL matches or surpasses state-of-the-art CIL methods while substantially reducing training time and carbon footprint.

Conclusion: HydraCIL offers a practical, energy-efficient solution for continual learning in real-world embedded settings where rapid adaptation and sustainability are critical.

Abstract: We present HydraCIL, a decoupled continual learning model based on prototype-guided multi-head classifiers, targeting sustainable deployment in embedded and resource-constrained environments. While most Class-Incremental Learning (CIL) methods rely on powerful hardware and long retraining cycles, real-world systems, such as robots or edge AI devices, must adapt quickly with limited resources. HydraCIL addresses this gap by freezing the backbone and decoupling feature extraction from learning. For each task, features are extracted once and a lightweight, task-specific classifier head is created, avoiding costly backbone retraining. At inference, HydraCIL selects the appropriate head via similarity with prototypes. Experiments on CIFAR-100, ImageNet-100, CoRe50, and Flowers102 datasets show that HydraCIL matches or outperforms state-of-the-art CIL methods while significantly reducing training time and carbon footprint, making it a practical solution for continual learning in real-world and embedded settings, where energy efficiency and rapid adaptation are critical.

</details>


### [235] [3SPO: State-Score-Supervised Policy Optimization for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09961)
*Yu Han,Kailing Li,Yang Jiao,Yulin Dai,Yuqian Fu,Linhai Zhuo,Tianwen Qian*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: 3SPO (State-Score-Supervised Policy Optimization) is a novel RL algorithm for LLM agents that performs post-step policy optimization using dynamic state-score supervision, addressing sparse/delayed rewards and credit assignment in multi-turn tasks. It achieves logarithmic allocation regret theoretically and outperforms GRPO by +22.6% on ALFWorld and +15.6 points on WebShop.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL algorithms for LLM agents (e.g., GRPO) operate at the trajectory level, optimizing only after complete episode rollouts. In multi-turn agent settings, rewards are sparse, delayed, and credit assignment across individual steps is critical — a coarse-grained trajectory-level approach fails to address these challenges effectively.

Method: The authors propose 3SPO, which operates at each step by: (1) computing a state score based on historical success rates to supervise step-wise credit assignment; (2) enabling adaptive rollout decisions per step; and (3) performing post-step policy optimization without requiring value function estimation or auxiliary models. They theoretically model this as a per-state bandit abstraction and prove logarithmic allocation regret, along with guarantees for action identification, score distinguishability, and filtering stability.

Result: On ALFWorld and WebShop benchmarks using Qwen2.5-1.5B/7B-Instruct models, 3SPO consistently outperforms GRPO by +22.6% on ALFWorld and +15.6 points on WebShop, while achieving 2.4× more state exploration and 1.8× faster convergence with comparable computational resources.

Conclusion: 3SPO demonstrates that fine-grained, step-level optimization with state-score supervision is both theoretically grounded and empirically superior to trajectory-level RL for multi-turn LLM agent tasks, offering faster convergence and better exploration efficiency.

Abstract: Training large language models (LLMs) as autonomous agents via reinforcement learning (RL) has enabled frontier models to achieve superhuman performance in long-horizon tasks. However, existing RL algorithms operate at the trajectory level, performing policy optimization only after collecting complete episode rollouts. This coarse-grained approach faces fundamental challenges in multi-turn agent settings where rewards are sparse, delayed, and credit assignment across individual steps is critical. In this work, we propose \textbf{State-Score-Supervised Policy Optimization (3SPO)}, a novel RL algorithm that performs post-step policy optimization with dynamic state score supervision. At each step, 3SPO computes the state score based on historical success rates, supervising step-wise credit assignment, adaptive rollout and post-step policy optimization without requiring value function estimation or additional auxiliary models. Theoretically, under a per-state bandit abstraction, we show that the proposed score-supervised allocation mechanism achieves logarithmic allocation regret and provide sample-complexity guarantees for action identification, score distinguishability, and filtering stability. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebShop with Qwen2.5-1.5B/7B-Instruct show that 3SPO consistently outperforms GRPO by $+22.6\%$ on ALFWorld and $+15.6$ points on WebShop, while using comparable resources to achieve $2.4\times$ more state exploration and $1.8\times$ faster convergence. Code is available at https://github.com/genalyu/3SPO.

</details>


### [236] [Optimality of FSQ Tokens for Continuous Diffusion for Categorical Data with Application to Text-to-Speech](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09962)
*Vadim Popov,Wenju Gu,Tasnima Sadekova,Georgii Aparin,Assel Yermekova*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates the latent space structure of discrete tokens in continuous diffusion for categorical data, demonstrating through theory and experiments that Finite Scalar Quantization (FSQ) tokenization yields optimal properties for correct token prediction. FSQ-based text-to-speech diffusion models outperform both other tokenization schemes and a strong LLM-based counterpart, while being smaller and faster.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand what makes a tokenization scheme suitable for continuous diffusion models over categorical data, and to find viable alternatives to autoregressive LLMs for discrete data generation.

Method: The authors analyze the latent space structure of discrete tokens using Kullback-Leibler divergence on diffusion path measures and derive conditions for optimal correct token prediction accuracy. They compare tokenization schemes (including FSQ) via rigorous theoretical analysis and numerical experiments, then validate findings by training several text-to-speech diffusion models with speech tokens as intermediate acoustic features and benchmarking against an LLM-based counterpart.

Result: FSQ tokenization yields a latent space structure best suited for continuous diffusion for categorical data. FSQ-based text-to-speech diffusion models outperform other tokenization schemes and a strong LLM-based counterpart, while being significantly smaller and faster.

Conclusion: FSQ tokenization is optimally suited for continuous diffusion over categorical data due to its favorable latent space geometry, offering a compelling alternative to autoregressive LLMs in applications like text-to-speech synthesis.

Abstract: Continuous diffusion for categorical data is a framework belonging to the diffusion family and aiming at generating discrete data. The scientific interest to such models has been constantly increasing these days because researchers try to achieve a challenging goal of finding reasonable alternatives to autoregressive large language models. In this paper, we study the properties of the structure of the latent space corresponding to discrete tokens expressed in terms of Kullback-Leibler divergence on diffusion path measures and accuracy of the correct token prediction by the optimally trained diffusion model. We find that FSQ tokenization scheme has the latent space structure with the properties that make it best suited for continuous diffusion for categorical data as verified through rigorous theoretical analysis and numerical experiments. To validate our findings in real-life scenario, we train several text-to-speech diffusion models having speech tokens as intermediate acoustic features, and show that the one based on FSQ tokens indeed performs the best, and, moreover, it outperforms its strong LLM-based counterpart, at the same time being significantly smaller and faster.

</details>


### [237] [Interpreting and Steering a Text-to-Speech Language Model with Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10029)
*Nikita Koriagin,Georgii Aparin,Nikita Balagansky,Daniil Gavrilov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sparse autoencoders trained on a TTS language model yield interpretable, causally manipulable features spanning phonemes, paralinguistics, and speaker attributes, enabling fine-grained control over synthesized speech.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Language models underpin modern TTS systems, but the internal representations formed when text and speech tokens share a single residual stream are poorly understood. The authors aim to open the black box of TTS LMs through mechanistic interpretability.

Method: BatchTopK sparse autoencoders are trained on the LM backbone of CosyVoice3. A modality-aware auto-interpretation pipeline is introduced that classifies each SAE feature as firing from text-prefix context, 1-second speech clips, or both (cross-modal). Steering experiments then test the causal nature of discovered features by intervening in the SAE latent space during generation.

Result: Recovered features are interpretable and span phonemes, laughter, accent prompts, and speaker gender. Steering interventions are causal: laughter probability rises from 0.02 to 0.79, perceived speaker gender can be flipped, and speech rate can be controlled while preserving spoken content.

Conclusion: SAE features serve dual roles as interpretability objects revealing what the TTS LM represents internally, and as reliable control directions for controllable TTS synthesis.

Abstract: Language models increasingly serve as the backbone of text-to-speech (TTS) systems, yet we understand little about the representations they build when text and generated speech tokens share a single residual stream. We train BatchTopK sparse autoencoders on the LM backbone of CosyVoice3 and introduce a modality-aware auto-interp pipeline that labels each feature from where it fires-text-prefix context, 1-second speech clips, or both. The recovered features are interpretable, spanning phonemes, laughter, accent prompts and speaker gender. Steering through the SAE latent space shows these features are causal rather than merely descriptive: targeted interventions raise laughter probability from 0.02 to 0.79, flip perceived speaker gender, and control speech rate while preserving spoken content. SAE features thus serve both as interpretability objects and as control directions for TTS synthesis.

</details>


### [238] [Bittensor Agent Arenas as a Trajectory Primitive: Distilling a Shopping Agent from ShoppingBench Subnet Traces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10064)
*Shardul Bansal,Seth Schilbe,Jarrod Barnes*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents a method to generate high-quality, incentive-aligned multi-turn agentic trajectories via a competitive arena (Bittensor's SN15/Subnet 15) instead of relying on biased frontier-model-synthesised data or noisy production logs. Post-training Qwen3-4B with these filtered trajectories yields 42.7% ASR on a held-out agentic-commerce benchmark, approaching the synthetic-data SFT baseline (43.6%), with a Dr. GRPO teacher-grounded reward further narrowing the gap to the SFT+GRPO upper bar (48.7%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic post-training is constrained by the quality of multi-turn trajectory data. Existing sources are problematic: frontier-synthesised data inherits synthesizer biases and collapses the long tail, while unfiltered production logs are unjudged and rife with shortcut behaviour. There is a need for a scalable, incentive-aligned data source providing per-trajectory supervision with diverse, anti-memorised evaluation.

Method: The authors leverage ORO Subnet 15 (SN15), a Bittensor deployment of the ShoppingBench agentic-commerce benchmark. Its race mechanism, LLM reasoning judge, and rotating leak-cluster-guarded problem suite produce trajectories with three desirable properties: incentive-aligned diversity, per-trajectory judging, and anti-memorised held-out evaluation. A structural-quality filter is introduced to separate agentic trajectories (model emits tool calls) from sub-task trajectories (model only classifies/narrates over deterministic search). The filtered corpus is used to post-train Qwen3-4B following the published ShoppingBench SFT-then-GRPO pipeline. A per-step teacher-grounded Dr. GRPO reward is also introduced to address the pass@8 vs. pass@1 gap.

Result: Post-training Qwen3-4B with the arena-derived filtered corpus lifts ASR from the published 18.0% to 42.7% on a leak-cluster-guarded held-out set, within noise of the synthetic-data SFT-only baseline (43.6%), using only a fraction of a single day's subnet output. The supervised stack shows a large pass@8 to pass@1 gap (53.3% vs. 34.8%). The Dr. GRPO teacher-grounded reward converts that headroom into process improvement, with the sub-task firehose identified as the primary lever for closing the gap to the 48.7% SFT+GRPO bar.

Conclusion: Incentive-aligned agent arenas can manufacture high-quality trajectory data at scale, rivaling synthetic-data pipelines while avoiding their biases. The sub-task firehose and teacher-grounded rewards are key for closing the gap to fully synthetic SFT+GRPO performance. The authors release the filter, corpus splits, and arena mechanics to facilitate further research.

Abstract: Small-model agentic post-training is bottlenecked less by the algorithm than by the trajectory substrate it consumes. Leading recipes (RLVR, group-relative RL, rejection-sampled re-SFT) all need multi-turn traces carrying per-trajectory supervision, and the two existing sources fall short: frontier-synthesised data inherits the synthesizer's biases and collapses the long tail, while unfiltered production logs are unjudged and contaminated by shortcut behaviour. We argue that an incentive-aligned agent arena can be engineered to manufacture such trajectories, and demonstrate this on ORO Subnet 15 (SN15), a Bittensor deployment of the ShoppingBench agentic-commerce benchmark. SN15's race mechanism, LLM reasoning judge, and rotating leak-cluster-guarded problem suite yield a corpus with three properties: incentive-aligned diversity, per-trajectory judging, and anti-memorised held-out evaluation. We introduce a structural-quality filter that converts the raw firehose into a trainable corpus by keeping agentic trajectories (the model itself emits the tool calls) and rejecting sub-task trajectories (the model only classifies or narrates over a deterministic search loop), then post-train Qwen3-4B with a recipe matched to the published ShoppingBench SFT-then-GRPO pipeline. On a leak-cluster-guarded held-out partition scored production-strict, the model lifts from the published Qwen3-4B base of 18.0% ASR to 42.7%, within single-problem noise of the synthetic-data SFT-only baseline (43.6%), while training on a fraction of a single day of subnet output. The supervised stack leaves a large pass@8 to pass@1 gap (53.3% vs 34.8%); a per-step teacher-grounded Dr. GRPO reward converts that headroom into process improvement, and we identify the sub-task firehose as the primary lever for closing the gap to the 48.7% SFT+GRPO bar. We release the filter, the corpus splits, and the arena mechanics.

</details>


### [239] [Importance-Aware Scheduling for High-Dimensional Hyperparameter Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10068)
*Ruinan Wang,Ian Nabney,Mohammad Golbabaee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GIF is a hyperparameter optimization strategy that estimates variable importance from a small warm-start sample, allocates trials proportionally to importance-based groups, and retains a full-space fallback, achieving faster convergence and better incumbents than TPE, BOHB, Random Search, and Sequential Grouping in high-dimensional settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional HPO optimizers struggle in high-dimensional spaces because evaluations are costly and search progress is diluted across many low-impact variables. There is a need for a method that concentrates the evaluation budget on the most influential hyperparameters while remaining robust.

Method: GIF uses a three-stage approach: (1) a small-sample warm-start to estimate hyperparameter importance (HIA component), (2) formation of importance-based groups with proportional trial allocation across groups, and (3) retention of a full-space fallback step. The method is plug-compatible with existing optimizers.

Result: On higher-dimensional benchmarks (anisotropic analytic functions, NAS-Bench-301), GIF achieves better incumbents with faster convergence than TPE, BOHB, Random Search, and Sequential Grouping. On Bayesmark (lower effective dimensionality), GIF remains competitive with smaller margins. Ablations confirm all three components (importance estimation, proportional allocation, fallback) contribute to gains. The HIA component recovers intended anisotropy on analytic benchmarks.

Conclusion: GIF provides a simple, plug-compatible method for improving sample efficiency in high-dimensional HPO by prioritizing influential hyperparameters based on importance estimates.

Abstract: Hyperparameter Optimization (HPO) is essential for building high-performing ML/DL models, yet conventional optimizers often struggle in high-dimensional spaces where evaluations are costly and progress is diluted across many low-impact variables. We propose Greedy Importance First (GIF), an importance-aware scheduling strategy that uses a small-sample warm start to estimate hyperparameter importance, forms importance-based groups, allocates trials proportionally, and retains a full-space fallback. We evaluate GIF under fixed evaluation budgets on five anisotropic analytic functions, Bayesmark, and NAS-Bench-301. On the higher-dimensional benchmarks, GIF reaches better incumbents with faster convergence than TPE, BOHB, Random Search, and Sequential Grouping. On Bayesmark, where the effective dimensionality is smaller, GIF remains competitive but the margins are smaller. Ablation studies show that importance estimation, proportional allocation, and the fallback step all contribute to the gains. We also verify that the HIA component recovers the intended anisotropy on the analytic benchmarks. These results suggest that GIF is a simple and plug-compatible way to improve sample efficiency in high-dimensional HPO.

</details>


### [240] [Spatiotemporal Seismic Hazard Assessment Using VQ-VAE and Seismic Statistical Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10069)
*Wei Quan,Denise Gorse*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper extends prior work on earthquake prediction by moving from whole-region to localized predictions (24 km radius) using 60 seismic statistical features (SSFs) with XGBoost, and introduces a novel spatial feature from a VQ-VAE trained on 2D seismic maps. The VQ-VAE-derived feature captures crustal stress buildup, enhances AUC performance, and largely replaces the traditional b-value in feature importance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work showed SSFs outperform generic time-series features for whole-region earthquake prediction (M≥5.0, 15-day horizon). The authors aim to (1) validate whether localized predictions (24 km circle) can maintain similar performance, and (2) explore whether a natively spatial feature derived from 2D seismic maps via deep learning (VQ-VAE) can complement or surpass traditional 1D catalogue-based features, particularly the b-value.

Method: Using a large earthquake catalogue from Japan, the authors (1) adapt XGBoost classification with 60 SSFs computed within a 24 km radius for localized M≥5.0 prediction; (2) train a Vector Quantized Variational Autoencoder (VQ-VAE) on 2D seismic maps, using reconstruction error as a proxy for localized crustal stress buildup; (3) combine SSFs with the VQ-VAE-derived feature and evaluate via AUC; (4) apply SHAP analysis to assess feature importance and substitution effects.

Result: Localized prediction using SSFs alone achieves test AUC values comparable to prior whole-region results. Adding the VQ-VAE-derived spatial feature improves performance further. SHAP analysis shows the VQ-VAE feature is top-ranked and nearly wholly replaces the traditionally computed b-value, indicating it captures similar or superior seismicity-related information.

Conclusion: Localized earthquake prediction with SSFs is effective and performs on par with whole-region approaches. Integrating a VQ-VAE-based spatial feature enhances predictive power and can substitute for the classical b-value, demonstrating the value of deep-learned spatial representations in seismicity forecasting.

Abstract: In this paper we build upon a previous study in which we demonstrated, using XGBoost and earthquake catalogue data from Japan and Chile, that a set of 60 seismic statistical features (SSFs) had much greater predictive value than a set of 428 generic time series features from the tsfresh package. We here extend this previous work in two key ways, focusing on data from Japan as a large dataset is necessary in order to allow for the training of a deep learning (autoencoder) model. First, we move from whole-region prediction (considering, for each candidate event, the likelihood of an event M $\geq$ 5.0 anywhere in the region in the next 15 days) to localised predictions in which both the region of feature computation and the region of prediction are restricted to a circle of radius 24 km around the candidate event, and we show that performance remains excellent, similar to our previous whole-region study for the same area. Second, we here couple this proven set of SSFs, based on one-dimensional (catalogue) data, with a novel feature based on two-dimensional seismic maps, obtained by training a VQ-VAE model to reproduce such maps as output and identifying a measure of its error in doing so with a localised build-up of crustal stress. We show that while localised prediction based on SSFs can be effective alone, with test AUC values as high as those obtained in the case of Japan in our previous whole-region study, the inclusion of the new natively-spatial VQ-VAE-derived feature, top-ranked by SHAP analysis, can enhance performance and additionally appears to near-wholly replace the traditionally-computed $b$-value in terms of feature usage.

</details>


### [241] [Temporal Sheaf Neural Networks with Dynamic Orthogonal Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10071)
*Md Sadek Hossain Asif,Tanzila Khan,Md. Mosaddek Khan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TSNN introduces time-varying orthogonal frames per node for temporal link prediction, transporting node states between local coordinate systems rather than using a shared global embedding space. It achieves state-of-the-art results on multiple temporal graph benchmarks, especially on graphs with strong node-role heterogeneity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing continuous-time graph models operate in a shared global embedding space, which fails to capture node-specific and evolving interaction semantics. Nodes in real-world temporal graphs exhibit distinct, time-varying roles that are poorly served by a single global coordinate system.

Method: TSNN equips each node with a time-varying orthogonal frame parameterized via low-rank Householder products. Node states are compared only after explicit transport between local coordinate systems. The model uses a geometric-residual decoder that anchors predictions on transported distances while learning residual corrections. All computations are strictly causal. The authors also provide theoretical analysis: the symmetric degree-normalized sheaf Laplacian is orthogonally similar to the symmetric normalized graph Laplacian, and the full-active feature-scaled diffusion corresponds to a metric-gradient step on the combinatorial sheaf Dirichlet energy with monotone-descent guarantees. Frame drift perturbs updates only linearly.

Result: TSNN matches or surpasses the strongest prior methods on most benchmarks across TGB v2 link-prediction, temporal-heterogeneous leaderboards, and the DGB benchmark suite. The largest improvements are observed on graphs exhibiting strong node-role heterogeneity. Ablations confirm the distinct benefit of dynamic frames, orthogonal transport, and geometric-residual decoding.

Conclusion: Modeling node-specific, time-evolving local coordinate systems via temporal sheaf structures is a principled and effective approach for temporal link prediction, offering both strong empirical performance and theoretical guarantees.

Abstract: We introduce Temporal Sheaf Neural Networks (TSNN), a temporal link prediction framework that equips each node with a time-varying orthogonal frame and compares node states only after explicit transport between local coordinate systems. In contrast to existing continuous-time graph models that operate in a shared global embedding space, TSNN models node-specific and evolving interaction semantics through dynamic local frames. The model parameterizes per-node frames via efficient low-rank Householder products, preserves stored hidden states exactly under frame updates, and uses a geometric-residual decoder that anchors predictions on transported distances while learning residual corrections. All computations are strictly causal and use only the pre-event history. We show that the symmetric degree-normalized sheaf Laplacian is orthogonally similar to the symmetric normalized graph Laplacian, with the random-walk normalized form similar in the corresponding degree metric; the full-active, feature-scaled diffusion used by TSNN is exactly a metric-gradient step on the combinatorial sheaf Dirichlet energy, with a degree-free monotone-descent and non-expansiveness guarantee. Frame drift perturbs updates only linearly. Across TGB v2 link-prediction and temporal-heterogeneous leaderboards, together with the DGB benchmark suite, TSNN matches or surpasses the strongest prior methods on most benchmarks, with the largest improvements on graphs exhibiting strong node-role heterogeneity. Ablations confirm the distinct benefit of dynamic frames, orthogonal transport, and geometric-residual decoding.

</details>


### [242] [VFUSE: Virulent Feature Understanding with Sparse autoEncoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10080)
*Michael Yu,Matthew L. Olson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VFUSE trains sparse autoencoders (SAEs) on diffusion-transformer activations from protein design models to discover interpretable, hazard-detecting features, achieving AUROC 0.84 for identifying hazardous protein designs without degrading model performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative protein models like RoseTTAFold3 and RFDiffusion3 enable powerful protein design but also risk opaque generation of hazardous proteins. There is a need for mechanistic interpretability methods to audit these models for hazard-related features.

Method: The authors propose VFUSE, which trains sparse autoencoders on the activations of diffusion-transformer blocks within RoseTTAFold3 (folding) and RFDiffusion3 (synthesis). Linear probes are then fit in the SAE latent space to detect hazardous designs, and individual SAE features are analyzed for monosemanticity — firing exclusively on hazardous inputs.

Result: Linear probes in SAE latent space outperform those in the original representation space for hazardous design detection. Individual monosemantic SAE features achieve up to AUROC 0.84 (q < 10⁻¹³) for firing only on hazardous designs.

Conclusion: This is the first SAE trained on an all-atom diffusion model and the first feature-level virulence audit of a protein design model, establishing a pathway toward safer and more interpretable protein design.

Abstract: Generative models have shown remarkable progress in a variety of domains such as protein design, but such power enables the opaque generation of hazardous proteins. In this work, we introduce VFUSE (Virulent Feature Understanding with Sparse autoEncoders), a mechanistic interpretability approach that trains SAEs on diffusion-transformer activations to audit protein models for hazard-aware features. We apply VFUSE to RoseTTAFold3 and RFDiffusion3, popular open-weight models for protein folding and synthesis. We find that for certain blocks, linear probes detect hazardous designs significantly better when fit in the SAE latent space over the original model's representations: improving interpretability without sacrificing model performance. Furthermore, we identify monosemantic features from the SAE that fire only on hazardous designs at up to AUROC $0.84$ ($q < 10^{-13}$). To our knowledge this is the first SAE trained on an all-atom diffusion model and the first feature-level virulence audit of a protein design model, paving the way towards safe and interpretable protein design.

</details>


### [243] [Divide-and-Conquer Modeling for the CTF-4-Science Lorenz Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10084)
*Shundong Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A divide-and-conquer strategy for chaotic-system forecasting is applied to the CTF-4-Science Lorenz benchmark, combining scenario-specific models (smoothing, NG-RC/NVAR, Lorenz transition correction, parametric interpolation) to achieve a public score of 79.63 across five evaluation families.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard monolithic models struggle across heterogeneous chaotic forecasting regimes (clean, noisy, few-shot, parametric). Rather than forcing one model class to handle all scenarios, the authors argue that matching each prediction block to the distinctive evaluation behavior of its task group yields better overall benchmark performance.

Method: A divide-and-conquer pipeline: (1) smoothing-based reconstruction for noisy full-trajectory denoising; (2) NG-RC/NVAR (Next-Generation Reservoir Computing / Nonlinear Vector AutoRegression) models tuned for noisy long-time attractor forecasting; (3) a fitted Lorenz transition correction restricted to the sensitive clean short-time prefix; and (4) a parametric prefix blend for the interpolation task. Each block targets a specific scenario family within the benchmark.

Result: The assembled system achieves a final public score of 79.63 on the CTF-4-Science Lorenz benchmark, which evaluates predictions across twelve hidden scores and five scenario families (clean forecasting, noisy reconstruction, noisy-input forecasting, few-shot learning, parametric generalization).

Conclusion: Bounded, scenario-specific updates can outperform broad model replacement on mixed chaotic forecasting benchmarks. The divide-and-conquer approach demonstrates that tailored, regime-aware modeling decisions are more effective than a single-model solution for heterogeneous dynamical system evaluation suites.

Abstract: This work presents a divide-and-conquer modeling strategy for the CTF-4-Science Lorenz benchmark, which evaluates chaotic-system prediction across twelve hidden scores and five scenario families: clean forecasting, noisy reconstruction, noisy-input forecasting, few-shot learning, and parametric generalization. Rather than forcing one model class to handle all regimes, the final system matched each prediction block to the evaluation behavior of its task group. The main contributions are: smoothing-based reconstruction for noisy full-trajectory denoising; NG-RC/NVAR models tuned for noisy long-time attractor forecasting; a fitted Lorenz transition correction restricted to the sensitive clean short-time prefix; and a parametric prefix blend for the interpolation task. The resulting system with final public score of 79.63 shows that bounded, scenario-specific updates can outperform broad model replacement on mixed chaotic forecasting benchmarks.

</details>


### [244] [Structured Adaptive Tensor Prediction for Streaming Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10085)
*Zhen Qin,Yang Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper develops an adaptive tensor regression framework for streaming matrix-valued time series forecasting, proposing Matrix-on-Matrix (MoM) and Tensor-on-Matrix (ToM) formulations with SGD-based online learning, and establishes recovery guarantees under low-dimensional structures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for matrix-valued time series are designed for static settings and lack adaptability to streaming/time-varying environments. Adaptive filtering techniques have been largely limited to scalar/vector data, leaving adaptive forecasting for matrix-valued time series inadequately understood.

Method: The authors propose an adaptive tensor regression framework with two formulations: Matrix-on-Matrix (MoM), which directly models matrix-valued outputs, and Tensor-on-Matrix (ToM), which stacks multiple responses into higher-order tensors to exploit temporal structure. Stochastic gradient descent (SGD) algorithms are developed for online learning under both formulations.

Result: Stacking multiple responses into higher-order tensors (ToM) yields lower steady-state error and stronger denoising capability than MoM. The tracking behavior of SGD under time-varying dynamics is characterized. Fixed-time recovery guarantees are established for ToM under general low-dimensional structures including sparsity, low-rankness, and joint sparse-low-rank models.

Conclusion: The ToM formulation is superior to MoM for adaptive matrix-valued time series forecasting, and the proposed SGD-based online learning framework is both theoretically grounded (with recovery guarantees) and practically effective for streaming, time-varying settings.

Abstract: Matrix-valued time series arise in a wide range of applications, such as spatio-temporal data from medical imaging and geophysics. Existing methods are mainly designed for static settings and lack adaptability to streaming and time-varying environments. Adaptive filtering techniques have also been largely limited to data with scalar or vector values, leaving adaptive forecasting for matrix-valued time series inadequately understood. To bridge these gaps, we develop an adaptive tensor regression framework that includes Matrix-on-Matrix (MoM) and Tensor-on-Matrix (ToM) formulations for streaming matrix-valued prediction. The two formulations differ in whether to directly model matrix-valued outputs or to exploit temporal structure via higher-order tensor representations. For the proposed tensor regression framework, we develop stochastic gradient descent (SGD) algorithms for online learning. We show that stacking multiple responses across time into higher-order tensors improves performance; in particular, the ToM achieves lower steady-state error and stronger denoising capability than MoM, motivating our focus on the ToM model. We further characterize the tracking behavior of SGD under time-varying dynamics. From a statistical perspective, we establish fixed-time recovery guarantees for ToM under general low-dimensional structures, including sparsity, low-rankness, and their joint sparselow-rank models.

</details>


### [245] [A Theory on Flow Matching with Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10089)
*Yihan He,Qishuo Yin,Yuan Cao,Jianqing Fan,Han Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper provides theoretical foundations for flow matching using over-parameterized 2-layer ReLU neural networks, establishing convergence guarantees for gradient descent, generalization bounds for conditional velocity-field matching, and Wasserstein-distance guarantees for generated samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Flow matching has emerged as a powerful generative modeling paradigm, but its theoretical underpinnings—particularly when using neural-network-parameterized conditional velocity fields—remain underdeveloped. The authors aim to bridge this gap by providing rigorous statistical and optimization guarantees.

Method: The analysis operates in the over-parameterized 2-layer ReLU neural network regime. The authors derive convergence guarantees for gradient descent applied to the flow matching objective, establish generalization bounds for the conditional velocity-field matching objective using a novel multi-task representation learning framework with unbounded losses, and then translate these into Wasserstein-distance guarantees for the generated samples.

Result: The paper establishes: (1) convergence of gradient descent under over-parameterization; (2) generalization bounds for the velocity-field matching objective; (3) Wasserstein-distance bounds between the generated distribution and the target distribution. Empirical validation is provided on synthetic and real-world image benchmarks.

Conclusion: The theoretical results confirm that flow matching with neural-network-parameterized velocity fields enjoys rigorous guarantees, and the generalization bound for multi-task representation learning with unbounded losses is of independent interest beyond generative modeling.

Abstract: In this work, we develop theoretical foundation for flow matching with neural-network-parameterized conditional velocity fields. We establish convergence guarantees for gradient descent in the over-parameterized 2-layered ReLU neural network regime. We derive generalization bounds for the conditional velocity-field matching objective. Building on these results, we provide Wasserstein-distance guarantees for the samples generated by the induced flow. Our analysis is based on generalization bound for multi-task representation learning with unbounded losses, which may be of independent interest beyond flow-based generative modeling. These theoretical results are validated through extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world image benchmarks.

</details>


### [246] [Decision-Making under Combinatorial Risk](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10092)
*Yifan Hong,Hongmiao Fan,Chen Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: People simplify combinatorial risk by focusing on core features like probability increments and initial success probabilities rather than evaluating full outcome distributions, but displaying the induced probability mass function shifts behavior toward lottery-like valuation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world risky decisions often involve multiple interacting risky components (combinatorial risk) where the outcome distribution is induced rather than given. Standard lottery-choice paradigms fail to capture this complexity, and it is unclear how people navigate such environments, especially when full distributional information is costly to evaluate.

Method: The authors introduce an investment-allocation task in which investing in a component raises its success probability and reshapes the outcome distribution. They manipulate whether the induced probability mass function (PMF) is displayed. Symbolic regression is used to discover compact descriptive models of choice behavior, moving beyond expected utility theory and prospect theory. A prospect-theoretic residual model is then fit to behavior under PMF display.

Result: Participants favor options with larger probability increments, and when increments are equal, those with higher initial success probabilities. Displaying the induced PMF reduces responsiveness to combinatorial-risk features and lowers choice variance. Symbolic regression yields models based on after-investment success probability and other combinatorial features, not full distribution evaluation. Under PMF display, behavior is explained by augmenting this model with prospect-theoretic valuation.

Conclusion: People navigate combinatorial risk by attending to core component-level features rather than integrating across the full induced distribution. Making the outcome distribution explicit shifts behavior toward standard lottery valuation, suggesting that representational format fundamentally shapes how combinatorial risk is processed.

Abstract: Decision-making under risk is typically studied through single-shot lottery choices. Yet many real decisions involve combinatorial risk, where risk arises from multiple risky components, so the lottery over outcomes is induced rather than given outright and can be costly to evaluate exactly. We introduce an investment-allocation task to study decision under combinatorial risk, where investing in a component raises its success probability and thereby reshapes the outcome distribution. Participants favor the option with the larger probability increment, and, when increments are equal, the option with the higher initial success probability. Revealing the induced probability mass function (PMF) substantially changes behavior, making participants less responsive to combinatorial-risk features and reducing choice variance. To explain these patterns, we move beyond standard benchmarks and hand-crafted hypotheses with symbolic regression to discover compact descriptive models. The discovered models rely mainly on combinatorial-risk features, such as the after-investment success probability, rather than exact evaluation of the full induced distribution. Behavior under the displayed PMF is then well explained by augmenting this model with a prospect-theoretic residual model. The results show that people navigate combinatorial risk primarily through its core features, shifting toward lottery valuation only when the induced PMF is displayed.

</details>


### [247] [Unsupervised Style Representation Learning for AI-Text Detection via Paraphrase Inversion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10099)
*Rafael Rivera Soto,Barry Chen,Nicholas Andrews*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a self-supervised method to learn discriminative writing style features for LLM-generated text detection without requiring authorship labels, using a reconstruction-based training paradigm that separates style from semantics. The learned representations enable both few-shot and zero-shot detection, outperforming baselines and generalizing to unseen LLMs and tasks like authorship verification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing neural style-based LLM detectors are effective and adversarially robust, but they rely on authorship labels for training and are limited to few-shot inference requiring in-distribution samples. The authors aim to learn style representations without costly authorship labels and enable zero-shot detection that generalizes to unseen LLMs.

Method: The method trains a style encoder to reconstruct human-authored text from its machine-generated paraphrase. A semantic encoder is frozen during training, which biases the style encoder to capture only non-semantic, style-related features needed for reconstruction — achieving style-semantic disentanglement without explicit supervision. Detection is evaluated via two strategies: a few-shot detector and a zero-shot DeepSVDD-based (one-class deep anomaly detection) detector.

Result: The method matches or surpasses all baselines in the few-shot setting. In the zero-shot regime, it is competitive with fully supervised classifiers on in-distribution test data and generalizes significantly better to unseen LLMs. The learned representations also transfer well to authorship verification and fine-grained style discrimination tasks without task-specific training.

Conclusion: Self-supervised learning of style representations via paraphrase reconstruction is a viable and effective alternative to authorship-labeled training. It enables robust LLM-generated text detection — including zero-shot detection — and yields general-purpose style representations useful for multiple downstream tasks beyond detection.

Abstract: The rapid development of large language models (LLMs) has raised concerns about misuse such as plagiarism, misinformation, and automated influence operations, motivating the need for robust detectors. Recent work has shown that neural representations of writing style are effective for detection and, crucially, robust to adversarial attacks that defeat most existing detectors. However, current style-based detectors rely on authorship labels for training, and are limited to few-shot inference for detection, requiring in-distribution samples that may not always be available. We learn discriminative style features without authorship labels by training a style encoder to reconstruct human-authored text from its machine-generated paraphrase; freezing a semantic encoder during training biases the style encoder to capture only the non-semantic features needed for reconstruction. We evaluate the learned representations via two detection strategies: a few-shot detector and a zero-shot DeepSVDD-based detector. Across benchmarks, our method matches or outperforms all baselines in the few-shot setting and, in the zero-shot regime, is competitive with fully supervised classifiers on in-distribution test data while generalizing better to unseen LLMs. Beyond detection, the learned representations generalize to unseen tasks, achieving competitive performance on authorship verification and fine-grained style discrimination despite never being trained on either objective.

</details>


### [248] [Nonlinear Estimator: Dual Bayesian Affine Estimators for Parameter Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10111)
*Sasan Vakili,Daniël Woonings,Pradyumna Paruchuri,Peyman Mohajerin Esfahani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes two coupled affine MMSE estimators for nonlinear Wiener-type state-space models: a dual basis-parameter estimator and a dual state-parameter estimator. The state-parameter variant, using Gaussian DBS mapping, achieves the lowest parameter MSE, outperforming Particle Gibbs and EM-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Parameter estimation in nonlinear Wiener-type state-space models is challenging because optimal estimators are generally intractable. The authors aim to develop a principled fixed-point architecture that retains the affine MMSE structure while incorporating nonlinear basis-function information through Dynamic Basis Statistics (DBS), bridging the gap between purely affine and fully optimal nonlinear estimators.

Method: A fixed-point architecture couples two affine MMSE estimators: one for parameters and one for latent variables. Two DBS construction strategies are introduced: (1) the dual basis-parameter estimator combines an affine basis estimator with an affine parameter estimator; (2) the dual state-parameter estimator computes affine state estimates and covariances, then maps these through a Gaussian DBS operator. Both estimators alternate between updating each component using the other's plug-in estimate statistics from the previous iteration.

Result: Monte Carlo experiments demonstrate the dual basis-parameter estimator achieves parameter MSE comparable to the purely affine parameter estimator. The dual state-parameter estimator yields the lowest parameter MSE, outperforming both the dual basis-parameter and purely affine parameter estimators, as well as sequential Monte Carlo variants of Particle Gibbs and Expectation-Maximization schemes.

Conclusion: The proposed fixed-point coupled affine MMSE architecture provides an effective framework for nonlinear Wiener model parameter estimation. The dual state-parameter estimator, leveraging Gaussian DBS mapping of state estimate statistics, offers superior performance and represents a competitive alternative to particle-based methods.

Abstract: This paper presents a nonlinear parameter estimator for Wiener-type state-space models obtained as a fixed-point architecture that couples two affine minimum mean-squared error (MMSE) estimators: one for the unknown parameters and one for latent variables. The architecture retains the functional structure of the optimal affine MMSE parameter estimator while incorporating Dynamic Basis Statistics (DBS) estimates that summarize nonlinear basis-function evaluations. Two DBS construction strategies are developed, leading to two nonlinear estimator frameworks. The dual basis-parameter estimator combines an affine basis estimator with the affine parameter estimator, whereas the dual state-parameter estimator first computes affine state estimates and their covariances, then maps these state-estimate statistics through a Gaussian DBS operator to obtain DBS estimates. Both dual estimators admit fixed-point characterizations that alternate between estimating each component using the updated prior of the other, obtained from that component's plug-in estimate statistics from the previous iteration. The efficacy of the proposed methods is examined via extensive Monte Carlo experiments, showing that the dual basis-parameter estimator attains parameter mean-squared errors comparable to those of the purely affine parameter estimator, while the dual state-parameter estimator achieves the lowest parameter mean-squared error, outperforming both the dual basis-parameter and purely affine parameter estimators, as well as sequential Monte Carlo variants of classical Particle Gibbs and Expectation-Maximization schemes.

</details>


### [249] [FedSteer: Taming Extreme Gradient Staleness in Federated Learning with Corrective Projections and Caching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10124)
*Haoran Zhang,Cainã Figueiredo Pereira,Marie Siew,Xutong Liu,Carlee Joe-Wong,Rachid El-Azouzi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedSteer addresses the problem of stale gradients in federated learning with irregular client participation by projecting gradients onto an evolving subspace constructed from a cache of recent client gradients, effectively "steering" outdated updates toward the current global objective, and it significantly improves accuracy over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
