Instead of extracting only the final-layer output, this router treats the hidden states from different Transformer depths as a reusable pool of multi-level hidden features, and employs a lightweight task-driven feature composition network to generate layer-wise aggregation weights, then adaptively combine hierarchical representations through weighted summation. This design enables each downstream task to access suitable mixture of low-, mid-, and high-level wireless features without modifying the pretrained backbone. Extensive experiments on four representative wireless tasks demonstrate that RAFC consistently outperforms conventional adaptation baselines while introducing fewer than 50K additional parameters. Moreover, the learned routing weights provide interpretable evidence of task-specific layer preferences, making the proposed framework a low-complexity, scalable, and explainable interface for adapting WFMs to diverse downstream scenarios.

</details>


### [273] [From Observation to Intervention: A Causal Audit of Expert Importance in Mixture-of-Experts Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10703)
*Leonard Engmann,Christian Medeiros Adriano,Holger Giese*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An interventional audit of three MoE architectures finds that commonly used observational metrics (utilization rates, activation norms, routing weights) fail to predict token-level causal expert importance. This exposes a flawed inferential leap from associational to interventional evidence. Early-layer redundancy, not metric efficacy, explains why pruning methods appear to work.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interpretability methods routinely conflate rung-1 (associational) population-level statistics with rung-2 (interventional) causal claims about model internals, without testing whether this inference is valid. The authors identify MoE pruning—where routing statistics guide expert removal—as a concrete, testable instance of this broader methodological problem and subject it to rigorous causal scrutiny.

Method: A token-level interventional audit across three high-redundancy MoE architectures (OLMoE-1B-7B-0924, Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B, DeepSeek-V2-Lite), examining 60 metric-layer combinations. For each token, experts are removed and the resulting functional change is measured. Observational metrics (utilization rates, activation norms, routing weight distributions) are tested as predictors of this causal expert importance, with multiple-comparison correction. A per-token routing weight control analysis rules out insufficient statistical power.

Result: No observational metric significantly predicts causal expert importance after multiple-comparison correction in any model; all effect sizes below Cohen's d = 0.17 across 60 metric-layer combinations. Only one Bonferroni-significant signal was recovered (OLMoE's final MoE layer, d = +0.231, p = 0.0013). Existing pruning methods succeed because early-layer redundancy makes most selection criteria interchangeable, not because metrics identify genuinely dispensable experts.

Conclusion: The paper provides an explicit counterexample to the common inferential step from population-level observational summaries to token-level interventional claims. It calls for interventional audits to calibrate evidential standards in interpretability research and cautions against treating associational evidence as sufficient for causal claims about model internals.

Abstract: Interpretability methods routinely use population-level summary statistics over observed model behaviour to license claims about the effects of targeted interventions on specific computations; in Pearl's terms, they treat rung-1 associational evidence as if it supported rung-2 interventional conclusions, a move whose validity is rarely tested. We examine one concrete instance: the use of routing statistics in Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) pruning, where utilization rates, activation norms, and routing weight distributions are treated as predictors of which experts can be removed without functional cost. A token-level interventional audit across three high-redundancy MoE architectures (OLMoE-1B-7B-0924, Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B, DeepSeek-V2-Lite) finds no observational metric predicts causal expert importance after multiple-comparison correction in any model, with effect sizes below Cohen's $d = 0.17$ across all 60 metric-layer combinations. A per-token routing weight control rules out insufficient power, recovering a single Bonferroni-significant signal at OLMoE's final MoE layer ($d = +0.231$, $p = 0.0013$). Existing pruning methods succeed in this regime not by identifying dispensable experts but because early-layer redundancy renders most selection criteria interchangeable. Our results provide an explicit counterexample to the common inferential step from population-level observational summaries to token-level interventional claims about expert importance, and illustrate how interventional audits can calibrate the evidential standards for interpretability claims.

</details>


### [274] [Revisiting Positive Samples in Graph Contrastive Learning: From the Perspective of Message Passing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10284)
*Lianze Shan,Ningchong Wang,Jitao Zhao,Di Jin,Dongxiao He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Contrastive learning on graphs works surprisingly well even without positive samples. The authors identify that message passing in graph encoders trivializes positive sample learning due to Dirichlet energy dynamics. They propose SPGCL, which propagates only high Dirichlet energy features and uses low-energy features for positive sampling, restoring the efficacy of positive samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors observed that Graph Contrastive Learning (GCL) can achieve competitive performance without positive samples, challenging the widely held assumption that positive samples are essential. This counterintuitive finding motivates a theoretical investigation into why positive samples fail to contribute meaningfully, leading to the discovery that message passing trivializes positive sample maximization.

Method: The paper analyzes the problem through the lens of Dirichlet energy, theoretically demonstrating that message passing in graph encoders causes trivialization of positive sample maximization. The proposed method, SPGCL (Separated Propagation Graph Contrastive Learning), decouples feature propagation: high Dirichlet energy features are propagated through message passing to provide effective learning signals, while low Dirichlet energy features are used to construct a probability matrix for reliable positive sampling without propagation.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of SPGCL, showing that by mitigating the trivialization effect of message passing on positive samples, the method restores the learning efficacy of positive samples and achieves strong performance compared to existing GCL approaches.

Conclusion: Positive samples in GCL are undermined by message passing, which trivializes their maximization. By separating propagation based on Dirichlet energy levels — propagating only high-energy features and using low-energy features for sampling — SPGCL successfully restores the value of positive samples and enhances graph contrastive learning performance.

Abstract: Graph Contrastive Learning (GCL), which trains graph encoders by maximizing similarity between positive samples and minimizing it between negative ones, has emerged as a mainstream graph pre-training paradigm. It is widely recognized that positive samples are essential in GCLs. Ideally, maximizing the similarity of positive samples enables graph encoders to capture intrinsic semantic and patterns of graph data. However, we discover an interesting phenomenon: GCLs can achieve competitive performance even without positive samples. This motivates us to revisit the fundamental mechanism of positive samples in GCLs. From the perspective of Dirichlet energy, we theoretically finds that message passing, a key mechanism in graph encoders, trivializes the maximization of positive samples, preventing GCLs from effectively learning from positive samples. To address this, we propose SPGCL to mitigate the trivialization caused by message passing and restore the learning efficacy of positive samples. Specifically, we find that high Dirichlet energy features help positive samples provide effective learning signals while low Dirichlet energy features contribute little to positive learning signal but is useful for positive sampling. Based on this, SPGCL propagates only high Dirichlet energy features and uses low energy features to construct a probability matrix for reliable positive sampling. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of SPGCL.

</details>


### [275] [Pre-AF 13: An Interpretable Atrial Fibrillation Risk Score Mined from Discharge Reports](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10725)
*Olga Shakhmatova,Dmitrii Kriukov,Daniil Larionov,Nikita Khromov,Iaroslav Bespalov,Alexander Zolotarev,Kirill Grishchenkov,Ekaterina Ivanova,Miron Kuznetsov,Ilya Sochenkov,Elizaveta Panchenko,Artem Shelmanov,Dmitry V. Dylov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Researchers developed interpretable machine learning models (Pre-AF 13 and Pre-AF 9) using routinely collected EHR data to predict medium-term atrial fibrillation risk in cardiovascular disease patients, significantly outperforming established clinical risk scores (CHARGE-AF, C2HEST, MHS, HAVOC).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AF risk scores (CHARGE-AF, C2HEST, etc.) rely on factors nearly ubiquitous in CVD patients (e.g., age, hypertension), yielding poor discrimination in this high-risk population. Additionally, most scores target long-term (5-10 year) rather than medium-term prediction, limiting clinical utility for near-term monitoring and intervention.

Method: Single-center retrospective study using EHR data from 17,562 CVD patients without pre-existing AF. A custom NLP pipeline (rule-based parser + transformer-based NER) extracted 73 structured features from unstructured discharge reports. Three models were built using LightAutoML: a full model (73 features), a simple model (13 features, named Pre-AF 13), and a linear bedside risk score (Pre-AF 9). Performance evaluated via ROC AUC against CHARGE-AF, C2HEST, MHS, and HAVOC, with SHAP for interpretability.

Result: The full model achieved ROC AUC 0.735 (24-month) and 0.696 (entire follow-up); the simple Pre-AF 13 model performed nearly identically (0.725, 0.696). All ML models outperformed the four clinical risk scores (ROC AUC 0.53-0.64). SHAP identified age and left atrial volume as dominant predictors. Pre-AF 9 stratified 24-month AF incidence from ~7% to 36%.

Conclusion: Interpretable ML models derived from routinely collected EHR data can effectively identify high-AF-risk CVD patients over medium-term horizons, substantially outperforming established clinical risk scores and offering actionable risk stratification for clinical practice.

Abstract: Background. Atrial fibrillation (AF) is the most prevalent cardiac arrhythmia and a major determinant of prognosis. Established AF risk scores rely on factors (older age, hypertension) nearly ubiquitous among patients with cardiovascular disease (CVD), offering limited stratification in this high-risk group. Most target long-term (5-10 year) rather than medium-term prediction. We developed interpretable ML models predicting AF risk over a 24-month and entire follow-up horizon in CVD patients using routinely collected hospital data.
  Methods. Single-center retrospective study of electronic health records from the National Research Cardiology Center (Russia) for patients aged >=18 with CVD but without pre-existing AF, hospitalized more than once between January 2012 and May 2019. A custom NLP pipeline transformed unstructured discharge reports into 73 structured features, combining a rule-based parser with transformer-based NER. Using LightAutoML we built a full model (73 features), a simple model (reduced subset), and a linear model for a bedside risk score. Performance was assessed by ROC AUC, compared with CHARGE-AF, C2HEST, MHS, and HAVOC, and interpreted via SHAP.
  Results. Of 80,576 records from 45,000 patients, 17,562 met inclusion criteria; 1,438 (8.19%) developed AF. The full model reached ROC AUC 0.735 (24-month) and 0.696 (entire follow-up); the simple model was nearly identical (0.725, 0.696). All non-linear models outperformed the four clinical risk scores (ROC AUC 0.53-0.64). The simple model uses 13 features and is named Pre-AF 13. SHAP identified age and left atrial volume as dominant predictors. A linear risk score (Pre-AF 9) stratified observed 24-month AF incidence from ~7% to 36%.
  Conclusion. Interpretable ML models built from routinely collected EHR data identify high-AF-risk CVD patients, outperforming established clinical risk scores.

</details>


### [276] [LLM-Guided Neural Architecture Search for Robust Co-Design of Physical Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10294)
*Tyler King,Timothee Leleu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UH-NAS is a hardware-agnostic, LLM-guided neural architecture search framework that co-optimizes accuracy and inference energy across unconventional hardware backends by treating hardware as a swappable backend with energy models, physical constraints, and non-ideality simulators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing NAS methods are tied to specific hardware families, preventing fair cross-platform comparisons. Unconventional hardware (e.g., optical, neuromorphic) introduces unique constraints like energy cost, physical non-idealities, and reduced numerical precision that require architecture-hardware co-design. There is a need for a unified search framework that generalizes across diverse unconventional backends.

Method: UH-NAS employs LLMs as evolutionary operators within a hardware-agnostic NAS framework. Hardware platforms are abstracted as swappable backends, each exposing per-platform energy models, physical constraints, and non-ideality simulators. This allows the same search algorithm to operate across different hardware targets without modification, enabling fair system-level comparisons.

Result: Tested on optical MZI hardware, UH-NAS discovers architectures that are more diverse and robust than conventional baselines, and outperforms existing LLM-to-NAS approaches. Ablation studies demonstrate the importance of architecture-hardware co-design under non-idealities and the influence of system prompts on search outcomes.

Conclusion: Hardware-agnostic NAS with LLM-guided evolutionary search is a promising approach for emerging computing platforms. Architecture-hardware co-design is critical for robustness under physical non-idealities, and the swappable-backend paradigm enables fair cross-platform benchmarking without algorithm modification.

Abstract: Deploying neural networks on unconventional hardware demands architectures that co-optimize task accuracy and platform-specific constraints such as energy cost, physical non-idealities, and numerical precision. Existing neural architecture search (NAS) methods are typically tailored to a single hardware family, limiting cross-platform comparison and generalization. We introduce Unconventional Hardware Neural Architecture Search (UH-NAS), a hardware-agnostic, LLM-guided NAS framework that integrates language models as evolutionary operators to co-optimize accuracy and inference energy. By exposing hardware as a swappable backend with per-platform energy models, physical constraints, and non-ideality simulators, UH-NAS enables fair system-level comparisons across various backends without modifying the search algorithm. Tested on optical MZI hardware, UH-NAS discovers more diverse, robust architectures than conventional baselines while outperforming existing LLM-to-NAS approaches. Additional ablations on architecture robustness under non-idealities and the role of system prompts highlight the importance of architecture-hardware co-design for emerging computing platforms.

</details>


### [277] [N-GRPO: Embedding-Level Neighbor Mixing for Enhanced Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10768)
*Xukun Zhu,Hang Yu,Peng Di,Linchao Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: N-GRPO introduces Semantic Neighbor Mixing, a novel exploration strategy that mixes anchor token embeddings with their semantic neighbors to inject diversity while maintaining semantic consistency, overcoming the trade-off between token-level sampling redundancy and embedding-level noise disruption in GRPO-based math reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current rollout techniques for LLM mathematical reasoning face a fundamental trade-off: token-level sampling produces redundant trajectories differing only in surface form, while embedding-level noise methods disrupt semantic consistency. There is a need for a diversity injection method that operates on the semantic manifold without degrading coherence.

Method: N-GRPO integrates Semantic Neighbor Mixing into the GRPO framework. For each anchor token, the method dynamically constructs input representations by mixing its embedding with those of its k-nearest semantic neighbors in the embedding space. This injects controlled diversity along the local semantic manifold, enabling varied yet semantically valid solution paths during rollout.

Result: Experiments on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen models of various sizes demonstrate that N-GRPO achieves consistent improvements over strong baselines on math reasoning benchmarks and exhibits robust generalization on out-of-distribution tasks.

Conclusion: Semantic Neighbor Mixing provides an effective middle ground between token-level and embedding-level exploration, enabling diverse yet semantically faithful rollouts that improve mathematical reasoning performance and generalization in GRPO-trained LLMs.

Abstract: The success of Large Language Models in mathematical reasoning relies heavily on the generation of diverse and valid solution paths during the rollout phase. However, current rollout techniques face a fundamental trade-off: token-level sampling often yields redundant trajectories that differ only in rephrasing, while embedding-level methods utilizing random noise frequently disrupt semantic consistency. To resolve this, we introduce N-GRPO, a novel exploration strategy integrated into the Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) framework. Rather than relying on token-level sampling or native embedding-level noise, our approach leverages Semantic Neighbor Mixing. This mechanism dynamically constructs input representations by mixing the embeddings of an anchor token and its nearest semantic neighbors, thereby injecting diversity while strictly adhering to the local semantic manifold. Experimental evaluations on the DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen models across different sizes show that N-GRPO not only achieves consistent improvements over strong baselines on math reasoning benchmarks but also exhibits robust generalization capabilities on out-of-distribution tasks.

</details>


### [278] [Baseline-Free Policy Optimization for Neural Combinatorial Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10321)
*Carlos S. Sepúlveda,Gonzalo A. Ruz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GRPO, a baseline-free RL algorithm from LLM alignment, eliminates training instability in neural combinatorial optimization by normalizing advantages within groups of sampled trajectories, avoiding the collapse seen with REINFORCE on harder TSP instances while matching strong baselines like POMO within 2%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: REINFORCE with a rollout baseline — the standard training algorithm for neural combinatorial optimization (NCO) — suffers from a structural vulnerability: on harder instances, a poor baseline yields noisy gradient estimates that destabilize training. The authors seek a baseline-free alternative that avoids this fragility entirely.

Method: The authors evaluate Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) and compare it against four other RL algorithms (including REINFORCE, POMO, and P3O) on TSP and CVRP benchmarks within the RL4CO framework. GRPO eliminates the baseline by normalizing advantages within groups of sampled trajectories from the current policy. P3O, a pairwise preference algorithm also from LLM alignment, is evaluated in parallel.

Result: (i) GRPO avoids the training collapse observed with REINFORCE on TSP-100, where performance degrades from cost 9.8 to 52.1 after warmup and never recovers; (ii) at matched gradient updates, GRPO achieves solution quality within 2% of POMO without an external baseline; (iii) P3O is competitive on TSP but shows higher variability on CVRP.

Conclusion: GRPO is a promising baseline-free alternative for NCO, particularly in settings where baseline-dependent training (i.e., REINFORCE with rollout) becomes fragile or collapses.

Abstract: Neural combinatorial optimization (NCO) trains autoregressive policies to solve routing problems. The standard training algorithm, REINFORCE with a rollout baseline, requires maintaining and periodically updating a frozen copy of the policy for variance reduction. This baseline introduces a structural vulnerability: on harder instances, a poor baseline produces noisy gradient estimates that can destabilize training. We evaluate Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), an algorithm from large language model alignment that eliminates the baseline entirely by normalizing advantages within groups of sampled trajectories. In a controlled comparison of five RL algorithms on TSP and CVRP benchmarks within the RL4CO framework, we find that: (i) GRPO avoids the training collapse observed with REINFORCE on TSP-100, where performance degrades from cost 9.8 to 52.1 immediately after the warmup phase and does not recover under extended training; (ii) at matched gradient updates, GRPO achieves solution quality within 2% of POMO, a strong AM-based multi-start baseline, while requiring no external baseline; and (iii) P3O, a pairwise preference algorithm also from the alignment literature, is competitive on TSP but shows higher variability on CVRP. These results identify GRPO as a promising baseline-free alternative for NCO, particularly in settings where baseline-dependent training becomes fragile.

</details>


### [279] [Rank Collapse, Fixed Points, and the Renormalization Group Structure of MLP Residual Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10324)
*Parviz Haggi-Mani,Irina Rish*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper provides the first quantitative evidence that MLP residual networks implement a selective renormalization group (RG) coarse-graining procedure, demonstrating that effective rank of residual streams decreases monotonically with depth only for short-correlation-length inputs, with inter-layer kernel drift concentrated at discrete transitions resembling RG fixed-point plateaus.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior literature has noted qualitative analogies between deep network forward passes and RG flows (depth as coarse-graining scale, representations flowing toward fixed points), but no existing work has defined a measurable RG order parameter, tested it under controlled input distribution variation, or made quantitative, empirically verified predictions. The authors aim to move from qualitative analogy to quantitative, testable claims.

Method: The authors study a pure MLP residual stack trained on masked token prediction over synthetic Markov chain sequences with known spectral properties (controlled correlation lengths). They measure three quantities: (1) effective rank of the residual stream across depth, (2) selectivity of rank collapse with respect to correlation length (short ~1 vs. long ~7), and (3) inter-layer kernel drift to identify fixed-point plateaus and discrete RG transitions.

Result: Three findings: (i) Effective rank decreases monotonically with depth after training, consistent with integration of irrelevant degrees of freedom. (ii) Rank collapse is selective — it occurs only for short-correlation-length chains (~1), not long (~7), meaning the network preserves task-relevant degrees of freedom per the RG relevance criterion. (iii) Inter-layer kernel drift concentrates at one or two specific transitions, with the rest of the network near a fixed point, consistent with discrete fixed-point plateaus in RG.

Conclusion: MLP residual networks implement a selective coarse-graining procedure governed by the spectral structure of the input distribution, providing the first quantitative, position-level evidence for the deep network–RG correspondence.

Abstract: The analogy between deep neural network forward passes and renormalization group (RG) flows has been repeatedly noted in the literature, but existing treatments remain qualitative: depth is described as a coarse-graining scale, attention is likened to a partition function, and representations are said to flow toward fixed points. No existing work has defined a measurable RG order parameter, tested it under controlled variation of the input distribution, or made quantitative predictions that are empirically verified. We study the simplest architecture for which the analogy is tractable: a pure MLP residual stack trained on masked token prediction over synthetic Markov chain sequences with known spectral properties. We report three findings. (i) The effective rank of the residual stream decreases monotonically with depth after training, consistent with progressive integration of irrelevant degrees of freedom. (ii) This rank collapse is selective: it occurs for chains with short correlation length approximately 1 but is absent for chains with long correlation length approximately 7, measured at the position level to control for mean-pooling artifacts. The network preserves exactly the degrees of freedom relevant to the prediction task, the content of the RG relevance criterion. (iii) Inter-layer kernel drift is concentrated at one or two specific transitions, with the remainder of the network near a fixed point, consistent with a discrete fixed-point plateau. Together these findings constitute the first quantitative, position-level evidence that MLP residual networks implement a selective coarse-graining procedure governed by the spectral structure of the input distribution.

</details>


### [280] [K-Forcing: Joint Next-K-Token Decoding via Push-Forward Language Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10820)
*Zhiwei Tang,Yuanyu He,Yizheng Han,Wangbo Zhao,Jiasheng Tang,Fan Wang,Bohan Zhuang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: K-Forcing introduces a push-forward language modeling paradigm that distills an autoregressive (AR) model into a conditional mapping capable of jointly decoding k future tokens in a single forward pass, achieving 2.4–3.5× inference speedup with modest quality degradation under high-load batch serving.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AR language models suffer from memory-bound, sequential token-by-token decoding that becomes a bottleneck in high-load batch serving at industrial scale. Existing acceleration methods (speculative decoding, diffusion LMs) do not directly address this batched inference regime, motivating a method that reduces the number of sequential forward passes per sequence.

Method: K-Forcing distills a pretrained AR teacher into a push-forward student model. The student maps independent uniform noise variables (concatenated with context) to a joint sample of k future tokens in one forward pass. Training uses progressive self-forcing distillation, which gradually expands the prediction window from 1 to k tokens, enabling the student to closely replicate the teacher's sequence distribution. The method preserves the AR backbone architecture and is compatible with standard AR serving infrastructure.

Result: Evaluated on LM1B and OpenWebText using a causal Transformer backbone with k=4 tokens per forward pass, K-Forcing achieves approximately 2.4–3.5× speedup across various batch sizes compared to standard AR decoding, with modest quality degradation relative to the AR teacher.

Conclusion: As inference dominates the lifetime compute cost of modern LLMs, K-Forcing provides a promising, deployment-compatible approach to accelerating AR generation under real-world high-load serving conditions.

Abstract: Autoregressive (AR) language modeling is the dominant paradigm for text generation, yet its sequential token-by-token decoding makes inference memory-bound and inefficient. Existing acceleration approaches, such as speculative decoding and diffusion language models, can yield speedups under certain conditions but do not directly address high-load batch serving--the scenario most critical for industrial-scale deployment. We introduce K-Forcing, a push-forward language modeling paradigm for joint next-k-token decoding. K-Forcing distills an existing AR model into a conditional push-forward mapping--one that transforms independent uniform noise variables into a joint sample of multiple future tokens in a single forward pass. This design preserves fixed-length outputs, reuses the AR teacher backbone, and remains compatible with standard AR serving infrastructure. We train this mapping via progressive self-forcing distillation, which gradually expands the prediction window while enabling the student to closely match the sequence distribution of the AR teacher. We evaluate K-Forcing on LM1B and OpenWebText using a standard causal Transformer backbone. When aggressively configured to generate k = 4 tokens per forward pass, K-Forcing delivers approximately 2.4-3.5x speedup across different batch sizes, while incurring modest quality degradation relative to its AR teacher. As inference increasingly dominates the lifetime compute cost of modern LLMs, K-Forcing offers a promising route toward accelerating AR generation under real-world high-load deployment.

</details>


### [281] [Beyond Explaining Predictions: Logic-Based Explanations for Confidence in Machine Learning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10347)
*Vinícius Peixoto Chagas,Carlos Henrique Leitão Cavalcante,Thiago Alves Rocha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces confidence-aware abductive explanations that guarantee both class preservation and a user-specified minimum confidence threshold, addressing a gap where traditional abductive explanations may cover low-confidence instances despite high model confidence on the explained instance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional logic-based abductive explanations focus solely on preserving the predicted class label, ignoring the model's confidence. This means an explanation may cover instances where the model is actually uncertain, undermining trustworthiness in high-stakes domains where both prediction correctness and confidence matter.

Method: The authors define the Minimum Confidence Threshold (MCT) as the weakest confidence guarantee an abductive explanation provides, formulated as an optimization problem. They then propose an algorithm to generate minimal confidence-aware abductive explanations that satisfy a user-specified confidence threshold, evaluated on boosted trees for binary classification.

Result: Traditional abductive explanations frequently provide confidence guarantees substantially weaker than the confidence of the explained instance. Confidence-aware explanations consistently improve the minimum confidence guaranteed, requiring only modest increases in explanation length.

Conclusion: Confidence-aware abductive explanations are especially suitable for high-stakes applications requiring both predictive correctness and reliable confidence, offering stronger trustworthiness guarantees with minimal additional complexity.

Abstract: Machine learning is increasingly used in critical domains, where both predictions and their associated confidence levels influence important decisions. To enhance transparency in such scenarios, it is important to understand why a model is confident or uncertain about its predictions. Recent logic-based approaches provide abductive explanations, minimal subsets of features sufficient to preserve the predicted class, with correctness guarantees. However, these methods focus solely on classification behavior and may produce explanations that cover instances with low predictive confidence. In this work, we introduce the concept of Minimum Confidence Threshold (MCT), which quantifies the weakest confidence guarantee provided by an abductive explanation. Building upon this concept, we propose confidence-aware abductive explanations, which preserve not only the predicted class but also a user-specified confidence guarantee. We formulate MCT computation as an optimization problem and introduce an algorithm for generating minimal explanations that satisfy a desired confidence threshold. We evaluate the proposed framework on boosted trees for binary classification, although the approach is applicable to other machine learning models that provide confidence scores. Experimental results show that traditional abductive explanations often provide substantially weaker confidence guarantees than the confidence associated with the explained instance itself. In contrast, confidence-aware explanations consistently improve the minimum confidence guaranteed by an explanation while requiring only a modest increase in explanation length. These properties make the proposed approach particularly suitable for applications where both predictive correctness and confidence are essential for trustworthy decision making.

</details>


### [282] [KG-SoftMAP: Soft Knowledge-Graph Priors for Bayesian Network Structure Learning from Sparse Discrete Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10358)
*Guoliang Xu,James E. Corter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KG-SoftMAP encodes a weighted knowledge graph as a soft, confidence-weighted edge prior in a MAP framework (BDeu score + logit prior) to learn Bayesian network structure from sparse discrete data. It recovers substantial directed structure even at very low data densities, degrades gracefully with KG quality, and on real educational data produces a diagnostic BN with calibrated joint probabilities and KG-consistent edges, though it trails discriminative logistic regression on pure predictive tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learning BN structure from sparse discrete data is fundamentally difficult because most variable pairs lack joint observations for reliable scoring, and data-only methods recover little structure. Yet imperfect domain knowledge (e.g., expert-curated or LLM-extracted weighted directed knowledge graphs) is often available and could guide structure learning if properly incorporated.

Method: KG-SoftMAP: encodes a weighted directed knowledge graph as a soft, confidence-weighted, data-overridable edge prior. The objective maximizes a MAP combination of the BDeu score with a logit-form prior. The KG can be expert-curated or extracted via LLMs. The prior is "soft" in that data can override KG-suggested edges when evidence is sufficient.

Result: On synthetic benchmarks (with ground-truth DAGs): at very low density ρ=0.05, KG-SoftMAP achieves DF1 0.14–0.29 (baselines near zero); at ρ≥0.2, DF1 reaches 0.46–0.96. Recovery degrades gracefully as KG quality drops. On real sparse educational data: the learned BN trails logistic regression by 0.03 F1_FAIL on SAF but provides KG-consistent edges, calibrated joint probabilities, and inference from arbitrary observed concept subsets.

Conclusion: When an informative (even imperfect) KG is available, KG-SoftMAP enables meaningful BN structure recovery from extremely sparse discrete data. The resulting BN is best used as a diagnostic model offering interpretable edges and flexible inference. When no meaningful KG exists, discriminative models like logistic regression are preferable for pure prediction.

Abstract: Learning Bayesian network (BN) structure from sparse discrete data is hard: when each instance records only a few variables, most variable pairs lack the joint observations needed for reliable scoring, and data-only methods recover little structure. Imperfect domain knowledge, expressible as a weighted directed knowledge graph (KG), is often available. We propose KG-SoftMAP, which encodes such a KG as a soft, confidence-weighted, data-overridable edge prior and maximizes a MAP objective combining the BDeu score with a logit-form prior; the KG may be expert-curated or LLM-extracted. On controlled synthetic benchmarks, the only setting with ground-truth DAGs, KG-SoftMAP recovers partial directed structure at $ρ=0.05$ (DF1 $0.14$ to $0.29$, versus near-zero baselines) and substantially more once $ρ\geq0.2$ (DF1 $0.46$ to $0.96$), when paired with an informative but imperfect KG; recovery degrades gracefully as KG quality drops. On real sparse educational data, which has no ground-truth DAG, we evaluate deployment-facing measures only: prediction, calibration, and KG-consistency. The learned BN is best read as a diagnostic model: on SAF it trails logistic regression by $0.03$ F1_FAIL while providing KG-consistent edges, calibrated joint probabilities, and inference from arbitrary observed concept subsets; when no meaningful KG exists, discriminative logistic regression is preferable.

</details>


### [283] [Beyond Absolute Imitation: Anchored Residual Guidance for Privileged On-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10385)
*Wenhao Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AR-OPD introduces a dual-view distillation framework that disentangles privileged supervision into a locally compatible anchor and a controlled residual, resolving the reachability mismatch in existing privileged on-policy distillation methods and significantly reducing hindsight bias in LLM reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Privileged OPD methods treat privileged information (e.g., oracle traces) as a monolithic imitation target, causing a "reachability mismatch" — the student is pushed to match a hindsight-biased distribution outside its local predictive support, incentivizing shortcut-taking over valid intermediate reasoning. The authors aim to disentangle locally reachable steps from future-conditioned oracle signals.

Method: AR-OPD proposes a dual-view framework: it establishes a locally compatible anchor using a partially privileged teacher (which has access only to locally reachable information), then isolates and injects oracle foresight as a controlled residual component. This residual provides destination-directed guidance without forcing strict full-view imitation, decoupling the reachable from the oracle-conditioned distribution.

Result: AR-OPD outperforms full privileged OPD by 2.3 points and SFT by 7.9 points on diverse reasoning tasks. It reduces hindsight leakage by 21.7%, mitigates late-stage drift, and yields up to a 7.2-point advantage on long-horizon trajectories exceeding 768 tokens.

Conclusion: Disentangling privileged supervision into an anchor and residual effectively resolves the reachability mismatch, enabling more robust reasoning distillation without inducing shortcut behaviors, especially on long-horizon tasks.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) has demonstrated strong empirical gains in enhancing complex reasoning in LLMs by aligning a student model with a teacher's predictive distribution over the student's own trajectories. An emerging variant, Privileged OPD, further strengthens this paradigm by employing a self-teacher model augmented with privileged information, such as oracle traces, to mitigate teacher-student capacity gaps while providing dense, answer-directed supervision. However, current methods treat privileged information as a monolithic imitation target, failing to disentangle locally reachable reasoning steps from future-conditioned oracle signals. Consequently, the student is encouraged to match a hindsight-biased distribution that often falls outside its local predictive support. This reachability mismatch incentivizes the student model to skip valid intermediate reasoning in favor of locally unsupported shortcuts. To resolve this, we introduce Anchored Residual On-Policy Distillation (AR-OPD), a dual-view framework that disentangles privileged supervision. Rather than enforcing strict full-view imitation, AR-OPD establishes a locally compatible anchor using a partially privileged teacher, isolating and injecting oracle foresight as a controlled residual to provide destination-directed guidance. Across diverse reasoning tasks, AR-OPD outperforms full privileged OPD by 2.3 points and SFT by 7.9 points. Crucially, this anchored residual mechanism reduces hindsight leakage by 21.7% and mitigates late-stage drift, yielding up to a 7.2-point advantage on challenging long-horizon trajectories exceeding 768 tokens.

</details>


### [284] [AuRA: Internalizing Audio Understanding into LLMs as LoRA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11033)
*Bo Cheng,Lei Shi,Zhanyu Ma,Yuan Wu,Jun Xu,Jiuchong Gao,Jinghua Hao,Renqing He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AuRA distills audio encoding into an LLM via layer-wise distillation from an ASR encoder to a LoRA-adapted LLM, enabling efficient parallel speech-language inference without costly multimodal training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches—cascaded ASR-LLM pipelines, end-to-end speech-language models, and bridge/distillation methods—suffer from transcript-interface latency, expensive multimodal training, or sequential speech-language coupling. A method is needed that achieves tight speech-language joint modeling with lightweight adaptation and parallel inference.

Method: AuRA uses a teacher-student distillation framework: the same speech input is fed to an ASR encoder (teacher) and a LoRA-adapted LLM (student) via a lightweight audio embedding layer. Layer-wise distillation aligns the student LLM's hidden states with the teacher ASR encoder's representations, internalizing speech representations into the LLM through low-rank adaptations without full multimodal training.

Result: AuRA consistently outperforms cascaded systems, speech-to-LLM adaptation baselines, and large-scale speech-language and multimodal models across multiple speech-language benchmarks, in both effectiveness and efficiency.

Conclusion: AuRA demonstrates that distilling audio encoding capability directly into an LLM offers a compelling alternative: tighter speech-language joint modeling with parallel end-to-end inference, while reusing pretrained components and avoiding the costs of large-scale multimodal training.

Abstract: Recent efforts to extend large language models (LLMs) to speech inputs typically rely on cascaded ASR-LLM pipelines, end-to-end speech-language models, or bridge/distillation-based adaptation. While these routes respectively reuse strong pretrained components, enable native speech-language interaction, or offer lightweight adaptation, they often suffer from transcript-interface latency, costly multimodal training, or sequential speech-language coupling. To address these limitations, we present AuRA, a method that distills audio encoding capability into the LLM. Specifically, AuRA feeds the same speech input to an ASR encoder (as a teacher) and a LoRA-adapted LLM (as a student) through a lightweight audio embedding layer, and uses layer-wise distillation to align the student's hidden states with corresponding teacher representations, thereby internalizing speech representations into lightweight LLM-side adaptations. Compared with cascaded and serial bridge methods, AuRA enables tighter speech-language joint modeling and efficient parallel end-to-end inference, while also reusing pretrained speech and language models rather than requiring large-scale multimodal training. On multiple speech-language benchmarks, AuRA consistently outperforms cascaded systems, speech-to-LLM adaptation baselines, and large-scale speech-language and multimodal models in both effectiveness and efficiency.

</details>


### [285] [Validation-Stage Combinatorial Fusion Analysis for Imbalanced Credit-Card Fraud Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10393)
*Xiao Han,Chenyu Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CFA (Combinatorial Fusion Analysis) improves credit-card fraud detection by selecting a small, complementary subset of models (Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM) with diversity-weighted score fusion, achieving AUC-ROC 0.9405 and outperforming soft voting and stacking on AUPRC/F1, while synthetic data augmentation via CTGAN degrades performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fraud detection is challenging due to rarity, high cost, and skewed distribution of fraudulent transactions. While gradient-boosted trees already perform well on structured data, the paper investigates whether CFA—a method that searches over model subsets and rank-score fusion rules—can provide additional gains beyond strong single models on the IEEE-CIS benchmark, where the value of another fusion method is not obvious.

Method: Using a leakage-free 60/20/20 train/validation/test split, the authors evaluate 480 fusion configurations built from seven base classifiers (including XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost, Random Forest, etc.). CFA searches over model subsets and fusion rules including rank fusion, score fusion, weighted variants, and diversity-weighted score fusion (DEF WtScore). Results are validated via bootstrap confidence intervals (1,000 resamples). A CTGAN-based synthetic fraud augmentation experiment is also conducted to test robustness.

Result: The best configuration is diversity-weighted score fusion of Random Forest, XGBoost, and LightGBM: AUC-ROC = 0.9405, AUPRC = 0.6699, F1 = 0.6373. Bootstrap CIs confirm gains over the strongest single model exclude zero on all three metrics. CFA matches soft voting on AUC-ROC, improves AUPRC and F1, and outperforms stacking. CTGAN-generated synthetic fraud samples degrade both individual and fused model performance.

Conclusion: CFA's primary value lies not in blindly combining all classifiers, but as a validation-stage method for selecting a small, complementary model subset and assigning diversity-aware fusion weights. In imbalanced fraud detection settings, this targeted fusion yields statistically significant improvements over single models and common ensemble strategies.

Abstract: Credit-card fraud detection is difficult because fraudulent transactions are rare, costly, and unevenly distributed. Strong gradient-boosted tree models already perform well on structured transaction data, so the value of another fusion method is not obvious. This paper examines whether Combinatorial Fusion Analysis (CFA), which searches over model subsets and rank-score fusion rules, can still add value on the IEEE-CIS Fraud Detection benchmark. Using a leakage-free 60/20/20 train/validation/test protocol, we evaluate 480 fusion configurations built from seven base classifiers. The best test-set result comes from diversity-weighted score fusion of Random Forest, XGBoost, and LightGBM (DEF WtScore), with AUC-ROC = 0.9405, AUPRC = 0.6699, and F1 = 0.6373. Bootstrap confidence intervals from 1,000 resamples show that the gains over the strongest single model exclude zero for all three metrics. CFA matches soft voting on AUC-ROC, improves AUPRC and F1, and outperforms stacking in this setting. A CTGAN augmentation experiment gives a negative result: synthetic fraud samples degrade both individual models and CFA. Overall, CFA is most useful here not as a way to combine every classifier, but as a validation-stage method for choosing a small, complementary subset and assigning diversity-aware weights.

</details>


### [286] [FOGO: Forgetting-aware Orthogonalization Optimizer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10406)
*Toan Nguyen,Yang Liu,Trung Le,Celso de Melo,Flora D. Salim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FOGO is an optimizer that treats forgetting as a general optimization problem (not just continual learning). It uses spectral orthogonalization of momentum and a random-projection codebook to resolve gradient interference at each step, improving convergence and knowledge retention across diverse tasks including LLM pretraining and continual fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors argue that forgetting is not unique to continual learning — it occurs during standard training when dominant mini-batch gradients suppress rare but useful update directions (short-term forgetting), which compounds into long-term forgetting when knowledge is never revisited. Existing optimizers lack mechanisms to detect and resolve this gradient interference.

Method: FOGO operates via two main mechanisms: (1) Spectral orthogonalization of momentum updates to prevent dominant directions from monopolizing optimization; (2) A compact codebook memory built on random projection that stores representative past gradient directions with provably preserved pairwise distances in low-dimensional space. At each step, conflicts between the current update and stored directions are resolved via lightweight orthogonal correction and a proximal step, with no data storage overhead.

Result: FOGO consistently improves convergence and knowledge retention, outperforming Adam and Muon across class-imbalanced classification, continual visual learning (domain and class shifts), continual fine-tuning of LLaVA-7B, and GPT-2 pretraining.

Conclusion: Forgetting is a pervasive optimization phenomenon, not just a continual learning problem. FOGO provides a scalable, general-purpose solution that resolves gradient interference on-the-fly, yielding better retention and convergence across a broad range of training regimes.

Abstract: We argue that forgetting is not confined to continual learning but is a general optimization phenomenon: during standard training, dominant mini-batch gradients suppress rare but useful update directions, causing short-term forgetting at every step. When such knowledge is never revisited, these losses compound into long-term forgetting-the classical failure mode of continual learning. We introduce FOGO, a scalable optimizer that continuously detects and resolves gradient interference across both regimes. FOGO spectrally orthogonalizes momentum updates to prevent dominant directions from monopolizing optimization, then stores representative past directions in a compact codebook memory built on random projection, where pairwise distances are provably preserved in low-dimensional space. At each step, conflicts between the current update and stored directions are resolved via lightweight orthogonal correction and lifted back through a proximal step, with minimal overhead and no data storage. Across class-imbalanced classification, continual visual learning under domain and class shifts, continual fine-tuning of LLaVA-7B, and GPT-2 pretraining, FOGO consistently improves convergence and knowledge retention, outperforming Adam and Muon.

</details>


### [287] [TRACE: A Unified Rollout Budget Allocation Framework for Efficient Agentic Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11119)
*Heming Zou,Qi Wang,Yun Qu,Yuhang Jiang,Lizhou Cai,Yixiu Mao,Ru Peng,Xin Xu,Weijie Liu,Kai Yang,Saiyong Yang,Xiangyang Ji*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TRACE proposes a tree-structured rollout allocation framework for multi-turn RLVR that allocates sampling budget to both prompt roots and intermediate prefixes predicted to yield mixed terminal rewards, enhancing reward contrast and policy-update signals. It achieves +2.8 points on Qwen3-14B Multi-Hop QA over baselines at equal cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RLVR suffers from insufficient reward contrast due to (1) low-variance feedback from overly simple/complex prompts, and (2) outcome-only rewards assigning identical terminal scores to all decisions in a multi-turn rollout. Existing methods only allocate budget at the prompt level, ignoring prefix-level informativeness variation across turns within a rollout.

Method: TRACE models each ReAct-style thought-action-observation turn as a semantically distinct node, enabling budget allocation from prompt roots to turn-level prefixes with further continuations, forming tree-structured rollouts. A shared generalizable predictor estimates conditional success probability at these anchor nodes from prefix histories to guide allocation toward prefixes most likely to yield mixed terminal rewards.

Result: TRACE achieves competitive performance and efficiency gains on typical agentic benchmarks, improving Qwen3-14B Multi-Hop QA average accuracy by 2.8 points over competitive baselines at equal sampling cost.

Conclusion: TRACE demonstrates that tree-structured, prefix-level rollout allocation effectively amplifies reward contrast in multi-turn agentic RLVR, providing stronger policy-update signals without increasing the sampling budget.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) is a promising approach for enhancing reasoning and agentic behavior in large language models. However, rollout-intensive policy optimization is often limited by insufficient reward contrast, arising when overly simple or complex prompts generate low-variance feedback and when outcome-only rewards assign the same terminal assessment to every decision in a multi-turn rollout. Past efforts have focused on allocating available rollout resources to promising prompts, yet they only leverage sample informativeness at the prompt level and neglect variation in prefix-level informativeness across turns within the same rollout. This work targets multi-turn agentic RL by modeling each ReAct-style thought-action-observation turn as a semantically distinct node, allowing budget allocation to extend from prompt roots to turn-level prefixes with further continuations, which naturally forms tree-structured rollouts. We introduce Tree Rollout Allocation for Contrastive Exploration (TRACE), a unified rollout allocation framework that enhances reward contrast within a fixed sampling budget. Technically, TRACE allocates rollout budget to both prompt roots and intermediate prefixes that are most likely to yield mixed terminal rewards. A shared generalizable predictor estimates conditional success probability at these anchors from prefix histories to guide this allocation. The resulting adaptive tree structure enriches outcome-only feedback and amplifies the policy-update signal. Empirically, TRACE achieves competitive performance and efficiency gains on typical agentic benchmarks, e.g., improving Qwen3-14B Multi-Hop QA average accuracy by 2.8 points over competitive baselines at equal sampling cost.

</details>


### [288] [A Comprehensive Inference-Time Augmentation Framework in Physiological Signals: Application to PPG-Based AF Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10410)
*Davood Fattahi,Runze Yan,Saurabh Kataria,Zhaoliang Chen,Xiao Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified inference-time augmentation (ITA) framework with 13 optimized augmentation methods is proposed for robust PPG-based atrial fibrillation detection, demonstrating consistent AUROC improvements (up to 8.5%) across diverse real-world datasets without requiring model retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Physiological signal classification in real-world deployments suffers from sensor noise, motion artifacts, and distribution shifts. While ITA offers a simple, model-agnostic solution, its use in physiological signals has been limited to narrow augmentation methods with fixed, unoptimized parameters. A unified, systematically optimized ITA framework is needed.

Method: A unified ITA framework integrating 13 augmentation methods across time-domain, amplitude-domain, frequency-domain, and artifact-injection transformations. Hyperparameters are optimized via Bayesian optimization. Evaluation uses two models (GPT-PPG and ResNet) on AF detection from 30-second PPG signals across five datasets (>400 patients, ~9,800 hours). Both standard ITA and selective ITA are assessed.

Result: Standard ITA improved AUROC by up to 8.5% (GPT-PPG) and 0.7% (ResNet), and AUPRC by up to 10.6% (GPT-PPG) and 0.8% (ResNet). Selective ITA reduced average false positive rate by up to 4.4% (GPT-PPG) and 1.3% (ResNet) on non-AF datasets.

Conclusion: ITA is a practical, model-agnostic strategy for enhancing the reliability of PPG-based AF classification in real-world deployment where retraining is infeasible. The framework has broader applicability to physiological signal analysis beyond AF detection.

Abstract: Objective: Accurate classification of physiological signals in real-world deployments is challenged by sensor noise, motion artifacts, and distribution shifts between training and deployment data. Inference-time augmentation (ITA), which applies augmentations during inference rather than retraining, offers a simple, model-agnostic mechanism to improve robustness. However, ITA application to physiological signals has remained narrow in scope, relying on limited augmentation methods with fixed, unoptimized parameters. This work proposes a unified ITA framework to address that gap.
  Approach: The framework incorporates 13 augmentation methods spanning time-domain, amplitude-domain, frequency-domain, and artifact-injection transformations, with hyperparameters optimized via Bayesian optimization. We evaluate on atrial fibrillation (AF) detection from 30-second PPG signals using GPT-PPG and ResNet across five datasets comprising more than 400 patients and ${\sim}$9,800 hours of recording.
  Main results: Standard ITA consistently improved AUROC (up to 8.5% for GPT-PPG and 0.7% for ResNet) and AUPRC (up to 10.6% for GPT-PPG and 0.8% for ResNet). Selective ITA further reduced average FPR by up to 4.4% (GPT-PPG) and 1.3% (ResNet) on non-AF datasets.
  Significance: These findings establish ITA as a practical, model-agnostic approach for improving PPG-based AF classification reliability in deployment settings where retraining is not feasible, with broader applicability to physiological signal analysis.

</details>


### [289] [A Unifying Lens on Supervised Fine-Tuning Through Target Distribution Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11189)
*Tong Xie,Yuanhao Ban,Yunqi Hong,Sohyun An,Yihang Chen,Cho-Jui Hsieh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper reinterprets supervised fine-tuning (SFT) as a token-level target distribution design problem, introducing the Q-target framework that decomposes SFT supervision into reliance on observed tokens and allocation of remaining probability mass. This unifies existing SFT variants and leads to Target-SFT, a method that directly constructs training objectives from desired target distributions, outperforming baselines across ten reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard SFT maximizes the likelihood of every observed token via a one-hot target, but real tokens can be noisy, non-unique, or misaligned with the model's pretrained knowledge prior. Strictly fitting such one-hot targets is suboptimal when the pretrained model already encodes rich knowledge. The authors argue for a more principled approach to designing token-level supervision targets.

Method: The Q-target framework decomposes SFT supervision into two design choices: (1) the strength of reliance on the observed token, and (2) how remaining probability mass is allocated among alternative tokens. This framework unifies existing SFT variants (e.g., label smoothing, distillation) as implicit Q-target choices. Target-SFT is then proposed, which directly specifies the desired target distribution Q and constructs the training objective accordingly, rather than implicitly deriving Q from a loss function.

Result: Target-SFT consistently outperforms baseline SFT methods across ten reasoning dataset-model combinations, demonstrating the effectiveness of explicitly designing the target distribution rather than relying on implicit one-hot or heuristically-smoothed targets.

Conclusion: The target-based formulation reveals a more fundamental design principle for SFT training. It opens a broader search space for designing SFT objectives beyond traditional loss-function engineering, with Target-SFT serving as a practical instantiation that validates the framework's utility.

Abstract: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) typically maximizes the likelihood of every token in a demonstrated trajectory. However, an observed token can be non-unique, noisy, or misaligned with the model prior. Strictly fitting toward this one-hot target may be suboptimal, especially when the pretrained model encodes a rich knowledge prior. In this work, we reinterpret SFT as target distribution design: instead of studying only the loss objective, we analyze the token-level target that the loss drives the model to match. We introduce the Q-target framework, which decomposes SFT supervision into two explicit choices: (1) how strongly to rely on the observed token, and (2) how to allocate the remaining probability mass over alternatives. This perspective unifies many existing SFT variants as implicit choices of the target distribution Q. Building on this view, we propose Target-SFT which constructs the training objective directly from the desired target distribution. This method consistently outperforms across the ten reasoning dataset-model settings evaluated, showing the effectiveness of this target-based approach. Overall, our formulation reveals a more fundamental design principle for SFT training and opens a broader search space for SFT objectives.

</details>


### [290] [Mitigating Bias in Low-SNR Financial Reinforcement Learning via Quantum Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10448)
*Zeyu Liu,Xuanzhi Feng,Sing Kwong Lai,Yuanchen Gao,Xiaoyi Pang,Hualei Zhang,Jingcai Guo,Jie Zhang,Song Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes FPQC-SAC, a hybrid quantum-classical variant of Soft Actor-Critic that uses a Parameterized Quantum Circuit as a bounded feature encoder to mitigate the "Financial Entropy Trap" — a failure mode in low-SNR financial markets where noisy Q-value estimates and bootstrapping errors destabilize maximum-entropy RL. It achieves 66.89% relative cumulative return gain over standard SAC and outperforms continuous-control baselines by ~27% on portfolio management tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Financial markets exhibit low signal-to-noise ratios, which cause off-policy maximum-entropy methods like SAC to suffer from unreliable Q-value estimates. The authors identify the "Financial Entropy Trap": noisy state representations lead to erroneous Q-values, and bootstrapping recursively amplifies these errors, destabilizing training. Existing solutions either filter raw inputs or regularize Q-values post-hoc, both of which have limitations.

Method: FPQC-SAC inserts a compact, bounded Parameterized Quantum Circuit (PQC) before the actor and critic networks. Unlike input filtering or post-bootstrapping regularization, the PQC operates at the representation level, constraining feature propagation through its inherently bounded transformation (due to unitary quantum gates). Trainable quantum entanglement within the PQC preserves flexible cross-asset interactions. The approach is plug-and-play and requires no architectural changes to the underlying SAC algorithm beyond inserting the PQC encoder.

Result: On real-world portfolio management tasks, FPQC-SAC achieves a 66.89% relative gain in cumulative return over standard unconstrained SAC and outperforms the best continuous-control deep RL baseline by approximately 27% in cumulative return, demonstrating substantially improved out-of-sample stability.

Conclusion: Constraining representation propagation via a bounded PQC is an effective, plug-and-play strategy to stabilize maximum-entropy RL in low-SNR financial environments. FPQC-SAC offers a principled middle ground between raw-input denoising and post-hoc Q-value regularization, with strong empirical performance and open-source reproducibility.

Abstract: The financial market is a typical low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) setting, which often destabilizes off-policy maximum-entropy methods like Soft Actor-Critic (SAC). Specifically, noisy state representations may produce unreliable Q-value estimates, and bootstrapping amplifies these errors, forming a failure mode we call the "Financial Entropy Trap". In this paper, we propose FPQC-SAC, an efficient and plug-and-play SAC variant that places a compact and bounded Parameterized Quantum Circuit (PQC) before the actor and critic networks to constrain feature propagation at the representation level, rather than filtering raw inputs or regularizing Q-values after bootstrapping. Notably, FPQC-SAC reduces the impact of extreme market fluctuations on Bellman target estimation, while trainable quantum entanglement preserves flexible cross-asset interactions. Empirical evaluations on real-world portfolio management tasks demonstrate that FPQC-SAC substantially enhances out-of-sample stability and cumulative returns by achieving a 66.89% relative gain in cumulative return over standard unconstrained SAC and outperforms the best continuous-control deep reinforcement learning baseline by approximately 27%. Open-source code is available at https://github.com/ZeyuLIU-UST/FPQC-SAC-main.

</details>


### [291] [UPLOTS: A Unified Pretrained Language Model for Constrained Time-series Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10466)
*Du Yin,Hao Xue,Jinliang Deng,Yang Yang,Shuang Ao,Arian Prabowo,Flora Salim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UPLOTS proposes a unified transformer-based framework with learned constraint prompts for generating time-series data across multiple domains, replacing the need for separate per-dataset models and enabling precise pattern control at inference time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current time-series generation methods require handcrafting or training separate models for each dataset, which limits scalability and fails to exploit shared temporal structures across domains. The authors aim to unify multi-domain time-series generation under a single framework.

Method: UPLOTS uses a single pre-trained transformer backbone guided by learned constraint prompts. Key techniques include: (1) dynamic multi-dataset loss re-weighting to balance training across diverse datasets, and (2) a prompt-to-pattern mapping mechanism that internalizes distinct temporal structures during training and enables conditional generation of specific patterns (e.g., peak-period, calendar, load-level, volatility) at inference.

Result: Evaluated on four real-world benchmarks across multiple constraint settings, UPLOTS demonstrates effective pattern-controlled generation. Held-out constraint-combination experiments show generalization beyond the original training patterns, and downstream forecasting tasks confirm improved data augmentation under scarce real-data regimes.

Conclusion: UPLOTS offers a scalable, unified solution for constrained time-series generation, eliminating the need for per-dataset models while achieving strong pattern control and cross-domain generalization, with practical benefits for data-scarce forecasting scenarios.

Abstract: In time-series generation, existing approaches typically handcraft ortrain a separate model for each dataset, which hinders their scalability and fails to leverage shared temporal structures across domains. To address this fragmentation, we propose UPLOTS, a Unified, Prompt-guided Language model framework fOr constrained Time-Series Generation across diverse domains. Instead of building task-specific models, UPLOTS leverages a single pre-trained transformer backbone guided by learned constraint prompts, enabling on-demand generation with precise pattern control. One key innovation is our dynamic multi-dataset loss re-weighting and prompt-to-pattern mapping, which allows UPLOTS to internalize diverse temporal structures during training and conditionally generate them at inference. We evaluate UPLOTS on four real-world benchmarks and multiple constraint settings, including peak-period, calendar, load-level, and volatility patterns. Additional held-out constraint-combination and downstream forecasting experiments further demonstrate that UPLOTS generalizes beyond the original peak-pattern setting and improves data augmentation under scarce real-data regimes. Our code and baselines are available at anonymous github repo: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/UPLOTS-6C36.

</details>


### [292] [Stop Early, Spend Less: Hidden-State Probes as a Practical Recipe for Streaming Moderation of LLM Outputs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10487)
*Huizhen Shu,Xuying Li,Piao Xue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Lightweight token-level probes on LLM hidden states enable real-time safety filtering within the decoding loop at negligible compute cost, replacing post-hoc moderation with continuous streaming intervention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deploying LLMs in user-facing systems requires output safety filtering, but existing moderation models add a separate forward pass after generation, doubling inference cost and only detecting violations post-hoc. The authors identify that moderation-relevant signals already exist in the generator's internal hidden states, motivating a more efficient approach.

Method: Train lightweight linear probes on internal LLM activations that produce per-token safety scores. These probes operate inside the decoding loop, reusing existing activations with no additional forward pass. A single mid-layer probe aggregates scores for offline evaluation or triggers online intervention (halting/modifying unsafe outputs mid-generation). The method includes practical guidance on layer selection, aggregation strategy, probing frequency, and triggering thresholds. The linear component is also shown to correspond to a direction in residual space, enabling activation steering.

Result: The probe applied to a single mid-layer recovers most decisions of a strong guard model. In streaming settings, it halts or modifies unsafe outputs before full generation. Compared to post-hoc and streaming guard models, it achieves orders-of-magnitude lower compute overhead with minimal latency cost (sub-millisecond per-token checks).

Conclusion: Token-level probing on internal activations provides a highly efficient, low-latency alternative to traditional safety moderation, enabling continuous monitoring within the decoding loop. The approach is practical for deployment and can also be leveraged for activation steering at negligible additional cost.

Abstract: Deploying large language models in user-facing systems requires efficient output safety filtering. Existing approaches typically rely on a separate moderation model applied after generation, which doubles inference cost and only detects violations after generation completes. We observe that the signal needed for moderation is already present in the model hidden states. Based on this, we train lightweight token-level probes that operate directly on internal activations, producing per-token safety scores that can be aggregated for both offline evaluation and online intervention. The probe reuses activations from the generator and requires no additional forward pass, enabling sub millisecond per-token safety checks inside the decoding loop. A probe applied to a single mid layer recovers most decisions of a strong guard model, acting as a low cost surrogate optimized for latency rather than accuracy. In streaming settings, it can halt or modify unsafe outputs before they are fully generated, replacing end of sequence moderation with continuous token level monitoring. Compared to post hoc and streaming guard models, our method achieves orders of magnitude lower compute overhead with minimal latency cost. We also provide a practical deployment recipe, including layer selection, aggregation strategy, probing frequency, and triggering thresholds. Finally, we show that the probe linear component corresponds to a direction in residual space, enabling both detection and activation steering at negligible cost.

</details>


### [293] [MoE Enhanced Federated Learning for Spatiotemporal Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10499)
*Zhehao Dai,Xiao Han,Zhaolin Deng,Zijian Zhang,Xiangyu Zhao,Guojiang Shen,Xiangjie Kong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MoE-FedTP is a privacy-preserving federated learning framework that uses lightweight Mixture-of-Experts networks to transfer traffic prediction knowledge from data-rich to data-scarce cities, dynamically fusing expert models via a gating mechanism to handle spatiotemporal heterogeneity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data-scarce cities lack sufficient traffic sensors, and while cross-city knowledge transfer can help, centralized methods compromise privacy and existing federated approaches fail to adequately address the pronounced spatiotemporal heterogeneity across different cities.

Method: MoE-FedTP employs spatiotemporal neural networks for feature extraction from both source and target cities, introduces multiple expert networks derived from different source cities via partial parameter sharing, and uses a gating mechanism to dynamically fuse experts for fine-grained heterogeneity modeling — all within a personalized federated learning framework.

Result: Experiments on four real-world traffic datasets demonstrate that MoE-FedTP consistently outperforms state-of-the-art cross-city and federated learning baselines, significantly improving prediction accuracy for data-scarce cities.

Conclusion: MoE-FedTP effectively addresses the dual challenges of privacy preservation and spatiotemporal heterogeneity in cross-city traffic prediction, offering a practical solution for improving forecasting in sensor-sparse urban environments.

Abstract: Traffic prediction is fundamental to intelligent transportation systems and urban computing, yet many cities continue to suffer from traffic data scarcity due to limited sensor deployment and uneven urban development. Cross-city knowledge transfer has thus attracted increasing attention, enabling data-rich cities to assist data-scarce ones. However, centralized approaches raise privacy concerns, while existing federated methods struggle with pronounced spatiotemporal heterogeneity across cities. To address these challenges, we propose MoE-FedTP, a personalized federated cross-city spatiotemporal prediction framework based on lightweight Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) networks. MoE-FedTP first employs spatiotemporal neural networks to extract features from both source and target cities, then introduces a set of expert networks derived from different source cities through partial parameter sharing. A gating mechanism dynamically fuses the experts to capture diverse traffic dynamics, achieving fine-grained modeling of urban heterogeneity while preserving privacy. Experiments on four real-world traffic datasets show that MoE-FedTP consistently outperforms state-of-the-art cross-city and federated learning baselines, demonstrating its effectiveness in enhancing prediction accuracy for data-scarce cities.

</details>


### [294] [Machine Learning Methods for Studying Latent Neural Activity Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10530)
*Shufeng Kong,Fumei Deng,Xinyi Dong,Caihua Liu,Weiwei Chen,Yingheng Wang,Daniel Cao,Azahara Oliva,Antonio Fernandez-Ruiz,Carla Gomes*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A comprehensive survey of latent variable models (LVMs) for neural population decoding, organized into three domains: single-region latent dynamics, multi-region communication, and behavior-aligned modeling, with discussion of large-scale neural foundation models and open challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The increasing scale and complexity of brain recordings demand machine learning tools that can decode latent structure from large neural populations. A structured survey is needed to organize the diverse LVM literature and identify open challenges for bridging interpretable brain dynamics with reliable neural decoding.

Method: The survey organizes LVM literature into three domains: (1) Single-Region Latent Dynamics — spanning linear dynamical systems, RNNs, and Neural ODEs for modeling within-region dynamics; (2) Multi-Region Communication — using probabilistic and subspace methods to capture inter-area information transfer with synaptic delays and connectivity constraints; (3) Behavior-Aligned Modeling — employing supervised or contrastive learning to separate task-relevant neural activity from other internal states. It also covers large-scale neural foundation models (Transformers, diffusion models) that leverage cross-subject pretraining.

Result: The survey provides a structured taxonomy of LVMs for neural data, tracing the evolution from classical state-space models to modern deep generative approaches, and identifies key challenges including causal link identification, directionality of communication, benchmarks, and evaluation criteria for future research.

Conclusion: LVMs have progressed from simple linear dynamical systems to sophisticated deep generative models and foundation models, but significant open challenges remain — particularly in establishing causal communication directionality and developing standardized benchmarks — to achieve both interpretability and reliable neural decoding.

Abstract: Recent developments in brain recording are driving a demand for machine learning tools capable of decoding the latent structure of large populations of neurons. In this paper, we provide a comprehensive survey that outlines the trajectory of Latent Variable Models (LVMs) from early state-space models to more recent deep generative models. We organize the literature into three closely related domains: (1) Single-Region Latent Dynamics, which includes models such as linear dynamical systems to more complex dynamics represented by Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs); (2) Multi-Region Communication, which employs probabilistic as well as subspace methods to study how information is transferred across different brain areas considering synaptic propagation delays and network connectivity; and (3) Behavior-Aligned Modeling, which seeks to disentangle neural activity related to task performance from other internal states via supervised or contrastive learning. This survey also includes large-scale neural foundation models, such as Transformers and diffusion models, that rely on large-scale pre-training for optimal performance across subjects. Finally, we conclude and discuss benchmarks, evaluation criteria, and open challenges, such as the ability to identify causal links or directionality of communication, to facilitate future research for bridging interpretable brain dynamics with reliable neural decoding.

</details>


### [295] [Flexible Flows for Biological Sequence Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10543)
*Yogesh Verma,Dani Korpela,Harri Lähdesmäki,Vikas Garg*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes improvements to Discrete Flow Matching (DFM) for biological sequence generation: a structured coupling that encodes domain-specific preferences, a latent edit-based rate parameterization for variable-length generation, latent classifier-free guidance, and Dirichlet-prior temperature scaling. These achieve SOTA on DNA and peptide sequence tasks.
