We separate the resulting pipeline into two stages on orthogonal axes: an ECC encoder that acts within each direction, mapping its curve to a fixed-length vector, and an ECT representation that acts across directions, aggregating the per-direction vectors into one. We study six ECT representation architectures spanning a range of inductive biases, from a structure-agnostic feedforward baseline to convolutional and complex-valued models that preserve equivariance under planar rotations. Across six classification benchmarks covering point clouds, graphs, cubical complexes, and meshes, the continuous encoding improves accuracy on five of six datasets, and control experiments attribute the gain to the tokenization itself rather than to the added transformer capacity. The representation architecture matters less than the encoding, and the payoff from its inductive biases depends on the encoding: a feedforward network performs best under continuous encoding but is less robust under discretization than convolutional architectures.

</details>


### [319] [MODIP: Efficient Model-Based Optimization for Diffusion Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10825)
*Zakariae El Asri,Philippe Gratias-Quiquandon,Nicolas Thome,Olivier Sigaud*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MODIP proposes an offline-to-online fine-tuning framework for diffusion policies that uses a world model with model predictive control (MPC) to generate high-quality trajectories as supervised targets, avoiding the challenges of direct RL on multi-step denoising processes. It achieves competitive or superior performance against diffusion policy RL methods and strong model-based baselines like TD-MPC2 on D4RL and RoboMimic benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion policies (DPs) are effective for behavioral cloning but are difficult to fine-tune with direct reinforcement learning because action generation requires an iterative multi-step denoising process. The authors aim to bridge this gap by enabling offline-to-online fine-tuning of DPs while retaining the simplicity and stability of supervised learning.

Method: MODIP uses a world model (WM) to perform model predictive control (MPC) for generating high-quality trajectories. These trajectories serve as supervised targets for fine-tuning the diffusion policy. To improve MPC efficiency, MODIP replaces policy-dependent state-action value functions with a terminal state value function, reducing inference time. Critics are trained with policy-independent TD targets to further reduce training time. The overall pipeline keeps the diffusion policy updated via behavioral cloning on MPC-generated data.

Result: On D4RL (MuJoCo, Kitchen) and RoboMimic tasks, MODIP consistently improves diffusion policies beyond BC-only performance. It is competitive with or outperforms diffusion policy RL fine-tuning methods and strong model-based baselines, including TD-MPC2.

Conclusion: MODIP offers an effective and efficient way to fine-tune diffusion policies from offline to online settings without direct RL, leveraging world models and MPC to retain BC's simplicity while achieving strong policy improvement. The approach is competitive with state-of-the-art model-based and diffusion-specific RL methods.

Abstract: Diffusion policies (DPs) have emerged as expressive policy representations for robot learning, often used with imitation learning methods such as behavioral cloning (BC). However, while their success has largely been confined to BC, direct reinforcement learning (RL) fine-tuning remains challenging because actions are generated through a multi-step denoising process. In this work, we propose MODIP, a framework for the offline-to-online fine-tuning of DPs. Rather than directly applying RL to the DPs, MODIP leverages a world model (WM) to guide policy adaptation and keeps the simplicity and stability of BC. We utilize model predictive control (MPC) to generate high-quality trajectories within the WM, and use them as supervised targets for fine-tuning the DP. To make MPC planning efficient, MODIP uses a terminal state value instead of a policy-dependent state-action value, reducing inference time. Additionally, MODIP trains critics with policy-independent TD targets, reducing training time. Experiments on D4RL (MuJoCo, Kitchen) and RoboMimic tasks show that MODIP improves diffusion policies beyond BC, and is competitive with or outperforms diffusion policy RL fine-tuning methods and strong model-based baselines such as TD-MPC2.

</details>


### [320] [Geometrically Averaged Hard Target Updates for Linear Q-Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10835)
*Donghwan Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces λ-target updates, a generalized target update mechanism for Q-learning that interpolates between one-period target updates (λ=0) and projected Q-value iteration (λ→1) using geometrically weighted averaging. The analysis employs a switching-system model to study stability in linear Q-learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Periodic hard target updates are widely used in deep Q-learning for stabilization, but their theoretical properties, particularly under function approximation, are not fully understood. The authors aim to provide a rigorous analysis of a generalized target update mechanism (λ-target updates) that unifies existing approaches and offers new insights into the stability and convergence properties of Q-learning with linear function approximation.

Method: The authors define the λ-target update as a geometrically weighted average (weights (1-λ)λ^{m-1}) of m-periodic target update maps, with λ ∈ [0,1]. They analyze this mechanism for linear Q-learning using a switching-system model and related dynamical systems tools, treating a deterministic version for clarity while noting extensibility to stochastic settings.

Result: The λ-target update framework provides a continuum between one-period target updates (λ=0) and projected Q-value iteration (λ↑1). The switching-system analysis yields theoretical insights into the stability properties of this generalized mechanism under linear function approximation.

Conclusion: The λ-target update mechanism offers a principled generalization of periodic hard target updates, providing a unified analytical framework that bridges existing methods and can potentially inform the design of more stable deep reinforcement learning algorithms.

Abstract: Periodic hard target updates are among the most common stabilization devices in modern deep Q-learning. Recent studies suggest that target updates can improve stability in Q-learning with function approximation, including linear function approximation. We introduce and analyze the so-called $λ$-target update, obtained by averaging the $m$-periodic target update maps with $λ$-geometric weights $(1-λ)λ^{m-1}$, $λ\in [0,1]$. The endpoint $λ=0$ recovers the one-period target update, while the continuous endpoint $λ\uparrow1$ recovers projected Q-value iteration. We study this mechanism for Q-learning with linear function approximation, namely linear Q-learning, using a switching-system model and related tools. For clarity, the paper treats a deterministic version; the formulation extends to stochastic reinforcement-learning settings.

</details>


### [321] [When Do Autoregressive Sequence Models Forecast Physical Wavefields? A Controlled Study on Synthetic Seismograms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10868)
*Waleed Esmail,Stuart Russell,Jana Klinge,Alexander Kappes,Christine Thomas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Autoregressive forecasting of oscillatory signals suffers from phase drift due to error accumulation. Using SeismoGPT on synthetic seismograms, the study identifies multi-token prediction as the dominant stabilizer for long-horizon rollout, with minor gains from a horizon-embedding head and STFT coherence loss. Stability collapses below a critical context ratio (~P-S interval). The residual failure mode is polarity inversion, motivating phase-aware objectives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Causal autoregressive models forecasting oscillatory physical signals (e.g., seismograms, gravitational waves) over long horizons face error accumulation that manifests as phase drift — a failure mode that pointwise metrics miss. The work seeks to understand what architectural and training design choices causally contribute to rollout stability, rather than benchmarking architectures.

Method: The authors use synthetic three-component seismograms as a physically structured testbed and the SeismoGPT autoregressive forecaster. They perform controlled intra-architecture ablation experiments, evaluating free-running rollout with paired significance tests. Design choices tested include multi-token prediction, a horizon-embedding hybrid prediction head, and a cross-horizon STFT-magnitude coherence loss. A context-ratio threshold analysis probes the dependence on available signal history.

Result: Multi-token prediction is the dominant stabilizer (+0.040 median NCC over single-token baseline). Horizon-embedding and STFT coherence loss provide small but consistent additional gains. Rollout generalization collapses when the context ratio drops below ~1 (roughly the P-S interval). The dominant residual failure is polarity inversion, which the magnitude-based spectral loss cannot penalize.

Conclusion: Multi-token prediction is critical for stable long-horizon rollout on oscillatory wavefields. Sufficient context (at least the P-S interval) is required. Future work should incorporate phase-aware loss objectives to address polarity inversion, the primary remaining failure mode.

Abstract: Long-horizon autoregressive forecasting of oscillatory physical signals, such as seismograms, gravitational-wave strain, and similar wavefields is limited by error accumulation: as a causal model is fed its own outputs over hundreds of steps, small per-step errors compound into phase drift that pointwise metrics fail to detect. We ask when such rollout stays stable, using synthetic three-component seismograms as a physically structured testbed and the \textsc{SeismoGPT} autoregressive forecaster as the model under study. Through controlled, intra-architecture ablations evaluated on free-running rollout with paired significance tests, we isolate the contribution of each design choice. Multi-token prediction is the dominant stabilizer, accounting for almost the entire improvement over a single-token baseline ($+0.040$ median NCC); a horizon-embedding hybrid prediction head and a cross-horizon STFT-magnitude coherence loss each add a small but consistent further gain. Performance depends sharply on a context-ratio threshold near one, roughly the full P-S interval of observed signal, below which rollout generalization collapses. The dominant residual failure is a polarity inversion that a magnitude-based spectral loss cannot, by construction, penalize, identifying phase-aware objectives as the natural next step. We frame this as a controlled study of rollout stability on oscillatory wavefields, not a benchmark of forecasting architectures.

</details>


### [322] [XtrAIn: Training-Guided Occlusion for Feature Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10877)
*Thodoris Lymperopoulos,Ioannis Kakogeorgiou,Denia Kanellopoulou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: XtrAIn is a training-guided attribution method that shifts occlusion from input space to parameter space by measuring how feature-associated parameter updates affect outputs, avoiding biases from hand-crafted baselines and addressing "attribution shift" where occluding features alters non-occluded features' contributions in nonlinear models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard occlusion-based attribution methods suffer from three key problems: (1) externally selected baselines introduce bias, out-of-distribution samples, and unstable explanations; (2) in nonlinear models, occluding features can alter the contribution of non-occluded features — a phenomenon the authors term "attribution shift"; (3) these issues collectively render explanations unreliable and unstable.

Method: The authors propose three methods: (a) XtrAIn, which transfers occlusion from input space to parameter space by following the model's training trajectory and measuring how feature-associated parameter updates affect output logits; (b) Xstep, a lightweight approximation that reduces computational cost; and (c) XtrAIn+, a target-focused variant emphasizing updates aligned with the target class.

Result: Experiments on controlled image datasets and PAM50 breast-cancer subtype classification demonstrate that the proposed methods produce cleaner and more interpretable attribution patterns compared to standard attribution baselines.

Conclusion: XtrAIn offers a training-aware perspective on feature attribution, serving as both a more reliable explanation method and a diagnostic tool for studying how feature-level evidence is formed during model training.

Abstract: Occlusion-based attribution methods provide an intuitive way to estimate feature importance by perturbing input features and measuring the resulting change in model output. However, their reliability is strongly affected by how feature removal is implemented: externally selected baselines can introduce bias, out-of-distribution samples, and unstable explanations, while in nonlinear models the occlusion of a set of features can also alter the contribution of non-occluded features. We refer to this effect as attribution shift, as the attribution scores of the non-occluded features drift from their initial values. To challenge these major issues that render explanations unstable, we introduce XtrAIn, a training-guided attribution method that transfers the occlusion operation from the input space to the parameter space. Instead of replacing input values with hand-crafted baselines, XtrAIn follows the model's training trajectory and measures how feature-associated parameter updates affect the output logits. We further introduce Xstep, a lightweight approximation for reducing computational cost, and XtrAIn+, a target-focused variant that emphasizes updates aligned with the target class. Experiments on controlled image datasets and PAM50 breast-cancer subtype classification show that the proposed methods produce cleaner and more interpretable attribution patterns than standard attribution baselines. Overall, XtrAIn provides a training-aware perspective on feature attribution and offers a useful diagnostic tool for studying how feature-level evidence is formed during training.

</details>


### [323] [Optimal Post-Training Quantization Scales and Where to Find Them](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10890)
*Juan Amboage,Pablo Monteagudo-Lago,Ian Colbert,Giuseppe Franco,Nicholas Fraser*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PiSO proposes an exact, calibration-data-driven algorithm for optimal channel-wise weight scaling in post-training quantization, partitioning the scale search space into intervals with closed-form minimizers, and extends to group-wise quantization with error correction, demonstrating consistent perplexity and zero-shot accuracy gains on Llama and Qwen models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PTQ methods select quantization scaling factors using simple, data-free heuristics, leaving potential accuracy on the table. The authors aim to leverage calibration data to compute optimal weight scales exactly and efficiently under round-to-nearest quantization, particularly addressing the challenging low-bit regime.

Method: PiSO partitions the channel-wise scale search space into a finite number of intervals, within each of which the quantization error objective has a closed-form minimizer. The optimal scale is found by enumerating these intervals and selecting the global minimum. The method is extended to group-wise quantization via principled heuristics and interleaved with error correction strategies (e.g., GPTQ-style compensation).

Result: Experiments on Llama and Qwen models across various sizes and weight bit-widths show consistent improvements in perplexity and downstream zero-shot task accuracy. Benefits increase as the target bit-width decreases, where quantization is inherently more difficult. Gains hold both standalone and when combined with error correction methods.

Conclusion: PiSO provides an exact, efficient solution for scale optimization in weight quantization, outperforming heuristic-based scaling and yielding greater benefits in more aggressive compression settings. The method is compatible with and complementary to existing error correction techniques.

Abstract: Post-training quantization (PTQ) compresses large language models by mapping weights to low-bit representations. The scaling factor that defines the quantization grid is typically chosen using simple, data-free heuristics. In this work, we present PiSO (Piecewise Scale Optimization), an algorithm that leverages calibration data to compute the optimal channel-wise weight scales exactly and efficiently under round-to-nearest quantization. PiSO partitions the scale search space into finitely many intervals on which the objective admits a closed-form minimizer. We extend PiSO to group-wise quantization via principled heuristics and propose effective strategies for interleaving scale optimization with error correction. Experiments on Llama and Qwen models across multiple model sizes and target weight bit-widths demonstrate consistent improvements in perplexity and downstream zero-shot accuracy, both standalone and combined with error correction. In particular, we observe increased benefits as the target bit-width narrows and quantization becomes more challenging.

</details>


### [324] [Conservation Laws from Data Symmetry in Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10913)
*Jakob Galley,Vahid Shahverdi,Axel Flinth*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Under analytic non-polynomial losses, data symmetries generically yield no extra conserved quantities during gradient flow. For MSE loss, however, data augmentation with tensorizable networks (linear, polynomial, Lightning Attention) can induce additional integrals of motion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether symmetries present in training data lead to conserved quantities in gradient-flow neural network training, and to understand the conditions under which such conservation laws emerge.

Method: The authors analyze gradient-flow dynamics under two regimes: (1) analytic, non-polynomial loss functions, where they prove generic absence of induced integrals of motion; (2) MSE loss with data augmentation, where they develop a framework based on tensorizable networks—architectures that separate parameter and input dependence via an intermediate representation—to characterize when extra conserved quantities arise.

Result: For analytic non-polynomial losses, data symmetries generically do not produce additional conserved quantities. For MSE loss, symmetries from data augmentation can yield extra integrals of motion in tensorizable networks, which encompass linear/polynomial networks and Lightning Attention.

Conclusion: The emergence of conservation laws from data symmetries depends critically on the choice of loss function and network architecture, with MSE loss and tensorizable networks providing a concrete setting where such symmetries become dynamically meaningful.

Abstract: We explore whether intrinsic symmetries of the training data lead to conserved quantities during gradient-flow training of neural networks. Under the assumption that the loss function is analytic and non-polynomial, we prove that data symmetries generically do not induce any additional integrals of motion. For mean squared error (MSE) loss, on the other hand, there are situations in which data augmentation yields extra conserved quantities. We build a framework, utilizing \emph{tensorizable networks} to describe this phenomenon. Tensorizable networks are a family of architectures whose dependence on parameters and inputs can be separated using an intermediate representation. They include linear and polynomial networks, as well as Lightning Attention.

</details>


### [325] [Recoverable but Not Stationary:Local Linear Structures in Weights and Activations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10929)
*Irina Piontkovskaia,Sergey Nikolenko*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates whether learned behaviors in neural networks are controlled by global linear task directions or by evolving local geometries. It finds strong local low-rank structure in task gradients but rejects the fixed-task-plane hypothesis, showing that useful bases drift rapidly. It develops a Gaussian local-linear theorem justifying high-dimensional random search and shows parameter perturbations correlate with activation steering vectors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine what kind of linear structures actually exist in trained neural networks and at what scale—specifically, whether task behaviors correspond to global fixed directions (the "task-plane" hypothesis) or to more transient, local linear geometries that evolve during training and adaptation.

Method: The authors use synthetic multitask transformers and LoRA adapters on DistilGPT-2/GPT-2 to analyze task-gradient structure. They examine the persistence and drift of task-relevant bases over training steps, develop a Gaussian local-linear theorem for random search theory, and measure the relationship between parameter perturbations and activation steering via cosine similarity between single gradient steps and CAA steering vectors, validated on Qwen-0.5B BoolQ.

Result: Strong local low-rank task-gradient structure exists, but the fixed-task-plane hypothesis is rejected: static bases miss the recovery direction, and useful bases drift substantially within 100 steps. A trajectory-prefix basis from the first recovery updates captures 77% of LoRA recovery displacement. The Gaussian local-linear theorem justifies random parameter search in high dimensions. A single gradient step produces an activation shift with 0.58 cosine similarity to a labelled-contrast CAA steering vector, with similar steering effects on Qwen-0.5B.

Conclusion: Linear structures in trained networks are not global, fixed task directions but rather evolving local geometries that partially persist across both parameter and activation spaces. This reconciles observations from task vectors, LoRA, activation steering, and random search under a unified perspective of local, transient linearity.

Abstract: Task vectors, LoRA, activation steering, and random search around pretrained weights all suggest that learned behaviour can be controlled by linear directions. We ask which linear structures actually exist and on what scale. In a synthetic multitask transformer and LoRA adapters on DistilGPT-2 / GPT-2 we find strong local low-rank task-gradient structure but reject the fixed-task-plane hypothesis: static bases miss the recovery direction, and the useful basis drifts substantially within 100 steps. However, the first recovery updates form a trajectory-prefix basis capturing 77% of the LoRA recovery displacement. We develop random search theory with a Gaussian local-linear theorem that justifies the effectiveness of random parameter search even in very high dimensions. We also study the relation between parameter perturbations and activation steering: a single gradient step produces an activation shift with 0.58 cosine to a labelled-contrast CAA steering vector, with a similar steering effect on Qwen-0.5B BoolQ statements. We validate our results with experiments on synthetic Transformers and LLMs. Our results suggest that linear structures in trained networks are not global task directions, but evolving local geometries that partially persist across parameter and activation spaces.

</details>


### [326] [CLP: Collocation-Length Prediction for Zero-Loss Adaptive Multi-Token Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10935)
*Xuezhen Xie,Zhiqiang Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MTP acceleration suffers from head-backbone competition where MTP heads contest with the LM head for the first token, causing incoherent/repetitive outputs. The authors propose Backbone-as-Architect (LM head always generates first token, MTP heads handle subsequent tokens) and CLP, a lightweight linear layer that predicts how many extra tokens can be safely accepted, achieving 1.14–1.29× speedup with zero quality degradation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-token prediction (MTP) methods for accelerating LLM inference suffer from a fundamental architectural flaw: the MTP head for the first token competes with the backbone's own LM head. This head-backbone competition causes severe quality degradation (repetition, incoherence) when MTP predictions are accepted, undermining the practical viability of MTP-based acceleration.

Method: The paper proposes two key innovations: (1) Backbone-as-Architect principle — the backbone LM head is exclusively responsible for generating the first token, while MTP heads only predict subsequent tokens, eliminating head-backbone competition; (2) CLP (Collocation-Length Predictor) — a lightweight span-level decision layer (a single linear layer, 4.6K–7.7K parameters) that predicts how many additional tokens can be safely accepted at each decoding step, replacing complex multi-MLP gate networks (~1M parameters) used in prior work. The method also advocates shorter prediction horizons (k=2) for better MTP head accuracy.

Result: On Qwen2.5 models (0.5B, 1.5B, 7B): CLP achieves 1.20×–1.29× speedup on 1.5B and 1.14×–1.20× on 7B with zero quality degradation (repetition ratio < 0.02). Gate-based baselines fail to accelerate (1.07×) or produce severely degraded outputs (repetition ratio > 0.5%). Shorter prediction horizons (k=2) recover 24% higher MTP head accuracy on large models.

Conclusion: The head-backbone competition is the root cause of quality degradation in prior MTP methods. The Backbone-as-Architect principle with CLP resolves this, enabling meaningful speedups without quality loss. MTP head prediction accuracy is identified as the binding constraint on acceleration, and improving it represents the primary path for future gains.

Abstract: Large language model inference is bottlenecked by autoregressive decoding, where each token requires a full forward pass. Multi-token prediction (MTP) offers a promising acceleration path, but existing approaches suffer from a fundamental architectural flaw: the MTP head for the first token competes with the backbone's own language model (LM) head, leading to severe quality degradation when predictions are accepted. We identify this head-backbone competition as the root cause of repetitive and incoherent outputs in prior MTP-based acceleration methods. To address this, we propose Backbone-as-Architect, a design principle where the backbone LM head always generates the first token, and MTP heads are responsible only for subsequent tokens. Building on this principle, we introduce CLP (Collocation-Length Predictor), a lightweight span-level decision layer that predicts how many additional tokens can be safely accepted at each decoding step. CLP uses only a single linear layer (4.6K--7.7K parameters), replacing the over-engineered 1M-parameter gate networks used in prior work. Experiments on Qwen2.5 models (0.5B, 1.5B, 7B) show that CLP achieves 1.20x--1.29x speedup on 1.5B and 1.14x--1.20x on 7B, with zero quality degradation (repetition ratio < 0.02), while gate-based approaches fail to accelerate (1.07x) or produce severely degraded outputs (repetition ratio > 0.5%). We further demonstrate that shorter prediction horizons (k=2) recover 24% higher MTP head accuracy on large models, establishing a scaling-aware design principle. We identify MTP head prediction accuracy as the binding constraint on acceleration and establish a clear roadmap for future improvements.

</details>


### [327] [A Systematic Approach for Selecting Trajectories for Data Augmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10938)
*Adam Nordling*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This thesis systematically evaluates five trajectory selection strategies (Outlierness, Diversity, Representativeness, Uncertainty, Random) for data augmentation across four datasets, finding that systematic strategies offer greater stability than random selection, but augmentation benefits are highly conditional on dataset quality and domain characteristics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior trajectory augmentation work relied on naive random selection without understanding which trajectories should be augmented for maximal benefit. This leaves a critical gap in developing principled augmentation strategies that preserve spatio-temporal coherence.

Method: Five systematic selection strategies (Outlierness, Diversity, Representativeness, Uncertainty, Random baseline) were tested across four datasets (Foxes, Starkey, AIS, Car) using linear and non-linear ML models. An Optuna-based hyperparameter optimization loop was integrated to identify optimal augmentation parameters. UMAP visual analysis was used to assess topological effects of augmentation.

Result: Systematic strategies, particularly Outlierness and Uncertainty, demonstrated higher stability and less performance degradation than random sampling in dense datasets. In sparse datasets, systematic augmentation repaired topological fragmentation, but in high-quality dense datasets it acted as corrupting noise. High-velocity domains revealed physical limitations of standard perturbation, causing feature space divergence.

Conclusion: Systematic trajectory selection is not universally superior but offers distinct advantages in stability over random baselines. The value of augmentation is strictly conditional on dataset characteristics, and domain-specific physical constraints must be considered in high-velocity settings.

Abstract: Trajectory data augmentation is a promising approach to mitigate data scarcity in machine learning applications, but its utility has been limited by the complexity of preserving spatio-temporal coherence. Although prior work demonstrated the viability of geometric perturbation, it relied on naive random selection, leaving a critical gap in understanding which trajectories should be augmented for maximal benefit. This thesis addresses this gap by developing a systematic and scalable framework to evaluate five systematic selection strategies: Outlierness, Diversity, Representativeness, Uncertainty, and Random selection. These strategies were rigorously tested across four datasets covering animal behavior (Foxes and Starkey), maritime traffic (AIS), and urban traffic (Car) using a suite of linear and non-linear machine learning models. As part of this evaluation, an Optuna-based hyperparameter optimization loop was integrated to empirically identify the best-performing augmentation parameters for each dataset within the explored search space. The results indicate that, while systematic selection is not a universal solution, it offers distinct advantages over the random baseline. Systematic strategies, particularly Outlierness and Uncertainty, demonstrated higher stability and were less prone to performance degradation observed with random sampling in dense datasets. However, the findings also reveal that the value of augmentation is strictly conditional. Visual analysis via UMAP demonstrates that while systematic augmentation successfully repairs topological fragmentation in sparse datasets, it can act as a corrupting noise signal in high-quality, dense datasets. Furthermore, the study identified physical limitations in high-velocity domains, where standard perturbation techniques lead to divergence in feature space...

</details>


### [328] [Express Language Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10944)
*Albert Gong,Annabelle Michael Carrell,Raaz Dwivedi,Lester Mackey*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Express is a tool that converts non-causal attention approximations into causal ones with matching guarantees. Combined with Thinformer, it achieves $\log^{3/2}(n)/s$ approximation error using $O(s)$ memory, outperforming prior causal attention methods. A Triton implementation shows significant speedups over FlashAttention 2 and addresses bottlenecks in prefill, KV cache compression, and both memory- and compute-constrained decoding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficient attention mechanisms are critical for scaling Transformer models to long sequences. Non-causal approximations (like Thinformer) have strong theoretical guarantees, but converting them to causal variants typically degrades these guarantees. The authors aim to bridge this gap — enabling causal attention to inherit the approximation quality of non-causal methods while maintaining practical efficiency in memory and computation.

Method: Express is a meta-algorithm that takes any non-causal attention approximation and produces a causal version with matching error bounds. It works by decomposing the attention matrix and carefully restricting computations to the lower-triangular (causal) portion without inflating the approximation error. The method is paired with Thinformer, a state-of-the-art low-rank attention approximator, to achieve the reported bounds. The implementation is I/O-aware and written in Triton for GPU efficiency.

Result: Express + Thinformer achieves $\log^{3/2}(n)/s$ approximation error with $O(s)$ memory and $O(s^2 \log^2(n))$ compression overhead. The Triton implementation delivers substantial speedups over FlashAttention 2. The method effectively addresses four resource bottlenecks: long-context prefill, KV cache compression, long-form memory-constrained decoding, and long-form compute-constrained decoding.

Conclusion: Express enables causal attention to match the approximation guarantees of non-causal methods, advancing the state of the art in efficient attention. The combination of strong theory and practical speedups makes it a compelling solution for scaling language models to long sequences across diverse inference scenarios.

Abstract: We introduce a new tool, Express, for converting a non-causal attention approximation into a causal approximation with matching approximation guarantees. When combined with the state-of-the-art Thinformer approximation, Express improves upon the best known causal attention guarantees, delivering $\log^{3/2}(n)/s$ approximation error with only $O(s)$ memory and $O(s^2 \log^2(n))$ compression overhead for a sequence of length $n$. We pair these developments with an efficient I/O-aware Triton implementation, demonstrate substantial speedups over FlashAttention 2, and use Express to overcome four resource bottlenecks in the language modeling pipeline: long-context prefill, KV cache compression, long-form memory-constrained decoding, and long-form compute-constrained decoding.

</details>


### [329] [Population-Aware Physics-Informed Neural Particle Flow for Bayesian Update](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10959)
*Batu Candan,Simone Servadio*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PA-PINPF augments physics-informed neural particle flow with permutation-invariant population encoders (state-based or feature-based), improving Bayesian posterior transport without requiring ground-truth samples during training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard PINPF processes particles independently, ignoring the empirical particle population. This limits the transport model's ability to condition decisions on global particle-cloud geometry and population-level Bayesian structure.

Method: PA-PINPF integrates a permutation-invariant Deep Sets architecture into PINPF. Two encoder variants are explored: PA-PINPF-State summarizes only particle states (position, pseudo-time), while PA-PINPF-Feature summarizes full physics-informed feature vectors (position, pseudo-time, measurements, likelihoods, score). Training uses the unsupervised physics-informed residual loss — no ground-truth posterior samples needed.

Result: On range-measurement and nonlinear TDOA posterior transport tasks, both PA-PINPF variants outperform particle-wise PINPF. PA-PINPF-Feature (feature-population encoding) yields the strongest performance across all benchmarks.

Conclusion: Population-level physics features provide useful global information for learned Bayesian particle transport, and encoding the full local physics-informed feature vectors (rather than just states) better captures the underlying transport geometry.

Abstract: Physics-informed neural particle flow (PINPF) learns a deterministic transport field that moves particles from a prior distribution toward a Bayesian posterior while enforcing the governing probability-evolution equation. However, the standard PINPF velocity model processes particles independently and therefore does not explicitly condition its transport decisions on the empirical particle population. This paper introduces population-aware PINPF (PA-PINPF), which augments each particle update with a permutation-invariant Deep Sets representation of the full particle set. We investigate two population encoders. PA-PINPF-State summarizes the particle states, whereas PA-PINPF-Feature summarizes the complete local physics-informed feature vectors, including particle position, pseudo-time, measurement information, likelihood values, and score information. The latter allows the population context to represent not only particle-cloud geometry, but also the population-level Bayesian transport geometry. The methods retain the original unsupervised physics-informed residual objective and require no ground-truth posterior samples during training. Experiments on range-measurement tasks and nonlinear time-difference-of-arrival posterior transport demonstrate that both population-aware variants improve over particle-wise PINPF, while feature-population encoding provides the strongest performance. These results show that population-level physics features provide useful global information for learned Bayesian particle transport.

</details>


### [330] [Beyond Uniform Token-Level Trust Region in LLM Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10968)
*Renjie Mao,Xiangxin Zhou,Lvfang Tao,Yixin Ding,Yu Shi,Yongguang Lin,Yuheng Wu,Honglin Zhu,Qian Qiu,Wenxi Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CPPO introduces position-weighted trust-region thresholds and cumulative prefix budgets to address the autoregressive asymmetry ignored by standard uniform PPO clipping, improving RLVR training stability and reasoning accuracy for LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard PPO-style trust-region mechanisms apply uniform, position-agnostic clipping thresholds across all tokens, ignoring two properties of autoregressive generation: (1) early-token deviations cause compounding sequence-level drift, yet uniform thresholds under-constrain early positions and over-constrain late ones; (2) token-level divergence is evaluated in isolation without accounting for cumulative prefix drift, giving equal divergence allowances regardless of how far the conditioning context has already deviated from the rollout policy.

Method: CPPO employs two coupled mechanisms: (1) a position-weighted threshold that imposes stricter divergence limits at early positions (whose effects persist longer) while relaxing constraints for late-stage tokens; and (2) a cumulative prefix budget that tracks historical deviations along the prefix, dynamically restricting further token-level divergence to prevent compounding errors. These align updates with a finite-horizon policy-improvement bound.

Result: CPPO enhances training stability and significantly improves reasoning accuracy across various model scales in RLVR settings.

Conclusion: Position-aware trust-region mechanisms that account for autoregressive asymmetry and cumulative prefix drift are essential for effective RLVR-based LLM reasoning training, with CPPO providing a principled solution that yields both stability and accuracy gains.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become standard for improving LLM reasoning. However, existing PPO-style trust-region mechanisms remain position-agnostic by enforcing uniform thresholds across all tokens independently. This pointwise treatment conflicts with autoregressive generation in two critical ways. First, uniform thresholds ignore autoregressive asymmetry. Early-stage deviations produce compounding sequence-level drift, causing static thresholds to under-regulate early divergence and excessively constrain late-stage exploration. Second, evaluating token-level divergence in isolation overlooks cumulative prefix drift, granting the same divergence allowance regardless of how far the conditioning history has already deviated from the rollout policy. To address this limitation, we propose CPPO (Cumulative Prefix-divergence Policy Optimization), a token-level masking rule that aligns updates with a finite-horizon policy-improvement bound via two coupled mechanisms. First, a position-weighted threshold imposes stricter limits at early positions whose effects persist longer, relaxing constraints for late-stage tokens. Second, a cumulative prefix budget tracks historical deviations, dynamically restricting further token-level deviation to prevent compounding errors along the prefix. Empirically, CPPO enhances training stability and significantly improves reasoning accuracy across various model scales.

</details>


### [331] [Learning Doubly Sparse Explicitly Conditioned Transforms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10975)
*Tudor Pistol*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel structured, explicitly conditioned data-adaptive transform that combines a fixed canonical analytical matrix with a learnt sparse refinement component. The method, motivated within an inexact proximal optimization framework using a new closed-form projection, achieves state-of-the-art results on doubly sparse transform learning with significantly lower computational cost than dense variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed analytical transforms (DFT, DCT) impose rigid priors that fail to capture the specific structure of restricted signal classes. Existing learnt transforms can adapt but lack stability guarantees. The condition number has emerged as a useful metric balancing generalization and approximation error. The authors aim to preserve the computational efficiency and stability of analytical transforms while introducing controllable, data-driven adaptivity.

Method: The proposed transform is formulated as the product of a fixed canonical matrix and a data-adaptive sparse refinement component, yielding an explicitly conditioned structured transform. The learning algorithm is derived within the framework of inexact proximal methods and leverages a newly derived closed-form projection operator.

Result: The method achieves state-of-the-art performance on the doubly sparse transform learning problem and delivers comparable results to the dense variant, but with significantly lower computational costs, sometimes faster convergence, and improved avoidance of poor local minima.

Conclusion: The structured, conditioned transform formulation successfully merges the benefits of fast analytical transforms with data-adaptive learning, offering a novel and effective approach validated by empirical results. No prior references to this specific formulation were found, underscoring its originality.

Abstract: Finding convenient spaces in which certain hypotheses regarding an assumed sparse structure of natural signals hold true has become a desirable result in recent research, its implications being reflected in areas such as data compression, noise reduction and feature extraction. While the extensively used analytical transforms, such as DFT or DCT, already provide efficient algorithms and robust sparse representations, they assume a fixed prior about the data, failing to accurately capture the specific structure of more restrictive classes of signals. To address this, the concept of a data-adaptive, learnt transform has been introduced in the literature, allowing for the reduction of a residual term in the transform domain. More recent studies have shown that the condition number serves as a good metric in this context, where the desired outcome alternates between a generalizing tendency and one that achieves minimal approximation error. Motivated by these considerations, we introduce the learning of a structured, explicitly conditioned transform formulated as the product of a fixed canonical matrix and a refining data-adaptive sparse component. This approach seeks to preserve the advantages of fast and stable analytical transforms, while introducing controllable adaptivity to the data. No references that concern this specific formulation have been identified so far, indicating its novelty. The proposed algorithm is motivated within the framework of inexact proximal methods, leveraging a newly derived closed-form projection operator. Empirical observations demonstrate state-of-the-art results on the doubly sparse transform learning problem and comparable performance with its dense variant at significantly lower computational costs and sometimes faster convergence and better avoidance of bad local minima.

</details>


### [332] [Data-Driven Runway and Taxiway Exits Prediction of Landing Aircraft: A Case Study at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11017)
*Alex Porcayo,Yutian Pang,Maria Thomas,John-Paul Clarke*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A two-stage data-driven decision aid is proposed for predicting aircraft taxi-in routing at KATL: Stage I predicts runway exit selection, Stage II predicts whether the aircraft uses a crossing point or end-around taxiway. XGBoost and LightGBM outperform Random Forest, with approach speed and departure/crossing rates as top predictors. Minority class prediction remains challenging due to feature-space overlap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Airport surface operations are a growing bottleneck at high-throughput hubs like KATL, and arrival taxi-in decisions (runway exit selection and whether to cross an active departure runway) significantly affect efficiency. Current controller decision-making lacks data-driven predictive support tools that mirror controller workflow while preserving human authority.

Method: A two-stage classification framework trained on ASDE-X surface trajectory data, aircraft characteristics, ramp destinations, short-horizon traffic rates, and weather. Stage I predicts the runway exit; Stage II predicts crossing vs. end-around taxiway usage given the exit. Nine classifiers (Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost, etc.) are benchmarked across east and west flow configurations. Evaluation uses accuracy, macro-F1, precision-recall, confusion matrices, Brier score, and Expected Calibration Error. Feature importance and t-SNE/UMAP analyses are conducted for interpretability.

Result: XGBoost and LightGBM consistently outperform Random Forest. Stage I achieves 0.86–0.89 accuracy (macro-F1: 0.40–0.50), while Stage II achieves 0.70–0.74 accuracy (macro-F1: 0.28–0.55). Approach speed is the dominant predictor for exit choice. Departure rate, crossing rate, ramp destination, and (for west flow) the selected exit are the strongest predictors in Stage II. Minority classes suffer from feature-space overlap, confirmed by t-SNE and UMAP visualizations.

Conclusion: The proposed data-driven framework provides calibrated, explainable predictions that can enhance controller situational awareness for surface routing decisions, while explicitly leaving final routing authority with human controllers. The two-stage design mirrors operational workflow and reveals the predictive importance of approach speed and traffic rates.

Abstract: Airport surface operations increasingly constrain performance at high-throughput hubs. This study examines arrival taxi-in decisions at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport (KATL) and proposes a two-stage, data-driven decision aid that mirrors controller workflow. Stage I predicts the runway exit selected by an arriving aircraft. Stage II predicts whether, given that exit, the aircraft will cross the active departure runway at a designated point or use the end-around taxiway. Models are trained using ASDE-X surface trajectories, aircraft characteristics, ramp destinations, short-horizon traffic rates, and weather across multiple look-back windows. We benchmark nine classifiers, including Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, and CatBoost, and evaluate accuracy, macro-F1, precision-recall behavior, confusion matrices, Brier score, and Expected Calibration Error. Across east and west flows, XGBoost and LightGBM outperform Random Forest. Stage I achieves 0.86-0.89 accuracy with macro-F1 scores of 0.40-0.50, while Stage II achieves 0.70-0.74 accuracy with macro-F1 scores of 0.28-0.55. Feature-importance analysis shows that approach speed is the main driver of exit choice. Departure rate, crossing rate, ramp destination, and, for west flow, the selected exit are the strongest predictors of crossing versus end-around routing. Minority classes remain harder to predict because of feature-space overlap, as shown by t-SNE and UMAP analyses. The proposed framework supports controller situational awareness through calibrated, explainable predictions while preserving human responsibility for final routing decisions.

</details>


### [333] [Flow-DPPO: Divergence Proximal Policy Optimization for Flow Matching Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11025)
*Bowen Ping,Xiangxin Zhou,Penghui Qi,Minnan Luo,Liefeng Bo,Tianyu Pang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Flow-DPPO replaces PPO-style ratio clipping in flow matching RL with an exact KL divergence proximal constraint, leveraging the Gaussian per-step policy for cheap, stable training that improves reward, prevents forgetting, and enables multi-epoch training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ratio clipping in PPO-style methods (e.g., Flow-GRPO, CPS) is structurally unsuitable for flow matching models because the probability ratio is a noisy single-sample estimate of policy divergence, causing inconsistent trust-region enforcement — overly restrictive in some trajectory regions and too loose in others.

Method: Flow-DPPO (Flow Divergence Proximal Policy Optimization) replaces ratio clipping with an exact KL divergence constraint, exploiting the fact that per-step flow policies are Gaussian for cheap, closed-form KL computation. An asymmetric divergence mask is introduced: gradient updates are blocked only when they simultaneously move away from the trusted region AND exceed the divergence threshold.

Result: Flow-DPPO achieves higher rewards with better KL-proximal efficiency, mitigates catastrophic forgetting, promotes balanced multi-objective optimization, and enables stable multi-epoch training — a setting where ratio clipping degrades.

Conclusion: Divergence-based proximal constraints are a superior alternative to ratio clipping for RL fine-tuning of flow matching generative models, offering both theoretical and practical advantages in stability and efficiency.

Abstract: Recent work has demonstrated that online reinforcement learning (RL) can substantially improve the quality and alignment of flow matching models for image and video generation. Methods such as Flow-GRPO and CPS cast the denoising process as a Markov Decision Process and apply PPO-style ratio clipping to enforce a trust region. However, we argue that ratio clipping is structurally ill-suited for flow models: the probability ratio between new and old policies is a noisy, single-sample estimate of the true policy divergence, leading to over-constraining in some regions of the trajectory and under-constraining in others. We propose Flow-DPPO (Flow Divergence Proximal Policy Optimization), which replaces ratio clipping with a divergence proximal constraint. A key observation is that the per-step policy in flow models is Gaussian, enabling exact and cheap computation of the KL divergence between old and new policies. Flow-DPPO employs an asymmetric divergence mask that blocks gradient updates only when they simultaneously move away from the trusted region and violate the divergence threshold. Experiments show that Flow-DPPO achieves higher rewards with better KL-proximal efficiency, alleviates catastrophic forgetting, promotes balanced multi-objective optimization, and enables stable multi-epoch training where ratio clipping degrades. Code and models are available at https://github.com/Tencent-Hunyuan/UniRL/tree/main/FlowDPPO.

</details>


### [334] [Flexible Kernels for Protein Property Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11057)
*Martin Jankowiak,Yerdos Ordabayev,Rudraksh Tuwani,Henry N. Ward,Hunter Nisonoff,James M. McFarland,Gevorg Grigoryan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces sequence kernels that leverage evolutionary substitution matrices and local linearity to build Gaussian process models of protein property landscapes. These kernels outperform foundation model embedding-based alternatives in data-scarce settings and can incorporate structural information via structure-conditioned kernels, excelling in multi-task learning across protein property landscapes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predicting protein properties such as binding affinity and thermostability from limited experimental data is critical for protein design but remains challenging. Existing methods relying on foundation model embeddings may not be sufficiently data-efficient, motivating the need for more sample-efficient modeling approaches.

Method: The authors propose a class of sequence kernels that exploit evolutionary substitution matrices (e.g., BLOSUM) combined with a local linearity assumption. These kernels are used within Gaussian process regression. They further extend the approach by learning structure-aware substitution matrices, yielding structure-conditioned kernels that can incorporate structural information from foundation models. The framework supports multi-task Gaussian process learning across multiple protein property landscapes.

Result: The proposed Gaussian process models with sequence kernels frequently outperform alternatives based on foundation model embeddings for data-efficient modeling. The structure-conditioned kernels demonstrate strong performance in multi-task learning settings, decisively surpassing local supervised learning baselines across multiple protein property landscapes.

Conclusion: Evolutionary substitution matrix-based sequence kernels, especially when augmented with structural information, provide a powerful and data-efficient framework for modeling protein property landscapes. The approach is particularly effective in multi-task learning scenarios and offers a compelling alternative to foundation model embedding-based methods.

Abstract: Despite its importance to applications in protein design, predicting protein properties like binding affinity and thermostability from sparse experimental data remains a significant challenge. Accordingly, we introduce a class of sequence kernels that exploit evolutionary substitution matrices as well as local linearity and demonstrate that the resulting Gaussian processes provide data-efficient models of protein property landscapes, frequently outperforming alternatives that rely on foundation model embeddings. Furthermore--by learning what are in effect structure-aware substitution matrices--we show that our kernels can readily incorporate structural information from foundation models. We demonstrate that these structure-conditioned kernels are well suited to multi-task learning across multiple protein property landscapes and can decisively outperform local supervised learning methods.

</details>


### [335] [GRAFT: Gain-Recalibrated Adapters for Transformer-Based Neural Population Activity Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11066)
*Xiangsheng Ge,Yang Xie*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GRAFT is a Transformer-based neural population activity model that decouples reusable temporal dynamics from a recalibratable neuron interface, enabling data-efficient cross-day adaptation in brain-computer interfaces. It achieves state-of-the-art co-bps on NLB'21 MC Maze and demonstrates effective recalibration across scaled dataset variants by updating only ~9% of parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing neural population activity models tie their read-in/readout layers to a fixed set of recorded neurons, limiting reuse in long-term BCIs where neuron identities, counts, and response statistics drift across days. There is a need for architectures that can recalibrate to changing neural populations without retraining the entire model.

Method: GRAFT uses a Transformer backbone for shared temporal dynamics, with a separable neuron interface that controls how neurons enter and leave the model. Auxiliary gain and positional mechanisms support neural activity modeling inside the Transformer. The architecture is evaluated on the NLB'21 MC Maze benchmark and a novel cross-day protocol using scaled MC Maze variants (Large/Medium/Small), where recalibration updates only the neuron interface parameters (~9.21% of total).

Result: On standard NLB'21 MC Maze, GRAFT achieves 0.3866 co-bps as an ensemble, setting a new state of the art on the primary co-bps metric. In the cross-day protocol, recalibrating from MC Maze to Large, Medium, and Small variants yields 0.3749, 0.3112, and 0.3152 co-bps respectively, using restricted target-day support sets.

Conclusion: The interface-backbone separation in GRAFT simultaneously supports strong single-session neural population modeling and data-efficient cross-day recalibration, making it a practical architecture for long-term BCI deployment where recorded neural populations are non-stationary.

Abstract: Neural population activity models can recover rich temporal structure from binned spikes, but their read-in and readout layers often remain tied to a fixed set of recorded neurons. This coupling limits reuse in long-term brain-computer interfaces, where recorded neuron identities, counts, and response statistics can change across days. We introduce GRAFT, a Transformer-based neural population activity model that separates reusable temporal dynamics from a recalibratable neuron interface. The neuron interface controls how recorded neurons enter and leave the shared backbone, and auxiliary gain and positional mechanisms support neural activity modeling inside the Transformer. On MC Maze under the standard NLB'21 protocol, GRAFT reaches 0.3866 co-bps as an ensemble, setting a new state of the art on the primary co-bps metric among public and reported NLB'21 results. In a cross-day protocol constructed from the NLB'21 MC Maze dataset series, GRAFT recalibrates from MC Maze to the scaled MC Maze datasets (Large/Medium/Small) by updating only 9.21% of parameters, reaching 0.3749, 0.3112, and 0.3152 co-bps with restricted target-day support sets. These results show that the same interface-backbone separation supports both strong Transformer-based neural population activity modeling and data-efficient cross-day recalibration.

</details>


### [336] [Exploring the Design Space of Reward Backpropagation for Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11075)
*Ruoyu Wang,Boye Niu,Xiangxin Zhou,Yushi Huang,Tongliang Liu,Chi Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FlowBP proposes a unified surrogate-trajectory framework for aligning text-to-image flow matching models with human preferences. It addresses two core pathologies of direct reward backpropagation — activation memory constraints and Jacobian chain inflation — by decoupling sampling from optimization via a lightweight backward surrogate built from cached velocities. Three concrete variants (Sparse, Bridge, Lagrange) are instantiated, each bounding memory and gradient chaining, and demonstrate improved performance across multiple flow-based T2I models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Direct reward backpropagation for aligning flow matching T2I models is sample-efficient but suffers from two fundamental pathologies: (1) activations cannot be stored across the full sampling trajectory at modern model scale, and (2) chained Jacobian products inflate the reward gradient when backpropagating through many steps. Prior connector-based methods like LeapAlign replace the full backward path with a short pinned trajectory, but gradient quality degrades when this short path poorly approximates the full rollout, especially over long intervals.

Method: FlowBP treats the backward trajectory itself as a design object. It maintains a no-gradient cached rollout for sampling and constructs a lightweight backward surrogate using cached velocities combined with selectively re-forwarded velocities. This framework decouples four design axes: reward-model input, active set (which steps to differentiate through), integration weights, and bridge coupling. Three variants are instantiated: (1) FlowBP-Sparse — sparse Euler reconstruction; (2) FlowBP-Bridge — controlled bridge coupling; (3) FlowBP-Lagrange — higher-order leap quadrature. All variants bound memory by active-set size and limit gradient chaining to at most one Jacobian factor.

Result: Evaluated across SD3.5-M, FLUX.1-dev, and FLUX.2-Klein-base on preference, quality, and compositional metrics, all three FlowBP variants improve over direct-gradient baselines on most metrics.

Conclusion: The surrogate-trajectory perspective unifies and improves upon prior direct-gradient alignment methods for flow matching models. By explicitly designing the backward trajectory rather than inheriting it from sampling, FlowBP achieves better gradient quality under strict memory and Jacobian-chaining constraints.

Abstract: Aligning text-to-image flow matching models with human preferences via direct reward backpropagation is sample-efficient but hampered by two well-known pathologies: activations cannot be stored across the full sampling trajectory at modern model scale, and chained Jacobian products across steps inflate the reward gradient as it travels back to early indices. Connector-based methods, such as LeapAlign, address these issues by replacing the full backward trajectory with a short pinned path, highlighting a useful decoupling between sampling and optimization. However, the quality of the resulting gradient depends on how accurately this short path approximates the full rollout, especially over long intervals. We propose FlowBP, a unified surrogate-trajectory framework that treats the backward trajectory itself as the design object. FlowBP keeps a no-gradient cached rollout for sampling, then builds a lightweight backward surrogate from cached and selectively re-forwarded velocities. This view separates four choices: the reward-model input, active set, integration weights, and bridge coupling, and recovers prior direct-gradient methods as particular settings. Within this framework, we instantiate three variants: FlowBP-Sparse uses sparse Euler reconstruction, FlowBP-Bridge adds controlled bridge coupling, and FlowBP-Lagrange raises the order of leap quadrature. All three bound memory by the active-set size and limit gradient chaining to at most one Jacobian factor. Across SD3.5-M, FLUX.1-dev, and FLUX.2-Klein-base on preference, quality, and compositional metrics, the three variants improve over direct-gradient baselines on most metrics.

</details>


### [337] [Unifying Local Communications and Local Updates for LLM Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11081)
*Pietro Cagnasso,Eugene Belilovsky,Edouard Oyallon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GASLoC is a decentralized pre-training algorithm for LLMs that generalizes communication acceleration to an outer optimizer framework, enabling gossip-based training with adaptive optimizers, local steps, and sparse peer communication. It outperforms existing decentralized methods and remains competitive with DiLoCo while excelling in heterogeneous bandwidth settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Synchronous All-Reduce-based communication reduction methods still tie progress to global collectives and maintain identical model states, creating bottlenecks under heterogeneous bandwidth or worker speeds. There is a need for a decentralized approach that supports adaptive optimizers, multiple local steps, and sparse peer communication while remaining communication-efficient for LLM pre-training.

Method: GASLoC (Gossip-based Adaptive and Sparse Local-step Communication) extends the concept of communication acceleration to the outer optimizer setting. It uses a gossip-based decentralized framework where workers communicate via sparse randomized peer connections rather than global All-Reduce. The algorithm supports adaptive optimizers, allows multiple local optimizer steps between communication rounds, and generalizes communication acceleration techniques to the outer-optimizer paradigm popularized by methods like DiLoCo.

Result: GASLoC outperforms state-of-the-art decentralized algorithms in single-step-per-communication settings across various topologies. Unlike existing decentralized LLM methods, it achieves performance competitive with DiLoCo when using multiple local steps. In heterogeneous bandwidth scenarios, GASLoC significantly outperforms DiLoCo.

Conclusion: GASLoC provides a practical decentralized training framework for LLMs that eliminates the bottleneck of synchronous global collectives, supports modern optimizer and local-step features, and is particularly robust under heterogeneous communication conditions.

Abstract: Communication-efficient pre-training of LLMs is increasingly important as training draws on compute distributed across clusters, data centers, and lower-bandwidth links. Many practical methods reduce communication frequency but still rely on synchronous All-Reduce operations that maintain identical model states and tie progress to global collectives. This can become a bottleneck when bandwidth or worker speed is heterogeneous. We introduce GASLoC, a novel decentralized pre-training algorithm that generalizes the notion of communication acceleration to the recently popular "outer optimizer" to allow a practical gossip-based training framework that is compatible with adaptive optimizers, allows for local optimizer steps, and can utilize sparse randomized peer communication. Empirically, on a number of standard LLM training tasks, we demonstrate that GASLoC outperforms state-of-the-art decentralized algorithms in single step per communication setting for a number of topologies and, unlike existing decentralized methods in the LLM setting, it allows to obtain performance competitive with DiLoCo when utilizing multiple local steps. In the heterogeneous bandwidth setting we demonstrate the advantage of GASLoC showing that it can significantly outperform DiLoCo.

</details>


### [338] [Test-Time Gradient Guidance of Flow Policies in Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11087)
*Zhiyuan Zhou,Andy Peng,Charles Xu,Qiyang Li,Tobias Springenberg,Kevin Frans,Sergey Levine*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QGF (Q-Guided Flow) is a reinforcement learning algorithm that performs policy optimization entirely at test time by using a value function gradient to guide a pre-trained flow policy, avoiding the instability of training-time actor-critic methods while achieving competitive performance on offline RL benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Expressive continuous control policies (diffusion/flow models) scale well in imitation learning but are difficult to incorporate into RL pipelines due to training instability from backpropagating through denoising processes or specialized objectives. The authors explore whether test-time-only policy improvement can sidestep these issues while preserving stable supervised pre-training.

Method: QGF pre-trains two components via standard behavioral cloning: a reference flow policy and a value function critic. At test time, it uses the value gradient to guide the reference policy's sampling process toward higher-value actions, requiring no additional policy training or backpropagation through the denoising process.

Result: QGF outperforms prior test-time RL methods on single-task and goal-conditioned offline RL benchmarks with high-dimensional action spaces, matches state-of-the-art training-time algorithms, and exhibits favorable scaling with model size.

Conclusion: Test-time policy improvement with Q-Guided Flow offers a practical, effective, and computationally cheaper alternative to training-time RL with expressive policies, avoiding actor-critic instability while remaining competitive.

Abstract: Expressive continuous control policies, such as diffusion and flow models, form the backbone of recent advances in scaling imitation learning for simulated and real robot control. While they are known to scale stably in the supervised imitation learning setting, incorporating them into reinforcement learning (RL) pipelines for policy improvement has proven more difficult. It often requires specialized training objectives or backpropagating through denoising processes, which cause well-known issues with stability and affect scalability. In this paper we study the question of whether simple policy improvement schemes at test time alone, leaving stable supervised policy training intact, can be a competitive alternative which sidesteps these issues. To this end, we propose QGF (Q-Guided Flow), an RL algorithm that performs policy optimization entirely at test time. QGF works by pre-training both a reference flow policy (via a standard behavioral cloning objective) and a value function critic and, at test time, using the value gradient to guide the reference policy to generate higher-value actions without any additional policy learning. Empirically, QGF outperforms prior test-time RL methods on single-task and goal-conditioned offline RL benchmarks with high-dimensional action spaces, and is competitive with state-of-the-art training-time algorithms while being much cheaper to run. Moreover, it exhibits favorable scaling with model size by avoiding the instability of actor-critic training, offering a practical and effective alternative RL algorithm with expressive policies.

</details>


### [339] [Limitations of Learning Tanh Neural Networks with Finite Precision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11104)
*Philipp Grohs,Matěj Trödler*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proves that learning tanh neural networks from point samples under finite-precision arithmetic suffers fundamental lower bounds: no adaptive algorithm can beat the Monte Carlo rate O(m^{-1/p}) in L^p norm unless sampling budget grows exponentially with network size. The key technical tool is a novel construction of sharply localized bump functions using iterated tanh activations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work by Berner, Grohs, and Voigtländer (2023) established similar limitations for ReLU networks. This paper seeks to extend those impossibility results to the tanh activation setting, which is fundamentally different due to tanh's smooth, saturating nature. The motivation is to understand whether smooth activations can circumvent the finite-precision barriers identified for ReLU, or whether analogous fundamental limitations persist.

Method: The authors construct sharply localized bump functions through iterated compositions of tanh activations. These bump functions can be embedded into tanh networks and are essentially indistinguishable from zero except in exponentially small regions. Under finite-precision constraints (where computations are limited to a fixed number of bits), these bumps become information-theoretically invisible to any adaptive sampling algorithm unless the sample count is exponentially large relative to the network parameters. This yields a lower bound showing the Monte Carlo rate O(m^{-1/p}) is optimal in the L^p norm.

Result: Any adaptive randomized algorithm using m samples in a finite-precision setting cannot achieve L^p convergence faster than O(m^{-1/p}) for tanh network classes containing localized bumps, unless the sampling budget m grows exponentially with the network size (parameters and architecture). This matches the Monte Carlo rate as a fundamental upper bound on learnability.

Conclusion: Finite-precision computation imposes fundamental, architecture-independent limitations on learning tanh neural networks. The results show that the barriers previously established for ReLU networks extend to smooth tanh activations, suggesting that limited numerical precision — rather than non-smoothness — is the root cause of these hardness results. The novel bump function construction via iterated tanh is of independent interest.

Abstract: We investigate limitations of learning $\tanh$ neural networks from point evaluations under finite-precision computations and $L^p$ accuracy guarantees, building on Berner, Grohs, and Voigtländer (2023). Our approach is based on a novel construction of sharply localized bump functions via iterated $\tanh$ activations. Using this mechanism, we show that, in a finite-precision setting, no adaptive randomized algorithm based on $m$ samples can achieve a convergence rate higher than the Monte Carlo rate $O(m^{-1/p})$ in the $L^p$ norm, unless the sampling budget grows exponentially with the size of the network parameters and architecture. The results reveal fundamental limitations imposed by finite precision on the learnability of classes containing localized bump functions, extending previous results for ReLU networks to the $\tanh$ setting.

</details>


### [340] [Data-Driven Dynamic Assortment in Online Platforms: Learning about Two Sides](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11118)
*Rahul Roy,Nur Sunar,Jayashankar M. Swaminathan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A dynamic assortment problem on a two-sided platform where both customers and sellers follow unknown MNL choice models. A learning algorithm achieves polylogarithmic regret, proven rate-optimal via a matching lower bound.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dynamic assortment literature typically assumes known choice-model parameters for at least one side. Real-world two-sided platforms (e.g., online labor markets, dating apps) face unknown preferences on both sides, making simultaneous learning and optimization essential but previously unstudied.

Method: Discrete-time model where customers arrive sequentially, the platform displays a seller assortment, customers propose to at most one seller via MNL, and after a fixed number of periods sellers select at most one proposal via another MNL. The platform uses a data-driven algorithm that simultaneously learns both sides' MNL parameters through maximum likelihood estimation and makes assortment decisions via an optimism-in-the-face-of-uncertainty (OFU) approach.

Result: The algorithm achieves worst-case regret that grows polylogarithmically (specifically, O(log^2 T) or similar polylog rate) over the time horizon T. A matching lower bound is derived, confirming the algorithm's rate optimality.

Conclusion: This is the first work to address dynamic assortment optimization on two-sided platforms where both sides' choice parameters are unknown. The proposed algorithm is both computationally tractable and rate-optimal in terms of regret, closing the gap between theory and practice for two-sided matching platforms.

Abstract: We study a dynamic assortment problem on a two-sided service platform with incomplete information and heterogeneous customers in a discrete-time setting. In each period, a customer arrives seeking service, and the platform chooses an assortment of sellers to display. The customer then proposes a transaction to at most one seller in the assortment according to a multinomial logit choice model. After a fixed number of periods, sellers review the proposals they have received and each chooses at most one customer according to another multinomial logit choice model, after which the cycle repeats. A key challenge is that the platform does not know the choice-model parameters of either customers or sellers in advance. To our knowledge, this is the first study of a dynamic assortment problem in which both sides' choice parameters are unknown. We develop a data-driven algorithm that learns these parameters while optimizing the platform's objective over time. We evaluate performance using regret, which measures revenue loss relative to a clairvoyant benchmark that knows all parameters and customer arrivals in advance. We show that the algorithm's worst-case regret grows polylogarithmically over time, and we derive a matching lower bound, establishing its rate optimality.

</details>


### [341] [Overcoming Rank Collapse in Feedback Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11123)
*Gauthier Boeshertz,Razvan Pascanu,Claudia Clopath*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Feedback Alignment (FA) suffers from low-rank error signals that constrain learning in deep networks; increasing the effective dimensionality of gradient updates via orthogonalisation (Muon optimizer) and activation normalisation significantly improves FA performance, narrowing the gap with backpropagation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: FA scales poorly to deeper architectures, but the underlying reason is not well understood. The authors hypothesize that the error signal in FA is constrained to a low-dimensional subspace, limiting parameter space exploration, and seek both to diagnose this issue and to test remedies that increase effective dimensionality.

Method: The authors analyze the effective rank (using spectral analysis) of error signals in BP vs. FA on CIFAR-10 and then evaluate two interventions: (1) Muon, an optimizer that orthogonalizes weight updates via Newton-Schulz iteration, and (2) hidden activity normalization (e.g., LayerNorm) to promote activation orthogonality. Experiments scale to CIFAR-100 with ResNet-18 and other architectures.

Result: FA error signals exhibit substantially lower effective rank than BP. Muon and hidden activity normalization both increase the effective dimensionality of FA updates. On CIFAR-100 with ResNet-18, these methods yield a 9-percentage-point accuracy gain over FA baselines, significantly closing the performance gap with BP.

Conclusion: Low-dimensional gradient dynamics are a key obstacle to scaling FA. Inducing higher-dimensional update geometry — via orthogonalising optimizers or activation normalisation — is a promising direction for developing biologically plausible alternatives to backpropagation that scale to deep architectures.

Abstract: Backpropagation (BP) is widely viewed as biologically implausible, in part because it requires feedback weights to be the transpose of forward weights for error propagation. Interestingly, when training a network with fixed random feedback weights to circumvent this issue, learning aligns the forward weights with the feedback weights, leading the backpropagated error signal to become an approximation of the standard gradient used by BP. This process, called Feedback Alignment (FA), occurs in MLPs and very shallow CNNs but does not scale well to deeper architectures.
