In this work, we first investigated differences between BP and FA models, trained on CIFAR10, specifically focusing on the effective rank of the signal. We found that the FA error has a considerably lower rank and hence is constrained to a lower-dimensional subspace compared to BP, limiting exploration of the parameter space. Motivated by this observation, we evaluated two mechanisms for increasing the effective dimensionality of FA: Muon, an optimiser that orthogonalises weight updates; and hidden activity normalisation, which promotes activation orthogonality. Across larger architectures and benchmarks, we find that these methods consistently improve over FA baselines, for example, on CIFAR100 with a Resnet-18, accuracy increases by 9 percentage points. Our results identify low-dimensional gradient dynamics as a key obstacle to scaling FA and suggest that inducing higher-dimensional update geometry is a promising route toward scaling alternatives to backpropagation.

</details>


### [342] [Robust Regression of General ReLUs with Queries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11130)
*Ilias Diakonikolas,Daniel M. Kane,Mingchen Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents the first computationally efficient interactive learner for general (non-homogeneous) ReLUs under Gaussian marginals, achieving squared loss error O(opt)+ε using only d·polylog(1/ε) + Õ(min{1/p, 1/ε}) label queries. The query complexity is shown to be nearly optimal, and query access is proven essentially necessary to surpass passive learning label complexity. In pool-based active learning, any learner needs Ω̃(d/ε) labels absent super-polynomial unlabeled samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Passive learning of general ReLUs under the Gaussian distribution requires poly(d, 1/ε) labeled examples. The authors investigate whether interactive learning (via label queries) can dramatically reduce label complexity—aiming for poly(d, log(1/ε)) rather than poly(d, 1/ε)—while maintaining computational efficiency and near-optimal error bounds.

Method: The paper proposes an interactive learning algorithm leveraging black-box label queries. The learner actively queries labels on adaptively chosen unlabeled examples, exploiting the structural properties of ReLU activations under Gaussian inputs. The method achieves d·polylog(1/ε) dependence plus an additive term Õ(min{1/p, 1/ε}) tied to the target ReLU's bias p. Lower bounds are established via information-theoretic arguments, demonstrating query complexity near-optimality and the necessity of query access for label complexity improvements over passive learning.

Result: Main algorithmic result: a computationally efficient interactive learner achieving error O(opt)+ε with d·polylog(1/ε) + Õ(min{1/p, 1/ε}) label queries. Lower bound: query complexity is qualitatively near-optimal. Additionally, for pool-based active learning, any learner requires Ω̃(d/ε) labels unless it draws a super-polynomial number of unlabeled examples, establishing that query access is essentially necessary to surpass passive label complexity.

Conclusion: Interactive learning with label queries enables exponential improvement in label complexity (in terms of 1/ε) over passive learning for general ReLUs under Gaussian marginals, while maintaining computational efficiency. The query complexity is nearly optimal. Query access is fundamentally necessary for this improvement, as pool-based active learning cannot achieve better label complexity without prohibitive unlabeled sample sizes.

Abstract: We study the task of agnostically learning general (as opposed to homogeneous) ReLUs under the Gaussian distribution with respect to the squared loss. In the passive learning setting, recent work gave a computationally efficient algorithm that uses $poly(d,1/ε)$ labeled examples and outputs a hypothesis with error $O(opt)+ε$, where $opt$ is the squared loss of the best fit ReLU. Here we focus on the interactive setting, where the learner has some form of query access to the labels of unlabeled examples. Our main result is the first computationally efficient learner that uses $d polylog(1/ε)+\tilde{O}(\min\{1/p, 1/ε\})$ black-box label queries, where $p$ is the bias of the target function, and achieves error $O(opt)+ε$. We complement our algorithmic result by showing that its query complexity bound is qualitatively near-optimal, even ignoring computational constraints. Finally, we establish that query access is essentially necessary to improve on the label complexity of passive learning. Specifically, for pool-based active learning, any active learner requires $\tildeΩ(d/ε)$ labels, unless it draws a super-polynomial number of unlabeled examples.

</details>


### [343] [First-Order Trajectory Matching: Fast Ensemble Predictions of Chaotic, Turbulent, Stochastic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11138)
*Shreya Jha,Timo Schorlepp,Nicholas Geissler,Jules Berman,Benjamin Peherstorfer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FTM learns the first-order probability current velocity directly from stochastic trajectories, matching time marginals and capturing flux/circulation/barrier-crossing quantities, with simulation-free one-step training that is stable under proper discretization-variance balance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing surrogate models for stochastic dynamical systems either estimate drift/diffusion/score separately or fail to capture current-like trajectory quantities (fluxes, circulations, barrier-crossing currents). The authors aim for a method that directly learns probability transport from trajectories while preserving ensemble averages and trajectory-aware statistics, at low rollout cost.

Method: FTM learns the symmetric first-order motion of trajectories to match the probability current velocity. The current velocity is learned directly from trajectory data without explicit drift, diffusion, or score estimation. The method uses a simulation-free one-step loss. A stability analysis is provided that decouples discretization error from sampling variance, identifying conditions for stability based on temporal resolution and sample size.

Result: Across stochastic dynamical systems and PDE examples, FTM empirically demonstrates trajectory-aware ensemble predictions at the computational cost of deterministic rollouts. The stability analysis confirms that the one-step simulation-free loss remains stable when temporal resolution and sample size are properly balanced.

Conclusion: FTM offers an efficient surrogate-modeling approach that captures both time-marginal ensemble averages and current-like trajectory quantities, achieving deterministic-rollout cost with trajectory-aware fidelity, supported by theoretical stability guarantees.

Abstract: We introduce First-Order Trajectory Matching (FTM), a surrogate-modeling method that learns the first-order local transport of probability mass from trajectories of stochastic systems. By matching the symmetric first-order motion of trajectories, FTM learns the probability current velocity, whose flow preserves time marginals to match ensemble averages, while also capturing current-like trajectory quantities such as fluxes, circulations, and barrier-crossing currents. FTM learns the current velocity directly from trajectories, avoiding drift, diffusion, and score estimation. Our stability analysis separates discretization error from sampling variance and shows that the one-step simulation-free FTM loss is stable when temporal resolution and sample size are properly balanced. Across stochastic dynamical systems and PDE examples, we empirically demonstrate that FTM provides trajectory-aware ensemble predictions at low, deterministic-rollout cost.

</details>


### [344] [OncoTraj: a public benchmark for longitudinal resistance prediction in EGFR-mutant non-small-cell lung cancer on osimertinib](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11144)
*Abhijoy Sarkar,Aarchi Singh Thakur*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OncoTraj is a public benchmark of 813 EGFR-mutant NSCLC patients on first-line osimertinib, defining three prediction tasks (progression classification, time-to-progression regression, resistance-mechanism classification). All models fail to exceed chance due to the limitation of single-timepoint tissue NGS input, establishing a modality ceiling that motivates a serial-ctDNA-enriched v2.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite osimertinib resistance in EGFR-mutant NSCLC being a canonical example of predictable clonal evolution under therapy, no public benchmark exists for training or evaluating computational models on longitudinal patient trajectories. The authors aim to fill this gap with a rigorously curated, leakage-audited resource.

Method: OncoTraj harmonizes data from three real-world clinical-genomic sources (MSK-CHORD, AACR GENIE BPC, FLAURA supplement) into 813 patients. It defines three locked tasks: (A) binary 12-month progression classification, (B) time-to-progression regression, (C) six-class resistance-mechanism classification. Six baselines are provided (majority-class, logistic regression, random forest, XGBoost, LSTM, multi-task transformer). The benchmark uses patient-level train/val/test splits with a verified no-leakage guarantee.

Result: No model cleared chance performance on within-source evaluation, indicating a uniform ceiling attributable to the input modality (single-timepoint tissue NGS) rather than algorithmic limitations. The benchmark did recover a known literature association: TP53 co-mutation raises 12-month progression rate from 29% to 59%.

Conclusion: OncoTraj establishes a reproducible benchmark demonstrating that single-snapshot tissue NGS is insufficient for predicting osimertinib resistance outcomes. The modality limit translates into concrete design requirements for a serial-ctDNA-enriched v2, providing a foundation for future computational modeling of clonal evolution under therapy.

Abstract: Resistance to first-line osimertinib in EGFR-mutant non-small-cell lung cancer (NSCLC) is the canonical example of predictable clonal evolution under therapeutic pressure, yet no public benchmark exists for training or evaluating computational models on the corresponding longitudinal patient trajectories. We introduce OncoTraj, a public benchmark of 813 EGFR-mutant NSCLC patients receiving first-line osimertinib, harmonized from three real-world clinical-genomic sources: MSK-CHORD (672 patients), AACR Project GENIE BPC NSCLC (34 patients), and the FLAURA molecular-resistance supplement (107 patients). OncoTraj defines three locked tasks: (A) binary classification of progression by a fixed 12-month landmark, (B) regression of time-to-first-progression in days, and (C) six-class classification of the dominant resistance mechanism. We release the harmonized dataset, patient-level train/validation/test splits with an audited no-leakage guarantee, an open-source evaluation harness, and six reference baselines spanning a majority-class predictor, logistic regression, random forest, XGBoost, an LSTM, and a multi-task transformer. With v1's single-timepoint snapshot features, no task clears chance on clean within-source evaluation: the uniformity of this ceiling across every model class localizes the limit to the input modality (single-snapshot tissue NGS rather than serial ctDNA), not the algorithm. The benchmark does recover a reproducible literature-consistent association: TP53 co-mutation raises the 12-month progression rate from 29% to 59% cohort-wide. OncoTraj establishes a reproducible, leakage-audited baseline and converts the modality limit into concrete design requirements for a serial-ctDNA-enriched v2.

</details>


### [345] [Efficiently Learning Drifting Halfspaces with Massart Noise](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11149)
*Mingchen Ma,Guyang Cao,Jelena Diakonikolas,Ilias Diakonikolas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies online learning of drifting halfspaces under Massart noise, proposing an efficient algorithm achieving error η + Õ(Δ^(1/3)/γ), where Δ is drift rate and γ is margin. They show this Δ^(1/3) scaling is essentially optimal for efficient algorithms via low-degree polynomial lower bounds, despite the information-theoretic optimum being Δ^(1/2).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world learning problems often involve both noisy labels and concept drift over time. While prior work studied these in isolation, the interplay between Massart noise and drifting concepts for fundamental classes like halfspaces was not well understood, particularly regarding computational vs. information-theoretic tradeoffs.

Method: The authors design a computationally efficient online learner for margin-separable halfspaces. Their algorithm leverages techniques for handling Massart noise and adapts them to the drifting setting, achieving an error bound expressed in terms of noise rate η, drift rate Δ, and margin γ. For lower bounds, they employ the framework of low-degree polynomial tests to establish that Δ^(1/3)-scaling is unavoidable for efficient algorithms, even under the simpler random classification noise model.

Result: Upper bound: an efficient learner with error η + Õ(Δ^(1/3)/γ). In the realizable (noise-free) setting, their techniques also improve over prior error rates. Lower bound: any low-degree polynomial test incurs Δ^(1/3) error scaling, indicating an information-computation gap, since information-theoretically Δ^(1/2) is achievable.

Conclusion: There exists a provable information-computation tradeoff in drifting halfspace learning under Massart noise: achieving the optimal Δ^(1/2) scaling appears to require exponential computation, while the authors' Δ^(1/3) algorithm is essentially optimal among efficient methods. This yields the first such separation for this fundamental problem.

Abstract: We study the problem of learning a drifting concept in the presence of Massart noise. In this framework, an online learner has access to a history of independent samples whose labels are noisy versions of a target concept that may change from round to round. The goal is to output, in each round, a hypothesis with small prediction error. We study the complexity of this learning problem for the fundamental class of margin-separable linear classifiers (halfspaces). On the positive side, we give a computationally efficient learner achieving error $η+ \tilde O(Δ^{1/3}/γ)$, where $η$ upper bounds the Massart noise rate, $Δ$ is the drift rate, and $γ$ is the margin. Interestingly, in the realizable setting, an adaptation of our techniques yields an efficient learner with an improved error rate over prior work. On the lower-bound side, we provide formal evidence of an information-computation tradeoff, strongly suggesting that our algorithm's performance is essentially optimal. Specifically, while the information-theoretically optimal error scales with $Δ^{1/2}$, we prove that $Δ^{1/3}$-scaling is unavoidable for low-degree polynomial tests, even in the special case of random classification noise.

</details>


### [346] [COGENT: Continuous Graph Emulators with Neural Ordinary Differential Equations for Long-Term Physical Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11162)
*Zesheng Liu,Maryam Rahnemoonfar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: COGENT is a continuous graph neural ODE framework for long-term physical forecasting on irregular geospatial meshes. It encodes system history into latent states, evolves them via a Neural ODE driven by future forcings, and decodes predictions at arbitrary times, achieving improved long-range stability over autoregressive baselines on ice-sheet simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional autoregressive graph-based forecasting models suffer from error accumulation and instability over long horizons, and are restricted to fixed temporal discretizations. There is a need for a continuous-time, mesh-based emulator that can stably predict physical system states at arbitrary future times, particularly for applications like ice-sheet dynamics modeling.

Method: COGENT combines (1) a graph-based history encoder that processes finite histories of system states, forcing fields, and external forcings into node-wise context vectors; (2) a latent Neural ODE initialized and conditioned by these context vectors, driven by interpolated future forcings and explicit relative rollout time; and (3) a residual decoder mapping latent trajectories to future states. Training is stabilized via rollout-horizon sampling and progressive rollout-horizon scheduling strategies.

Result: Evaluated on transient ice-sheet simulations from the Ice-sheet and Sea-level System Model (ISSM), COGENT demonstrates improved long-range stability over autoregressive graph baselines, enabling stable multi-step forecasting without iterative error accumulation.

Conclusion: Continuous graph Neural ODEs offer a promising methodology for scalable physical forecasting on irregular geospatial meshes, especially where stable long-horizon predictions and arbitrary-time querying of system states are required.

Abstract: In this work, we present COGENT, a continuous graph emulator with Neural Ordinary Differential Equations for long-term physical forecasting on irregular geospatial meshes. COGENT encodes a finite history of system states and associated forcing fields and external forcings with a graph-based history encoder, producing node-wise context vectors that capture both local spatial interactions and temporal evolution. These context vectors initialize and condition a latent Neural Ordinary Differential Equation whose dynamics are driven by interpolated future forcings and explicit relative rollout time. By modeling the forecast trajectory as a continuous latent dynamical system, COGENT can generate predictions at arbitrary future times rather than being restricted to a fixed temporal discretization. A residual decoder maps the resulting latent trajectories back to future physical states, enabling direct multi-step forecasting without repeatedly feeding predicted states back into the model. This formulation combines graph-based spatial representation, history-conditioned latent dynamics, and continuous-time rollout in a unified framework for mesh-based physical simulation emulation. In order to stabilize training with long-horizon supervision, we also propose effective rollout-horizon sampling and a progressive rollout-horizon scheduling strategy. We evaluate COGENT on transient ice-sheet simulations generated by the Ice-sheet and Sea-level System Model, demonstrating improved long-range stability over autoregressive graph baselines. These results suggest that continuous graph Neural ODEs provide a promising methodology for scalable physical forecasting on irregular geospatial meshes, particularly in applications that require stable long-horizon predictions and the ability to query system states at arbitrary times.

</details>


### [347] [Algorithmic and Minimax Complexities in Kernel Bandits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11171)
*Yunbei Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper unifies GP-UCB and DEC/MAMS approaches to kernel bandits under a common algorithmic-information framework (MAIR), showing that algorithmic complexity can be more informative than minimax or DEC certificates in overparameterized settings, and proposes a hybrid "safeguarded master" algorithm combining both advantages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GP-UCB and DEC-based methods appear theoretically distinct. The authors seek to reconcile these two perspectives within a unified framework for frequentist RKHS bandits, and to demonstrate that algorithmic (trajectory-based) complexity measures can reveal gaps invisible to class-wide minimax or DEC certificates, particularly in overparameterized models.

Method: The paper employs a unified MAIR (Maximum Achievable Information Ratio) framework with heterogeneous positive-semidefinite algorithmic priors. This generalizes both the GP-UCB analysis (which uses an algorithmic, rather than true, Gaussian-process prior and exploits realized-trajectory complexity) and the MAMS algorithm (which optimizes a robust class-wide MAIR/DEC envelope). A kernel-bandit construction is provided to illustrate the theoretical distinctions.

Result: The paper generalizes both GP-UCB analysis and the MAMS algorithm; proposes a safeguarded master algorithm that combines their respective advantages; and demonstrates via a kernel-bandit construction that algorithmic complexity can be strictly more informative than class-wide minimax or DEC certificates in overparameterized models.

Conclusion: Algorithmic information and class-wide minimax coefficients answer fundamentally different questions and can yield different gaps. Kernel bandits provide a mathematically clean setting where this distinction becomes visible, suggesting that practitioners should consider trajectory-level algorithmic complexity alongside traditional minimax guarantees.

Abstract: Gaussian-process upper confidence bound (GP-UCB) and decision-estimation-coefficient (DEC) methods may appear, at first sight, to belong to different theories. This paper places the two viewpoints in a common algorithmic-information language for frequentist RKHS bandits. GP-UCB fixes an algorithmic, rather than true, Gaussian-process prior and exploits realized-trajectory complexity together with computational tractability, whereas MAMS optimizes a robust class-wide MAIR/DEC envelope. Through the unified MAIR framework and heterogeneous positive-semidefinite algorithmic priors, we generalize both the GP-UCB analysis and the MAMS algorithm, propose a safeguarded master that combines their advantages, and provide a kernel-bandit construction showing that algorithmic complexity can be more informative than class-wide minimax or DEC certificates in overparameterized models. The resulting message is that algorithmic information and class-wide minimax coefficients answer different questions and can lead to different gaps; kernel bandits provide a clean setting in which this distinction becomes mathematically visible.

</details>


### [348] [Predicting Future Behaviors in Reasoning Models Enables Better Steering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11172)
*Evgenii Kortukov,Piotr Komorowski,Florian Klein,Paula Engl,Gabriele Sarti,Seong Joon Oh,Sebastian Lapuschkin,Wojciech Samek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Large reasoning models exhibit unexpected behaviors. The paper distinguishes between detection features (identifying behaviors in already-generated text) and prediction features (forecasting future behaviors from intermediate steps). By training probes on prediction features (64-91% accuracy), the authors introduce Future Probe Controlled Generation (FPCG), a text-level steering method that selects among candidate sentences to control behavior with minimal output quality degradation, succeeding where activation steering fails.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing test-time steering methods for LRMs intervene on hidden representations but degrade output quality. The authors identify a fundamental flaw: prior work targets detection features that recognize behaviors in already-generated text, which are poor predictors of future behavioral outcomes. A better intervention target is needed.

Method: The authors first train activation probes to predict future behavior likelihood from intermediate reasoning steps, achieving 64-91% accuracy and revealing internal prediction features distinct from detection features. Building on this, they propose Future Probe Controlled Generation (FPCG): at inference time, multiple candidate sentences are sampled, and a probe scores each based on predicted future behavior likelihood. The sentence minimizing (or maximizing) the target behavior likelihood is selected, enabling steering without direct activation intervention.

Result: Prediction probes forecast the most likely future behavior with 64-91% accuracy. FPCG enables effective steering with almost no output quality degradation, and succeeds in several evaluation settings where activation steering fails.

Conclusion: Distinguishing between detection and prediction features enables a more nuanced and effective approach to controlling LRM behaviors. FPCG demonstrates that text-level intervention guided by prediction probes outperforms activation-level steering in both output quality preservation and steering reliability.

Abstract: Deployed large reasoning models (LRMs) often behave unexpectedly. Test-time steering controls LRM outputs by intervening on their hidden representations, but it can degrade output quality. We argue that prior steering work implicitly relies on internal features that detect behavior in already generated text. We show that these detection features are poor predictors of future behavioral outcomes, and thus not the natural intervention target. Instead, we train activation probes to predict future behavior likelihoods from intermediate reasoning steps. These probes predict the most likely behavior with 64%-91% accuracy, revealing a separate type of internal prediction features. Building on these prediction features, we introduce a text-level steering method, Future Probe Controlled Generation. FPCG samples multiple candidate sentences and chooses the best one according to a probe predicting the future behavior likelihood. This enables steering with almost no output quality degradation. FPCG also enables steering in several evaluations where activation steering fails. These results show that distinguishing detection and prediction features enables a more nuanced approach to controlling LRM behaviors.

</details>


### [349] [EEVEE: Towards Test-time Prompt Learning in the Real World for Self-Improving Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11182)
*Weixian Xu,Shilong Liu,Mengdi Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EEVEE is a multi-dataset test-time prompt learning framework for LLM agents that uses a router to cluster heterogeneous input streams and co-evolves routing with prompt configurations, achieving substantial gains over baselines on multi-benchmark evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing test-time prompt learning methods are limited to single-dataset settings, whereas real-world applications involve heterogeneous input streams across multiple datasets, domains, and task distributions. This gap causes cross-dataset interference and limits practical applicability, motivating a framework that can handle diverse, streaming inputs without sacrificing single-benchmark performance.

Method: EEVEE introduces a router that partitions incoming inputs into task clusters and assigns each cluster to a suitable prompt configuration. The core innovation is a router-prompt co-evolution strategy: interleaved phases of router learning and prompt learning that jointly optimize both components to resolve their mutual dependency. This enables dynamic adaptation to heterogeneous data streams at test time.

Result: EEVEE improves average multi-benchmark scores by 10.38 points over Qwen3-4B-Instruct and 24.32 points over DeepSeek-V3.2. It surpasses state-of-the-art methods GEPA and ACE by up to 37.2% and 48.2%, respectively, while maintaining single-benchmark learning capability and efficiency.

Conclusion: EEVEE demonstrates that a router-prompt co-evolution approach effectively addresses cross-dataset interference in test-time prompt learning, enabling robust performance under real-world heterogeneous task streams and significantly outperforming both base models and existing SOTA methods.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose EEVEE, the first multi-dataset test-time prompt learning framework for LLM agents, enabling test-time prompt learning under real-world task streams. Existing methods are largely designed for single-dataset settings, while real-world applications require models to handle heterogeneous input streams drawn from multiple datasets, domains, and task distributions, limiting their practical applicability. To mitigate cross-dataset interference, EEVEE introduces a router that partitions incoming inputs into task clusters and assigns them to suitable prompt configurations. This design is optimized via a router-prompt co-evolution strategy, which employs interleaved router and prompt learning phases to address their mutual dependency. Experiments across multiple datasets demonstrate that the framework improves robustness under heterogeneous data streams while maintaining single-benchmark learning capability and efficiency. Specifically, EEVEE improves average multi-benchmark scores by 10.38 and 24.32 points over Qwen3-4B-Instruct and DeepSeek-V3.2, surpassing SOTA methods GEPA and ACE by up to 37.2% and 48.2%.

</details>


### [350] [When to Align, When to Predict: A Phase Diagram for Multimodal Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11190)
*Ilay Kamai,Hugues Van Assel,Aviv Regev,Hagai B. Perets,Randall Balestriero*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper develops a unified linear framework to analyze when cross-modal alignment (CA) and cross-modal prediction (CP) succeed or fail in multimodal representation learning. Under a spiked signal-plus-noise model with structured nuisance correlation, it derives separation ratios that reveal complementary failure modes: CA whitens modalities and fails under strong cross-modal nuisance correlation; CP performs one-sided whitening governed by source-modality quality. This yields a four-regime phase diagram (Both, CA only, CP only, Neither), and a data-driven procedure to locate datasets in this diagram using a small labeled subsample, validated on synthetic and real-world data including astrophysics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Practitioners in scientific domains (biomedicine, astrophysics) with heterogeneous instruments and multi-level measurement structures lack a systematic understanding of why standard multimodal methods underperform the best single modality. There is no diagnostic framework to determine whether CA, CP, or any cross-modal training helps for a given dataset.

Method: The authors propose a unified linear framework based on a spiked signal-plus-noise model with structured cross-modal nuisance correlation. They analytically derive separation ratios for both CA and CP objectives, characterizing when the signal of interest can be recovered. The analysis yields a phase diagram partitioning problems into four regimes. Additionally, they introduce a data-driven diagnostic procedure using a small labeled subsample to locate real-world datasets in the phase diagram without full cross-modal training.

Result: The analysis reveals complementary failure modes: CA whitens each modality and fails when nuisance is strongly correlated across views; CP recovers signal governed by source-modality quality through one-sided whitening. Experiments on synthetic data, stereo-vision (KITTI), image-caption pairs (COCO), and astrophysical data validate the phase diagram predictions even in nonlinear regimes, including a Neither regime where cross-modal training is actively harmful.

Conclusion: The framework provides practitioners with a principled way to diagnose multimodal problems and select the optimal objective (alignment, prediction, or neither) before committing to expensive cross-modal training. The data-driven subsample procedure makes this practical for real-world scientific datasets.

Abstract: Cross-modal alignment (CA) and cross-modal prediction (CP) are the dominant paradigms for multimodal representation learning, yet there is no systematic understanding of when each succeeds, when each fails, and when cross-modal training helps at all -- a gap that leaves practitioners, especially in scientific domains like biomedicine or astrophysics, with heterogeneous instruments and multiple levels of organization and measurement, unable to diagnose why standard methods underperform the best single modality. We develop a unified linear framework that addresses both questions. Under a spiked signal-plus-noise model with structured cross-modal nuisance correlation, we derive separation ratios for both objectives that expose complementary failure modes: alignment whitens each modality and fails when nuisance is strongly correlated across views; prediction encodes whatever is cross-predictable through a one-sided whitening, with recovery governed by source-modality quality. The resulting phase diagram partitions multimodal problems into four regimes: Both, CA only, CP only, and Neither. We present a data-driven procedure to locate real-world datasets in this diagram using a small labeled subsample, identifying the preferred objective and prediction direction before any cross-modal training. Experiments on synthetic data, stereo-vision benchmarks, image-caption pairs, and real astrophysical data validate the predictions in the nonlinear regime, including the Neither regime where cross-modal training is actively harmful. Our framework lets practitioners diagnose their multimodal problem and choose the right objective before committing to training. Code to reproduce the results is available at https://github.com/IlayMalinyak/mm_align_vs_pred.

</details>


<div id='cs.MA'></div>

# cs.MA [[Back]](#toc)

### [351] [Failure Modes of Deep Multi-Agent RL in Asynchronous Pricing: Reproducible Triggers, Trace Diagnostics, and a Partial Fix](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09884)
*Shree Murthy,Rohan Pandey*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: Deep multi-agent RL in continuous-time pricing markets exhibits two failure modes: tacit collusion among DDPG agents and actor-critic instability at high event rates. Asynchrony with latency partially mitigates collusion but introduces costs and fails under critic divergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically identify, reproduce, and analyze failure modes in deep MARL for continuous-time pricing markets, and to evaluate whether a microstructure fix (asynchrony with observation latency) can mitigate tacit collusion without introducing new instabilities.

Method: The authors construct a single continuous-time MARL benchmark with Poisson-clocked price updates, observation latency δ, and interior-optimum logit demand. They test synchronous DDPG agents, then introduce asynchrony and latency as a partial microstructure fix. Collusion is measured via a scalar collusion index Δ, and trajectory-level trace diagnostics are used to expose within-episode signaling collapse and post-shock non-recovery. A phase diagram across event rate λ and latency δ maps the interaction of both failure modes.

Result: Synchronous DDPG agents reliably form tacit cartels (Δ = 0.69 ± 0.11). Asynchrony alone reduces collusion by 48%; adding latency drives Δ to a minimum of 0.28. However, the fix is partial (Δ stays supra-Bertrand), non-monotone in δ, and collapses at λ=5 due to Failure Mode 2 (DDPG critic divergence), corrupting the phase-diagram cell at (λ=5, δ=1).

Conclusion: Tacit collusion and high-frequency instability are robust, reproducible pathologies in CT-MARL pricing. Asynchrony with latency offers a partial but fragile remedy — it reduces collusion but is non-monotone, incomplete, and breaks down under critic divergence at elevated event rates. Both failure modes warrant explicit attention in market simulator and mechanism design.

Abstract: We study two reproducible failure modes of deep multi-agent reinforcement learning in continuous-time pricing markets: (i) tacit cartel formation between competing DDPG agents, and (ii) actor--critic instability at high event rates. We instantiate both inside a single CT-MARL benchmark (Poisson-clocked price updates, observation latency $δ$, interior-optimum logit demand), show that synchronous DDPG agents reliably trigger Failure Mode 1 with collusion index $Δ= 0.69 \pm 0.11$, and quantify a partial microstructure fix: asynchrony alone cuts collusion by 48\% and adding latency drives it to a minimum of $Δ= 0.28$. The fix has clearly documented costs: it is partial ($Δ$ remains supra-Bertrand), it is non-monotone in $δ$, and it does not survive Failure Mode 2, which emerges as DDPG critic divergence at $λ= 5$ and corrupts the phase-diagram cell at $(λ{=}5, δ{=}1)$. We accompany the scalar collusion index with trajectory-level trace diagnostics that expose the within-episode signalling collapse and the post-shock non-recovery.

</details>


### [352] [Decoupling Thought from Speech: Knowledge-Grounded Counterfactual Reasoning for Resilient Multi-Agent Argumentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10475)
*Jakub Masłowski,Jarosław A. Chudziak*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: KG-CFR introduces a dual-stage architecture separating a private, retrieval-augmented planning buffer from a public execution layer to prevent identity loss, logic degradation, and argument repetition in multi-agent debates under sustained perturbations, demonstrating over 95% prevention of critical post-shock degradation and significant quality improvement in a dedicated 1v1v1 resource allocation environment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multi-agent debate frameworks prioritize output accuracy over process stability, leading to logic degradation, argument repetition, and role drift during long-horizon exchanges under sustained perturbations. A structural solution is needed to maintain process fidelity and prevent identity loss without sacrificing quality.

Method: The authors propose Knowledge-Grounded Counterfactual Reasoning (KG-CFR), a dual-stage architecture that strictly separates a private, retrieval-augmented planning buffer from a public execution layer. The system is evaluated in a custom 1v1v1 environment called Dynamic Resource Allocation under Uncertainty (DRAU), using 270 factorial crisis simulation trajectories with stochastic environmental shocks. Custom vector metrics for discourse divergence and plan-execution alignment are introduced to measure operational stability.

Result: KG-CFR prevents judge-detected critical post-shock degradation (quality shift Δ ≤ -0.20) in over 95% of perturbed runs and raises overall argument quality from 0.694 to 0.822. Ablations indicate doctrinal grounding is as important as prospective planning for argument quality. The architecture reduces semantic looping by preserving agent consistency with the original plan.

Conclusion: Architectural decoupling between planning and execution is a key factor for systemic resilience under sustained pressure, enabling quality preservation without degradation. The introduced discourse divergence and plan-execution alignment metrics provide directionally consistent evidence of operational stability.

Abstract: Multi-agent debate frameworks have been shown to improve large language model performance in convergent tasks, but they are currently optimized in a way that heavily favors final output accuracy rather than stability of the process. During long-horizon exchanges reactive systems under sustained perturbations often experience logic degradation, argument repetition, and role drift. To structurally prevent the identity loss and maintain the process fidelity, we introduce Knowledge-Grounded Counterfactual Reasoning (KG-CFR), a dual-stage architecture that enforces a strict separation of concerns between a private, retrieval-augmented planning buffer, and a public execution layer. We assess this system in Dynamic Resource Allocation under Uncertainty (DRAU), a dedicated 1v1v1 environment, introducing diversity as distinct from standard debate settings. Over 270 completely factorial crisis simulation trajectories with stochastic environmental shocks, KG-CFR prevents judge-detected critical post-shock degradation (defined as a quality shift, $Δ\le -0.20$) in more than 95% of perturbed runs, increasing the overall argument quality from 0.694 to 0.822. Our primary contribution is the demonstration of architectural decoupling being an important factor of systemic resilience enhancement under sustained pressure without quality loss. Furthermore, we introduce custom vector metrics for discourse divergence and plan-execution alignment that provide strong, directionally consistent evidence of operational stability. Our ablation experiments suggest that the proper doctrinal grounding can be an equally important factor for argument quality, as the prospective planning. KG-CFR, according to our initial metric evaluations, reduces semantic looping, by preserving the agent's consistency with the original plan.

</details>


### [353] [SkillAxe: Sharpening LLM-Authored Agent Skills Through Evaluation-Guided Self-Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10546)
*Srishti Gautam,Arjun Radhakrishna,Sumit Gulwani*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: SkillAxe is an unsupervised framework that enables LLMs to iteratively diagnose and refine their own agent skills by decomposing skill quality into four dimensions, yielding 28% relative improvement on SkillsBench and raising pass rates from 16% to 52% on SpreadsheetBench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents depend on skill documents (structured natural-language instructions), but LLMs are poor at authoring effective skills. Human-authored skills substantially improve agent performance, while LLM-authored skills offer negligible gains. There is a need for automated, unsupervised skill improvement without requiring ground-truth labels, test suites, or environment rewards.

Method: SkillAxe is a fully unsupervised framework that decomposes skill quality into four interpretable dimensions: (1) quality impact, (2) trigger precision, (3) instruction compliance with fault attribution, and (4) solution-path coverage. It produces structured improvement briefs that guide iterative self-refinement of skills, requiring no labels, test suites, or reward signals.

Result: On SkillsBench, SkillAxe improves pass rates by 28% relative over unimproved LLM skills and closes 47–67% of the gap to human-authored skills. On SpreadsheetBench, SkillAxe builds a skill library from past agent trajectories, raising pass rates from 16.0% to 52.0% using only 22 skills.

Conclusion: SkillAxe provides a practical, unsupervised mechanism for LLMs to continuously improve their own skill documents, substantially narrowing the gap between LLM-authored and human-authored skills and demonstrating real-world efficacy as a continuous improvement engine.

Abstract: Skill documents, structured natural-language instructions that guide Large Language Model (LLM) agents, are critical to modern agent frameworks, yet LLMs struggle to write skills that actually work. On SkillsBench, human-authored skills improve pass rates by 16.2 percentage points, while LLM-authored skills provide no measurable gain. We introduce SkillAxe, a fully unsupervised framework that enables LLMs to iteratively diagnose and refine their own skills. SkillAxe decomposes skill quality into four interpretable dimensions (quality impact, trigger precision, instruction compliance with fault attribution, and solution-path coverage), producing structured improvement briefs that require no ground-truth labels, test suites, or environment rewards. On SkillsBench, SkillAxe improves pass rates by 28\% relative over unimproved LLM skills and closes 47--67\% of the gap to human-authored skills. We validate the approach as a continuous improvement engine in the wild on SpreadsheetBench, where a SkillAxe-built skill library learns from past agent trajectories and raises pass rate from 16.0\% to 52.0\% using only 22 skills.

</details>


### [354] [Decentralized Multi-Agent Systems with Shared Context](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10662)
*Yuzhen Mao,Azalia Mirhoseini*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: DeLM proposes a decentralized multi-agent framework for LLMs that replaces centralized orchestration with a shared verified context and task queue, enabling agents to asynchronously collaborate without a bottleneck controller. It achieves state-of-the-art results on SWE-bench Verified and LongBench-v2 while halving costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-agent systems for LLM reasoning rely on centralized orchestration, where a main agent assigns tasks and merges results. This central controller becomes a communication and integration bottleneck as the number of subtasks scales, limiting both efficiency and scalability of test-time reasoning.

Method: DeLM introduces a decentralized MAS framework with three core components: (1) parallel agents that asynchronously claim subtasks from a shared queue, (2) a shared verified context that serves as a common communication substrate where agents read accumulated progress and write back compact verified updates, and (3) a task queue for work distribution. Agents build on one another's verified progress without routing updates through a central controller.

Result: On SWE-bench Verified, DeLM achieves best performance across Avg.@1, Pass@2, and Pass@4, improving up to 10.5 percentage points over the strongest baseline while reducing cost per task by ~50%. On LongBench-v2 Multi-Doc QA, DeLM achieves the highest average accuracy across four frontier model families, improving over the strongest baseline by up to 5.7 percentage points.

Conclusion: Decentralized coordination via a shared verified context and asynchronous task claiming effectively eliminates the central controller bottleneck, enabling more scalable and cost-efficient multi-agent LLM reasoning at test time.

Abstract: Multi-agent systems (MAS) can scale large language model reasoning at test time by decomposing complex problems into parallel subtasks. However, most existing MAS rely on centralized orchestration, where a main agent assigns work, collects outputs, and merges results. As the number of subtasks grows, this controller becomes a communication and integration bottleneck. We propose Decentralized Language Models (DeLM), a MAS framework that decentralizes coordination through parallel agents, a shared verified context, and a task queue. Agents asynchronously claim subtasks, read accumulated progress, perform local reasoning, and write back compact verified updates. The shared context acts as a common communication substrate, enabling agents to build on one another's verified progress without routing every update through a central controller. Empirically, DeLM improves both software-engineering test-time scaling and long-context reasoning. On SWE-bench Verified, DeLM achieves the best performance across Avg.@1, Pass@2, and Pass@4, with gains of up to 10.5 percentage points over the strongest baseline, while reducing cost per task by roughly 50%. On LongBench-v2 Multi-Doc QA, DeLM achieves the highest average accuracy across four frontier model families, improving over the strongest baseline by up to 5.7 percentage points. The code is available on our project website at https://yuzhenmao.github.io/DeLM/.

</details>


### [355] [LLM-Mediated Demand Response Coordination in Smart Microgrids](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11050)
*J. de Curtò,I. de Zarzà*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: A multi-agent simulation shows that structured LLM-compiled directives, combined with a hybrid agent architecture separating strategic reasoning from narrative evaluation, improve demand-response cooperation in smart microgrids. Network topology-aware targeting amplifies effects, offering design principles for scalable energy coordination.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Demand response in smart microgrids is a social coordination problem akin to a repeated Prisoner's Dilemma. LLMs exhibit cooperation bias from RLHF alignment when used directly as decision-makers, producing unrealistic flat dynamics. A method is needed to leverage LLM capabilities for coordination signals without this bias, while generating realistic heterogeneous prosumer behavior.

Method: A multi-agent simulation with an LLM Influence Compiler issuing structured demand-response directives to heterogeneous prosumer agents. Agents use a hybrid decision architecture: game-theoretic base probability (payoff history, neighbor imitation, exploitation memory) combined with LLM narrative evaluation of coordination signals. Six personality archetypes are modeled. Three intervention conditions are compared: structured compiled directives, unstructured messaging, and no-intervention baseline. Dissemination strategies (hub-targeted, peripheral, random) are tested across grounded and idealized agent substrates and varying resistance levels (R=0.1–0.7).

Result: Structured compiled directives achieved 33.3% demand-curtailment cooperation vs. 27.0% for unstructured messaging and 28.0% for no-intervention (Δ_comp = +0.063). The advantage persisted across grounded and idealized substrates (Δ = +0.083) and all resistance levels. Hub-targeted dissemination via high-centrality nodes outperformed peripheral or random targeting, confirming network topology provides mechanistic amplification independent of message content. Baseline cooperation was near 50% with clear differentiation across personality archetypes.

Conclusion: Structured LLM compilation, grounded agent reasoning (separating strategic from narrative evaluation), and network-aware targeting are complementary design principles for scalable, interpretable demand-response coordination in smart-city energy systems.

Abstract: Effective demand response in smart microgrids requires prosumers to cooperate voluntarily under strategic self-interest, a coordination problem structurally equivalent to a repeated Prisoner's Dilemma on a social network. This paper presents a multi-agent simulation in which a Large Language Model (LLM) Influence Compiler issues structured demand-response directives to a population of heterogeneous prosumer agents, each governed by a hybrid decision architecture combining game-theoretic base probability (derived from payoff history, neighbour imitation, and exploitation memory) with LLM narrative evaluation of incoming coordination signals. The hybrid architecture resolves a key methodological challenge: LLMs aligned via Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) exhibit strong cooperation bias when used as direct decision-makers, producing flat dynamics regardless of grid conditions. By separating strategic reasoning from grounded narrative evaluation, the model generates realistic prosumer behaviour across six personality archetypes, with baseline cooperation near 50% and clear differentiation under influence. Compiled structured directives achieve 33.3% demand-curtailment cooperation versus 27.0% for unstructured messaging and 28.0% for a no-intervention baseline ($Δ_\mathrm{comp} = +0.063$), with the advantage preserved across both grounded and idealized agent substrates ($Δ= +0.083$) and across all resistance levels ($R = 0.1$ to $0.7$). Hub-targeted dissemination via high-centrality network nodes outperforms peripheral or random targeting, confirming that grid topology provides mechanistic amplification independent of message content. These results suggest that structured LLM compilation, grounded agent reasoning, and network-aware targeting are complementary design principles for scalable, interpretable demand-response coordination in smart-city energy systems.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [356] [Business World Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10044)
*Cecil Pang,Hiroki Sayama*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces the Business World Model (BWM), an architecture that integrates semantic data, probabilistic ML, deterministic rules, and explicit action spaces into an executable internal simulator enabling AI systems to move from instruction-following to goal-driven autonomous planning in business environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI-enabled business tools focus on automating predefined tasks. The authors argue that AI's transformative potential lies in enabling systems to plan, optimize, and execute initiatives from high-level strategic objectives — requiring a world model specialized for business contexts that can simulate outcomes, evaluate trade-offs, and support autonomous decision-making under uncertainty.

Method: Proposes a business-semantics-centric formulation linking states, dynamics, and actions to key business entities. The BWM architecture integrates four components: (1) semantic data representations, (2) probabilistic machine learning models, (3) deterministic business rules, and (4) explicit action space definitions — organized as a coherent internal simulator that supports simulation of action sequences, counterfactual reasoning, and trade-off evaluation.

Result: The paper delivers a conceptual architecture and formulation, not empirical results. The contribution is the novel organization of existing techniques into an executable business world model framework that enables goal-driven planning and execution for autonomous business systems.

Conclusion: The BWM establishes a conceptual foundation for next-generation autonomous business systems, bridging the gap between instruction-based AI execution and strategic, goal-driven planning. While individual components are not novel, their integration into a unified business simulator represents a meaningful architectural contribution.

Abstract: Businesses are increasingly adopting AI-enabled tools to improve productivity, reduce costs, and enhance products and services. However, the transformative potential of AI extends beyond automating predefined tasks: it lies in enabling intelligent systems to plan, optimize, and execute business initiatives from high-level strategic objectives. This paper introduces the concept and architecture of a business world model (BWM), a world model specialized for business and organizational environments. Inspired by world models in artificial intelligence, cognitive science, and control theory, a BWM encodes business states, dynamics, constraints, objectives, and feasible action space to support autonomous decision-making. We propose a business-semantics-centric formulation in which business states, dynamics and actions are linked to key business entities. Within this framework, agents can simulate alternative action sequences, estimate their effects on future business outcomes, and evaluate trade-offs under uncertainty. The proposed architecture integrates semantic data representations, probabilistic machine learning models, deterministic business rules, and explicit action space into a coherent structure for planning and counterfactual reasoning. Although its individual components are not new, the contribution of BWM lies in organizing them as an executable internal simulator for business initiatives. This work establishes a conceptual foundation for autonomous business systems capable of moving from instruction-based execution toward goal-driven planning and execution.

</details>


### [357] [Deployment-Time Memorization in Foundation-Model Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10062)
*Lei,Chen,Guilin Zhang,Kai Zhao,Dalmo Cirne,Andy Olsen,Xu Chu,Zeke Miller,Alet Blanken,Amine Anoun,Jerry Ting*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper re-conceptualizes agent memory as a "deployment-time memorization" mechanism, introducing a privacy-utility framework measured by Personalization Recall (PR), Adversarial Extraction Rate (AER), and Forgetting Residue Score (FRS). Through sweeping summarization aggressiveness, retrieval breadth, and deletion mode, the authors show that aggressive summarization dramatically reduces canary extraction while preserving personalization, but creates deletion-fidelity failures where deleted raw data remains recoverable from derived summaries unless full-pipeline purge or tombstone redaction is applied.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As foundation-model agents become long-lived systems that remember users across interactions, memorization shifts from being solely a property of model weights (parametric memorization) to an explicit deployment-time function. Existing work focuses on parametric memorization or audits fixed memory configurations, but fails to characterize how memory-design choices jointly shape personalization utility, adversarial extraction risk, and deletion fidelity — a critical gap as agents are deployed in production with persistent user memory.

Method: The authors formulate agent memory as a privacy-utility frontier using two metrics: Personalization Recall (PR) measuring how well the agent recalls user-specific information, and Adversarial Extraction Rate (AER) measuring how readily an adversary can extract sensitive facts. They sweep three memory-design knobs: (1) summarization aggressiveness (from raw storage to progressively compressed summaries), (2) retrieval breadth k (number of chunks retrieved), and (3) deletion mode (raw-only, summary-only, full-pipeline purge, tombstone redaction). They introduce Forgetting Residue Score (FRS) to quantify whether deleted information remains recoverable from derived memory tiers. Experiments are conducted on LongMemEval using Gemma 3 12B and GPT-4o-mini.

Result: Key results: (1) Aggressive key-fact summarization reduces canary extraction by 76% on Gemma 3 12B and 64% on GPT-4o-mini while preserving nearly all personalization recall. (2) Once content is compressed away via summarization, increasing retrieval breadth k no longer restores leakage — compression is an effective extraction barrier. (3) A critical deletion-fidelity failure emerges: raw-only deletion leaves derived summary copies recoverable in approximately 20% of instances. Only full-pipeline purge or tombstone redaction drives worst-tier residue to zero, demonstrating that deleting raw storage alone is insufficient when derived memory representations persist.

Conclusion: Persistent agent memory constitutes a first-class memorization mechanism that must be evaluated along three axes: what it helps agents recall (utility), what it makes extractable (privacy risk), and what it can truly erase (deletion fidelity). The three metrics — PR, AER, and FRS — provide a principled framework for navigating this design space, with aggressive summarization emerging as an effective privacy-utility operating point, provided deletion is performed across all memory tiers.

Abstract: Foundation-model agents are increasingly long-lived systems that remember users across interactions, making memorization an explicit deployment-time function rather than solely a property of model weights. Existing work addresses parametric memorization or audits fixed memory configurations, but does not characterize how memory-design choices jointly shape personalization utility, extraction risk, and deletion fidelity. We study this surface as deployment-time memorization, formulating agent memory as a privacy-utility frontier measured by Personalization Recall (PR) and Adversarial Extraction Rate (AER), and sweeping three memory-design knobs: summarization aggressiveness, retrieval breadth (k), and deletion mode. We further introduce the Forgetting Residue Score (FRS) to quantify whether deleted information remains recoverable from derived memory tiers. On LongMemEval, key-fact summarization reduces canary extraction by 76% on Gemma 3 12B and 64% on GPT-4o-mini while preserving nearly all personalization recall; critically, once content is compressed away, increasing k no longer restores leakage. The same compression, however, induces a deletion-fidelity failure: raw-only deletion leaves derived summary copies recoverable in approximately 20% of instances, and only full-pipeline purge or tombstone redaction drives worst-tier residue to zero. Together, these results establish that persistent agent memory must be evaluated as a first-class memorization mechanism -- assessed by what it helps agents recall, what it makes extractable, and what it can truly erase.

</details>


### [358] [Exploratory Responsiveness and Adaptive Rigidity under AI-Assisted Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10086)
*Balaraju Battu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper presents a dynamical theory of how AI-assisted optimization affects long-term adaptation. The key insight is that AI's impact depends on whether it substitutes for or amplifies a system's exploratory responsiveness—the capacity to traverse unfamiliar conceptual/institutional trajectories. Under convergent predictive regimes, AI reduces exploration, causing rigidity and trapping; under exploration-enhancing regimes, AI amplifies adaptive mobility. Systems with weak exploratory routines are most vulnerable to substitution effects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the long-run adaptive consequences of AI-assisted optimization, particularly the under-theorized mechanism by which predictive AI systems may erode or enhance the exploratory capacities of cognitive, institutional, and technological systems navigating complex epistemic landscapes.

Method: A dynamical systems framework modeling cognitive, institutional, and technological evolution over rugged epistemic landscapes with multiple locally reinforced configurations. The model centers on a key state variable—adaptive responsiveness—and analyzes how the AI substitution parameter interacts with this variable to produce distinct dynamical regimes: metastable trapping, hysteresis, premature convergence, and exploration-collapse, versus exploration-enhancing regimes.

Result: The effective substitution effect is responsiveness-dependent: weak-exploratory systems are vulnerable to exploratory substitution and collapse into locally efficient but globally rigid states; systems with high adaptive responsiveness can leverage AI to expand exploratory mobility. Long-run outcomes depend on institutional structure, developmental context, and human-machine interaction architecture, not just AI capability.

Conclusion: AI's long-run adaptive effects are contingent on the interaction between predictive assistance and the pre-existing exploratory responsiveness of the system. Policy and design should therefore attend to institutional structure and interaction architecture to steer outcomes toward exploration-enhancing rather than exploration-substituting regimes.

Abstract: This paper develops a theory of exploratory adaptation under AI-assisted optimization. The central argument is that the long-run adaptive effects of AI systems depend critically on how predictive assistance interacts with exploratory responsiveness itself. We formalize this mechanism using a dynamical framework in which cognitive, institutional, and technological systems evolve over rugged epistemic landscapes characterized by multiple locally reinforced configurations. A central state variable in the model is adaptive responsiveness, which measures the capacity of a system to traverse unfamiliar conceptual and institutional trajectories under changing conditions. Under convergent predictive regimes, AI systems substitute for exploratory engagement, reducing adaptive responsiveness and generating metastable trapping, hysteresis, premature convergence, and exploration-collapse dynamics in which systems become locally efficient but globally rigid. The framework also identifies contrasting exploration-enhancing regimes in which AI systems amplify exploratory search, conceptual traversal, and adaptive mobility. The effective substitution parameter is therefore responsiveness-dependent: systems possessing weak exploratory routines are more vulnerable to exploratory substitution, whereas systems already possessing high adaptive responsiveness may use AI assistance to expand exploratory mobility across rugged landscapes. The long-run adaptive effects of AI consequently depend not only on AI capability itself, but also on institutional structure, developmental context, and the architecture of human-machine interaction.

</details>


### [359] [Predictive Assistance and the Temporal Dynamics of Exploratory Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10094)
*Balaraju Battu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper develops a geometric dynamical framework modeling how predictive AI systems reshape exploratory cognition by compressing search trajectories before endogenous exploration unfolds, showing that early predictive stabilization can reduce exploratory responsiveness, create hysteresis effects, and narrow future representational diversification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical theories treat problem solving as exploratory search through problem spaces where structure emerges gradually. Predictive AI systems invert this dynamic by supplying solutions before internal exploration occurs, potentially altering the fundamental geometry of cognitive search. The motivation is to formally model this new regime and understand its consequences for learning, adaptability, and representational development.

Method: A geometric dynamical framework where attention evolves over a strategy landscape governed by three forces: stabilizing drift, endogenous exploratory perturbation, and responsiveness-gated learning. Predictive assistance is formalized as exogenous exploratory compression that stabilizes trajectories before self-generated exploration can broaden the accessible strategy space.

Result: Three main results: (1) Sustained predictive stabilization reduces exploratory responsiveness by attenuating intrinsic perturbations even when variability persists. (2) Curvature accumulates and relaxes asymmetrically, producing hysteresis and delayed recovery of exploratory mobility after assistance withdrawal. (3) Developmental outcomes are timing-dependent: early stabilization narrows future exploratory traversal before broad diversification occurs.

Conclusion: Predictive AI systems may fundamentally reshape the geometry of exploratory cognition. The framework yields testable predictions regarding exploratory entropy, premature convergence, and delayed recovery following predictive stabilization, suggesting that the timing and duration of predictive assistance carry critical developmental consequences.

Abstract: Classical theories of cognition describe problem solving as exploratory search through structured problem spaces in which repeated interaction gradually compresses search into efficient representational structures. Predictive artificial intelligence systems introduce a distinct regime in which stabilization may occur before exploratory diversification unfolds, supplying solutions and decision trajectories prior to internally generated search. This paper develops a geometric dynamical framework in which attention evolves over a landscape of strategies shaped by stabilizing drift, endogenous exploratory perturbation, and responsiveness-gated learning. Predictive assistance is modeled as a process of exogenous exploratory compression that stabilizes trajectories before self-generated exploration broadens the accessible regions of strategy space. The framework yields three main results. First, sustained predictive stabilization reduces exploratory responsiveness by attenuating the effective influence of intrinsic perturbations even when exploratory variability remains present. Second, curvature accumulates and relaxes asymmetrically, producing hysteresis and delayed recovery of exploratory mobility after assistance withdrawal. Third, developmental outcomes depend critically on the timing of stabilization, with early intervention narrowing future exploratory traversal before broad representational diversification has occurred. The framework generates empirically testable predictions concerning exploratory entropy, premature convergence, and delayed recovery following predictive stabilization. More broadly, the results suggest that predictive systems may reshape the geometry of exploratory cognition itself.

</details>


### [360] [From Senses to Decisions: The Information Flow of Auditory and Visual Perception in Multimodal LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10147)
*Wish Suharitdamrong,Muhammad Awais,Xiatian Zhu,Sara Atito*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper maps how audio and visual information flows through Audio-Visual Large Language Models (AVLLMs), revealing that video inputs follow a sequential pathway proportional to modality reliance, while interleaved audio-visual items trigger parallel routing streams, and shows that audio-visual tokens can be pruned after information transfer with minimal performance loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite the growing deployment of MLLMs that process both audio and visual inputs, the internal mechanisms governing how these modalities traverse the network, interact, and contribute to predictions remain opaque. Understanding these pathways is critical for interpretability, efficient inference, and principled model design.

Method: The authors analyze information flow in two AVLLMs (Qwen2.5-Omni and Video-SALMONN2 Plus at 3B and 7B scales) across two input configurations: (1) audio-visual video, and (2) multiple interleaved audio-visual items. They trace token-level contributions through the network, examining routing patterns, modality utilization, and the effects of discarding audio-visual tokens at various stages.

Result: For video inputs, AVLLMs follow the established sequential information flow pathway seen in VLMs/VideoLLMs, with audio and visual contributions proportional to task modality reliance. For interleaved multi-item settings, routing shifts to parallel streams. Audio-visual and other non-text tokens can be discarded once their information is transferred to the LLM backbone, with negligible or slightly positive impact on prediction quality across tasks and datasets.

Conclusion: The work provides the first coherent mechanistic account of audio-visual information flow in AVLLMs, demonstrating modality-dependent routing strategies that vary by input structure. The finding that non-text tokens are disposable post information-transfer enables more efficient inference and lays groundwork for future interpretability and architectural advances in multimodal LLMs.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) can listen and see, but how do audio and visual signals actually travel through the network to shape an answer? Despite their growing role in research and real-world applications, the internal pathways through which audio and visual tokens influence the final prediction remain poorly understood. In this study, we examine audio-visual information flow inside Audio-Visual Large Language Models (AVLLMs), tracing how AVLLMs route, utilize, and integrate audio and visual information across two input configurations, audio-visual video and multiple interleaved audio-visual items. We find that for audio-visual video, AVLLMs follow the sequential information flow pathway established for VLMs and VideoLLMs, with audio and visual contribution flowing along this pathway in proportion to the task's reliance on each modality. In settings with multiple interleaved audio-visual items, this routing shifts to different parallel streams. Furthermore, we demonstrate that audio-visual and other token types can be discarded once their information is transferred to LLM, with minimal impact on the model's prediction or even slight improvement, generalizing across multiple tasks and datasets, enabling more efficient inference. These findings hold across multiple models and scales, Qwen2.5-Omni and Video-SALMONN2 Plus at 3B and 7B scales, leading to hypotheses on why these flow structures emerge. Together, these results deliver the first coherent picture of how AVLLMs orchestrate sound and sight inside the network and lay the groundwork for the next wave of interpretability, design, and efficiency advances in audio-visual and broader MLLMs.

</details>


### [361] [Less Context, Better Agents: Efficient Context Engineering for Long-Horizon Tool-Using LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10209)
*Abhilasha Lodha,Mahsa Pahlavikhah Varnosfaderani,Abir Chakraborty,Abhinav Mithal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: In enterprise AI workflows, verbose tool responses cause context overflow and inefficiency. Evaluating GPT-5 on an expense itemization benchmark, the study finds that pruning conversation history to the last 5 tool calls plus automated summarization achieves the best performance (91.6% completion, 99.64% amount itemized) while drastically reducing token usage (553K vs. 1.48M) and runtime (5.79 vs. 14.56 hours) compared to full-context retention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous LLM agents in enterprise settings (specifically Microsoft Dynamics 365) suffer from verbose tool responses that lead to context-window overflow, stale-state errors, and high inference costs. Existing approaches either discard context (hurting accuracy) or retain full history (prohibitively expensive), motivating the search for an optimal context-engineering strategy.

Method: The authors design a 50-task hotel expense benchmark using Model Context Protocol tools in Microsoft Dynamics 365 Finance and Operations. They evaluate four GPT-5 configurations: (1) no user model, (2) full conversation history, (3) context pruned to the last 5 tool call/response pairs, and (4) pruning plus automated summarization. Each configuration is run 5 times with the user model held constant. Metrics include completion rate, amount-itemized percentage, token consumption, and runtime. Additional analyses cover confidence intervals, effect sizes, sensitivity to pruning/summary window sizes, failure modes, expense-type breakdowns (five types across three categories), and cross-model validation with Claude Sonnet 4.5.

Result: The no-user-model baseline achieves only 8.0% completion. Full-context retention reaches 71.0% but consumes 1,480,996 tokens and 14.56 hours. Pruning to the last 5 tool calls improves completion to 79.0% while cutting tokens to 535,274 and runtime to 5.39 hours. Adding summarization yields the best results: 91.6% complete itemization, 99.64% average amount itemized, 553,374 tokens, and 5.79 hours. These findings hold across sensitivity analyses, expense categories, and are corroborated by Claude Sonnet 4.5.

Conclusion: For enterprise tool-use workflows characterized by verbose tool responses, selective retention of recent tool interactions combined with compact summarization outperforms both naive pruning and full-history retention, simultaneously improving task reliability and computational efficiency.

Abstract: Large language models deployed as autonomous agents for enterprise workflows face a key challenge: verbose tool responses from enterprise systems can cause context overflow, stale-state errors, and high inference cost. We study this problem in automated expense itemization in Microsoft Dynamics 365 Finance and Operations using Model Context Protocol tools. We evaluate four GPT-5 configurations on a 50-task hotel expense benchmark: no user model, full conversation history, context pruned to the last 5 tool call/response pairs, and pruning with automated summarization. Results are averaged across 5 independent runs, with the user model held constant for the context-engineering comparison. The no-user-model baseline achieves only 8.0% complete itemization. Full-context retention improves completion to 71.0%, but consumes 1,480,996 tokens and 14.56 hours per benchmark. Pruning to the last 5 tool calls improves completion to 79.0% while reducing token use to 535,274 and runtime to 5.39 hours. Adding summarization achieves the best result: 91.6% complete itemization and 99.64% average amount itemized, with 553,374 tokens and 5.79 hours. We further report confidence intervals, effect-size analysis, sensitivity over pruning and summary windows, failure analysis, results across five expense types grouped into three categories, and cross-model evidence with Claude Sonnet 4.5. These results show that, for this class of enterprise tool-use workflow, selective retention of recent tool interactions plus compact summarization can improve both reliability and efficiency compared with full-history retention.

</details>


### [362] [Minimalist Genetic Programming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10237)
*Leonardo Trujillo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Minimalist Genetic Programming (MGP) replaces evolution in GP with a syntactic derivation process inspired by the Minimalist Program in linguistics, using a binary MERGE operator to incrementally build symbolic expressions, and demonstrates superior performance over standard GP on difficult symbolic regression tasks with bloat issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard GP relies on evolutionary search for program induction, which often suffers from bloat and struggles on certain symbolic regression tasks. The authors propose rethinking the second core insight of GP—posing program induction as a syntactic derivation problem rather than a search problem—drawing from linguistic theory to achieve more efficient and precise model construction.

Method: MGP adopts the Minimalist Program from human language theory, where syntax is viewed as an optimal linking mechanism between mental systems. The core operator is MERGE, a binary set formation operator that incrementally constructs complex syntactic structures via a Markovian process. MGP discovers core building blocks of symbolic expressions and combines them using MERGE, guided by a properly chosen lexicon of atomic syntactic objects.

Result: On symbolic regression benchmarks known for inducing bloat in standard GP, MGP consistently produced the exact ground truth model, outperforming standard GP systems.

Conclusion: Minimalist linguistic principles offer valuable insights for program induction. MGP's strong performance suggests that the Minimalist Program framework merits further exploration in genetic programming and related areas.

Abstract: Genetic programming (GP) is based on two important insights. First, that any learning task can fundamentally be posed as a program induction problem, where the goal is to construct a symbolic hierarchical model that is expressed as a syntax tree. Second, to pose this task as a search problem, and use evolution to locate the desired model. Since it was proposed, GP has produced notable results in a wide range of tasks and problem domains. This work presents an alternative view by modifying the second core insight of GP, posing the problem as a syntactic derivation task instead. In particular, this paper presents Minimalist Genetic Programming (MGP), an algorithm that like GP is biologically inspired, but instead of evolution it takes inspiration from the Minimalist Program to human language, in which syntax is understood as an optimal solution to the problem of linking two other mental systems. In minimalism, the core computational process is a binary set formation operator called $MERGE$, than can be used to incrementally construct complex syntactic structures using a simple Markovian process. MGP is able to discover the core building blocks of the symbolic expressions, and to incrementally combined them using $MERGE$. The proposed system is benchmarked on symbolic regression tasks that are known to be difficult to solve with standard GP systems because of the propensity for bloat. Results show that when a proper lexicon of atomic syntactic objects are chosen, MGP is able to consistently produce the exact ground truth model on a set of symbolic regression where standard GP struggles to do the same.
