A global optimizer (differential evolution) also reaches this optimum, so the LLM is not the only route; its advantage is sample efficiency and interpretability: a usable controller in 18 evaluations (where the global optimizer is worse than open loop) plus a stated rationale. This edge grows with dimension, reaching ~6x fewer evaluations on a 3x3 plant. The behaviour generalizes across four open models, and on a benign plant the LLM offers no advantage, sharpening the boundary. We contribute a reproducible benchmark delimiting when open LLMs help in control tuning: not as optimizers, but as a sample-efficient, interpretable structural prior.

</details>


### [407] [Superficial Beliefs in LLM Decision-Making](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11016)
*Gabriel Freedman,Francesca Toni*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs exhibit "superficial belief" in binary decisions — their choices are systematic and predictable from visible attributes, but their self-reported reasons only partially align with the attributes that behaviourally drive those choices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether LLM decision-making in binary choices reflects genuine, articulable reasoning or merely imitative pattern-matching, by testing the alignment between what models *say* drove their choice versus what a behavioural model infers actually drove it.

Method: Synthetic binary decision settings where LLMs choose between profiles with graded attributes. A behavioural model is fit to prior choices to identify which attribute best explains decisions. This behaviourally inferred driver is compared against: (1) the model's direct self-report of what mattered most, and (2) a separate score-based judge. Robustness is tested via prompt-order perturbations, sampling variation, alternative behavioural models, occlusion analyses, and varied decision structures.

Result: The behavioural model predicts held-out choices well, confirming systematic (non-random) behaviour tied to visible attributes. However, both direct self-reports and the score-based judge only imperfectly recover the behaviourally inferred driver. This dissociation between predicted structure and explicit reasons holds robustly across all perturbations and settings tested.

Conclusion: LLMs display "superficial belief": they behave as if guided by probabilistic local priorities over attributes (structured enough for prediction) but possess only limited verbal access to the attributes actually driving their decisions, suggesting a gap between behavioural consistency and introspective articulation.

Abstract: We ask whether large language models (LLMs) merely imitate rationales when choosing between two options, or whether their choices reflect a systematic underlying decision structure. Using synthetic binary decision settings in which models choose between profiles defined by graded attributes, we compare the attribute a model says mattered most with the attribute that best explains its choice under a behavioural model fit to prior decisions. The behavioural model predicts held-out choices well, showing that model behaviour is systematically related to the visible attributes rather than being random. However, direct self-reports and a separate score-based judge recover the behaviourally inferred driver only partially. The resulting picture is neither one of arbitrary behaviour nor one of fully articulated belief - outputs are structured enough to support prediction, but explicit reasons track the recovered driver only imperfectly. This qualitative pattern persists across prompt-order and sampling perturbations, alternative behavioural models, targeted occlusion analyses, and structurally varied decision settings. We interpret this as evidence for ``superficial belief'' in LLM decision-making: models behave as if guided by probabilistic local priorities over attributes, while having only limited verbal access to the attributes that drive their decisions.

</details>


### [408] [Workflow-GYM: Towards Long-Horizon Evaluation of Computer-use Agentic tasks in Real-World Professional Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11042)
*Liya Zhu,Jingzhe Ding,Jian Zhang,Jianbo Xue,Shihao Liang,Ge Zhang,Xiang Gao,Qingshui Gu,Mailun Gao,Huimin Che,Yan Zhao,Peiheng Zhou,Haojun Wang,Chaobo Xian,Lili Le,Chi Wu,Yiwei Liu,Shengda Long,Jiale Yang,Fangzhi Xu,Sijin Wu,Haodong Duan,Yi Zhu,Chao He,Zhaojian Li,Minchao Wang,Huan Zhou,Jiani Hou,Chuqian Yu,Weiran Shi,Hongwan Gao,Jiamin Chen,Guanhong Chen,Tingqin Luo,Kaiyuan Zhang,Zhixin Yao,Qing Hua,Yuhao Jiang,Jin Chen,Pu Chen,Zhenyu Hu,Xingyu Li,Zhengxuan Jiang,Meng Cao,Tianfeng Long,Haozhe Wang,Mingzhang Wang,Yichen Zhang,Yiming Dai,Chenchen Zhang,Jiaying Wang,Zhiyong Wu,Shen Yan,Yujia Qin,Wenhao Huang,Zaiyuan Wang,Xiaolong Chang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Workflow-GYM is a benchmark for evaluating AI agents on long-horizon GUI tasks in professional, domain-specific software. Even the strongest models achieve only ~30% success rates, revealing major challenges in workflow consistency, error propagation, objective drift, and environment understanding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GUI benchmarks focus on general-purpose software with simple, short-horizon tasks, leaving a gap in evaluating whether AI agents can autonomously operate domain-specific professional software to complete economically valuable, end-to-end workflows.

Method: The authors introduce Workflow-GYM, a benchmark designed for long-horizon GUI tasks in professional domains using specialized software environments. They conduct extensive experiments with state-of-the-art models to evaluate performance on these workflows.

Result: Even the strongest current models achieve only slightly above 30% success rates. Detailed failure analysis reveals four key issues: workflow stage omission, error propagation, objective drift, and insufficient understanding of professional software environments.

Conclusion: Professional long-horizon GUI workflows remain highly challenging for current GUI agents. The findings highlight fundamental limitations in maintaining workflow consistency and suggest critical directions for advancing next-generation GUI-agent research.

Abstract: Recent years have witnessed the rapid evolution of AI agents toward handling increasingly complex, real-world tasks. However, existing benchmarks rarely evaluate whether agents can operate graphical user interfaces to complete long-horizon, high-value professional workflows across diverse domains. Current GUI benchmarks still predominantly focus on general-purpose software, relatively simple applications, and short-horizon tasks, leaving it largely unknown whether modern agents can follow user instructions to autonomously operate domain-specific professional software and accomplish economically valuable work in an end-to-end manner. To bridge this gap, we introduce Workflow-GYM, a benchmark for long-horizon GUI tasks centered on professional domains and specialized software environments. Through extensive experiments on state-of-the-art models, we find that even the strongest models achieve only slightly above 30% success rates, highlighting that professional long-horizon GUI workflows remain highly challenging for current GUI agents. Further analysis reveals that current agents struggle to maintain long-horizon workflow consistency, frequently exhibiting workflow stage omission, error propagation, objective drift, and insufficient understanding of professional software environments. Our findings provide important insights into the limitations of current agent systems and suggest key directions for the next generation of GUI-agent research.

</details>


### [409] [What Fits (Into Few Tokens) Doesn't Overfit: Compression and Generalization in ML Research Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11045)
*Martin Andres Bertran,Aaron Roth,Zhiwei Steven Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper investigates why benchmark-driven ML research rarely overfits to held-out benchmarks, hypothesizing that successful ML strategies are highly compressible. Using LLM-driven research agents with two information bottlenecks (output compression via short prompts, input compression via one-bit feedback), the authors show across 8 diverse datasets that high-performance models can be reproduced and discovered under severe information constraints. The hypothesis is falsifiable: deliberately induced overfitting breaks reproducibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite the theoretical risk that adaptively reusing held-out benchmarks should cause overfitting, the ML community has observed remarkably little overfitting in practice. The authors propose and test a compressibility hypothesis: successful strategies occupy a low-complexity region of strategy space, making them robust to information bottlenecks and resistant to overfitting.

Method: The paper employs LLM-driven research agents as experimenters and introduces two complementary information bottlenecks: (1) **Output compression**: an exploration agent searches for high-performance models using a validation set, then a separate "reproducer agent" attempts to replicate the result given only a short prompt plus training data; (2) **Input compression**: the explorer receives only 1-bit feedback per submission (whether it beats the current best). The framework is tested across 8 datasets covering tabular classification, vision, language modeling, diffusion modeling, and reward modeling. Falsification is tested by deliberately inducing validation-set overfitting.

Result: Both information bottlenecks have minimal impact on performance: short prompts suffice for reproducing high-performance models, and 1-bit feedback suffices for discovering them. When validation-set overfitting is deliberately induced, results fail to reproduce under short prompts, confirming the hypothesis is falsifiable.

Conclusion: The findings support a description-length based explanation: successful ML strategies are inherently low-complexity and thus compressible. This explains the empirical robustness of benchmark-driven ML against overfitting. The compressibility of good strategies may be a fundamental property of the ML strategy space rather than an artifact of community practices.

Abstract: Reusing a held-out benchmark adaptively should, in principle, invite overfitting. Yet benchmark-driven machine learning (ML) has produced surprisingly little overfitting in practice. An attractive hypothesis is that successful ML strategies are highly compressible. We study this in the setting of LLM-driven research agents, where the hypothesis becomes directly testable via two complementary information bottlenecks. In \emph{output compression}, an exploration agent adaptively searches for high-performance models using a validation set, and we test whether a fresh ``reproducer agent'' can reproduce its performance given only an extremely short prompt and the training data. In \emph{input compression}, the explorer receives only one-bit feedback indicating whether each submitted model improves on the running best. Across 8 datasets spanning tabular classification, vision, language modeling, diffusion modeling, and reward modeling, we find that these bottlenecks have little effect on performance: short prompts and compressible feedback are sufficient to reproduce and find high-performance models. The hypothesis is falsifiable: when we deliberately induce validation-set overfitting, the results fail to reproduce with short prompts. Taken together, our results support a description-length explanation for the lack of overfitting in benchmark-driven ML: successful strategies occupy a low-complexity region of strategy space.

</details>


### [410] [CIAware-Bench: Benchmarking Control Intervention Awareness Across Frontier LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11063)
*Joachim Schaeffer,Thomas Jiralerspong,Alexander Panfilov,Guillaume Lajoie,Jonas Geiping,Yoshua Bengio,Roland S. Zimmermann*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CIAware-Bench is a benchmark that measures whether frontier AI models can detect when their outputs have been modified by a trusted control/monitor model, finding that detection accuracy varies significantly across models and tasks, with models generally better at detecting interventions from different model families.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI control protocols rely on trusted models to monitor and intervene on untrusted models' actions. However, if the untrusted model becomes aware of these interventions, it could infer properties of the monitor and adapt its behavior to evade future control. Measuring this "control intervention awareness" is critical for designing robust, hard-to-detect control protocols.

Method: The authors introduce CIAware-Bench, a benchmark comprising four task domains (essay writing, BigCodeBench, Bash Arena, SHADE-Arena) with systematic variations in trajectory watermarking, side-task presence, and control protocol type. The benchmark frames CI awareness as a binary classification task: can a model distinguish its own unmodified trajectories from those altered by a control intervention? Eleven frontier models are evaluated across these conditions.

Result: Under default settings, CI awareness accuracy ranges up to 0.87 (chance = 0.5), indicating low to moderate but non-negligible detection capability. Awareness varies substantially across task domains and model pairs. Detection is generally easier when the untrusted model and the monitor belong to different model families, suggesting models exploit provider-specific stylistic or post-training differences.

Conclusion: CI awareness is not a fixed, intrinsic property of a model; it is highly context-dependent and must be measured for each new model release and deployment scenario. CIAware-Bench is released as a tool to track this property and guide the design of control protocols with less detectable interventions.

Abstract: AI control protocols oversee untrusted models by monitoring their actions and modifying potentially unsafe steps, often using a trusted model. This partially tampers with the untrusted model's trajectory. If the trusted model detects such an intervention, it may infer properties of the monitor and adapt to evade control. We introduce \textbf{CIAware-Bench}, a benchmark for measuring \textbf{c}ontrol \textbf{i}ntervention (CI) awareness across frontier models. CIAware-Bench tests whether models can distinguish their own trajectories from those modified by a control intervention. The benchmark is comprised of a suite of four task domains (essay writing, BigCodeBench, Bash Arena, and SHADE-Arena), while varying trajectory watermarking, side-task presence, and the control protocol. Evaluating eleven frontier models, we find low to moderate CI awareness under default settings (up to 0.87; random chance balanced binary classification accuracy is 0.5) with substantial variation across task domains and model pairs. Detection is generally easier across model families, suggesting that models exploit provider-specific differences in style or post-training. Overall, CI awareness is not a fixed model-level property, and should be measured for each new model release and deployment scenario. We release CIAware-Bench to track CI awareness and inform control protocols whose interventions are harder to detect.

</details>


### [411] [A History-Aware Visually Grounded Critic for Computer Use Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11078)
*Jaewoo Lee,Zaid Khan,Archiki Prasad,Justin Chih-Yao Chen,Supriyo Chakraborty,Kartik Balasubramaniam,Sambit Sahu,Elias Stengel-Eskin,Hyunji Lee,Mohit Bansal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HiViG is a test-time framework for Computer Use Agents that combines macro-action history summarization with visually grounded critique to intercept flawed GUI actions before execution, outperforming existing critics by 5.8–9.0% across web, mobile, and desktop benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing test-time critic models for GUI agents suffer from two key limitations: (1) they are short-sighted, focusing on immediate decisions while forgetting earlier actions in long-horizon tasks, and (2) they lack visual grounding, making them unable to verify whether actions like clicks actually target the correct UI elements. These weaknesses lead to accumulated planning errors and execution mistakes.

Method: HiViG is built around a multimodal critic trained on real GUI trajectories. It abstracts past interactions into a compact macro-action history (summarizing the policy's completed achievements) and provides visually grounded critique by verifying raw execution coordinates against the current screenshot. At test time, the critic is integrated into the policy decision loop: the macro-action history mitigates short-sighted planning, while the visually grounded critique intercepts erroneous actions (e.g., misaligned clicks) before execution.

Result: HiViG consistently outperforms existing scalar and verbal critics across web, mobile, and desktop benchmarks, achieving average success rate improvements of 5.8% for Qwen3-VL-32B and 9.0% for Gemini-3-Flash over the strongest baseline. Ablation studies confirm that both components are critical: macro-action history addresses short-sighted planning and visually grounded critique reduces execution errors, with both being essential for test-time scaling in long-horizon GUI tasks.

Conclusion: The HiViG framework demonstrates that combining history-aware summarization with visually grounded action verification is a highly effective test-time intervention for GUI agents, enabling significant performance gains and strong cross-platform generalization on long-horizon tasks.

Abstract: Various test-time interventions for Computer Use Agents (CUAs), including critic models, have been developed to improve performance through pre-execution action evaluation in complex Graphical User Interface (GUI) environments. However, existing critics suffer from two key limitations: they (1) focus primarily on short-sighted decision loops (e.g., forgetting earlier actions) and (2) lack the visual grounding needed to detect flawed actions (e.g., clicking wrong UI elements). To address these, we introduce HiViG, a History-aware Visually Grounded test-time framework, built around a multimodal critic trained on real GUI trajectories to abstract past interactions into a compact record and to evaluate actions with visual grounding. At test time, HiViG integrates the critic into the policy decision loop to provide macro-action history, which summarizes the policy's completed achievements, and visually grounded critique, which verifies raw execution coordinates against the current screenshot to intercept errors before execution. Across web, mobile, and desktop benchmarks, HiViG consistently outperforms existing scalar and verbal critics, improving average success rates over the strongest baseline by 5.8% for Qwen3-VL-32B and 9.0% for Gemini-3-Flash, and demonstrates strong cross-platform generalization. Ablations show that macro-action history mitigates short-sighted planning and visually grounded critique reduces execution errors, with both components being critical for test-time scaling in long-horizon GUI tasks.

</details>


### [412] [Monte Carlo Pass Search: Using Trajectory Generation for 3D Counterfactual Pass Evaluation in Football](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11120)
*Andrew Kang,Priya Narasimhan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces Monte Carlo Pass Search (MCPS), which frames football pass evaluation as an MCTS-like problem. Using Bundesliga 3D tracking data, MCPS samples pass variants, rolls them forward with a ball-conditioned world model (adapted from autonomous driving's SMART), and scores outcomes with a learned value model. This yields a value distribution, enabling two complementary execution-surplus scores (mean-based and percentile-based) for nuanced pass attribution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional pass evaluation in football typically relies on binary outcomes (completion/success) or simple expected possession value, failing to capture the full distribution of potential outcomes and the nuanced execution quality that separates similar passes. The authors aim to provide distribution-aware attribution that distinguishes whether a pass succeeded due to excellent execution or despite poor execution, and whether it failed due to poor execution or despite good execution.

Method: The method, Monte Carlo Pass Search (MCPS), has three components: (1) inferring kick parameters from observed passes, then sampling execution variants (with noise) and option variants (alternative passes); (2) rolling each candidate forward using a ball-conditioned world model (SMART, adapted from autonomous driving, a discrete-token autoregressive trajectory generator) until the next ball interaction; (3) scoring outcomes with a learned value model to obtain a distribution over gained value. Two execution-surplus scores are derived: mean-based (expected value difference) and percentile-based (probability of outperforming the original outcome).

Result: The adapted SMART world model achieves strong best-of-20 forecasting accuracy compared to baselines despite limited public data, while supporting fully hypothetical rollouts. The MCPS framework produces interpretable execution-surplus scores that enable fine-grained pass analysis and ranking.

Conclusion: Recasting pass evaluation as MCTS-like search with distribution-aware attribution provides richer, more actionable insights than traditional methods. The release of model checkpoints and code facilitates further research. The adapted SMART model proves that discrete-token trajectory generation can be sample-efficient for sports analytics under data constraints.

Abstract: We recast pass evaluation in football (soccer) as a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)-like evaluation problem whose components mostly exist in the literature under different names: a value model (possession value), a world model (multi-agent trajectories with ball interactions), and a policy over counterfactual actions (sampling pass variants with noise). Building on the first public high-fidelity tracking dataset with 3D ball trajectories from the Bundesliga, we introduce Monte Carlo Pass Search (MCPS), which infers kick parameters for each observed pass, samples execution variants and option variants, rolls each candidate forward with a ball-conditioned world model until the next ball interaction, and scores outcomes with a learned value model to obtain a distribution over gained value. This distribution enables distribution-aware attribution with two complementary execution-surplus scores used for analysis and ranking: mean-based and percentile-based scores. To make the world model sample-efficient under limited public data, we adapt a discrete-token, autoregressive trajectory generator from autonomous driving (SMART) and show it yields strong best-of-20 forecasting accuracy compared to baselines, while supporting fully hypothetical rollouts for downstream evaluation. We have released model checkpoints and code.

</details>


### [413] [ABC-Bench: An Agentic Bio-Capabilities Benchmark for Biosecurity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11150)
*Andrew Bo Liu,Samira Nedungadi,Bryce Cai,Alex Kleinman,Harmon Bhasin,Seth Donoughe*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ABC-Bench is a benchmark evaluating LLM agents on dual-use biology tasks (liquid handling robot scripting, DNA fragment design, synthesis screening evasion). All tested agents outperformed human baseliners; wet-lab validation confirmed o4-mini-high could successfully assemble DNA via OpenTrons robot scripts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly capable of performing in silico biology tasks that previously required expert humans, creating both opportunities for biomedical advances and new biosecurity risks. There is a need to systematically measure and characterize these emerging agentic capabilities, particularly those with dual-use potential.

Method: The authors introduce ABC-Bench, comprising three tasks spanning benign and dual-use biology: (1) writing code to operate liquid handling robots, (2) designing DNA fragments for in vitro assembly, and (3) evading DNA synthesis screening. Tasks require combined biology and software expertise. LLM agents were evaluated against median expert human baseliners. Wet-lab validation used OpenAI's o4-mini-high scripts on an OpenTrons liquid handling robot to assemble DNA, with sequences confirmed by sequencing.

Result: All tested LLM agents outperformed the median expert human baseliner across all three tasks. Agents excelled on tasks leveraging published knowledge and well-documented protocols but performed more weakly on tasks requiring novel bioinformatics reasoning. In wet-lab validation, o4-mini-high-generated scripts successfully assembled DNA with the expected sequences when executed on an OpenTrons robot.

Conclusion: LLM agents now possess tangible, experimentally validated capabilities relevant to both legitimate biological research and potential misuse. While performance on known-protocol tasks is strong, novel reasoning remains a limitation. Systematic benchmarking via tools like ABC-Bench is essential for monitoring the evolving biosecurity risk landscape as these capabilities continue to advance.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are rapidly acquiring capabilities relevant to biological research, from literature synthesis to interpretation of experimental data. Increasingly, LLM agents can also perform in silico biology tasks that previously required experienced human biologists. These emerging AI capabilities offer new opportunities for scientific discovery and biomedical advances, but they also shift the landscape of biosecurity risks. To address this, we introduce the Agentic Bio-Capabilities Benchmark (ABC-Bench), a suite of tasks to measure agentic biosecurity-relevant capabilities. ABC-Bench evaluates LLM agents on both benign and dual-use biology tasks: writing code to operate liquid handling robots, designing DNA fragments for in vitro assembly, and evading DNA synthesis screening. These tasks require a combination of biology and software expertise. All tested LLM agents outperformed the median expert human baseliner on all three tasks. Agents performed highly on tasks drawing on published knowledge and well-documented protocols, and more weakly on a task requiring novel bioinformatics reasoning. In three wet-lab validation experiments, we found that OpenAI's o4-mini-high produced scripts that, when run on an OpenTrons liquid handling robot, successfully assembled DNA with expected sequences.

</details>


### [414] [ReasonAlloc: Hierarchical Decoding-Time KV Cache Budget Allocation for Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11164)
*Wenhao Liu,Hao Shi,Yunhe Li,Weizhi Fei,Xiangyuan Wang,Mengzhe Ruan,Hanxu Hou,Peisong Wang,Linqi Song,Shuang Qiu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ReasonAlloc is a training-free, two-level KV cache budget allocation framework for long CoT reasoning that combines offline layer-wise preallocation (capturing a "Reasoning Wave" demand pattern) with online head-wise reallocation to outperform uniform and static non-uniform baselines, especially at small budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long chain-of-thought reasoning in LLMs causes severe KV cache growth, creating inference bottlenecks. Existing decoding-time compression methods use uniform budget distribution across layers/heads, while non-uniform methods are designed for the static prefill phase and fail to capture the stepwise, dynamic context demands of autoregressive reasoning.

Method: ReasonAlloc formulates decoding-time KV compression as a hierarchical budget allocation problem: (1) an offline layer-wise preallocation strategy that identifies an architecture-driven "Reasoning Wave" demand pattern, and (2) an online head-wise reallocation strategy that dynamically redistributes resources to information-rich heads based on real-time utility during decoding. The framework is training-free and plug-and-play with existing token-eviction policies.

Result: Evaluated on MATH-500 and AIME 2024 using DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-14B, and AceReason-14B, ReasonAlloc consistently outperforms uniform-budget R-KV, SnapKV, and Pyramid-RKV, with the largest gains observed at small budgets (128–512 tokens).

Conclusion: ReasonAlloc effectively addresses the KV cache bottleneck in long CoT reasoning by hierarchically allocating budgets based on architectural demand patterns and real-time head utility, achieving superior compression-performance trade-offs with negligible inference overhead and seamless integration with existing eviction policies.

Abstract: Long chain-of-thought (CoT) trajectories in large language model (LLM) reasoning cause severe inference bottlenecks due to rapid key-value (KV) cache growth. Current decoding-time compression methods mitigate this issue via token eviction, but typically assume a uniform budget distribution across all layers and heads. In contrast, existing non-uniform budget allocation methods are predominantly designed for the static prompt prefill phase, and they do not capture the stepwise context demands of autoregressive reasoning. To bridge this gap, we propose ReasonAlloc, a training-free framework that recasts decoding-time KV compression as a hierarchical budget allocation problem. ReasonAlloc operates at two complementary levels: an offline layer-wise preallocation strategy captures an architecture-driven demand pattern which we call ``\textit{Reasoning Wave}'', while an online head-wise strategy reallocates resources during decoding to information-rich heads based on real-time utility. Evaluations on mathematical reasoning benchmarks (MATH-500, AIME~2024) using DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-14B, and AceReason-14B show that ReasonAlloc outperforms uniform-budget R-KV, SnapKV, and Pyramid-RKV (a baseline enforcing a static, monotonically decreasing layer budget), with the largest gains at small budgets (128-512 tokens). ReasonAlloc is plug-and-play with existing token-eviction policies and introduces negligible inference-time overhead.

</details>


### [415] [The Role of Feedback Alignment in Self-Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11173)
*Semih Kara,Oğuzhan Ersoy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper investigates how the design of conditioning context affects self-distillation in language models. The authors find that step-aligned critique—feedback structurally aligned with the solver's reasoning trace—yields the largest performance gains, outperforming binary rewards (GRPO) by 16.11 points and reference-solution conditioning by 5.27 points. Per-token advantage analysis shows that aligned feedback selectively targets failing tokens while preserving correct behavior, whereas reference solutions pressure changes at every token.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-distillation improves language model responses by training the model to retain improvements from conditioning context even when that context is absent. However, what the model learns depends heavily on what context the self-teacher receives, and the design of this context has been largely unexplored. The authors aim to systematically study context design to understand what drives self-distillation effectiveness.

Method: The authors train a solver model using feedback from a frozen critic and compare three context conditions for self-distillation: (i) a binary reward (GRPO), (ii) the reference solution, and (iii) a step-by-step critique aligned to the solver's own reasoning trace. They evaluate performance and conduct per-token advantage analysis to understand how each condition affects token-level learning dynamics.

Result: Step-aligned critique achieves the largest gains: +16.11 points over GRPO and +5.27 points over reference-solution-conditioned self-distillation (Avg@12). Per-token advantage analysis reveals that step-aligned feedback selectively targets only tokens where reasoning fails while leaving correct tokens intact. In contrast, conditioning on the reference solution pressures the model to alter behavior at every token, including correct steps, because an alternative derivation inevitably differs in phrasing and approach.

Conclusion: Structural alignment between feedback and the solver's reasoning trace is a key driver of self-distillation effectiveness. Step-aligned critique outperforms both binary reward signals and reference-solution conditioning by precisely targeting reasoning errors without disturbing correct behavior.

Abstract: Conditioning a language model on additional context, such as feedback on a previous attempt, typically improves its response. Self-distillation trains the model to retain this improvement when the context is not present. The method works by matching the model's output distribution under two settings: a student that sees only the question, and a self-teacher that also sees the context. What the model learns therefore depends on what context the self-teacher receives, yet the design of this context remains largely unexplored.
  We study context design for self-distillation by training a solver on feedback from a frozen critic. We compare three conditions: (i) a binary reward (GRPO), (ii) the reference solution, and (iii) a step-by-step critique aligned to the solver's reasoning trace.
  Step-aligned critique yields the largest gains, outperforming GRPO by 16.11 points and reference-solution-conditioned self-distillation by 5.27 points (Avg@12). Per-token advantage analysis reveals why: step-aligned feedback targets only the tokens where reasoning fails, leaving correct behavior intact. Conditioning on the reference solution, by contrast, pressures the model to change its behavior at every token (even correct steps) because an alternative derivation inevitably differs in phrasing and approach. This suggests that structural alignment between feedback and the solver's reasoning is a key driver of self-distillation effectiveness.

</details>
